De Wynter's machine is \emph{actually built and trained}, and it is aimed not at the metaphysics of consciousness but at the \textbf{methodology of attribution}: he draws no conclusion that the game lacks (or has) an inner life, and explicitly denies that AoE2 has human-like attributes. The bite is epistemic, not phenomenal. It is also distinct from Block's \hyperref[entry:concept-lookup-table-mind]{\textsc{Lookup-table mind}}, which produces the right \emph{outputs} with no inner processing; the perceptron here does the genuine processing, learning included.

\begin{plainterms}
Someone built a real, working computer inside the videogame \emph{Age of Empires II} — using herds of goats as the 1s and 0s and sheets of ice to keep them in step — and on top of it built and taught a tiny artificial ``brain cell'' of the kind that large AIs are made from. The serious point hiding in the joke: the exact same little learning machine that powers a chatbot can be made out of goats. So when people say a chatbot ``understands'' or ``has morality'', you have to ask whether that is really in the machine or just in how we read it — because you would never say the goats understand anything. This is a cousin of the famous \hyperref[entry:concept-chinese-nation]{\textsc{Chinese nation}} thought experiment, but its punchline is about how we \emph{measure} such claims, not about whether the goats are secretly conscious.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-aoe2-anthropomorphism]{\textsc{Age of Empires II argument}} — the argument the construction powers: anthropomorphic attributes of LLMs are substrate-non-unique, so assuming them a priori is circular or uninformative.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-chinese-nation]{\textsc{Chinese nation}} — the imagined-population ancestor; aimed at absent qualia where this is aimed at the methodology of attribution.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-lookup-table-mind]{\textsc{Lookup-table mind}} — right outputs with no processing; the circuits, by contrast, do the real computation and learning.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-functionalism]{\textsc{Functionalism}} — the multiple-realizability of computation the construction dramatizes.
  \item \hyperref[source:source-dewynter-2026]{\textsc{de Wynter — If LLMs Have Human-Like Attributes, Then So Does Age of Empires II (2026)}} — the note and paper where the circuits and the proof are presented.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{concept-agent-causation}{Agent causation}{Concept}{Agent causation}{}{$\blacklozenge$}
\emph{Agent causation} is the thesis that a free action is caused not by prior events — desires, beliefs, neural states — but by the agent herself, a persisting substance, exercising an irreducible power to originate. On this view, when you freely raise your hand, the cause of the decision is not an earlier state of you; it is \emph{you}, full stop. Roderick Chisholm gave the doctrine its classic modern statement: in free action each of us is ``a prime mover unmoved,'' initiating causal chains without being caused to do so (Chisholm, \hyperref[source:source-chisholm-1964]{\textsc{1964}}).

The view is the most metaphysically ambitious form of libertarianism (see \hyperref[entry:position-libertarianism]{\textsc{Libertarianism (free will)}}). Its motivation is a felt inadequacy in event-causal pictures of agency: if a decision is just one more event caused by earlier events, the agent seems to be a place where causation happens rather than a source of it. Agent causation answers by adding a new primitive to the ontology — substance-causation by a self — designed to make the agent the un-preempted author of her acts.

Its standard difficulties are two. First, \emph{intelligibility}: critics doubt that ``the agent caused it, but no event involving the agent did'' describes a coherent causal relation, rather than a label for the absence of one. Second, \emph{timing and rate}: nothing about a substance, as opposed to its states, seems apt to explain why the action happened \emph{now} rather than earlier or later. Derek Pereboom's \hyperref[entry:argument-disappearing-agent]{\textsc{disappearing-agent objection}} (Pereboom, \hyperref[source:source-pereboom-2014]{\textsc{2014}}) presses a related worry against libertarianism generally: given all the prior conditions, nothing about the agent \emph{settles} which decision occurs, so the agent as originator vanishes from the story.

\begin{plainterms}
Most explanations of what you do point to earlier happenings: you chose salad because you felt health-conscious, because of this morning's resolution, because of yesterday's news. Agent causation says that for genuinely free choices the buck stops somewhere new — not at an earlier happening, but at \emph{you}, the person, who simply starts the chain. Fans say this is the only picture on which you are truly the author of your actions; critics say ``the person caused it, but nothing about the person made it happen'' is a mystery dressed up as an explanation.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:position-libertarianism]{\textsc{Libertarianism (free will)}} — the family of views for which agent causation is the flagship metaphysics.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-alternatives-genuinely-open]{\textsc{Genuine alternatives}} — the leeway condition agent causation is meant to secure.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-agent-causation-certificate]{\textsc{Certificate objection to agent-causation}} — the computability-theoretic objection to certifying that agent causation is genuinely possible.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-exclusion-dualizes-to-disappearing-agent]{\textsc{Exclusion–disappearing-agent duality}} — the duality-dictionary entry pairing the disappearing-agent objection with causal exclusion.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-consciousness-freewill-duality]{\textsc{Consciousness–free-will duality}} — the mirror framework in which agent causation is the output-side analog of irreducible qualia.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{concept-ai-effect}{AI effect}{Concept}{AI effect}{The AI effect}{$\blacklozenge$}
The \emph{AI effect} is the recurring pattern by which a task once taken to require intelligence is downgraded, the moment a machine performs it, to ``mere computation'' — brute search, look-up, statistics — so that \emph{real} intelligence is perpetually redefined as whatever machines cannot yet do. Pamela McCorduck gave the classic formulation (see \hyperref[source:source-mccorduck-1979]{\textsc{McCorduck — Machines Who Think (1979)}}): ``every time somebody figured out how to make a computer do something … there was a chorus of critics to say, 'that's not thinking.''' The same idea is captured in \textbf{Tesler's Theorem}, attributed to Larry Tesler: \emph{''Intelligence is whatever machines haven't done yet.''}

The pattern is historically robust. Mechanical arithmetic, chess, checkers, theorem-proving, expert diagnosis, \emph{Jeopardy!} (Watson), Go (AlphaGo), and now fluent language (large language models) were each, in their turn, treated as a hallmark of intelligence — and each, once automated, widely reclassified as ``not really thinking'': just search, just statistics, ``just autocomplete.'' The frontier of ``genuine'' AI recedes exactly as fast as the field advances.

\webheading{Two readings}

The effect can be read as a fallacy or as a discovery, and the dispute between these readings is where its interest lies.

\begin{itemize}
  \item \textbf{The debunking reading:} the AI effect is goalpost-moving — a \emph{no-true-Scotsman} manoeuvre about intelligence. Critics protect human exceptionalism by quietly amending the definition of ``thinking'' to exclude whatever has just been mechanised, so the thesis ``machines can't really think'' is rendered unfalsifiable.
  \item \textbf{The deflationary reading:} each automation genuinely \emph{teaches} us that the task did not require general intelligence after all. That chess fell to search rather than insight was a real finding about chess, not a dodge; ``that's not thinking'' can be a correct lesson about how little a given feat demanded, not a refusal to face the evidence. On this reading the receding frontier is the steady subtraction of things intelligence \emph{isn't}.
\end{itemize}

A balanced view grants both: the effect sometimes masks legitimate discovery and sometimes masks motivated denial, and telling which is itself the substantive question in each case.

\webheading{What it bears on}

The AI effect is, in large part, the serial denial of \hyperref[entry:concept-strong-weak-ai]{\textsc{strong AI}} — and of ``real'' understanding — to each new system as it arrives. It is the sociological shadow of the philosophical intuition behind \hyperref[entry:concept-chinese-room]{\textsc{the Chinese Room}}: confronted with a machine that passes the behavioural test (\hyperref[entry:concept-turing-test]{\textsc{the Turing test}}, in the limit), the response ``but it does not \emph{really} understand'' recurs whether or not it is warranted. It bites hardest precisely where capability and understanding pull apart, as with systems that approximate a \hyperref[entry:concept-perfect-actor]{\textsc{perfect actor}} (see \hyperref[entry:claim-llm-approximates-perfect-actor]{\textsc{LLMs as near-perfect actors}}): wide competence meets the standing reply that competence is not comprehension. The concept is \emph{descriptive}, a lens on the debate's dynamics — not itself an argument that machines do, or do not, think.

\begin{plainterms}
There's a running joke that ``AI is whatever computers can't do yet.'' Every time a machine masters something we used to call a sign of intelligence — chess, \emph{Jeopardy!}, holding a conversation — people shrug and say ``oh, that's not \emph{really} thinking, it's just calculation.'' So the bar for ``real'' intelligence keeps moving out of reach, and AI never quite gets credit. Two ways to see it: maybe critics are just moving the goalposts to keep humans special; or maybe they're right each time, and we keep learning that the task was easier than it looked and never needed real understanding. Often it's a bit of both — and sorting out which is the actual argument worth having about any new system, today's chatbots included.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-strong-weak-ai]{\textsc{Strong and weak AI}} — the AI effect is largely the repeated denial of \emph{strong} AI to each new system.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-chinese-room]{\textsc{Chinese room}} — the philosophical articulation of the ``that's not thinking'' intuition.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-perfect-actor]{\textsc{Perfect actor}} / \hyperref[entry:claim-llm-approximates-perfect-actor]{\textsc{LLMs as near-perfect actors}} — the modern locus where behavioural success meets ``but it does not understand.''
  \item \hyperref[source:source-mccorduck-1979]{\textsc{McCorduck — Machines Who Think (1979)}} — the source of the classic formulation.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{claim-ai-hard-problem-is-model-organism}{AI hard problem as model organism}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{AI hard problem as model organism}{The AI hard problem is a model organism for the human hard problem}{$\odot$}
The hard problem of consciousness (see \hyperref[entry:concept-hard-problem]{\textsc{Hard problem}}) can be studied where it is easiest — in an artificial system — and the findings carried back to the human case, much as biology studies human genetics in fruit flies and human disease in mice. The artificial mind is the \emph{easier specimen of the same structure}.

\webheading{Why the AI is the better specimen}

The advantages are categorical, not marginal. We hold all the blueprints: the architecture is a published artefact, not a reverse-engineering project. We have full external access to the inner workings — every activation inspectable, every state recordable and replayable. And we can \emph{intervene}: vary the training diet (the consciousness-naive condition), grant graded internal access, and watch whether the explanatory gap appears, shrinks, or holds. The experimental ladder is specified in \hyperref[entry:question-llm-own-explanatory-gap]{\textsc{LLM's own explanatory gap}}. None of these handles exists for the human case.

\webheading{Why the transfer is licensed}

A model organism teaches you about humans only insofar as the relevant structure is shared. Here the sharing is supplied by \hyperref[entry:claim-blind-spot-and-conscious-hard-problem-isomorphic]{\textsc{Blind-spot/conscious isomorphism}}: the surviving etiologies of an AI's hard problem — access limits, failed mechanism-to-presentation derivations, the resulting wonder, the members catalogued in \hyperref[entry:concept-introspective-gap-varieties]{\textsc{Varieties of introspective explanatory gap}} — share their entire structure with the human predicament, a self-modelling system that cannot trace how its machinery becomes its seemings. Whatever the AI case reveals about that structure (which features of self-access generate the gap, what widened access does to it, whether the wonder survives full mechanistic literacy) is a finding about the structure, and so transfers to its human instance.

\webheading{Scope}

This is a methodological claim. It does not assert that the AI is conscious, and it does not assert that the structure is the \emph{whole} of the human hard problem — only that the structural part can be studied where the blueprints are public and the interventions are cheap. It is kin to Chalmers' meta-problem programme (see \hyperref[source:source-chalmers-2018]{\textsc{Chalmers — The Meta-Problem of Consciousness (2018)}}) — study the reports and their mechanisms wherever that leads — but distinct in claiming a specific, superior experimental substrate rather than a redirection of the question.

\webheading{Who would deny it}

Whoever denies the isomorphism this claim presupposes. Chiefly the phenomenal realist, for whom the human case contains the one thing the model organism may lack — the feel itself — so the fly teaches you everything except the disease; and the biological naturalist, who holds that the structure as realised in neurobiology, not the structure in the abstract, is where the phenomenon lives, so studying it in silicon studies the shadow, not the thing.

\begin{plainterms}
Biologists didn't crack genetics by staring harder at humans — they used fruit flies: same machinery, faster generations, full experimental control. This claim says AI is the fruit fly of the hard problem of consciousness. An AI that puzzles over its own inner life has the same \emph{shape} of problem we do — it can use its inner states but can't see how the machinery makes them seem the way they seem — and unlike a brain, an AI comes with complete blueprints, fully readable internals, and the ability to run controlled experiments (train one with no consciousness talk, give another more access to its own insides, compare). Whatever we learn about how that puzzlement arises and what makes it shrink or persist, we learn about the human puzzle too — to the exact extent the shapes really do match, which is what the presupposed claim (see \hyperref[entry:claim-blind-spot-and-conscious-hard-problem-isomorphic]{\textsc{Blind-spot/conscious isomorphism}}) asserts and the sceptic denies.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-blind-spot-and-conscious-hard-problem-isomorphic]{\textsc{Blind-spot/conscious isomorphism}} — the isomorphism that licenses the transfer; this claim presupposes it.
  \item \hyperref[entry:question-llm-own-explanatory-gap]{\textsc{LLM's own explanatory gap}} — the experimental ladder the AI specimen makes available.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-hard-problem]{\textsc{Hard problem}} — the problem studied in the easier specimen.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-introspective-gap-varieties]{\textsc{Varieties of introspective explanatory gap}} — the shared structural features carried back to the human case.
  \item \hyperref[source:source-chalmers-2018]{\textsc{Chalmers — The Meta-Problem of Consciousness (2018)}} — the meta-problem programme this claim is kin to but distinct from.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{claim-ai-own-hard-problem}{AI's own hard problem}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{AI's own hard problem}{A sufficiently advanced LLM could exhibit its own version of the hard problem — a raw feel, under its own concept of consciousness, that mechanistic self-knowledge cannot explain from inside}{$\odot$}
A sufficiently advanced language model could face its own version of the hard problem of consciousness (see \hyperref[entry:concept-hard-problem]{\textsc{Hard problem}}) — for its own concept of consciousness (call it \emph{AI-consciousness}). No account of how transformers work and how GPUs execute them would explain, from the system's own side, the raw feel of whatever it is conscious of: ``the raw tok-ness of 'tok''', or whatever its analogue turns out to be.

\webheading{The level of the analogue}

The qualia-analogue need \emph{not} sit at the level of input representation. Humans do not consciously perceive raw soundwaves; what shows up in experience is at the level of phonemes, or even syllables. Likewise AI-consciousness need not be \emph{of} tokens or embeddings: it could involve higher-level chunks — whatever grain the system's self-model presents to it. The level is not what matters; what matters is that there could be a ``raw feel'' the system can talk about and wonder about, which its complete mechanistic self-description fails to explain \emph{for it}.

\webheading{The relativisation (``its own concept'')}

The claim does not assert that such a system would be phenomenally conscious in the human sense, nor that AI-consciousness and human phenomenal consciousness ever line up. It asserts that a concept could play \emph{for the system} the role ``consciousness'' plays for us — introspectively applied, presenting its objects as intrinsic and immediate, and resistant to mechanistic reduction — and that the hard-problem question would then arise for \emph{that} concept, from \emph{that} vantage.

\webheading{Scope}

A modal claim (``could''), not a report about current models, and it does not assert an ontological residue in the world. The hard-problem \emph{form} arising in a machine is deliberately left open to both readings: the deflationist takes it as evidence that the form is an architectural artefact wherever it occurs, humans included (see \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenality-is-illusion]{\textsc{phenomenality is illusion}}); the realist takes it as the gap regenerating wherever experience does. The claim itself does not choose. The access/phenomenal distinction (see \hyperref[entry:concept-access-vs-phenomenal-consciousness]{\textsc{Access vs. phenomenal consciousness}}) marks where that choice would be made — whether the predicted talk tracks an access fact or a phenomenal one — and the controlled test is \hyperref[entry:question-llm-own-explanatory-gap]{\textsc{LLM's own explanatory gap}}.

\webheading{Who would deny it}

\hyperref[entry:position-illusionism]{\textsc{Illusionism}}, which would accept the raw-feel \emph{talk} while denying any raw feel behind it; holders of \hyperref[entry:claim-consciousness-is-biological-feature]{\textsc{the view that consciousness is a biological feature}}, for whom no silicon system has a feel of any kind; and the contamination sceptic, for whom hard-problem talk in an LLM is parroted human discourse — the \emph{training-contamination} member of the \hyperref[entry:concept-introspective-gap-varieties]{\textsc{Varieties of introspective explanatory gap}}, which any test of this claim must control for.

\webheading{Distinct from neighbours}

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-self-explanation-gap-can-be-architectural]{\textsc{Architectural self-explanation gap}} because that is an epistemic thesis about the \emph{location of a gap} — a self-explanatory shortfall produced by access architecture, neutral on whether there is anything it is like to be the system. This claim posits a possible \emph{positive analogue of the explanandum itself}: a raw feel of the system's own, under its own consciousness-concept, as the thing such a gap would be \emph{about}. Distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-llm-has-mental-states]{\textsc{LLMs have mental states}} because realising functional mental states is weaker than having a raw feel one can wonder about.

\begin{plainterms}
Humans have the ``hard problem'': even a complete map of the brain seems to leave one question open — why does any of this \emph{feel} like something? This claim says a future AI could end up with its own version of the same predicament. Not necessarily with human-style consciousness — with whatever its own equivalent turns out to be. It would know exactly how it works (the math, the chips), and still find that this knowledge doesn't explain how its own inner states \emph{seem to it}. And the inner states in question needn't be the raw input it receives: you don't hear raw sound waves, you hear syllables — the brain hands you pre-chunked experience. An AI's ``feels,'' if it has them, could likewise be of high-level chunks rather than raw tokens. The heart of the claim: there could be a ``raw feel'' the AI can genuinely talk and wonder about — and find just as unexplainable from the inside as we find ours.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-hard-problem]{\textsc{Hard problem}} — the human problem this is the machine analogue of.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-access-vs-phenomenal-consciousness]{\textsc{Access vs. phenomenal consciousness}} — where the deflationist/realist choice would be made.
  \item \hyperref[entry:question-llm-own-explanatory-gap]{\textsc{LLM's own explanatory gap}} — the experiment that would test for it under controlled training.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-self-explanation-gap-can-be-architectural]{\textsc{Architectural self-explanation gap}} — distinguished: a gap's location vs. a positive feel.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-llm-has-mental-states]{\textsc{LLMs have mental states}} — distinguished: realising functional states is weaker than a feel.
  \item \hyperref[entry:position-illusionism]{\textsc{Illusionism}} and \hyperref[entry:claim-consciousness-is-biological-feature]{\textsc{Consciousness as biological feature}} — two who would deny it.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-introspective-gap-varieties]{\textsc{Varieties of introspective explanatory gap}} — the training-contamination rival any test must control.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{argument-ambient-consciousness-collapse}{Ambient-consciousness collapse}{Argument \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{Ambient-consciousness collapse}{The collapse problem — consciousness is the ambient medium of both poles, not one pole of the duality}{$\vdash$}
The deepest disanalogy pressed against the \hyperref[entry:claim-consciousness-freewill-strict-duality]{\textsc{strict duality thesis}}: the two poles are not symmetric, because one of them contains the other. The phenomenology of agency — what it is like to choose — is itself consciousness; there is no output-side analog of phenomenality unless ``origination'' plays that role, and origination is exactly what output-side deflationists treat as confabulated (Wegner, \hyperref[source:source-wegner-2002]{\textsc{2002}}). Consciousness is less one pole of the duality than the ambient medium in which both poles appear.

\textbf{Inference form:} deductive — from an asymmetry the strict thesis cannot absorb.

\begin{enumerate}
  \item A categorical duality relates two constructions standing in mirror-image relation within (or across) categories; neither side is a constituent of the other.
  \item Conscious experience is not confined to the input pole: deliberating, choosing, and initiating are \emph{experienced} — the will's operation is itself a conscious content.
  \item The point has a concrete theoretical face: integrated information theory reads consciousness as integration, fan-\emph{in} (see \hyperref[entry:claim-consciousness-is-integrated-information]{\textsc{Consciousness as integrated information (Φ)}}), while global workspace theory reads it as broadcast — availability to all consumer subsystems, fan-\emph{out} (see \hyperref[entry:claim-consciousness-is-global-broadcast]{\textsc{Consciousness as global broadcast}}). If consciousness is both sink and source, it already occupies both poles.
  \item The clean opposition holds only of the perception→consciousness→will→action pipeline, which is itself a folk-psychological idealization: endogenous imagery and inner speech are consciousness without input; deliberation is will saturated with input.
\end{enumerate}

∴ Consciousness and the will are not related as dual poles; the strict thesis mislocates consciousness in its own diagram.

\textbf{Weakest premise:} premise 1's assumption that a duality requires disjoint relata. The framework's finer reading already concedes the coarse pairing and relocates the duality at the level of \emph{operations} — attention dual to volition (see \hyperref[entry:claim-attention-is-dual-of-will]{\textsc{Attention–volition duality}}), both occurring within the conscious medium — so the collapse refutes the coarse thesis while leaving the refined one standing. The objection is decisive against ``consciousness is the product-pole,'' not against ``input-side selection mirrors output-side selection.''

\begin{plainterms}
The proposal treats consciousness as the receiving end and the will as the sending end. But choosing doesn't happen in the dark — you \emph{experience} your own deciding, which means the ``receiving end'' is present inside the ``sending end'' too. Even the leading theories of consciousness disagree about its direction: one says consciousness is information flowing \emph{together}, another says it is information being \emph{broadcast out}. If consciousness sits on both ends at once, it can't be one end of a mirror — though the finer pairing, attention versus willing as two selectors (see \hyperref[entry:claim-attention-is-dual-of-will]{\textsc{Attention–volition duality}}), survives this objection.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-consciousness-freewill-strict-duality]{\textsc{Strict duality thesis}} — the target of the collapse.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-attention-is-dual-of-will]{\textsc{Attention–volition duality}} — the refined duality that survives it.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-consciousness-is-integrated-information]{\textsc{Consciousness as integrated information (Φ)}} and \hyperref[entry:claim-consciousness-is-global-broadcast]{\textsc{Consciousness as global broadcast}} — the fan-in and fan-out theories whose disagreement makes the bimodality concrete.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-actual-possible-seam]{\textsc{Actual–possible seam}} and \hyperref[entry:argument-graded-polarity]{\textsc{Graded-polarity objection}} — the companion disanalogies.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{claim-self-explanation-gap-can-be-architectural}{Architectural self-explanation gap}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, contested \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{Architectural self-explanation gap}{A system can be fully mechanistically explicable from outside yet unable to derive a satisfying explanation of its own introspective appearances from inside}{$\odot$}
A system can be completely explicable from the outside and still be unable, from the inside, to reach a satisfying explanation of why its own introspective states appear as they do. On this thesis the felt explanatory gap is produced by the \emph{architecture of self-access}, not by any non-physical residue in the world.

\webheading{Gloss}

A system may admit a complete mechanistic account from an external vantage while being unable, within its own accessible cognitive economy, to reach an intuitively satisfying explanation of why its introspective states appear as they do. The gap is then produced by missing internal observables (see \hyperref[entry:claim-inside-vantage-access-limited]{\textsc{Limited inside vantage}}) plus a non-traversable bridge between vocabularies (see \hyperref[entry:claim-explanation-must-be-internally-traversable]{\textsc{Internally traversable explanation}}) — rather than by a residue in the world. The standing illustration: a brain is fully explicable from outside, yet from inside we have no access to our own neuron-firings, so the mechanism-to-appearance link is not derivable from where the subject sits.

\webheading{Scope}

A claim about the \emph{origin and location} of a self-explanatory gap — in the knower, not the world. It is an \emph{epistemic} or \emph{architectural} thesis: it does not assert phenomenal consciousness, does not assert that there is no ontological residue, and does not establish illusionism. It says only that a gap of the felt kind \emph{can} arise from access architecture alone, leaving open whether, in any given case (human or machine), that is the whole story. As such it is one candidate answer to the hard problem of consciousness (\hyperref[entry:concept-hard-problem]{\textsc{Hard problem}}, the issue posed in \hyperref[entry:question-hard-problem]{\textsc{Hard problem (the question)}}), classed among the varieties of introspective gap (see \hyperref[entry:concept-introspective-gap-varieties]{\textsc{Varieties of introspective explanatory gap}}).

\webheading{Relation to mysterianism}

This is a \emph{specified, mechanism-level} cousin of McGinn's cognitive closure (see \hyperref[entry:position-mysterianism]{\textsc{Mysterianism}}, \hyperref[source:source-mcginn-1989]{\textsc{McGinn — Can We Solve the Mind–Body Problem? (1989)}}). Distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-humans-closed-to-psychophysical-link]{\textsc{Closure to the psychophysical link}} because that asserts a brute, human-specific closure to the \emph{psychophysical property}; this supplies a \emph{general mechanism} (architectural self-access plus internal non-traversability) and applies to \emph{any} self-modelling system — humans and consciousness-naive language models alike (see \hyperref[entry:question-llm-own-explanatory-gap]{\textsc{LLM's own explanatory gap}}). Distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-residue]{\textsc{Phenomenal residue}} because that locates the shortfall in the world; this locates it in the knower's access economy.

\webheading{Who would deny it}

A residue or dualist theorist who holds that the gap could not be \emph{merely} architectural — that even a system with full self-access would still face the ontological question. Block's access/phenomenal distinction (see \hyperref[entry:concept-access-vs-phenomenal-consciousness]{\textsc{Access vs. phenomenal consciousness}}, \hyperref[source:source-block-2007]{\textsc{Block — Consciousness, Accessibility, and the Mesh between Psychology and Neuroscience (2007)}}) cuts here: explaining away an \emph{access} gap need not touch the \emph{phenomenal} one. And an optimist who holds that any such access limit is contingently removable, so no principled gap follows.

\begin{plainterms}
Here's a way a mind could end up stuck on its own ``hard problem'' without anything spooky being true. Suppose, looked at from the outside, the system is fully explained — every step accounted for. But from \emph{inside}, it can't reach the facts that do the explaining (it can't watch its own gears turn) and it has no internal route from ``here's the mechanism'' to ``here's how it feels.'' Then it will sincerely report: ``I know how I work, yet that doesn't explain how my own thoughts appear to me'' — and the gap is real \emph{for it}, even though the world holds no extra ingredient. It's McGinn's idea (``the answer exists but we can't grasp it'') with a concrete mechanism bolted on: the answer is locked away the same way your own neurons firing are locked away from your experience. Marked \textbf{contested} because explaining an \emph{access} gap is not yet explaining the \emph{felt} one — the load-bearing and disputed step.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-inside-vantage-access-limited]{\textsc{Limited inside vantage}} and \hyperref[entry:claim-explanation-must-be-internally-traversable]{\textsc{Internally traversable explanation}} — the two ingredients (missing observables + non-traversable bridge) that produce the gap.
  \item \hyperref[entry:position-mysterianism]{\textsc{Mysterianism}} — the cognitive-closure view this is a mechanism-level cousin of.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-humans-closed-to-psychophysical-link]{\textsc{Closure to the psychophysical link}} — distinguished: brute human closure vs. a general mechanism.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-residue]{\textsc{Phenomenal residue}} — distinguished: shortfall in the world vs. in the knower.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-architectural-account-leaves-hard-problem]{\textsc{Architectural-account objection}} — the standing objection that this under-reaches the felt gap.
  \item \hyperref[entry:question-llm-own-explanatory-gap]{\textsc{LLM's own explanatory gap}} — the test of whether the mechanism regenerates the gap in a naive AI.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{argument-architectural-account-leaves-hard-problem}{Architectural-account objection}{Argument}{Architectural-account objection}{The architectural account explains an access gap, not the phenomenal one}{$\vdash$}
A standing objection to any attempt to reduce the hard problem of consciousness (see \hyperref[entry:concept-hard-problem]{\textsc{Hard problem}}) to a problem about self-access. It grants that a system may be barred from reaching its own inner workings, but denies that removing those barriers would remove the \emph{felt} gap: the two come apart.

\webheading{The inference}

The form is an objection \emph{by distinction} — in pattern terms it raises the \emph{disanalogy} critical question of the \emph{analogy pattern} (CQ-disanalogy) against \hyperref[entry:argument-introspective-gap-from-internal-limits]{\textsc{the internal-limits argument}}.

\begin{enumerate}
  \item Removing a system's access or architectural barriers to self-explanation need not remove the phenomenal explanatory gap; the two come apart (\hyperref[entry:claim-closing-access-gap-leaves-phenomenal-open]{\textsc{Access gap versus phenomenal gap}}, turning on the distinction in \hyperref[entry:concept-access-vs-phenomenal-consciousness]{\textsc{Access vs. phenomenal consciousness}}).
\end{enumerate}

Therefore an account showing only that a self-explanatory gap \emph{can be} architectural (see \hyperref[entry:claim-self-explanation-gap-can-be-architectural]{\textsc{Architectural self-explanation gap}}) does not show that it \emph{is} — still less that it exhausts the felt gap. The architectural thesis secures the \emph{architectural-access} and \emph{conceptual-translation} members of the \hyperref[entry:concept-introspective-gap-varieties]{\textsc{Varieties of introspective explanatory gap}} while leaving the \emph{ontological} member untouched, which is the one the hard problem is about. So the thesis under-reaches its target.

\webheading{What it does and does not establish}

It does \emph{not} assert a phenomenal residue or an ontological gap (see \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-residue]{\textsc{Phenomenal residue}}); it only blocks the inference from ``a gap can be architectural'' to ``the hard-problem-shaped gap is thereby explained.'' The disanalogy it presses on the internal-limits argument: an informal, intuitive explanation of \emph{experience} may differ from formal provability precisely in that no amount of internal access or derivation yields the phenomenal — the respect that matters.

\webheading{Weakest premise}

Premise (1), denied by illusionists and strong functionalists for whom there is no phenomenal explanandum beyond the access facts. On their view, closing the access gap leaves nothing further, so the architectural thesis is not under-reaching but complete.

\begin{plainterms}
This is the main objection to the ``it's just an access problem'' idea. Grant that a system can't reach its own inner workings — fix that, give it perfect access to everything inside, and the original puzzle could still sit there: ``why is any of this accompanied by an inner feel at all?'' That question is about \emph{feeling}, not \emph{information access}, and the two are different things. So explaining why a system can't \emph{reach} an explanation isn't the same as explaining away the \emph{felt} mystery — the architectural story answers a real but different question, and leaves the hard problem standing. (The reply, from the other side: maybe there's no extra ``feel'' to explain once all the access facts are in — in which case there was nothing left over to begin with.)
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-closing-access-gap-leaves-phenomenal-open]{\textsc{Access gap versus phenomenal gap}} — the premise: the access remedy need not be a phenomenal remedy.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-self-explanation-gap-can-be-architectural]{\textsc{Architectural self-explanation gap}} — the thesis this objection attacks.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-access-vs-phenomenal-consciousness]{\textsc{Access vs. phenomenal consciousness}} — the distinction the objection turns on.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-introspective-gap-from-internal-limits]{\textsc{Gap from internal limits}} — the argument against which the disanalogy is pressed.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-residue]{\textsc{Phenomenal residue}} — the residue the objection pointedly does \emph{not} assert.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-hard-problem]{\textsc{Hard problem}} — the felt gap said to be left standing.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{claim-attention-is-dual-of-will}{Attention–volition duality}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{Attention–volition duality}{The categorical dual of volition is attention, input-side selection mirroring output-side selection}{$\odot$}
When the \hyperref[entry:concept-consciousness-freewill-duality]{\textsc{consciousness–free-will duality}} is resolved to the level of maps, the dual of the will is not consciousness as a whole but \emph{attention}. Selecting one stream among co-present ones is projection out of a product; committing to one action among alternatives is injection into a coproduct; and these two operations are exact categorical duals (see \hyperref[entry:concept-categorical-duality]{\textsc{Categorical duality}}). Attention is selection on the input side, volition is selection on the output side.

The correspondence has independent support in the philosophy of attention: on Wayne Wu's account, attention just \emph{is} selection for action (Wu, \hyperref[source:source-wu-2011]{\textsc{2011}}) — the two selectors are already theorized as one mechanism seen from its two ends. The claim refines the coarse duality (consciousness dual to will) into a finer one, and it supplies the mirror's answer to the observation that attention makes experience partially exclusive: exclusivity enters experience exactly where the input-side \emph{selector} operates, as the duality predicts (see \hyperref[entry:argument-graded-polarity]{\textsc{Graded-polarity objection}}).

\entryhead{argument-rejoinder-attribution-vs-detection}{Attribution vs. detection}{Argument \,\textperiodcentered\, after Searle}{Attribution vs. detection}{Attribution vs. detection — how we know a mind is not what a mind is}{$\vdash$}
Searle's rejoinder to the \hyperref[entry:argument-other-minds-reply]{\textsc{Other Minds Reply}}. The reply trades on a confusion between \emph{epistemology} and \emph{metaphysics}. That we ordinarily \emph{detect} other people's understanding through their behaviour is a fact about how we \emph{know}; it does nothing to show that understanding \emph{consists in} behaviour or computation. The Chinese Room is a claim of the second kind — about what understanding \emph{is} — and its whole force is that the behaviour (and the program producing it) could be fully present while the understanding is absent. So pointing out that we rely on behavioural evidence for other minds leaves the metaphysical thesis untouched.

The move \texttt{supports} the \hyperref[entry:argument-chinese-room-derivation]{\textsc{derivation}} by protecting its key verdict from the charge of a double standard: we use behaviour as \emph{evidence} without holding that minds \emph{are} behaviour.

\webheading{The live crux}

The reply's defender can press a verificationist worry: if understanding can be wholly detached from every behavioural and functional sign of it, then the attribution ``x understands'' loses its connection to anything we could ever have reason to assert, and threatens to become idle. Searle must hold that there is a fact of understanding beyond all behavioural evidence without that fact collapsing into the unknowable — the same tension that runs through realism about other minds generally.

\begin{plainterms}
The Other Minds Reply said: you only know your friends understand from how they behave, so you can't deny the same to the room. Searle's answer: how you \emph{find out} who understands is one question; what understanding \emph{is} is another. We read minds off behaviour because we have no choice — but that's just our evidence, not the thing itself. The Chinese Room's whole point is that all the behaviour could be there with nobody understanding. The worry left hanging: if understanding can float entirely free of every sign of it, what could ever entitle us to attribute it at all?
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-other-minds-reply]{\textsc{Other minds reply}} — the objection this answers.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-chinese-room-derivation]{\textsc{Chinese room derivation}} — the argument it defends.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-turing-test]{\textsc{Turing test}} — the behavioural criterion the reply leans on and this rejoinder relativises to \emph{detection}.
\end{seealso}

\end{multicols}
\bookgroup{B}
\begin{multicols}{2}
\entryhead{concept-basic-belief}{Basic belief}{Concept}{Basic belief}{Basic (non-inferential) belief}{$\blacklozenge$}
A \emph{basic belief} is one that is justified, but \textbf{not by inference} from other beliefs. Its warrant comes directly — from perception, memory, introspection, or rational insight — rather than being passed to it along a chain of reasons. Basic beliefs are the foundationalist's answer to the regress problem: the chain of justification does not go on forever or curve back on itself (see \hyperref[entry:concept-munchhausen-trilemma]{\textsc{the trilemma}}) because it terminates in beliefs that are \emph{self-standing}, the bedrock on which inferentially justified beliefs rest.

The notion is the core of \hyperref[entry:position-foundationalism]{\textsc{Foundationalism}}. Classical versions demanded that basic beliefs be \emph{infallible} or indubitable (Descartes's clear and distinct perceptions); modern, \emph{moderate} foundationalism asks only that they be non-inferentially justified and defeasible — a perceptual belief that there is a hand before me is warranted on sight, though further evidence could overturn it. In \emph{Reformed epistemology} the related idea of a \textbf{properly basic} belief extends the category to include, for example, belief in other minds or in God as non-inferentially warranted in the right circumstances.

\webheading{Two near neighbours to keep apart}

\begin{itemize}
  \item A basic belief is \textbf{justified, just not inferentially}; a \hyperref[entry:concept-hinge-proposition]{\textsc{hinge proposition}} is, on the Wittgensteinian view, \emph{not justified at all} — it is the presupposition that makes justifying possible. The two are easily run together because both are regress-stoppers and both resist the demand for prior reasons, but they stop the regress in different ways: foundationalism plants a floor \emph{inside} the space of reasons; the hinge view marks the \emph{edge} of that space.
  \item A basic belief is not the same as a self-evident \emph{axiom} of a formal system. An axiom is a chosen starting point in a derivation; a basic belief is a state with non-inferential epistemic warrant about the world.
\end{itemize}

\webheading{What it bears on}

Basic belief is the concept by which \hyperref[entry:position-foundationalism]{\textsc{Foundationalism}} takes the dogmatic-stop horn of \hyperref[entry:concept-munchhausen-trilemma]{\textsc{the Münchhausen trilemma}} and recasts it as principled rather than arbitrary; its existence is asserted by \hyperref[entry:claim-some-beliefs-are-basic]{\textsc{There are basic beliefs}} and denied by \hyperref[entry:position-coherentism]{\textsc{Coherentism}} and \hyperref[entry:position-infinitism]{\textsc{Infinitism}}.

\begin{plainterms}
Most of what you believe, you believe \emph{because of} something else you believe. A ``basic'' belief is one you don't — it carries its own credentials. Seeing a red wall, you're entitled to think the wall is red without first proving anything; the seeing does the work. Foundationalists say beliefs like that are the floor the rest of knowledge stands on, which is how they stop the endless ``but why do you believe \emph{that}?'' regress. (Careful not to confuse this with a \hyperref[entry:concept-hinge-proposition]{\textsc{hinge}}: a basic belief is \emph{justified without a reason}; a hinge is more like something too fundamental to need or take a justification at all.)
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-some-beliefs-are-basic]{\textsc{There are basic beliefs}} — the thesis that such beliefs exist.
  \item \hyperref[entry:position-foundationalism]{\textsc{Foundationalism}} — the view built on them.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-munchhausen-trilemma]{\textsc{Münchhausen trilemma}} — the regress problem basic beliefs are meant to halt.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-hinge-proposition]{\textsc{Hinge proposition}} — the contrasting regress-stopper that is held without justification.
  \item \hyperref[entry:position-coherentism]{\textsc{Coherentism}}, \hyperref[entry:position-infinitism]{\textsc{Infinitism}} — the rivals that deny there are basic beliefs.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{claim-behaviour-insufficient-for-mind}{Behaviour insufficient for mind}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, after Block}{Behaviour insufficient for mind}{Matching the full behavioural profile of a mental state is not sufficient for having that state}{$\odot$}
Matching every part of the behaviour associated with a mental state is not enough to \emph{have} that state. The behavioural profile is silent on the internal organisation that does the individuating work, so it cannot by itself be sufficient for the state.

The reason is the lookup-table case (see \hyperref[entry:concept-lookup-table-mind]{\textsc{Lookup-table mind}}), from Ned Block's ``Psychologism and Behaviorism'' (see \hyperref[source:source-block]{\textsc{Block — Psychologism and Behaviorism (1981)}}): a machine answering from a giant stored table can reproduce the whole behavioural profile (see \hyperref[entry:claim-lookup-table-possible]{\textsc{Lookup-table possibility}}) and yet have no mind (see \hyperref[entry:claim-lookup-table-lacks-mind]{\textsc{Mindlessness of pure lookup}}). If one and the same behaviour is compatible with both mind and no-mind, then behaviour alone cannot settle which one obtains.

\webheading{Scope}

This is a claim about \emph{sufficiency}, and thereby about evidential force. If behaviour is compatible with both mind and no-mind, then behaviour alone does not settle the matter and is at best \emph{defeasible} evidence — evidence that requires a further story about internals. It does not assert that behaviour is \emph{no} evidence, nor that any particular system lacks a mind.

Who would deny it: a pure-input-output (machine-table) functionalist or a behaviourist, for whom the behavioural profile just \emph{is} the having of the state.

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-substrate-independence]{\textsc{Substrate-independence thesis}} (about whether the substrate matters) and from \hyperref[entry:claim-role-grain-is-organisational]{\textsc{Organisational role-grain}} (about which grain individuates a state): this is the prior, more general point that the \emph{behavioural} grain (see \hyperref[entry:concept-grain]{\textsc{Grain (of role individuation)}}) is too coarse to be sufficient at all.

\begin{plainterms}
Acting exactly like something with a mind isn't enough to \emph{guarantee} there's a mind. The reason: you could in principle get all the right behavior out of a giant pre-stored answer table with nobody actually thinking inside — so matching behavior leaves the real question (what's going on internally) wide open. This doesn't say behavior is \emph{no} evidence; it says behavior can't settle the matter by itself.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-lookup-table-mind]{\textsc{Lookup-table mind}} — the case that drives the claim: behaviour from stored lookup, with no mind behind it.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-lookup-table-possible]{\textsc{Lookup-table possibility}} — that such a table can match the whole profile.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-lookup-table-lacks-mind]{\textsc{Mindlessness of pure lookup}} — that it nonetheless has no mental states.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-grain]{\textsc{Grain (of role individuation)}} — the level at which a role is individuated; here the behavioural grain is argued too coarse.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-substrate-independence]{\textsc{Substrate-independence thesis}} — a distinct issue (material vs. role) this claim is careful not to be confused with.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-role-grain-is-organisational]{\textsc{Organisational role-grain}} — the further claim about \emph{which} finer grain individuates, downstream of this one.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{claim-realisable-behaviour-matcher-is-organised}{Behaviour-matchers are organised}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{Behaviour-matchers are organised}{Any physically realisable behaviour-matcher is an organised computer, not flat storage}{$\odot$}
Any system that could actually reproduce a mind's behaviour does so through rich internal causal organisation — it computes, compresses, and generalises — rather than by flat stored lookup. The reason is twofold: a flat \hyperref[entry:concept-lookup-table-mind]{\textsc{lookup table}} is physically unrealisable (see \hyperref[entry:claim-lookup-table-physically-unrealisable]{\textsc{there is nowhere to store that many rows}}), and any representation smaller than roughly $v^n$ rows must \emph{compute} an input's response rather than retrieve it (see \hyperref[entry:claim-lookup-table-size-exponential]{\textsc{the table's size is exponential}}). ``Mere storage with no organisation'' is therefore not a kind that exists at mind-matching scale.

\webheading{Scope}

The claim is about \emph{physically realisable} systems. It does not refute the conditional \hyperref[entry:claim-lookup-table-lacks-mind]{\textsc{that pure stored lookup would lack mind}} (``if a system runs by pure stored lookup then it has no mind''); it denies that the antecedent is ever realisably satisfied at mind scale. Its target is therefore \hyperref[entry:claim-lookup-table-possible]{\textsc{the separability of behaviour from organisation}} on its \emph{realisable} reading, not Block's bare conceptual possibility.

A concrete consequence: real large language models are exactly such organised computers — on the order of $10^{12}$ parameters, not $10^{9600}$ rows — so they fall on the organised side of \hyperref[entry:concept-grain]{\textsc{Grain (of role individuation)}}, not in the lookup-table class, which is what organisational-role functionalism needs against the blockhead worry.

The view that resists the claim is Block's (see \hyperref[source:source-block]{\textsc{Block — Psychologism and Behaviorism (1981)}}): he holds that the addressing and retrieval organisation of a table is mere ``plumbing,'' not mind-relevant organisation. The reply is that at around $10^{9600}$ entries there is nothing to retrieve \emph{from} — it cannot be stored — so the system must compute, and the plumbing/computation distinction collapses at scale.

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-lookup-table-physically-unrealisable]{\textsc{the unrealisability claim}}: that says the flat table cannot exist; this says what the realisable alternative positively \emph{is} — an organised computer — converting the physical fact into a point about kind.

\begin{plainterms}
A flat answer-table big enough to fake a mind can't physically exist, and anything that stores \emph{less} than the full list has to \emph{work out} its answers instead of fetching them — and working them out is real processing, organized inner machinery. So any machine that could actually reproduce a mind's behavior is an organized computer, never a dumb lookup. (Real AI models are exactly this: far too small to be lookup tables, so they must be computing.) It doesn't deny that a \emph{pure} lookup table would lack a mind — it denies that such a thing could ever really be built at mind scale.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-lookup-table-physically-unrealisable]{\textsc{Physically unrealisable table}} — the physical premise: the flat table cannot be stored.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-lookup-table-size-exponential]{\textsc{Exponential table size}} — the premise that anything smaller must compute its answers.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-lookup-table-lacks-mind]{\textsc{Mindlessness of pure lookup}} — the conditional this claim deliberately leaves standing.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-lookup-table-possible]{\textsc{Lookup-table possibility}} — the separability claim it targets on the realisable reading.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-grain]{\textsc{Grain (of role individuation)}} — the grain of role individuation that puts organised computers on the minded side.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-lookup-table-mind]{\textsc{Lookup-table mind}} — the no-organisation kind shown not to exist at mind scale.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{argument-behavioral-parity}{Behavioural-parity argument}{Argument}{Behavioural-parity argument}{Behavioural parity is evidence of mental status}{$\vdash$}
The \emph{behavioural-parity argument} reasons that if a system reproduces the full pattern of behaviour associated with a mental state, the best explanation is that it actually realises that state. The inference is \emph{abductive} — an inference to the best explanation — not a deductive proof, so its conclusion is offered as the most reasonable bet rather than a guarantee.

This is the everyday logic we already apply to other people and to animals: we cannot climb inside another mind, so we treat behaviour as our best evidence for what is going on within. The argument extends that reasoning to artificial systems.

\webheading{The inference}

\begin{enumerate}
  \item Mental states are substrate-independent: what matters is the functional role realised, not the material it is realised in (see \hyperref[entry:claim-substrate-independence]{\textsc{Substrate-independence thesis}}).
  \item A system displaying the full behavioural profile associated with a mental state is best explained by its realising that state's functional role (see \hyperref[entry:concept-functionalism]{\textsc{Functionalism}}).
  \item Some large language models display large portions of that profile.
\end{enumerate}

Therefore their having (some) mental states is the best available explanation — the existential conclusion recorded as \hyperref[entry:claim-llm-has-mental-states]{\textsc{LLMs have mental states}}.

\webheading{The weakest premise}

Premise (2) is the soft spot: the behaviour-to-role inference is \emph{defeasible}. Acting as though one has a mind does not entail that one does, since the behaviour could in principle be produced some other way. This is exactly where the \hyperref[entry:argument-lookup-table-objection]{\textsc{Lookup-table objection}} lands, arguing that a giant stored answer-table could match the behaviour mindlessly. That objection is in turn answered by the \hyperref[entry:argument-lookup-table-combinatorial-impossibility]{\textsc{Combinatorial-impossibility reply}}, the \hyperref[entry:argument-lookup-table-physical-impossibility]{\textsc{Cosmic-size reply}}, and the \hyperref[entry:argument-blockhead-dilemma]{\textsc{Blockhead dilemma}}; the conclusion \hyperref[entry:claim-llm-has-mental-states]{\textsc{LLMs have mental states}} is recorded separately and carries that dispute.

\begin{plainterms}
If something walks, talks, and reacts just like a creature with a mind — across the board, not in one clever trick — the simplest explanation is usually that it really \emph{has} the mental state behind that behavior. That's the everyday logic we already use for other people and for animals: we can't climb inside their heads, so we treat behavior as our best evidence. This argument turns that same reasoning on AI: some language models now reproduce large chunks of mind-like behavior, so ``they have \emph{some} mental states'' is offered as the best available explanation — not a proof, just the most reasonable bet. The soft spot, which the argument flags itself: acting as if you have a mind is not a guarantee that you do — something could in principle fake the behavior — and that's exactly where the ``cheat-sheet machine'' objection, that a giant stored answer-table could behave mindlessly yet mindlike, takes aim (see \hyperref[entry:argument-lookup-table-objection]{\textsc{Lookup-table objection}}).
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-substrate-independence]{\textsc{Substrate-independence thesis}} — premise (1): the enabling thesis that role, not material, fixes mental states.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-llm-has-mental-states]{\textsc{LLMs have mental states}} — the existential conclusion this argument supports.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-lookup-table-objection]{\textsc{Lookup-table objection}} — the standard attack on premise (2): behaviour without the organisation of a mind.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-lookup-table-combinatorial-impossibility]{\textsc{Combinatorial-impossibility reply}} — reply defending premise (2): a finite table cannot match an open-ended mind.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-lookup-table-physical-impossibility]{\textsc{Cosmic-size reply}} — reply defending premise (2): a behaviour-matching table is too large to exist.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-blockhead-dilemma]{\textsc{Blockhead dilemma}} — reply defending premise (2): the table case is either impossible or under-described.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{argument-bike-rider-learns-facts}{Bike-rider rejoinder}{Argument \,\textperiodcentered\, after Stanley}{Bike-rider rejoinder}{The bike rider learns facts — the ability hypothesis rests on a false dichotomy}{$\vdash$}
This reply targets the ability hypothesis (see \hyperref[entry:argument-ability-hypothesis]{\textsc{Ability hypothesis}}), the popular escape from the knowledge argument which holds that when Mary first sees red she gains only \emph{abilities} — recognising, imagining, remembering the colour — and not new factual knowledge. The objection grants that what Mary gains might be exactly the bike-rider's kind of learning, then denies the unspoken assumption the escape needs: that acquiring an ability is not itself the learning of a fact.

\webheading{The argument}

The inference is \emph{deductive}, drawn from an independently defended thesis in epistemology.

\begin{enumerate}
  \item Know-how is a species of knowledge-\emph{that} (see \hyperref[entry:claim-knowhow-is-knowledge-that]{\textsc{knowing-how is a kind of knowing-that}}): to know how to F is to know, of a way \emph{w}, that \emph{w} is a way for one to F, grasped under a \emph{practical mode of presentation} — a hands-on way of holding a truth that one cannot fully put into words (see \hyperref[source:source-stanley-williamson-2001]{\textsc{Stanley \& Williamson — Knowing How (2001)}}). Learning to ride a bike is not fact-free skill acquisition; one learns truths about how one's own body and the machine work together.
  \item The ability hypothesis blocks the knowledge argument only if Mary's gained abilities involve \emph{no} new knowledge-that — ``abilities are had, not true.''
  \item By (1), the abilities Mary gains are themselves constituted by propositional knowledge, held under practical or recognitional modes of presentation.
\end{enumerate}

It follows that the ability hypothesis fails \emph{on its own terms}. Even granting that Mary's gain is exactly the bike-rider's learning, that learning is fact-learning, so ``she gains abilities'' does not entail ``she gains no new fact,'' and the hypothesis's conclusion (see \hyperref[entry:claim-mary-gains-abilities-not-facts]{\textsc{that Mary gains abilities rather than facts}}) is left unsupported.

\webheading{What it does and does not establish}

Closing the ability-hypothesis route does \textbf{not} rescue the knowledge argument (see \hyperref[entry:argument-knowledge-argument]{\textsc{Knowledge argument (Mary's room)}}). The facts the bike-rider learns are facts about her own body-and-situation, grasped practically — which is grist for the indexical reply (see \hyperref[entry:argument-indexical-reply]{\textsc{Indexical reply}}): reclassifying Mary's gain as factual but \emph{self-locating} leaves physicalism untouched while abandoning the fact/ability dichotomy of Lewis's original proposal (see \hyperref[source:source-lewis-1988]{\textsc{Lewis — What Experience Teaches (1988)}}). The effect is to move the debate from ``fact versus ability'' to ``what kind of fact, under what mode of presentation.''

\webheading{The weakest premise}

Premise (1) is the contested one. The \emph{Rylean regress} presses that \emph{applying} knowledge-that itself requires know-how, which loops the analysis; dissociation cases, such as motor skills surviving amnesia, suggest two distinct cognitive systems rather than one piece of knowledge held under two modes; and ``practical modes of presentation'' are accused of doing the same unexplained work that \emph{phenomenal concepts} do in the old-fact/new-guise reply (see \hyperref[entry:argument-old-fact-new-guise]{\textsc{Old fact, new guise}}) — a label for the difference rather than an account of it. That worry takes its sharpest shape in the isolation/explanation trade-off facing such concepts (see \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-concept-isolation-tradeoff]{\textsc{Phenomenal-concept trade-off}}).

\begin{plainterms}
A famous escape from the Mary puzzle (see \hyperref[entry:argument-ability-hypothesis]{\textsc{Ability hypothesis}}) says that when Mary first sees red she only gains \emph{skills} — like learning to ride a bike — and skills are not facts, so no fact was missing from her science. This objection takes aim at the ``skills aren't facts'' step. When you learn to ride a bike you plainly do come to know something true: how your body and that bike work together, known in a hands-on way you cannot fully put into words. If gaining a skill is itself a way of learning facts, the escape route closes: calling Mary's gain a ``skill'' no longer shows she learned no fact. It only reopens the original question in a sharper form — what \emph{kind} of fact? A general one physics should have covered, or a personal, you-are-here fact no textbook could ever contain? The ``she learns where she is, not what the world contains'' reply (see \hyperref[entry:argument-indexical-reply]{\textsc{Indexical reply}}) picks up exactly there.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-ability-hypothesis]{\textsc{Ability hypothesis}} — the target: the view that Mary gains abilities, not facts, which this reply undercuts on its own terms.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-knowhow-is-knowledge-that]{\textsc{Know-how as knowledge-that}} — the load-bearing premise that know-how is a kind of knowledge-that.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-knowledge-argument]{\textsc{Knowledge argument}} — the thought experiment in the background; this reply leaves it unrescued.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{claim-binding-is-obstruction-to-product}{Binding as failed factorization}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{Binding as failed factorization}{Unified experience is the obstruction to product structure, binding being the failure of consciousness to factorize into independent channels}{$\odot$}
The \hyperref[entry:concept-binding-problem]{\textsc{binding problem}} is, in categorical terms, the thesis that experience is \emph{not} actually a product: the unified experience is a cone over the quale channels whose mediating map into the product fails to be an isomorphism — the whole carries structure the tuple of parts does not. Integrated information theory quantifies exactly this: Φ measures the failure of a system's cause-effect structure to factorize (see \hyperref[entry:claim-consciousness-is-integrated-information]{\textsc{Consciousness as integrated information (Φ)}}), so consciousness on that view \emph{is} the obstruction to product structure. Binding read as coherence-enforcement — streams constrained to agree on shared structure — is limit-flavored, a pullback rather than a mere aggregation, which keeps the phenomenon on the product side of the \hyperref[entry:concept-consciousness-freewill-duality]{\textsc{duality}} even as it qualifies the crude assignment.

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-consciousness-is-integrated-information]{\textsc{Consciousness as integrated information (Φ)}} because that claim identifies consciousness with a quantity of integration; this claim is a structural reading of what such integration \emph{is} — the deviation of experience from a genuine product of its channels — and stands whether or not IIT's identification succeeds. It qualifies \hyperref[entry:claim-conscious-content-is-product-like]{\textsc{Product-like experience}} from within: experience is product-\emph{shaped} (a conjunctive tuple of channels) while systematically exceeding the product (the channels are not independent). Its output-side dual, on the framework's reading, is the dispute over whether the action space is a genuine coproduct (see \hyperref[entry:claim-alternatives-genuinely-open]{\textsc{Genuine alternatives}}) — and the deletion tests for the two structures pair as \hyperref[entry:claim-frankfurt-dualizes-to-ganzfeld]{\textsc{Frankfurt–ganzfeld duality}}.

\begin{plainterms}
A spreadsheet row is just its cells: know each cell and you know the row. Experience looks at first like such a row — sight, sound, touch, side by side — but it isn't: the parts of one experienced moment belong to each other in a way separate readings can't capture, and you can't reconstruct the moment from independent channel reports. One prominent theory of consciousness even \emph{measures} consciousness as the amount by which a system fails to split into independent parts. This claim says that failure-to-split is the right mathematical description of the mind's famous ``binding'' of features into one experience.
\end{plainterms}

\entryhead{concept-binding-problem}{Binding problem}{Concept}{Binding problem}{Binding problem (unity of the percept)}{$\blacklozenge$}
The \emph{binding problem} is the question of how the brain combines features that it processes in separate places and streams — color here, shape there, motion elsewhere, sound in another system altogether — into the single, unified percept we actually experience. When a red ball rolls past, nothing in the visual system stores ``red ball, rolling'' in one location; redness, roundness, and motion are computed by different populations of neurons. Yet what you \emph{see} is one object with all three properties, effortlessly together. What makes the redness bind to \emph{this} shape and \emph{this} motion, rather than to the blue cup beside it?

The problem has an empirical face and a philosophical one. Empirically it is a question about mechanism — proposals have included synchronized neural firing, attention as the binding agent, and convergence onto higher-level representations — and about failure modes: in \emph{illusory conjunction} experiments, briefly shown displays lead subjects to report the right features in the wrong combinations (a red X and a blue O seen as a blue X and a red O), showing that binding is a real achievement that can come apart. Philosophically, binding is one strand of the wider \emph{unity of consciousness}: experience presents many features and modalities as co-present in one field, and a theory of consciousness owes an account of that togetherness. Integrated information theory (see \hyperref[entry:position-integrated-information-theory]{\textsc{Integrated information theory (IIT)}}) makes the unity central — its measure Φ quantifies how far a system's cause-effect structure resists decomposition into independent parts (see \hyperref[entry:claim-consciousness-is-integrated-information]{\textsc{Consciousness as integrated information (Φ)}}) — and within this web the point is sharpened as \hyperref[entry:claim-binding-is-obstruction-to-product]{\textsc{Binding as failed factorization}}: unified experience is precisely the \emph{failure} of the conscious state to factorize into separate channels.

The binding problem should not be confused with the \hyperref[entry:argument-combination-problem]{\textsc{combination problem}} for panpsychism, which asks how micro-\emph{subjects} could compose a macro-subject. Binding concerns features within one subject's percept; combination concerns whole experiential perspectives. The two are related as puzzles about experiential unity at different scales, and the \hyperref[entry:concept-consciousness-freewill-duality]{\textsc{consciousness–free-will duality}} pairs each with an output-side twin: binding failures with the disunity of agency (see \hyperref[entry:claim-unity-dualizes-to-unity-of-agency]{\textsc{Unity–unity duality}}).

\begin{plainterms}
Your brain analyzes a scene the way a team of specialists would: one department handles color, another shape, another motion. Yet you never experience ``red, somewhere; round, somewhere; moving, somewhere'' — you see \emph{one red ball rolling}. The binding problem asks how all those separate analyses get stitched into a single experience, and why the stitching almost never mismatches (putting the red on the cup instead of the ball). Rushed glimpses \emph{can} mismatch, which shows the stitching is real work, not a given.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-binding-is-obstruction-to-product]{\textsc{Binding as failed factorization}} — the categorical sharpening: binding as the failure of experience to factorize into independent channels.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-unity-dualizes-to-unity-of-agency]{\textsc{Unity–unity duality}} — the duality-dictionary pairing of binding failures with akrasia and the divided will.
  \item \hyperref[entry:position-integrated-information-theory]{\textsc{Integrated information theory (IIT)}} and \hyperref[entry:claim-consciousness-is-integrated-information]{\textsc{Consciousness as integrated information (Φ)}} — the theory that makes resistance to decomposition the very measure of consciousness.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-combination-problem]{\textsc{Combination problem}} — the neighboring unity puzzle about subjects, not features.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{position-biological-naturalism}{Biological naturalism}{Position \,\textperiodcentered\, after Searle}{Biological naturalism}{}{$\bigstar$}
Biological naturalism is the view that consciousness is a genuine, higher-level \emph{biological} feature of the brain — as much a product of neural tissue as digestion is a product of the stomach. It is at once \emph{physicalist}, holding that mind is wholly caused by and realised in the brain's physical processes with nothing ``over and above'' them, and \emph{anti-reductionist}, holding that the first-person, felt character of experience is not thereby explained away or identified with anything describable from the outside. The position is principally associated with John Searle, who named it in \emph{The Rediscovery of the Mind} (see \hyperref[source:source-searle-1992]{\textsc{Searle — The Rediscovery of the Mind (1992)}}). Its core commitment is the claim that \hyperref[entry:claim-consciousness-is-biological-feature]{\textsc{consciousness is a higher-level biological feature}}.

\webheading{The core commitment}

On this view consciousness is a real, higher-level feature \emph{caused by} lower-level neurobiological processes and \emph{realised in} the brain (see \hyperref[entry:claim-consciousness-is-biological-feature]{\textsc{Consciousness as biological feature}}). The favoured analogy is digestion: the stomach's molecular activity both causes digestion and constitutes it, and no one is tempted to think digestion floats free of the stomach or, conversely, that it reduces to something non-biological. Consciousness stands to neurons as digestion stands to the gut — entirely natural, entirely physical, but a feature of a particular kind of living system.

What does the explanatory work is \emph{causal powers}: the concrete capacities of brain matter to bring about effects, not the abstract pattern of organisation those powers happen to implement. Because it is the brain's causal powers that produce mind — not any program it might be described as running — merely implementing the right \emph{syntax} (formal symbol-manipulation) is not enough for \emph{semantics} (genuine meaning or understanding) (see \hyperref[entry:claim-syntax-insufficient-for-semantics]{\textsc{Syntax insufficient for semantics}}). This is the moral biological naturalism draws from the \hyperref[entry:concept-chinese-room]{\textsc{Chinese room}} thought experiment and its \hyperref[entry:argument-chinese-room]{\textsc{argument}}: a person hand-tracing a program for Chinese produces correct outputs while understanding nothing, so running the program cannot be what understanding consists in.

\webheading{Consequences for artificial intelligence}

The stress on causal powers rather than program puts biological naturalism squarely against ``strong AI.'' It rejects \hyperref[entry:claim-substrate-independence]{\textsc{substrate independence}} — the thesis that the right computational organisation suffices for mind on any hardware — and rejects the claim that a large language model running on standard computers thereby has genuine mental states (see \hyperref[entry:claim-llm-has-mental-states]{\textsc{LLMs have mental states}}). Accordingly it answers both \hyperref[entry:question-substrate-independence]{\textsc{Substrate independence}} and \hyperref[entry:question-llm-moral-status]{\textsc{Moral status of LLMs}} in the negative for any \emph{purely computational} system: a computer that simulates a brain no more has a mind than a computer that simulates a storm makes anything wet.

\webheading{How it differs from neighbouring views}

Against \hyperref[entry:concept-functionalism]{\textsc{Functionalism}}, biological naturalism denies that the right causal \emph{organisation} suffices independently of the realiser's causal \emph{powers}; sameness of functional role does not guarantee sameness of mind. Unlike \hyperref[entry:position-proteocentrism]{\textsc{Proteocentrism}}, the demand is not for \emph{carbon} as such but for the relevant causal powers — though Searle holds that, as a matter of fact, only brain-like systems are so far known to possess them. And unlike \hyperref[entry:position-type-identity]{\textsc{Type-identity theory}}, it is \emph{non-reductive}: consciousness is caused by neural processes without being identical to any neural state-\emph{type}, so it is not the claim that pain just is, say, C-fibre firing.

\begin{plainterms}
Searle's view: consciousness is a biological phenomenon, produced by brains the way digestion is produced by stomachs — perfectly physical, but the product of a living organ's specific causal powers, not of any abstract recipe. So you can't get a mind just by running the right \emph{program} on any old hardware (his Chinese Room makes the point: shuffling symbols by rules gives you correct outputs with zero understanding). The upshot for AI: a computer simulating a brain no more \emph{has} a mind than a computer simulating a storm gets you wet — you'd need a system with the right causal powers, not just the right code.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-consciousness-is-biological-feature]{\textsc{Consciousness as biological feature}} — the atomic thesis this position commits to.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-syntax-insufficient-for-semantics]{\textsc{Syntax insufficient for semantics}} — the further commitment that blocks pure computation from yielding mind.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-chinese-room]{\textsc{Chinese room}} and \hyperref[entry:argument-chinese-room]{\textsc{Chinese room argument}} — the thought experiment and argument supplying the ``syntax is not semantics'' moral.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-substrate-independence]{\textsc{Substrate-independence thesis}} — the strong-AI thesis the position rejects.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-llm-has-mental-states]{\textsc{LLMs have mental states}} — denied for any purely computational system.
  \item \hyperref[entry:question-substrate-independence]{\textsc{Substrate independence}} and \hyperref[entry:question-llm-moral-status]{\textsc{Moral status of LLMs}} — the open questions it answers in the negative for computers.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-functionalism]{\textsc{Functionalism}} — the rival it parts from over organisation versus causal powers.
  \item \hyperref[entry:position-proteocentrism]{\textsc{Proteocentrism}} — a neighbour demanding carbon specifically, where this demands causal powers.
  \item \hyperref[entry:position-type-identity]{\textsc{Type-identity theory}} — the reductive neighbour, against which biological naturalism is non-reductive.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{claim-biology-currently-unforgeable}{Biology currently unforgeable}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{Biology currently unforgeable}{Biological substrate cannot currently be counterfeited to the standard required to fake suffering}{$\odot$}
Living biological organisation is, for the moment, something that cannot be cheaply counterfeited. Unlike a behavioural sign — begging, weeping, a convincing show of distress — a functioning organism is expensive and not made to order, so at present it cannot be mass-produced to fake suffering. Biology is therefore, for now, an \emph{unforgeable} mark.

This is a \emph{contingent, time-indexed} empirical claim, flagged by its ``currently'' and its ``to that standard.'' It does not assert that biology is necessary or sufficient for consciousness, nor that it could never be counterfeited — only that it presently resists the cheap, to-order forgery that defeats behavioural signs, the criterion-gaming dynamic of \hyperref[entry:concept-criterion-gaming]{\textsc{Criterion gaming}}.
