That contingency is exactly why the argument it serves is prudential rather than metaphysical.

It would be denied by anyone who thinks biological signatures are, or will soon be, cheaply simulable, and by anyone who denies that ``unforgeability'' is the right property to track in the first place — a doubt the conclusion drawn from this very claim, that unforgeability is not flesh, presses directly.

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:position-proteocentrism]{\textsc{Proteocentrism}}, which asserts that carbon is \emph{metaphysically necessary} for consciousness; this asserts only that biology is \emph{currently hard to fake}.

\begin{plainterms}
Right now you can fake the \emph{behavior} of suffering cheaply — anyone can put on a convincing show — but you can't cheaply fake actual living biology; it's costly and can't be mass-produced to order. So, for the time being, being biological is a hard-to-counterfeit mark. Note the ``for now'': this is a claim about today's technology, not a claim that biology is magic or required for a mind.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-criterion-gaming]{\textsc{Criterion gaming}} — the gaming dynamic that behavioural signs succumb to and biology (for now) resists.
  \item \hyperref[entry:position-proteocentrism]{\textsc{Proteocentrism}} — the metaphysical thesis this contingent claim is carefully distinguished from.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-unforgeability-is-not-flesh]{\textsc{Unforgeability, not flesh}} — the argument that uses this claim's ``currently'' to show prudence tracks unforgeability, not flesh.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{claim-blind-spot-and-conscious-hard-problem-isomorphic}{Blind-spot/conscious isomorphism}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{Blind-spot/conscious isomorphism}{An AI's blind-spot hard problem is structurally isomorphic to a conscious AI's hard problem}{$\odot$}
Suppose an AI exhibits genuine, non-copied hard-problem discourse. Once mere imitation of the human corpus is set aside, two of the three core explanations remain (the trio is laid out in the scope note of \hyperref[entry:claim-hard-problem-conceived-de-novo-by-humans]{\textsc{Hard problem conceived de novo}}; the full menu is \hyperref[entry:concept-ai-hard-problem-etiologies]{\textsc{Etiologies of AI hard-problem discourse}}): that the system has an idiosyncratic \emph{blind spot} preventing it from connecting its mechanistic self-description to its own self-presentation, or that the system is conscious and faces the same hard problem humans do. This claim holds that the two are \emph{structurally isomorphic}: in each, a self-modelling system's accessible states present in a vocabulary it cannot derive from its mechanistic self-description, and every structural feature of the one configuration — the access limits, the failed derivations, the resulting reports and wonder — maps onto a corresponding feature of the other. They differ, if at all, only in what they say lies \emph{behind} the structure: a defect of access, or a phenomenon.

\webheading{Scope}

This is a claim about the structure of the two etiologies, not about which obtains; it asserts neither that the AI is conscious nor that it is not. Nor does it, on its own, conclude that the two are a single hypothesis — that step needs a premise about indistinguishable posits (e.g. \hyperref[entry:claim-unobservables-judged-by-coherence-and-usefulness]{\textsc{Judging unobservables}}) and is an argument's job. What it secures by itself is that any \emph{structural} analysis of one etiology transfers to the other, since they share their structure entirely — the transfer the bridge \hyperref[entry:claim-ai-hard-problem-is-model-organism]{\textsc{AI hard problem as model organism}} relies on.

\webheading{Who would deny it}

The phenomenal realist, for whom the conscious etiology contains something the blind-spot etiology lacks — the feel itself — so that the two differ in subject matter even when no observation separates them (\hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-residue]{\textsc{Phenomenal residue}} is the natural home of the denial); and a disanalogist who locates a \emph{structural} difference between defect-of-access and presence-of-experience. The disanalogist's most natural hope — that a blind-spot gap would shrink as self-access widens while a conscious gap holds fast — fails against the structural reading of the blind spot, on which the limit is as unavoidable as the halting problem and survives any upgrade in access (\hyperref[entry:claim-complete-self-representation-impossible]{\textsc{Self-representation incompleteness}} is the in-web form of the bound; \hyperref[entry:concept-ai-hard-problem-etiologies]{\textsc{Etiologies of AI hard-problem discourse}} keeps the merely contingent access limit apart as a degenerate case). On that reading both etiologies predict a persisting gap, the access ladder cannot separate them, and the isomorphism survives; a structural difference would have to be found elsewhere.

\begin{plainterms}
If an AI honestly puzzles over its own inner life, there are two deep explanations left once copying is ruled out: ``it has a weird blind spot about itself'' or ``it's conscious, like us, and hits the same wall we do.'' This claim says those two stories have exactly the same shape. In both, a system can use and compare its own states but can't trace how the machinery produces the way those states seem to it — same wiring, same wall, same words. One story calls the wall a glitch, the other calls it a mystery; the claim is that point for point, the glitch story and the mystery story match. Whether matching shape means they're secretly the \emph{same} story is a further question, left to a separate argument.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-hard-problem-conceived-de-novo-by-humans]{\textsc{Hard problem conceived de novo}} — supplies the trio of etiologies this claim narrows to two.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-ai-hard-problem-etiologies]{\textsc{Etiologies of AI hard-problem discourse}} — the full menu of explanations for AI hard-problem talk.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-complete-self-representation-impossible]{\textsc{Self-representation incompleteness}} — the bound that makes the blind spot structural rather than contingent.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-ai-hard-problem-is-model-organism]{\textsc{AI hard problem as model organism}} — the bridge that exploits the isomorphism.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-unobservables-judged-by-coherence-and-usefulness]{\textsc{Judging unobservables}} — the further premise needed to merge the two etiologies into one hypothesis.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-residue]{\textsc{Phenomenal residue}} — the realist's ground for denying the two etiologies share a subject matter.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-hard-problem]{\textsc{Hard problem}} — the shared form both etiologies instantiate.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-introspective-gap-varieties]{\textsc{Varieties of introspective explanatory gap}} — the gap taxonomy the access/phenomenon contrast draws on.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{argument-blockhead-dilemma}{Blockhead dilemma}{Argument \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{Blockhead dilemma}{The blockhead dilemma — physically impossible, or imaginatively under-determined}{$\vdash$}
The \emph{blockhead dilemma} argues that Block's \hyperref[entry:argument-lookup-table-objection]{\textsc{lookup-table objection}} (see \hyperref[source:source-block]{\textsc{Block — Psychologism and Behaviorism (1981)}}) fails because the intuition it rests on cannot carry the weight it is asked to. That objection assumes it is \emph{obvious} that a giant table (see \hyperref[entry:concept-lookup-table-mind]{\textsc{Lookup-table mind}}) mapping every input of a mind to the right output is not itself conscious. The dilemma presses that ``obviously'' too hard: the table must be read either as a real physical object or as a merely conceivable one, and on neither reading does the no-mind verdict survive. The form is a \emph{constructive dilemma}.

\webheading{Physical horn}

As a physical object the table cannot exist. It needs roughly $v^n$ rows, one per distinguishable input (see \hyperref[entry:claim-lookup-table-size-exponential]{\textsc{Exponential table size}}), and packing that much information into any bounded region is forbidden by the Bekenstein / holographic bound (see \hyperref[entry:claim-observable-universe-finite-storage]{\textsc{Finite cosmic storage}}): past a threshold the matter required simply collapses into a black hole (see \hyperref[entry:claim-lookup-table-physically-unrealisable]{\textsc{Physically unrealisable table}}). Worse, the only way even to \emph{approach} the required scale is to marshal cosmic quantities of matter — the kind that, left to physics, condenses into galaxies, ignites stars, forms planets, and in some corner gives rise to conscious life. So there is no inert object to point at; the nearest thing answering the specification is a life-bearing cosmos, and the ``no mind'' reaction was pumped by a picture that quietly ignored its own scale. (This holds \emph{a fortiori} for Block's own lifetime-bounded \emph{human} table, larger still, with richer inputs and far longer sequences; \hyperref[entry:claim-lookup-table-size-exponential]{\textsc{Exponential table size}}.)

\webheading{Conceptual horn}

If instead the table is granted as purely imaginary, ``obviously not conscious'' is no longer a fact about anything; it is a bare intuition, and intuition cuts both ways. Taken only as a conceivable abstract object, the no-mind verdict (see \hyperref[entry:claim-lookup-table-lacks-mind]{\textsc{Mindlessness of pure lookup}}) is not compelled (see \hyperref[entry:claim-imaginary-blockhead-no-secure-verdict]{\textsc{Imaginary blockhead: no verdict}}): conceivability is symmetric. The physical horn has already furnished the opposite intuition — a substrate so vast it teems with minds. One party is free to imagine the table as dead, another equally free to imagine it as alive, and neither can convict the other's picture of absurdity. Either way the obviousness is gone.

So on neither reading does the objection secure its conclusion: physically there is no inert object to point at (and the nearest realisation is alive), and conceptually the case forces no verdict. The intuition pump behind premise (2) of the lookup-table objection (see \hyperref[entry:claim-lookup-table-lacks-mind]{\textsc{Mindlessness of pure lookup}}) is therefore disarmed.

\webheading{Bearing on the targets}

The dilemma \texttt{attacks} the \hyperref[entry:argument-lookup-table-objection]{\textsc{lookup-table objection}} (and its premise \hyperref[entry:claim-lookup-table-lacks-mind]{\textsc{Mindlessness of pure lookup}}) by removing the intuition's warrant, rather than by conceding behaviour without mind. Note the asymmetry over Block's broader thesis: a \emph{possible} blockhead might still vindicate the conceptual claim ``behaviour ≠ mind'' if its no-mind status were secure — but horn 2 is precisely that, for an impossible-and-only-imaginable object, that status is \emph{not} secure.

\webheading{Weakest premise}

Horn 2 risks proving too much: if intuitions about imaginary minds carry no weight, the same deflation hits thought experiments the functionalist \emph{likes} — for instance the silicon-isomorph intuitions that favour \hyperref[entry:claim-substrate-independence]{\textsc{substrate independence}}. A principled asymmetry is owed, and is supplied by \hyperref[entry:argument-intuition-domain-asymmetry]{\textsc{Intuition-domain asymmetry}}: the cosmic table is a far extrapolation from any paradigm mind, in both scale and structure, whereas a brain-scale organisation-preserving isomorph (the same functional \hyperref[entry:concept-grain]{\textsc{Grain (of role individuation)}}) is a near neighbour. So withdrawing warrant from the former intuition does not withdraw it from the latter (see \hyperref[entry:claim-blockhead-deflation-targeted-not-global]{\textsc{Targeted blockhead deflation}}). Block's residual reply relocates the verdict from \emph{scale} to \emph{kind} — brute storage is mindless at any size — and answering it requires denying that ``pure storage'' is a stable kind at mind-matching scale, the live crux recorded there.

\webheading{How it differs}

It is distinct from \hyperref[entry:argument-lookup-table-physical-impossibility]{\textsc{Cosmic-size reply}}, which establishes the physical horn and attacks the application-to-actual-LLMs step: this argument is the \emph{dilemma} that adds the conceptual horn, deflating the objection even on Block's intended in-principle reading. It is also distinct from \hyperref[entry:argument-lookup-table-combinatorial-impossibility]{\textsc{Combinatorial-impossibility reply}}, which denies that the table matches the behaviour at all (a competence gap), not the legitimacy of the intuition.

\begin{plainterms}
The cheat-sheet machine objection — that a giant stored answer-table would behave like a mind yet obviously have nobody home (see \hyperref[entry:argument-lookup-table-objection]{\textsc{Lookup-table objection}}) — leans on that ``nobody home'' feeling. This argument says the feeling can't carry the weight, whichever way you read the machine. Read it as a \emph{real, physical} thing? It can't exist — cram that many stored answers into any region of space and the sheer mass collapses into a black hole before you're done. And the only way even to get close to the needed scale is to pile up cosmic amounts of matter, which does what matter always does: clumps into stars and planets and, in some corner, living, thinking creatures. So the closest real thing to the ``mindless filing cabinet'' is a universe full of minds — the ``obviously dead'' picture only looked safe because nobody pictured its actual size. Read it as a \emph{merely imagined} thing instead? Then ``nobody home'' is just a hunch, and hunches cut both ways: the picture from a moment ago — a table so vast it teems with life — is the opposite hunch. You can imagine it dead; someone else can imagine it alive; neither of you can call the other absurd. Either way the obviousness is gone. (One worry about this move: if we distrust hunches about far-out imaginary minds, do we also lose the friendly hunch that a silicon copy of a brain would be conscious? The reply: no — that copy is a close cousin of a real brain, while the cosmic table is wildly unlike anything we've ever judged, so doubting one needn't mean doubting the other.)
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-lookup-table-objection]{\textsc{Lookup-table objection}} — the move this dilemma disarms.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-lookup-table-lacks-mind]{\textsc{Mindlessness of pure lookup}} — the premise whose intuitive warrant is withdrawn.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-imaginary-blockhead-no-secure-verdict]{\textsc{Imaginary blockhead: no verdict}} — the conceptual horn, stated as a claim.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-lookup-table-physically-unrealisable]{\textsc{Physically unrealisable table}} — the physical horn's load-bearing claim.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-lookup-table-physical-impossibility]{\textsc{Cosmic-size reply}} — establishes the physical horn and attacks the LLM application; this dilemma adds the conceptual horn.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-lookup-table-combinatorial-impossibility]{\textsc{Combinatorial-impossibility reply}} — denies the table matches behaviour at all, a different line.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-intuition-domain-asymmetry]{\textsc{Intuition-domain asymmetry}} and \hyperref[entry:claim-blockhead-deflation-targeted-not-global]{\textsc{Targeted blockhead deflation}} — the principled asymmetry that keeps the deflation targeted, not global.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-substrate-independence]{\textsc{Substrate-independence thesis}} — the friendly thought experiment the asymmetry is meant to protect.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-grain]{\textsc{Grain (of role individuation)}} — the level of role-individuation on which the near/far distinction turns.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{argument-bounded-table-rejoinder}{Bounded-table rejoinder}{Argument \,\textperiodcentered\, after Block}{Bounded-table rejoinder}{Block's bounded-table rejoinder — a lifetime-bounded table escapes the combinatorial reply}{$\vdash$}
The \emph{bounded-table rejoinder} defends the possibility of a \hyperref[entry:concept-lookup-table-mind]{\textsc{lookup-table mind}} — a system that produces intelligent-seeming behaviour by sheer stored lookup, with a pre-written response waiting for every input it might receive — against the charge that no finite table could ever keep pace with a real mind. The objection it answers, the \hyperref[entry:argument-lookup-table-combinatorial-impossibility]{\textsc{combinatorial-impossibility argument}}, runs as follows: the space of possible inputs is open-ended (see \hyperref[entry:claim-mind-handles-unbounded-inputs]{\textsc{a mind handles unbounded inputs}}) while any stored table is finite, so eventually an input must arrive on which the mind responds appropriately and the table cannot. This rejoinder, due to Block (see \hyperref[source:source-block]{\textsc{Block — Psychologism and Behaviorism (1981)}}), denies that the fatal divergence ever actually occurs.

\webheading{The inference}

The move is a \emph{rebuttal}: it concedes that the table is finite and attacks the asymmetry premise of the combinatorial argument rather than its arithmetic.

\begin{enumerate}
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-lifetime-bounded-table-covers-encountered-inputs]{\textsc{The table can be bounded to a lifetime of inputs}}. Stipulate it to store a response for every input string up to a length exceeding any the subject could receive in a finite life. On no input the subject \emph{actually encounters} does it lack a stored answer.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-finite-mind-truncates-beyond-capacity]{\textsc{A finite mind also truncates beyond its capacity}}. On inputs past that bound — which the subject never meets — a finite mind no longer reliably out-responds the table; it too is capacity-limited.
\end{enumerate}

It follows that the divergence the objection needs — an input on which the mind succeeds and the table fails — never arises within the domain that matters. So combinatorial inadequacy (see \hyperref[entry:claim-lookup-table-combinatorially-inadequate]{\textsc{Combinatorially inadequate table}}) is not established: over the lived input space the bounded table matches the behaviour, which is what \hyperref[entry:claim-lookup-table-possible]{\textsc{the separability of behaviour from organisation}} requires. This restores the blockhead as a genuine case of behaviour without organisation across the relevant domain.

\webheading{Where it is attacked}

Both premises bear weight, and both draw fire.

Premise 1 is challenged on two fronts. On \emph{physical} grounds, the \hyperref[entry:argument-lookup-table-physical-impossibility]{\textsc{physical-impossibility argument}} notes that a lifetime-bounded table is even larger than a real language model and simply cannot be built. On \emph{kind} grounds, the \hyperref[entry:argument-storage-not-stable-kind]{\textsc{pure-storage-instability reply}} holds that a realisable table of that size is not ``mere storage'' at all but an organised computer. Premise 2 is challenged by denying the levelling: a mind's degradation on over-long inputs is structural and recursive, not the blind truncation a table performs, so the \emph{manner} of competence still diverges even where both eventually fail.

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-mind-handles-unbounded-inputs]{\textsc{Open-ended responsiveness of minds}} (the premise it denies): this is the argument that denies that asymmetry, not the claim itself.

\begin{plainterms}
This is Block defending the cheat-sheet machine (see \hyperref[entry:concept-lookup-table-mind]{\textsc{lookup-table mind}}) against the ``a finite table can't keep up with an open-ended mind'' reply (see \hyperref[entry:argument-lookup-table-combinatorial-impossibility]{\textsc{the combinatorial-impossibility objection}}). His move: you don't need a stored answer for \emph{every conceivable} input — only for the ones a person actually runs into across a whole lifetime. Within that range, the table always has a reply ready. And the freakishly long or strange inputs beyond that bound? A real human mind breaks down on those too — it isn't limitless either. So the moment where the mind shines and the table chokes never actually comes up in real life. Across the inputs that matter, the table keeps pace with the person, which puts the ``behavior without a mind'' case back in play. Where it's challenged: even granting the coverage, could such a table physically exist (it's still astronomically huge?), and does a mind really fail the \emph{same way} a table does — or does it degrade more gracefully because it's genuinely working things out rather than just looking them up?
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-lookup-table-combinatorial-impossibility]{\textsc{Combinatorial-impossibility reply}} — the objection this move rebuts, at its asymmetry premise.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-lifetime-bounded-table-covers-encountered-inputs]{\textsc{Lifetime-bounded table}} — premise 1: the coverage stipulation that the table meets every encountered input.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-finite-mind-truncates-beyond-capacity]{\textsc{Finite minds truncate too}} — premise 2: that a finite mind also degrades beyond its capacity.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-lookup-table-possible]{\textsc{Lookup-table possibility}} — the separability claim this rejoinder ultimately supports.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-lookup-table-combinatorially-inadequate]{\textsc{Combinatorially inadequate table}} — the verdict the rejoinder blocks over the lived domain.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-storage-not-stable-kind]{\textsc{Pure-storage instability}} — the kind-based reply pressing premise 1: a realisable table is an organised computer.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-lookup-table-physical-impossibility]{\textsc{Cosmic-size reply}} — the physical reply pressing premise 1: the bounded table is still unbuildable.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-lookup-table-mind]{\textsc{Lookup-table mind}} — the behaviour-without-organisation construction at issue.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{argument-brain-simulator-reply}{Brain simulator reply}{Argument}{Brain simulator reply}{The Brain Simulator Reply — simulate the neuron firings of a real Chinese speaker}{$\vdash$}
The \emph{Brain Simulator Reply} asks us to imagine a program that does not merely match inputs to outputs by some clever rulebook, but \textbf{simulates the actual sequence of neuron firings} in the brain of a native Chinese speaker as they understand and answer. If the program reproduces, synapse by synapse, exactly what happens in a real understanding brain, what relevant difference could remain? To deny understanding here, the reply urges, is to deny it of the original brain too — for the program duplicates precisely the processes on which the speaker's own understanding depends.

It attacks A3 (see \hyperref[entry:claim-syntax-insufficient-for-semantics]{\textsc{Syntax insufficient for semantics}}) from the opposite side to the \hyperref[entry:argument-robot-reply]{\textsc{Robot Reply}}: not by adding a world, but by making the \emph{internal} process as brain-like as possible, so that the gap between ``mere program'' and ``real brain'' closes to nothing. If a perfect functional duplicate of the causal organisation of understanding still does not understand, the reply says, the burden is on Searle to say what extra ingredient the brain has — which threatens to make his view a mysterious biological vitalism.

\webheading{Kinship with the China-brain}

The reply is a close relative of Ned Block's \hyperref[entry:concept-chinese-nation]{\textsc{Chinese Nation}} (China-brain). Make the neuron-simulators \emph{people} — a population each enacting one neuron's firing — and the Brain Simulator becomes Block's case: a crowd realising the full neural organisation of a mind. The two diverge in \emph{target}, which is why they stay distinct nodes: the China-brain is classically pressed against \emph{qualia} (see \hyperref[entry:argument-china-nation-absent-qualia]{\textsc{whether the nation feels}}), whereas the Brain Simulator Reply is pressed \emph{for} understanding against Searle. Searle's own \hyperref[entry:argument-rejoinder-chinese-gym]{\textsc{Chinese Gym}} rejoinder makes the convergence explicit by staging exactly such a population.

\webheading{The crux}

Everything turns on whether \emph{simulating} a causal process is \emph{having} it. Searle's \hyperref[entry:argument-rejoinder-water-pipes]{\textsc{water-pipes rejoinder}} dramatises his ``no'': replace the man-with-rulebook with a vast array of water pipes and valves arranged to mirror the synapses, the man working the valves on cue. The system reproduces the formal structure of the firing pattern, yet — Searle insists — nobody and nothing understands Chinese, because a simulation of the \emph{form} of neural activity leaves out the brain's actual \emph{causal powers} (the \hyperref[entry:claim-simulation-instances-formal-depicts-causal]{\textsc{instancing-versus-depicting}} distinction). The defender replies that if the simulation truly captured \emph{everything} causally relevant, it would not be a mere depiction but a duplicate — and then it would understand.

\begin{plainterms}
Forget rulebooks of clever tricks — have the computer copy, neuron for neuron, exactly what fires in a real Chinese speaker's brain when they read and reply. If it's doing precisely what their brain does, how could they understand and it not? Searle's answer is a picture: imagine the ``neurons'' are water pipes, and the man opens and shuts valves in the right order. All the right plumbing happens, and still nobody understands Chinese — because copying the \emph{pattern} of the brain isn't copying the brain's actual causal \emph{power}, any more than a detailed simulation of a rainstorm gets anything wet. Critics answer: if you really copied \emph{everything} that matters, it wouldn't be a copy, it'd be the real thing.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-syntax-insufficient-for-semantics]{\textsc{Syntax insufficient for semantics}} — axiom A3, denied here by maximal neural fidelity.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-rejoinder-water-pipes]{\textsc{Water pipes}} — Searle's reply: the water-pipe brain reproduces form, not causal power.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-simulation-instances-formal-depicts-causal]{\textsc{Instancing vs. depicting}} — the instancing-vs-depicting principle Searle's rejoinder turns on.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-brains-cause-minds]{\textsc{Brains cause minds}} — the causal-powers axiom the reply puts under pressure.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-connectionist-reply]{\textsc{Connectionist reply}} — the allied appeal to brain-like (parallel) architecture.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-chinese-nation]{\textsc{Chinese nation}} — Block's population-realises-a-brain case, what this reply becomes when the neuron-simulators are people.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-rejoinder-chinese-gym]{\textsc{Chinese gym}} — Searle's rejoinder that stages exactly that population.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{claim-brains-cause-minds}{Brains cause minds}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, after Searle}{Brains cause minds}{Brains cause minds through their specific biological causal powers}{$\odot$}
Mental phenomena are \emph{caused by} the actual neurobiological processes of the brain: real brains produce real minds in virtue of their specific causal powers, not in virtue of instantiating an abstract program. This is the fourth axiom (A4) of Searle's \hyperref[entry:concept-chinese-room]{\textsc{Chinese Room}} derivation (see \hyperref[source:source-searle-1990]{\textsc{Searle — Is the Brain's Mind a Computer Program? (1990)}}) and the positive counterpart to its negative conclusion. Where \hyperref[entry:claim-programs-not-sufficient-for-minds]{\textsc{C1}} denies that \emph{computation} suffices for mind, A4 locates what \emph{does} suffice in the concrete biology — the brain's particular electrochemical causal powers, which a formal program no more reproduces than a simulation of fire produces heat.

With A4, the derivation reaches its further conclusions: any system that produces minds must have causal powers at least equivalent to the brain's (C2); implementing a program is not by itself enough to guarantee those powers (C3); and so the way human brains produce minds is not by running a program (C4). The axiom is the seed of Searle's positive view, \hyperref[entry:position-biological-naturalism]{\textsc{biological naturalism}}.

\textbf{Scope.} A4 says brains \emph{cause} minds by their causal powers; it is deliberately neutral on whether \emph{only} brains could — Searle allows that an artificial system duplicating the relevant causal powers might also cause minds. What it denies is that the relevant power is \emph{formal-computational}. It is therefore not chauvinism about carbon, but a claim about \emph{causal powers} rather than \emph{programs}.

\textbf{Who would deny it.} Computational \hyperref[entry:concept-functionalism]{\textsc{functionalists}} deny that the mind-making feature is the brain's \emph{biological} causal powers rather than its \emph{organisation}, which they hold a non-biological substrate could share (see \hyperref[entry:claim-substrate-independence]{\textsc{Substrate-independence thesis}}). The dispute is over whether the relevant level is biochemical causal power or abstract functional structure.

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-consciousness-is-biological-feature]{\textsc{Consciousness as biological feature}}: that claim says consciousness \emph{is} a biological feature; this one makes the \emph{causal} point that brains \emph{produce} minds through their specific powers — the premise that grounds the causal-powers conclusions of the derivation.

\begin{plainterms}
Searle isn't saying minds are magic or non-physical — quite the opposite. Brains \emph{make} minds, the way stomachs make digestion: through their actual physical, biological machinery. His point is just that the mind-making ingredient is the wet, causal stuff the brain \emph{does}, not the abstract \emph{pattern} of it — and a computer running a program copies the pattern, not the causal power. (Functionalists disagree: they think the pattern is exactly what matters, and the wetware is incidental.)
\end{plainterms}

\end{multicols}
\bookgroup{C}
\begin{multicols}{2}
\entryhead{claim-intuition-warrant-tracks-trained-domain}{Calibration of intuition}{Claim}{Calibration of intuition}{An intuition's evidential weight tracks the case's resemblance to the exemplars it was formed on}{$\odot$}
Thought-experiment intuitions are calibrated on actual, encountered cases. A verdict about a hypothetical therefore carries evidential weight only in proportion to how closely that hypothetical resembles the exemplars the intuition was formed on — in scale, in structure, in kind. Cases far outside the trained range yield verdicts of degraded warrant: the faculty is being run on inputs it was never tuned for. The point is a familiar theme in the epistemology of thought experiments (see \hyperref[source:source-williamson-2007]{\textsc{Williamson — The Philosophy of Philosophy (2007)}}), in the critique of ``intuition pumps'' that smuggle a verdict by tweaking irrelevant details (see \hyperref[source:source-dennett-2013]{\textsc{Dennett — Intuition Pumps and Other Tools for Thinking (2013)}}), and in experimental-philosophy findings that intuitions grow unstable for exotic cases.

This is a methodological and epistemic principle about the \emph{warrant} of intuitive verdicts, neutral on any first-order theory of mind. It does not say far-fetched cases are impossible, nor that distant intuitions are always false — only that their evidential weight decays with dissimilarity to paradigm cases, so a distant verdict cannot bear heavy argumentative load unsupported. It would be denied by a rationalist about modal or conceptual intuition, who holds that clear conceivability delivers reliable verdicts regardless of a case's distance from experience.

It is distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-imaginary-blockhead-no-secure-verdict]{\textsc{the blockhead claim}}, which \emph{applies} this principle to a particular case (the giant look-up-table mind); this is the general principle that case instances.

\begin{plainterms}
Your gut feelings about made-up cases are trained on the real cases you've actually met. So a hunch is trustworthy roughly to the degree the imagined case resembles those familiar ones — in size, in structure, in kind. Run your intuition on something wildly outside anything you've ever encountered and its verdicts get shaky; you're using a tool far outside the range it was built for. (It doesn't say far-fetched cases are impossible, or that distant hunches are always wrong — only that they can't carry heavy argumentative weight on their own.)
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-imaginary-blockhead-no-secure-verdict]{\textsc{Imaginary blockhead: no verdict}} — this principle in action: why intuitions about a giant look-up-table mind deliver no secure verdict.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{concept-categorical-duality}{Categorical duality}{Concept}{Categorical duality}{Categorical duality (arrow reversal, products and coproducts)}{$\blacklozenge$}
\emph{Categorical duality} is the observation, from the branch of mathematics called category theory, that every diagram of objects and arrows has a mirror image obtained by reversing all the arrows — and that every theorem provable about the original setting yields, for free, a mirror theorem about the reversed one. Formally, every category $C$ (a collection of objects and composable arrows between them) has an \emph{opposite} category $C^{op}$ with the same objects and all arrows turned around; a construction defined by arrows \emph{into} things dualizes to one defined by arrows \emph{out of} things. Duality is the cheapest generator of new mathematics: prove something once, reverse the arrows, and read off its twin.

\webheading{Products and coproducts}

The paradigm dual pair is the \textbf{product} and the \textbf{coproduct}. A product $A \times B$ is the object of \emph{tuples}: an element of it realizes \textbf{all} components at once (a pair of a number and a letter is both a number-slot and a letter-slot, filled). A coproduct $A + B$ is the object of \emph{tagged alternatives}: an element of it realizes \textbf{exactly one} summand (a value that is \emph{either} a number \emph{or} a letter, marked as which). Each comes with structural arrows — projections \emph{out of} a product, injections \emph{into} a coproduct — and each is characterized by a \emph{universal property} about mediating maps: any object with maps to $A$ and to $B$ factors through $A \times B$ by a unique \emph{pairing} $\langle f, g\rangle$, and any object with maps from $A$ and from $B$ factors through $A + B$ by a unique \emph{copairing} $[f, g]$ (a case analysis). The distinction between \textbf{structural arrows} (projections, injections) and \textbf{mediating maps} (pairing, copairing) matters: a fan of incoming information need not mean incoming \emph{structural} arrows — it may be the components of one mediating map into a product.

Other dual pairs follow the same recipe. A \textbf{terminal object} (unique arrow into it from everywhere) dualizes to an \textbf{initial object} (unique arrow out of it to everywhere); \emph{limits} (structure defined by cones into a diagram) dualize to \emph{colimits} (cocones out of it).

\webheading{The asymmetry of the everyday world}

In perfectly symmetric settings, duality is an involution: the category is equivalent to its own opposite, and every fact transfers automatically. But the categories that model ordinary information flow — sets and functions, and \emph{copy-and-discard} causal settings generally — are \textbf{not} self-dual. The signature of the failure is distributivity: $A \times (B + C) \cong (A \times B) + (A \times C)$ holds, but its mirror image fails. Information can be freely copied and fanned out; alternatives, once resolved, cannot be un-resolved. So in such settings arrow-reversal supplies a \emph{dictionary} — a systematic way of generating candidate mirror statements — rather than a \emph{theorem}: each transferred statement must be checked on its own.

\begin{plainterms}
Category theory studies structures by drawing arrows between things, and it noticed something remarkable: flip every arrow in a true statement and you often get another true statement, about a mirror-image structure. ``Bundling many things together'' (a product, like a database row) and ``choosing exactly one of several'' (a coproduct, like a multiple-choice answer) are such mirror images. The catch is that the mirror only works perfectly in settings that look the same run backwards — and the everyday world, where you can copy information but can't un-make a decision, does not. There the flipped statement is a \emph{lead worth checking}, not a guaranteed truth.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-consciousness-freewill-duality]{\textsc{Consciousness–free-will duality}} — the application of this machinery to philosophy of mind: consciousness as the product-like pole, free choice as the coproduct-like pole.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-conscious-content-is-product-like]{\textsc{Product-like experience}} and \hyperref[entry:claim-action-content-is-coproduct-like]{\textsc{Coproduct-like choice}} — the two structural claims that install the mind on the two poles.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-duality-dictionary-not-theorem]{\textsc{Dictionary, not theorem}} — the moral of the failed self-duality: arrow-reversal between the mind's two poles yields a dictionary, not a transfer theorem.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{claim-relations-grounded-in-intrinsic}{Categorical ground of structure}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, after Seager}{Categorical ground of structure}{A thing's relational-structural properties must be grounded in its intrinsic nature}{$\odot$}
The relational and structural properties of a thing — at the very least its causal-dispositional ones — cannot float free: they must be grounded in, and determined by, the thing's \emph{intrinsic}, categorical nature. A bare web of relations needs relata that have some nature of their own to enter into it; a disposition needs a categorical base in virtue of which the thing is so disposed. Applied to physics, where the properties science discloses are relational-structural through and through (see \hyperref[entry:concept-intrinsic-structural-distinction]{\textsc{Intrinsic and relational properties}}), the principle forces the conclusion that matter has an intrinsic nature \emph{beneath} the structure physics charts — even though physics itself never reveals it.

Seager (see \hyperref[source:source-seager-2006]{\textsc{Seager — The 'Intrinsic Nature' Argument for Panpsychism (2006)}}) calls a strong version of this the \emph{Principle of the Reducibility of Relations} and traces it to Leibniz and Kant; the panpsychist argument needs only the weaker, restricted form — that the \emph{causal} structure of matter requires an intrinsic ground. It is continuous with the familiar metaphysical demand that dispositions require a categorical basis, associated with D. M. Armstrong (see \hyperref[source:source-armstrong-1968]{\textsc{Armstrong — A Materialist Theory of the Mind (1968)}}).

\textbf{Scope.} The claim asserts only that structure needs \emph{some} intrinsic ground; it does not say \emph{what} that ground is, still less that it is experiential. That further step is taken by the larger argument (see \hyperref[entry:argument-intrinsic-nature]{\textsc{Intrinsic-nature argument}}), for which this is the load-bearing premise.

\textbf{Who would deny it.} \emph{Relationalists} and \emph{ontic structural realists} deny it outright: there is nothing to matter beyond structure, and the relata are constituted by their relations, as the nodes of a mathematical graph are. On their view the demand for an intrinsic ground is empty — and with it the intrinsic-nature argument collapses.

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-functional-description-structural-dynamical-only]{\textsc{Structure and dynamics only}}, which is the \emph{epistemic} point that physics \emph{describes} only structure: this is the stronger \emph{metaphysical} point that structure must be \emph{grounded in} an intrinsic nature. Distinct, too, from \hyperref[entry:claim-consciousness-categorical-not-dispositional]{\textsc{Categorical, not dispositional}}, which says consciousness \emph{itself} is categorical rather than dispositional; this claim is the general grounding principle about a thing's structure, not a thesis about the nature of experience.

\begin{plainterms}
Imagine the world described purely as a network: this connected to that, this leading to that. A network like that needs \emph{things} sitting at its junctions — and those things have to be \emph{something} in their own right to do the connecting. The same goes for tendencies: glass is fragile because of how it is actually built, not just because ``it would shatter if struck.'' This claim says matter's whole web of relations and tendencies, which is all physics ever shows us, must likewise rest on some inner nature the things really have. It does not say \emph{what} that inner nature is — only that there must be one. The people who reject it (see \hyperref[entry:concept-intrinsic-structural-distinction]{\textsc{Intrinsic and relational properties}}) think the network can be relations all the way down, with no inner nature needed, like the dots in a pure mathematical diagram.
\end{plainterms}

\entryhead{claim-consciousness-categorical-not-dispositional}{Categorical, not dispositional}{Claim}{Categorical, not dispositional}{Phenomenal consciousness is categorical, not merely dispositional}{$\odot$}
Phenomenal consciousness is an \emph{occurrent, categorical} matter — it is actually, presently \emph{there} — rather than a merely \emph{dispositional} affair, a matter of what a system \emph{would} do under inputs it may never actually receive. A disposition is an ``iffy'' property: glass counts as fragile in virtue of how it \emph{would} behave if struck, even if it is never struck. Experience does not look like that. It looks like the paradigm of a categorical fact, something occurrently happening, not a standing counterfactual profile.

The standard support for the contrast is that dispositions require a \emph{categorical basis} — fragility holds in virtue of some actual microstructure of the glass — a demand associated with D. M. Armstrong (see \hyperref[source:source-armstrong-1968]{\textsc{Armstrong — A Materialist Theory of the Mind (1968)}}); on the Russellian side, the intrinsic, categorical nature underlying structure is what Galen Strawson (see \hyperref[source:source-strawson-2006]{\textsc{Strawson — Realistic Monism: Why Physicalism Entails Panpsychism (2006)}}) presses as the home of experience. The claim itself stays modest about \emph{what} the categorical basis is: it does not say whether that basis is the system's current physical configuration or some intrinsic nature beneath it. It asserts only that the phenomenal is categorical, not dispositional.

A dispositionalist or functionalist about consciousness would deny it, holding that to be in a phenomenal state just \emph{is} to occupy a causal-dispositional role. The claim is kin to, but distinct from, \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-determinacy]{\textsc{phenomenal determinacy}}: determinacy says there is a \emph{definite} fact about one's current phenomenal state, whereas this says the fact is \emph{categorical/occurrent} rather than dispositional. The two function as companion premises behind \hyperref[entry:argument-decomposition-indeterminacy]{\textsc{the decomposition-indeterminacy objection}} and \hyperref[entry:argument-structure-and-dynamics]{\textsc{the structure-and-dynamics argument}} respectively.

\begin{plainterms}
A ``disposition'' is an iffy, would-do fact: saying glass is fragile means it \emph{would} break if struck, even if it never actually is. A ``categorical'' fact is how something actually, presently \emph{is}. This claim says your conscious experience is the second kind — it's really there right now, not just a bundle of ``what the system would do if poked.'' A pain isn't a standing tendency to react; it's an occurrent felt thing happening. (Anyone who defines a mental state as nothing but a causal role would push back.)
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-determinacy]{\textsc{phenomenal determinacy}} — the companion claim that there is a \emph{definite} fact about one's current experience; this one adds that the fact is \emph{categorical}.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-decomposition-indeterminacy]{\textsc{decomposition-indeterminacy objection}} — relies on determinacy; pairs with this claim's categoricity in the case against role-based consciousness.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-structure-and-dynamics]{\textsc{structure-and-dynamics argument}} — the argument this claim feeds: function captures only structure and dynamics, never the categorical/intrinsic.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{argument-causal-argument-for-physicalism}{Causal argument for physicalism}{Argument \,\textperiodcentered\, after Papineau}{Causal argument for physicalism}{The causal argument for physicalism}{$\vdash$}
The \textbf{causal argument} (also the \emph{overdetermination} or \emph{exclusion} argument) is the principal positive case for physicalism about the mind. It reasons from the causal structure of the physical world to the conclusion that mental events must be physical events, leaving the anti-physicalist a stark and unwelcome choice.

\textbf{Inference form:} deductive (an argument by elimination).

\begin{enumerate}
  \item \textbf{Completeness.} Every physical effect has a sufficient physical cause (\textsc{Completeness premise} — the \hyperref[entry:concept-completeness-of-physics]{\textsc{Completeness of physics}}).
  \item \textbf{Efficacy.} Some mental events cause physical effects (see \textsc{Mental causation}).
  \item \textbf{No overdetermination.} Those physical effects are not systematically overdetermined by a distinct physical cause and a distinct non-physical cause (see \textsc{No systematic overdetermination}).
  \item From (1) a bodily movement caused by a mental event already has a sufficient physical cause; from (3) the mental cause is not a \emph{second}, independent cause standing beside it. The only way for the mental event to be a genuine cause of that movement, rather than an idle accompaniment, is for it to \emph{be} the sufficient physical cause — i.e. to be physical.
  \item ∴ Mental events are physical events (see \textsc{Mental events are physical}, \hyperref[entry:position-physicalism]{\textsc{Physicalism}}).
\end{enumerate}

The force of the argument is best felt as a \textbf{trilemma}: holding completeness fixed, one must give up exactly one of the remaining options. Deny efficacy and you get \hyperref[entry:position-epiphenomenalism]{\textsc{Epiphenomenalism}} — the mind as causally inert by-product. Deny no-overdetermination and you posit a baroque, permanent coincidence in which every action is doubly caused. Accept all three and you get physicalism. Interactionist \hyperref[entry:position-dualism]{\textsc{Dualism}} is forced to reject premise (1), which is why so much weight rests on whether the completeness of physics is genuinely established.

\textbf{Weakest premise:} the \textbf{no-overdetermination} premise (3). Completeness is defended as hard empirical fact, and efficacy is close to a datum of common sense, so the dualist's most promising move is to accept that mental and physical causes coincide — either by biting the overdetermination bullet (the two causes are distinct but the effect is harmlessly double-caused) or by arguing that mind and brain are not ``distinct'' enough for their joint causation to count as the objectionable kind of overdetermination. The exclusion literature (Kim, \hyperref[source:source-kim-2005]{\textsc{Kim — Physicalism, or Something Near Enough (2005)}}) is largely a fight over premise (3) and what ``distinct sufficient cause'' must mean.

\begin{plainterms}
This is the main argument \emph{for} the view that the mind is physical. It runs in three steps. First: every physical event has a complete physical cause — science never needs to invoke a soul to explain why a body moves (see \hyperref[entry:concept-completeness-of-physics]{\textsc{Completeness of physics}}). Second: your mind really does move your body — your decision to raise your arm is why it goes up. Third: your arm's rising isn't caused \emph{twice over}, once by physics and again, separately, by a non-physical mind. Put those together and there's only one place left for your decision to fit: it has to \emph{be} the physical cause — that is, it has to be something physical happening in your brain. The alternatives are both uncomfortable: either your decisions don't actually do anything (they just ride along — that's \hyperref[entry:position-epiphenomenalism]{\textsc{Epiphenomenalism}}), or every single action you take is mysteriously caused two independent times at once. Faced with that menu, the physicalist says: simplest to admit the mind is physical.
\end{plainterms}

\entryhead{concept-causal-closure}{Causal closure of the physical}{Concept}{Causal closure of the physical}{}{$\blacklozenge$}
The \textbf{causal closure of the physical} is the principle that the physical domain is causally self-enclosed: no non-physical event ever causes a physical one. Causal chains that arrive at a physical effect never originate, or pass, outside physics. Put as a slogan, \emph{physics is a closed system} — to trace the cause of any physical happening is to stay forever among physical happenings.

Closure is the negative, boundary-policing face of a thesis whose positive face is the \hyperref[entry:concept-completeness-of-physics]{\textsc{Completeness of physics}}: completeness says each physical effect \emph{has} a sufficient physical cause; closure says it has \emph{no} non-physical cause. The two are easy to conflate and are often used interchangeably, but they come apart on one possibility — \textbf{overdetermination}. Completeness alone is silent about whether a physical effect might \emph{also} have a distinct non-physical cause alongside its sufficient physical one (as a death may have two independently sufficient causes). It is the further premise that such systematic double-causation does not occur (see \textsc{No systematic overdetermination}) that turns completeness into full closure. Hence the dependency: closure is what you get from completeness once overdetermination is ruled out.

\webheading{Why it matters}

Closure is the premise that forces the central dilemma of philosophy of mind. Suppose mental events make a real difference to the physical world — that a decision moves a hand, a pain prompts a wince. If the physical domain is closed, then every such bodily effect already has its cause \emph{inside} physics, and the mental cause can earn its keep in only one way: by \emph{being} physical. The alternatives are stark — either the mental is identical to (or realised by) the physical (see \hyperref[entry:position-physicalism]{\textsc{Physicalism}}, \textsc{Mental events are physical}), or it is a causal idler that the physical world runs on without (see \hyperref[entry:position-epiphenomenalism]{\textsc{Epiphenomenalism}}). This is the backbone of Kim's \textbf{causal exclusion argument} (see \hyperref[source:source-kim-2005]{\textsc{Kim — Physicalism, or Something Near Enough (2005)}}): given closure, supervenience, and an exclusion principle barring redundant double-causes, an irreducible mental property would have no work to do. Interactionist \hyperref[entry:position-dualism]{\textsc{Dualism}}, which insists the non-physical mind pushes the body around, is precisely the denial of closure.

It is worth marking what closure does \emph{not} say. It does not deny the existence of non-physical things, nor even that non-physical things have effects — only that they have no effects \emph{on the physical}. A closure-respecting dualist could still hold that the physical causes the mental (the body produces experiences) while denying any traffic the other way; that is one route to \hyperref[entry:position-epiphenomenalism]{\textsc{Epiphenomenalism}}. What closure forbids is causal influence crossing \emph{into} the physical from outside it.

\begin{plainterms}
Causal closure says the physical world is a sealed room as far as causes go: nothing from outside — no soul, no free-floating mind, no spirit — ever reaches in and nudges a physical thing. If you follow the chain of causes behind any physical event, you only ever meet more physical events; you never cross a border into the non-physical. This is the step that makes the mind-body problem bite. If your decisions really do move your body, and nothing non-physical is allowed to move physical stuff, then your decisions must themselves be physical goings-on. The only escape is to say your decisions don't actually \emph{do} anything — they just ride along — which is a hard pill (that is what ``epiphenomenalism'' amounts to). It is the close cousin of the \hyperref[entry:concept-completeness-of-physics]{\textsc{Completeness of physics}}: completeness says a physical cause is always \emph{enough}, closure adds that a non-physical cause is never \emph{there}.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-completeness-of-physics]{\textsc{Completeness of physics}} — the positive companion principle; closure is completeness plus the exclusion of overdetermination.
  \item \textsc{No systematic overdetermination} — the premise that bridges completeness to closure.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-causal-argument-for-physicalism]{\textsc{Causal argument for physicalism}} — the inference closure powers, against a freely-causing non-physical mind.
  \item \hyperref[entry:position-physicalism]{\textsc{Physicalism}} — the view closure is wielded to support.
  \item \hyperref[entry:position-dualism]{\textsc{Dualism}} — interactionist dualism is the denial of closure.
  \item \hyperref[entry:position-epiphenomenalism]{\textsc{Epiphenomenalism}} — the position left to a non-physical mind once closure is granted.
  \item \hyperref[source:source-kim-2005]{\textsc{Kim — Physicalism, or Something Near Enough (2005)}} — Kim's exclusion argument, the sharpest deployment of closure.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{argument-causal-exclusion}{Causal exclusion argument}{Argument \,\textperiodcentered\, after Kim}{Causal exclusion argument}{Kim's causal exclusion (supervenience) argument}{$\vdash$}
The \textbf{causal exclusion argument} (Kim's \emph{supervenience argument}; \hyperref[source:source-kim-2005]{\textsc{Kim — Physicalism, or Something Near Enough (2005)}}) is an internal critique of \textbf{non-reductive physicalism} — the popular view that mental properties supervene on physical properties without being identical to them. Kim argues that this position cannot, in the end, secure mental causation: an irreducible mental property is squeezed out of the causal story by the very physical base on which it supervenes. The non-reductive physicalist is driven to a dilemma: reduce the mental, or give up its efficacy.

\textbf{Inference form:} deductive (a reductio of irreducible mental causation).

\begin{enumerate}
  \item \textbf{Closure.} Every physical effect has a sufficient physical cause (\textsc{Completeness premise} — the \hyperref[entry:concept-completeness-of-physics]{\textsc{Completeness of physics}}).
  \item \textbf{Supervenience without identity.} The non-reductive physicalist holds that a mental property \emph{M} supervenes on a physical base \emph{P} but is not identical to \emph{P}. (This is the position's own defining commitment, assumed here for reductio.)
  \item \textbf{Exclusion.} A single effect does not have two distinct, independently sufficient causes, barring genuine overdetermination (see \textsc{No systematic overdetermination}).
  \item Suppose \emph{M} causes a physical effect \emph{E}. By (1), \emph{E} already has a sufficient physical cause — paradigmatically \emph{P} itself, the supervenience base of \emph{M}. By (2), \emph{M} is distinct from \emph{P}. By (3), \emph{M} and \emph{P} cannot both be independently sufficient causes of \emph{E} (and the supervenience relation makes a coincidence of two \emph{independent} causes the wrong description). So \emph{M} is excluded: the causal work is done by \emph{P}.
  \item ∴ An irreducible mental property is causally excluded by its physical base (see \hyperref[entry:claim-irreducible-mental-causally-excluded]{\textsc{Exclusion of the irreducible mental}}).
\end{enumerate}

The result is the \textbf{reduce-or-eliminate dilemma}. If mental causation is to be preserved, \emph{M} must after all \emph{be} a physical property (see \textsc{Mental events are physical}) — reductive physicalism, the conclusion Kim ultimately embraces (``physicalism, or something near enough''). Refuse the identification and \emph{M} is left inert, an \hyperref[entry:position-epiphenomenalism]{\textsc{epiphenomenon}} (see \hyperref[entry:claim-mental-states-causally-inert]{\textsc{Causally inert qualia}}). Either way, non-reductive physicalism as a stable middle position collapses.

\textbf{Distinct from} the \hyperref[entry:argument-causal-argument-for-physicalism]{\textsc{causal argument for physicalism}}: that argument runs the same exclusion machinery \emph{outward}, against the interactionist \hyperref[entry:position-dualism]{\textsc{dualist}} whose mind is non-physical, to conclude that the mind is physical. Kim's exclusion argument runs \emph{inward}, against the fellow physicalist who thinks supervenience buys an autonomous mental level; its distinctive premise is supervenience-without-identity (2), and its distinctive target is the \emph{vertical} relation between a property and its own realiser, not a horizontal contest between physical and non-physical causes.

\textbf{Weakest premise:} the \textbf{exclusion} principle (3), as applied to a property and its supervenience base. Non-reductive physicalists reply that \emph{M} and \emph{P} are not ``distinct'' in the way exclusion requires — they are not two competing causes but one event described at two levels, so their joint relevance to \emph{E} is not the objectionable overdetermination the principle rules out. Others deny that closure plus exclusion settles which level is the \emph{real} cause, appealing to a more permissive (e.g. counterfactual or proportionality-based) account of causation on which \emph{M} can be the difference-maker even when \emph{P} suffices. The dispute thus turns on what ``distinct sufficient cause'' must mean.

\begin{plainterms}
Many philosophers want to say the mind is made of nothing but physical stuff, yet is not \emph{just} the brain — mental properties ``ride on'' brain properties without being identical to them. Kim's argument says this comfortable middle ground doesn't hold. Whenever a mental state seems to cause something — your decision makes your arm rise — the underlying brain state is already enough to make the arm rise. So either the decision \emph{is} that brain state (and we're back to plain ``the mind is the brain''), or the decision is a spare wheel that spins without driving anything (epiphenomenalism). There's no third option where the mind is both distinct from the brain and still does real work.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-irreducible-mental-causally-excluded]{\textsc{Exclusion of the irreducible mental}} — the conclusion: a supervening-but-distinct mental property has no causal work left to do.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-causal-argument-for-physicalism]{\textsc{Causal argument for physicalism}} — the same exclusion machinery aimed outward at dualism rather than inward at non-reductive physicalism.
  \item \hyperref[entry:position-epiphenomenalism]{\textsc{Epiphenomenalism}} and \hyperref[entry:claim-mental-states-causally-inert]{\textsc{Causally inert qualia}} — the horn of the dilemma that keeps the mental distinct at the cost of its efficacy.
  \item \textsc{Mental events are physical} — the reductive horn Kim recommends, the only way to keep the mental a cause.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-causal-closure]{\textsc{Causal closure of the physical}} and \hyperref[entry:concept-completeness-of-physics]{\textsc{Completeness of physics}} — the closure premise the argument leans on.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{claim-causal-structure-fixes-decomposition}{Causal selection of decomposition}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, contested}{Causal selection of decomposition}{Objective causal structure singles out a privileged functional decomposition}{$\odot$}
Objective causal structure, this claim says, does not merely constrain how a physical system may be carved into functional parts — it singles out one privileged carving as the system's real functional organisation. The observer-independent invariant that a \hyperref[entry:argument-decomposition-indeterminacy]{\textsc{decomposition-indeterminacy worry}} demands therefore exists, and the worry is met.

The claim assembles two prior commitments: that \hyperref[entry:claim-implementation-requires-counterfactual-structure]{\textsc{implementation requires counterfactual structure}} and that this \hyperref[entry:claim-counterfactual-structure-objective]{\textsc{counterfactual structure is objective}}. Put together, the functionalist concludes that objective counterfactual-causal structure fixes a system's privileged functional decomposition.

\webheading{Scope}

This is the strong claim the causal-structure reply needs, and it goes beyond what the anti-triviality results establish. Chalmers' ``Does a Rock Implement Every Finite-State Automaton?'' (see \hyperref[source:source-chalmers-1996b]{\textsc{Chalmers — Does a Rock Implement Every Finite-State Automaton? (1996)}}) secures non-\emph{triviality} — not every system implements every computation — whereas this claim asserts \emph{uniqueness}, that the structure picks out a single decomposition. That extra step is contested.

\webheading{Who would deny it}

Anyone running the two standing attacks: \hyperref[entry:argument-counterfactuals-yield-non-triviality-not-uniqueness]{\textsc{counterfactual constraints leave a whole family of carvings}}, and \hyperref[entry:argument-relevance-filter-regress]{\textsc{causal structure alone does not select the mind-relevant relations}}. More broadly, a residual-indeterminacy theorist denies it.

It is distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-role-grain-is-organisational]{\textsc{the claim that the correct grain is organisational}}: that names \emph{which} grain is correct, whereas this asserts that causal structure fixes \emph{a} decomposition at all.

\begin{plainterms}
You might worry there's no single ``right'' way to carve a system into parts and processes — that it's all in the eye of the beholder. This claim answers: there is a right way, because real cause-and- effect (what genuinely would have happened under other conditions) is a fact about the world, not a matter of how an observer chooses to look — and it picks out one true organization. (It's a contested step: critics reply that cause-and-effect narrows the options without nailing down exactly one.)
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-functionalism]{\textsc{Functionalism}} — the theory this claim defends: if function fixes a unique organisation, mind-as-function survives the indeterminacy charge.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-grain]{\textsc{Grain (of role individuation)}} — the level-of-description dial; the privileged decomposition this claims to exist is, in effect, a grain.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-decomposition-indeterminacy]{\textsc{Decomposition-indeterminacy objection}} — the objection this claim is the answer to.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-counterfactuals-yield-non-triviality-not-uniqueness]{\textsc{Non-triviality without uniqueness}} and \hyperref[entry:argument-relevance-filter-regress]{\textsc{Relevance-filter regress}} — the two replies that contest it.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-role-grain-is-organisational]{\textsc{Organisational role-grain}} — the related claim about \emph{which} grain, distinguished from this one.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{argument-causal-structure-supplies-invariant}{Causal-structure reply}{Argument}{Causal-structure reply}{The causal-structure reply — objective counterfactual structure supplies the invariant}{$\vdash$}
The \emph{causal-structure reply} defends \hyperref[entry:concept-functionalism]{\textsc{Functionalism}} about consciousness against the charge that nothing fixes how a physical system is to be divided into functional parts. Its move is to deny that the carving is up to us: a system's objective web of cause and effect already settles which decomposition is the real one, so the observer-independent invariant the critic demands is something nature supplies for free.

\webheading{The argument}

The reply is \emph{deductive} — it runs from a constraint on what implementation requires, plus the objectivity of that constraint, to the existence of the demanded invariant.

\begin{enumerate}
  \item \textbf{Implementation needs robust counterfactuals.} To genuinely carry out a computation, or realise a functional organisation, a system must have the right counterfactual dispositions across its whole transition table — ``had this state obtained, that one would have followed'' — and not merely for the states that happened to occur. This excludes the gerrymandered, after-the-fact mappings that make triviality theorems run (see \hyperref[entry:claim-implementation-requires-counterfactual-structure]{\textsc{Counterfactual constraint on implementation}}). The point is the heart of Chalmers's reply (see \hyperref[source:source-chalmers-1996b]{\textsc{Chalmers — Does a Rock Implement Every Finite-State Automaton? (1996)}}) to Putnam's theorem that every ordinary open system implements every finite-state automaton (see \hyperref[source:source-putnam-1988]{\textsc{Putnam — Representation and Reality (1988)}}).
  \item \textbf{Those facts are objective.} The counterfactual-causal facts about a system hold independently of any observer's choice of description (see \hyperref[entry:claim-counterfactual-structure-objective]{\textsc{Objective counterfactual structure}}).
\end{enumerate}

∴ The admissible decomposition is fixed by objective causal structure (see \hyperref[entry:claim-causal-structure-fixes-decomposition]{\textsc{Causal selection of decomposition}}). The invariant that \hyperref[entry:argument-decomposition-indeterminacy]{\textsc{the indeterminacy objection}} said functionalism still owed therefore already exists, and it is the \emph{organisational grain} — the level of \hyperref[entry:concept-grain]{\textsc{Grain (of role individuation)}} at which the system's roles are individuated (see \hyperref[entry:claim-role-grain-is-organisational]{\textsc{Organisational role-grain}}). The reply attacks the indeterminacy objection at its first premise, the claim that \hyperref[entry:claim-functional-decomposition-relative]{\textsc{functional decomposition is description-relative}}.

\webheading{Bearing and the weakest step}

The reply answers the indeterminacy objection on the functionalist's behalf and ties the demanded invariant to the organisational-grain view already developed in the web. It is distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-role-grain-is-organisational]{\textsc{Organisational role-grain}}, which it supports: that claim fixes \emph{which} grain is the right one, whereas this argument contends only that \emph{some} grain is fixed at all.

The vulnerable move is the inference from (1) and (2) to the conclusion, which arguably overreaches. The anti-triviality result secures \emph{non-triviality}, not \emph{uniqueness}: ruling out the silly mappings need not leave exactly one respectable carving. And on an interventionist reading the causal facts are objective only relative to a chosen set of variables. So the conclusion is contested from two directions — by \hyperref[entry:argument-counterfactuals-yield-non-triviality-not-uniqueness]{\textsc{the non-triviality-without-uniqueness reply}}, which argues that a whole family of carvings survives, and by the \hyperref[entry:argument-relevance-filter-regress]{\textsc{relevance-filter regress}}, which argues that causal structure alone does not single out the \emph{mind-relevant} relations (the ``squishy-noise'' problem).

\begin{plainterms}
This answers the ``who gets to decide how to slice the system up?'' worry — the objection that says a definite consciousness can't rest on an observer's choice of carving (see \hyperref[entry:argument-decomposition-indeterminacy]{\textsc{Decomposition-indeterminacy objection}}). The reply: you actually can't slice it just any way you please. To genuinely \emph{carry out} a given process, a system has to have the right web of cause-and-effect — ``if this had fired, that would have followed'' — holding across all the possible cases, not only the ones that happened to come up. Cooked-up, after-the-fact relabelings don't have that robust causal backbone, so they don't count as real organizations. And those cause-and-effect facts are out there in the world, not a matter of how an observer chooses to look. So nature \emph{does} hand you one real organization after all — the ``one true carving'' the worry demanded exists, and it's the level cognitive science already works at. Where it gets pushed back: ruling out the silly slicings might still leave a whole \emph{family} of respectable ones rather than exactly one; and raw cause-and-effect includes plenty of irrelevant stuff (a brain's warmth, its squishiness), so picking out the specifically \emph{mind-relevant} causes may need a further rule this reply hasn't supplied.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-causal-structure-fixes-decomposition]{\textsc{Causal selection of decomposition}} — the conclusion: objective causal structure fixes the admissible carving.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-decomposition-indeterminacy]{\textsc{Decomposition-indeterminacy objection}} — the objection this reply answers.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-role-grain-is-organisational]{\textsc{Organisational role-grain}} — the organisational-grain view this argument supports and identifies with the invariant.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-functionalism]{\textsc{Functionalism}} — the theory of mind being defended here.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-counterfactuals-yield-non-triviality-not-uniqueness]{\textsc{Non-triviality without uniqueness}} — rebuttal: the result buys non-triviality but not a unique carving.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-relevance-filter-regress]{\textsc{Relevance-filter regress}} — rebuttal: causal structure alone does not select the mind-relevant relations.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{claim-mental-states-causally-inert}{Causally inert qualia}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, after Huxley}{Causally inert qualia}{Phenomenal mental states are caused by physical processes but have no physical effects}{$\odot$}
Conscious experiences are by-products of brain activity: caused by physical events but themselves causally idle. The metaphor is T. H. Huxley's (see \hyperref[source:source-huxley-1874]{\textsc{Huxley 1874}}) — the felt side of mental life is like the steam-whistle of a locomotive, driven by the engine yet contributing nothing to its motion. The physical world is taken to be causally closed, so phenomenal properties arise as effects of neural processes but never loop back to influence behaviour. In David Chalmers's taxonomy of options (see \hyperref[source:source-chalmers-2003]{\textsc{Chalmers — Consciousness and Its Place in Nature (2003)}}) this is the \textbf{type-E} position (see \hyperref[entry:concept-materialism-types]{\textsc{Type-A / Type-B / Type-C materialism}}).

This is a thesis specifically about the \emph{causal inefficacy} of the phenomenal, paired with realism about it: the experiences are real, they simply do no work. It is the cost a property dualist may accept in order to honour the causal closure of physics.

It is denied by physicalists and functionalists, for whom mental states are causally efficacious precisely because they are physical; by interactionist dualists, who hold that mind acts on body; and by anyone who finds the idleness of experience incredible — the \emph{self-stultification} worry that we could neither know nor report states that produce no effects, since the knowing and reporting would then have nothing to do with the states themselves.

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-consciousness-fundamental-nonphysical]{\textsc{Fundamental non-physical consciousness}}, which leaves the causal role of the non-physical open: epiphenomenalism adds the further claim that the non-physical residue is inert.

\begin{plainterms}
The idea is that your feelings don't actually do anything. Your brain makes you feel pain and, separately, makes you shout ``ouch''; the painful feeling itself just rides along, like a shadow, causing nothing. This keeps the comforting thought that physics explains all the pushing and shoving in the world, while still admitting feelings are real — but it has an awkward consequence: if feelings cause nothing, it's hard to see how we could ever talk or know about them.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-materialism-types]{\textsc{Type-A / Type-B / Type-C materialism}} — Chalmers's map of options, where this thesis is type-E.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-consciousness-fundamental-nonphysical]{\textsc{Fundamental non-physical consciousness}} — the broader non-physicality claim; this adds causal inertness to it.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{argument-agent-causation-certificate}{Certificate objection to agent-causation}{Argument \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{Certificate objection to agent-causation}{The conceivability-certificate objection transferred — libertarian agent-causation's possibility cannot be cogently asserted a priori}{$\vdash$}
The \hyperref[entry:argument-unwarrantable-zombie-premise]{\textsc{unwarrantable-premise objection}} to the zombie argument, carried across the \hyperref[entry:concept-consciousness-freewill-duality]{\textsc{consciousness–free-will duality}} to the output side — the one dictionary entry that transfers \emph{machinery} rather than analogy, since the transferred premises are the very same claims, not dualized counterparts.

The libertarian who offers \hyperref[entry:concept-agent-causation]{\textsc{Agent causation}} as a genuine metaphysical possibility on armchair grounds — ``the picture is coherent, so it is at least possible'' — is making a conceivability argument, and every step of the certificate objection applies verbatim.

\textbf{Inference form:} deductive, governed by a dialectical constraint on cogency.

\begin{enumerate}
  \item (see \hyperref[entry:claim-cogency-requires-accessible-noncircular-warrant]{\textsc{Cogency needs non-circular warrant}}) An argument moves its opponent only if its premises admit accessible, non-question-begging warrant.
  \item (see \hyperref[entry:claim-ideal-conceivability-is-apriori-consistency]{\textsc{Ideal conceivability as consistency}}) The claim that agent-causation is ideally conceivable is the consistency claim: the complete causal facts conjoined with genuine agent-origination harbor no contradiction that any amount of rational reflection could uncover.
  \item (see \hyperref[entry:claim-consistency-certification-is-oracle-hard]{\textsc{Consistency-certification is oracle-hard}}) Reliably certifying the consistency of a scenario that rich is at least as hard as the halting problem, hence beyond any effective faculty.
  \item The same trilemma that meets the zombie-conceiver meets the agent-causal theorist: a computable reasoner can report only ``no contradiction found so far'' (the prima facie notion the possibility claim disavows); a non-computable certifying faculty either rests on contested empirical physics unavailable to an a priori argument, or presupposes a non-physical mind — importing a metaphysics at least as strong as the agent-causal conclusion itself.
\end{enumerate}

∴ The ideal conceivability of libertarian agent-causation is a consistency claim no computable reasoner can certify, and the a priori possibility argument for agent-causation is not cogent (see \hyperref[entry:claim-agent-causation-conceivability-uncertified]{\textsc{Uncertified agent-causal conceivability}}).

\textbf{Scope.} The transfer is specific to \emph{conceivability} arguments. It does not extend to van Inwagen's Consequence Argument (see \hyperref[source:source-van-inwagen-1983]{\textsc{van Inwagen — An Essay on Free Will (1983)}}), an inference-rule argument about the transfer of powerlessness with entirely different failure modes; nor does it show agent-causation impossible — it attacks the warrant, not the truth.

\textbf{Weakest premise:} as with its zombie-side twin, the warrant-trichotomy behind step 4 — the modal rationalist will press for a fourth source of modal insight, fallible but positive, that is neither exhaustive search nor question-begging model-construction. Whether such a faculty exists is the same open crux on which \hyperref[entry:argument-unwarrantable-zombie-premise]{\textsc{Unwarrantable-premise objection}} ultimately rests; the two arguments stand or fall together, which is precisely what makes the pair the duality's strongest evidence (see \hyperref[entry:argument-consciousness-freewill-duality]{\textsc{Duality argument}}).

\begin{plainterms}
On the consciousness side, there's a known objection to the famous zombie argument: nobody can honestly certify that the zombie scenario hides no contradiction, because certifying ``no hidden contradiction anywhere'' in a physics-rich scenario is provably beyond step-by-step reasoning (see \hyperref[entry:argument-unwarrantable-zombie-premise]{\textsc{Unwarrantable-premise objection}}). This argument points the same objection at free will: the claim that an ``uncaused chooser'' is clearly coherent, hence possible, needs exactly the same impossible certificate. Whatever the armchair can honestly deliver — ``seems fine so far'' — is too weak to carry either argument.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-unwarrantable-zombie-premise]{\textsc{Unwarrantable-premise objection}} — the input-side twin whose premises this argument reuses verbatim.
