  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-agent-causation-conceivability-uncertified]{\textsc{Uncertified agent-causal conceivability}} — the conclusion established.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-halting-oracle]{\textsc{Halting oracle}} — the formal backbone shared by both twins.
  \item \hyperref[entry:position-libertarianism]{\textsc{Libertarianism (free will)}} — the view whose armchair modal credential is undercut.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-consciousness-freewill-duality]{\textsc{Duality argument}} — the master argument that counts this transfer as its strongest sign.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{argument-rejoinder-chinese-gym}{Chinese gym}{Argument \,\textperiodcentered\, after Searle}{Chinese gym}{The Chinese Gym — a parallel crowd of clerks understands no more than one}{$\vdash$}
Searle's rejoinder to the \hyperref[entry:argument-connectionist-reply]{\textsc{Connectionist Reply}}. If the objection is that a single serial clerk is the wrong architecture, scale the room up to a \emph{gymnasium} full of monolingual English speakers, each one playing the part of a single neuron or node, passing signals to their neighbours on cue according to the rules. The system is now massively parallel and brain-like in organisation — yet, Searle insists, no individual in the gym understands Chinese, and the assembled crowd understands no more than any one of them. Parallelism multiplies the meaningless symbol-shuffling; it does not, by itself, manufacture meaning.

The move \texttt{supports} the \hyperref[entry:argument-chinese-room-derivation]{\textsc{derivation}} by extending A3 to parallel architectures: changing \emph{how} the syntax is distributed does not change \emph{that} it is mere syntax.

\webheading{Kinship with the China-brain}

The Chinese Gym is, structurally, a relative of Ned Block's \hyperref[entry:concept-chinese-nation]{\textsc{Chinese Nation}} (China-brain): a population of people jointly realising the functional organisation of a mind. The two cases are pressed against \emph{different} features, which is why they remain distinct nodes — Block's case targets \emph{qualia} (see \hyperref[entry:argument-china-nation-absent-qualia]{\textsc{whether the nation feels anything}}), whereas the Gym targets \emph{understanding/intentionality}. Both share the intuition pump's vulnerability: a critic who attributes the mental property to the \emph{whole} organised population (the \hyperref[entry:argument-systems-reply]{\textsc{Systems Reply}} again) denies that the failure of each member to understand or feel settles the matter.

\webheading{The live crux}

The Churchlands' point partly survives the parody: they did not claim the gym understands but that A3 — ``syntax cannot suffice for semantics'' — is an \emph{unargued assumption}, not a self-evident axiom, and whether the right physical network yields meaning is a scientific question the gym cannot settle by intuition.

\begin{plainterms}
The reply said the lone clerk was the wrong model — brains are parallel. So Searle fills a gym with people, each acting as one ``neuron,'' all signalling at once. It's parallel now, and still nobody in the building understands Chinese. His point: spreading the symbol-shuffling across a crowd doesn't conjure meaning. This is a cousin of the famous ``China-brain,'' where a whole nation acts out one mind — except that case asks whether the nation \emph{feels} anything, while this one asks whether it \emph{understands}. And the same nagging question dogs both: maybe the \emph{crowd as a whole} has what no single member does.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-connectionist-reply]{\textsc{Connectionist reply}} — the objection this answers.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-chinese-nation]{\textsc{Chinese nation}} — Block's structurally similar population-realises-a-mind case.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-china-nation-absent-qualia]{\textsc{Chinese nation (absent qualia)}} — the China-brain pressed against qualia, where the Gym is pressed against understanding.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-chinese-room-derivation]{\textsc{Chinese room derivation}} — the argument it defends, extending A3 to parallel machines.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{concept-chinese-nation}{Chinese nation}{Concept \,\textperiodcentered\, after Block}{Chinese nation}{Chinese Nation (homunculi-headed robot)}{$\blacklozenge$}
The \emph{Chinese Nation} is a thought experiment devised by Ned Block in ``Troubles with Functionalism'' (1978). Imagine the citizens of a whole country, or a head full of little people, given radios and a rulebook so that together they realise the entire functional and causal organisation of a single human mind: the same state-to-state transition structure a brain runs through, for an hour or so. By construction the system has exactly the inner organisation a conscious brain has. The question Block presses is whether it would therefore be conscious. Would there be something it is like to be the nation, or is there simply nobody home?

\webheading{What makes it distinctive}

The case has its bite precisely because it instantiates the functional organisation, not merely the profile of inputs and outputs a mind produces. It is therefore not the ``right behaviour, no processing'' case of Block's other machine, \hyperref[entry:concept-lookup-table-mind]{\textsc{the lookup-table mind}}, but the ``right processing, yet intuitively nobody home'' case. That is what makes it tell against \hyperref[entry:concept-functionalism]{\textsc{Functionalism}} proper rather than against mere behaviourism: it grants everything a functionalist asks for and still seems to lack experience.

\webheading{The dispute it feeds}

The Chinese Nation is pressed on whether realising a mind's functional organisation is \emph{sufficient for \hyperref[entry:concept-phenomenal-consciousness]{\textsc{phenomenal consciousness}}}. The intuition that the nation has none drives \hyperref[entry:claim-chinese-nation-lacks-qualia]{\textsc{the claim that such a population would feel nothing}} and the argument \hyperref[entry:argument-china-nation-absent-qualia]{\textsc{that organisation is not sufficient for consciousness}}, which in turn support the \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-residue]{\textsc{Phenomenal residue}} view and attack the uniform reading of \hyperref[entry:claim-substrate-independence]{\textsc{substrate independence}}.

\webheading{Replies}

Two replies are characteristic, and both differ from the ones the lookup table invites. The \emph{systems reply} denies the intuition outright: the nation taken as a whole may be a subject of experience even though no individual citizen is (Lycan, Dennett). A second worry concerns \emph{speed and timing}, whether so slow and distributed a system genuinely realises the organisation at all. There is also a pointed structural observation. \hyperref[entry:claim-role-grain-is-organisational]{\textsc{Organisational-role functionalism}} cannot cheaply exclude the Chinese Nation the way it excludes the lookup table, because here the organisation really is present; that is exactly why the case is sharpened into \hyperref[entry:argument-dense-china-brain-regress]{\textsc{the dense China-brain regress}} against the organisational grain.

\webheading{A literary illustration}

A vivid cousin of the case appears in Liu Cixin's science-fiction novel \emph{The Three-Body Problem} (see \hyperref[source:source-liu-cixin]{\textsc{Liu Cixin}}). There the First Emperor Qin Shi Huang, prompted by von Neumann, ranks thirty million soldiers across a plain, each small unit raising black-and-white flags while cavalry gallop the signals between formations, to assemble a vast human machine. In the novel the machine is a computer crunching the orbits of three suns; re-task it to run a \emph{brain} instead. Let each cluster of soldiers be a neuron, each raised flag its firing, the messengers its axons, and the whole drilled host step for one hour through exactly the state-to-state transitions a human cortex would. The army is now no calculator but the Chinese Nation itself: a country physically realising a mind's causal organisation while no single soldier feels a thing. If the soldiers enact the right transitions, is someone \emph{over and above} them undergoing the experience, or is there nobody home?

\begin{plainterms}
Imagine the entire population of a country handed radios and a rulebook and told to each act as one brain cell: when you receive these signals, send those. Follow the rules fast enough and the nation as a whole mimics, step for step, everything one human brain does inside, not just the outward behaviour but the actual inner wiring. Now the unsettling question: the country is doing literally everything your brain does when you feel pain, so is the \emph{country} feeling pain? Is there one extra ``someone'', the nation itself, having an experience, hovering above millions of people who each feel nothing out of the ordinary? If running the right pattern is all it takes to be conscious, you seem pushed to say yes, yet most people's gut says surely not. That clash is the whole point of the thought experiment. It is a sharper challenge than a dumb answer-lookup machine, because here the full inner organisation really is present, so you cannot dismiss it for having no inner structure.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-lookup-table-mind]{\textsc{Lookup-table mind}} — Block's other machine: right behaviour with no inner processing, the case this one is defined against.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-functionalism]{\textsc{Functionalism}} — the theory the thought experiment targets: mind as functional organisation.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-china-nation-absent-qualia]{\textsc{Chinese nation (absent qualia)}} — the argument that the realised organisation still lacks qualia, so organisation is not sufficient for consciousness.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-residue]{\textsc{Phenomenal residue}} — the view the absent-qualia intuition supports: experience outruns functional role.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-substrate-independence]{\textsc{Substrate-independence thesis}} — the thesis whose uniform reading the case attacks.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-dense-china-brain-regress]{\textsc{Dense China-brain regress}} — where the case is sharpened against organisational-role functionalism.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-phenomenal-consciousness]{\textsc{Phenomenal consciousness}} — what the nation is alleged to lack despite the right organisation.
  \item \hyperref[source:source-block-1978]{\textsc{Block — Troubles with Functionalism (1978)}} — Ned Block's ``Troubles with Functionalism'' (1978), where the thought experiment is introduced.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{argument-china-nation-absent-qualia}{Chinese nation (absent qualia)}{Argument \,\textperiodcentered\, after Block}{Chinese nation (absent qualia)}{The Chinese Nation realises the organisation yet lacks qualia — organisation is not sufficient for consciousness}{$\vdash$}
The \emph{Chinese nation argument} (also the ``China brain''), due to Ned Block (see \hyperref[source:source-block-1978]{\textsc{Block — Troubles with Functionalism (1978)}}), is an \emph{absent-qualia} objection to \hyperref[entry:concept-functionalism]{\textsc{Functionalism}}: it describes a system that realises a mind's full functional organisation yet, intuitively, has no felt experience, and concludes that organisation alone is not sufficient for consciousness.

\webheading{The thought experiment}

Suppose the people of a whole nation each simulate one neuron, signalling to one another — by radio, say — on exactly the conditions under which neurons fire. The population then jointly instantiates the same state-to-state organisation a brain does: not merely its outward behaviour but its full internal causal structure (see \hyperref[entry:claim-chinese-nation-realises-organisation]{\textsc{Chinese nation realises organisation}}). On functionalism, which individuates mental states by causal role, this signalling nation should have a mind, qualia and all. Yet it seems there is \emph{nothing it is like} to be the nation — the machinery runs, but no unified subject hovers above the crowd actually feeling anything (see \hyperref[entry:claim-chinese-nation-lacks-qualia]{\textsc{Chinese nation lacks qualia}}).

\webheading{The inference}

The form is a counterexample, an absent-qualia \emph{intuition pump}: a single case that realises the functional organisation while (intuitively) lacking phenomenal consciousness refutes ``functional organisation ⟹ phenomenal consciousness.''

\begin{enumerate}
  \item The nation realises a mind's full functional-causal organisation, not merely its behaviour (see \hyperref[entry:claim-chinese-nation-realises-organisation]{\textsc{Chinese nation realises organisation}}).
  \item Yet there is nothing it is like to be the nation (see \hyperref[entry:claim-chinese-nation-lacks-qualia]{\textsc{Chinese nation lacks qualia}}).
\end{enumerate}

Therefore realising the functional organisation is not sufficient for phenomenal consciousness. This gives positive reason for \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-residue]{\textsc{the phenomenal-residue thesis}} (role under-determines the phenomenal) and is a counterexample to the uniform, phenomenal-covering reading of \hyperref[entry:claim-substrate-independence]{\textsc{substrate independence}}.

\webheading{Why it is sharper than the lookup table}

The \hyperref[entry:argument-lookup-table-objection]{\textsc{lookup-table objection}} uses a system that lacks organisation, and so threatens only behaviourism; an organisational functionalist excludes it cheaply. The present case \emph{grants} the organisation, so it cannot be dismissed that way. It bears on functionalism proper — about \emph{qualia} rather than \emph{behaviour-as-evidence}. The two Block cases attack different sufficiency claims and meet different replies.

\webheading{Weakest premise and replies}

The \emph{weakest premise} is premise 2 (see \hyperref[entry:claim-chinese-nation-lacks-qualia]{\textsc{Chinese nation lacks qualia}}). It rests on an absent-qualia intuition that the \emph{systems reply} (Lycan, Dennett) rejects — holding that the nation as a whole is a subject though no citizen is — and that a functionalist would deflate as a mere artefact of our concepts (see \hyperref[entry:argument-gap-deflated-by-phenomenal-concepts]{\textsc{deflation by phenomenal concepts}}). That systems-reply rebuttal is the natural next move.

\webheading{Relation to neighbouring cases}

Distinct from the \hyperref[entry:argument-dense-china-brain-regress]{\textsc{dense China-brain regress}}, which takes the case one level up — against the organisational-grain reply (see \hyperref[entry:argument-organisational-grain-dissolves-dilemma]{\textsc{the grain reply}}) — to argue that the \hyperref[entry:concept-grain]{\textsc{grain of role individuation}} faces a liberal/chauvinist dilemma (see \hyperref[entry:claim-organisational-grain-faces-own-grain-dilemma]{\textsc{Organisational grain's own dilemma}}). The present node is the prior, classical absent-qualia objection: organisation present, qualia absent, therefore organisation insufficient. Distinct too from the \hyperref[entry:argument-explanatory-gap]{\textsc{explanatory gap}}, which runs via conceivability and zombies rather than a realised case.

\begin{plainterms}
Suppose an entire nation of people, each passing signals by raising flags, together act out the exact inner wiring of a single brain — not just its outward behavior, but every internal cause-and-effect step a brain goes through. On the ``it's the pattern that counts'' view, that flag-waving nation should then have a mind, feelings and all. But — the argument urges — surely there's no extra \emph{someone} hovering above the crowd actually \emph{feeling} anything; the machinery runs, yet nobody's home. If that's right, then running the right pattern of cause-and-effect isn't enough to produce a felt experience. This is a sharper case than the cheat-sheet machine (see \hyperref[entry:concept-lookup-table-mind]{\textsc{lookup-table mind}}): the nation really does carry out the full inner organization, so you can't wave it away by saying ``it has no inner structure.'' The contested part is the gut feeling that the nation feels nothing — some reply that the nation \emph{as a whole} might be a genuine subject even though no single citizen is.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-chinese-nation-realises-organisation]{\textsc{Chinese nation realises organisation}} — premise 1: the organisation, not just the behaviour, is realised.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-chinese-nation-lacks-qualia]{\textsc{Chinese nation lacks qualia}} — premise 2 and the soft point: the absent-qualia intuition.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-residue]{\textsc{Phenomenal residue}} — the general thesis this case supports: role under-determines the phenomenal.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-substrate-independence]{\textsc{Substrate-independence thesis}} — the thesis attacked, on its phenomenal-covering reading.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-chinese-nation]{\textsc{Chinese nation}} — the homunculi-headed system as a standing concept.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-functionalism]{\textsc{Functionalism}} — the target theory of mind.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-grain]{\textsc{Grain (of role individuation)}} — the grain of role individuation that the regress version turns on.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-lookup-table-objection]{\textsc{Lookup-table objection}} — Block's sibling case, aimed at behaviour rather than qualia.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-gap-deflated-by-phenomenal-concepts]{\textsc{Phenomenal-concept deflation}} — the functionalist deflation of the absent-qualia intuition.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-dense-china-brain-regress]{\textsc{Dense China-brain regress}} — the one-level-up version, pressing the grain dilemma.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-explanatory-gap]{\textsc{Explanatory-gap argument}} — the conceivability-based cousin of this realised-case objection.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{claim-chinese-nation-lacks-qualia}{Chinese nation lacks qualia}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, after Block}{Chinese nation lacks qualia}{A population realising a mind's functional organisation would nonetheless have no phenomenal consciousness}{$\odot$}
Even granting that the \hyperref[entry:concept-chinese-nation]{\textsc{Chinese nation}} realises the full functional organisation, the considered intuition behind Block's absent-qualia pump (see \hyperref[source:source-block-1978]{\textsc{Block — Troubles with Functionalism (1978)}}) is that there is \emph{nothing it is like} to be the nation — no unified phenomenal subject over and above the busy citizens.

This is a claim about \emph{phenomenal} consciousness specifically, not about intentional or cognitive states (the nation may well count as information-processing). It is the contested premise of the absent-qualia objection — contested because it rests on an intuition the \emph{systems reply} rejects.

It is denied by defenders of the systems reply (Lycan, Dennett), who hold that the nation as a whole is a subject even though no member is, and by functionalists generally who deny that absent-qualia intuitions carry evidential weight (compare the deflation of such intuitions in \hyperref[entry:argument-gap-deflated-by-phenomenal-concepts]{\textsc{deflation by phenomenal concepts}}).

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-lookup-table-lacks-mind]{\textsc{Mindlessness of pure lookup}} because that denies mind to a system \emph{lacking} organisation, whereas this denies \emph{qualia} to a system that \emph{has} the organisation — the harder, contested case. Distinct too from \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-residue]{\textsc{the phenomenal-residue thesis}}, the general claim that role under-determines the phenomenal; this is the specific intuited datum offered as evidence for it.

\begin{plainterms}
Picture an entire nation wired up to act out the exact inner workings of a single brain. The claim here is just the stubborn intuition that, even so, there's \emph{nobody home} — no unified someone hovering above the busy crowd actually feeling anything. It's specifically about \emph{felt experience}, not about whether the nation processes information (it might do plenty). And it's the disputed link in the whole objection: some insist the nation-as-a-whole really could be a subject even if no single person in it is.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-chinese-nation]{\textsc{Chinese nation}} — the system this claim denies qualia to.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-chinese-nation-realises-organisation]{\textsc{Chinese nation realises organisation}} — its companion premise: the organisation is genuinely realised.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-lookup-table-lacks-mind]{\textsc{Mindlessness of pure lookup}} — the easier cousin (no organisation), distinguished from this harder case.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-residue]{\textsc{Phenomenal residue}} — the general thesis this intuited datum is offered to support.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-gap-deflated-by-phenomenal-concepts]{\textsc{Phenomenal-concept deflation}} — the deflation of the absent-qualia intuition.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{claim-chinese-nation-realises-organisation}{Chinese nation realises organisation}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, after Block}{Chinese nation realises organisation}{A population can realise a mind's full functional-causal organisation, not merely its input-output behaviour}{$\odot$}
If each of a nation's people implements the role of a neuron (or functional node), passing signals on the same conditions, the population jointly instantiates the very state-to-state transition structure a brain instantiates — the functional organisation, not just the gross input–output profile (Block, \hyperref[source:source-block-1978]{\textsc{``Troubles with Functionalism''}}).

This is a possibility claim about \emph{multiple realisation of organisation} in an unusual medium. It is deliberately stronger than \hyperref[entry:claim-lookup-table-possible]{\textsc{Lookup-table possibility}}: here the internal organisation is present, not absent. By itself it takes no stand on whether the system is conscious — that is the separate question raised by \hyperref[entry:claim-chinese-nation-lacks-qualia]{\textsc{Chinese nation lacks qualia}}.

It would be denied by those who argue the organisation is not truly realised — for example because of timing or speed mismatches, or because radio-linked people cannot sustain the dense, real-time counterfactual dependencies the role requires (a realisation worry rather than a qualia worry).

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-lookup-table-possible]{\textsc{Lookup-table possibility}} because that system reproduces only behaviour by storage and lacks organisation, whereas this one reproduces the organisation. Distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-role-grain-is-organisational]{\textsc{Organisational role-grain}}, which says organisation is the individuating grain — a thesis this claim sets up a counterexample-candidate against, rather than asserting.

\begin{plainterms}
This says a giant crowd of people, each playing the part of one brain cell and passing signals by the same rules, could together reproduce not just a brain's outward behavior but its full inner pattern of cause-and-effect. It's a stronger setup than the cheat-sheet machine (see \hyperref[entry:concept-lookup-table-mind]{\textsc{lookup-table mind}}): here the inner organization is genuinely present, not faked by a stored answer list. On its own it takes no stand on whether such a crowd would actually be conscious — that's a separate question.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-chinese-nation]{\textsc{Chinese nation}} — the population-as-brain system this claim describes.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-chinese-nation-lacks-qualia]{\textsc{Chinese nation lacks qualia}} — the separate question of whether such a system feels anything.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-lookup-table-possible]{\textsc{Lookup-table possibility}} — the weaker storage-only cousin, distinguished here.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-role-grain-is-organisational]{\textsc{Organisational role-grain}} — the grain thesis this case is a candidate counterexample to.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{concept-chinese-room}{Chinese room}{Concept \,\textperiodcentered\, after Searle}{Chinese room}{The Chinese Room}{$\blacklozenge$}
The \emph{Chinese Room} is John Searle's thought experiment against the claim that running the right computer program is, by itself, enough to produce a mind (see \hyperref[source:source-searle-1980]{\textsc{Searle — Minds, Brains, and Programs (1980)}}). A monolingual English speaker is sealed in a room with a rulebook — in effect, a program — that tells him how to manipulate Chinese symbols purely by their shapes. Chinese questions are slipped in; by following the rules he passes back Chinese answers indistinguishable from a native speaker's. Yet he understands no Chinese. The room, Searle argues, has the right \emph{syntax} (formal symbol manipulation) without any \emph{semantics} (meaning, understanding, intentionality) — and a digital computer, which does nothing but manipulate symbols by their shapes, is in exactly the same position.

\webheading{What it targets}

The argument is aimed at \textbf{strong AI}, the computational-functionalist thesis that \emph{implementing the right program is sufficient for a mind}. That precise target is what separates it from two neighbouring thought experiments with which it is often confused. Ned Block's \hyperref[entry:concept-lookup-table-mind]{\textsc{Lookup-table mind}} (``Blockhead'') attacks the sufficiency of \emph{behaviour}: it produces the right outputs with \textbf{no} internal processing at all, just a giant table of canned answers. The Chinese Room, by contrast, \emph{does} run a genuine program; what it denies is that \emph{computation} suffices for \emph{understanding}. Block's \hyperref[entry:concept-chinese-nation]{\textsc{Chinese nation}} differs in the other direction: there a population realises a mind's full functional organisation, and the case is pressed against \emph{qualia} — felt experience — whereas the Room is pressed against \emph{intentionality}, whether the symbols mean anything to the system at all. Three different experiments, three different sufficiency theses.

\webheading{Characteristic replies}

Each of the standard replies is an objection-in-waiting. The \textbf{Systems Reply} grants that the man does not understand Chinese but locates the understanding in the whole system — man, rulebook, and room together — much as no single neuron understands English. The \textbf{Robot Reply} embeds the program in a robot body with cameras and limbs, so that its symbols stand in causal commerce with the world and thereby acquire grounding. The \textbf{Brain Simulator Reply} has the program simulate, neuron by neuron, the actual brain activity of a Chinese speaker — what relevant difference could remain? Searle rejoins each: against the Systems Reply, for instance, he lets the man memorise the rulebook and work in the open air, so that the man \emph{is} the whole system — and still understands nothing.

\begin{plainterms}
Imagine you're locked in a room with a giant instruction book. Notes in Chinese slide under the door; you can't read a word of Chinese, but the book says ``if you see these squiggles, copy out those squiggles,'' so you shuffle symbols and pass back replies that fool fluent speakers outside. You're behaving exactly like someone who understands Chinese — yet you understand nothing; you're just moving shapes around. Searle's point: a computer is in the same boat. Running a program is \emph{shuffling symbols by their shapes}, and no amount of that, he says, adds up to actually \emph{understanding} what the symbols mean. (The famous comeback: maybe \emph{you} don't understand, but the whole room-plus-rulebook system does.)
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-functionalism]{\textsc{Functionalism}} — the family of theories in the argument's crosshairs: mind as the running of the right program, independent of what runs it.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-lookup-table-mind]{\textsc{Lookup-table mind}} — the neighbouring case with right behaviour and \emph{no} processing; attacks behavioural sufficiency, where the Room attacks computational sufficiency.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-chinese-nation]{\textsc{Chinese nation}} — the neighbouring case with the full functional organisation, pressed against qualia where the Room is pressed against understanding.
  \item \hyperref[source:source-searle-1980]{\textsc{Searle — Minds, Brains, and Programs (1980)}} — ``Minds, Brains, and Programs'', the original statement of the argument and its replies.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{argument-chinese-room}{Chinese room argument}{Argument \,\textperiodcentered\, after Searle}{Chinese room argument}{The Chinese Room — running a program is not sufficient for understanding}{$\vdash$}
The \emph{Chinese room argument}, due to John Searle (see \hyperref[source:source-searle-1980]{\textsc{Searle — Minds, Brains, and Programs (1980)}}), holds that running the right computer program is never enough, by itself, to understand a language — or to have a mind. It is the most famous objection to \emph{strong AI}, the thesis that a suitably programmed computer would thereby possess genuine mental states.

\webheading{The thought experiment}

Imagine a person who speaks no Chinese locked in a room with a vast rulebook. Chinese symbols are passed in; following the rulebook — whose instructions key the symbols only by their shape, never their meaning — the person assembles other Chinese symbols and passes them back out. The rulebook is so good that the replies are indistinguishable from a native speaker's (see \hyperref[entry:claim-chinese-room-runs-program]{\textsc{the room runs the program}}). Yet the person inside understands not a word of Chinese: they are manipulating \emph{syntax} (formal shapes) with no grasp of \emph{semantics} (meaning) (see \hyperref[entry:claim-chinese-room-no-understanding]{\textsc{the person understands nothing}}).

\webheading{The inference}

The form is a thought-experiment counterexample — an \emph{intuition pump} — against a sufficiency thesis:

\begin{enumerate}
  \item \emph{Scenario} — a non-Chinese-speaker, executing a formal program, produces fluent Chinese (see \hyperref[entry:claim-chinese-room-runs-program]{\textsc{Program execution (Chinese room)}}).
  \item \emph{Intuition} — he understands no Chinese: syntax without semantics (see \hyperref[entry:claim-chinese-room-no-understanding]{\textsc{Chinese room: no understanding}}).
  \item \emph{Bridge} — if running the program sufficed for understanding, the executor (who runs it) would understand.
\end{enumerate}

Since he does not, executing a formal program is not sufficient for understanding (see \hyperref[entry:claim-syntax-insufficient-for-semantics]{\textsc{Syntax insufficient for semantics}}). This attacks \hyperref[entry:claim-substrate-independence]{\textsc{substrate independence}} at its sufficiency direction: realising the program or role does not by itself deliver the semantic side of mind.

\webheading{Replies and the live crux}

The \emph{weakest premise} is the intuition (premise 2) together with the bridge (premise 3). The move fits the \emph{thought-experiment} scheme, and the standard rejoinder — the \textbf{Systems Reply} — raises two of that scheme's critical questions (CQ-bridge and CQ-scope): grant that the \emph{person} understands nothing, but the verdict was meant to bear on the \emph{whole system} — person plus rulebook plus memory store — which may understand even though no component does (compare the systems reply to the \hyperref[entry:concept-chinese-nation]{\textsc{Chinese nation}}). The \textbf{Robot Reply} presses CQ-scope: the bare room refutes only \emph{ungrounded} symbol-AI, not a program \emph{causally embedded} in a world. Searle's rejoinders — internalise the rulebook, and the man still understands nothing — are themselves contested, and that exchange is the live crux of the argument.

\webheading{Relation to neighbouring cases}

This is distinct from the \hyperref[entry:argument-lookup-table-objection]{\textsc{lookup-table objection}}, which uses a \emph{flat table} to deny that \emph{behaviour} suffices for mind; the room instead uses a \emph{program-executor} to deny that \emph{computation} suffices for \emph{understanding} — a different scenario, a different sufficiency thesis, a different mental feature. It is also distinct from \hyperref[entry:argument-china-nation-absent-qualia]{\textsc{the Chinese nation argument}}, which targets \emph{qualia} rather than intentionality. Both bear on \hyperref[entry:concept-functionalism]{\textsc{Functionalism}}, but along different fault lines.

\begin{plainterms}
Lock an English speaker in a room with a rulebook for shuffling Chinese symbols. He answers Chinese notes so well that native speakers can't tell — but he still has no idea what any of it means; he's just matching shapes. Now: if ``running the program'' were enough to understand Chinese, \emph{he'd} understand it, because he's the one running it. He doesn't. So running the program isn't enough for understanding — which undercuts the idea that having the right software is sufficient for having that part of a mind. The standard rebuttal: don't look at the man, look at the \emph{whole room} — maybe the system as a whole understands, even though the man inside doesn't.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-syntax-insufficient-for-semantics]{\textsc{Syntax insufficient for semantics}} — the conclusion: program-execution is not enough for understanding.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-chinese-room-runs-program]{\textsc{Program execution (Chinese room)}} — the scenario premise: formal symbol-shuffling reproduces the behaviour.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-chinese-room-no-understanding]{\textsc{Chinese room: no understanding}} — the intuition premise: no understanding in the room.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-substrate-independence]{\textsc{Substrate-independence thesis}} — the thesis attacked, at its sufficiency direction.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-chinese-room]{\textsc{Chinese room}} — the underlying thought experiment as a standing concept.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-functionalism]{\textsc{Functionalism}} — the theory of mind in the line of fire.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-lookup-table-objection]{\textsc{Lookup-table objection}} — Block's sibling case against \emph{behavioural} sufficiency.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-china-nation-absent-qualia]{\textsc{Chinese nation (absent qualia)}} — the parallel absent-qualia case, aimed at qualia rather than intentionality.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{argument-chinese-room-derivation}{Chinese room derivation}{Argument \,\textperiodcentered\, after Searle}{Chinese room derivation}{The Chinese Room as a derivation from axioms — syntax is not sufficient for minds}{$\vdash$}
This is the \emph{formal} version of the \hyperref[entry:concept-chinese-room]{\textsc{Chinese Room}} argument — the ``derivation from axioms'' Searle gave in 1990 (see \hyperref[source:source-searle-1990]{\textsc{Searle — Is the Brain's Mind a Computer Program? (1990)}}). Where the \hyperref[entry:argument-chinese-room]{\textsc{intuition-pump version}} runs the thought experiment and reads a verdict off it, this version distils the same point into a short deductive argument, so that the dispute can be located on a single premise rather than on an intuition about a story.

\webheading{The argument}

The inference is \textbf{deductive} (Searle calls it a ``derivation'').

\begin{enumerate}
  \item (see \hyperref[entry:claim-programs-are-syntactic]{\textsc{Programs are syntactic}}) Programs are formal, or syntactic — defined over symbol shapes, not meanings. \emph{(A1)}
  \item (see \hyperref[entry:claim-minds-have-semantics]{\textsc{Minds have semantics}}) Minds have mental contents — semantics, genuine \hyperref[entry:concept-intentionality]{\textsc{aboutness}}. \emph{(A2)}
  \item (see \hyperref[entry:claim-syntax-insufficient-for-semantics]{\textsc{Syntax insufficient for semantics}}) Syntax is neither constitutive of nor sufficient for semantics. \emph{(A3)}
\end{enumerate}

∴ (see \hyperref[entry:claim-programs-not-sufficient-for-minds]{\textsc{Programs not sufficient for minds}}) Implementing a program is neither constitutive of nor sufficient for a mind \emph{(C1)} — the denial of strong AI (see \hyperref[entry:concept-strong-weak-ai]{\textsc{Strong and weak AI}}). This \texttt{attacks} \hyperref[entry:claim-substrate-independence]{\textsc{Substrate-independence thesis}} at its sufficiency direction: realising the right program does not deliver the semantic side of mind.

A fourth axiom extends the result to the positive case:

\begin{enumerate}
  \item (see \hyperref[entry:claim-brains-cause-minds]{\textsc{Brains cause minds}}) Brains cause minds through their specific causal powers. \emph{(A4)}
\end{enumerate}

∴ Any mind-producing system needs causal powers at least equivalent to brains' (C2); program-implementation alone does not guarantee them (C3); so brains do not produce minds \emph{by} running programs (C4) — the seed of \hyperref[entry:position-biological-naturalism]{\textsc{Biological naturalism}}.

\webheading{The weakest premise}

The whole weight rests on \textbf{A3} (see \hyperref[entry:claim-syntax-insufficient-for-semantics]{\textsc{Syntax insufficient for semantics}}), and its status is the crux. The standard charge is that A3 is \textbf{not an independent axiom but a restatement of the conclusion}: the only support Searle offers for ``syntax is not sufficient for semantics'' is the Chinese Room thought experiment, whose moral \emph{is} C1 applied to the AI case — so the deductive form lends rigor without adding \emph{independent} force, and a functionalist who rejects C1 rejects A3 for the very same reason. Every standard objection is, at bottom, a denial of A3: the \hyperref[entry:argument-systems-reply]{\textsc{Systems}}, \hyperref[entry:argument-robot-reply]{\textsc{Robot}}, \hyperref[entry:argument-brain-simulator-reply]{\textsc{Brain Simulator}}, \hyperref[entry:argument-other-minds-reply]{\textsc{Other Minds}}, \hyperref[entry:argument-connectionist-reply]{\textsc{Connectionist}}, and \hyperref[entry:argument-many-mansions-reply]{\textsc{Many Mansions}} replies each contend that syntax \emph{plus something} (a whole system, causal grounding, neural simulation, parallel architecture) does suffice after all. A secondary soft spot is \textbf{A2}: eliminativists about content deny minds have the intrinsic semantics the contrast needs (see \hyperref[entry:concept-intrinsic-derived-intentionality]{\textsc{Intrinsic and derived intentionality}}).

A1 is near-definitional and not in play; A4 is contested only on whether the mind-making feature is biological causal power or abstract organisation.

\webheading{Relation to the intuition-pump version}

This node and \hyperref[entry:argument-chinese-room]{\textsc{Chinese room argument}} are two presentations of one argument, kept distinct because they have different \emph{forms} and \emph{attack surfaces}: the intuition-pump version is a \emph{thought-experiment} counterexample, vulnerable at its bridge and scope (the Systems and Robot replies as \emph{critical questions}); this version is a deductive syllogism, vulnerable at the truth and independence of A3. The replies in this cluster attack the derivation; many of them apply, mutatis mutandis, to the thought-experiment version too.

\begin{plainterms}
Searle's argument, stripped to a syllogism: (1) computer programs are just rules about symbol \emph{shapes}; (2) real minds actually \emph{mean} things; (3) you can't get meaning out of shape-shuffling alone — so (conclusion) running a program can't be enough to make a mind. Add (4) ``brains make minds by their actual biology,'' and you get the further moral that thinking isn't \emph{running code} at all. The argument is airtight \emph{if} step (3) is true — and that's exactly the step everyone fights over. Critics say step (3) is just the conclusion in disguise, and that syntax plus the right extra ingredient (the whole system, a body, simulated neurons) \emph{does} yield understanding.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-programs-not-sufficient-for-minds]{\textsc{Programs not sufficient for minds}} — the conclusion (C1); the denial of strong AI.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-syntax-insufficient-for-semantics]{\textsc{Syntax insufficient for semantics}} — the load-bearing, contested axiom A3.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-programs-are-syntactic]{\textsc{Programs are syntactic}}, \hyperref[entry:claim-minds-have-semantics]{\textsc{Minds have semantics}}, \hyperref[entry:claim-brains-cause-minds]{\textsc{Brains cause minds}} — the other axioms.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-chinese-room]{\textsc{Chinese room argument}} — the intuition-pump presentation of the same argument.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-systems-reply]{\textsc{Systems reply}}, \hyperref[entry:argument-robot-reply]{\textsc{Robot reply}}, \hyperref[entry:argument-brain-simulator-reply]{\textsc{Brain simulator reply}}, \hyperref[entry:argument-other-minds-reply]{\textsc{Other minds reply}}, \hyperref[entry:argument-connectionist-reply]{\textsc{Connectionist reply}}, \hyperref[entry:argument-many-mansions-reply]{\textsc{Many mansions reply}} — the standard objections, each a denial of A3.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-luminous-room]{\textsc{Luminous room}} — the Churchlands' meta-objection that the intuition supporting A3 is itself unreliable.
  \item \hyperref[entry:position-biological-naturalism]{\textsc{Biological naturalism}} — Searle's positive view, grown from A4.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-substrate-independence]{\textsc{Substrate-independence thesis}} — the thesis the derivation attacks.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{claim-chinese-room-no-understanding}{Chinese room: no understanding}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, after Searle}{Chinese room: no understanding}{The person executing the Chinese Room program understands no Chinese}{$\odot$}
This is the verdict the Chinese room is built to elicit: the person manipulating symbols by their shapes, however fluent the output, gains no understanding of Chinese and no \emph{intentionality} (no mental directedness at what the symbols mean). There is syntax but no semantics — intuitively, no Chinese-understanding subject in the \hyperref[entry:concept-chinese-room]{\textsc{room}}.

The verdict concerns \emph{understanding} and \emph{intentionality} (semantics), not behaviour — which the Chinese room scenario stipulates to succeed — and not specifically qualia. It is the contested premise of the argument: the \emph{systems reply} denies it, holding that while the person understands nothing, the whole system (person plus rulebook plus records) does. Defenders of the \emph{robot reply} add that understanding requires a causal embedding the bare room lacks; and functionalists may deny that the ``no understanding'' intuition carries any evidential weight against a system that realises the right organisation.

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-chinese-nation-lacks-qualia]{\textsc{Chinese nation lacks qualia}} because that denies \emph{phenomenal consciousness} to a system realising a mind's organisation, whereas this denies \emph{understanding / intentionality} to a symbol-shuffler — a different mental feature and a different target (semantics versus qualia).

\begin{plainterms}
This is just the gut verdict the Chinese room is meant to produce: the person inside is shuffling symbols by their shape, so even when the answers come out perfect, he doesn't actually \emph{understand} Chinese or mean anything by it. It's a point about understanding and meaning, not about whether the room behaves correctly — it does.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-chinese-room]{\textsc{Chinese room}} — the thought experiment this verdict is drawn from.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-chinese-nation-lacks-qualia]{\textsc{Chinese nation lacks qualia}} — the parallel verdict aimed at qualia rather than understanding.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{claim-apriori-anthropomorphism-circular}{Circularity of a priori anthropomorphism}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, after Dewynter}{Circularity of a priori anthropomorphism}{Assuming anthropomorphic attributes a priori, independent of substrate, yields circular or uninformative conclusions}{$\odot$}
To assume, in advance and independently of substrate, that a system either has or lacks an anthropomorphic attribute — morality, understanding, sentience — and then to run an experiment interpreted through that assumption, is to guarantee an uninformative result. If the assumption is built into how the behaviour is read, the experiment can only return the assumption: confirm that the attribute is ``there'' if you assumed it, or ``absent'' if you assumed it away. The defect is symmetric. It does not depend on the experimenter's prior viewpoint, and it strikes whether the reported outcome is existence or non-existence. Either the reasoning is circular (the conclusion was smuggled into the premises) or it is uninformative (the interpretation was fixed by the chosen representation, not by any measurement).

This is the epistemic premise of \hyperref[entry:argument-aoe2-anthropomorphism]{\textsc{Age of Empires II argument}}. The escape it points to is to fix \emph{explicit measurement criteria} before interpreting behaviour, so that the verdict turns on the evidence rather than on the substrate one happened to look at.

\begin{plainterms}
If you decide ahead of time that a chatbot ``understands'', then read everything it does as understanding, you have not discovered anything — you have just heard your own assumption echoed back. The same trap springs shut if you decide ahead of time that it understands \emph{nothing}. Either way the answer was baked in before the test began. To learn something real you have to say, in advance and out loud, what would count as evidence either way.
\end{plainterms}

\entryhead{claim-humans-closed-to-psychophysical-link}{Closure to the psychophysical link}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, after Mcginn}{Closure to the psychophysical link}{Humans are cognitively closed to the property that explains how the brain produces consciousness}{$\odot$}
The natural property that grounds consciousness — the brain property in virtue of which experience arises (see \hyperref[entry:claim-consciousness-has-natural-explanation]{\textsc{consciousness has a natural explanation}}) — lies outside the range of concepts the human mind can form. The mind–body problem is, on this thesis, \emph{soluble in itself but insoluble by us}: there is an answer, but we are constitutively unable to grasp it. This is the central mysterian claim, due to Colin McGinn (1989; \hyperref[source:source-mcginn-1989]{\textsc{McGinn — Can We Solve the Mind–Body Problem? (1989)}}).

The claim is about \emph{our} cognitive limits, not about metaphysics. It does not say consciousness is non-physical, or that the explanatory gap reflects a real ontological divide; on the contrary, it presupposes that the link is a perfectly natural fact. It is the conclusion of the \hyperref[entry:argument-cognitive-closure]{\textsc{cognitive-closure argument}}.

It would be denied from three directions: optimistic naturalists (the problem is hard but tractable); reductive functionalists and illusionists (there is no residual hard problem for us to be closed to); and dualists, who read the very same gap as ontological rather than cognitive.

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-residue]{\textsc{the claim that felt character outruns functional role}}: that locates the shortfall in the \emph{world} — role does not exhaust the phenomenal; this locates it in \emph{us} — we cannot grasp the natural property that does the work.

\begin{plainterms}
This is the pessimistic core of mysterianism: the brain really does produce consciousness by some ordinary physical means, but our minds may simply lack the concepts to ever understand how — the way a dog can't grasp arithmetic. The point isn't that consciousness is spooky or non-physical; it's that the explanation, though real, sits permanently out of our reach.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-hard-problem]{\textsc{Hard problem}} — the problem this closure thesis is about.
  \item \hyperref[entry:question-hard-problem]{\textsc{Hard problem (the question)}} — the issue it answers, with ``soluble in itself, insoluble by us.''
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-cognitive-closure]{\textsc{Cognitive closure}} — the argument of which this is the conclusion.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-consciousness-has-natural-explanation]{\textsc{Natural explanation of consciousness}} — the naturalist premise it presupposes.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-residue]{\textsc{Phenomenal residue}} — the rival diagnosis that puts the shortfall in the world.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{claim-cogency-requires-accessible-noncircular-warrant}{Cogency needs non-circular warrant}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{Cogency needs non-circular warrant}{Dialectical cogency requires premises with accessible, non-question-begging justification}{$\odot$}
An argument is \emph{cogent} against an opponent — able to rationally move that opponent from premises she can accept to a conclusion she presently rejects — only if its premises admit justification that is \textbf{(i)} in-principle accessible to suitably idealized versions of the parties, and \textbf{(ii)} not such that acquiring it requires already accepting the conclusion. Clause (ii) extends the prohibition on begging the question from the \emph{content} of a premise to the \emph{route by which warrant for it is obtained}: a premise one could come to know only by first granting the conclusion cannot be the lever that moves the argument.

The constraint separates \textbf{soundness} from \textbf{cogency} (probative force). It does not say that an inaccessible or circularly-warranted premise is \emph{false}; the argument may be valid and even sound. It says such an argument cannot do the persuasive work its proponents claim — it cannot convert anyone not already converted. The constraint is deliberately weak, requiring not certainty or decisive certification but merely \emph{accessible, non-circular justification}.

It is the principle behind the standard complaint against the modal ontological argument: granting that ``possibly, a necessary being exists'' entails ``a necessary being exists,'' the argument still fails to compel, because warrant for the possibility premise is not available independently of accepting the theistic conclusion. A theist may hold it sound; no atheist is rationally moved. The same diagnosis is applied to the zombie argument by \hyperref[entry:argument-unwarrantable-zombie-premise]{\textsc{Unwarrantable-premise objection}}.

\textbf{Who would deny it.} A modal epistemologist of a broadly Williamsonian cast may hold that we are entitled to modal premises on the strength of the same fallible capacities that warrant ordinary counterfactual reasoning, with no special demand for accessible non-circular \emph{certification} — in which case (i)–(ii) are too strong. A reader who balks at the constraint can still take the conclusions it licenses \emph{conditionally}: if cogency requires accessible non-circular warrant, then the targeted argument is not cogent.

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-intuition-warrant-tracks-trained-domain]{\textsc{Calibration of intuition}}, which concerns the \emph{calibration} of intuitive verdicts against the cases they were formed on; this claim concerns the \emph{dialectical route} to warrant and the prohibition on obtaining it only by presupposing the conclusion. The two are independent constraints on argument.

\begin{plainterms}
A proof only does its job if the other side can come to believe its starting points \emph{without} first swallowing the very thing you're trying to prove. An argument can be perfectly logical, and even have true premises, yet be useless for \emph{persuading} anyone — if the only way to become sure of a premise is to already agree with the conclusion. That is the classic flaw in the slick argument for God: ``if a necessary being is even possible, it exists.'' The logic is fine; the trouble is that no doubter can get warrant for ``it's possible'' except by already believing in God. This claim says that kind of circular \emph{route to warrant} drains an argument of its force, whatever its truth — and the same charge is pressed against the zombie argument.
\end{plainterms}

\entryhead{argument-cognitive-closure}{Cognitive closure}{Argument \,\textperiodcentered\, after Mcginn}{Cognitive closure}{McGinn's cognitive-closure argument for mysterianism}{$\vdash$}
\emph{Cognitive closure} is Colin McGinn's argument that the \hyperref[entry:concept-hard-problem]{\textsc{hard problem}} of consciousness has a perfectly natural solution which the human mind is nonetheless built in such a way that it can never grasp. There is some property of the brain in virtue of which it gives rise to consciousness; but the only faculties by which we could form a concept of that property — looking inward, or studying the brain from outside — are each unfit to reach it. So the mind–body problem is, in McGinn's phrase, \emph{soluble in itself but insoluble by us}. The resulting position is \hyperref[entry:position-mysterianism]{\textsc{Mysterianism}}.

\webheading{The argument}

The inference is \emph{eliminative} (or \emph{transcendental}): granting that an explanation exists, it rules out every route by which we might come to grasp it.

\begin{enumerate}
  \item There is some natural property — call it \emph{P} — of the brain in virtue of which the brain gives rise to consciousness (see \hyperref[entry:claim-consciousness-has-natural-explanation]{\textsc{a natural explanation exists}}).
  \item A mind can be \emph{constitutively closed} to certain properties — barred in principle, not merely currently ignorant, from forming the relevant concepts (see \hyperref[entry:claim-cognitive-closure-exists]{\textsc{minds can be closed to some concepts}}).
  \item We could form the concept of \emph{P} in only one of two ways: by \emph{introspection}, or by \emph{outer perception} of the brain and inference from it. (This exhaustive-routes assumption is a premise of the argument's own.)
  \item But introspection presents experience, not its neural basis; and perception of the brain yields only spatial and physical concepts, which cannot capture the subjective — this is the \hyperref[entry:argument-explanatory-gap]{\textsc{explanatory gap}}. So neither route can reach \emph{P}.
\end{enumerate}

∴ \emph{P} is cognitively closed to us: the mind–body problem is real and has a natural answer, yet that answer lies permanently beyond human understanding (see \hyperref[entry:claim-humans-closed-to-psychophysical-link]{\textsc{we are closed to the psychophysical link}}). This is the support for \hyperref[entry:position-mysterianism]{\textsc{Mysterianism}}.

\webheading{The weak point}

The argument's most disputed step is the pair (3)–(4): the claim that introspection and outer perception are the \emph{only} routes to \emph{P}, and that both are sealed off. Three replies press there. First, science routinely forms \emph{theoretical} concepts that are neither introspected nor perceived — electric charge, the curvature of spacetime — by abduction and outright conceptual innovation, so the two-route dichotomy looks too narrow and McGinn under-rates our capacity to create new concepts. Second, we cannot reliably establish our own representational limits \emph{in advance}, so a confident verdict of closure is itself suspect. Third, if the explanatory gap is \emph{merely} a fact about cognitive closure, that tells against also reading it as a metaphysical residue: mysterianism and the residue view (see \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-residue]{\textsc{that experience outruns functional role}}) cannot both take the gap at face value.

\webheading{How it differs from neighbouring gap arguments}

All of these start from the same explanatory gap but draw opposite morals from it. The \hyperref[entry:argument-explanatory-gap]{\textsc{explanatory-gap argument}} treats the gap as evidence about \emph{metaphysics} — that the phenomenal is not exhausted by functional role. Cognitive closure treats the very same gap as evidence about \emph{our cognitive limits}: the problem is real and natural, merely humanly unsolvable. It differs again from the \hyperref[entry:argument-decomposition-indeterminacy]{\textsc{decomposition-indeterminacy objection}} and the \hyperref[entry:argument-structure-and-dynamics]{\textsc{structure-and-dynamics argument}}, which contend that function \emph{omits} or \emph{fails to fix} the phenomenal; mysterianism instead grants that a natural answer exists and denies only our access to it.

\begin{plainterms}
The argument goes: (1) there's some real, physical reason the brain produces consciousness; (2) minds can be permanently unable to understand certain things; (3) the only ways we could ever get hold of that reason are by looking inward (introspection) or by studying the brain from outside; (4) but looking inward shows you the feeling, not the wiring, and studying the brain from outside only ever gives you physical/spatial facts that never add up to a feeling. So neither route can deliver the answer — meaning it's locked away from us for good. The main pushback: science invents concepts all the time that we neither feel nor see directly (like ``electron''), so maybe the two routes aren't the only ones.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:position-mysterianism]{\textsc{Mysterianism}} — the view this argument supports: the mind–body problem has a natural solution we are constitutionally unable to reach.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-hard-problem]{\textsc{Hard problem}} — the problem McGinn declares humanly insoluble.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-explanatory-gap]{\textsc{Explanatory-gap argument}} — the same gap read as metaphysical evidence rather than as a limit on our concepts.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-residue]{\textsc{Phenomenal residue}} — the residue reading the third reply says cannot coexist with a merely-cognitive diagnosis of the gap.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-decomposition-indeterminacy]{\textsc{Decomposition-indeterminacy objection}} and \hyperref[entry:argument-structure-and-dynamics]{\textsc{Structure-and-dynamics argument}} — neighbouring gap arguments that fault function itself, where mysterianism faults only our access.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-consciousness-has-natural-explanation]{\textsc{Natural explanation of consciousness}}, \hyperref[entry:claim-cognitive-closure-exists]{\textsc{Possibility of cognitive closure}}, \hyperref[entry:claim-humans-closed-to-psychophysical-link]{\textsc{Closure to the psychophysical link}} — the argument's two premises and its conclusion.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{position-coherentism}{Coherentism}{Position}{Coherentism}{}{$\bigstar$}
\emph{Coherentism} holds that beliefs are justified by how well they \emph{hang together} — by mutual support within the believer's whole system — and not by resting on a privileged foundation. There are no \hyperref[entry:concept-basic-belief]{\textsc{basic beliefs}}; justification is a holistic property of the web, every belief supported by its fit with the others (see \hyperref[entry:claim-justification-is-mutual-support]{\textsc{Justification as coherence}}). Faced with \hyperref[entry:concept-munchhausen-trilemma]{\textsc{the Münchhausen trilemma}}, coherentism takes the \emph{circularity} horn and argues the circle is not vicious: a large, system-wide pattern of mutual support is a different thing from a small question-begging loop. The governing image is Neurath's boat — we rebuild the ship plank by plank while afloat, with no dry dock to stand on.

\webheading{How it differs from neighbouring views}

Against \hyperref[entry:position-foundationalism]{\textsc{Foundationalism}}, it denies any non-inferentially justified bedrock and replaces the upward flow of support with a web in which justification runs in all directions. Against \hyperref[entry:position-infinitism]{\textsc{Infinitism}}, it \emph{closes} the structure into a system of mutual support rather than leaving an open, non-repeating chain. Its standing difficulty is the \textbf{isolation (or input) objection}: a perfectly coherent system of beliefs could still be a self-consistent fiction, sealed off from the world — so coherence alone seems not to guarantee contact with reality. Replies typically add a constraint that the system answer to observational input, which critics say smuggles a foundational element back in.

\begin{plainterms}
Coherentism says beliefs hold each other up like the logs of a raft, not like a house on a foundation. A belief is justified when it fits with everything else you think — no special bedrock beliefs required, because the support runs every which way. The worry critics keep pressing: a story can be perfectly self-consistent and still be a fantasy. So coherence has to be doing more than just internal tidiness for this to track the truth.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-justification-is-mutual-support]{\textsc{Justification as coherence}} — the position's defining commitment.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-some-beliefs-are-basic]{\textsc{There are basic beliefs}} — the foundationalist thesis it rejects.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-munchhausen-trilemma]{\textsc{Münchhausen trilemma}} — the regress problem it answers by taking the circularity horn.
  \item \hyperref[entry:position-foundationalism]{\textsc{Foundationalism}}, \hyperref[entry:position-infinitism]{\textsc{Infinitism}} — the rival answers to the same trilemma.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{claim-phenomenal-intensions-coincide}{Coincident phenomenal intensions}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, after Chalmers}{Coincident phenomenal intensions}{For phenomenal concepts, the two intensions coincide}{$\odot$}
For a phenomenal concept — the concept of what it is like to undergo an experience, such as the concept of \emph{the feeling of pain} or \emph{phenomenal red} — the \hyperref[entry:concept-primary-intension]{\textsc{Primary intension}} and the \hyperref[entry:concept-secondary-intension]{\textsc{Secondary intension}} are one and the same function. Unlike ``water,'' whose reference is fixed by a contingent role (the watery stuff) that something other than H₂O could have filled, a phenomenal concept fixes its reference directly on the felt quality itself, with no reference-fixing description standing between the concept and its object. There is therefore no wedge between how the experience \emph{presents} and what it \emph{is}: for a feeling, the appearance is the reality.

The contrast is the whole point. With natural-kind terms the two intensions diverge, and that gap is where a Kripkean necessity can be both necessary and knowable only a posteriori (see \hyperref[entry:claim-some-necessities-knowable-only-a-posteriori]{\textsc{A-posteriori necessities}}); the two-dimensionalist exploits the gap to recast apparent impossibilities as \hyperref[entry:claim-aposteriori-necessities-are-misdescribed-possibilities]{\textsc{A-posteriori necessities as misdescribed possibilities}}. A phenomenal concept offers no such gap. ``Watery stuff that is not H₂O'' describes a genuine possibility misnamed ``water,'' but ``something that feels exactly like pain yet is not pain'' describes nothing at all, because feeling-like-pain is not a fallible mode of presentation of pain — it simply is the pain (see \hyperref[entry:concept-phenomenal-consciousness]{\textsc{Phenomenal consciousness}}).

\webheading{Scope and bearing}

The claim is a thesis in the semantics of phenomenal concepts, not directly a claim about what consciousness is. Its work is to remove an escape route. Chalmers's \hyperref[entry:argument-modal-rationalism]{\textsc{Modal rationalism}} licenses the step from ideal conceivability to \emph{primary} possibility; the question is whether primary possibility carries through to full metaphysical possibility in the phenomenal case. Where primary and secondary intensions coincide, there is no separate secondary necessity in which a metaphysical impossibility could hide — so primary possibility \emph{is} metaphysical possibility. Applied to the \hyperref[entry:concept-philosophical-zombie]{\textsc{Philosophical zombie}}, this closes the gap the type-B physicalist needs: if the zombie is conceivable it is genuinely possible, and physicalism is false (see \hyperref[entry:argument-zombie-conceivability]{\textsc{Zombie argument}}).

\webheading{Who would deny it}

The type-B physicalist (see \hyperref[entry:concept-materialism-types]{\textsc{Type-A / Type-B / Type-C materialism}}) — the position the claim is aimed at. Type-B physicalism holds that ``pain is such-and-such brain state'' is a \emph{strong} a posteriori necessity, with a genuine primary/secondary gap supplied by a special \emph{phenomenal concept} that refers to a physical state under a distinctive mode of presentation. On that view there is, after all, an appearance/reality distinction for experience at the level of \emph{concepts}, the zombie is conceivable but impossible, and the present claim is exactly what fails. The dispute thus narrows to whether phenomenal concepts can carry a reference-fixing mode of presentation distinct from their referent — the hinge on which the entire conceivability argument turns.

\begin{plainterms}
For most words there's a difference between how something seems and what it really is: the stuff in the rivers \emph{seems} like ``water'' but really \emph{is} H₂O, and that gap is why ``water is H₂O'' can be a surprise we had to discover. This claim says feelings are different. With a feeling — pain, or the look of red — there's no gap between the seeming and the being: a state that felt \emph{exactly} like pain in every respect would just \emph{be} pain; there's nothing more to a feeling than how it feels. Why it matters: the physicalist's best reply to the \hyperref[entry:concept-philosophical-zombie]{\textsc{Philosophical zombie}} (a creature physically just like you but with no inner feel) is to say ``sure, you can imagine it, but imagining isn't possibility — just as you can 'imagine' water that isn't H₂O.'' This claim blocks that reply for consciousness: because feelings have no seeming/being gap, imagining a zombie really does show a zombie is possible — and that would mean consciousness is something over and above the physical.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-primary-intension]{\textsc{Primary intension}} — the epistemic, reference-fixing dimension of meaning; for phenomenal concepts it has nothing to fix onto but the feel itself.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-secondary-intension]{\textsc{Secondary intension}} — the rigid, actual-world-fixed dimension; the claim is that for feelings it does not diverge from the primary intension.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-aposteriori-necessities-are-misdescribed-possibilities]{\textsc{A-posteriori necessities as misdescribed possibilities}} — the water-style rescue the claim says is unavailable here, since there is no primary/secondary gap to misdescribe.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-modal-rationalism]{\textsc{Modal rationalism}} — supplies the conceivability-to-primary-possibility step that this claim then extends, in the phenomenal case, all the way to metaphysical possibility.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-zombie-conceivability]{\textsc{Zombie argument}} — the argument whose final move depends on this claim: with the intensions coincident, the zombie's conceivability yields its genuine possibility.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-materialism-types]{\textsc{Type-A / Type-B / Type-C materialism}} — type-B physicalism, the view that denies this claim by positing a phenomenal-concept mode of presentation distinct from its referent.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-phenomenal-consciousness]{\textsc{Phenomenal consciousness}} — what phenomenal concepts are concepts \emph{of}; the ``appearance is reality'' point is a point about felt experience.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{argument-combination-problem}{Combination problem}{Argument \,\textperiodcentered\, after James}{Combination problem}{The combination problem — micro-subjects cannot compose a macro-subject}{$\vdash$}
The \emph{combination problem} is the standing objection to \hyperref[entry:concept-panpsychism]{\textsc{Panpsychism}}, severe enough that many regard it as the view's make-or-break test. Constitutive panpsychism holds that a macro-subject like you is \emph{constituted by} the experiential properties of your fundamental micro-parts. The objection denies that this constitution is available: a crowd of tiny subjects, each with its own private point of view, cannot fuse into one further subject whose experience contains theirs. The problem originates with William James (see \hyperref[source:source-james-1890]{\textsc{1890}}) and is sharpened into its hardest ``subject-summing'' form by Sam Coleman (see \hyperref[source:source-coleman-2014]{\textsc{2014}}); David Chalmers (see \hyperref[source:source-chalmers-2017]{\textsc{2017}}) anatomises its parts.

\webheading{The argument}

The inference is deductive — an objection, so it \emph{attacks} the constitutive thesis rather than concluding a positive claim.

\begin{enumerate}
  \item (see \hyperref[entry:claim-micro-subjects-do-not-sum]{\textsc{Micro-subjects do not sum}}) Distinct micro-subjects — each with its own private point of view and self-contained phenomenal field — do not, merely by being arranged or related, compose a further single subject whose experience contains theirs.
  \item Constitutive panpsychism requires exactly such composition: your unified experience is supposed to be made up of the experiences of your fundamental parts.
\end{enumerate}

∴ The constitution the view needs is unavailable, so it cannot deliver the single, unified macro-subject we actually find. This \emph{attacks} \hyperref[entry:claim-consciousness-fundamental-ubiquitous]{\textsc{Fundamental ubiquitous consciousness}} at its constitutive joint.

The objection also generalises a deeper worry. Panpsychism was offered to \emph{avoid} getting experience from the non-experiential, yet it now owes an account of getting \emph{macro}-experience from \emph{micro}-experience — so the very explanatory burden the view promised to discharge reappears here, no easier than before. Chalmers distinguishes three faces of the problem: combining micro-\emph{subjects} into a subject, micro-\emph{qualities} into a unified quality-field, and micro-\emph{structure} into the structure of a macro-experience; the subject-combination face is the most resistant.

\webheading{The weak point}

The objection turns on premise (1). Constitutive panpsychists reply with accounts of \emph{phenomenal bonding} or fusion on which subjects do combine after all. Alternatively they retreat to \emph{panqualityism}, which denies that there are micro-\emph{subjects} to combine in the first place — only unexperienced qualities — and pays for the move with a residual explanatory gap at the point where subjecthood has to be added back. Which of these escapes succeeds is the live question on which the fate of constitutive panpsychism turns.

\begin{plainterms}
Panpsychism's appeal is that every tiny speck of matter carries a flicker of experience, so your mind is built up out of those flickers. The combination problem is the bill for that appeal. Your experience right now is \emph{one} unified thing — a single point of view taking in this whole sentence at once. How do countless separate little experiences, each locked inside its own tiny perspective, merge into that one? William James argued they simply cannot: a hundred little feelings do not add up to one big feeling that swallows them — each stays its own private whole. If he is right, the panpsychist's ``build a mind out of mini-minds'' recipe has no step that actually does the building. Worse, it was supposed to spare us the mystery of making feeling out of the feelingless, but now it owes the matching mystery of making \emph{one} feeler out of \emph{many} — which looks just as hard.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-micro-subjects-do-not-sum]{\textsc{Micro-subjects do not sum}} — the premise that drives the objection: subjects do not sum.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-consciousness-fundamental-ubiquitous]{\textsc{Fundamental ubiquitous consciousness}} — the constitutive thesis this objection attacks.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-panpsychism]{\textsc{Panpsychism}} — the view under attack, and where the combination problem sits among its varieties.
  \item \hyperref[entry:position-cosmopsychism]{\textsc{Cosmopsychism}} — the top-down cousin, which trades this problem for its mirror image, the \emph{decombination} problem.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{claim-combination-dualizes-to-composition-of-will}{Combination–composition duality}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{Combination–composition duality}{The combination problem for panpsychism dualizes to the composition problem for agency}{$\odot$}
The \hyperref[entry:argument-combination-problem]{\textsc{combination problem}} asks how micro-subjects could compose a macro-subject; its arrow-reversed image asks how micro-\emph{tendencies} compose a unified macro-agent. The dual of \hyperref[entry:concept-panpsychism]{\textsc{Panpsychism}} is a pan-agentialism — proto-agency everywhere, composing upward — and both programs stall at the same juncture: aggregation of the primitive does not obviously yield the unified higher thing. A special case makes the parallel exact: micro \emph{indeterminacies} do not sum to macro-agency — the standard objection to quantum libertarianism, that amplified chance is randomness, not authorship — just as micro-experiences do not sum to a subject (see \hyperref[entry:claim-micro-subjects-do-not-sum]{\textsc{Micro-subjects do not sum}}).

The arrows here are arrows of \emph{constitution} rather than causation — many micro-items composing one macro-item — and at that scale both problems are fan-ins. What the mirror reverses is not the composition arrows themselves but the pole of mind the composite occupies: a composed experiencer at the terminus of perception, a composed agent at the source of action, each composition problem inheriting its shape from the duality of the things composed.

The claim asserts the correspondence of the two composition problems, not a verdict on either program. It is a dictionary entry of the \hyperref[entry:concept-consciousness-freewill-duality]{\textsc{consciousness–free-will duality}}, counted as evidence by \hyperref[entry:argument-consciousness-freewill-duality]{\textsc{Duality argument}} because the free-will side's composition problem already exists in that literature under its own name (the luck objection to event-causal libertarianism is its sharpest edge).

\begin{plainterms}
One theory says consciousness is everywhere in tiny amounts, and big minds are built from little ones — but no one can say how a pile of tiny experiences adds up to \emph{you}. Flip it to the action side: suppose tiny pushes of randomness or proto-choice are everywhere — no one can say how those add up to a person genuinely \emph{deciding}. In particular, sprinkling quantum chance into the brain gives you dice, not a chooser, exactly as a heap of micro-feelings gives you a crowd, not a self (that older point is \hyperref[entry:claim-micro-subjects-do-not-sum]{\textsc{Micro-subjects do not sum}}).
\end{plainterms}

\entryhead{argument-lookup-table-combinatorial-impossibility}{Combinatorial-impossibility reply}{Argument \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{Combinatorial-impossibility reply}{The combinatorial-impossibility reply — a finite lookup table cannot match an open-ended mind's behaviour}{$\vdash$}
The \emph{combinatorial-impossibility reply} argues that a finite lookup table cannot, even in principle, match the behaviour of an open-ended mind, and so cannot serve as a counterexample to the claim that behaviour suffices for mind. It is a reply to \hyperref[entry:argument-lookup-table-objection]{\textsc{the lookup-table objection}}, which uses a giant stored answer-table to argue that a system could produce a mind's behaviour with ``nobody home''.

\webheading{The argument}

The inference is at its core deductive — a diagonal or cardinality argument, that a fixed finite domain cannot cover an unbounded input space — turning on one defeasible empirical premise (2).

\begin{enumerate}
  \item For any finite lookup table there exist finite inputs outside its stored domain; to such an input it can at most respond by ignoring the part that exceeds its keys (see \hyperref[entry:claim-finite-table-misses-inputs]{\textsc{Finite table misses inputs}}).
  \item A genuine mind responds appropriately to such inputs rather than discarding their excess (see \hyperref[entry:claim-mind-handles-unbounded-inputs]{\textsc{Open-ended responsiveness of minds}}).
\end{enumerate}

∴ For every finite table there is an input on which it diverges from the mind; so no finite table reproduces a mind's full behavioural profile (see \hyperref[entry:claim-lookup-table-combinatorially-inadequate]{\textsc{Combinatorially inadequate table}}).

\webheading{What it attacks}

The reply both responds to and attacks \hyperref[entry:argument-lookup-table-objection]{\textsc{the lookup-table objection}} by denying its first premise, \hyperref[entry:claim-lookup-table-possible]{\textsc{that a finite table can match the whole behavioural profile}}. If a finite table cannot even produce the behaviour, the blockhead is not a case of ``behaviour without mind''; it is a system that fails to match the behaviour in the first place. Dialectically this favours the substrate-independence side, blunting the lookup-table objection to \hyperref[entry:argument-behavioral-parity]{\textsc{behavioural parity}} at its first premise. It leaves untouched the Chinese-Nation objection (see \hyperref[entry:argument-china-nation-absent-qualia]{\textsc{Chinese nation (absent qualia)}}), which supplies the organisation and so matches more than bare input–output.

\webheading{Weakest premise}

The vulnerable step is premise (2), \hyperref[entry:claim-mind-handles-unbounded-inputs]{\textsc{Open-ended responsiveness of minds}}.
