Block's ``Blockhead'' (see \hyperref[source:source-block]{\textsc{Block — Psychologism and Behaviorism (1981)}}) is built to escape exactly this: stipulate a table covering all inputs up to a length exceeding any the subject meets in a lifetime, and note that a finite mind also truncates inputs past its own capacity — so the divergence asserted in premise (2) may never arise within the bounded domain that matters. The conclusion is therefore filed \texttt{contested}. That escape is \hyperref[entry:argument-bounded-table-rejoinder]{\textsc{the bounded-table rejoinder}}, itself answered on physical grounds by \hyperref[entry:argument-lookup-table-physical-impossibility]{\textsc{the cosmic-size reply}} (the bounded table cannot be built) and on kind grounds by \hyperref[entry:argument-storage-not-stable-kind]{\textsc{Pure-storage instability}} (a realisable table is not ``mere storage'').

\webheading{Distinct from neighbouring moves}

It differs from \hyperref[entry:argument-lookup-table-objection]{\textsc{the lookup-table objection}} itself: that move \emph{uses} the lookup table to attack behavioural sufficiency (behaviour ⇒ mind), whereas this attacks that move, denying the lookup table reproduces the behaviour at all — opposite dialectical direction, different conclusion. It also differs from \hyperref[entry:argument-organisational-grain-dissolves-dilemma]{\textsc{Organisational-grain middle}}, which excludes the blockhead by its lack of internal \emph{organisation}; this excludes it by its inability to match the \emph{input–output profile} over an unbounded input space, a different ground of exclusion.

\begin{plainterms}
This pushes back on the cheat-sheet machine objection (see \hyperref[entry:argument-lookup-table-objection]{\textsc{the lookup-table objection}}). That whole worry assumed you \emph{could} store a ready-made answer for every possible input. But a real mind faces an open-ended world — there is no end to the fresh sentences, situations, and questions it can sensibly deal with. Any finite stored table, however gigantic, eventually meets an input nobody anticipated, and then all it can do is choke or ignore the unfamiliar part. A genuine mind does not choke; it responds sensibly to the new thing. So no finite lookup table can really keep pace with a mind across the board — which means the ``behaviour with nobody home'' machine cannot even generate all the behaviour to begin with. The soft spot: maybe you only ever need to cover the inputs a being actually meets in one finite lifetime, and a real mind, too, gives out on inputs past \emph{its} limits — which is the escape route the next arguments go after.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-lookup-table-mind]{\textsc{Lookup-table mind}} — the stored-answer machine (``Blockhead'') this reply is about.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-lookup-table-objection]{\textsc{Lookup-table objection}} — the move this reply answers, by denying its first premise.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-lookup-table-combinatorially-inadequate]{\textsc{Combinatorially inadequate table}} — the conclusion this argument establishes.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-finite-table-misses-inputs]{\textsc{Finite table misses inputs}} and \hyperref[entry:claim-mind-handles-unbounded-inputs]{\textsc{Open-ended responsiveness of minds}} — its two premises.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-lookup-table-physical-impossibility]{\textsc{Cosmic-size reply}} — the physical follow-up that closes this argument's bounded-table escape.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-bounded-table-rejoinder]{\textsc{Bounded-table rejoinder}} — Block's escape at premise (2), why this conclusion is contested.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{claim-lookup-table-combinatorially-inadequate}{Combinatorially inadequate table}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, contested \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{Combinatorially inadequate table}{No finite lookup table can reproduce the full behavioural profile of a genuine mind}{$\odot$}
A \emph{finite lookup table} — a fixed list pairing each anticipated input with a stored response — cannot match everything a real mind does. Because any such table omits some possible inputs while a mind still answers those inputs sensibly, for every finite table there is an input on which the two diverge. The table is therefore \emph{combinatorially} inadequate, not merely impractically large: its shortfall is one of range, not size.

The reasoning rests on two facts. Any finite table misses some inputs (see \hyperref[entry:claim-finite-table-misses-inputs]{\textsc{Finite table misses inputs}}), and a genuine mind responds appropriately to those very inputs rather than discarding their excess (see \hyperref[entry:claim-mind-handles-unbounded-inputs]{\textsc{Open-ended responsiveness of minds}}). Hence no finite table reproduces a mind's \emph{full} behavioural profile.

\webheading{Scope}

The claim concerns \emph{pure finite lookup} only. It does not deny that some other, non-table system might match a mind, nor that a table could match a mind over a bounded fragment of its inputs — only that finite lookup cannot match the whole open-ended profile. It is effectively the negation of \hyperref[entry:claim-lookup-table-possible]{\textsc{the claim that a finite table can reproduce the profile}}.

\webheading{Why it is contested}

The disputed step is the second supporting claim, \hyperref[entry:claim-mind-handles-unbounded-inputs]{\textsc{Open-ended responsiveness of minds}}. On Block's lifetime-bounded construction (see \hyperref[source:source-block]{\textsc{Block — Psychologism and Behaviorism (1981)}}) the table need only cover inputs up to a length the subject will actually receive, and a finite mind would itself truncate longer inputs — so the asserted divergence may never arise in the domain that matters. The attacking move is \hyperref[entry:argument-bounded-table-rejoinder]{\textsc{the bounded-table rejoinder}} (see \hyperref[source:source-block]{\textsc{Block — Psychologism and Behaviorism (1981)}}); this claim's \texttt{contested} status records that dispute. The bounded table is in turn answered by \hyperref[entry:argument-lookup-table-physical-impossibility]{\textsc{the cosmic-size reply}} (it cannot be built) and by \hyperref[entry:argument-storage-not-stable-kind]{\textsc{Pure-storage instability}} (a realisable table is not ``mere storage'').

A defender of Blockhead, that is, of pure input–output functionalism, would deny the claim. Note, though, that it \emph{helps} organisational-role functionalism: if a lookup table cannot even match behaviour, then \hyperref[entry:argument-lookup-table-objection]{\textsc{the lookup-table objection}} fails at its first premise.

It should be distinguished from \hyperref[entry:claim-lookup-table-lacks-mind]{\textsc{Mindlessness of pure lookup}}, which grants the table the behaviour but denies it a mind; the present, stronger and prior claim denies the table even has the behaviour.

\begin{plainterms}
No finite stored answer-table, however enormous, can match everything a real mind does — because a mind handles an open-ended stream of fresh inputs, and any finite table eventually hits one it has no entry for, at which point the two part ways. So the cheat-sheet is not merely impractically big; it cannot reproduce the \emph{whole} range of a mind's behaviour at all. (Disputed: maybe you only ever need to cover the inputs of a single lifetime — the bounded-table reply, \hyperref[entry:argument-bounded-table-rejoinder]{\textsc{Bounded-table rejoinder}}.)
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-lookup-table-mind]{\textsc{Lookup-table mind}} — the stored-answer machine this claim says falls short.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-lookup-table-possible]{\textsc{Lookup-table possibility}} — the claim this one is effectively the negation of.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-finite-table-misses-inputs]{\textsc{Finite table misses inputs}} and \hyperref[entry:claim-mind-handles-unbounded-inputs]{\textsc{Open-ended responsiveness of minds}} — the two facts the verdict rests on.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-lookup-table-lacks-mind]{\textsc{Mindlessness of pure lookup}} — the weaker sibling, granting behaviour but denying mind.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-bounded-table-rejoinder]{\textsc{Bounded-table rejoinder}} — Block's reply at the contested premise.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{position-commonsense-realism}{Common-sense realism}{Position}{Common-sense realism}{}{$\bigstar$}
\emph{Common-sense realism} holds that the existence of a mind-independent external world (see \hyperref[entry:claim-external-world-exists]{\textsc{The external world exists}}) is a \emph{first principle} — something we are entitled to take as known without proof — and that the burden of argument lies with the skeptic, not the plain believer. The view is Thomas Reid's (see \hyperref[source:source-reid-1785]{\textsc{Reid — Essays on the Intellectual Powers of Man (1785)}}), framed against Humean and Cartesian skepticism: the deliverances of our constitution (that there are external objects, other minds, a remembered past) are principles we cannot help believing and have every right to trust; a philosophical argument that concludes otherwise is more likely to have a false premise than to overturn what every sane person knows. G. E. Moore's \hyperref[entry:argument-moore-proof-external-world]{\textsc{``here is a hand''}} is the position's modern emblem.

\webheading{How it differs from neighbouring views}

Common-sense realism is, in spirit, a foundationalism of \emph{first principles}: the world's existence is a non-inferentially warranted starting point, akin to a \hyperref[entry:concept-basic-belief]{\textsc{basic belief}}, not a conclusion. But it adds a distinctive \emph{dialectical} claim — that the skeptic bears the burden of proof, so that a clash between a skeptical argument and a common-sense certainty is resolved \emph{against} the argument (the \hyperref[entry:argument-moore-proof-external-world]{\textsc{Moorean shift}}). It is close to, yet distinct from, \hyperref[entry:position-hinge-epistemology]{\textsc{Hinge epistemology}}: the Reidian first principle is held as a \emph{known truth}, a deliverance of our cognitive constitution, whereas a Wittgensteinian \hyperref[entry:concept-hinge-proposition]{\textsc{hinge}} is arational — neither justified nor doubted, but the framework presupposed by justifying. Where \hyperref[entry:position-fallibilism]{\textsc{Fallibilism}} would deny that the first principle is \emph{certain}, common-sense realism treats it as a fixed point against which doubts are measured.

\begin{plainterms}
Common-sense realism says: of course there's a real world — that's not a conclusion you argue \emph{to}, it's a starting point you argue \emph{from}. If some philosophical chain of reasoning ends in ``so maybe nothing outside your mind exists,'' the sensible response is that one of its premises must be wrong, because you're far surer of the world than of any clever premise. Reid said our basic convictions are gifts of our nature we're entitled to trust; Moore just held up a hand. The burden, on this view, is on the doubter.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-external-world-exists]{\textsc{The external world exists}} — the first principle the position is committed to.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-moore-proof-external-world]{\textsc{Moore's proof}} — its modern emblem and the Moorean burden-shift.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-basic-belief]{\textsc{Basic belief}} — the non-inferential warrant a first principle resembles.
  \item \hyperref[entry:position-hinge-epistemology]{\textsc{Hinge epistemology}} — the neighbour that treats the same certainty as arational framework, not known truth.
  \item \hyperref[entry:position-foundationalism]{\textsc{Foundationalism}} — the general structure common-sense realism instances.
  \item \hyperref[source:source-reid-1785]{\textsc{Reid — Essays on the Intellectual Powers of Man (1785)}} — the source of the philosophy of common sense.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{position-compatibilism}{Compatibilism}{Position}{Compatibilism}{}{$\bigstar$}
\emph{Compatibilism} is the view that free will and determinism can both be true: an action is free when it comes about in the right way — flowing from the agent's own deliberation, values, and responsiveness to reasons, without compulsion, coercion, or manipulation — regardless of whether the laws of nature and the past fixed that it would occur (see \hyperref[entry:claim-freedom-compatible-with-determinism]{\textsc{Compatibility of freedom and determinism}}). The freedom worth wanting, on this view, is a matter of \emph{how you act}, not of whether the universe left the future open.

The tradition runs from Hobbes and Hume, who located freedom in the absence of external impediment, to contemporary actual-sequence accounts on which what matters is \emph{guidance control} — ownership of a reasons-responsive mechanism — rather than \emph{regulative control}, the power to do otherwise (Fischer \& Ravizza, \hyperref[source:source-fischer-ravizza-1998]{\textsc{1998}}). \hyperref[entry:concept-frankfurt-cases]{\textsc{Frankfurt cases}} supply the classic pressure toward that reorientation: agents seem fully responsible even when, unbeknownst to them, no alternative was open. What distinguishes compatibilism from its neighbors is what it denies: against \hyperref[entry:position-libertarianism]{\textsc{Libertarianism (free will)}} it denies that freedom needs indeterministic openness or \hyperref[entry:concept-agent-causation]{\textsc{agent causation}}; against \hyperref[entry:position-hard-determinism]{\textsc{hard determinism}} it denies that determinism would unseat freedom in the first place. Within the \hyperref[entry:concept-consciousness-freewill-duality]{\textsc{consciousness–free-will duality}}, compatibilism occupies the deflationary pole of the free-will debate — the mirror image of deflationism about phenomenal consciousness (see \hyperref[entry:claim-deflationism-entails-compatibilism]{\textsc{Deflation–compatibilism parity}}).

\begin{plainterms}
Suppose a complete physics of your brain could have predicted your choice of career. Does that mean you never really chose? Compatibilists say no: what makes a choice yours is that \emph{you} weighed the reasons and nothing forced your hand — not that the outcome was cosmically unfixed. Freedom is about acting from your own mind, not about escaping cause and effect.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-freedom-compatible-with-determinism]{\textsc{Compatibility of freedom and determinism}} — the defining commitment.
  \item \hyperref[entry:position-libertarianism]{\textsc{Libertarianism (free will)}} and \hyperref[entry:position-hard-determinism]{\textsc{Hard determinism}} — the two incompatibilist rivals, agreeing against compatibilism that determinism would matter.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-frankfurt-cases]{\textsc{Frankfurt cases}} — the thought experiments that motivate actual-sequence versions.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-deflationism-entails-compatibilism]{\textsc{Deflation–compatibilism parity}} — the duality-dictionary link binding this view to deflationism about consciousness.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{claim-freedom-compatible-with-determinism}{Compatibility of freedom and determinism}{Claim}{Compatibility of freedom and determinism}{Acting freely is compatible with the truth of determinism}{$\odot$}
Even if the laws of nature and the state of the world long before your birth jointly fix everything you will ever do, you can still act freely — because freedom, properly understood, is a matter of \emph{how} an action comes about, not of whether the future was open. An action is free when it flows from the agent's own deliberation, values, and reasons-responsiveness, unimpeded by compulsion, coercion, or manipulation; determinism threatens none of that.

This is the defining commitment of \hyperref[entry:position-compatibilism]{\textsc{Compatibilism}}. It asserts a compatibility, not the truth of determinism, and it takes no stand on whether any agent is in fact free. It is denied by both \hyperref[entry:position-libertarianism]{\textsc{libertarians}} and \hyperref[entry:position-hard-determinism]{\textsc{hard determinists}}, who agree with each other that freedom requires what determinism excludes (see \hyperref[entry:claim-alternatives-genuinely-open]{\textsc{Genuine alternatives}}) and disagree only about whether we have it.

\entryhead{claim-full-self-access-achievable}{Complete internal self-access}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, contested}{Complete internal self-access}{A finite system can be given complete internal access to its own current state}{$\odot$}
A sufficiently reflective finite system can, in principle, be engineered to have complete, transparent access to \emph{all} of its own current state from within. On this optimistic view the familiar limits on introspection are contingent and removable, not structural — the position denied by \hyperref[entry:claim-inside-vantage-access-limited]{\textsc{the limited-inside-vantage claim}}.

\webheading{Scope}

The substantive reading is \emph{internal} and \emph{complete}. It is trivially true if ``access'' is allowed to be \emph{external} instrumentation — but an external instrument is not part of the system whose state is represented, so that reading abandons the self-access claim.

\webheading{Why contested}

The claim is attacked on capacity grounds by \hyperref[entry:argument-finite-self-model-incomplete]{\textsc{Finite self-model incompleteness}}: a proper part of a finite state cannot losslessly encode the whole. Anyone holding that bound denies it.

\webheading{Distinct from neighbours}

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-llm-functional-introspection]{\textsc{LLM functional introspection}}: that asserts \emph{limited, unreliable} self-access exists; this asserts that \emph{complete} self-access is achievable — the strong thesis the impossibility argument targets.

\begin{plainterms}
The optimistic bet: with clever enough engineering, a system could be built to see everything about its own current inner state, perfectly and all at once — so the reason we (and today's AIs) can't fully introspect would be just a fixable shortcoming, not a hard wall. The opposing camp says there is a hard wall, provable by a counting argument (see \hyperref[entry:argument-finite-self-model-incomplete]{\textsc{Finite self-model incompleteness}}); that dispute is why this claim is marked contested.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-inside-vantage-access-limited]{\textsc{Limited inside vantage}} — the claim this optimism denies.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-finite-self-model-incomplete]{\textsc{Finite self-model incompleteness}} — the impossibility argument that contests it.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-llm-functional-introspection]{\textsc{LLM functional introspection}} — the weaker, actual capacity, distinguished from this strong thesis.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{concept-completeness-of-physics}{Completeness of physics}{Concept}{Completeness of physics}{}{$\blacklozenge$}
The \textbf{completeness of physics} is the thesis that the physical world is causally self-contained: every physical effect that has a cause at all has a \emph{sufficient} physical cause. Whenever a physical event is produced, one need never step outside physics to find what produced it — a complete physical story can in principle be told for every physical happening. The principle is sometimes called the \emph{causal completeness}, \emph{causal closure}, or \emph{causal self-sufficiency} of the physical domain, and it is the empirical engine of the modern argument for physicalism (see \hyperref[entry:argument-causal-argument-for-physicalism]{\textsc{Causal argument for physicalism}}, \hyperref[entry:position-physicalism]{\textsc{Physicalism}}).

The careful formulation matters. Completeness does not say that \emph{every} event is physical, nor that the physical causes are the \emph{only} causes; it says only that the physical causes already on the books are \textbf{sufficient} — enough, by themselves, to fix the physical effect. So stated, it leaves logical room for a second, non-physical cause of the same effect. Closing that room is the work of a separate premise about overdetermination (see \textsc{No systematic overdetermination}); the two together yield the strong conclusion that nothing non-physical does any \emph{distinctive} causal work in the physical world.

Papineau (see \hyperref[source:source-papineau-2002]{\textsc{Papineau — Thinking about Consciousness (2002)}}) stresses that completeness is not an a priori truth but a hard-won \textbf{empirical discovery}. His ``history of the completeness of physics'' traces how the inventory of basic forces was progressively closed: vital and mental forces, once posited to explain living and intelligent behaviour, were squeezed out as physiology and physics found sufficient physical causes for the bodily movements they were meant to produce. The conservation laws are the usual modern evidence — a non-physical cause injecting energy or momentum into a body would show up as a violation, and none is observed.

\webheading{Quantum indeterminism}

A naive statement of completeness is \emph{deterministic}: every physical effect is fully fixed by prior physical states under physical law. On the standard reading of quantum mechanics that is simply false. When an atom decays or a photon meets a half-silvered mirror, the prior physical state fixes only a \emph{probability distribution} over outcomes, not the outcome itself. There is genuine chance in the basement.

Completeness survives because it was never a claim about \emph{determination} in the first place; it is a claim about \emph{probabilities}. The careful formulation runs: in so far as a physical effect is determined at all, it is determined by physical antecedents, and in so far as it is merely chancy, its objective probabilities are fixed by physical law \emph{alone}. The dial may sometimes land at random, but nothing non-physical sets the odds it lands on. So stated, completeness rules out non-physical \emph{causes}, not physical \emph{chance}: randomness is internal to physics, not a leak in its wall, and the thesis is compatible with genuine indeterminism rather than a disguised commitment to determinism.

This matters for the mind because quantum chance is sometimes floated as a loophole for the \emph{interactionist} — a place where an immaterial will might ``make a difference'' without violating the conservation laws, by nudging which outcome a brain's quantum events take. The probabilistic formulation is exactly what closes the loophole, and it does so by reposing the same dilemma the \hyperref[entry:concept-causal-closure]{\textsc{Causal closure of the physical}} poses elsewhere. Either the mind merely rides along with whatever the physical chances throw up, in which case it does no distinctive work (see \hyperref[entry:position-epiphenomenalism]{\textsc{Epiphenomenalism}}); or it \emph{biases} the probabilities, making an outcome likelier than the physical law prescribes, in which case physics is not complete after all. The second horn is a genuine empirical claim, and the physicalist bets it is false: no deviation from quantum statistics has ever been measured in a brain. So indeterminism hands the \hyperref[entry:position-dualism]{\textsc{dualist}} no free mental causation. It only restates the choice between riding inertly and breaking completeness, with the breach now statistical rather than energetic.

Two cautions. First, the picture is interpretation-dependent: on Bohmian mechanics the world is deterministic again and completeness reverts to its strong form, while on Everettian quantum mechanics there is no single collapse-outcome to be ``caused'' at all. Completeness holds across these readings because in each the \emph{physics} remains causally self-contained, but the gloss on ``randomness'' differs. Second, indeterminism is not by itself a resource for free will: an outcome that is merely random is no more an agent's own doing than one that is determined, so nothing about how completeness treats quantum chance smuggles libertarian agency in through the back door.

\webheading{The completeness premise at work}

Completeness is the load-bearing premise of the \textbf{causal argument for physicalism}: if every physical effect already has a sufficient physical cause, then a mental event that genuinely causes a physical effect (a hand's rising, a word's being spoken) must \emph{be} one of those physical causes, on pain of either doing no work at all (see \hyperref[entry:position-epiphenomenalism]{\textsc{Epiphenomenalism}}) or redundantly duplicating a cause already present. This is why anti-physicalists who want the mind to make a difference — interactionist dualists especially — must reject or restrict completeness, and why much of the dispute over \hyperref[entry:concept-hard-problem]{\textsc{Hard problem}} turns on whether the principle is as secure as its defenders claim.

\begin{plainterms}
The idea is that the physical world is a closed shop, causally speaking: whenever something physical happens, there is always a complete physical explanation for it — some earlier physical stuff that was enough to make it happen. You never \emph{have} to bring in a soul, a spirit, or any non-physical push to finish the story. It is offered not as an armchair assumption but as something science has gradually found to be true: every time people expected a special ``life force'' or ``mind force'' to be needed, a purely physical cause turned up instead. If that is right, then a thought that really moves your arm has to be a physical thing too — otherwise the arm would already be fully accounted for and the thought would have nothing left to do.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-causal-closure]{\textsc{Causal closure of the physical}} — the closely allied ``no causes cross into the physical from outside'' formulation; completeness plus no-overdetermination yields closure.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-causal-argument-for-physicalism]{\textsc{Causal argument for physicalism}} — the inference for which this thesis is the chief premise.
  \item \textsc{Completeness premise} — completeness stated as the truth-apt premise that argument rests on.
  \item \textsc{No systematic overdetermination} — the companion premise that closes the loophole completeness leaves open.
  \item \hyperref[entry:position-physicalism]{\textsc{Physicalism}} — the view this principle is marshalled to support.
  \item \hyperref[entry:position-epiphenomenalism]{\textsc{Epiphenomenalism}} — the fate completeness forces on a mental cause that is not itself physical.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{claim-conceivability-entails-possibility}{Conceivability entails possibility}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, contested \,\textperiodcentered\, after Chalmers}{Conceivability entails possibility}{Ideal conceivability entails metaphysical possibility}{$\odot$}
If a scenario is \emph{ideally} conceivable — coherent under idealized rational reflection, with no concealed contradiction — then it is metaphysically possible. This is the modal-epistemology bridge of the zombie argument, and its most contested step. It is emphatically \emph{not} the trivial claim that whatever we can loosely picture is possible; it concerns \emph{ideal} conceivability, what survives unlimited rational scrutiny. On the two-dimensional version (David Chalmers, \hyperref[source:source-chalmers-1996]{\textsc{Chalmers — The Conscious Mind (1996)}}; \hyperref[source:source-chalmers-2002]{\textsc{Chalmers — On Sense and Intension (2002)}}), the principle reads more precisely: ideal \emph{primary} conceivability entails \emph{primary} possibility, and for the zombie case the primary and secondary intensions are taken to coincide.

The bridge is what converts \hyperref[entry:claim-zombie-conceivable]{\textsc{Zombie conceivability}} into a conclusion against physicalism, so the whole dispute concentrates here.

\webheading{Argued both ways}

It is not a brute premise. It is \textbf{defended} by modal rationalism (see \hyperref[entry:argument-modal-rationalism]{\textsc{Modal rationalism}}): once the \hyperref[entry:concept-primary-intension]{\textsc{Primary intension}} and \hyperref[entry:concept-secondary-intension]{\textsc{Secondary intension}} are distinguished, the apparent counterexamples are misdescribed possibilities (see \hyperref[entry:claim-aposteriori-necessities-are-misdescribed-possibilities]{\textsc{A-posteriori necessities as misdescribed possibilities}}), so ideal primary conceivability tracks primary possibility. It is \textbf{attacked} by the a-posteriori-necessity objection (see \hyperref[entry:argument-conceivability-not-guide-to-possibility]{\textsc{A-posteriori-necessity objection}}): Kripkean necessities (see \hyperref[entry:claim-some-necessities-knowable-only-a-posteriori]{\textsc{A-posteriori necessities}}, \hyperref[source:source-kripke-1980]{\textsc{Kripke — Naming and Necessity (1980)}}) already break the link, so a zombie may be conceivable yet impossible — the type-B physicalist reading (see \hyperref[entry:concept-materialism-types]{\textsc{Type-A / Type-B / Type-C materialism}}). A deeper, allied \textbf{phenomenal-concept strategy} (see \hyperref[entry:argument-gap-deflated-by-phenomenal-concepts]{\textsc{Phenomenal-concept deflation}}, \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-concept-isolation-tradeoff]{\textsc{Phenomenal-concept trade-off}}) explains the conceivability as an artefact of how phenomenal concepts work. Throughout, the crux is the same: whether the \emph{phenomenal} case has the appearance/reality gap that lets the water case escape.

The claim is distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-zombie-conceivable]{\textsc{Zombie conceivability}} (the antecedent it operates on) and from \hyperref[entry:claim-intuition-warrant-tracks-trained-domain]{\textsc{Calibration of intuition}} (an epistemology of \emph{intuitions about cases}); this one is specifically about the conceivability→possibility inference.

\begin{plainterms}
This is the hinge the whole zombie argument swings on: that \emph{if you can coherently imagine something — really coherently, with no hidden contradiction — then it is genuinely possible}. Grant it, and ``I can imagine a zombie'' turns into ``a zombie is possible,'' which would mean experience is something extra beyond the physical. The classic pushback borrows from science: ``water is H₂O'' is something you \emph{can} imagine being false, yet it is necessarily true — so being able to imagine otherwise does not always prove real possibility. Maybe zombies are like that: imaginable but impossible.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-zombie-conceivable]{\textsc{Zombie conceivability}} — the antecedent this bridge operates on.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-modal-rationalism]{\textsc{Modal rationalism}} — the defence of the bridge.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-conceivability-not-guide-to-possibility]{\textsc{A-posteriori-necessity objection}} — the objection to it.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-aposteriori-necessities-are-misdescribed-possibilities]{\textsc{A-posteriori necessities as misdescribed possibilities}} — the premise the defence rests on.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-some-necessities-knowable-only-a-posteriori]{\textsc{A-posteriori necessities}} — the Kripkean cases the objection wields.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-materialism-types]{\textsc{Type-A / Type-B / Type-C materialism}} — type-B physicalism, the reading on which the bridge fails.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-gap-deflated-by-phenomenal-concepts]{\textsc{Phenomenal-concept deflation}} / \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-concept-isolation-tradeoff]{\textsc{Phenomenal-concept trade-off}} — the allied phenomenal-concept strategy.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-intuition-warrant-tracks-trained-domain]{\textsc{Calibration of intuition}} — a distinct point about the warrant of intuitions.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{claim-conceivability-not-sufficient-for-physical-realizability}{Conceivability without realizability}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{Conceivability without realizability}{Conceivability does not entail physical realizability}{$\odot$}
That something is coherently conceivable — even rigorously, mathematically coherent — does not entail that it can be physically built or realized. The inference from conceivable to \emph{physically} possible fails.

The claim concerns the conceivability → \textbf{physical} possibility bridge specifically. It is deliberately silent on the conceivability → \textbf{metaphysical} possibility bridge (see \hyperref[entry:claim-conceivability-entails-possibility]{\textsc{Conceivability entails possibility}}): the witnessing case, the halting oracle, is \emph{metaphysically} possible (a consistent structure) and merely \emph{physically} unrealizable, so it is no counterexample to the metaphysical bridge. To threaten \emph{that} bridge one would need a \emph{Turing-machine} halting-decider, which is metaphysically impossible — but on ideal reflection that is not even \emph{ideally} conceivable, since Turing's contradiction is a priori. So the metaphysical bridge keeps its \emph{prima facie / ideal} refinement, parallel to the primary/secondary move on the water case.

The cleanest witnessing instance is the halting oracle (see \hyperref[entry:claim-halting-oracle-coherent-but-unrealizable]{\textsc{Halting oracle (coherent but unrealizable)}}) — coherent, theorized, provably not Turing-computable, and on standard physics unbuildable, with \emph{no} ``you mislabelled it'' escape. The behaviour-matching lookup table (see \hyperref[entry:claim-lookup-table-physically-unrealisable]{\textsc{Physically unrealisable table}}) is a second instance, failing instead on resource bounds (see \hyperref[entry:claim-observable-universe-finite-storage]{\textsc{Finite cosmic storage}}).

Who would deny it: someone who collapses physical possibility into logical or mathematical possibility (a strong digital-physics or modal-collapse view); or a hypercomputation theorist who holds that the witnessing cases are realizable after all.

It is distinct from the metaphysical bridge (see \hyperref[entry:claim-conceivability-entails-possibility]{\textsc{Conceivability entails possibility}}): same antecedent, different consequent — \emph{physical} realizability, not \emph{metaphysical} possibility.

\begin{plainterms}
Being able to coherently imagine — or even rigorously define and theorize — something doesn't mean nature can build it. The halting oracle is the showcase: airtight as an idea, impossible as a machine. This is a \emph{better} version of the old ``water that isn't H₂O'' point, because there's no wriggle room about whether you've really conceived it — there's a whole branch of mathematics about it. One careful boundary: this is about what can be \emph{built}, not about what's \emph{possible in the broadest sense} — the oracle is still a consistent abstract object; it just can't be made real.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-halting-oracle-coherent-but-unrealizable]{\textsc{Halting oracle (coherent but unrealizable)}} — the showcase witnessing instance.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-lookup-table-physically-unrealisable]{\textsc{Physically unrealisable table}} and \hyperref[entry:claim-observable-universe-finite-storage]{\textsc{Finite cosmic storage}} — a second instance, failing on resource limits.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-conceivability-entails-possibility]{\textsc{Conceivability entails possibility}} — the metaphysical bridge this claim leaves untouched.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-halting-oracle]{\textsc{Halting oracle}} — the underlying object behind the witnessing case.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{argument-conceivable-not-physically-realizable}{Conceivable yet unrealizable}{Argument \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{Conceivable yet unrealizable}{The halting oracle shows conceivability does not entail physical realizability}{$\vdash$}
This argument draws a general moral from a single rigorous case: that something can be coherently conceivable — even rigorously, mathematically coherent — without being something nature could ever build.

\webheading{The inference}

The form is a \emph{counterexample}: one rigorous instance is enough to refute a universal sufficiency claim.

\begin{enumerate}
  \item The halting oracle (see \hyperref[entry:concept-halting-oracle]{\textsc{Halting oracle}}) is mathematically coherent — indeed positively, fruitfully theorized — yet physically unrealizable (see \hyperref[entry:claim-halting-oracle-coherent-but-unrealizable]{\textsc{Halting oracle (coherent but unrealizable)}}).
\end{enumerate}

Therefore conceivability, including rigorous mathematical coherence, does not entail physical realizability (see \hyperref[entry:claim-conceivability-not-sufficient-for-physical-realizability]{\textsc{Conceivability without realizability}}): here is one coherent thing that cannot be built.

\webheading{Why it beats the water case}

The standard \emph{type-B} or \emph{two-dimensional} reply to the classic ``water that isn't H₂O'' example says the conceiver only imagined a \emph{misdescribed} possibility — some watery stuff that isn't water — with the necessity hidden in the definition. That reply has no purchase here. The oracle is specified exactly and studied as such; there is no other thing one is ``really'' conceiving, and its unbuildability is a \emph{proof} (see \hyperref[entry:claim-halting-problem-undecidable]{\textsc{Undecidability of the halting problem}}), not an empirical identity that could be redescribed away.

\webheading{Scope: what it does not show}

The target is narrow and deliberate: the bridge from conceivability to \emph{physical} realizability. It is \textbf{not} an attack on the metaphysical bridge from conceivability to possibility (see \hyperref[entry:claim-conceivability-entails-possibility]{\textsc{Conceivability entails possibility}}, \hyperref[entry:argument-conceivability-not-guide-to-possibility]{\textsc{the broader anti-conceivability argument}}). The oracle is metaphysically possible; and the device that would be metaphysically impossible — a \emph{Turing-machine} halting-decider — is not even \emph{ideally} conceivable, since Turing's contradiction is a priori. So Chalmers' conceivability bridge keeps its prima-facie/ideal refinement. The point is cleaner than that: coherent ≠ buildable.

\webheading{The weakest premise}

The argument inherits the contingency of the physical Church–Turing thesis (see \hyperref[entry:claim-physical-church-turing-thesis]{\textsc{Physical Church–Turing thesis}}) through its premise: if hypercomputation is physically possible, the witnessing instance dissolves. But the \emph{generalisation} survives on other instances — notably the resource-bounded lookup table (see \hyperref[entry:claim-lookup-table-physically-unrealisable]{\textsc{Physically unrealisable table}}) — so the conclusion is robust even if the oracle case is contested.

\begin{plainterms}
From one airtight example you get a general lesson. The halting oracle is completely coherent as an idea yet impossible to build — so ``I can coherently conceive it'' plainly does not guarantee ``it can be physically made.'' And it's a cleaner example than ``water that isn't H₂O,'' because nobody can say you secretly meant something else: the oracle is defined exactly, and its unbuildability is a \emph{proof}. Just note the modest target — this is about what can be \emph{built}, not a claim that the oracle is impossible in every sense.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-halting-oracle-coherent-but-unrealizable]{\textsc{Halting oracle (coherent but unrealizable)}} — the premise; the worked case.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-conceivability-not-sufficient-for-physical-realizability]{\textsc{Conceivability without realizability}} — the conclusion.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-conceivability-entails-possibility]{\textsc{Conceivability entails possibility}} and \hyperref[entry:argument-conceivability-not-guide-to-possibility]{\textsc{A-posteriori-necessity objection}} — the metaphysical bridge this argument pointedly leaves alone.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-lookup-table-physically-unrealisable]{\textsc{Physically unrealisable table}} — a second witnessing instance, robust to hypercomputation.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-halting-oracle]{\textsc{Halting oracle}} — the object the counterexample turns on.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{argument-connectionist-reply}{Connectionist reply}{Argument \,\textperiodcentered\, after Churchland}{Connectionist reply}{The Connectionist Reply — a massively parallel, brain-like architecture is the real candidate for understanding}{$\vdash$}
The \emph{Connectionist Reply}, pressed by Paul and Patricia Churchland (see \hyperref[source:source-churchland-1990]{\textsc{Churchland \& Churchland — Could a Machine Think? (1990)}}), grants that a single clerk serially executing rules — a classical von Neumann machine — may well not understand, but denies this tells against \emph{connectionist} systems. Real cognition, they argue, runs on a massively \emph{parallel}, brain-like architecture of many simple units adjusting weighted connections at once. Searle's room models the wrong kind of machine, so its failure is uninformative: of course the lone clerk, handling one tiny step at a time, grasps nothing of a process whose understanding (if any) is a global, distributed, emergent property of the whole parallel net.

The reply attacks the \hyperref[entry:argument-chinese-room-derivation]{\textsc{derivation}} by denying that A3 is self-evident: the Churchlands hold that whether syntax suffices for semantics is an open empirical question about architectures, not an axiom to be settled from the armchair, and that the brain itself is an existence proof that the right physical network yields meaning. It is the \hyperref[entry:argument-brain-simulator-reply]{\textsc{Brain Simulator Reply}}'s cousin, trading neuron-by-neuron simulation for the abstract parallel \emph{style} of neural computation.

\webheading{The crux}

Searle's \hyperref[entry:argument-rejoinder-chinese-gym]{\textsc{Chinese Gym rejoinder}} scales the room up to a \emph{gymnasium} full of monolingual English speakers, each playing the role of one node in the parallel net, passing signals on cue. The architecture is now parallel, yet — Searle insists — no individual understands Chinese and neither does the assembled crowd; parallelism multiplies the syntax-shuffling without adding a drop of semantics. The Churchlands' deeper point survives the parody, though: they do not claim the \emph{gym} understands, but that A3 is an unargued assumption, and that meaning may be a feature of the right \emph{physical} network that no purely formal redescription captures.

\begin{plainterms}
The room imagines a single worker plodding through rules one at a time — but that's not how brains work. Brains are huge parallel networks, billions of simple units firing together, and (the Churchlands say) understanding might be a property of that whole parallel pattern, not something you'd find by watching one slow clerk. So the room is rigged: it models the wrong kind of machine. Searle's answer: fine, fill a gymnasium with people, each acting as one ``neuron,'' all working at once — it's parallel now, and \emph{still} nobody in the gym understands Chinese. The Churchlands' lasting point is subtler: maybe the real lesson is that Searle just \emph{assumed} shape-shuffling can't make meaning, when whether it can is a question for science, not a self-evident axiom.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-chinese-room-derivation]{\textsc{Chinese room derivation}} — the argument attacked, by denying A3 is self-evident.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-rejoinder-chinese-gym]{\textsc{Chinese gym}} — Searle's reply: a parallel ``Chinese gym'' still understands nothing.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-brain-simulator-reply]{\textsc{Brain simulator reply}} — the allied appeal to neural fidelity, here at the level of architecture.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-llm-representations-reply]{\textsc{LLM-representations reply}} — the modern empirical descendant: evidence that text-trained networks actually do build human-aligned representations, vindicating the Churchlands' bet.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-luminous-room]{\textsc{Luminous room}} — the Churchlands' \emph{other} objection in the same paper: a meta-attack on the reliability of Searle's intuition, where this one attacks his architecture.
  \item \hyperref[source:source-churchland-1990]{\textsc{Churchland \& Churchland — Could a Machine Think? (1990)}} — the Churchlands' statement of the reply.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{concept-consciousness}{Consciousness}{Concept}{Consciousness}{}{$\blacklozenge$}
\emph{Consciousness} is the fact that, for some creatures, there is \emph{something it is like} to be them — Thomas Nagel's phrase (see \hyperref[source:source-nagel-1974]{\textsc{Nagel}}) for the felt, subjective character of experience. To be conscious of the smell of coffee, the ache of a bruise, or the blue of the sky is for those states to \emph{seem} some way \emph{from the inside}. The word covers several things — being awake rather than unconscious, being aware \emph{of} something, being aware \emph{of oneself} — but its philosophically central sense is this one: \emph{phenomenal} consciousness, raw felt experience. This entry surveys the topic and serves as a hub for the cluster of problems, theories, and arguments it generates; the question of how consciousness fits the physical world is treated under the \hyperref[entry:concept-mind-body-problem]{\textsc{Mind-body problem}}, of which it is the hardest strand.

\webheading{Access and phenomenal consciousness}

A distinction owed to Ned Block (see \hyperref[source:source-block-1995]{\textsc{Block — On a Confusion about a Function of Consciousness (1995)}}) organises much of the field. A state is \emph{access}-conscious when its content is available to the rest of the system — poised for reasoning, verbal report, and the rational control of behaviour. It is \emph{phenomenally} conscious when there is something it is like to be in it (see \hyperref[entry:concept-access-vs-phenomenal-consciousness]{\textsc{Access vs. phenomenal consciousness}}). The two usually travel together in us, which is why they are easily run together; but they come apart in principle, and whether they can come apart in fact — whether there could be access without any feel, or feel without access — is a live question. The distinction matters above all because it marks where explanation seems to run out: we can see how a brain makes information \emph{available}; why that availability should be \emph{felt} is another matter.

\webheading{The easy and the hard problems}

David Chalmers (see \hyperref[source:source-chalmers-1995]{\textsc{Chalmers — Facing Up to the Problem of Consciousness (1995)}}) sorted the questions into the \emph{easy problems} and the \emph{hard problem}. The easy problems — how the brain integrates information, discriminates stimuli, reports its states, controls behaviour — are ``easy'' only by comparison: they ask for \emph{mechanisms}, and we know what a successful answer would look like. The \hyperref[entry:concept-hard-problem]{\textsc{hard problem}} (see \hyperref[entry:question-hard-problem]{\textsc{why there is experience at all}}) is to say why the performance of any such functions should be accompanied by experience — why it doesn't all go on ``in the dark.'' Closely related is the \hyperref[entry:argument-explanatory-gap]{\textsc{explanatory gap}}: even a complete physical account of the brain seems to leave us with no idea \emph{how} the physical constitutes the felt.

\webheading{Theories of consciousness}

Scientific and philosophical theories try to say what consciousness \emph{is} or what brings it about. The \hyperref[entry:position-global-workspace]{\textsc{global workspace}} view identifies it with information broadcast widely across the brain for use by many subsystems. \hyperref[entry:position-integrated-information-theory]{\textsc{Integrated information theory}} identifies it with a system's integrated information ($\Phi$), making consciousness a structural feature any sufficiently integrated system would have. \hyperref[entry:position-higher-order-theories]{\textsc{Higher-order theories}} hold that a state is conscious when the mind represents \emph{itself} as being in it. \hyperref[entry:position-representationalism]{\textsc{Representationalism}} reduces the felt character of experience to its representational content. \hyperref[entry:position-illusionism]{\textsc{Illusionism}} takes the boldest line: that phenomenal consciousness, as usually conceived, is an introspective \emph{illusion} — there seems to be a further felt residue, but the seeming is all there is. \hyperref[entry:position-biological-naturalism]{\textsc{Biological naturalism}} treats consciousness as a higher-level biological feature caused by, but not reducible to, neurobiology.

\webheading{Why it resists explanation}

Several arguments keep consciousness philosophically intractable by pressing that no amount of physical or functional fact seems to \emph{entail} the facts of experience. The \hyperref[entry:argument-zombie-conceivability]{\textsc{conceivability of zombies}} — beings physically just like us but with no inner light on — suggests experience is a further fact beyond the physical. The \hyperref[entry:concept-knowledge-argument]{\textsc{knowledge argument}} (Mary, who knows all the physics of colour yet seems to learn something on first seeing red) presses the same point through what complete knowledge leaves out. And the bedrock datum of \hyperref[entry:concept-first-person-access]{\textsc{first-person access}} — that each subject is directly acquainted with its own experience — is what makes the gap feel undeniable from the inside, even as it stays invisible from the outside.

\webheading{The machine question}

Because consciousness is defined by felt subjectivity rather than by any particular biology, it is an open question which systems have it. If consciousness depends only on functional organisation, it could be \hyperref[entry:claim-substrate-independence]{\textsc{substrate-independent}} and arise in artificial systems; if it depends on the intrinsic causal powers of living tissue, it could not. The stakes are not merely theoretical: whether an AI is phenomenally conscious bears directly on whether it has interests and \hyperref[entry:question-llm-moral-status]{\textsc{moral status}}, and the access/phenomenal distinction warns that fluent self-report is at most evidence of \emph{access}, never by itself of \emph{feeling}.

\begin{plainterms}
Consciousness is the plain fact that your life is \emph{lived from the inside} — there's something it's like to taste chocolate, stub your toe, or see red, rather than those just being events in a machine with nobody home. Philosophers split it in two: the ``access'' part (your brain having information on hand to talk about and act on) is the kind science is steadily explaining; the ``felt'' part (why any of it actually \emph{feels} like anything) is the one nobody can yet explain — the famous \emph{hard problem}. There are many theories about what produces it, from ``information shared widely in the brain'' to ``it's partly an illusion,'' and a family of puzzles — philosophical zombies, the colour scientist who's never seen colour — suggesting the felt part slips through every physical net. And the same mystery now faces AI: we can tell a machine has information available, but whether there's anyone \emph{in there} feeling anything is exactly what we don't know how to check.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-mind-body-problem]{\textsc{Mind-body problem}} — the parent problem; consciousness is its hardest strand.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-hard-problem]{\textsc{Hard problem}} / \hyperref[entry:question-hard-problem]{\textsc{Hard problem (the question)}} — why physical functioning is accompanied by experience at all.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-access-vs-phenomenal-consciousness]{\textsc{Access vs. phenomenal consciousness}} — the access/phenomenal distinction (see \hyperref[source:source-block-1995]{\textsc{Block — On a Confusion about a Function of Consciousness (1995)}}).
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-first-person-access]{\textsc{First-person access}} — the direct self-acquaintance that poses the gap from the inside.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-explanatory-gap]{\textsc{Explanatory-gap argument}}, \hyperref[entry:argument-zombie-conceivability]{\textsc{Zombie argument}}, \hyperref[entry:concept-knowledge-argument]{\textsc{Knowledge argument}} — the arguments that keep consciousness resistant to physical explanation.
  \item \hyperref[entry:position-global-workspace]{\textsc{Global workspace theory}}, \hyperref[entry:position-integrated-information-theory]{\textsc{Integrated information theory (IIT)}}, \hyperref[entry:position-higher-order-theories]{\textsc{Higher-order theories of consciousness}}, \hyperref[entry:position-representationalism]{\textsc{Representationalism}}, \hyperref[entry:position-illusionism]{\textsc{Illusionism}}, \hyperref[entry:position-biological-naturalism]{\textsc{Biological naturalism}} — the principal theories of what consciousness is.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-substrate-independence]{\textsc{Substrate-independence thesis}}, \hyperref[entry:question-llm-moral-status]{\textsc{Moral status of LLMs}} — whether consciousness can arise in machines, and why it matters.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{claim-consciousness-is-biological-feature}{Consciousness as biological feature}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, after Searle}{Consciousness as biological feature}{Consciousness is a higher-level feature caused by neurobiological processes}{$\odot$}
Consciousness is a real, higher-level biological feature \emph{caused by} lower-level neurobiological processes and \emph{realised in} the brain — much as digestion is caused by and realised in the stomach. On this claim it is \emph{ontologically irreducible}, having an essentially first-person character that no third-person description captures, yet it is causally explained by, and nothing ``over and above,'' the brain's specific causal powers. This is the core thesis of the \emph{biological naturalism} John Searle set out in \emph{The Rediscovery of the Mind} (see \hyperref[source:source-searle-1992]{\textsc{Searle — The Rediscovery of the Mind (1992)}}).

The claim is physicalist but both anti-reductionist and anti-computationalist. Because it is the brain's concrete \emph{causal powers} that produce consciousness — and not the abstract program it might be said to run — merely implementing the right syntax is insufficient for mind (\hyperref[entry:claim-syntax-insufficient-for-semantics]{\textsc{Syntax insufficient for semantics}}, the moral of the \hyperref[entry:concept-chinese-room]{\textsc{Chinese room}}).

It is denied by strong-AI functionalists (see \hyperref[entry:concept-functionalism]{\textsc{Functionalism}}, \hyperref[entry:claim-substrate-independence]{\textsc{substrate independence}}), who hold that the right causal \emph{organisation} suffices regardless of substrate. It is also distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-mental-states-are-brain-state-types]{\textsc{type-identity}} — a reductive identification of mental states with neural state-types — since biological naturalism is non-reductive; and from proteocentrism, since what it demands is the right \emph{causal powers}, which need not be carbon-specific, even if Searle holds that only suitably brain-like systems are so far known to have them.

\begin{plainterms}
Brains make consciousness the way stomachs make digestion: it is a perfectly physical product of a living organ, not a ghostly extra and not a piece of software. So you cannot get a mind just by running the right program on any machine — you would need something with the brain's actual physical powers to cause it.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-syntax-insufficient-for-semantics]{\textsc{Syntax insufficient for semantics}} — the companion thesis ruling out pure computation.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-chinese-room]{\textsc{Chinese room}} — the thought experiment supplying the ``syntax is not semantics'' moral.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-functionalism]{\textsc{Functionalism}} — the rival whose ``organisation suffices'' this claim denies.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-substrate-independence]{\textsc{Substrate-independence thesis}} — the strong-AI thesis the claim is set against.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-mental-states-are-brain-state-types]{\textsc{Mental states as brain types}} — the reductive type-identity from which this non-reductive claim is distinguished.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{claim-consciousness-is-global-broadcast}{Consciousness as global broadcast}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, after Baars}{Consciousness as global broadcast}{A state is conscious only if its content is globally broadcast to the brain's specialist systems}{$\odot$}
A state is conscious only if its content is \emph{globally broadcast} — made widely available to the brain's many specialist subsystems (memory, language, motor control) for report and flexible use. On this claim consciousness is a matter of a content entering a ``global workspace'' and being shared widely, rather than remaining locked inside a single module. It is the core of global workspace theory, introduced by Bernard Baars (see \hyperref[source:source-baars-1988]{\textsc{Baars — A Cognitive Theory of Consciousness (1988)}}); Stanislas Dehaene's \emph{global neuronal workspace} supplies the neural implementation (see \hyperref[source:source-dehaene-naccache-2001]{\textsc{Dehaene \& Naccache — Towards a Cognitive Neuroscience of Consciousness (2001)}}).

This is most naturally a thesis about the \emph{access} side of the \hyperref[entry:concept-access-vs-phenomenal-consciousness]{\textsc{access/phenomenal distinction}}: it explains what makes a content widely usable and reportable. Whether global broadcast also constitutes \emph{phenomenal} character — the felt quality of experience — is exactly what critics say it leaves open.

It is denied by those who hold that phenomenal consciousness can \emph{overflow} access (Ned Block \hyperref[source:source-block-2007]{\textsc{Block — Consciousness, Accessibility, and the Mesh between Psychology and Neuroscience (2007)}}), so that broadcast is not necessary for felt experience, and by phenomenal realists who deny that wide availability could ever suffice for felt character.

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-consciousness-is-higher-order-representation]{\textsc{higher-order representation}}, which requires a dedicated state \emph{about} another mental state rather than mere broadcast, and from \hyperref[entry:claim-consciousness-is-integrated-information]{\textsc{integrated information}}, which turns on intrinsic cause-effect structure rather than functional broadcast.

\begin{plainterms}
The idea: a thought or perception becomes conscious when it is shared across the whole brain — put on a kind of central noticeboard that the speech, memory, and action systems can all read — rather than staying trapped in one specialist circuit. The doubt some raise: this explains how information becomes usable and reportable, but maybe not why it \emph{feels} like anything to have it.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-access-vs-phenomenal-consciousness]{\textsc{Access vs. phenomenal consciousness}} — the distinction that places this claim on the access side and frames the overflow objection.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-consciousness-is-higher-order-representation]{\textsc{Consciousness as higher-order representation}} — a rival requiring meta-representation, not mere broadcast.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-consciousness-is-integrated-information]{\textsc{Consciousness as integrated information (Φ)}} — a rival locating consciousness in intrinsic cause-effect integration rather than functional availability.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{claim-consciousness-is-higher-order-representation}{Consciousness as higher-order representation}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, after Rosenthal}{Consciousness as higher-order representation}{A mental state is conscious only if it is the object of a higher-order representation}{$\odot$}
A first-order mental state is \emph{conscious} in virtue of being represented by a suitable \emph{higher-order} state, and not otherwise. What it is for a state to be conscious is thus a relational, representational fact about it, not an intrinsic glow it carries on its own. (The first-/higher-order contrast is set out in \hyperref[entry:concept-order-of-representation]{\textsc{First-order vs. higher-order states}}.) The higher-order state may be an occurrent \emph{higher-order thought} that one is in the lower state (see \hyperref[source:source-rosenthal-2005]{\textsc{Rosenthal — Consciousness and Mind (2005)}}) or a quasi-perceptual \emph{higher-order perception} of it (see \hyperref[source:source-lycan-1996]{\textsc{Lycan — Consciousness and Experience (1996)}}); on a dispositional variant the higher-order content need only be poised to occur, not actually entertained (see \hyperref[source:source-carruthers-2000]{\textsc{Carruthers — Phenomenal Consciousness: A Naturalistic Theory (2000)}}).

The claim is a reductive account of \emph{state} consciousness — what makes a state conscious — most naturally read as a theory of the access side of \hyperref[entry:concept-access-vs-phenomenal-consciousness]{\textsc{the access/phenomenal distinction}}. It does not by itself settle the hard problem: critics say it explains a state's availability, not its felt character.

It is denied by first-order representationalists, who hold that consciousness needs no higher-order state, and by phenomenal realists, who hold that felt character is intrinsic rather than constituted by being represented.

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-consciousness-is-global-broadcast]{\textsc{the global-broadcast claim}}, on which consciousness is global availability rather than a dedicated higher-order representation, and from \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-character-is-representational]{\textsc{the first-order representational claim}}, which locates phenomenal character in first-order, world-directed content.

\begin{plainterms}
A mental state — a pain, say — counts as \emph{conscious} only when your mind also represents itself as having it: a thought, or an inner perception, about the state. On this view there is no special inner shine that makes a state conscious; it is conscious because it is being monitored from a level above. The much-debated worry is that this explains your being \emph{aware} of the pain without explaining the \emph{hurt} itself.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-order-of-representation]{\textsc{First-order vs. higher-order states}} — the first-/higher-order vocabulary the claim turns on.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-access-vs-phenomenal-consciousness]{\textsc{Access vs. phenomenal consciousness}} — the access side this claim is naturally read as theorising.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-consciousness-is-global-broadcast]{\textsc{Consciousness as global broadcast}} — the rival on which consciousness is availability, not a meta-state.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-character-is-representational]{\textsc{Phenomenal character as representational}} — the first-order rival locating feel in world-directed content.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{claim-consciousness-is-integrated-information}{Consciousness as integrated information (Φ)}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, after Tononi}{Consciousness as integrated information (Φ)}{Consciousness is identical to a system's integrated information (Φ)}{$\odot$}
A system is conscious to the degree that it has \emph{integrated information} — $\Phi$, the irreducible intrinsic cause-effect power it exerts over itself — and the \emph{quality} of its experience is the geometry of that cause-effect structure. On this claim consciousness is graded, intrinsic, and read off a system's physical organisation rather than inferred from its behaviour. It is the central identity of Giulio Tononi's integrated information theory (see \hyperref[source:source-tononi-2004]{\textsc{Tononi — An Information Integration Theory of Consciousness (2004)}}), developed with Christof Koch (see \hyperref[source:source-tononi-2016]{\textsc{Tononi, Boly, Massimini \& Koch — Integrated Information Theory: From Consciousness to Its Physical Substrate (2016)}}).

The claim is an \emph{identity} derived from phenomenological ``axioms'' (intrinsic existence, integration, exclusion), not a functional account. Its sharp, testable corollary concerns substrates: because $\Phi$ depends on a system's actual cause-effect architecture, two systems with the same \emph{input-output behaviour} can differ in consciousness — notably, a feed-forward digital simulation can have $\Phi \approx 0$.

It is denied by functionalists and computationalists, since it makes consciousness depend on more than the computation performed, and by those who reject either its axioms or its panpsychist consequences (very simple systems can carry non-zero $\Phi$).

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-consciousness-is-global-broadcast]{\textsc{global broadcast}}: $\Phi$ is a matter of \emph{intrinsic} cause-effect integration, not functional availability — which is why IIT, unlike workspace theory, denies \hyperref[entry:claim-substrate-independence]{\textsc{substrate independence}} and bears against \hyperref[entry:claim-llm-has-mental-states]{\textsc{LLMs have mental states}}.

\begin{plainterms}
This theory says consciousness is just how much a system is a tightly-knit whole that shapes itself — a quantity called phi (Φ). The more its parts make a difference to each other in a way you cannot break apart without loss, the more conscious it is, and the exact pattern of those interactions \emph{is} the exact experience. A surprising result: a computer could copy a brain's behaviour perfectly yet, because of how its chips are wired, have almost no Φ — so on this view it would feel almost nothing.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-consciousness-is-global-broadcast]{\textsc{Consciousness as global broadcast}} — the functional rival this claim is contrasted with.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-substrate-independence]{\textsc{Substrate-independence thesis}} — denied by this claim.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-llm-has-mental-states]{\textsc{LLMs have mental states}} — borne against: low Φ on standard hardware.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-hard-problem]{\textsc{Hard problem}} — the problem the identity is meant to address.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{concept-consciousness-freewill-duality}{Consciousness–free-will duality}{Concept \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{Consciousness–free-will duality}{The mirror duality between the consciousness and free-will debates}{$\blacklozenge$}
The \emph{consciousness–free-will duality} is a proposed structural mirror between two of philosophy's hardest problem-families. Consciousness is \textbf{input-facing}: it integrates many perceptual streams, the apparent final destination of perception. Free will is \textbf{output-facing}: it selects among many available actions, the apparent first source of action. Reversing the direction of the arrows — the basic operation of \hyperref[entry:concept-categorical-duality]{\textsc{categorical duality}} — appears to map one domain onto the other, and if the mapping is sound, concepts, paradoxes, and whole arguments should transfer between the two literatures. The framework's claim is not that the transfer is automatic, but that arrow-reversal is a systematic \emph{generator} of candidate correspondences, a striking number of which turn out to already exist in the other literature under different names.

\webheading{What the arrows are}

Talk of arrow-reversal is only as precise as the arrows, so the diagrams behind the dictionary should be fixed once. The \emph{objects} are states: world-states, sensory registrations, conscious experiences, deliberative states, motor commands, actions. An \emph{arrow} $A \to B$ is a directed dependence — $A$ feeds, informs, or causally settles $B$. Perception is a chain of arrows running world → sense channels → experience; agency is a chain running deliberation → motor command → world. A philosophical thesis is then read as a claim about the \emph{shape} of this diagram: \hyperref[entry:position-epiphenomenalism]{\textsc{Epiphenomenalism}} says the conscious state is a node with only incoming arrows; libertarian origination demands a node with only outgoing ones; integration says many arrows converge on one apex; choice says one apex commits to exactly one of many candidate arrows out.

``X is the arrow-reversed image of Y'' is accordingly a three-step recipe. Draw Y as a diagram of dependence-arrows. Reverse every arrow, so that \emph{receives-from} becomes \emph{issues-to}, sinks become sources, and convergence becomes branching. Then relabel every input-side item as its output-side counterpart — sensory stream ↔ action option, integration ↔ commitment, attention ↔ volition, ``what arriving is like'' ↔ ``originating''. Read the resulting diagram back as a thesis: that thesis is X.

Two cautions govern the recipe. Read as bare causal flow it is nearly trivial — inputs and outputs run in opposite temporal directions by definition — so an entry earns its place only when what the reversal preserves is combinatorial structure (which product/coproduct role a state plays) or argument-shape (which premise deletes which arrow), never mere temporal direction (see \hyperref[entry:argument-no-universal-property]{\textsc{Missing universal property}}). And these dependence-arrows must not be conflated with the \emph{structural} arrows of a product or coproduct (projections, injections) — the sign problem dissolved below turns exactly on keeping the two kinds apart.

\webheading{Two axes, and which one carries the substance}

The informal statement runs together two different dualities. The first axis is \textbf{initial/terminal}: ``final destination of perception'' makes consciousness a terminal object (a unique arrow into it from everywhere), and ``first source of action'' makes the will an initial object. This is the strictest reading and the emptiest: uniqueness of routing is flatly false of consciousness (most of the organism's states never reach it), and terminal objects carry no internal structure, so nothing about the \emph{content} of experience or choice is captured.

The second axis is \textbf{product/coproduct}, and it carries the substance (see \hyperref[entry:claim-conscious-content-is-product-like]{\textsc{Product-like experience}}, \hyperref[entry:claim-action-content-is-coproduct-like]{\textsc{Coproduct-like choice}}). Conscious content behaves like a \emph{product} of qualia channels: an element of a product realizes all of its components simultaneously, and experience is likewise conjunctive — visual, auditory, and proprioceptive channels co-present in a single moment. The content of a free choice behaves like a \emph{coproduct} of actions: an element of a coproduct realizes exactly one summand, and incompatible motor programs cannot co-execute. Co-presence versus commitment.

An apparent sign problem dissolves on inspection. Drawn naively, ``many streams in'' makes consciousness the apex of a \emph{cocone} (coproduct-shaped) and ``many options out'' makes the will the apex of a \emph{cone} (product-shaped) — the reverse assignment. But this conflates structural arrows with mediating maps. In the product reading, the incoming streams are not arrows into the apex; they are the components of a single mediating map from the world-state into the product — the pairing $\langle q_1, q_2, \ldots\rangle$ — while the structural arrows (projections) point outward. Dually, the action options are not arrows out of the will; the choice is one mediating injection in.

The duality then splits into two exact pairs at the level of maps. The \textbf{combination pair}: pairing into a product (integration of streams) is dual to copairing out of a coproduct (a standing case-analysis over possible actions — behavioral competence rather than choice itself). The \textbf{selection pair}: projection out of a product (selecting one stream among co-present ones) is dual to injection into a coproduct (committing to one action among alternatives). On this reading the dual of the will is not consciousness as a whole but \textbf{attention} (see \hyperref[entry:claim-attention-is-dual-of-will]{\textsc{Attention–volition duality}}): attention is input-side selection, volition is output-side selection.

\webheading{The dictionary}

Applying arrow-reversal to standard arguments yields a dictionary; entries that land on independently existing positions are the framework's evidence (see \hyperref[entry:argument-consciousness-freewill-duality]{\textsc{Duality argument}}).

\begin{itemize}
  \item \textbf{Exclusion ↔ disappearing agent.} Kim's \hyperref[entry:argument-causal-exclusion]{\textsc{causal-exclusion argument}} makes consciousness all sink and no source; its dual is Pereboom's \hyperref[entry:argument-disappearing-agent]{\textsc{disappearing-agent objection}} and the luck objection generally (see \hyperref[entry:claim-exclusion-dualizes-to-disappearing-agent]{\textsc{Exclusion–disappearing-agent duality}}).
  \item \textbf{Zombies ↔ volitional zombies.} The \hyperref[entry:concept-philosophical-zombie]{\textsc{philosophical zombie}} dualizes to a behaviorally identical system with no real choosing; the compatibilist reply is structurally the deflationist reply to zombies (see \hyperref[entry:claim-zombie-dualizes-to-volitional-zombie]{\textsc{Volitional zombie}}). By parity, deflationism about phenomenality pairs with \hyperref[entry:position-compatibilism]{\textsc{Compatibilism}} (see \hyperref[entry:claim-deflationism-entails-compatibilism]{\textsc{Deflation–compatibilism parity}}); \hyperref[entry:position-illusionism]{\textsc{Illusionism}} pairs with Wegner's illusion of conscious will.
  \item \textbf{Conceivability certificates.} The Π₁ objection to the zombie argument's conceivability premise transfers verbatim to libertarian agent-causation (see \hyperref[entry:argument-agent-causation-certificate]{\textsc{Certificate objection to agent-causation}}) — the one entry that transfers machinery rather than analogy.
  \item \textbf{Unity ↔ unity.} The unity of consciousness dualizes to the unity of agency — binding failures mirror akrasia and the divided will (see \hyperref[entry:claim-unity-dualizes-to-unity-of-agency]{\textsc{Unity–unity duality}}).
  \item \textbf{Combination ↔ composition.} The \hyperref[entry:argument-combination-problem]{\textsc{combination problem}} for panpsychism dualizes to the composition of will (see \hyperref[entry:claim-combination-dualizes-to-composition-of-will]{\textsc{Combination–composition duality}}).
  \item \textbf{Frankfurt ↔ ganzfeld.} Frankfurt cases (freedom survives deletion of alternatives) dualize to the ganzfeld objection (does consciousness survive deletion of differentiation?) — an apparently novel prediction (see \hyperref[entry:claim-frankfurt-dualizes-to-ganzfeld]{\textsc{Frankfurt–ganzfeld duality}}).
\end{itemize}

\webheading{Status and limits}

Free-will problems do not live in the opposite category — they live in the same time-forward causal world as the consciousness problems — so arrow-reversal supplies a dictionary, not a transfer theorem; each entry must be verified by hand (see \hyperref[entry:claim-duality-dictionary-not-theorem]{\textsc{Dictionary, not theorem}}, \hyperref[entry:argument-no-universal-property]{\textsc{Missing universal property}}). The main disanalogies pressed against the strict reading (see \hyperref[entry:claim-consciousness-freewill-strict-duality]{\textsc{Strict duality thesis}}) are the actual/possible seam (see \hyperref[entry:argument-actual-possible-seam]{\textsc{Actual–possible seam}}), the collapse of agency-phenomenology back into consciousness (see \hyperref[entry:argument-ambient-consciousness-collapse]{\textsc{Ambient-consciousness collapse}}), and the graded structure of both sides (see \hyperref[entry:argument-graded-polarity]{\textsc{Graded-polarity objection}}). A further meta-claim holds that every genuine disanalogy traces to the thermodynamic arrow of time (see \hyperref[entry:claim-disanalogies-trace-to-entropy]{\textsc{Entropy as the sole asymmetry}}). The sharpest defensible version is therefore not ``consciousness and free will are category-theoretic duals'' but: the two debates are the same dialectic mirror-imaged along the perception–action axis, and the categorical framing is the scaffolding that generates the transferable moves (see \hyperref[entry:claim-consciousness-freewill-mirror]{\textsc{Mirror thesis}}).

\begin{plainterms}
Perception is many-streams-in: sights, sounds, and body sense arrive together in one experienced moment. Action is one-choice-out: of the many things you could do next, you do exactly one. This entry describes a proposal that these two shapes are mathematical mirror images, so that famous puzzles about consciousness turn into famous puzzles about free will when you ``flip the arrows'' — and, surprisingly often, the flipped puzzle already exists in the other literature under its own name. The mirror is a lead-generator, not a proof machine: each translated puzzle has to be checked on its own merits.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-categorical-duality]{\textsc{Categorical duality}} — the mathematical machinery: arrow reversal, products, coproducts, and why the everyday world is not perfectly mirror-symmetric.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-consciousness-freewill-mirror]{\textsc{Mirror thesis}} — the hedged master thesis this framework supports.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-consciousness-freewill-strict-duality]{\textsc{Strict duality thesis}} — the strict reading, target of the disanalogy arguments.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-consciousness-freewill-duality]{\textsc{Duality argument}} — the abductive case from rediscovered correspondences.
  \item \hyperref[entry:question-free-will]{\textsc{Free will}} and \hyperref[entry:question-hard-problem]{\textsc{Hard problem (the question)}} — the two governing questions the mirror spans.
\end{seealso}
