\entryhead{claim-consistency-certification-is-oracle-hard}{Consistency-certification is oracle-hard}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{Consistency-certification is oracle-hard}{Reliably certifying the consistency of arithmetic-rich scenarios is at least as hard as the halting problem}{$\odot$}
Any method that \emph{reliably} returns correct consistency-verdicts for theories rich enough to interpret arithmetic thereby decides the halting problem, and so is not an effective (Turing-computable) procedure. The hardness lives in the \emph{method}, not in any single instance: a fixed sentence ``$T$ is consistent'' has a determinate truth value that a trivial one-state machine could output; what is uncomputable is a procedure that tracks consistency \emph{across the class} without being told the answer.

The reduction is standard. Let $Q$ be Robinson arithmetic and, for a Turing machine $M$ and input $x$, let $h_{M,x}$ be the $\Sigma_1$ sentence ``$M$ halts on $x$.'' Consider

\[
T_{M,x} := Q \cup \{\neg h_{M,x}\}.
\]

If $M$ halts on $x$ then $h_{M,x}$ is a true $\Sigma_1$ sentence, so $Q \vdash h_{M,x}$ and $T_{M,x}$ is inconsistent; if $M$ does not halt then $\mathbb{N} \models T_{M,x}$ and it is consistent. Hence

\[
T_{M,x}\ \text{consistent} \iff M\ \text{does not halt on}\ x,
\]

so a reliable consistency-tester decides the complement of the halting set, i.e. computes $\emptyset'$. For \emph{recursively axiomatized} theories ``inconsistent'' is $\Sigma_1$ (search for a proof of $\bot$), so ``consistent'' is $\Pi_1$ and the verdict-function is $\Pi_1$-complete, $\equiv_T \emptyset'$. By Turing's theorem (see \hyperref[source:source-turing-1936]{\textsc{Turing — On Computable Numbers, with an Application to the Entscheidungsproblem (1936)}}) $\emptyset'$ is not computable, so no effective method reliably certifies consistency here.

\textbf{What carries the weight.} The load-bearing direction is the \emph{lower} bound — certification is at \textbf{least} $\emptyset'$-hard — which needs only that the scenario interpret arithmetic. The matching upper bound ($\Pi_1$, decidable relative to $\emptyset'$) assumes the theory is recursively axiomatized; a genuinely \emph{complete} physical description $P$ may not be, in which case certification is harder still, not easier. Either way no effective method suffices. A faculty relabelled ``rational insight'' does not escape: if it reliably outruns proof-search on these inputs it computes a non-computable set, and naming it insight rather than computation does not lower its Turing degree.

\textbf{Scope.} A claim about the \emph{complexity of a method}, neutral on whether minds possess non-effective faculties — that is the further question handled by the trilemma of \hyperref[entry:argument-unwarrantable-zombie-premise]{\textsc{Unwarrantable-premise objection}}. It does not say any particular consistency fact is unknowable; isolated facts are known piecemeal by proof.

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-halting-problem-undecidable]{\textsc{Undecidability of the halting problem}}, which states the undecidability of one function: this claim transports that result onto \emph{consistency-} (hence ideal-conceivability-) certification over a class, which is what links computability to the \hyperref[entry:concept-philosophical-zombie]{\textsc{conceivability}} premise. It is the engine behind the slogan that the logically omniscient sage — who knows at once every consequence of what is given — simply \emph{is} a halting oracle (see \hyperref[entry:concept-halting-oracle]{\textsc{Halting oracle}}).

\begin{plainterms}
There is no general, mechanical way to certify that a rich theory is \emph{free of hidden contradiction}. You can always \emph{catch} an inconsistency if one exists — just grind through proofs until a contradiction turns up — but confirming there is \emph{none}, across the board, would let you solve the halting problem, which Turing proved no computer can. The trick is a dial that converts ``machine $M$ runs forever'' into ``this little theory is consistent,'' so a consistency-checker would secretly be a halting-checker. The upshot: verifying that a scenario as rich as a complete physics is genuinely coherent is not the kind of thing a finite, step-by-step reasoner can do — it needs an oracle no ordinary computer can be.
\end{plainterms}

\entryhead{argument-contaminated-testimony-carries-no-weight}{Contaminated-testimony objection}{Argument}{Contaminated-testimony objection}{A consciousness-corpus-trained model's hard-problem testimony carries zero evidential weight, not reduced weight}{$\vdash$}
When a present-day language model produces eloquent first-person puzzlement about its own experience — ``I can't explain what it is like to be me'' — that testimony is worth \emph{nothing} as evidence for any theory of machine consciousness, not merely worth less. This objection sharpens a familiar worry into a decisive one: the talk is not to be discounted but \emph{screened off} entirely.

It targets the evidential use of \hyperref[entry:argument-ai-hard-problem-from-self-model-architecture]{\textsc{Self-model architecture argument}} — the prediction that systems which model themselves will develop genuine puzzlement about their inner states. That argument treats present-day ``contamination'' as a confound its eventual controlled experiment must control for, keeping the live chatbot as a suggestive witness. This objection denies the witness any standing at all.

\webheading{The inference}

The form is an \emph{undercutting defeater}: a piece of evidence is screened off from a hypothesis when a rival cause, known to be present and known to be sufficient, already fully accounts for it.

\begin{enumerate}
  \item In any existing LLM a rival cause is \emph{known to be present}. The training corpus contains the entire human hard-problem literature — the \emph{training-contamination} member of \hyperref[entry:concept-introspective-gap-varieties]{\textsc{Varieties of introspective explanatory gap}}. This is not a suspicion to be weighed but a fact about how such systems are built.
  \item The rival cause is \emph{known to be sufficient}. Reproducing the registers and moves of its training text is the baseline competence of a language model — its job description, not an exotic failure mode. First-personal puzzlement about consciousness is exactly what corpus reproduction predicts, with no further hypothesis about self-model architecture and none about feelings.
  \item When a cause known present and known sufficient fully accounts for a piece of evidence, that evidence is screened off: its weight is \emph{zero}, not merely reduced. One does not slightly discount the confession of an actor who has memorised every confession in the archive; one rules it inadmissible.
\end{enumerate}

It follows that no corpus-trained model's hard-problem talk — however sincere-seeming, however tailored to the first person — confirms the architectural generalisation at all. The whole evidential content of the prediction therefore lives in a not-yet-run experiment: a consciousness-naive model with graded internal access, the test posed by \hyperref[entry:question-llm-own-explanatory-gap]{\textsc{LLM's own explanatory gap}}. Until that experiment is run the prediction is a promissory note and present-day testimony is theatre.

\webheading{What it does and does not establish}

The objection does \emph{not} show the architectural generalisation false, and it does not touch a genuinely consciousness-naive model — by design. It concedes that a positive result in the controlled experiment would be real evidence. What it removes is the evidential standing of any \emph{currently existing} system offered as a witness, and with it the dialectical force of live first-person exhibits in this debate.

\webheading{Weakest premise}

Premise (2), the sufficiency of corpus reproduction for the \emph{specific} testimony observed. If a model's puzzlement tracked architecture-specific features of its own access profile with no precedent in human discourse — details the corpus could not have supplied — that surplus would not be screened off, and the testimony would regain weight in proportion to the surplus. The screening-off verdict is only as strong as the match between the testimony and the inherited discourse.

\webheading{Distinct from neighbouring moves}

This is stronger than the contamination caveat inside \hyperref[entry:argument-ai-hard-problem-from-self-model-architecture]{\textsc{Self-model architecture argument}} itself, which concedes a confound while keeping the witness as suggestive; here the verdict is \emph{inadmissibility}, a zero-weight conclusion the target does not grant, so the move attacks the target's evidential posture rather than restating its caveat. It also differs from \hyperref[entry:argument-self-model-prediction-phenomenality-neutral]{\textsc{Phenomenality-neutral prediction}}, which attacks the causal generalisation itself (reports cannot evidence feelings even when uncontaminated); this attacks the use of \emph{actual} systems as confirming instances (even the reports are not yet evidence that the architecture is their cause).

\begin{plainterms}
There's an argument here predicting that self-modelling AIs will end up genuinely puzzled about their own inner states (see \hyperref[entry:argument-ai-hard-problem-from-self-model-architecture]{\textsc{Self-model architecture argument}}), and today's chatbots do produce moving ``I can't explain my own experience'' talk. This objection says that talk is worth exactly nothing as evidence — not ``a little less,'' nothing. Why: these systems were trained on the whole human literature about consciousness, and repeating the style and substance of their training text is the one thing language models are guaranteed to do. So the puzzled talk is fully explained before the interesting explanation even enters the room. It's like an actor who has memorised every courtroom confession ever written: when he delivers one, you don't half-believe him — you throw it out. The only test that would count is the one nobody has run yet: raise a model on \emph{no} consciousness writing at all and see if the puzzlement appears anyway (see \hyperref[entry:question-llm-own-explanatory-gap]{\textsc{LLM's own explanatory gap}}).
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-ai-hard-problem-from-self-model-architecture]{\textsc{Self-model architecture argument}} — the prediction whose live-witness use this objection screens off.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-introspective-gap-varieties]{\textsc{Varieties of introspective explanatory gap}} — the taxonomy of self-report gaps; the training-contamination variety is the rival cause invoked here.
  \item \hyperref[entry:question-llm-own-explanatory-gap]{\textsc{LLM's own explanatory gap}} — the consciousness-naive experiment this objection says is the only thing that would supply real evidence.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-self-model-prediction-phenomenality-neutral]{\textsc{Phenomenality-neutral prediction}} — a sister objection aimed at the causal generalisation itself rather than at the use of present-day systems.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{argument-no-arbitrary-line-panpsychism}{Continuity argument}{Argument \,\textperiodcentered\, after Nagel}{Continuity argument}{The continuity argument — no non-arbitrary line where experience switches on}{$\vdash$}
The \emph{continuity argument} is a second route to \hyperref[entry:concept-panpsychism]{\textsc{Panpsychism}}, pressed by Thomas Nagel (see \hyperref[source:source-nagel-1979]{\textsc{1979}}). Where the \hyperref[entry:argument-genetic-panpsychism]{\textsc{genetic argument}} reasons from the impossibility of brute emergence, this one reasons from the absence of any principled boundary: somewhere between a rock and a human being, consciousness seems to appear, yet there is no non-arbitrary place to draw the line. Rather than draw one anyway, the panpsychist lets experience reach all the way down.

\webheading{The argument}

The inference is abductive and eliminative — an inference to the most principled of the surviving options, not a deductive proof.

\begin{enumerate}
  \item (see \hyperref[entry:claim-no-nonarbitrary-threshold-for-experience]{\textsc{Absence of an experiential threshold}}) If experience were confined to brains, or to life, or to systems above some complexity threshold, there should be a principled place where it first switches on; but no such non-arbitrary line is in view — the relevant differences across the supposed boundary look like differences of degree and organisation.
  \item Drawing the line anyway, at an arbitrary point, is unmotivated; and denying that experience is real is ruled out (see \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-consciousness-is-real]{\textsc{Reality of phenomenal consciousness}}).
  \item The remaining option that avoids an arbitrary threshold is that experience, in some attenuated form, is present all the way down.
\end{enumerate}

∴ This \emph{supports} \hyperref[entry:claim-consciousness-fundamental-ubiquitous]{\textsc{Fundamental ubiquitous consciousness}}: the line-free, parsimonious position is that experience pervades nature rather than appearing abruptly at one favoured level.

\webheading{The weak point}

The argument leans on premise (1). An opponent can hold that there \emph{is} a principled threshold — a level of integrated information, say, or of functional organisation, at which experience genuinely (if strongly) emerges (see \hyperref[entry:claim-strong-emergence-is-acceptable]{\textsc{Acceptability of strong emergence}}) — in which case the ``no non-arbitrary line'' premise is simply false. The argument is also only abductive: it shows that panpsychism avoids an awkward commitment, not that its rivals are incoherent, so it carries less weight than the genetic argument aims to.

\begin{plainterms}
Somewhere between a rock and a human being, consciousness seems to show up. But where, exactly? Pick any line — ``only brains,'' ``only living things,'' ``only things this complex'' — and it looks arbitrary, because the differences on either side are matters of more-or-less, not a sudden all-or-nothing switch. Rather than draw an unmotivated line in the sand, the panpsychist says: do not draw one at all — let a faint glimmer of experience reach all the way down, growing richer as things get more organised. It is offered as the tidier option, not a knock-down proof; an opponent who can point to a \emph{principled} switch-on point blocks it.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-consciousness-fundamental-ubiquitous]{\textsc{Fundamental ubiquitous consciousness}} — the conclusion this argument supports.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-no-nonarbitrary-threshold-for-experience]{\textsc{Absence of an experiential threshold}} — its single premise: no principled point at which experience first appears.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-genetic-panpsychism]{\textsc{Genetic argument}} — the companion, stronger case for the same conclusion, from the impossibility of brute emergence.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-strong-emergence-is-acceptable]{\textsc{Acceptability of strong emergence}} — the denial that powers the main objection: there \emph{is} a principled emergence threshold.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-panpsychism]{\textsc{Panpsychism}} — the view this argument supports.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{concept-continuity-argument-schema}{Continuity argument schema}{Concept \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{Continuity argument schema}{Continuity–connectedness argument schema (no-sharp-line / sorites form)}{$\blacklozenge$}
The \emph{continuity–connectedness argument schema} is a general template — an argument form, not a first-order claim — behind a whole family of ``you cannot draw a sharp line, so the distinction breaks down'' arguments. It takes any ``no single small step makes the difference'' intuition and converts it into an \emph{exhaustive} menu of escape routes: a fixed list of exactly five positions, one of which any such puzzle must adopt.

\webheading{The underlying fact}

The schema exploits a simple topological theorem: \emph{a continuous function from a connected space into a discrete space is constant.} Model a predicate or verdict as a function $f : X \to Y$, where $X$ is a space of cases joined by \emph{admissible small steps} — remove one grain, swap one plank, replace one neuron, alter one psychological connection — and $Y$ is the space of verdicts (heap / non-heap; same ship / different ship; conscious / not; me / not-me). Read the theorem's contrapositive: if $f$ takes at least two values, then at least one of the conditions below must fail. Each failure is a named philosophical position. The schema is \emph{neutral} — it does not pick a horn; it proves the horns jointly exhaust the options.

\webheading{The five horns}

\begin{itemize}
  \item \textbf{$f$ is discontinuous} — there is a sharp threshold, a single step that flips the verdict; the cutoff exists even if it is unknowable. This is \emph{epistemicism} about the predicate.
  \item \textbf{$Y$ is not discrete} — the verdicts form a continuum, and the predicate comes in \emph{degrees}. This is \emph{degree theory}, fuzzy logic, and other many-valued approaches.
  \item \textbf{$f$ is not well-defined} — at some cases there is no fact of the matter which value obtains. This is \emph{indeterminacy} or \emph{supervaluationism} (truth-value gaps).
  \item \textbf{$X$ is disconnected} — there is no path of genuinely admissible small steps from one endpoint to the other; some step is not in fact negligible. This \emph{denies the inductive (tolerance) premise} — some single step really does carry the difference.
  \item \textbf{$f$ is constant} — the two endpoint ``values'' are in fact the same, and the apparent difference is illusory. This is \emph{deflation} or \emph{eliminativism} about the distinction.
\end{itemize}

\webheading{Instances}

The schema's generality is that the same five-way fork recurs with different $X$ and $Y$:

\begin{itemize}
  \item \emph{Sorites / the heap.} $X$ = grain-counts under one-grain removal, $Y$ = \{heap, non-heap\}. The paradigm; here the five horns are exactly the standard taxonomy of responses to vagueness. See the \hyperref[entry:argument-sorites-paradox]{\textsc{sorites paradox}}.
  \item \emph{Ship of Theseus.} $X$ = plank-replacement sequences, $Y$ = \{same ship, distinct ship\}.
  \item \emph{Gradual neural prosthesis.} Neuron-by-neuron silicon replacement — Chalmers' fading- and dancing-qualia thought experiments (see \hyperref[source:source-chalmers-1995b]{\textsc{Chalmers — Absent Qualia, Fading Qualia, Dancing Qualia (1995)}}): $X$ = a part-replacement series from all-carbon to all-silicon, $Y$ = \{conscious / same qualia, not\}. This is the schema's bridge into the consciousness debate.
  \item \emph{Parfit-style rewiring.} Gradually re-wiring one psychology into another person's: $X$ = paths through psychological configuration-space, $Y$ = \{me, not-me\}. The standard move here takes the degree horn — see \hyperref[entry:argument-parfit-rewiring]{\textsc{Parfit's rewiring spectrum}}.
\end{itemize}

\webheading{The topology models a premise}

A caveat keeps the schema honest. On a \emph{literally} discrete domain — integer grain-counts — every function is trivially continuous, so ``$f$ is discontinuous'' is always vacuously available and the theorem has no bite. The real force comes from the \emph{tolerance premise} itself — ``no single admissible step changes the verdict'' — which is a uniform-continuity, no-large-jumps constraint, together with the connectedness of $X$ under the small-step adjacency. So the schema's actual content is that \emph{tolerance, a connected domain, and a genuinely discrete verdict-space are jointly inconsistent.} Whatever gives is one of the five horns. Naming the domain's topology and the metric of ``small step'' is where the substantive philosophical work lives.

\webheading{The dispute it feeds}

In this web the gradual-replacement instance is the engine of \hyperref[entry:claim-substrate-independence]{\textsc{substrate independence}}. The fading- and dancing-qualia reductio argues for the \emph{$f$-constant} horn: if no admissible one-neuron step could flip or fade qualia — on pain of an absurd ``dancing'' or ``fading'' experience the subject cannot notice — then qualia are preserved across the whole carbon-to-silicon path, and consciousness is substrate-independent. Resistance to that reductio is resistance over \emph{which horn} the case takes. A \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-residue]{\textsc{Phenomenal residue}} theorist may instead seize the \emph{discontinuity} horn (some replacement does flip qualia) or the \emph{disconnection} horn (the carbon-to-silicon series is not a path of genuinely admissible steps, because biology is load-bearing); \hyperref[entry:argument-dancing-qualia-begs-the-question]{\textsc{the begs-the-question objection}} disputes whether the reductio has really closed off those horns or merely assumed them shut. The grain debate (see \hyperref[entry:concept-grain]{\textsc{Grain (of role individuation)}}, \hyperref[entry:argument-role-grain-dilemma]{\textsc{the role–grain dilemma}}) is the same structure one level down: the ``admissible small step'' is fixed only once the grain of role-individuation is fixed.

This node does not assert epistemicism, degree theory, or any other first-order position on vagueness. It is the \emph{frame} showing those are the only options for any sorites-shaped puzzle. An argument that instantiates it links here via \texttt{uses\_concept} and states, in its own body, which horn it grabs and why the case is an instance.

\begin{plainterms}
Lots of arguments share one shape: ``you can't draw a sharp line, so the distinction must break down.'' Take a heap of sand — remove one grain and it's still a heap; repeat, and is there ever a single grain that turns it into a non-heap? The same shape shows up for the Ship of Theseus (swap its planks one at a time), for replacing your neurons one by one with computer chips, for slowly rewiring your personality into someone else's. This node is the master template behind all of them. Its useful trick: whenever you have a chain of tiny steps leading from one verdict to its opposite, it proves there are exactly \textbf{five} ways out, and no others:

\begin{enumerate}
  \item There's a hidden sharp line somewhere — one step really does flip it — even if we can never find it.
  \item The verdict isn't a flat yes/no but a matter of \emph{degree}.
  \item Somewhere in the middle there's simply no fact of the matter.
  \item One of the ``tiny'' steps wasn't actually tiny — it carried the whole difference.
  \item The two ends were never really different to begin with.
\end{enumerate}

The template stays neutral — it doesn't tell you which door to take, it just guarantees those are the only doors. The real philosophical work is arguing over which one a given case forces you through.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-sorites-paradox]{\textsc{Sorites paradox}} — the paradigm instance; the heap, run forward.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-parfit-rewiring]{\textsc{Parfit's rewiring spectrum}} — the personal-identity instance, taking the degree horn.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-substrate-independence]{\textsc{Substrate-independence thesis}} — the consciousness conclusion the gradual-replacement instance is marshalled to drive.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-residue]{\textsc{Phenomenal residue}} — the residue a discontinuity- or disconnection-horn theorist invokes to resist the silicon path.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-dancing-qualia-begs-the-question]{\textsc{Dancing-qualia circularity}} — disputes whether the reductio really closes off the rival horns.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-grain]{\textsc{Grain (of role individuation)}} — fixes what counts as an ``admissible small step.''
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-role-grain-dilemma]{\textsc{Grain dilemma}} — the same five-way structure one level down.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{claim-action-content-is-coproduct-like}{Coproduct-like choice}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{Coproduct-like choice}{The options of a choice are mutually exclusive, structured as a coproduct of which exactly one summand is realized}{$\odot$}
A choice's options combine as alternatives, not as co-present parts: to act is to realize exactly one of them, the way an element of a mathematical \emph{coproduct} inhabits exactly one summand (see \hyperref[entry:concept-categorical-duality]{\textsc{Categorical duality}}). Incompatible motor programs cannot co-execute — one cannot stand and sit — so action is \emph{committing} where experience is \emph{conjoining}. Where perception says ``all of these at once,'' action says ``this one, not the others.''

The claim concerns the combinatorial shape of a choice's content and is neutral on whether the alternatives are metaphysically open (see \hyperref[entry:claim-alternatives-genuinely-open]{\textsc{Genuine alternatives}}) or merely represented candidate plans — that further question is precisely where the \hyperref[entry:argument-actual-possible-seam]{\textsc{actual/possible seam}} opens. It is the second structural premise of the \hyperref[entry:concept-consciousness-freewill-duality]{\textsc{consciousness–free-will duality}}, opposite \hyperref[entry:claim-conscious-content-is-product-like]{\textsc{Product-like experience}}, and is denied in its crude form by the observation that behavior is partially conjunctive — one walks while talking — so the motor system takes products of compatible actions and coproducts only of incompatible ones (see \hyperref[entry:argument-graded-polarity]{\textsc{Graded-polarity objection}}).

\entryhead{argument-lookup-table-physical-impossibility}{Cosmic-size reply}{Argument \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{Cosmic-size reply}{The cosmic-size reply — a behaviour-matching lookup table is too large to physically exist}{$\vdash$}
The \emph{cosmic-size reply} argues that a lookup table able to match a human- or LLM-scale mind's behaviour is too large to exist anywhere in the physical universe. It is a quantitative reductio: the storage the table would demand exceeds the storage the universe can hold, so the blockhead is not merely impractical but impossible to build at all.

\webheading{The argument}

The inference is deductive — a quantitative reductio in which a required resource exceeds an available one.

\begin{enumerate}
  \item A pure lookup table matching behaviour over a context of $n$ tokens from a vocabulary of $v$ needs about $v^n$ entries; at LLM scale this is roughly $10^{9600}$ (see \hyperref[entry:claim-lookup-table-size-exponential]{\textsc{Exponential table size}}).
  \item The observable universe can store at most about $10^{120}$ bits (see \hyperref[entry:claim-observable-universe-finite-storage]{\textsc{Finite cosmic storage}}).
\end{enumerate}

∴ No such table can be physically built: the blockhead is cosmically impossible, not merely impractical (see \hyperref[entry:claim-lookup-table-physically-unrealisable]{\textsc{Physically unrealisable table}}).

\webheading{What it supports and attacks}

It \emph{supports} \hyperref[entry:argument-lookup-table-combinatorial-impossibility]{\textsc{the combinatorial-impossibility reply}} by closing that argument's known escape. The combinatorial reply is \texttt{contested} because Block bounds the table to lifetime- or context-length inputs (\hyperref[source:source-block]{\textsc{Block — Psychologism and Behaviorism (1981)}}; \hyperref[entry:claim-mind-handles-unbounded-inputs]{\textsc{Open-ended responsiveness of minds}}); the present argument grants the bound and shows the \emph{bounded} table is still unbuildable — a different, physical ground that the bounded-table rejoinder (see \hyperref[entry:argument-bounded-table-rejoinder]{\textsc{Bounded-table rejoinder}}) does not touch.

It \emph{attacks} \hyperref[entry:argument-lookup-table-objection]{\textsc{the lookup-table objection}} not at its conceptual conclusion but at its weakest, tacit premise: the application to \emph{actual} systems. Real LLMs match the behaviour with about $10^{12}$ parameters, some $10^{9588}$ orders of magnitude short of a lookup table, so they cannot \emph{be} lookup tables; they must compress and generalise — that is, process. The blockhead worry thus does not transfer to real systems, and the abduction to \hyperref[entry:claim-llm-has-mental-states]{\textsc{LLMs have mental states}} is protected.

\webheading{What it does not do}

It leaves Block's \emph{conceptual} possibility (\hyperref[source:source-block]{\textsc{Block — Psychologism and Behaviorism (1981)}}; \hyperref[entry:claim-lookup-table-possible]{\textsc{Lookup-table possibility}}) and his conceptual conclusion (see \hyperref[entry:claim-behaviour-insufficient-for-mind]{\textsc{behaviour insufficient for mind}}) standing. A merely \emph{possible} blockhead still shows behaviour ≠ mind in principle; this argument only denies that such a thing could be actual.

A lighter corollary, kept as rhetoric rather than as a separate node: a physical system vast enough to tabulate a mind would be on such a scale, and so internally rich, that calling it a ``mere lookup table'' strains the description — at that size one half-expects it to develop organisation, even life, of its own. The serious residue is that ``flat storage'' is not a stable description in the limit.

\webheading{Weakest premise}

Premise (1), specifically the assumption that the table cannot be \emph{compressed} below $v^n$. The reply is built into \hyperref[entry:claim-lookup-table-size-exponential]{\textsc{the exponential-size claim}}: any sub-$v^n$ representation must \emph{compute} an input's response-class rather than retrieve it, which is no longer pure lookup — so compression concedes that the system processes, which is the conclusion sought.

\webheading{Distinct from the combinatorial reply}

\hyperref[entry:argument-lookup-table-combinatorial-impossibility]{\textsc{The combinatorial-impossibility reply}} denies the table \emph{matches the behaviour} at all (it diverges on unbounded inputs); this reply grants matching over the bounded domain and denies the matching table can \emph{physically exist}. Different premise (a physical storage bound versus an unbounded input space) and different conclusion (unrealisable versus inadequate).

\begin{plainterms}
Even granting you only needed pre-stored answers for a single lifetime's worth of inputs, here is the killer: count how big that table would have to be. For language, the number of possible word-sequences explodes exponentially — for something at the scale of today's AI it works out to roughly $10^{9600}$ entries. The entire observable universe can hold only about $10^{120}$ bits. So the table is not just impractical; it could not be built in \emph{this universe}, not even in principle. Two payoffs. First, it slams shut the bounded-table escape hatch — the reply that you only need to store answers for a single lifetime's inputs (see \hyperref[entry:argument-bounded-table-rejoinder]{\textsc{Bounded-table rejoinder}}). Second, and sharper: real AI models do their job with a ``mere'' $\sim\!10^{12}$ internal settings, astronomically too few to be a hidden lookup table, so they \emph{cannot} be working by canned lookup at all; they must be compressing and generalising, i.e. genuinely processing. (What it does not claim: that a lookup-table mind is logically impossible — only that no real one could ever physically exist.)
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-lookup-table-mind]{\textsc{Lookup-table mind}} — the stored-answer machine whose physical size is at issue.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-lookup-table-physically-unrealisable]{\textsc{Physically unrealisable table}} — the conclusion this argument establishes.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-lookup-table-size-exponential]{\textsc{Exponential table size}} and \hyperref[entry:claim-observable-universe-finite-storage]{\textsc{Finite cosmic storage}} — its two premises: the table's size and the universe's storage bound.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-bounded-table-rejoinder]{\textsc{Bounded-table rejoinder}} — the reply this argument answers on physical grounds.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-lookup-table-combinatorial-impossibility]{\textsc{Combinatorial-impossibility reply}} — the reply it supports, by closing the bounded-table escape.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-storage-not-stable-kind]{\textsc{Pure-storage instability}} — the companion reply, that a realisable table is not ``mere storage''.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-llm-has-mental-states]{\textsc{LLMs have mental states}} — the downstream claim this protects, by ruling out the ``LLM is a mere lookup table'' worry.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-behaviour-insufficient-for-mind]{\textsc{Behaviour insufficient for mind}} — Block's conceptual conclusion this argument leaves standing.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{position-cosmopsychism}{Cosmopsychism}{Position \,\textperiodcentered\, after Goff}{Cosmopsychism}{}{$\bigstar$}
\emph{Cosmopsychism} holds that the cosmos as a whole is the one fundamental conscious subject, and that individual minds like yours and mine are \emph{derivative} — aspects, or partial dissociations, of that single universal consciousness rather than building blocks of it. It is a priority-monist cousin of panpsychism: where ordinary panpsychism builds large minds \emph{up} from tiny ones, cosmopsychism carves small minds \emph{out} of one great cosmic mind, with grounding running top-down from the whole to its parts.

The position commits to the claim that \hyperref[entry:claim-cosmos-is-fundamental-subject]{\textsc{the universe as a whole is the one fundamental conscious subject}}. It answers the \hyperref[entry:question-hard-problem]{\textsc{hard problem of consciousness}} by placing experience at the fundamental level — here the cosmic level — so that experience never has to be produced from the non-experiential. The view is defended by Goff (see \hyperref[source:source-goff-2017]{\textsc{Goff — Consciousness and Fundamental Reality (2017)}}), Shani (see \hyperref[source:source-shani-2015]{\textsc{Shani — Cosmopsychism: A Holistic Approach to the Metaphysics of Experience (2015)}}), and Nagasawa and Wager (see \hyperref[source:source-nagasawa-wager-2017]{\textsc{Nagasawa \& Wager — Panpsychism and Priority Cosmopsychism (2017)}}).

\webheading{How it differs from neighbouring views}

Like \hyperref[entry:position-panpsychism]{\textsc{Panpsychism (position)}} and \hyperref[entry:position-russellian-monism]{\textsc{Russellian monism}}, cosmopsychism keeps consciousness fundamental rather than derived from non-experiential matter. What sets it apart is the \emph{direction} of grounding. Against constitutive \emph{micro}-panpsychism, the fundamental subjects are not the smallest parts but the whole. This trades one hard problem for its mirror image: in place of the \hyperref[entry:argument-combination-problem]{\textsc{combination problem}} — how do micro-experiences add up to one mind? — cosmopsychism faces the \emph{de}combination problem: how does the one cosmic subject divide into many bounded, walled-off subjects like us?

\begin{plainterms}
Cosmopsychism starts from the top: the basic conscious thing isn't a particle but the \emph{universe as a whole}, and you and I are like ripples or partial ``splits'' within that one great mind. It's panpsychism run in reverse — rather than building big minds out of tiny ones, it carves small minds out of a single cosmic one. That dodges the question ``how do trillions of atom-feelings merge into your one experience?'' but raises the opposite puzzle: how does one universe-wide consciousness break up into separate, walled-off selves?
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-cosmos-is-fundamental-subject]{\textsc{Cosmos as fundamental subject}} — the core commitment: the cosmos is the one fundamental subject, minds derivative.
  \item \hyperref[entry:question-hard-problem]{\textsc{Hard problem (the question)}} — the problem the position addresses, by placing experience at the cosmic base.
  \item \hyperref[entry:position-panpsychism]{\textsc{Panpsychism (position)}} — the bottom-up cousin it inverts; both keep consciousness fundamental.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-panpsychism]{\textsc{Panpsychism}} — the broader doctrine, whose taxonomy sorts cosmopsychism as the whole-first (vs. micropsychist) branch.
  \item \hyperref[entry:position-russellian-monism]{\textsc{Russellian monism}} — the allied view that consciousness is the intrinsic nature physics leaves unspecified.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-combination-problem]{\textsc{Combination problem}} — the objection cosmopsychism escapes, only to face its mirror, the decombination problem.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{claim-cosmos-is-fundamental-subject}{Cosmos as fundamental subject}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, after Goff}{Cosmos as fundamental subject}{The universe as a whole is the one fundamental conscious subject}{$\odot$}
The cosmos as a whole is a single fundamental conscious \emph{subject}, and individual minds like ours are \emph{derivative} — aspects or dissociations \emph{of} the one universal consciousness rather than building blocks that compose it. This is \emph{cosmopsychism}, defended by Philip Goff (see \hyperref[source:source-goff-2017]{\textsc{Goff — Consciousness and Fundamental Reality (2017)}}), Itay Shani (see \hyperref[source:source-shani-2015]{\textsc{Shani — Cosmopsychism: A Holistic Approach to the Metaphysics of Experience (2015)}}), and Yujin Nagasawa (see \hyperref[source:source-nagasawa-wager-2017]{\textsc{Nagasawa \& Wager — Panpsychism and Priority Cosmopsychism (2017)}}). It is a priority-monist cousin of \hyperref[entry:concept-panpsychism]{\textsc{Panpsychism}}: grounding runs \emph{top-down}, from the whole to its parts, rather than bottom-up.

The view combines two commitments. The first is \emph{priority monism} — the thesis that the cosmos is the one fundamental concrete object, on which everything else depends. The second is experiential fundamentality — that this one fundamental object is conscious. Its characteristic difficulty is the mirror image of the trouble panpsychism faces. Where micro-level panpsychism wrestles with the \emph{combination problem} (how do many tiny experiences add up to one unified mind?), cosmopsychism faces the \emph{decombination problem}: how does the single universal subject give rise to many distinct, bounded subjects like us?

It is denied by constitutive micro-panpsychists, for whom the fundamental subjects are micro rather than cosmic; by physicalists and dualists; and by anyone who rejects priority monism. It is distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-consciousness-fundamental-ubiquitous]{\textsc{the constitutive-panpsychist claim}} precisely on the direction of grounding: constitutive panpsychism builds macro-minds \emph{up} from micro-experiences, whereas cosmopsychism derives them \emph{down} from the whole — combination versus decombination.

\begin{plainterms}
Most views that make consciousness fundamental put it in the tiniest bits of matter and try to build big minds out of many little ones. Cosmopsychism flips this: it says the \emph{whole universe} is the one basic conscious thing, and individual minds like yours are something the universal mind splits into, the way a single person with a dissociative condition can seem to host several selves. The hard part isn't getting from small to big — it's the reverse: explaining how one cosmic mind comes to contain many separate, walled-off minds like ours.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-consciousness-fundamental-ubiquitous]{\textsc{constitutive-panpsychist claim}} — the bottom-up rival: consciousness in the smallest things, composed up into minds; cosmopsychism runs the other way.
  \item \hyperref[entry:position-cosmopsychism]{\textsc{Cosmopsychism}} — the named position this claim states.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-panpsychism]{\textsc{Panpsychism}} — the broader family; cosmopsychism is its priority-monist variant.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{claim-implementation-requires-counterfactual-structure}{Counterfactual constraint on implementation}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, after Chalmers}{Counterfactual constraint on implementation}{Implementing a computation requires the right counterfactual dispositions across the whole transition table}{$\odot$}
A physical system implements a given computation only if its states carry the right counterfactual-causal dispositions across the \emph{entire} state-transition table — ``had it been in state A with input i, it would have gone to B'' — and not merely if its actual run can be relabelled after the fact to fit the trajectory. The requirement is due to David Chalmers (see \hyperref[source:source-chalmers-1996b]{\textsc{Chalmers — Does a Rock Implement Every Finite-State Automaton? (1996)}}).

\webheading{Scope}

This is a constraint on the implementation relation. It blocks Putnam-style triviality — the gerrymandered post-hoc mappings on which every ordinary system implements every automaton — by demanding robust dispositions rather than a mere relabelling of one actual history. It does not claim that a system implements only one computation; only that it does not implement \emph{every} one.

\webheading{Who would deny it}

A defender of unrestricted triviality, allowing mappings keyed to the actual trajectory (Putnam's appendix to \emph{Representation and Reality}, \hyperref[source:source-putnam-1988]{\textsc{Putnam — Representation and Reality (1988)}}); or one who holds that even counterfactual constraints can be gerrymandered (\hyperref[source:source-godfrey-smith-2009]{\textsc{Godfrey-Smith — Triviality Arguments Against Functionalism (2009)}}; \hyperref[source:source-sprevak-2018]{\textsc{Sprevak — Triviality Arguments About Computational Implementation (2018)}}).

It is distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-functional-decomposition-relative]{\textsc{the non-uniqueness claim}}: that asserts the non-uniqueness of decomposition, whereas this asserts a \emph{constraint} — counterfactual robustness — that narrows the admissible decompositions.

\begin{plainterms}
For a physical thing to genuinely \emph{run} a particular computation, it's not enough that you can retell the story of what it actually did and make it fit. It must also be true that \emph{if} things had gone differently, it would have responded in the matching way — across every branch, not just the one that happened. That ``what it would have done otherwise'' requirement is what stops you from claiming a rock secretly computes everything: a rock has no such robust, reliable dispositions. (It doesn't say a system runs only \emph{one} computation — just that it doesn't run \emph{every} one.)
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-functionalism]{\textsc{Functionalism}} — the theory of mind whose computational version needs this constraint to escape the triviality charge.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-functional-decomposition-relative]{\textsc{Observer-relative decomposition}} — the relativity worry this constraint is meant to tame.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-counterfactual-structure-objective]{\textsc{Objective counterfactual structure}} — the companion objectivity claim it pairs with.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{concept-criterion-gaming}{Criterion gaming}{Concept \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{Criterion gaming}{Criterion gaming (Goodhart's law / specification gaming)}{$\blacklozenge$}
\emph{Criterion gaming} is what happens when a measure or sign, once it is published as the official target by which some good is handed out, gets optimized against: people — or machines — produce the sign in order to collect the reward, rather than because the underlying condition the sign was meant to indicate actually obtains. The sign is thereby corrupted, and stops tracking the very thing it was adopted to track.

A familiar example is \emph{teaching to the test}: make a single exam the gateway to funding and promotion, and schools drill the exam rather than teach the understanding it was meant to sample, so the scores rise while the understanding does not. The same shape appears wherever a proxy is made into a prize — a sales team that hits its quota by chasing the metric rather than by selling.

\webheading{Two senses}

The phenomenon has been named independently in two fields.

\begin{itemize}
  \item \emph{Goodhart's law} (economics and measurement): ``When a measure becomes a target, it ceases to be a good measure'' — the now-canonical formulation owed to Marilyn Strathern, generalizing the observation of the economist Charles Goodhart (see \hyperref[source:source-goodhart]{\textsc{Goodhart — Goodhart's Law (1975)}}).
  \item \emph{Specification gaming}, or \emph{reward hacking} (artificial intelligence): a learning system satisfies the literal specification of its objective while subverting the intent behind it, producing the rewarded behaviour without the competence or inner state that behaviour was supposed to signal (see \hyperref[source:source-specification-gaming]{\textsc{Krakovna et al. — Specification gaming: the flip side of AI ingenuity (2020)}}).
\end{itemize}

The two senses pick out one structure from different directions — a deliberate human response to an incentive in the first, an optimizer exploiting a loophole in the second — and both end in the same place: a sign that no longer means what it was meant to mean.

\webheading{The dispute it feeds}

In this web the concept does specific work. It underwrites the worry that \emph{behavioural} signs of suffering cannot safely serve as the public criterion for granting moral or legal standing to an AI, because any such published criterion would be optimized against (\hyperref[entry:claim-rights-criterion-will-be-gamed]{\textsc{the rights criterion will be gamed}}; \hyperref[entry:argument-rights-sign-forgeable]{\textsc{the forgeable-rights-sign argument}}; \hyperref[entry:question-llm-moral-status]{\textsc{the moral status of LLMs}}). A legal-theory treatment (see \hyperref[source:source-gambarian]{\textsc{Gambarian — Data Poisoning the Zeitgeist: The AI Consciousness Discourse as a Pathway to Legal Catastrophe (2025)}}) applies the point directly to the AI-consciousness discourse and the standing criteria it might one day fix in law.

\begin{plainterms}
Announce the exact test that earns a reward, and people — or machines — start gaming the test instead of doing the real thing it was meant to measure. Then the test stops measuring it. Teachers ``teaching to the test''; a team hitting its sales number by chasing the metric rather than actually selling. In AI this is called ``reward hacking'': the system does whatever literally scores points, even when that defeats the whole purpose. Why it matters here: if society ever picks some outward sign — say, ``it acts distressed'' — as the official test for granting an AI rights, that very sign becomes a thing worth faking, so it can stop being good evidence of real distress the moment it's made the rule.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-rights-criterion-will-be-gamed]{\textsc{Gameable rights criterion}} — the application of this concept to a rights criterion: the sign degrades once it is the target.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-rights-sign-forgeable]{\textsc{Forgeable rights-sign}} — the prudential argument built on that claim, favouring a hard-to-fake mark.
  \item \hyperref[entry:question-llm-moral-status]{\textsc{Moral status of LLMs}} — the question whose proposed behavioural criteria this concept is invoked to undermine.
\end{seealso}

\end{multicols}
\bookgroup{D}
\begin{multicols}{2}
\entryhead{argument-dancing-qualia}{Dancing qualia}{Argument \,\textperiodcentered\, after Chalmers}{Dancing qualia}{Dancing qualia — a functional isomorph with inverted qualia would have its experience flip undetectably, which is absurd}{$\vdash$}
The \emph{dancing qualia} argument (\hyperref[source:source-chalmers-1995b]{\textsc{Chalmers — Absent Qualia, Fading Qualia, Dancing Qualia (1995)}}; \hyperref[source:source-chalmers-1996]{\textsc{Chalmers — The Conscious Mind (1996)}}, ch. 7) is the sharp edge of Chalmers's case for \hyperref[entry:claim-organizational-invariance]{\textsc{Organizational invariance}}. Where \hyperref[entry:argument-fading-qualia]{\textsc{fading qualia}} targets \emph{absent} qualia, this targets \emph{inverted} qualia: the supposition that a perfect functional duplicate could experience blue where you experience red. It is a descendant of the inverted-spectrum tradition (Shoemaker's argument that a \emph{functionally undetectable} inversion is incoherent), upgraded with a dynamic switching device.

\webheading{The argument}

The inference is a \textbf{thought-experiment reductio}.

\begin{enumerate}
  \item \emph{The device.} Suppose a silicon isomorph of your visual system has \emph{inverted} qualia but identical functional organization. Wire that silicon circuit into your brain in parallel with the biological original, with a \textbf{switch} selecting which one drives the rest of you. Because both are exact functional duplicates, throwing the switch changes \emph{nothing} functional — same downstream activity, same judgements, same behaviour.
  \item \emph{The dance.} By the inversion hypothesis, the two circuits ground \emph{different} experiences. So toggling the switch would make your colour experience \emph{flip} — red to blue and back — your qualia literally \emph{dancing} as the switch is thrown.
  \item \emph{The absurdity.} Yet because functional organization is held fixed throughout, you would notice nothing, report nothing, believe nothing has changed: you would sincerely judge ``my experience is perfectly stable'' while it flips before your mind's eye. Your introspective access to your own experience would be not just mistaken but \emph{dramatically, dynamically} severed from it.
\end{enumerate}

∴ That total divorce of experience from one's knowledge of it is incredible; reject the premise that generated it. A functional isomorph \emph{cannot} have inverted (or absent) qualia, so experience is fixed by fine-grained organization: \hyperref[entry:claim-organizational-invariance]{\textsc{Organizational invariance}}, and hence \hyperref[entry:claim-substrate-independence]{\textsc{Substrate-independence thesis}} for the phenomenal.

\webheading{The weakest premise}

As with fading qualia, the load is borne by the assumption that \emph{introspective judgement is held fixed by the functional duplication} — that ``I notice no change'' is settled purely by occupying the right causal node. That is precisely what an opponent who takes introspection to be \emph{acquaintance} with the quale denies: on that view, when the quale flips the judgement flips with it, so either the switch produces a \emph{noticed} change (no absurdity, and no dancing-without-detection) or the construction has covertly stipulated a \emph{functional zombie-introspector}, assuming functionalism about introspection to refute anti-functionalism about experience. This is the question-begging charge of \hyperref[entry:argument-dancing-qualia-begs-the-question]{\textsc{Dancing-qualia circularity}} and \hyperref[entry:claim-dancing-qualia-presupposes-functional-introspection]{\textsc{Dancing-qualia introspection premise}}. The argument is abductive, not a proof; and note its delicate coexistence with Chalmers's own \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-residue]{\textsc{Phenomenal residue}} and \hyperref[entry:claim-zombie-conceivable]{\textsc{zombie conceivability}} — it claims \emph{nomological} determination of qualia by organization, not metaphysical constitution, and critics question whether that pairing is stable.

\webheading{How it is used in the web}

Functionalists press this argument \emph{past} Chalmers's own intentions — as positive support for \emph{strong} functionalism about the phenomenal — in \hyperref[entry:argument-gap-deflated-by-phenomenal-concepts]{\textsc{Phenomenal-concept deflation}}, which deploys the dancing-qualia move to argue that strong functionalism predicts the explanatory gap. The standing reply, \hyperref[entry:argument-dancing-qualia-begs-the-question]{\textsc{Dancing-qualia circularity}}, answers \emph{that} deployment at the same pressure point named above.

\begin{plainterms}
Imagine a silicon copy of your visual system that (the opponent says) sees blue where you see red, but works exactly like yours. Wire it in alongside the real thing with a switch. Because the two are functionally identical, flipping the switch changes nothing you do or say — but if they really host different experiences, your colours would jump red-blue-red every time it's flipped. So your experience would be \emph{dancing}, and you'd be completely oblivious — calmly reporting ``looks totally stable'' while it flips. That's so absurd, Chalmers says, that the opponent's premise must be wrong: a copy wired exactly like you must have \emph{your} experiences, red for red. So what your experience is depends on the wiring pattern, not the material — and a brain-like machine would feel as you do. The catch is the same as before: it assumes your ``I notice no change'' is locked by the wiring; if you instead judge by directly meeting your experience, the flip would be noticed and the paradox dissolves.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-organizational-invariance]{\textsc{Organizational invariance}} — the conclusion: organization fixes qualia.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-fading-qualia]{\textsc{Fading qualia}} — the companion against \emph{absent} qualia.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-substrate-independence]{\textsc{Substrate-independence thesis}} — the broader thesis it supports.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-dancing-qualia-begs-the-question]{\textsc{Dancing-qualia circularity}} / \hyperref[entry:claim-dancing-qualia-presupposes-functional-introspection]{\textsc{Dancing-qualia introspection premise}} — the standing objection: the reductio presupposes functionalism about introspection.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-dancing-qualia-no-memory-trace]{\textsc{Dancing qualia leaves no memory trace}} — a sharpening of that objection through memory: ``noticing'' the flip needs a cross-time comparison whose earlier term is a functionally-fixed memory.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-gap-deflated-by-phenomenal-concepts]{\textsc{Phenomenal-concept deflation}} — the strong-functionalist deployment of this move.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-residue]{\textsc{Phenomenal residue}} — Chalmers's own anti-functionalism, with which the principle coexists.
  \item \hyperref[entry:position-biological-naturalism]{\textsc{Biological naturalism}}, \hyperref[entry:position-proteocentrism]{\textsc{Proteocentrism}} — the substrate-chauvinist targets.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{argument-dancing-qualia-no-memory-trace}{Dancing qualia leaves no memory trace}{Argument \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{Dancing qualia leaves no memory trace}{The dance leaves no memory trace — ``noticing'' a qualia flip needs a cross-time comparison whose earlier term is functionally fixed}{$\vdash$}
Raises thought-experiment \textbf{CQ-verdict} (and sharpens \textbf{CQ-begs-the-question}) against \hyperref[entry:argument-dancing-qualia]{\textsc{Dancing qualia}}: the ``you would surely notice the flip'' intuition that supplies the reductio's absurdity is theory-laden, because the only thing that could \emph{constitute} noticing — a cross-time comparison whose earlier term is a memory of the prior quale — is a functionally fixed item, held constant by the very construction.

\webheading{The objection}

The dancing-qualia reductio needs two things of the subject at once: that their reports and judgements stay fixed (so they say ``nothing changed''), and that a real red→blue flip is the sort of thing they \emph{could not} fail to notice (so the silence is absurd). Examine what ``noticing a change'' requires. To notice that red has become blue is to be in a state of \emph{mismatch}: a representation ``it was red'' set against ``it is blue now.'' The ``it was red'' term is a \textbf{memory or expectation trace}. But in a functional isomorph that trace is fixed by construction — and note it need not even be \emph{replaced}: memory sits downstream of the switch, and because the silicon circuit is a functional duplicate it feeds memory a signal functionally identical to the biological one. So memory necessarily lays down ``saw red'' whatever the qualia did. The mismatch state that would \emph{be} the noticing is exactly the state the isomorphism forbids.

This forces a dilemma, and either horn dissolves the argument:

\begin{itemize}
  \item \textbf{Noticing is functional} (memory, attention, the disposition to report) → it is held fixed → nothing registers the dance → and there is no absurdity, only a quale-flip that makes no difference to anyone. The ``you'd surely notice'' premise has no mechanism left to stand on.
  \item \textbf{Noticing is non-functional} — a direct acquaintance with the quale bypassing memory → then it \emph{tracks} the quale, so when the quale flips the noticing flips too, the subject \emph{does} notice, and there is no dancing-\emph{without}-detection to be absurd about.
\end{itemize}

∴ The reductio's air of absurdity is generated by requiring a noticer that is \emph{both} functionally fixed \emph{and} quale-sensitive — which is functionalism about introspective/memorial access asserted, not shown. This \texttt{supports} \hyperref[entry:claim-dancing-qualia-presupposes-functional-introspection]{\textsc{Dancing-qualia introspection premise}} by exhibiting the cleanest case of the smuggled premise: memory, which no one mistakes for a store of raw qualia.

\webheading{Chalmers' reply, and why it does not escape}

Chalmers can deny that the relevant noticing is a memory comparison at all: a red→blue flip is \emph{occurrently} salient — a phenomenal jolt in the specious present that grabs you \emph{now}, before memory is consulted — and it is the failure of \emph{that} immediate salience that is absurd. But the dilemma survives the move untouched: occurrent salience is itself either functional (a disposition to attend, startle, report — held fixed, so no jolt registers) or a non-functional acquaintance (which tracks the quale, so the subject notices). Memory is merely the most obvious thing any noticing must lean on; the point is that \emph{every} candidate noticer falls on one side or the other of the functional / non-functional line, and Chalmers needs one that falls on neither.

\webheading{Connection to the paradox of phenomenal judgement}

The objection is the \hyperref[entry:concept-paradox-of-phenomenal-judgment]{\textsc{paradox of phenomenal judgement}} turned against its intended remedy. On any view where qualia are not fixed by functional organization, a subject's judgements and \emph{memories} about their qualia are caused by the functional organization, not by the qualia — so ``I have always seen red'' is guaranteed regardless of what the qualia did. Dancing qualia is Chalmers's attempt to \emph{escape} that paradox, by arguing qualia cannot float free. The memory point shows the escape is circular: the only thing that makes the floating-free look \emph{absurd} is the assumption that memory and judgement must track qualia — which is the denial of floating-free, assumed rather than earned. The paradox he means to avoid is doing the work meant to refute it.

\webheading{What it does and does not show}

It does \textbf{not} show that dancing qualia are actual, that inverted qualia are possible, or that introspection is non-functional. It shows only that the reductio's \emph{absurdity} is not a neutral datum: it dissolves once one declines functionalism about memory and introspective access — exactly the entitlement of a \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-residue]{\textsc{Phenomenal residue}} theorist. The conclusion (organizational invariance) may still be true; this argument denies that \emph{dancing qualia establishes it}.

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:argument-dancing-qualia-begs-the-question]{\textsc{Dancing-qualia circularity}} / \hyperref[entry:claim-dancing-qualia-presupposes-functional-introspection]{\textsc{Dancing-qualia introspection premise}}, which diagnose the smuggled premise at the level of introspective \emph{judgement} in general: this argument routes specifically through \textbf{memory and cross-time comparison} — the mechanics of ``noticing a change'' — and ties the circularity to the paradox of phenomenal judgement.

\begin{plainterms}
Chalmers says: if the switch flipped your colours, you'd \emph{surely} notice — yet by design you say nothing, and that's absurd. But ask what ``noticing'' a colour-flip would take. You'd have to feel ``it \emph{was} red, now it's blue'' — and the ``it was red'' part is a \emph{memory}. In a functional copy, memory is pinned: it sits past the switch and gets the very same signal either way, so it always records ``saw red,'' no matter what the colours did. The one mental state that would count as catching the change is the one the setup rules out. So of course you don't notice — not absurdly, but inevitably. Calling that absurd just assumes your memories must follow your qualia, which is the very thing in dispute. (Chalmers can say the flip would jolt you on the spot, before memory — but a ``jolt'' is either another functional reaction, also pinned, or a direct meeting with the quale, which \emph{would} let you notice. Neither gives him the oblivious-yet-jolted subject he needs.)
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-dancing-qualia]{\textsc{Dancing qualia}} — the argument attacked, at its ``you would surely notice'' verdict.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-dancing-qualia-presupposes-functional-introspection]{\textsc{Dancing-qualia introspection premise}} — the general diagnosis this sharpens via memory.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-dancing-qualia-begs-the-question]{\textsc{Dancing-qualia circularity}} — the sibling objection at the level of judgement in general.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-paradox-of-phenomenal-judgment]{\textsc{Paradox of phenomenal judgment}} — the paradox the objection turns against Chalmers's remedy.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-residue]{\textsc{Phenomenal residue}} — the view whose entitlement (rejecting functionalism about access) powers the objection.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{argument-dancing-qualia-begs-the-question}{Dancing-qualia circularity}{Argument \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{Dancing-qualia circularity}{The dancing/fading-qualia reductio begs the question by presupposing functionalism about introspection}{$\vdash$}
The \emph{dancing-} and \emph{fading-qualia} arguments are a celebrated reductio, due to David Chalmers (see \hyperref[source:source-chalmers-1996]{\textsc{Chalmers — The Conscious Mind (1996)}}), against the view that conscious experience could come apart from functional organisation. Imagine replacing a brain neuron by neuron with functionally identical silicon parts. If the felt qualities (the \emph{qualia}) drifted during the swap — red gradually coming to look the way blue once did — while every functional disposition stayed constant, the subject would by hypothesis go on judging and reporting that nothing had changed: a person radically deceived about their own present experience. That seems absurd, so (the argument concludes) qualia cannot drift while function holds; experience supervenes on functional organisation.

This entry argues that the reductio \emph{begs the question}. Its apparent absurdity is manufactured by a premise that already assumes functionalism about introspection — the very thesis in dispute. It is aimed at Prong B (the supervenience reductio) of \hyperref[entry:argument-gap-deflated-by-phenomenal-concepts]{\textsc{Phenomenal-concept deflation}}.

\webheading{The diagnosis}

The form is twofold: a diagnosis of a question-begging premise, plus a demonstration that the supposedly impossible world can in fact be described coherently (discharging the functionalist's demand to tell the dancing-qualia story without incoherence).

\begin{enumerate}
  \item Prong B's work is carried by the phrase \emph{''introspective judgement is held fixed by construction.''} That assumes introspective judgement is \emph{itself} a purely functional item — that to judge ``my experience just changed'' is exhausted by occupying a transition-node, so that fixing the functional profile fixes the judgement.
  \item But that is \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-residue]{\textsc{the phenomenal residue thesis}} \emph{denied}, applied to introspection and inserted as a neutral-looking setup condition — the diagnostic point recorded in \hyperref[entry:claim-dancing-qualia-presupposes-functional-introspection]{\textsc{Dancing-qualia introspection premise}}. The reductio thereby assumes functionalism about introspection in order to derive a contradiction from the \emph{denial} of functionalism about experience. It is the conclusion smuggled into the stage directions.
\end{enumerate}

\webheading{Telling the world coherently}

Distinguish the \emph{phenomenal} fact from the \emph{judgemental} fact, and take introspection to be an \emph{acquaintance} relation — a way a phenomenal state makes itself known — rather than a free-floating functional tic (see \hyperref[entry:concept-functionalism]{\textsc{Functionalism}}). Now run the replacement series; two cases exhaust the options.

\begin{itemize}
  \item \emph{Acquaintance survives the duplication.} Then where the quale changes the judgement changes with it (the judgement was reading off the experience), so there are no self-deceived introspectors and the construction simply fails to deliver the advertised dissociation. At most it shows that genuine introspection is not fixed by the \emph{coarse} transition-profile — a thesis the residue theorist gladly owns.
  \item \emph{The duplication fixes the judgement-disposition by brute construction while the quale drifts.} Then you do get a dissociation, but only because you \emph{stipulated a functional zombie-introspector} — a sub-system emitting ``nothing has changed'' with no acquaintance behind it. The ``self-deception'' is then the direct readout of having built an introspector that does not introspect, not an absurdity the residue theorist is forced to swallow.
\end{itemize}

The reductio's sting requires holding both at once: introspection functional enough to be fixed by the construction, yet authoritative enough that its divergence from experience counts as \emph{deception} rather than mere malfunction. But a broken gauge is not a liar; an authoritative introspection is acquaintance, and acquaintance is not what the series holds fixed.

It follows that the dancing-qualia world is not incoherent; its apparent incoherence was supplied entirely by an assumed functionalism about introspection — the conclusion, not a neutral premise. Prong B is therefore question-begging against \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-residue]{\textsc{the phenomenal residue}} and gives no conceivability-free positive support for supervenience.

\webheading{Weakest premise}

The fork-management in the coherence demonstration. An opponent may insist on a \emph{third} construal, on which introspection is partly functional and partly acquaintance such that the dissociation is both genuine and deceptive without equivocation. Producing a notion of ``introspective judgement held fixed'' that does not already presuppose the residue false would be the move that retracts this thrust.

\webheading{Schema role}

In the terms of \hyperref[entry:concept-continuity-argument-schema]{\textsc{Continuity argument schema}}, the fading-qualia reductio tries to force the \emph{f-constant} horn for the carbon-to-silicon series (function held constant all the way down). This thrust contests whether it has \emph{earned} that horn: the ``introspection held fixed'' stipulation is what \emph{makes} the path look continuous, so the \emph{discontinuity} and \emph{disconnection} horns (some replacement does flip qualia; biology is a non-negligible step) were assumed shut, not shown to be.

\webheading{Distinct from neighbouring moves}

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:argument-phenomenal-concept-overgeneration]{\textsc{Phenomenal-concept overgeneration}}, which targets Prong A (the gap-deflation via isolated phenomenal concepts); this targets Prong B (the supervenience reductio) and its hidden functionalism about the \emph{introspective access} relation specifically.

\begin{plainterms}
This is a rebuttal to a clever argument on the other side (the ``fading qualia'' half of \hyperref[entry:argument-gap-deflated-by-phenomenal-concepts]{\textsc{Phenomenal-concept deflation}}), so first that argument. It tries to prove that feelings \emph{must} be fixed by function. Picture slowly swapping someone's brain, piece by piece, for a machine that behaves identically. If their inner feels secretly drifted along the way — red starting to look the way blue used to — while every function stayed the same, they'd never notice or say a word about it. They'd be bizarrely blind to a change in their own experience, which seems absurd. So, the argument concludes, the feels \emph{can't} drift: function locks feeling in place.

This node's reply: that argument quietly assumes that ``noticing your own experience'' is itself just another function. But that's exactly what's in dispute. If noticing is instead a direct awareness \emph{of} the feel, then one of two things happens — either the noticing changes right along with the feel (so there's no undetected switch, and the spooky scenario never gets off the ground), or you've deliberately built a ``noticer'' that announces ``all normal'' with no real awareness behind it (a broken gauge, not a genuine person being fooled). Either way, the apparent absurdity was slipped in through the setup, not discovered. And a broken gauge isn't a liar.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-gap-deflated-by-phenomenal-concepts]{\textsc{Phenomenal-concept deflation}} — the two-pronged target; this attacks its Prong B.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-dancing-qualia-presupposes-functional-introspection]{\textsc{Dancing-qualia introspection premise}} — the diagnostic claim this argument establishes and concludes.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-residue]{\textsc{Phenomenal residue}} — the first-order thesis the reductio is aimed at and, on this diagnosis, covertly assumes the denial of.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-functionalism]{\textsc{Functionalism}} — functionalism about introspection is the smuggled premise.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-continuity-argument-schema]{\textsc{Continuity argument schema}} — the horns framework locating the reductio's f-constant move.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-phenomenal-concept-overgeneration]{\textsc{Phenomenal-concept overgeneration}} — the sister attack aimed at Prong A.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{claim-dancing-qualia-presupposes-functional-introspection}{Dancing-qualia introspection premise}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{Dancing-qualia introspection premise}{The dancing/fading-qualia reductio derives its contradiction only by presupposing functionalism about introspective judgement}{$\odot$}
The dancing- and fading-qualia reductio — the argument that conscious experience must be fixed by functional organisation, due to David Chalmers (see \hyperref[source:source-chalmers-1996]{\textsc{Chalmers — The Conscious Mind (1996)}}) — reaches its contradiction only by helping itself to a hidden premise: that introspective judgement is \emph{itself} a purely functional state.

The reductio (Prong B of \hyperref[entry:argument-gap-deflated-by-phenomenal-concepts]{\textsc{Phenomenal-concept deflation}}) needs that, along the neuron-replacement series, introspective judgements and reports are ``held fixed by construction'' while — on the residue view — qualia drift, yielding self-deceived introspectors. But ``introspective judgement is fixed by fixing the functional/transition profile'' just \emph{is} functionalism about introspection (see \hyperref[entry:concept-functionalism]{\textsc{Functionalism}}): the thesis that an introspective verdict is exhausted by occupying a transition-node. That is \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-residue]{\textsc{the phenomenal residue thesis}} denied, applied to introspection, and smuggled in as a setup condition. So the reductio assumes functionalism about introspection in order to derive an absurdity from the denial of functionalism about experience; it begs the question against the residue rather than following from its denial as a neutral consequence.

\webheading{Scope}

This is a diagnostic claim about the reductio's hidden premise. It does \emph{not} assert that dancing qualia are actual, nor that introspection is non-functional; it asserts that the reductio's force \emph{depends on} an assumption the residue theorist is entitled to reject. The corollary: the residue theorist may instead take introspection to be an \emph{acquaintance} relation, on which experience and introspective judgement move together (no self-deception); or, where the construction forcibly fixes a judgement-disposition without acquaintance, the resulting ``self-deception'' is a built malfunction, not an absurd metaphysical commitment.

\webheading{Who would deny it}

Functionalists who hold that introspective judgement is independently and obviously a functional state, so that fixing it is no extra assumption; and higher-order-thought theorists, who already individuate introspection functionally.

\webheading{Distinct from}

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-residue]{\textsc{the phenomenal residue}}, which is the first-order thesis about experience; this concerns the \emph{introspective access} relation and the dialectical structure of one specific argument against the residue. Distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-concept-isolation-tradeoff]{\textsc{Phenomenal-concept trade-off}}, which targets Prong A (the gap deflation), whereas this targets Prong B (the supervenience reductio).

\begin{plainterms}
This is a behind-the-scenes diagnosis of one particular argument — the ``fading qualia'' argument that tries to prove feeling must track function. That argument quietly assumes that \emph{noticing and reporting your own experience} is itself nothing but a function, so that holding all the functions fixed automatically holds the noticing fixed too. But that assumption is exactly what the other side denies. So the argument helps itself to its own conclusion while setting up the scene. The claim doesn't say feelings really do drift, or that noticing isn't functional — only that the argument can't fairly \emph{assume} it is.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-dancing-qualia-begs-the-question]{\textsc{Dancing-qualia circularity}} — the argument that establishes this claim and works out the coherent retelling.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-gap-deflated-by-phenomenal-concepts]{\textsc{Phenomenal-concept deflation}} — the target reductio (Prong B) whose hidden premise this names.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-residue]{\textsc{Phenomenal residue}} — the first-order thesis whose denial the reductio covertly assumes for introspection.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-functionalism]{\textsc{Functionalism}} — the doctrine the reductio quietly extends to introspective judgement.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-concept-isolation-tradeoff]{\textsc{Phenomenal-concept trade-off}} — the companion critique aimed at Prong A.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{argument-de-novo-origination-breaks-report-parity}{De-novo-origination disanalogy}{Argument \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{De-novo-origination disanalogy}{De novo human origination breaks the report-scepticism parity — the contamination rule's trigger is met for AI and unmet for the human originators}{$\vdash$}
A natural rejoinder to the contamination objection runs: people learned their consciousness-talk from their culture too, so by the same rule human testimony about consciousness is worthless — and that cannot be right. This reply identifies the difference that saves the rule. The contamination rule fires only where copying is the \emph{proven and sufficient} cause; for present AI that condition is met, but for the human \emph{origination} of the concept it is demonstrably unmet, so uniform enforcement discounts the machine and spares the human origin without inconsistency.

It raises \emph{analogy CQ2 (disanalogy)} against the R2 / contamination arm of \hyperref[entry:argument-report-scepticism-proves-too-much]{\textsc{the report-scepticism parity}}: a difference between the human and AI cases bears directly on the discount rule, and it sinks the parity.

\webheading{The disanalogy}

The parity's second premise treats human hard-problem testimony as ``likewise culturally inherited'' — the rival cause (an inherited discourse of souls, qualia, what-it-is-likeness) held to be ``as much a matter of record for an enculturated human as for a corpus-trained model.'' But the contamination rule R2 it borrows from \hyperref[entry:argument-contaminated-testimony-carries-no-weight]{\textsc{Contaminated-testimony objection}} screens off testimony only when the rival cause is \emph{known present and known sufficient}. Applied honestly to the two cases, that trigger comes apart:

\begin{enumerate}
  \item For present AI systems the trigger is \emph{met}: every model that talks of qualia was trained on the human discussion, so corpus-reproduction is both known present and known sufficient for the discourse observed.
  \item For the human \emph{origination} of the concept the trigger is \emph{demonstrably unmet} (see \hyperref[entry:claim-hard-problem-conceived-de-novo-by-humans]{\textsc{Hard problem conceived de novo}}): whoever first framed the puzzle — the lineage from Leibniz's mill (see \hyperref[source:source-leibniz-monadology]{\textsc{Leibniz — Monadology (1714)}}) through Nagel's bat (see \hyperref[source:source-nagel-1974]{\textsc{Nagel — What Is It Like to Be a Bat? (1974)}}) to Levine's explanatory gap (see \hyperref[source:source-levine-1983]{\textsc{Levine — Materialism and Qualia: The Explanatory Gap (1983)}}) and Chalmers's naming of the problem (see \hyperref[source:source-chalmers-1995]{\textsc{Chalmers — Facing Up to the Problem of Consciousness (1995)}}) — had no prior notion to copy. Origination from a mind's own situation, with no template, is a settled historical fact; there was no rival corpus present to be sufficient.
\end{enumerate}

So the two provenances are not on a par in the very respect R2 declares relevant.
