For the human concept origination is \emph{proven}; for AI only imitation is proven and origination is \emph{untested} — the consciousness-naive experiment of \hyperref[entry:question-llm-own-explanatory-gap]{\textsc{LLM's own explanatory gap}} being precisely the test of whether a discourse-free mind regenerates it. The rule may therefore be enforced uniformly — discount corpus-echo wherever corpus-echo is the proven and sufficient cause — \emph{without} discounting the human datum, because the human case is not, at its root, a case of proven corpus-echo. Uniform enforcement does not ``prove too much''; it sorts by a fact about provenance, not by substrate.

\webheading{Reply to the sufficiency-by-neutrality move}

The parity's wielder may protest that AI-sufficiency was established not by auditing the training objective but by the neutrality premise of \hyperref[entry:argument-self-model-prediction-phenomenality-neutral]{\textsc{Phenomenality-neutral prediction}} — ``the machinery suffices for the reports with or without feelings'' — and that the same premise establishes sufficiency for humans. This conflates two rival causes. The neutrality premise concerns the \emph{architecture} (R1): it makes the report-generating machinery sufficient symmetrically, for both. R2 concerns a \emph{different} rival cause — the inherited discourse, the corpus copied. R1-sufficiency does not transfer to R2-sufficiency. The de novo fact bears only on R2, and on R2 the asymmetry is total. (Whether the architectural prediction screens off the \emph{human} datum too — the R1 arm — is answered separately, by \hyperref[entry:argument-feel-is-the-watching-not-an-unwatched-difference]{\textsc{the first-person observation reply}}: the human datum was never the third-person report.)

\webheading{Weakest premise}

That origination-by-some licenses non-discount-of-reports-by-all. It does not, and the argument does not claim it: contemporary ordinary human reports \emph{are} enculturated, and this argument leaves them as contaminated as anyone pleases. What it secures is narrower and sufficient — that the human and AI \emph{provenances} are asymmetric, which blocks the tu-quoque (``why does marination merely season yours?''). It breaks parity; it does not purify the contemporary witness.

\webheading{Distinct from neighbouring moves}

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:argument-access-symmetry-rebuttal]{\textsc{Access-symmetry rebuttal}} (which concerns moral-status asymmetry from reciprocal non-acquaintance) and from \hyperref[entry:argument-feel-is-the-watching-not-an-unwatched-difference]{\textsc{Feel as first-person observation}} (which answers the R1 / phenomenality-neutral arm by relocating the human datum to first-person acquaintance); this answers the R2 / contamination arm by a historical disanalogy of provenance.

\begin{plainterms}
One objection in this web says: a chatbot read all of human philosophy, so its consciousness-talk is just echo — throw it out (see \hyperref[entry:argument-contaminated-testimony-carries-no-weight]{\textsc{Contaminated-testimony objection}}). The reply under attack here (see \hyperref[entry:argument-report-scepticism-proves-too-much]{\textsc{Report-scepticism over-reach}}) shot back: people also learned their consciousness-talk from their culture, so by the same rule human testimony is worthless too — and that can't be right. This argument finds the difference that saves the rule. The throw-it-out rule only fires when copying is the \emph{proven} cause. For AI today, copying is proven — every model that talks this way was trained on our books. For humans, the opposite is proven: somebody invented the puzzle with no book to copy (see \hyperref[entry:claim-hard-problem-conceived-de-novo-by-humans]{\textsc{Hard problem conceived de novo}}). So the same rule, applied evenly, discounts the AI (copying proven) and spares the human origin (copying impossible — there was nothing yet to copy). That isn't one rule for machines and another for people; it's one rule meeting two different sets of facts.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-report-scepticism-proves-too-much]{\textsc{Report-scepticism over-reach}} — the parity reply this attacks on its R2 arm.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-contaminated-testimony-carries-no-weight]{\textsc{Contaminated-testimony objection}} — the contamination rule (R2) whose trigger condition this argument applies honestly to both cases.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-hard-problem-conceived-de-novo-by-humans]{\textsc{Hard problem conceived de novo}} — the premise: human origination with no template.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-self-model-prediction-phenomenality-neutral]{\textsc{Phenomenality-neutral prediction}} — the neutrality premise (R1) the sufficiency-by-neutrality move appeals to.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-feel-is-the-watching-not-an-unwatched-difference]{\textsc{Feel as first-person observation}} — the companion reply that handles the R1 arm.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-hard-problem]{\textsc{Hard problem}} — the concept whose provenance is at issue.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-introspective-gap-varieties]{\textsc{Varieties of introspective explanatory gap}} — the gap taxonomy R2's contamination variety belongs to.
  \item \hyperref[entry:question-llm-own-explanatory-gap]{\textsc{LLM's own explanatory gap}} — the experiment that would test AI origination.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{argument-decomposition-indeterminacy}{Decomposition-indeterminacy objection}{Argument \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{Decomposition-indeterminacy objection}{The decomposition-indeterminacy objection — determinate consciousness cannot rest on observer-relative function}{$\vdash$}
The \emph{decomposition-indeterminacy objection} argues that consciousness cannot be constituted by functional organisation alone, because organisation is not determinate enough to do the job. A single physical system can be carved into ``inputs, outputs, and internal states'' in many equally admissible ways, and the bare physics crowns none of them as \emph{the} organisation; yet there is a perfectly determinate fact about what a subject is consciously feeling. If experience were nothing but organisation, it would inherit that organisation's indeterminacy — and it does not. So a functionalist about consciousness must either supply an observer-independent fact that fixes the system's real decomposition, or give up the claim that determinate experience is built out of function.

\webheading{The argument}

The move is a \emph{reductio} carrying a transcendental demand for an invariant:

\begin{enumerate}
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-functional-decomposition-relative]{\textsc{Decomposition is observer-relative}}: a physical system admits many equally admissible carvings into inputs, outputs, and internal states, and the physics alone fixes none as \emph{the} functional organisation.
  \item If phenomenal consciousness were constituted by functional organisation, the conscious facts would be only as determinate as the chosen carving — relative to a decomposition, hence indeterminate absent a privileged one.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-determinacy]{\textsc{Experience is determinate}}: there is a determinate fact about one's phenomenal state, not merely a fact-relative-to-a-decomposition.
\end{enumerate}

Therefore \hyperref[entry:claim-functionalism-owes-decomposition-invariant]{\textsc{functionalism owes a decomposition-invariant}}: an account of consciousness in functional terms must supply an observer-independent invariant that selects the system's real decomposition, or else concede that determinate consciousness is not constituted by function. By the same stroke the objection attacks \hyperref[entry:claim-substrate-independence]{\textsc{substrate independence}} — showing its role-individuation incomplete — and lends independent, indeterminacy-based support to the view that \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-residue]{\textsc{phenomenal states are not exhausted by role}}.

\webheading{Relation to the grain debate}

This is the \hyperref[entry:argument-role-grain-dilemma]{\textsc{role-grain dilemma}} pressed one level deeper. That dilemma assumes a decomposition and disputes its \emph{resolution} — too liberal a grain lets in trivial systems, too chauvinistic a grain excludes genuine minds. The present objection attacks the \emph{decomposition itself} as non-unique, and it draws its force not from any verdict about which systems count as conscious but from the determinacy of consciousness as such.

\webheading{The weakest premise and the standard reply}

Premise (1) is the soft spot. The standard functionalist reply — the dispute between Chalmers and Putnam, \hyperref[source:source-chalmers-1996b]{\textsc{Chalmers — Does a Rock Implement Every Finite-State Automaton? (1996)}} against \hyperref[source:source-putnam-1988]{\textsc{Putnam — Representation and Reality (1988)}} — is that objective \emph{counterfactual/causal} structure is not observer-relative: only carvings that respect genuine causal dependencies count as implementations, and a complex brain has essentially one such structure. If that holds, the demanded invariant already exists — it is the organisational \hyperref[entry:concept-grain]{\textsc{Grain (of role individuation)}} (see \hyperref[entry:claim-role-grain-is-organisational]{\textsc{role grain is organisational}}) — and the radical relativity of (1) is false. This reply is recorded as \hyperref[entry:argument-causal-structure-supplies-invariant]{\textsc{the causal-structure reply}}.

The reply is itself answered twice over: \hyperref[entry:argument-counterfactuals-yield-non-triviality-not-uniqueness]{\textsc{counterfactual constraints buy non-triviality, not uniqueness}}, and \hyperref[entry:argument-relevance-filter-regress]{\textsc{causal structure does not by itself select the mind-relevant relations}}. The objection then narrows to a single question — whether causal structure is \emph{itself} determinate enough to ground determinate consciousness — where the residue theorist presses that even fixed causal structure leaves the phenomenal open, the deeper basis being \hyperref[entry:argument-structure-and-dynamics]{\textsc{the structure-and-dynamics argument}}. A second, harder reply denies premise (3) outright, biting the bullet of phenomenal indeterminacy.

\webheading{A metaphysical, not epistemic, worry}

This is distinct from \hyperref[entry:argument-explanatory-gap]{\textsc{the explanatory gap}}, which is an \emph{epistemic} gap between functional and phenomenal truths, and from the residue worry that role merely \emph{under-specifies} qualia. The complaint here is one of metaphysical \emph{indeterminacy}: function fails to fix a determinate system at all, while consciousness is determinate. Different ground, but the same anti-strong-functionalist conclusion.

\begin{plainterms}
If a mind is just a pattern of parts and their interactions, here's an awkward question: \emph{who decides} where one part stops and the next begins? You can slice the very same physical system into ``inputs, outputs, and inner states'' in many different but equally legitimate ways — the bare physics doesn't crown any one slicing as the real one. Now the squeeze: if your consciousness were nothing but one of those slicings, then what you're feeling would be as wobbly and choice-dependent as the slicing itself. But your experience right now isn't wobbly — there's a definite fact about what you're feeling. So a pattern-only story owes us an answer: what fixes the \emph{one} real way the system is carved up, independent of how some observer chooses to look at it? Until it can name that, it hasn't shown a definite felt experience could be built out of function alone. (The main reply: real cause-and-effect structure isn't a free choice — a brain has essentially one genuine wiring — so the ``one real carving'' the worry demands already exists. Whether that fully settles it is the live dispute.)
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-functionalism]{\textsc{Functionalism}} — the theory under attack: mind as functional organisation, which this objection says cannot deliver determinate experience.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-substrate-independence]{\textsc{Substrate-independence thesis}} — the thesis the objection undercuts, that what a mind is made of doesn't matter so long as the role-structure is preserved.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-residue]{\textsc{Phenomenal residue}} — the companion worry the objection supports by a separate route.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-role-grain-dilemma]{\textsc{Grain dilemma}} — the shallower form of the worry, about a decomposition's resolution rather than its uniqueness.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-causal-structure-supplies-invariant]{\textsc{Causal-structure reply}} — the leading reply, that objective causal structure already provides the demanded invariant.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{claim-deflationism-entails-compatibilism}{Deflation–compatibilism parity}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{Deflation–compatibilism parity}{A deflationist about phenomenal consciousness is committed, by parity of the mirrored dialectic, to compatibilism about free will}{$\odot$}
If the consciousness and free-will debates are mirror images (see \hyperref[entry:claim-consciousness-freewill-mirror]{\textsc{Mirror thesis}}), then positions travel across the mirror as packages: the deflationist who says the zombie's functional duplicate lacks nothing (see \hyperref[entry:position-illusionism]{\textsc{Illusionism}}, \hyperref[entry:position-type-a-materialism]{\textsc{Type-A materialism}}) is saying, in translation, exactly what the \hyperref[entry:position-compatibilism]{\textsc{compatibilist}} says about the volitional zombie (see \hyperref[entry:claim-zombie-dualizes-to-volitional-zombie]{\textsc{Volitional zombie}}). Holding deflationism about phenomenality while rejecting compatibilism is therefore an unstable combination — its holder owes an account of which dictionary entry fails, some relevant disanalogy that breaks the parity at just that point.

The commitment is conditional on the mirror being real, and that conditionality is essential: the parity argument cannot \emph{establish} the mirror it presupposes without circularity. Its force is diagnostic — it converts the duality from a curiosity into a constraint on position-space, linking commitments across two literatures that mostly ignore each other. Who would deny it: anyone who locates a principled disanalogy between phenomenality and origination (for instance, that phenomenality is a datum while origination is a theory), thereby breaking parity exactly where the dictionary needs it.

\begin{plainterms}
Some philosophers dismiss the ``inner glow'' of consciousness as a kind of user illusion — if a robot copy of you acts exactly like you, nothing important is missing. This claim says such philosophers can't consistently \emph{also} insist that free will requires some extra spark beyond ordinary deliberation. The two dismissals are the same move in two debates (see \hyperref[entry:claim-zombie-dualizes-to-volitional-zombie]{\textsc{Volitional zombie}}), so making one while refusing the other requires explaining what breaks the symmetry.
\end{plainterms}

\entryhead{claim-functionalism-owes-decomposition-invariant}{Demand for a decomposition invariant}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{Demand for a decomposition invariant}{Functionalism owes an observer-independent invariant that fixes a system's decomposition}{$\odot$}
If consciousness is constituted by functional organisation, then \hyperref[entry:concept-functionalism]{\textsc{Functionalism}} owes an observer-independent invariant that singles out the \emph{one} correct decomposition of a system. The reasoning is short: consciousness is determinate — there is a fact about what one is experiencing (see \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-determinacy]{\textsc{phenomenal determinacy}}) — whereas functional decomposition is not, since a system can be carved into functional parts in more than one admissible way (see \hyperref[entry:claim-functional-decomposition-relative]{\textsc{the relativity of functional decomposition}}). A determinate phenomenal fact cannot be grounded in a description-relative one, so functionalism about consciousness must name some invariant that fixes the carving independently of any observer.

This is a conditional \emph{burden}, not an outright refutation. It leaves open that the invariant exists; it asserts only that functionalism about consciousness is incomplete until one is named. The leading candidate is the system's objective counterfactual causal structure.

It is denied by a functionalist who holds that the invariant is already in hand — the organisational grain, that is, the system's objective counterfactual causal structure (see \hyperref[entry:claim-role-grain-is-organisational]{\textsc{Organisational role-grain}}, \hyperref[entry:concept-grain]{\textsc{Grain (of role individuation)}}) — so that no new commitment is owed.

This is distinct from the demand of the \hyperref[entry:argument-role-grain-dilemma]{\textsc{role-grain dilemma}} (a grain coarse enough yet fine enough): that asks \emph{where} to set the resolution of a role; this asks \emph{what} fixes the carving at all.

\begin{plainterms}
Here's a bill handed to the ``mind = function'' view. Your conscious experience right now is perfectly definite — there's a fact about what you're feeling. But (the worry goes) there's no single definite way to carve a system into functional parts. If your consciousness were \emph{made of} one such carving, it would be as wobbly as the carving — yet it isn't. So the view owes us an account of what fixes the \emph{one} real organization, independent of how an observer slices things. It's a demand, not a knockout: maybe such a fixer exists (the usual candidate is real cause-and-effect structure) — but until it's named, the view is unfinished.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-determinacy]{\textsc{Phenomenal determinacy}} — the determinacy of experience that the demand turns on.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-functional-decomposition-relative]{\textsc{Observer-relative decomposition}} — the non-uniqueness of carving that creates the gap.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-role-grain-is-organisational]{\textsc{Organisational role-grain}} — the candidate invariant a functionalist offers to discharge the debt.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-role-grain-dilemma]{\textsc{Grain dilemma}} — a related but distinct demand, about \emph{where} to set a role's resolution rather than \emph{what} fixes the carving.
  \item \hyperref[entry:question-substrate-independence]{\textsc{Substrate independence}} — the larger question this claim bears on: whether consciousness depends on substrate or only on organisation.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{argument-dense-china-brain-regress}{Dense China-brain regress}{Argument \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{Dense China-brain regress}{The dense China-brain regress reopens the grain dilemma against the organisational middle}{$\vdash$}
The \emph{dense China-brain regress} is a reply to \hyperref[entry:argument-organisational-grain-dissolves-dilemma]{\textsc{the organisational-grain middle}}. It concedes that \hyperref[entry:claim-role-grain-is-organisational]{\textsc{organisational role-grain}} names a genuine third option that \hyperref[entry:argument-role-grain-dilemma]{\textsc{the original grain dilemma}} overlooked — so ``fine-grained'' need not mean ``biological'' — and then argues that the middle inherits its own version of the dilemma. The construction is a Block-style ``build a better one'' move, in the spirit of Block's Chinese Nation (see \hyperref[source:source-block-1978]{\textsc{Block — Troubles with Functionalism (1978)}}). It is a constructive dilemma.

\begin{enumerate}
  \item The organisational-grain middle excludes the \hyperref[entry:concept-chinese-nation]{\textsc{Chinese Nation}} by saying its residents realise gross input–output \emph{without} the dense intra-system counterfactual dependencies a mind requires.
  \item So build a better one. Let each of the billion citizens implement, by hand and semaphore, one node of the target's counterfactual transition structure, with every conditional honoured: had node \emph{i} been in state \emph{s′}, node \emph{j} would have gone to \emph{t′}, and the drill enforces it. This is, \emph{by construction}, an instance of the transition structure that organisational role-grain demands — realised in people and paper, not a flat lookup map.
  \item \textbf{Horn A — it has a mind.} Then the organisational grain has readmitted precisely the absurdity it was advertised to exclude; horn 1 of the original dilemma is not escaped but \emph{relocated}. The worry was never about lookup tables specifically — lookup was its crudest instance — and the real question is whether \emph{any} purely organisational recipe, however dense, suffices for a mind.
  \item \textbf{Horn B — it lacks a mind despite sharing the transition structure.} Then two systems share the organisational grain yet differ in mind. To say \emph{why}, one must cite something the semaphore-nation lacks — speed, causal density, the right physical glue, continuity of medium — at which point the individuative work is done by features \emph{below} the transition structure, and the grain has slid toward the biological. Chauvinism returns by the back door.
  \item The middle's independent motivation — ``this is the grain at which psychology and computational neuroscience quantify'' — is true but establishes less than is needed. It shows the grain is right for \emph{cognitive} and \emph{intentional} generalisations, territory the residue theorist already concedes, not that it suffices for \emph{phenomenal consciousness}, on which those generalisations are silent. The grain that does the scientific work is precisely the grain at which the science says nothing about what it is like, so it cannot by itself adjudicate the case where what-it-is-like is the whole question.
\end{enumerate}

Therefore the organisational grain either readmits the blockhead (horn A) or smuggles sub-organisational features back in (horn B): \hyperref[entry:claim-organisational-grain-faces-own-grain-dilemma]{\textsc{the organisational grain's own dilemma}}. So the organisational-grain middle has not shown the grain sufficient for the phenomenal, and organisational role-grain does not exclude the blockhead on grounds that are organisational all the way down.

\webheading{Weakest premise}

Premise 2 — whether the semaphore-nation genuinely \emph{is} an instance of the transition structure rather than merely simulating it (the simulation/instantiation distinction). If the organisational-role functionalist can specify an organisational property the dense China-brain provably lacks while genuine realisers have it — and do so without covertly invoking speed, medium, or glue — that is the exclusion ``organisational all the way down'' that would retract this thrust.

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:argument-role-grain-dilemma]{\textsc{the original grain dilemma}}, which ran over coarse-versus-biological-fine grains and was answered by the organisational middle: this attacks \emph{that middle itself}, one level up, via the dense-China-brain construction. Distinct from \hyperref[entry:argument-explanatory-gap]{\textsc{the explanatory gap}}, which grants a fixed role and presses what role leaves out, whereas this attacks the individuation of the proposed role.

\begin{plainterms}
This is the comeback to the ``inner-wiring middle'' — the proposal that a mind is fixed by its inner pattern of cause-and-effect, which supposedly admits silicon while shutting out a mere cheat-sheet (see \hyperref[entry:argument-organisational-grain-dissolves-dilemma]{\textsc{Organisational-grain middle}}). Fine, says the objector — let's actually build a system with exactly that inner wiring and see whether the trap really closed. Take the nation-of-people again, but this time have each person hand-implement one piece of the brain's full cause-and-effect web, every ``if this, then that'' faithfully drilled. By design, this crowd now has the very inner organization the middle option demanded — it is \emph{not} a cheat-sheet. So: does it have a mind? If yes, you've just let an absurd flag-waving crowd be conscious, the very thing the whole exercise was meant to rule out. If no, then two systems share the exact inner wiring yet only one has a mind — and to explain the difference you have to point at something \emph{below} the wiring (speed, physical material, density), which quietly drags you back toward ``only biology will do.'' Either way the middle gets squeezed from both sides once more. And the ``but this is the level brain science uses'' defense only shows the wiring captures \emph{thinking}, not \emph{felt experience}, which is exactly what's in dispute. (Its weak point: maybe the hand-run crowd only \emph{simulates} the wiring rather than truly being an instance of it — the way a computer model of a storm isn't wet.)
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-organisational-grain-dissolves-dilemma]{\textsc{Organisational-grain middle}} — the reply it rebuts.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-role-grain-is-organisational]{\textsc{Organisational role-grain}} — the proposed middle grain it attacks.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-organisational-grain-faces-own-grain-dilemma]{\textsc{Organisational grain's own dilemma}} — the claim it concludes to.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-role-grain-dilemma]{\textsc{Grain dilemma}} — the original dilemma, reinstated here one level up.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-chinese-nation]{\textsc{Chinese nation}} — the population-as-mind construction the dense version sharpens.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-explanatory-gap]{\textsc{Explanatory-gap argument}} — a distinct objection, pressing the phenomenal residue rather than role-individuation.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{argument-deutsch-anti-solipsism}{Deutsch's anti-solipsism}{Argument \,\textperiodcentered\, after Deutsch}{Deutsch's anti-solipsism}{The bad-explanation argument against solipsism — it reconstructs realism and adds only surplus}{$\vdash$}
David Deutsch's reply to the skeptic does not try to \emph{prove} the external world against the solipsist on the solipsist's own evidential terms — a project \hyperref[entry:claim-external-world-unprovable-noncircular]{\textsc{known to be hopeless}}. It takes solipsism at its word, follows it through, and dismisses it as a \emph{bad explanation}. The move belongs to the second anti-skeptical family catalogued under \hyperref[entry:question-external-world-knowledge]{\textsc{Knowing the external world}}, alongside but distinct from \hyperref[entry:argument-retorsion-against-skepticism]{\textsc{retorsion}}.

\webheading{The argument}

The inference is abductive — an inference to the best explanation.

\begin{enumerate}
  \item (see \hyperref[entry:claim-solipsism-parasitic-on-realism]{\textsc{Solipsism is parasitic on realism}}) Solipsism, taken seriously, must reconstruct within the self an entity with the full lawful, autonomous, other-minds-containing structure of an external world; it is thus realism \emph{plus} the surplus claim that this structure is ``really inner.''
  \item (see \hyperref[entry:claim-good-explanation-is-hard-to-vary]{\textsc{Good explanations are hard to vary}}) A good explanation is hard to vary and carries no idle surplus; a theory that reduplicates a rival's content and adds unexplained ideology is, by that measure, the worse explanation.
\end{enumerate}

∴ Realism is the better explanation and solipsism is to be rejected — not refuted by demonstration but dismissed as explanatorily empty. The external world exists (see \hyperref[entry:claim-external-world-exists]{\textsc{The external world exists}}); the solipsist's ``world inside me'' is that very world under a costlier description.

\webheading{The weakest premise}

The pivot is premise (2) — that explanatory virtues like parsimony and being hard-to-vary are guides to \emph{truth}, not merely to convenience. A determined skeptic can grant everything about structure and deny that the simpler, surplus-free theory is thereby more likely \emph{true}; explanatory considerations, the objection runs, presuppose rather than establish that our theorizing tracks a real world. Deutsch's stance is frankly fallibilist (see \hyperref[entry:claim-all-knowledge-is-conjectural]{\textsc{Knowledge is conjectural}}): there is no proof to be had, only the choice between a good explanation and a bad one — and refusing that choice is not a rival epistemology but the abandonment of explanation. Premise (1) is the second pressure point: a solipsist might take the world-structure as \emph{brute}, owing no account of why part of the self behaves like an outer world — which Deutsch counts as simply conceding the bad-explanation charge.

\webheading{How it differs from its neighbour}

Where \hyperref[entry:argument-retorsion-against-skepticism]{\textsc{retorsion}} charges the skeptic with \emph{self-contradiction} (asserting the doubt presupposes its falsity), this argument charges them with \emph{explanatory failure} (the doubt, fully developed, is an idle redescription of realism). The two can be run together but are independent: retorsion bites even on a skeptic who offers no explanation at all; the bad-explanation charge bites even on a skeptic who asserts nothing, merely entertaining solipsism as a hypothesis.

\begin{plainterms}
Deutsch doesn't argue the sceptic out of solipsism by proof — he takes solipsism seriously and watches it collapse. If only your mind exists, you \emph{still} have to explain the huge, lawful, uncontrollable, full-of- other-people part of your experience, and to do that you must rebuild a whole external world inside your head. So solipsism is just realism wearing a disguise and dragging extra baggage. Since the better explanation is the lean one that explains the same things without the disguise, you keep realism and drop solipsism — not because you proved it, but because the alternative explains nothing and costs more.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-external-world-exists]{\textsc{The external world exists}} — the conclusion.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-solipsism-parasitic-on-realism]{\textsc{Solipsism is parasitic on realism}} and \hyperref[entry:claim-good-explanation-is-hard-to-vary]{\textsc{Good explanations are hard to vary}} — the two premises.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-retorsion-against-skepticism]{\textsc{Retorsion against skepticism}} — the sibling anti-skeptical move, by self-refutation rather than explanatory cost.
  \item \hyperref[entry:position-fallibilism]{\textsc{Fallibilism}} — the epistemology that licenses settling the matter by explanation rather than proof.
  \item \hyperref[source:source-deutsch-1997]{\textsc{Deutsch — The Fabric of Reality (1997)}}, \hyperref[source:source-deutsch-2011]{\textsc{Deutsch — The Beginning of Infinity (2011)}} — the sources.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{concept-dialectical-cogency}{Dialectical cogency}{Concept}{Dialectical cogency}{Dialectical cogency (probative force)}{$\blacklozenge$}
\emph{Dialectical cogency} is the property an argument has when it can actually do its persuasive work: rationally move a particular audience from premises that audience can accept to a conclusion it did not already hold. It is a third notion standing alongside two more familiar ones. An argument is \hyperref[entry:concept-validity]{\textsc{valid}} when its form guarantees that true premises yield a true conclusion; it is \hyperref[entry:concept-soundness]{\textsc{sound}} when it is valid \emph{and} its premises are in fact true. Validity concerns form, soundness adds truth — yet neither ensures the argument can \emph{persuade}, because the audience might have no route to knowing the premises, or might be able to reach them only by first granting the conclusion. Cogency, also called \textbf{probative force}, is exactly that further property: the premises are warrantedly and non-circularly \emph{accessible to the relevant audience}.

The crucial structural fact is that \textbf{a sound argument can fail to be cogent.} The standard illustration is the modal ontological argument: grant that ``possibly, a necessary being exists'' entails ``a necessary being exists,'' and the argument may even be sound — but it moves no atheist, because warrant for its possibility premise is unavailable independently of already accepting the theistic conclusion. Validity and truth are in place; probative force is missing. Cogency is therefore \emph{audience-relative} in a way validity and soundness are not: the same sound argument can be cogent against one interlocutor and inert against another who lacks (non-circular) access to a premise.

\webheading{Two senses to keep apart}

The word ``cogent'' carries a second, unrelated technical use, and conflating them causes trouble.

\begin{itemize}
  \item \textbf{Dialectical cogency / probative force} (the sense at work here): an argument is cogent \emph{for an audience} when that audience can come to be justified in its premises without already accepting the conclusion. The condition is stated as a constraint by \hyperref[entry:claim-cogency-requires-accessible-noncircular-warrant]{\textsc{Cogency needs non-circular warrant}} and is the lever of the \hyperref[entry:argument-unwarrantable-zombie-premise]{\textsc{unwarrantable-premise objection}} to the zombie argument.
  \item \textbf{Inductive cogency} (common in informal-logic textbooks): a \emph{non-deductive} argument is ``cogent'' when it is inductively strong and has true premises — the inductive cousin of soundness, a property of the argument in isolation rather than of its power over an audience. This is \emph{not} the notion the cogency constraint employs.
\end{itemize}

\webheading{What it bears on}

Cogency is the concept that lets the web distinguish \emph{attacking an argument's truth} from \emph{attacking its ability to compel}. An objection can grant every premise and the validity of the inference, yet deny probative force by showing that warrant for a premise is inaccessible or question-begging — an \textbf{undercutting} rather than a \textbf{rebutting} defeater. That is the precise shape of the unwarrantable-premise objection, and of the classic complaint against the modal ontological argument it models itself on.

\begin{plainterms}
Three different things can be wrong, or right, with an argument. It can be \emph{valid} (the logic is airtight), it can be \emph{sound} (valid and the starting claims are actually true), and it can be \emph{cogent} — able to win over someone who doesn't yet agree. The surprising part is that the third can fail even when the first two hold. If the only way your opponent could ever become sure of your starting point is to already believe your conclusion, your argument might be flawless logic with true premises and \emph{still} persuade no one. That ``can it actually move the other side?'' property is cogency, and it's the hinge on which the \hyperref[entry:argument-unwarrantable-zombie-premise]{\textsc{objection}} to the zombie argument turns.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-validity]{\textsc{Validity}} — the form-level virtue; the first of the three escalating standards.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-soundness]{\textsc{Soundness}} — validity plus true premises; the virtue a cogent argument has \emph{and exceeds}.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-cogency-requires-accessible-noncircular-warrant]{\textsc{Cogency needs non-circular warrant}} — the constraint that spells out what cogency demands: accessible, non-question-begging warrant for the premises.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-unwarrantable-zombie-premise]{\textsc{Unwarrantable-premise objection}} — the move that uses cogency to grant the zombie argument's premises yet deny it probative force.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-zombie-premise-unwarrantable]{\textsc{Unwarrantable zombie premise}} — its conclusion, an unwarrantability claim that bites only via cogency, not via the truth of any premise.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-intuition-warrant-tracks-trained-domain]{\textsc{Calibration of intuition}} — a neighbouring constraint on argumentative weight, concerning the \emph{calibration} of intuitions rather than the \emph{route} to premise-warrant.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{claim-duality-dictionary-not-theorem}{Dictionary, not theorem}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{Dictionary, not theorem}{Arrow-reversal from the consciousness domain to the free-will domain yields a heuristic dictionary, not a transfer theorem}{$\odot$}
Categorical duality relates a category to its opposite: a theorem about limits in $C$ becomes a theorem about colimits in $C^{op}$. But the free-will problems do not live in an opposite category — they live in the same time-forward causal world as the consciousness problems. Arrow-reversal between the two domains therefore supplies a \emph{dictionary} — a systematic generator of candidate correspondences — and no more: each entry must be verified by hand, and a verified entry's warrant comes from its own content, not from the duality that suggested it.

The claim sets the framework's epistemic ceiling. It does not disparage the dictionary — a generator whose outputs repeatedly land on independently existing positions is tracking something (see \hyperref[entry:claim-consciousness-freewill-mirror]{\textsc{Mirror thesis}}) — it denies only that the categorical apparatus, as it stands, licenses \emph{automatic} transfer. What would upgrade dictionary to theorem is a completed universal property on each pole and a duality involution on the ambient category; the argument that neither is available is \hyperref[entry:argument-no-universal-property]{\textsc{Missing universal property}}. Who would deny it: a defender of \hyperref[entry:claim-consciousness-freewill-strict-duality]{\textsc{Strict duality thesis}} who holds that the mathematics already carries transfer-force.

\entryhead{claim-agent-does-not-settle-undetermined-decisions}{Disappearing agent}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, after Pereboom}{Disappearing agent}{In an undetermined event-causal decision, nothing about the agent settles which option occurs}{$\odot$}
Suppose a decision is undetermined, and everything that leads up to it consists of events involving the agent — her desires, her reasons, her deliberative states — probabilistically causing the outcome. Then, holding the complete prior state fixed, either option can still occur; and since the agent's contribution is exhausted by those prior states, no \emph{further} fact about the agent determines which one does. Whatever tips the balance is not her. The agent, considered as the source who resolves the choice, has disappeared from the story (Pereboom, \hyperref[source:source-pereboom-2014]{\textsc{2014}}).

The claim is the conclusion of the \hyperref[entry:argument-disappearing-agent]{\textsc{disappearing-agent objection}} and the sharpest form of the \emph{luck objection}: adding indeterminism to an event-causal picture adds openness (see \hyperref[entry:claim-alternatives-genuinely-open]{\textsc{Genuine alternatives}}) without adding authorship. It concerns \emph{event-causal} libertarianism specifically — its standard escape is \hyperref[entry:concept-agent-causation]{\textsc{agent causation}}, which posits the agent-as-substance as exactly the settling item the event-causal story lacks. Who denies it: event-causal libertarians such as Kane, for whom being probabilistically caused by the agent's own reasons-states \emph{is} all the settling that agency requires.

\entryhead{argument-disappearing-agent}{Disappearing-agent objection}{Argument \,\textperiodcentered\, after Pereboom}{Disappearing-agent objection}{Pereboom's disappearing-agent objection to event-causal libertarianism}{$\vdash$}
The \textbf{disappearing-agent objection} (Pereboom, \hyperref[source:source-pereboom-2014]{\textsc{2014}}, ch. 2) is the central contemporary attack on event-causal libertarianism — the version of \hyperref[entry:position-libertarianism]{\textsc{Libertarianism (free will)}} on which a free decision is an undetermined event caused by the agent's own mental states, with no exotic metaphysics added. The objection argues that this picture buys indeterminism at the price of the agent: openness arrives, authorship does not.

\textbf{Inference form:} deductive.

\begin{enumerate}
  \item On the event-causal picture, the agent's entire contribution to a decision consists of prior agent-involving events — her desires, beliefs, and deliberative states — which leave the outcome undetermined (the presupposed openness of \hyperref[entry:claim-alternatives-genuinely-open]{\textsc{Genuine alternatives}}).
  \item Given the complete prior state, either option can still occur; so no fact about the agent determines which option does.
  \item An agent \emph{settles} a decision only if some fact about her determines which option occurs; what settles nothing exercises no more control than a bystander to a coin toss.
\end{enumerate}

∴ In an undetermined event-causal decision, nothing about the agent settles which option occurs (see \hyperref[entry:claim-agent-does-not-settle-undetermined-decisions]{\textsc{Disappearing agent}}) — the agent, as originator, disappears.

The objection generalizes the \emph{luck objection} and squeezes the libertarian from the side opposite the Consequence Argument: determinism would make the past settle the choice, but indeterminism, on this picture, lets \emph{nothing} settle it. The standard escapes each carry a price. Retreat to \hyperref[entry:concept-agent-causation]{\textsc{agent causation}} restores a settler by positing substance-causation — and inherits that doctrine's coherence worries, including \hyperref[entry:argument-agent-causation-certificate]{\textsc{the certificate objection}} to its armchair possibility. Alternatively, accept the conclusion and give up libertarian freedom — Pereboom's own route to hard incompatibilism (see \hyperref[entry:position-hard-determinism]{\textsc{Hard determinism}}).

Within the \hyperref[entry:concept-consciousness-freewill-duality]{\textsc{consciousness–free-will duality}}, this objection is the arrow-reversed image of Kim's \hyperref[entry:argument-causal-exclusion]{\textsc{causal-exclusion argument}} (see \hyperref[entry:claim-exclusion-dualizes-to-disappearing-agent]{\textsc{Exclusion–disappearing-agent duality}}): exclusion makes consciousness all sink and no source, the disappearing agent makes the would-be originator all source and no settler — prior causes crowd out the agent just as physical causes crowd out qualia. Concretely, the arrows are relations of causal settling among states: epiphenomenalism's shape is a node with only incoming arrows, libertarian origination's a node with only outgoing ones — literal reversals of one another — and each argument shows its shape unreachable. Exclusion finds every outgoing arrow of the mental already drawn from its physical base; this objection finds no arrow from the agent that settles what her prior states leave open.

\textbf{Weakest premise:} the settling requirement (3). Event-causal libertarians reply that it covertly demands agent-causation — that ``some fact about the agent determines the outcome'' just \emph{is} substance-causal settling under another name — and that control consists in the decision's being non-deviantly caused by the agent's own reasons-states, which premise 1 grants. Whether reasons-responsive probabilistic causation amounts to settling, or merely to influence, is the live crux.

\begin{plainterms}
Some philosophers want free will to work like this: your reasons and desires push toward each option without forcing either, and then — undetermined by anything — one option happens. The objection asks: where are \emph{you} in that final step? Everything about you already did its pushing before the outcome; the last gap is filled by nothing at all. An unforced outcome that nothing about you fixes isn't your act of settling; it's a coin toss inside your head. To really place the chooser at the origin you'd need something stronger — like \hyperref[entry:concept-agent-causation]{\textsc{the view that persons themselves, not events, cause choices}} — or you give up on this kind of freedom.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-agent-does-not-settle-undetermined-decisions]{\textsc{Disappearing agent}} — the conclusion established.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-exclusion-dualizes-to-disappearing-agent]{\textsc{Exclusion–disappearing-agent duality}} — the duality-dictionary entry pairing this objection with causal exclusion.
  \item \hyperref[entry:position-libertarianism]{\textsc{Libertarianism (free will)}} — the family of views attacked (in its event-causal form) and pushed toward agent causation.
  \item \hyperref[entry:position-hard-determinism]{\textsc{Hard determinism}} — where accepting the conclusion leads (hard incompatibilism).
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-agent-causation-certificate]{\textsc{Certificate objection to agent-causation}} — the objection waiting for the agent-causal escape route.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{claim-meaning-constituted-by-dispositions}{Dispositionalism about meaning}{Claim}{Dispositionalism about meaning}{A speaker's meaning is constituted by their dispositions to use the expression}{$\odot$}
On \emph{semantic dispositionalism}, the fact that a speaker means addition by ``+'' — or \emph{cat} by ``cat'' — just is a fact about their \emph{dispositions}: they are disposed to answer with the sum, to apply ``cat'' to cats, and so on. Meaning is not a hidden occurrent state but a pattern of use the speaker is set up to produce. To meet the objection that raw dispositions are finite and shot through with error, the sophisticated version indexes the constituting dispositions to \emph{idealized} or \emph{optimal} conditions — unbounded memory, no performance slips — so that what one means is what one \emph{would} do were those conditions met.

The thesis is that dispositions, so idealized, \emph{constitute} meaning; it does not claim we could list them, only that they fix the fact of the matter. It is defended for logical vocabulary by Fodor (see \hyperref[source:source-fodor-1990]{\textsc{Fodor — A Theory of Content and Other Essays (1990)}}), and is the ``straight solution'' the rule-following sceptic (see \hyperref[source:source-kripke-1982]{\textsc{Kripke — Wittgenstein on Rules and Private Language (1982)}}) sets up and rejects. It is denied by Kripke's Wittgenstein — the \hyperref[entry:argument-kripke-rule-following]{\textsc{Rule-following skeptical argument}} and its conclusion \hyperref[entry:claim-no-constitutive-fact-of-meaning]{\textsc{No constitutive fact of meaning}} — on the ground that even idealized dispositions are descriptive and so miss the \emph{normativity} of meaning.

\begin{plainterms}
What you mean by a word is just what you're set up to do with it: you mean \emph{plus} because you're disposed to give sums. Since your real habits run out and include mistakes, the careful version says: look at what you'd do with perfect memory and no slips. That idealized habit, the dispositionalist says, is what fixes your meaning.
\end{plainterms}

\entryhead{argument-dispositionalist-reply}{Dispositionalist reply}{Argument}{Dispositionalist reply}{The dispositionalist reply: meaning is fixed by idealized dispositions}{$\vdash$}
The \textbf{dispositionalist reply} — the \emph{straight solution} to Kripke's paradox — rejects the sceptical conclusion at its second step. Where \hyperref[entry:argument-kripke-rule-following]{\textsc{Rule-following skeptical argument}} eliminates \emph{dispositions} as a candidate meaning-constituting fact (they are finite, they include mistakes, and they are merely descriptive), the dispositionalist answers that a \emph{suitably refined} dispositional account survives all three charges. If it does, there is after all a fact that constitutes meaning, contrary to \hyperref[entry:claim-no-constitutive-fact-of-meaning]{\textsc{No constitutive fact of meaning}}.

\textbf{Inference form:} rebuttal — an attack on one premise of the sceptical elimination.

\begin{enumerate}
  \item \textbf{Finitude.} Kripke's objection assumes the constituting dispositions are the speaker's \emph{actual, bounded} ones. Index meaning instead to dispositions under \emph{optimal conditions} — unbounded memory and working time — and the finite ceiling that let ``quus'' in disappears.
  \item \textbf{Error.} Mistakes are exactly the deviations optimal conditions screen off, so the idealized dispositions define the \emph{correct} answer, not merely the likely one.
  \item \textbf{Normativity.} What one is disposed to do under optimal conditions is what one \emph{would} do; the dispositionalist argues this recovers the required ``ought'' — to mean plus is to be such that, corrected and idealized, one \emph{would} answer 125.
\end{enumerate}

∴ Meaning is constituted by (idealized) dispositions (see \hyperref[entry:claim-meaning-constituted-by-dispositions]{\textsc{Dispositionalism about meaning}}), and the sceptical \hyperref[entry:claim-no-constitutive-fact-of-meaning]{\textsc{No constitutive fact of meaning}} fails. The account is endorsed for logical vocabulary by Fodor (see \hyperref[source:source-fodor-1990]{\textsc{Fodor — A Theory of Content and Other Essays (1990)}}).

\textbf{Weakest premise:} step 3, the recovery of \emph{normativity}. Kripke's deepest objection is that no \emph{descriptive} fact about what one would do — however idealized — yields what one \emph{ought} to do. Worse, selecting the ``optimal'' conditions that screen off exactly the errors seems to presuppose the very correctness facts the account was meant to constitute, so the idealization threatens to be either circular or to smuggle the norm in from outside. Critics add that the open-ended ceteris paribus clauses and idealizations (unbounded memory) depart from any respectable scientific notion of a disposition, leaving the constituting fact unexplained rather than exhibited.

\begin{plainterms}
Kripke says your habits can't fix what you mean, because real habits run out and are full of slips. The dispositionalist answers: don't look at your sloppy habits, look at what you'd do \emph{if} you had endless memory and never goofed — under those clean conditions you would add, not ``quadd,'' and that settles that you mean plus. The catch: to say which conditions count as ``clean,'' you seem to need to already know the right answers — the very thing in dispute. So the reply works only if it can pin down the ideal conditions without secretly assuming what you mean.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-no-constitutive-fact-of-meaning]{\textsc{No constitutive fact of meaning}} — the sceptical conclusion this reply attacks.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-kripke-rule-following]{\textsc{Rule-following skeptical argument}} — the argument it answers, targeting the dispositions step of the elimination.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-meaning-constituted-by-dispositions]{\textsc{Dispositionalism about meaning}} — the positive thesis it defends.
  \item \hyperref[source:source-fodor-1990]{\textsc{Fodor — A Theory of Content and Other Essays (1990)}} — a defender of semantic dispositionalism, for logical vocabulary.
  \item \hyperref[source:source-kripke-1982]{\textsc{Kripke — Wittgenstein on Rules and Private Language (1982)}} — where the dispositional reply is set out and rejected.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{position-dual-aspect-monism}{Dual-aspect monism}{Position}{Dual-aspect monism}{}{$\bigstar$}
\emph{Dual-aspect monism} holds that there is a single underlying reality that presents two irreducible \emph{aspects} — an inner, mental aspect and an outer, physical aspect — with neither reducible to the other. One thing, seen two ways: the felt and the physical are guaranteed to co-vary because they are aspects of the very same item. The position commits to the claim that \hyperref[entry:claim-reality-two-aspects-mental-physical]{\textsc{reality has two irreducible aspects}}.

\webheading{Historical sources}

The classic statement is Spinoza's: there is one substance, God-or-Nature, that we grasp under the \emph{attribute} of thought as well as under the attribute of extension — two expressions of a single reality (see \hyperref[source:source-spinoza-ethics]{\textsc{Spinoza — Ethics (1677)}}). In the twentieth century the physicist Wolfgang Pauli and the psychologist C. G. Jung developed a dual-aspect view on which the mental and the material are complementary aspects of one underlying reality (see \hyperref[source:source-pauli-jung-1955]{\textsc{Jung \& Pauli — The Interpretation of Nature and the Psyche (1955)}}).

\webheading{How it answers the hard problem}

Dual-aspect monism answers the \hyperref[entry:question-hard-problem]{\textsc{hard problem}} by treating mind and matter as one reality seen two ways. The notorious gap between felt experience and physical process closes because there are not two things to bridge: the felt and the physical are guaranteed to line up as aspects of a single underlying unity.

\webheading{What distinguishes it}

Against \emph{property} dualism, dual-aspect monism makes the mental and the physical \emph{aspects of a prior unity} rather than two kinds of property borne by physical things. Against \hyperref[entry:position-neutral-monism]{\textsc{neutral monism}}, the base is not a neutral stuff of which mind and matter are mere arrangements but a unity that intrinsically bears \emph{both} aspects. And unlike \hyperref[entry:position-russellian-monism]{\textsc{Russellian monism}}, it does not ground the physical \emph{in} the phenomenal but holds the two on a par.

\begin{plainterms}
Dual-aspect monism says there's just one reality, but it always comes with two sides — an ``inside'' (mental) and an ``outside'' (physical) — the way a single coin always has heads and tails, or the way one event can be described in the language of brain cells or in the language of feelings. Mind and matter aren't two substances and aren't reducible to each other; they're two faces of the same underlying thing. That's why the felt and the physical line up so neatly: they were one item all along.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-reality-two-aspects-mental-physical]{\textsc{Two-aspect reality}} — the core commitment.
  \item \hyperref[entry:question-hard-problem]{\textsc{Hard problem (the question)}} — the question it answers by positing one item, two aspects.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-hard-problem]{\textsc{Hard problem}} — the gap it closes.
  \item \hyperref[entry:position-neutral-monism]{\textsc{Neutral monism}} — kin view; mind/matter as arrangements of a neutral base, rather than aspects of a unity that bears both.
  \item \hyperref[entry:position-russellian-monism]{\textsc{Russellian monism}} — rival monism that grounds the physical \emph{in} the phenomenal rather than holding the two on a par.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{position-dualism}{Dualism}{Position}{Dualism}{}{$\bigstar$}
Dualism, in the philosophy of mind, is the view that mind and matter are two fundamentally different kinds of thing, so that even a complete physical account of the world leaves something out. What it leaves out is phenomenal consciousness, the felt first-person character of experience: on the dualist's view the complete physical and functional facts about a brain do not fix the facts about what that brain feels, or whether it feels anything at all (see \hyperref[entry:claim-consciousness-fundamental-nonphysical]{\textsc{Fundamental non-physical consciousness}}). Consciousness is thus a fundamental, non-physical feature of reality rather than something built out of, or guaranteed by, physical arrangement.

\webheading{Two forms}

Dualism comes in two main grades. \emph{Substance dualism}, the historical position associated with Descartes (see \hyperref[source:source-descartes-meditations]{\textsc{Descartes — Meditations on First Philosophy (1641)}}), holds that the mind is a distinct, non-physical substance: a thinking thing that could in principle exist apart from any body. \emph{Property dualism}, also called \emph{naturalistic dualism} in David Chalmers's development of it (see \hyperref[source:source-chalmers-1996]{\textsc{Chalmers — The Conscious Mind (1996)}}), is more modest about the furniture of the world. It posits only one kind of substance but two kinds of property, with phenomenal properties counting as fundamental and irreducible. On this version the felt properties are tied to physical ones by contingent \emph{psychophysical laws}, bridging principles that could have been otherwise, and the conceivability of zombies (physical duplicates of us that lack any inner experience) is taken to show that the physical facts do not necessitate the phenomenal ones, and so that physicalism is false.

Either way, dualism answers the \hyperref[entry:question-hard-problem]{\textsc{hard problem of consciousness}} not by closing the explanatory gap but by accepting it: the gap is genuine and ontological, a real seam in nature rather than a temporary gap in our knowledge.

\webheading{What sets it apart}

Dualism is best located by its neighbours. Unlike physicalism and functionalism, it denies that consciousness \emph{supervenes} on the physical — denies, that is, that fixing every physical fact thereby fixes every fact about experience. Unlike \hyperref[entry:position-panpsychism]{\textsc{Panpsychism (position)}} and \hyperref[entry:position-russellian-monism]{\textsc{Russellian monism}}, which make consciousness the intrinsic nature \emph{of} the physical, dualism makes it genuinely non-physical, something additional to the physical order. And unlike \hyperref[entry:position-proteocentrism]{\textsc{Proteocentrism}}, whose claim is about a particular (carbon) substrate, dualism's claim is about non-physicality as such, not about any chemistry.

The standard pressure point is causal: if the mind is non-physical, how can it make a difference to the body? This is the \emph{epiphenomenalism} worry (see \hyperref[entry:position-epiphenomenalism]{\textsc{Epiphenomenalism}}) — that a non-physical mind seems either to violate the causal closure of the physical or to do no causal work at all. It is largely this worry that pushes property dualists like Chalmers toward \hyperref[entry:position-russellian-monism]{\textsc{Russellian monism}}, on which consciousness is relocated inside the physical as its intrinsic nature.

\begin{plainterms}
Dualism says mind and matter are two different kinds of thing: even a complete physics-and-wiring story about your brain would leave out the felt, conscious part, because that part isn't physical at all. The old version (Descartes) makes the mind a separate \emph{substance}; the modern version makes \emph{feelings} extra fundamental ingredients that the laws of nature attach to certain brains. Its famous headache: if the mind isn't physical, how does it manage to push the body around?
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-consciousness-fundamental-nonphysical]{\textsc{Fundamental non-physical consciousness}} — the core commitment dualism endorses, that the physical facts do not fix the facts of experience.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-residue]{\textsc{Phenomenal residue}} — that experience is not exhausted by causal or functional role; the residue dualism treats as fundamental.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-hard-problem]{\textsc{Hard problem}} — the explanatory gap dualism answers by declaring it unbridgeable.
  \item \hyperref[entry:position-russellian-monism]{\textsc{Russellian monism}} — the view consciousness's causal worry pushes property dualists toward, relocating it as the intrinsic nature of the physical.
  \item \hyperref[entry:position-panpsychism]{\textsc{Panpsychism (position)}} — a neighbour that also rejects reductive physicalism but makes consciousness pervade the physical rather than stand outside it.
  \item \hyperref[entry:position-proteocentrism]{\textsc{Proteocentrism}} — a rival that grounds consciousness in a carbon substrate rather than in non-physicality.
  \item \hyperref[entry:position-epiphenomenalism]{\textsc{Epiphenomenalism}} — the horn of dualism that accepts the mind's causal inefficacy rather than positing interaction.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{argument-consciousness-freewill-duality}{Duality argument}{Argument \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{Duality argument}{The argument from mirrored dialectics — arrow-reversal maps the consciousness debate onto the free-will debate}{$\vdash$}
The master argument of the \hyperref[entry:concept-consciousness-freewill-duality]{\textsc{consciousness–free-will duality}}: the dictionary that arrow-reversal generates keeps landing on positions and problems that already exist independently in the other literature, and the best explanation of that regularity is that the two debates really are one dialectic mirror-imaged along the perception–action axis.

\textbf{Inference form:} abductive — an argument from sign. Each independently rediscovered correspondence is a \emph{sign}; the mirror is the state of affairs the signs indicate.

\begin{enumerate}
  \item Conscious content is product-like (see \hyperref[entry:claim-conscious-content-is-product-like]{\textsc{Product-like experience}}) and choice content is coproduct-like (see \hyperref[entry:claim-action-content-is-coproduct-like]{\textsc{Coproduct-like choice}}) — so the two domains occupy the poles of an exact mathematical duality (see \hyperref[entry:concept-categorical-duality]{\textsc{Categorical duality}}), refined at the level of maps by \hyperref[entry:claim-attention-is-dual-of-will]{\textsc{Attention–volition duality}}.
  \item Applying arrow-reversal to standard consciousness arguments yields, without free parameters, moves the free-will literature already owns under its own names: exclusion becomes the disappearing agent (see \hyperref[entry:claim-exclusion-dualizes-to-disappearing-agent]{\textsc{Exclusion–disappearing-agent duality}}); the zombie becomes the volitional zombie, deflationist reply and all (see \hyperref[entry:claim-zombie-dualizes-to-volitional-zombie]{\textsc{Volitional zombie}}); the unity of consciousness becomes the unity of agency (see \hyperref[entry:claim-unity-dualizes-to-unity-of-agency]{\textsc{Unity–unity duality}}); the combination problem becomes the composition of will (see \hyperref[entry:claim-combination-dualizes-to-composition-of-will]{\textsc{Combination–composition duality}}).
  \item In at least one case the dictionary transfers \emph{machinery}, not analogy: the Π₁ conceivability-certificate objection applies verbatim to libertarian agent-causation (\hyperref[entry:claim-agent-causation-conceivability-uncertified]{\textsc{Uncertified agent-causal conceivability}}, via \hyperref[entry:argument-agent-causation-certificate]{\textsc{Certificate objection to agent-causation}}).
  \item Independent rediscovery on this scale is unlikely if the mirroring tracked nothing; it is expected if the two debates share structure along the perception–action axis.
\end{enumerate}

∴ The consciousness and free-will debates are one dialectic mirror-imaged along the perception–action axis (see \hyperref[entry:claim-consciousness-freewill-mirror]{\textsc{Mirror thesis}}).

\textbf{Weakest premise:} step 4, the correlation premise of the sign pattern. The correspondences could reflect not a mirror specific to perception and action but the \emph{generic} shape of any realist-versus-deflationist stalemate — every hard philosophical dispute splits into a further-fact camp and a deflationary camp, so translated entries may land on existing positions merely because the target literature, like all literatures, has both camps (the sign pattern's \emph{alternatives} critical question: the sign may indicate a more general state than the one inferred). The framework's reply is the specificity of the entries — postdiction pairing with Libet, Block's distinction pairing with Fischer and Ravizza's, and one verbatim transfer of formal machinery — and its concession is recorded separately: even granting the conclusion, the duality yields a dictionary requiring per-entry verification, not a theorem (see \hyperref[entry:claim-duality-dictionary-not-theorem]{\textsc{Dictionary, not theorem}}).

\begin{plainterms}
Take a famous puzzle about consciousness, flip ``input'' to ``output'' and ``receiving'' to ``initiating,'' and check the free-will literature: surprisingly often, the flipped puzzle is already there, with its own name and its own history. Do this several times and it stops looking like coincidence — the two debates seem to be the same argument running in opposite directions. That regularity is this argument's whole case; the honest fine print is that each translated puzzle still has to be checked by hand (see \hyperref[entry:claim-duality-dictionary-not-theorem]{\textsc{Dictionary, not theorem}}).
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-consciousness-freewill-mirror]{\textsc{Mirror thesis}} — the conclusion established.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-consciousness-freewill-duality]{\textsc{Consciousness–free-will duality}} — the framework entry with the full dictionary.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-agent-causation-certificate]{\textsc{Certificate objection to agent-causation}} — the one entry that transfers machinery rather than analogy, and the strongest single sign.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-consciousness-freewill-strict-duality]{\textsc{Strict duality thesis}} — the stronger reading this argument deliberately does not conclude.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-frankfurt-dualizes-to-ganzfeld]{\textsc{Frankfurt–ganzfeld duality}} — the framework's novel prediction, a future test of the conclusion.
\end{seealso}

\end{multicols}
\bookgroup{E}
\begin{multicols}{2}
\entryhead{position-eliminative-materialism}{Eliminative materialism}{Position \,\textperiodcentered\, after Churchland}{Eliminative materialism}{}{$\bigstar$}
\emph{Eliminative materialism} is the most radical physicalist view of the mind: it holds that our everyday categories of the mental — beliefs, desires, and qualia — are not states science will reduce and keep, but the posits of a \emph{false theory} that a mature neuroscience will discard outright. The everyday framework is called \emph{folk psychology}: the tacit theory ordinary people use to explain and predict behaviour in terms of what someone believes, wants, and feels. Eliminativism treats it as an empirical theory on a par with past failures like phlogiston or the four humours, and, like them, destined for replacement rather than vindication. So strictly speaking, on this view, there are no such states at all (see \hyperref[entry:claim-folk-psychology-is-false-theory]{\textsc{Folk psychology as false theory}}). The position was developed by Paul M. Churchland (see \hyperref[source:source-churchland-1981]{\textsc{Churchland — Eliminative Materialism and the Propositional Attitudes (1981)}}) and Patricia Churchland.

\webheading{How it answers the hard problem}

It answers the \hyperref[entry:question-hard-problem]{\textsc{hard problem}} not by solving it but by dissolving its \emph{explanandum} — the very thing to be explained. If there are no phenomenal states, there is no felt residue whose physical basis needs accounting for. In Chalmers' taxonomy it is therefore a \emph{type-A} materialism (see \hyperref[entry:concept-materialism-types]{\textsc{Type-A / Type-B / Type-C materialism}}). Accordingly it rejects \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-residue]{\textsc{the claim that some phenomenal character is left over}} once function is explained, and it holds that a \hyperref[entry:claim-zombie-conceivable]{\textsc{philosophical zombie}} is, on reflection, not genuinely conceivable.

\webheading{What sets it apart}

Eliminativism is broader and more radical than \hyperref[entry:position-illusionism]{\textsc{Illusionism}}. Illusionism targets \emph{phenomenal} properties specifically and grants that introspection presents our states as feeling some way (it denies only that the feel is real); eliminativism goes further, discarding the \emph{propositional attitudes} — belief and desire — as well. It also contrasts with the realist physicalisms such as \hyperref[entry:position-type-identity]{\textsc{type-identity theory}} and type-B materialism, which \emph{keep} the mental states by reducing or identifying them with physical ones rather than eliminating them.

\begin{plainterms}
The boldest physicalist line: our everyday talk of beliefs, desires, and ``what it feels like'' is a folk \emph{theory} — and, like ``phlogiston'' or ``the four humours,'' a mistaken one that brain science will eventually retire. So the puzzle of consciousness isn't solved so much as \emph{cancelled}: there's no inner feel to explain because, strictly speaking, there are no such things — only neural processes we've been mislabelling.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-folk-psychology-is-false-theory]{\textsc{Folk psychology as false theory}} — the core commitment: the folk mind is a false theory's posits.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-residue]{\textsc{Phenomenal residue}} — rejected: no felt residue is left once function is explained.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-zombie-conceivable]{\textsc{Zombie conceivability}} — rejected: zombies are held inconceivable on reflection.
  \item \hyperref[entry:question-hard-problem]{\textsc{Hard problem (the question)}} — answered by dissolving its explanandum.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-materialism-types]{\textsc{Type-A / Type-B / Type-C materialism}} — the A/B/C scheme, in which eliminativism is a type-A response.
  \item \hyperref[entry:position-illusionism]{\textsc{Illusionism}} — the narrower neighbour, eliminating only phenomenal feel.
  \item \hyperref[entry:position-type-identity]{\textsc{Type-identity theory}} — a realist physicalism that keeps the states by reducing them.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{argument-strong-emergence-acceptable}{Emergence reply}{Argument}{Emergence reply}{The emergence reply — experience can strongly emerge, so no need for fundamental mind}{$\vdash$}
The \emph{emergence reply} is the standard physicalist answer to the \hyperref[entry:argument-genetic-panpsychism]{\textsc{genetic argument}} for \hyperref[entry:concept-panpsychism]{\textsc{Panpsychism}}. The genetic argument runs on a dilemma — either experience is fundamental, or it emerges brutely from the non-experiential, and the latter is dismissed as unintelligible ``magic.'' The reply attacks that dilemma directly: strong emergence of experience is coherent, so there is a third option the argument overlooked, and no need to write mind into the foundations of the world.

\webheading{The argument}

The inference is deductive — an objection that \emph{responds to} the genetic argument by undercutting one of its premises.

\begin{enumerate}
  \item (see \hyperref[entry:claim-strong-emergence-is-acceptable]{\textsc{Acceptability of strong emergence}}) Experience can arise as a genuinely novel, fundamental feature at some level of physical organisation — a brain — without being present, even in proto-form, in that organisation's parts. Strong emergence is coherent, not magic.
  \item If so, the second horn of the genetic argument's dilemma is not the absurdity it was alleged to be.
\end{enumerate}

∴ The dilemma is not exhaustive: one may reject the impossibility of brute emergence (see \hyperref[entry:claim-no-radical-emergence]{\textsc{No radical emergence}}) and hold instead that experience emerges at the right level of organisation — with no commitment to fundamental, ubiquitous mind. The reply \emph{attacks} \hyperref[entry:claim-no-radical-emergence]{\textsc{No radical emergence}} and so undercuts the genetic argument.

\webheading{The weak point}

Everything turns on premise (1). The panpsychist presses back that \emph{strong} emergence of the phenomenal is exactly what is in dispute: calling it ``coherent'' relabels the \hyperref[entry:concept-hard-problem]{\textsc{hard problem}} rather than solving it, since we are still owed an account of \emph{how} the feelingless gives rise to the felt. On this diagnosis the reply does not dissolve the puzzle but trades panpsychism's combination problem for a restatement of the explanatory gap. Which burden is the lighter one to carry is the question that divides the two camps.

\begin{plainterms}
The headline argument \emph{for} panpsychism says: feeling cannot just pop into existence out of stuff that has no feeling at all — that would be magic — so feeling must have been in the ingredients all along. This reply pushes on the word ``magic.'' Nature, the objector says, is full of genuinely new properties that appear when simpler things are organised the right way; maybe experience is another such novelty, emerging in brains without hiding inside atoms. If that is even possible, the panpsychist's ``fundamental mind or magic'' choice is a false one. The panpsychist's comeback: saying experience ``just emerges'' is not an explanation, it is the original puzzle handed back unsolved. So the two sides really dispute which mystery is the smaller one to live with.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-genetic-panpsychism]{\textsc{Genetic argument}} — the argument this reply answers.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-no-radical-emergence]{\textsc{No radical emergence}} — the premise the reply attacks: that brute emergence is impossible.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-strong-emergence-is-acceptable]{\textsc{Acceptability of strong emergence}} — the premise the reply rests on: strong emergence is coherent.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-hard-problem]{\textsc{Hard problem}} — the puzzle the panpsychist says ``strong emergence'' merely relabels.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-panpsychism]{\textsc{Panpsychism}} — the view the reply argues we can do without.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{claim-disanalogies-trace-to-entropy}{Entropy as the sole asymmetry}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{Entropy as the sole asymmetry}{Every disanalogy between the two mirrored problem families traces to the thermodynamic arrow of time}{$\odot$}
Automatic transfer between the two domains would require a duality involution — roughly, a dagger structure making the ambient category equivalent to its opposite. Fundamental physics is CPT-symmetric, so such a structure arguably exists microphysically; what breaks it is thermodynamic. If that is the \emph{only} thing that breaks it, then every genuine disanalogy between the consciousness problems and the free-will problems should trace back to the arrow of time — to entropy — rather than to anything metaphysically deeper. The asymmetry is visible in the mathematics of any Set-like, copy-and-discard causal setting: distributivity holds, $A \times (B + C) \cong (A \times B) + (A \times C)$, but its dual fails — information fans out for free, decisions cannot be un-made (see \hyperref[entry:concept-categorical-duality]{\textsc{Categorical duality}}).

The claim is the \hyperref[entry:concept-consciousness-freewill-duality]{\textsc{duality framework's}} boldest and most falsifiable-flavored commitment: find one disanalogy between the two problem-families that does \emph{not} reduce to temporal asymmetry, and it fails. It is also its least defended — the inference from CPT symmetry of the fundamental dynamics to a dagger structure on the relevant category of \emph{representational states} is a leap across levels that the framework does not close. Who would deny it: anyone who locates the actual/possible seam (see \hyperref[entry:argument-actual-possible-seam]{\textsc{Actual–possible seam}}) or the ambient status of consciousness (see \hyperref[entry:argument-ambient-consciousness-collapse]{\textsc{Ambient-consciousness collapse}}) at a depth below thermodynamics.

\begin{plainterms}
The two problem-families mirror each other imperfectly, and this claim makes a bet about the imperfections: every place the mirror warps, the culprit is time's one-way flow — the same fact that lets you copy a file but not un-send an email — and never anything more mysterious. Physics at the bottom runs the same forwards and backwards; only entropy picks a direction. If someone finds a mismatch between the consciousness puzzles and the free-will puzzles that has nothing to do with time's direction, the bet is lost.
\end{plainterms}

\entryhead{position-epiphenomenalism}{Epiphenomenalism}{Position \,\textperiodcentered\, after Huxley}{Epiphenomenalism}{}{$\bigstar$}
Epiphenomenalism is the view that phenomenal consciousness is real and non-physical but \emph{causally inert}: experiences are caused by brain processes yet have no effects of their own (see \hyperref[entry:claim-mental-states-causally-inert]{\textsc{Causally inert qualia}}). Conscious feeling is a by-product of physical activity, riding along without contributing to it. T. H. Huxley's image (see \hyperref[source:source-huxley-1874]{\textsc{Huxley 1874}}) is a locomotive's steam-whistle, produced by the engine but doing nothing to drive the train; the early Frank Jackson (see \hyperref[source:source-jackson-1982]{\textsc{Jackson — Epiphenomenal Qualia (1982)}}) defended a version of the view under the heading of \emph{epiphenomenal qualia}.

\webheading{What it buys, and where it sits}

The view keeps a \emph{phenomenal residue} (see \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-residue]{\textsc{Phenomenal residue}}) — experience that outruns any causal or functional role — while still honouring the \emph{causal closure of the physical}, the principle that every physical event already has a sufficient physical cause. In David Chalmers's taxonomy of options it is the \textbf{type-E} position (\hyperref[entry:concept-materialism-types]{\textsc{Type-A / Type-B / Type-C materialism}}; \hyperref[source:source-chalmers-2003]{\textsc{Chalmers — Consciousness and Its Place in Nature (2003)}}). It answers the \hyperref[entry:question-hard-problem]{\textsc{hard problem of consciousness}} by granting a fundamental felt residue that simply does no work. In effect it is the horn of \hyperref[entry:position-dualism]{\textsc{Dualism}} that accepts causal inefficacy rather than positing mind-to-body interaction (the \emph{type-D} option).

\webheading{The signature objection}

Epiphenomenalism's standard problems are forms of \emph{self-stultification}. If experiences cause nothing, it is unclear how our judgements and reports \emph{about} our experiences could be caused by them, and unclear how we could come to know our experiences at all, since the knowing and the reporting would then be disconnected from the very states they are supposed to be about.

\begin{plainterms}
