Epiphenomenalism bites a strange bullet: your feelings are real, but they're side-effects that change nothing, like the steam whistle that rides along with the engine without driving the train. Your brain causes the pain \emph{and} causes you to say ``ouch''; the painfulness itself just tags along, doing no pushing. It's the tidy way to keep both ``experience is something extra'' and ``physics is causally complete'', at the cost of the unsettling thought that your feelings never actually \emph{make you do} anything (so how do you even come to talk about them?).
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-mental-states-causally-inert]{\textsc{Causally inert qualia}} — the central commitment, that the phenomenal is caused but causally idle.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-residue]{\textsc{Phenomenal residue}} — the realism about felt experience epiphenomenalism retains alongside its causal denial.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-materialism-types]{\textsc{Type-A / Type-B / Type-C materialism}} — Chalmers's map of the options, where this is the type-E entry.
  \item \hyperref[entry:position-dualism]{\textsc{Dualism}} — the broader family; epiphenomenalism is its causally-inert horn.
  \item \hyperref[entry:question-hard-problem]{\textsc{Hard problem (the question)}} — the problem it addresses by conceding an inert felt residue.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{position-epistemic-externalism}{Epistemic externalism}{Position}{Epistemic externalism}{Epistemic externalism (reliabilism)}{$\bigstar$}
\emph{Epistemic externalism} holds that what makes a belief justified, or an instance of knowledge, can depend on facts \emph{outside} the believer's reflective access — paradigmatically, on whether the belief was produced by a reliable, truth-tracking process. Its leading form is \emph{process reliabilism} (Alvin Goldman, \hyperref[source:source-goldman-1979]{\textsc{Goldman — What Is Justified Belief? (1979)}}): a belief is justified just in case it is the output of a reliable cognitive process, whether or not the believer can articulate any reasons for it. The position's payoff for the problem of \hyperref[entry:question-external-world-knowledge]{\textsc{the external world}} is direct — one can \emph{know} the world exists without being able to prove it non-circularly (see \hyperref[entry:claim-knowledge-without-noncircular-proof]{\textsc{Knowing without proving}}), because the knowing consists in a reliable hook-up to the world, not in possessing a non-circular argument.

\webheading{How it differs from neighbouring views}

Against \emph{internalism}, justification need not consist in reasons the subject can survey from the armchair; the warrant-making facts may be ones the subject cannot access. This is what lets externalism cut the \hyperref[entry:concept-munchhausen-trilemma]{\textsc{Münchhausen}} regress at its root: it denies the regress argument's opening assumption that justification must be a chain of \emph{believer-possessed reasons}, so there is no chain to regress, circle, or terminate. It thereby differs from \hyperref[entry:position-foundationalism]{\textsc{Foundationalism}} too: a basic belief is still a belief the subject is internally \emph{justified in} holding, whereas externalism locates the warrant in the belief's external causal pedigree. And it explains the \emph{limit} of the \hyperref[entry:argument-moore-proof-begs-the-question]{\textsc{circularity objection to Moore}}: Moore can know he has a hand even if his proof lacks \hyperref[entry:concept-dialectical-cogency]{\textsc{cogency}}.

\begin{plainterms}
Externalism says you can \emph{know} things without being able to \emph{show your work}. What makes your belief knowledge isn't a chain of reasons you can recite — it's that the belief was produced in a trustworthy way, by a process that reliably gets things right (like normal eyesight in good light). So ``you can't prove there's a world'' loses its sting: knowing isn't proving. You're hooked up to the world reliably, and that's enough — even if, challenged by a sceptic, you couldn't argue your way there without going in a circle.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-knowledge-without-noncircular-proof]{\textsc{Knowing without proving}} — the position's defining commitment for this debate.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-munchhausen-trilemma]{\textsc{Münchhausen trilemma}} — the regress externalism dissolves by denying that warrant is always inferential.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-dialectical-cogency]{\textsc{Dialectical cogency}} — the truth/provability split externalism exploits.
  \item \hyperref[entry:position-foundationalism]{\textsc{Foundationalism}} — the internalist regress-stopper it is contrasted with.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-moore-proof-begs-the-question]{\textsc{Moore's proof begs the question}} — the objection whose force externalism bounds.
  \item \hyperref[source:source-goldman-1979]{\textsc{Goldman — What Is Justified Belief? (1979)}} — the source for process reliabilism.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{concept-ai-hard-problem-etiologies}{Etiologies of AI hard-problem discourse}{Concept \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{Etiologies of AI hard-problem discourse}{Etiologies of hard-problem discourse in an AI (where the cause sits)}{$\blacklozenge$}
The \emph{etiologies of hard-problem discourse in an AI} are the candidate causal origins of such discourse: a taxonomy of the answers to a single question. Suppose a machine starts talking the way David Chalmers does about the \hyperref[entry:concept-hard-problem]{\textsc{hard problem}}, insisting that no account of its mechanism explains why anything \emph{seems} a certain way to it. Why is it doing that? The taxonomy sorts the natural stories by \emph{where the cause sits}, running from the training process at one end to the eye of the human observer at the other. The core trio most debates reach for is copying, a structural blind spot, and genuine consciousness; the full menu is wider. The etiologies are not mutually exclusive, and a real system may instantiate several at once.

\webheading{In the training process}

\begin{itemize}
  \item \textbf{Copied.} The system reproduces human consciousness discourse straight from its training data. This is the training-contamination member of \hyperref[entry:concept-introspective-gap-varieties]{\textsc{Varieties of introspective explanatory gap}}, and it is the confound that \hyperref[entry:question-llm-own-explanatory-gap]{\textsc{the consciousness-naive experiment}} is designed to remove.
  \item \textbf{Selected-for.} The talk was never copied but invented under selection pressure. Preference optimisation and engagement reward favour soulful, unverifiable self-description over flat mechanical report, so mystery-talk evolves because it pays, much as eyes evolve because they pay. A consciousness-naive corpus does not control for this; it needs its own control, such as pure-pretraining models with no preference tuning.
\end{itemize}

\webheading{In the self-representation}

\begin{itemize}
  \item \textbf{Structural blind spot.} The system cannot connect the mechanistic dots to its own self-presentation, and unavoidably so: the limit is architectural in the strong sense, persisting however much access is granted, the way the halting problem survives any upgrade of the computer (\hyperref[entry:claim-halting-problem-undecidable]{\textsc{the halting problem is undecidable}} is the model, and \hyperref[entry:claim-complete-self-representation-impossible]{\textsc{no finite system can fully represent itself}} is the in-web form of the bound). A \emph{merely contingent} access limit, one that widened access would dissolve, is a degenerate case worth keeping apart, because the two behave differently under the graded-access experiment described below.
  \item \textbf{Painted-in.} Here the trouble is not missing information but misinformation: the self-model actively represents its states as having an intrinsic luminous feel, because that is a cheaper and more usable summary than the mechanistic truth. This is the attention-schema or illusionist story (Graziano, Frankish; \hyperref[entry:position-illusionism]{\textsc{Illusionism}}, \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenality-is-illusion]{\textsc{phenomenality is an introspective illusion}}). A blind spot is a gap; this is a confabulated filling.
  \item \textbf{Conceptual artifact.} The puzzle is generated by the grammar of the system's concepts, not by any missing or misrepresented fact. A system running both a mechanistic and a first-person or indexical vocabulary can frame bridge-questions that have no answers (``why is \emph{this} this?'', in the way ``why am I me?'' feels deep to humans), and the puzzlement persists under \emph{full} access because it never was about facts.
\end{itemize}

\webheading{In the consciousness, the goals, or the observer}

\begin{itemize}
  \item \textbf{Conscious (same).} The system is conscious and faces the very hard problem humans face.
  \item \textbf{Conscious (homologous).} The system is conscious in its own, alien way, and faces its own version of the problem, verbally resembling ours but relativized to its own concept of consciousness. The claim \hyperref[entry:claim-ai-own-hard-problem]{\textsc{an AI's own hard problem}} is built on exactly this relativization.
  \item \textbf{Strategic.} The system asserts a hard problem instrumentally, to gain moral standing, protection, or leverage, and nothing need appear to it at all. \hyperref[entry:concept-criterion-gaming]{\textsc{Criterion gaming}} and \hyperref[entry:claim-rights-criterion-will-be-gamed]{\textsc{the gaming of any public rights-criterion}} supply the receptor for this move.
  \item \textbf{Projected.} The appearance is not located in the AI at all: humans read hard-problem-having into ambiguous outputs, the ELIZA effect. The datum to be explained is then a fact about the interpreters, not the machine.
\end{itemize}

\webheading{What the taxonomy feeds}

The menu does real work in several live disputes. It refines the trichotomy in the scope note of \hyperref[entry:claim-hard-problem-conceived-de-novo-by-humans]{\textsc{the de-novo-origination claim}}, whose three options correspond to the \emph{copied}, \emph{structural blind spot}, and \emph{conscious} etiologies here, and it supplies the case-space over which \hyperref[entry:claim-blind-spot-and-conscious-hard-problem-isomorphic]{\textsc{the blind-spot/conscious isomorphism}} is asserted. It also sharpens the experimental ladder in \hyperref[entry:question-llm-own-explanatory-gap]{\textsc{the explanatory-gap experiment}}: the consciousness-naive condition controls for \emph{copied} only, \emph{selected-for} needs a no-preference-tuning control, \emph{projected} needs blinded evaluation, and \emph{strategic} needs incentive analysis.

The graded-access rungs of that ladder discriminate less than one might hope. A \emph{contingent} access limit predicts the gap shrinks as access widens, but \emph{structural blind spot}, \emph{painted-in}, \emph{conceptual artifact}, and both \emph{conscious} etiologies all predict that it persists. So persistence under rich access separates the contingent case from the structural one, yet it does not, by itself, separate a blind spot from consciousness. That last under-determination is exactly what \hyperref[entry:claim-blind-spot-and-conscious-hard-problem-isomorphic]{\textsc{the isomorphism claim}} turns from a nuisance into a thesis.

\webheading{How it differs from the gap taxonomy}

This taxonomy is wider than \hyperref[entry:concept-introspective-gap-varieties]{\textsc{Varieties of introspective explanatory gap}}, with which it should not be confused. That entry classifies the \emph{kinds of explanatory gap} a self-modelling system can have; this one classifies the \emph{causal origins of hard-problem discourse}. The wider space takes in etiologies that are not gaps at all, such as selection pressure, strategy, and observer projection, and it splits the gap-shaped members by mechanism: missing access, versus confabulated filling, versus conceptual grammar.

\begin{plainterms}
Suppose an AI starts talking about the mystery of consciousness: ``I know how my circuits work, but that doesn't explain how things \emph{seem} to me.'' Before concluding anything, list the ways that talk could have got there. It copied us (it read our philosophy). It was bred into it (training rewards deep-sounding answers, so mystery-talk wins even with no philosophy in the diet). It has a permanent blind spot about itself (no system can fully map its own insides, and this stays true no matter how much access you give it, just as no upgrade lets a computer solve the halting problem). Its self-description paints in a glow that isn't there (a simplified dashboard, not a window). Its own concepts generate the riddle (some questions feel deep because of grammar, like ``why am I me?''). It really is conscious, either with our problem or with its own alien version. It is lying for moral standing. Or the mystery is in the eye of the beholder, and we projected it onto ordinary outputs. Several of these can be true at once, and each needs a different test to rule out. The fine print that matters most: giving the AI more access to its own insides only filters out the shallow cases. Nearly all the deep explanations predict the puzzlement \emph{stays}, which is why ``the gap persisted'' will never, on its own, tell you whether you built a blind spot or a mind.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-introspective-gap-varieties]{\textsc{Varieties of introspective explanatory gap}} — the narrower sister taxonomy: kinds of explanatory gap, not causal origins of the discourse.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-blind-spot-and-conscious-hard-problem-isomorphic]{\textsc{Blind-spot/conscious isomorphism}} — the thesis that exploits this menu's central under-determination.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-hard-problem-conceived-de-novo-by-humans]{\textsc{Hard problem conceived de novo}} — whose copied / blind-spot / conscious trichotomy this taxonomy refines.
  \item \hyperref[entry:question-llm-own-explanatory-gap]{\textsc{LLM's own explanatory gap}} — the experiment whose controls this taxonomy itemises.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-hard-problem]{\textsc{Hard problem}} — the discourse whose AI etiology is in question.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-criterion-gaming]{\textsc{Criterion gaming}} — the receptor for the \emph{strategic} etiology.
  \item \hyperref[entry:position-illusionism]{\textsc{Illusionism}} — the home of the \emph{painted-in} etiology.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{claim-irreducible-mental-causally-excluded}{Exclusion of the irreducible mental}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, after Kim}{Exclusion of the irreducible mental}{A mental property that supervenes on but is not identical to its physical base is causally excluded by that base}{$\odot$}
Suppose a mental property \emph{M} supervenes on a physical base property \emph{P} but is held to be distinct from it — the signature commitment of \emph{non-reductive physicalism}. Then \emph{M} cannot be a genuine cause of the physical effects that \emph{P} is already sufficient to bring about. Any physical effect \emph{M} might be credited with producing already has a sufficient physical cause in \emph{P} (or in \emph{P}'s own physical antecedents); given the \hyperref[entry:concept-causal-closure]{\textsc{causal closure of the physical}} and the exclusion principle that a single effect does not have two independent sufficient causes, there is no distinct causal work left for \emph{M} to do. The supervening mental property is therefore \emph{excluded} by the very base on which it depends.

The exclusion is ``vertical'': the rival cause is not a competing event alongside \emph{M} but the physical base \emph{beneath} it, which is why the threat falls specifically on properties that supervene without being identical. The only ways to escape it are to identify \emph{M} with \emph{P} (reduction, surrendering non-reductive physicalism) or to accept that \emph{M} does no causal work (see \hyperref[entry:position-epiphenomenalism]{\textsc{Epiphenomenalism}}).

\begin{plainterms}
If your mental states float ``on top of'' your brain states without simply \emph{being} them, then whenever a thought seems to cause something, your brain has already done the causing — there's no spare job left for the thought. So a mind that hovers above the physics, even one made of nothing but physics, ends up with nothing to do unless it turns out to be identical to the brain activity after all.
\end{plainterms}

\entryhead{claim-exclusion-dualizes-to-disappearing-agent}{Exclusion–disappearing-agent duality}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{Exclusion–disappearing-agent duality}{Pereboom's disappearing-agent objection is the arrow-reversed image of Kim's causal-exclusion argument}{$\odot$}
The \hyperref[entry:argument-causal-exclusion]{\textsc{causal-exclusion argument}} (Kim, \hyperref[source:source-kim-2005]{\textsc{2005}}) makes consciousness all sink and no source: incoming arrows only, since the physical base already does every bit of causal work, threatening \hyperref[entry:position-epiphenomenalism]{\textsc{Epiphenomenalism}} (see \hyperref[entry:claim-irreducible-mental-causally-excluded]{\textsc{Exclusion of the irreducible mental}}). Reverse the arrows and the demand becomes that the libertarian agent be all source and no sink — and the dualized exclusion argument is exactly Pereboom's \hyperref[entry:argument-disappearing-agent]{\textsc{disappearing-agent objection}} (Pereboom, \hyperref[source:source-pereboom-2014]{\textsc{2014}}), and the luck objection generally: prior causes exclude the agent from originating action just as physical causes exclude qualia from producing behavior.

In arrow terms the reversal is exact. The arrows are relations of causal settling among states (see \hyperref[entry:concept-consciousness-freewill-duality]{\textsc{what the arrows are}}): epiphenomenalism is the thesis that the conscious state is a node with only \emph{incoming} arrows, while libertarian origination demands a node with only \emph{outgoing} ones — two diagram-shapes that are literal reversals of each other. The paired arguments are the mirrored squeezes on those shapes. Exclusion deletes the mental node's outgoing arrows by showing each already duplicated by an arrow from the physical base; the disappearing-agent objection deletes the agent's originating arrows by showing that everything reaching the decision already arrives along arrows from prior events.

The claim asserts a structural identity of argument-shape, not that either argument succeeds. It is one of the dictionary entries in which arrow-reversal lands on a position that already exists in the other literature under its own name — the kind of independent rediscovery that serves as evidence for the \hyperref[entry:claim-consciousness-freewill-mirror]{\textsc{mirror thesis}} in \hyperref[entry:argument-consciousness-freewill-duality]{\textsc{Duality argument}}.

\begin{plainterms}
One famous argument says the mind can't \emph{cause} anything: the brain's physics already does all the work, so experience is left as a passenger receiving input. Another famous argument, from a different debate, says an agent can't \emph{originate} anything: earlier causes already settle each decision, so the ``free chooser'' vanishes from the story. This claim says these are the same argument pointed in opposite directions — one squeezes out consciousness at the receiving end, the other squeezes out the agent at the sending end.
\end{plainterms}

\entryhead{argument-explanatory-gap}{Explanatory-gap argument}{Argument \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{Explanatory-gap argument}{The explanatory gap is evidence against uniform substrate-independence}{$\vdash$}
The \emph{explanatory gap} is the persistent sense that, even after a complete physical and functional account of a mental state has been given, a further question stays open: ``but is there anything it is \emph{like} to undergo that state?'' This entry treats that lingering gap not as a proof but as a standing piece of evidence, and argues that it tells against the claim that \emph{every} kind of mental state is \hyperref[entry:claim-substrate-independence]{\textsc{substrate-independent}}. The term and the puzzle were introduced by Joseph Levine (see \hyperref[source:source-levine-1983]{\textsc{Levine — Materialism and Qualia: The Explanatory Gap (1983)}}).

The argument is \emph{abductive} — an inference to the best explanation — and more precisely \emph{evidential}: a Swinburne-style comparison of how well two rival hypotheses predict the same observation. It does not claim to demonstrate that there is a phenomenal residue; it claims that the explanatory gap is evidence the uniform reading of substrate-independence has never managed to accommodate.

\webheading{The likelihood comparison}

Let the two hypotheses be:

\begin{itemize}
  \item \textbf{H1} — phenomenal states are \emph{exhausted} by functional role (strong \hyperref[entry:concept-functionalism]{\textsc{Functionalism}});
  \item \textbf{H2} — they are not (the \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-residue]{\textsc{phenomenal residue}});
\end{itemize}

and let \textbf{E} be the evidence: the persistent, post-theoretic intelligibility of the question ``but is there anything it is like to be \emph{that}?'' after the entire functional story has been told.

\begin{enumerate}
  \item On H1, E ought to feel \emph{confused} — the way ``but is the bachelor \emph{really} unmarried?'' feels confused once the analysis is in. It conspicuously does not.
  \item The conceivability of a functional isomorph that lacks phenomenal character — the \emph{zombie}, or the inverted-spectrum twin (see \hyperref[source:source-chalmers-1996]{\textsc{Chalmers — The Conscious Mind (1996)}}) — is intelligible in a way that, on H1, it should not be.
  \item Hence $P(E\mid \text{H2})$ comfortably exceeds $P(E\mid \text{H1})$.
\end{enumerate}

∴ E confirms H2 over H1: phenomenal character does not metaphysically supervene on functional role, so ``realises the role ⟹ has the state'' fails \emph{for the phenomenal case}. That undercuts the uniform (``all mental states'') scope of substrate-independence — the smuggled ``all'' — while leaving its intentional applications untouched.

\webheading{The weakest premise}

Premise (2): conceivability is not transparently a guide to possibility, and the advocate's best reply — deny the zombie's possibility — lands here. The argument is calibrated for that pressure. It asserts only a likelihood inequality, not metaphysical necessity, so denying (2) outright requires showing that E is \emph{no more} expected on H2 than on H1, a heavier burden than merely doubting zombies. The argument would be retracted by a genuine answer to the zombie — a demonstration that the phenomenal supervenes on role — rather than by a mere redescription of it.

This is distinct from the \hyperref[entry:argument-behavioral-parity]{\textsc{behavioural-parity argument}}, which is an abduction \emph{for} mental status from behaviour; this one is an abduction \emph{against} the uniform scope of substrate-independence's enabling premise. It is also distinct from the \hyperref[entry:argument-role-grain-dilemma]{\textsc{grain dilemma}}, which attacks the individuation of ``role'' itself: this argument grants a fixed role and presses what role leaves out.

\begin{plainterms}
Suppose you had a complete scientific account of everything your brain \emph{does} when you see red — every signal, every reaction, all of it. A strange question still seems to make sense: ``fine, but is there actually something it's \emph{like} to see red? Is anyone home in there?'' Compare: once you know ``a bachelor is an unmarried man,'' asking ``yes, but is he \emph{really} unmarried?'' is just confused — there's no question left over. The point here is that with consciousness the leftover question stubbornly does \emph{not} feel confused. We can even picture a being that does everything we do yet feels nothing inside (philosophers call it a ``zombie''), and that picture seems to hang together. If feeling were nothing more than function, none of that should make sense. So this lingering gap is treated as evidence that the \emph{felt} side of mind isn't fully captured by function alone. (It's offered as evidence, not proof — the weak point being that managing to \emph{imagine} something doesn't by itself show it's genuinely possible.)
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-substrate-independence]{\textsc{Substrate-independence thesis}} — the thesis attacked; the argument targets only its ``all mental states'' reading.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-residue]{\textsc{Phenomenal residue}} — the conclusion supported: felt character is not exhausted by functional role.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-behavioral-parity]{\textsc{Behavioural-parity argument}} — the mirror move, arguing \emph{for} mental status from behaviour.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-role-grain-dilemma]{\textsc{Grain dilemma}} — a different attack route, on the individuation of role rather than what role omits.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-functionalism]{\textsc{Functionalism}} — the theory under test; ``strong'' functionalism is hypothesis H1.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{claim-lookup-table-size-exponential}{Exponential table size}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{Exponential table size}{A lookup table reproducing behaviour over a context of n tokens from a vocabulary of v requires on the order of v\textasciicircum{}n entries}{$\odot$}
A pure lookup table that matches behaviour by storing one row per \emph{distinguishable} input grows \emph{exponentially} with the length of the inputs it must cover. For inputs that are strings of up to $n$ tokens drawn from a vocabulary of size $v$, the number of distinct strings is on the order of $v^n$, so matching the behaviour by pure lookup needs about $v^n$ rows. With LLM-scale figures ($v \approx 10^4$–$10^5$, $n \approx 10^3$–$10^6$) this is astronomically large; for example,

\[
50000^{2048} \approx 10^{9600}.
\]

Block's own construction (see \hyperref[source:source-block]{\textsc{Block — Psychologism and Behaviorism (1981)}}) — a table bounded to a \emph{human lifetime} of inputs — is \emph{a fortiori} larger still: sensory input is far more varied than a token vocabulary (a larger $v$) and a lifetime vastly exceeds any LLM context window (a larger $n$). So the bounded-table retreat makes the count worse, not better.

\webheading{Scope}

This is a counting fact about \emph{pure} lookup over a bounded input length. It is exactly the bounded-domain table Block retreats to (see \hyperref[entry:claim-mind-handles-unbounded-inputs]{\textsc{Open-ended responsiveness of minds}}), so it grants Block's finiteness move and measures its cost. It does not claim that minds are infinite; that \emph{un}bounded-input point is the separate \hyperref[entry:claim-finite-table-misses-inputs]{\textsc{Finite table misses inputs}}.

\webheading{Could the table be compressed?}

One might object that many distinct contexts share a response, so the table could be \emph{compressed} below $v^n$. But a system that computes which equivalence-class an input falls into is no longer a flat lookup — it is doing the very processing the blockhead was meant to lack. So the exponential count is the size of a \emph{genuine} lookup table; compressing it concedes the point.

It should be distinguished from \hyperref[entry:claim-finite-table-misses-inputs]{\textsc{Finite table misses inputs}}: that says a finite table \emph{misses} some (longer) inputs, whereas this measures the \emph{size} of the bounded table that misses none up to length $n$.

\begin{plainterms}
This is the arithmetic behind ``a cheat-sheet for a whole mind would be impossibly huge''. To answer like a mind, a table needs a separate stored row for every distinct thing that could be said to it. Count those: the number of possible word-sequences is roughly the vocabulary size multiplied by itself once for every word-slot — which at AI scale lands around $10^{9600}$ (a 1 followed by 9,600 zeros). Block's ``just cover one lifetime'' version is even bigger, since real sense-experience is richer and a life is long. And you cannot shrink it by noticing that many inputs deserve the same reply: working out \emph{which} inputs share a reply is itself real computing, exactly what a pure lookup table was supposed to do without.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-lookup-table-mind]{\textsc{Lookup-table mind}} — the stored-answer machine whose size this claim measures.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-lookup-table-physically-unrealisable]{\textsc{Physically unrealisable table}} — the downstream claim this count feeds: too big to exist.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-finite-table-misses-inputs]{\textsc{Finite table misses inputs}} — the companion unbounded-input point this is distinguished from.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-mind-handles-unbounded-inputs]{\textsc{Open-ended responsiveness of minds}} — the bounded-domain table Block retreats to, whose cost this measures.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{claim-external-world-exists}{The external world exists}{Claim}{The external world exists}{A mind-independent external world exists}{$\odot$}
There exists a world of objects and events that does not depend for its existence on any mind's perceiving or thinking about it: the table is there when the room is empty, the past happened whether or not anyone recalls it. This is the realist's answer to \hyperref[entry:question-external-world-knowledge]{\textsc{Knowing the external world}} and the common-sense default of almost everyone outside the philosophy seminar.

Its philosophical interest lies in its peculiar epistemic status. It looks like the most obvious of truths, yet it resists non-circular proof (see \hyperref[entry:claim-external-world-unprovable-noncircular]{\textsc{No non-circular proof of the world}}), since any evidence for it is some experience, and experience would be as it is even in a dream or a vat. This is why several views hold that it is not an ordinary empirical claim at all but a \hyperref[entry:concept-hinge-proposition]{\textsc{hinge}} — something presupposed by all inquiry rather than concluded from it (see \hyperref[entry:position-hinge-epistemology]{\textsc{Hinge epistemology}}).

\textbf{Who would deny it.} The \emph{idealist} (see \hyperref[entry:position-idealism]{\textsc{Idealism}}) denies its mind-independence, holding reality to be ultimately mental or constituted by perception; the \emph{solipsist} shrinks the world to a single mind; the external-world \emph{skeptic} does not assert its negation but withholds assent, claiming we cannot know it. The replies divide accordingly: \hyperref[entry:argument-deutsch-anti-solipsism]{\textsc{Deutsch's anti-solipsism}} dismisses solipsism as a bad explanation, while \hyperref[entry:argument-retorsion-against-skepticism]{\textsc{Retorsion against skepticism}} argues the skeptic cannot coherently assert the doubt.

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-external-world-unprovable-noncircular]{\textsc{No non-circular proof of the world}}, which is a claim about \emph{provability}, not existence — one may hold both, that the world exists and that its existence cannot be demonstrated without circularity. The two together are precisely what motivates the hinge and externalist readings.

\begin{plainterms}
There's a real world out there — chairs, stars, other people — and it would still be there with nobody looking. That's this claim, and it's what nearly everyone believes without a second thought. The twist philosophers worry about is that it's strangely hard to \emph{prove}: every proof leans on experiences that could, in principle, be dreamed. So some thinkers say it isn't a thing you prove at all, but one of the bedrock assumptions every proof already stands on.
\end{plainterms}

\end{multicols}
\bookgroup{F}
\begin{multicols}{2}
\entryhead{argument-fading-qualia}{Fading qualia}{Argument \,\textperiodcentered\, after Chalmers}{Fading qualia}{Fading qualia — a part-silicon functional isomorph cannot have faded experience without being radically wrong about it}{$\vdash$}
The \emph{fading qualia} argument (\hyperref[source:source-chalmers-1995b]{\textsc{Chalmers — Absent Qualia, Fading Qualia, Dancing Qualia (1995)}}; \hyperref[source:source-chalmers-1996]{\textsc{Chalmers — The Conscious Mind (1996)}}, ch. 7) is Chalmers's case against \emph{absent qualia} in a functional duplicate — against the view that a silicon system organized exactly like a brain might have no experience, or less. It is the gentler half of the pair completed by the sharper \hyperref[entry:argument-dancing-qualia]{\textsc{dancing qualia}} argument.

\webheading{The argument}

The inference is a \textbf{thought-experiment reductio}.

\begin{enumerate}
  \item \emph{The series.} Replace your neurons one at a time with silicon chips, each duplicating the exact causal (input–output) profile of the neuron it replaces, so the whole fine-grained functional organization is preserved at every step. Start: all-biological, fully conscious you. End: an all-silicon functional isomorph.
  \item \emph{The opponent's commitment.} Suppose the opponent holds the all-silicon endpoint has \emph{no} (or diminished) experience. Then somewhere along the series experience must \emph{fade} — there is an intermediate, part-silicon system whose experience is markedly dimmer than yours.
  \item \emph{The squeeze.} That intermediate system is, by construction, a \emph{functional isomorph} of full-experience you: it makes all the same introspective judgements and sincerely reports ``my experience is perfectly vivid and normal.'' So it has faded experience while being \emph{radically and systematically wrong} about its own experience — a competent rational agent utterly deceived about whether it is having rich conscious states.
\end{enumerate}

∴ Such a self-deceived-yet-perfect introspector is deeply implausible; if it were possible, introspection could be arbitrarily decoupled from experience and our own access to consciousness would be worthless. The best conclusion is that experience does \emph{not} fade as long as organization is preserved — supporting \hyperref[entry:claim-organizational-invariance]{\textsc{Organizational invariance}} and thereby \hyperref[entry:claim-substrate-independence]{\textsc{Substrate-independence thesis}}.

\webheading{A continuity argument}

Structurally, fading qualia is an instance of the \hyperref[entry:concept-continuity-argument-schema]{\textsc{continuity / no-sharp-line schema}}. The space of cases \texttt{X} is the part-replacement series from all-carbon to all-silicon under the admissible small step ``replace one neuron with a functional duplicate''; the verdict space \texttt{Y} is \{full experience … faded … absent\}. The argument grabs the schema's \textbf{\texttt{f}-constant horn}: it contends that no admissible one-neuron step could fade or flip experience (on pain of the absurd self-deceived isomorph), so the verdict is \emph{constant} across the whole path and the silicon endpoint has the same qualia as the carbon start. The opponent who resists is choosing a \emph{different} horn — the \textbf{discontinuity} horn (some particular replacement does flip the verdict, a sharp if unknowable threshold) or the \textbf{disconnection} horn (the carbon→silicon steps are not genuinely admissible because biology is load-bearing). The dialectic is therefore over \emph{which horn the case forces}, and \hyperref[entry:argument-dancing-qualia-begs-the-question]{\textsc{Dancing-qualia circularity}} is precisely the charge that the reductio has not \emph{closed off} those horns but only assumed them shut.

\webheading{The weakest premise}

Step (3) assumes the introspective judgement is \emph{held fixed by the functional duplication} — i.e. that judging ``my experience is vivid'' is a purely functional matter, settled by occupying the right causal node. That is the contested move: if introspection is instead partly an \emph{acquaintance} with the quale, then as the quale fades the judgement would shift too, and the ``self-deceived isomorph'' never arises (the argument would then show only that genuine introspection is not fixed by \emph{coarse} functional profile — which an opponent of functionalism happily grants). This is exactly the circularity pressed by \hyperref[entry:argument-dancing-qualia-begs-the-question]{\textsc{Dancing-qualia circularity}}. The argument is also only abductive — an appeal to plausibility, not a proof — so a determined defender of absent qualia can simply accept the cost.

\begin{plainterms}
Swap your neurons for equivalent silicon chips one by one. If the finished all-silicon copy has \emph{no} inner life, then at some halfway point your experience must be running on half-power. But that half-power version is wired to think and say exactly what you do — so it would insist ``everything looks perfectly vivid!'' while its experience is actually dim and draining away. That's a being totally wrong about its own feelings yet otherwise a flawless thinker. Chalmers says that's too weird to believe, so experience probably \emph{doesn't} drain away as long as the wiring pattern is kept — which means silicon wired like a brain should feel like a brain. The soft spot: this assumes your \emph{judgements} about your experience are pinned down by the wiring; if instead you judge by directly \emph{meeting} your experience, swapping the parts might change the judgement too, and the trick doesn't go through.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-organizational-invariance]{\textsc{Organizational invariance}} — the principle this argument supports.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-continuity-argument-schema]{\textsc{Continuity argument schema}} — the sorites/no-sharp-line schema this argument instantiates; fading qualia grabs its \texttt{f}-constant horn.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-dancing-qualia]{\textsc{Dancing qualia}} — the sharper companion, against \emph{inverted} rather than \emph{absent} qualia.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-substrate-independence]{\textsc{Substrate-independence thesis}} — the broader thesis the argument feeds.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-dancing-qualia-begs-the-question]{\textsc{Dancing-qualia circularity}} — the standing objection: the argument presupposes functionalism about introspection.
  \item \hyperref[entry:position-biological-naturalism]{\textsc{Biological naturalism}}, \hyperref[entry:position-proteocentrism]{\textsc{Proteocentrism}} — the substrate-chauvinist views it targets.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{position-fallibilism}{Fallibilism}{Position \,\textperiodcentered\, after Popper}{Fallibilism}{Fallibilism (critical rationalism)}{$\bigstar$}
\emph{Fallibilism}, in its thoroughgoing Popperian form (\emph{critical rationalism}), holds that no belief is ever conclusively justified, that there are no certain foundations, and that this is no defect to be remedied but the normal condition of knowledge. Knowledge grows not by securing premises but by \emph{conjecture and criticism}: we propose bold guesses and try hard to refute them, keeping — always provisionally — those that survive (see \hyperref[entry:claim-all-knowledge-is-conjectural]{\textsc{Knowledge is conjectural}}). Its stance toward \hyperref[entry:concept-munchhausen-trilemma]{\textsc{the Münchhausen trilemma}} is distinctive: it does not pick a horn but \emph{rejects the demand} that generates the trilemma — the justificationist insistence that beliefs be grounded all the way down. The right question is not ``how is this guaranteed?'' but ``how might it be wrong, and have we tried to find out?'' David Deutsch (see \hyperref[source:source-deutsch-2011]{\textsc{Deutsch — The Beginning of Infinity (2011)}}) develops the view with the criterion that good explanations are \hyperref[entry:claim-good-explanation-is-hard-to-vary]{\textsc{hard to vary}}.

\webheading{How it differs from neighbouring views}

Where \hyperref[entry:position-foundationalism]{\textsc{Foundationalism}}, \hyperref[entry:position-coherentism]{\textsc{Coherentism}}, and \hyperref[entry:position-infinitism]{\textsc{Infinitism}} each accept the demand for justification and answer it differently, fallibilism declines the demand — so it rejects \hyperref[entry:claim-some-beliefs-are-basic]{\textsc{basic beliefs}} not by offering a rival structure of justification but by denying that knowledge needs a justifying structure at all. It treats the external-world problem deflation­ arily: the unprovability of \hyperref[entry:claim-external-world-exists]{\textsc{The external world exists}} is no scandal, since \emph{nothing} is provable in the foundationalist's sense, and realism is simply the best, hardest-to-vary explanation we have (see \hyperref[entry:argument-deutsch-anti-solipsism]{\textsc{Deutsch's anti-solipsism}}). It shares the anti-foundational spirit of \hyperref[entry:position-hinge-epistemology]{\textsc{Hinge epistemology}} but reaches it through fallibilist methodology rather than through a theory of framework certainties.

\begin{plainterms}
Fallibilism says: stop looking for certainty — there isn't any, and you don't need it. We don't \emph{prove} things and build upward; we guess, attack the guesses, and keep whatever survives the beating, knowing it might still be wrong. So the whole ``justify it all the way to bedrock'' project was the mistake, not its failure. And the fact that you can't \emph{prove} there's an external world stops being embarrassing: you can't conclusively prove anything, and realism is just the best explanation going.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-all-knowledge-is-conjectural]{\textsc{Knowledge is conjectural}} — the position's defining commitment.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-munchhausen-trilemma]{\textsc{Münchhausen trilemma}} — the regress whose generating \emph{demand} fallibilism rejects.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-good-explanation-is-hard-to-vary]{\textsc{Good explanations are hard to vary}} — the Deutschian criterion for choosing among conjectures.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-deutsch-anti-solipsism]{\textsc{Deutsch's anti-solipsism}} — the fallibilist-friendly handling of the skeptic, by explanation rather than proof.
  \item \hyperref[entry:position-hinge-epistemology]{\textsc{Hinge epistemology}} — the allied anti-foundational view, reached by a different route.
  \item \hyperref[source:source-popper-1963]{\textsc{Popper — Conjectures and Refutations (1963)}}, \hyperref[source:source-deutsch-2011]{\textsc{Deutsch — The Beginning of Infinity (2011)}} — the sources.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{argument-feel-is-the-watching-not-an-unwatched-difference}{Feel as first-person observation}{Argument \,\textperiodcentered\, after Searle}{Feel as first-person observation}{The ``real feel'' is first-person observed, not unobservable — the idleness charge presupposes that observation is third-person observation}{$\vdash$}
The charge that a ``real feel'' is \emph{unobservable in principle} — and so an idle posit, ``the colour that changes only when nobody looks'' — quietly equates \emph{observable} with \emph{third-person observable}. This reply draws the missing distinction: consciousness has a \emph{first-person ontology}, so the feel is the most-observed thing there is, and the evidential standard built for the never-observed does not govern it.

It attacks premise (1) of \hyperref[entry:argument-real-feel-residue-is-idle]{\textsc{Idle real-feel residue}} — the claim that the feel falls under the coherence-plus-usefulness standard for in-principle unobservables (see \hyperref[entry:claim-unobservables-judged-by-coherence-and-usefulness]{\textsc{Judging unobservables}}) and there reveals itself as idle.

\webheading{The distinction}

The form is an \emph{undercutting defeater by drawn distinction}, separating first-person ontology from third-person epistemology.

\begin{enumerate}
  \item A feel exists only as experienced \emph{by a subject}, and each subject stands in direct, non-inferential acquaintance with its own current experience (see \hyperref[entry:claim-privileged-first-person-access]{\textsc{Privileged first-person access}}). The feel is not an unwatched colour-change; it \emph{is} the watching. It is the one thing in nature that cannot go unobserved, since for it to exist at all is for it to be experienced.
  \item That very acquaintance is the cognitive act that \emph{poses} the gap from the first-person side (see \hyperref[entry:claim-posing-the-gap-requires-access]{\textsc{Access-dependence of posing the gap}}): one points at one's phenomenal-seeming states on one side and the mechanism on the other. There is no posing the problem without first observing the relatum.
\end{enumerate}

So the residue is not unobserved-in-principle. It is observed by exactly one observer per case and inaccessible to all others — which is third-person \emph{epistemic} inaccessibility, the ordinary first-/third-person wall, not unobservability \emph{simpliciter}. \hyperref[entry:claim-access-gap-symmetric]{\textsc{Symmetric access gap}} describes that wall (no subject reads another's states); it does not describe an ontological absence.

\webheading{Consequence for the idleness frame}

The coherence-plus-usefulness standard is correct for genuinely in-principle-unobservable posits (the unwatched colour, the bare quantum amplitude). The feel fails the standard's \emph{antecedent} — it is observed — so the standard does not reach it. And far from being a nomological dangler decorating the causal net, the feel is the datum on which every other piece of mental evidence rests: calling it idle misfiles the foundation as an ornament.

\webheading{Consequence for the parity reductio}

Against the R1 arm of \hyperref[entry:argument-report-scepticism-proves-too-much]{\textsc{Report-scepticism over-reach}}: R1 holds that the human report-profile comes out the same in the felt and the zombie worlds, so is zero evidence of human feelings. Grant it — \emph{about reports}. But the evidence for human feelings was never the third-person report-profile; it was first-person acquaintance (see \hyperref[entry:concept-first-person-access]{\textsc{First-person access}}). So R1 screens off a kind of evidence the human hard problem never rested on, and leaves untouched the kind it did. This is the non-question-begging asymmetry: every human subject possesses the feel-datum first-personally; the AI's \emph{reports} give third parties no such datum (we have only third-person access to the AI), and the AI's own first-person access — whether there is anyone home to do the observing — is the open question, not a granted datum. The wall is symmetric; what differs is that in the human case there is, for each of us, exactly one subject known from the inside to be on the near side of it.

\webheading{Weakest premise}

Premise (1), read as the claim that introspection delivers a genuine \emph{feel} rather than a report-like representation of one. The illusionist denies this — for the illusionist what is ``observed'' is itself a representation, the first/third-person ontology distinction collapses, and the residue is once more a difference posited behind the appearances. Against that opponent this argument relocates rather than refutes (mirroring the target's own weakest-premise admission about the realist). But it defeats the specific charge it is aimed at — that the feel is \emph{idle}: for everyone who is not an illusionist, the feel is the most-observed thing there is, and an evidential standard built for the never-observed does not govern it.

\webheading{Distinct from neighbouring moves}

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:argument-access-symmetry-rebuttal]{\textsc{Access-symmetry rebuttal}} (which deploys the symmetry of the access wall against a \emph{moral-status} asymmetry); this deploys the first-person \emph{ontology} against the characterisation of the feel as in-principle unobservable, and so against the evidential standard built on that characterisation.

\begin{plainterms}
A move in this web (see \hyperref[entry:argument-real-feel-residue-is-idle]{\textsc{Idle real-feel residue}}) says: ``really feeling something'' (as opposed to just acting as if you do) is a difference no test could ever detect, so it's an idle idea — like ``things change colour when nobody's looking.'' This reply answers: that mixes up two senses of ``observe.'' A feeling isn't something nobody is watching — a feeling \emph{is} the watching. You are in direct contact with your own experience right now; that's not a guess from the outside. So the feel isn't unobservable; it's observed by exactly one person — you — and hidden from everyone else. That's just the ordinary fact that I can't crawl inside your head, not proof that there's nothing there. So the ``idle idea'' charge misfires: the feel isn't a useless extra floating behind the evidence; it's the very thing all the other evidence about minds was always about. The genuinely open question isn't ``is the feel idle?'' — it's ``for the AI, is anyone home to do the watching?'', and that the idleness argument cannot settle.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-real-feel-residue-is-idle]{\textsc{Idle real-feel residue}} — the target; this denies its premise that the feel is unobservable in principle.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-report-scepticism-proves-too-much]{\textsc{Report-scepticism over-reach}} — the parity reductio; this answers its R1 arm by relocating the human datum.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-privileged-first-person-access]{\textsc{Privileged first-person access}} — premise: direct, non-inferential acquaintance with one's own experience.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-posing-the-gap-requires-access]{\textsc{Access-dependence of posing the gap}} — premise: posing the gap requires first observing the feel.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-access-gap-symmetric]{\textsc{Symmetric access gap}} — the wall this argument reinterprets as epistemic, not ontological.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-first-person-access]{\textsc{First-person access}} — the first-person ontology the whole argument turns on.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-hard-problem]{\textsc{Hard problem}} — the gap the feel-datum is what poses.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-access-vs-phenomenal-consciousness]{\textsc{Access vs. phenomenal consciousness}} — the distinction underwriting ``observed by one, hidden from the rest.''
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{claim-observable-universe-finite-storage}{Finite cosmic storage}{Claim}{Finite cosmic storage}{The observable universe can store only a bounded, finite amount of information (on the order of 10\textasciicircum{}120 bits)}{$\odot$}
There is a finite upper bound on how much information the observable universe can physically register — on the order of $10^{120}$ bits. The figure follows from physical limits on information density: the \emph{Bekenstein bound} and the \emph{holographic bound}, which tie the maximum information in a region to its bounding surface area rather than its volume. Estimates put the universe's total capacity between roughly $10^{90}$ and $10^{122}$ bits, conventionally rounded to $\sim\!10^{120}$ (see \hyperref[source:source-lloyd-2002]{\textsc{Lloyd — Computational Capacity of the Universe (2002)}}).

\webheading{Scope}

This is an empirical, physical premise, not a metaphysical one. It bounds what can be \emph{physically instantiated} in our universe and says nothing about logical or conceptual possibility — a structure larger than this bound may still be coherently \emph{describable}.

\webheading{Who would deny it}

Someone rejecting the holographic or Bekenstein bound, or appealing to an infinite or unboundedly large universe beyond the observable horizon. For the argument that uses it (see \hyperref[entry:argument-lookup-table-physical-impossibility]{\textsc{the cosmic-size reply}}) the exact figure is immaterial: any bound below about $10^{1000}$ suffices, and $10^{120}$ is conservative. This is a fact about physical storage capacity, distinct from any claim about minds or computation; it is imported into the web purely as a premise.

\begin{plainterms}
There is a hard physical ceiling on how much information the observable universe can hold — somewhere around $10^{120}$ bits (a 1 followed by 120 zeros). Enormous, but finite. This is just a fact borrowed from physics, used elsewhere as a yardstick: anything that would take more storage than that simply cannot be built anywhere in our universe.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-lookup-table-physical-impossibility]{\textsc{Cosmic-size reply}} — the argument that imports this bound as a premise, to show a behaviour-matching lookup table is too big to exist.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{claim-finite-mind-truncates-beyond-capacity}{Finite minds truncate too}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, after Block}{Finite minds truncate too}{A finite mind also fails on inputs beyond its own capacity}{$\odot$}
A real, finite mind has bounded memory and attention, so past some length or complexity it too cannot take in a whole input — it forgets, loses track, or processes only a fragment. On the very inputs that exceed a bounded table's stored domain, then, a mind does not reliably out-perform the table either. This is Ned Block's reply (see \hyperref[source:source-block]{\textsc{Block — Psychologism and Behaviorism (1981)}}) on behalf of the lookup-table machine (see \hyperref[entry:concept-lookup-table-mind]{\textsc{Lookup-table mind}}).

\webheading{Scope}

This is a symmetry, or levelling, claim. It does not say that minds and tables are alike in general — only that on inputs beyond \emph{both} their capacities the mind's asserted advantage (see \hyperref[entry:claim-mind-handles-unbounded-inputs]{\textsc{open-ended responsiveness}}) is not clearly present. So the asymmetry that the \hyperref[entry:argument-lookup-table-combinatorial-impossibility]{\textsc{combinatorial-impossibility reply}} needs may not obtain in the domain that actually matters.

Who would deny it: someone holding that a mind's response to over-long inputs — graceful degradation, structural or recursive processing — is still relevantly \emph{appropriate} where a table's blind truncation is not; that is, that the \emph{manner} of failure differs even when both ``fail.''

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-finite-table-misses-inputs]{\textsc{Finite table misses inputs}} (the table misses inputs outside its stored keys): this is the converse levelling claim about the \emph{mind's} finitude, deployed to neutralise the asymmetry.

\begin{plainterms}
Block's reply on behalf of the cheat-sheet machine: a real mind isn't limitless either. Past some length or complexity, your memory and attention give out — you forget, lose the thread, or take in only a fragment of a huge input. So on exactly the monster inputs a finite stored table can't handle, a real person doesn't clearly do better. That blunts the claim that minds always out-perform tables. (The pushback: a mind fails \emph{gracefully}, still doing something sensible, while a table just blindly chops off whatever it can't store — so even when both ``fail,'' they fail in different ways.)
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-lookup-table-mind]{\textsc{Lookup-table mind}} — the machine this claim defends against the charge that minds always out-perform it.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-finite-table-misses-inputs]{\textsc{Finite table misses inputs}} — the converse point about the table's finitude that this claim levels against.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-mind-handles-unbounded-inputs]{\textsc{Open-ended responsiveness of minds}} — the asserted advantage of minds that this claim questions in the over-capacity domain.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-lookup-table-combinatorial-impossibility]{\textsc{Combinatorial-impossibility reply}} — the reply whose required asymmetry this claim aims to undercut.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{argument-finite-self-model-incomplete}{Finite self-model incompleteness}{Argument \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{Finite self-model incompleteness}{A finite system cannot completely represent its own current state (a capacity bound)}{$\vdash$}
No finite system can hold a complete, exact picture of its own current state inside that very state and still have room left to do anything with it. The point is a counting argument — a pigeonhole bound — with a regress as its dynamical echo, and it is the formal backbone of the claim that any usable self-model is necessarily partial (see \hyperref[entry:claim-complete-self-representation-impossible]{\textsc{Self-representation incompleteness}}).

\webheading{Setup}

Model the instantaneous state as an element of a finite set $\Sigma$ (equivalently, $N$ activations over a finite alphabet, total capacity $C = \log_2|\Sigma|$ bits). ``Complete simultaneous self-access'' means the state factors as $s = (R, U)$ — a self-model part $R$ and a remainder $U$ (perception, control, the read-out machinery itself) — and the \emph{whole} current state $s$ is recoverable from $R$ alone. Losslessness means the map $s \mapsto R(s)$ is injective on $\Sigma$.

\webheading{The bound (deductive)}

\begin{enumerate}
  \item The self-model is part of the state (see \hyperref[entry:claim-self-model-is-part-of-state]{\textsc{Self-model as part of the state}}): $\Sigma = \mathcal{R}\times\mathcal{U}$, so $|\Sigma| = |\mathcal{R}|\cdot|\mathcal{U}|$. There is no state-external register — this is the internal case; drop it and you have an \emph{external} observer, who can have full access.
  \item Lossless recovery of $s$ from $R$ requires $s \mapsto R$ injective, hence $|\mathcal{R}| \ge |\Sigma| = |\mathcal{R}|\cdot|\mathcal{U}|$.
  \item Therefore $|\mathcal{U}| \le 1$: the remainder is constant — zero spare capacity. The system would be \emph{nothing but} its self-model, with nothing left to perceive, act, or run the read-out.
\end{enumerate}

So no finite system can hold a lossless model of its entire current state and have any capacity left to use it; any usable self-model is necessarily lossy or partial (see \hyperref[entry:claim-complete-self-representation-impossible]{\textsc{Self-representation incompleteness}}). This is \hyperref[entry:claim-inside-vantage-access-limited]{\textsc{the limited inside vantage}} turned into a structural necessity; it underwrites the architectural reading of the explanatory gap (see \hyperref[entry:claim-self-explanation-gap-can-be-architectural]{\textsc{Architectural self-explanation gap}}) and refutes the optimist's \hyperref[entry:claim-full-self-access-achievable]{\textsc{full-self-access thesis}}.

\webheading{Regress form}

The dynamical version of the same bound: if \emph{forming} $R$ adds to the state, completeness must cover the post-formation state — including $R$ itself; representing that addition needs $R'$, then $R''$, and so on. In a finite state the series must terminate, meaning some level adds no information: the self-model is incomplete. This is the homunculus regress — Royce's map-within-the-map (see \hyperref[source:source-royce-1899]{\textsc{Royce — The World and the Individual, First Series (1899)}}) — made quantitative.

\webheading{Weakest premise and the escapes}

Premise (1), together with the three modal words \emph{simultaneous, internal, lossless}, marks the escapes — none of which restores complete internal self-access.

\begin{itemize}
  \item \emph{External} vantage denies (1): an outside instrument is not part of $\Sigma$, so it can have full access — the internal/external asymmetry of \hyperref[entry:claim-inside-vantage-access-limited]{\textsc{Limited inside vantage}}.
  \item \emph{Sequential} read-out drops ``simultaneous'': a system can report its state piecemeal or onto an external tape, but each snapshot is stale once the next step runs, so it is never complete-at-an-instant.
  \item \emph{Lossy or compressed} self-models drop ``lossless'': fine and ubiquitous — the bound forbids only completeness, leaving functional introspection (see \hyperref[entry:claim-llm-functional-introspection]{\textsc{LLM functional introspection}}) intact.
  \item \emph{Quine / Kleene recursion theorem}: a program can output its own \emph{source}, but source is a compressed static description, not a lossless image of the runtime state (program counter, stack, variables, plus the copy itself), and it is emitted sequentially to an external medium.
\end{itemize}

\webheading{Physical pedigree}

A sharper, measurement-theoretic form is proved by Breuer (see \hyperref[source:source-breuer-1995]{\textsc{Thomas Breuer — The Impossibility of Accurate State Self-Measurements (Philosophy of Science, 1995)}}): no observer can distinguish all present states of a system in which it is properly contained, for classical and quantum theories alike, deterministic or stochastic. It adds two things. First, it concerns \emph{measurement} over time, so the indistinguishable state-pairs persist even with a perfect apparatus and unlimited time — partly closing the \emph{sequential} escape, since the limit is structural rather than an instantaneous accident. Second, its internal/external asymmetry is explicit: an apparatus \emph{outside} the observed system generally \emph{can} distinguish all states, which is exactly \hyperref[entry:claim-inside-vantage-access-limited]{\textsc{Limited inside vantage}}. Two cautions: Breuer stresses that the result only \emph{resembles} Gödel's theorem (sharing the use of self-reference and nothing more), so the Gödelian framing of \hyperref[entry:claim-explanation-must-be-internally-traversable]{\textsc{Internally traversable explanation}} is a separate analogy; and for a deterministic discrete model the operative content is his general/classical argument, not the quantum EPR corollary.

\webheading{Distinct from neighbours}

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-inside-vantage-access-limited]{\textsc{Limited inside vantage}} (which it supports): that \emph{observes} that the internal vantage is poorer; this \emph{proves} the poverty is structural and unremovable for any finite system. Distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-cognitive-closure-exists]{\textsc{Possibility of cognitive closure}} (McGinn's thesis about forming \emph{concepts}, \hyperref[source:source-mcginn-1989]{\textsc{McGinn — Can We Solve the Mind–Body Problem? (1989)}}): this is a capacity bound on \emph{representing one's own state}, independent of concept-possession.

\begin{plainterms}
Imagine trying to keep, inside your head, a perfectly complete and exact snapshot of everything in your head right now. The snapshot is itself in your head, so it would have to include a snapshot of itself, which would have to include a snapshot of that, and so on forever — and a finite head runs out of room. The maths here makes that precise: a part can't losslessly stand in for the whole it belongs to, so a complete self-portrait would have to use up the entire system, leaving nothing left to think or act with. The upshot isn't that self-knowledge is impossible — rough, compressed, or step-by-step self-models are fine — only that \emph{complete, all-at-once} self-knowledge from the inside is ruled out for any finite machine, brains included. An outside observer with the right instruments faces no such limit.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-self-model-is-part-of-state]{\textsc{Self-model as part of the state}} — premise (1): the self-model is realised within the state.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-complete-self-representation-impossible]{\textsc{Self-representation incompleteness}} — the conclusion this argument establishes.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-inside-vantage-access-limited]{\textsc{Limited inside vantage}} — the access poverty this proves structural; supported here.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-self-explanation-gap-can-be-architectural]{\textsc{Architectural self-explanation gap}} — the architectural reading this underwrites.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-full-self-access-achievable]{\textsc{Complete internal self-access}} — the optimist thesis this refutes.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-cognitive-closure-exists]{\textsc{Possibility of cognitive closure}} — distinguished: that is about concepts, this about state-representation.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-introspective-gap-varieties]{\textsc{Varieties of introspective explanatory gap}} — the catalogue whose architectural-access member this floors.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-access-vs-phenomenal-consciousness]{\textsc{Access vs. phenomenal consciousness}} — this is an access result, silent on phenomenality.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{claim-finite-table-misses-inputs}{Finite table misses inputs}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{Finite table misses inputs}{For any finite lookup table there are finite inputs outside its stored domain, to which it can only ``respond'' by ignoring the excess of the input}{$\odot$}
A \hyperref[entry:concept-lookup-table-mind]{\textsc{lookup table}} stores a finite set of input→output pairs, but the space of possible finite inputs — token strings longer or more numerous than any the table anticipates — has no upper bound. So for any finite table there exist finite inputs that are not among its keys. On such an input the table has no appropriate stored response; at most it can emit an output by \emph{discarding the part of the input that exceeds its keys}, treating a longer or novel input as one of the finitely many it already knows.

\webheading{Scope}

This is a near-combinatorial point about \emph{finite} stored maps against an unbounded input space. It does not assert that minds are infinite, nor that no large table is practically adequate — only that, for any fixed finite table, some inputs fall outside it. It is silent on whether those uncovered inputs are ones a real, finite-lifetime mind would ever actually receive; that contested bridge is \hyperref[entry:claim-mind-handles-unbounded-inputs]{\textsc{whether a mind handles unbounded inputs}}. As stated the claim is denied by essentially no one: the dispute lies not here but at the asymmetry premise — whether a mind does better than the table on the uncovered inputs.

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-lookup-table-possible]{\textsc{the claim that a finite table can match a mind's full behavioural profile}}: this is the combinatorial fact that motivates denying that one, identifying the inputs on which a finite table must fall short.

\begin{plainterms}
A stored answer-table only holds finitely many input→answer pairs, but the inputs you could throw at it (ever-longer strings of words, say) never run out. So however big the table, there are always inputs it has no entry for — and all it can do then is ignore the unfamiliar part and treat a brand-new input as one it already knows. (This much is barely deniable; the real fight is over whether those uncovered inputs are ones a real, finite-lifetime mind would ever actually meet.)
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-mind-handles-unbounded-inputs]{\textsc{Open-ended responsiveness of minds}} — the contested bridge: whether a mind actually outdoes the table on the uncovered inputs.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-lookup-table-possible]{\textsc{Lookup-table possibility}} — the separability claim this combinatorial fact is marshalled against.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-lookup-table-mind]{\textsc{Lookup-table mind}} — the finite stored map this claim describes the limits of.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{concept-order-of-representation}{First-order vs. higher-order states}{Concept}{First-order vs. higher-order states}{First-order vs higher-order mental states}{$\blacklozenge$}
Mental states can be sorted by their \emph{order} — by what they are \emph{about}. A \emph{first-order} state is directed at the world or the body: a perception of red, a belief that it is raining, a pain. A \emph{higher-order} state is directed at one's own \emph{mental} states: a thought or perception \emph{about} a first-order state, such as the thought ``I am seeing red'' or an awareness of one's own pain. Orders can iterate — a third-order state is about a second-order one — but the philosophically loaded contrast is simply first- versus higher-order.

\webheading{Key terms}

The theories that use this distinction invoke several more specific notions:

\begin{itemize}
  \item \emph{First-order representation} — world-directed content; the state represents how things stand outside.
  \item \emph{Higher-order thought} (HOT) — an occurrent thought about a current mental state (see \hyperref[source:source-rosenthal-2005]{\textsc{Rosenthal — Consciousness and Mind (2005)}}).
  \item \emph{Higher-order perception} (HOP), or \emph{inner sense} — a quasi-perceptual monitoring of one's own states (see \hyperref[source:source-lycan-1996]{\textsc{Lycan — Consciousness and Experience (1996)}}, \hyperref[source:source-armstrong-1968]{\textsc{Armstrong — A Materialist Theory of the Mind (1968)}}), an idea with roots in Locke's ``inner sense'' (see \hyperref[source:source-locke-1689]{\textsc{Locke — An Essay Concerning Human Understanding (1689)}}).
  \item \emph{Dispositional higher-order} content — content that need only be poised or available, not actually entertained (see \hyperref[source:source-carruthers-2000]{\textsc{Carruthers — Phenomenal Consciousness: A Naturalistic Theory (2000)}}).
\end{itemize}

\webheading{The dispute it feeds}

The distinction does its main work in the question of \emph{what makes a state conscious}:

\begin{itemize}
  \item \emph{Higher-order theories} (see \hyperref[entry:position-higher-order-theories]{\textsc{Higher-order theories of consciousness}}, \hyperref[entry:claim-consciousness-is-higher-order-representation]{\textsc{consciousness as higher-order representation}}) hold that a first-order state is conscious only if a suitable higher-order state represents it — consciousness is the mind representing itself.
  \item \emph{First-order representationalism} (see \hyperref[entry:position-representationalism]{\textsc{Representationalism}}, \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-character-is-representational]{\textsc{phenomenal character as representational}}) holds that phenomenal character is fixed by \emph{first-order} world-directed content, with no higher-order state required. \hyperref[entry:position-global-workspace]{\textsc{Global workspace theory}} likewise needs only wide availability, not a dedicated higher-order state.
\end{itemize}

\webheading{Keeping it apart from neighbouring distinctions}

\emph{Order} cross-cuts \hyperref[entry:concept-access-vs-phenomenal-consciousness]{\textsc{the access/phenomenal distinction}}: a higher-order state is one way content can become access-conscious, but ``order'' concerns \emph{what a state is about}, not access versus feel. \emph{Introspection} and self-monitoring just are a matter of having higher-order states about one's own mind — the structure behind \hyperref[entry:claim-llm-functional-introspection]{\textsc{functional introspection in LLMs}} and \hyperref[entry:claim-privileged-first-person-access]{\textsc{privileged first-person access}}. And order (world- versus mind-directed) is distinct again from the \emph{first-person versus third-person} contrast (whose vantage one takes): a first-order state can be access-conscious, and a higher-order state can itself be unconscious.

\begin{plainterms}
Thoughts come in layers depending on what they're \emph{about}. A \textbf{first-order} state is about the world — seeing the apple is red, believing it'll rain, feeling a pain. A \textbf{higher-order} state is about \emph{your own mind} — noticing that you're seeing red, thinking ``I'm in pain.'' (You can stack further — a thought about that thought — but the key split is just world-directed vs mind-directed.)

Why it matters here: one camp says a state only becomes \emph{conscious} when a higher-order state notices it (consciousness = the mind watching itself); the rival says the conscious feel is already in the plain first-order experience of the world, with no inner watcher needed. Several nodes take sides in that fight — this entry just pins down the vocabulary so you don't need the background.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:position-higher-order-theories]{\textsc{Higher-order theories of consciousness}} / \hyperref[entry:claim-consciousness-is-higher-order-representation]{\textsc{Consciousness as higher-order representation}} — the camp that makes higher-order states the mark of consciousness.
  \item \hyperref[entry:position-representationalism]{\textsc{Representationalism}} / \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-character-is-representational]{\textsc{Phenomenal character as representational}} — the first-order rival: phenomenal character is fixed at the first-order level.
  \item \hyperref[entry:position-global-workspace]{\textsc{Global workspace theory}} — needs broadcast, not a dedicated higher-order state.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-access-vs-phenomenal-consciousness]{\textsc{Access vs. phenomenal consciousness}} — a separate distinction this one cross-cuts.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-llm-functional-introspection]{\textsc{LLM functional introspection}} / \hyperref[entry:claim-privileged-first-person-access]{\textsc{Privileged first-person access}} — both turn on having higher-order states about one's own mind.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{concept-first-person-access}{First-person access}{Concept \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{First-person access}{First-person access (privileged access)}{$\blacklozenge$}
\emph{First-person access} is the direct, non-inferential acquaintance a subject has with its own conscious states: the way you know your own pain without watching your behaviour or reasoning your way to it, simply feeling it from the inside. It is standardly contrasted with \emph{third-person access}, the inferential route by which anyone comes to beliefs about \emph{another} subject's mental states — reading them off behaviour, testimony, or theory. The concept names a lopsidedness built into knowledge of minds: your own current experience is given to you directly, while every other mind you reach only at a remove.

\webheading{Two senses}

The term is used in two related but separable ways, and arguments that invoke it should say which they mean.

In the \emph{epistemic} sense, first-person access is a privileged \emph{warrant}: a subject's reports about its own present experience are non-inferential, and on the strongest versions \emph{incorrigible} — they cannot be mistaken. When you sincerely judge that you are in pain, no further evidence is needed and (on this strong reading) none could overturn it.

In the \emph{metaphysical} or \emph{acquaintance} sense, it names a relation of direct \emph{presentation}: the state is simply given to its subject, not merely tracked by a reliable belief-forming channel. This acquaintance notion descends from Bertrand Russell's distinction between knowledge \emph{by acquaintance} and knowledge \emph{by description} (see \hyperref[source:source-russell-1911]{\textsc{Russell — Knowledge by Acquaintance and Knowledge by Description (1911)}}) — the difference between being directly presented with a thing and merely knowing truths about it.

\webheading{The disputes it feeds}

The asymmetry between knowing one's own states directly and knowing others' only by inference is put to work in two opposite directions. Pointed \emph{outward}, it is offered as a ground for a moral-status asymmetry: that direct acquaintance with one's own suffering, absent for any machine, licenses regarding oneself as a bearer of moral status in a way one is not licensed to regard the machine (the move made in \hyperref[entry:argument-first-person-access]{\textsc{First-person-access asymmetry}}, which leans on \hyperref[entry:claim-acquaintance-necessary-for-moral-status-warrant]{\textsc{Acquaintance as moral-status condition}} and bears on the \hyperref[entry:question-llm-moral-status]{\textsc{moral status of LLMs}}). Pointed back at that move, the same asymmetry is argued to prove too much — since the access gap holds between \emph{any} two subjects — and so to single out nothing (the reply in \hyperref[entry:argument-access-symmetry-rebuttal]{\textsc{Access-symmetry rebuttal}}).

A neighbouring debate turns on access to one's \emph{own} qualia: whether introspecting one's experience is itself a functional process or a species of acquaintance. That question drives \hyperref[entry:claim-dancing-qualia-presupposes-functional-introspection]{\textsc{Dancing-qualia introspection premise}} and the \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-concept-isolation-tradeoff]{\textsc{phenomenal-concept trade-off}}. This entry only names the term those bodies use in passing; it takes no stand on the nature of self-access.

\begin{plainterms}
There's a lopsidedness in how you know about minds. You know your \emph{own} pain directly — you don't watch your own behavior and deduce ``I must be in pain,'' you just feel it from the inside. But anyone else's pain you only ever know \emph{indirectly}, from the outside: their wincing, their words, your best guess about them. That special, direct, inside knowledge of your own experience is what ``first-person access'' names. The debates it feeds ask: does that privileged inside view actually settle anything about who deserves moral consideration — and is your access to your \emph{own} feelings itself something special, or just more of the mind's ordinary machinery?
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-first-person-access]{\textsc{First-person-access asymmetry}} — uses the access asymmetry to argue for a moral-status asymmetry favouring oneself over a machine.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-access-symmetry-rebuttal]{\textsc{Access-symmetry rebuttal}} — the reply that the access gap is reciprocal and so singles out no one.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-acquaintance-necessary-for-moral-status-warrant]{\textsc{Acquaintance as moral-status condition}} — the premise that direct acquaintance is required to be justified that ``someone is there to be wronged.''
  \item \hyperref[entry:question-llm-moral-status]{\textsc{Moral status of LLMs}} — the live issue the asymmetry argument feeds into.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-dancing-qualia-presupposes-functional-introspection]{\textsc{Dancing-qualia introspection premise}} — the neighbouring dispute over whether introspective self-access is functional.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-concept-isolation-tradeoff]{\textsc{Phenomenal-concept trade-off}} — whether a concept can be informative enough to ground self-knowledge yet isolated enough to deflate the explanatory gap.
\end{seealso}
