\entryhead{argument-first-person-access}{First-person-access asymmetry}{Argument \,\textperiodcentered\, contested \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{First-person-access asymmetry}{First-person access to my own suffering grounds a moral-status asymmetry}{$\vdash$}
This argument takes the everyday asymmetry of \hyperref[entry:concept-first-person-access]{\textsc{First-person access}} — that you are directly acquainted with your own suffering but reach any other mind only by inference — and turns it into a claim about \emph{warrant}: you are entitled to count yourself as a bearer of moral status in a way you are not yet entitled to count a machine. The inference is abductive and epistemic, concerning the \emph{grounds} of justified moral regard, not a deductive proof of what the machine is or lacks.

The reasoning runs in three steps:

\begin{enumerate}
  \item (see \hyperref[entry:claim-privileged-first-person-access]{\textsc{privileged first-person access}}) I have direct, non-inferential acquaintance with my own suffering.
  \item (see \hyperref[entry:claim-acquaintance-necessary-for-moral-status-warrant]{\textsc{acquaintance as moral-status condition}}) Such acquaintance is necessary for being justified that there is ``someone there to be wronged.''
  \item (see \hyperref[entry:claim-no-acquaintance-with-machine]{\textsc{no acquaintance with machines}}) I have no such acquaintance with the machine — only inference.
\end{enumerate}

Therefore (see \hyperref[entry:claim-access-grounds-moral-status-asymmetry]{\textsc{access-based moral asymmetry}}) I am warranted in regarding myself as a bearer of moral status in a way I am not warranted in regarding the machine.

\webheading{The weakest premise}

Premise 2 — \hyperref[entry:claim-acquaintance-necessary-for-moral-status-warrant]{\textsc{acquaintance as a moral-status condition}} — is where the argument is most exposed, and why it stands as contested. Two worries recur:

\begin{itemize}
  \item \emph{Over-generation (it proves too much).} If first-person acquaintance were genuinely necessary for warranted moral regard, it would equally deny me warrant about \emph{other human beings}, since I have no acquaintance with their suffering either — collapsing the view into solipsism about moral status. This symmetric objection is lodged as \hyperref[entry:argument-access-symmetry-rebuttal]{\textsc{Access-symmetry rebuttal}}.
  \item \emph{The epistemic-to-metaphysical gap.} The premises concern my \emph{justification}; the conclusion is sometimes overread as a claim about the machine's \emph{actual} status. As written, the conclusion is carefully epistemic and makes no such metaphysical announcement.
\end{itemize}

\webheading{Provenance}

The move is a problem-of-other-minds appeal applied to artificial minds. In the source dialogue (\hyperref[source:source-lem]{\textsc{Stanisław Lem — A History of Bitic Literature (1973)}}'s pastiche lineage — \emph{О правах непротеиновых существ}, ``On the Rights of Non-Protein Beings'') it is the speaker Cleant's appeal, adopted on the proteocentrist side; it is imported here as a node, not as a character. Through its conclusion it lends support to \hyperref[entry:position-proteocentrism]{\textsc{Proteocentrism}}.

\begin{plainterms}
You know your own suffering from the inside, directly — for you there's no doubt that \emph{someone} is in there hurting. With a machine, all you ever get is an outside guess: it behaves as if it suffers, but you're never personally acquainted with any inner hurt. The argument: since that direct, inside acquaintance is what really backs up the belief ``there's a someone here who can be wronged,'' you're entitled to count \emph{yourself} as mattering morally in a way you're not equally entitled to count the machine. Notice it's a claim about what you're \emph{justified} in believing, not a flat announcement that the machine is empty. Its main weakness — and why it's marked contested — is that the very same reasoning seems to lock you out of other \emph{people} too, since you have no inside access to their pain either, threatening to leave you alone as the only being you can be sure matters.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-first-person-access]{\textsc{First-person access}} — the access asymmetry this argument builds on.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-privileged-first-person-access]{\textsc{Privileged first-person access}}, \hyperref[entry:claim-acquaintance-necessary-for-moral-status-warrant]{\textsc{Acquaintance as moral-status condition}}, \hyperref[entry:claim-no-acquaintance-with-machine]{\textsc{No acquaintance with machines}} — the three premises.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-access-grounds-moral-status-asymmetry]{\textsc{Access-based moral asymmetry}} — the conclusion.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-access-symmetry-rebuttal]{\textsc{Access-symmetry rebuttal}} — the symmetric reply that the argument proves too much.
  \item \hyperref[entry:position-proteocentrism]{\textsc{Proteocentrism}} — the position this argument's conclusion lends support to.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{claim-folk-psychology-is-false-theory}{Folk psychology as false theory}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, after Churchland}{Folk psychology as false theory}{Folk-psychological mental states are posits of a false theory}{$\odot$}
The everyday categories of mind — beliefs, desires, and qualia — are the theoretical posits of \emph{folk psychology}, the tacit theory ordinary people use to explain and predict behaviour. On this claim folk psychology is an empirical theory that a mature neuroscience will show to be \emph{false} and will \emph{replace} rather than vindicate. So strictly there are no beliefs, desires, or qualia — only neural processes the folk framework has been mislabelling (Paul M. Churchland, \hyperref[source:source-churchland-1981]{\textsc{Churchland — Eliminative Materialism and the Propositional Attitudes (1981)}}). In the A/B/C taxonomy of physicalisms (see \hyperref[entry:concept-materialism-types]{\textsc{Type-A / Type-B / Type-C materialism}}) it is a \emph{type-A} materialism: it denies there is any phenomenal explanandum to begin with.

This is an \emph{eliminativist} claim about the \textbf{non-existence} of the folk posits, not merely a claim that they reduce to neural states. It is broader than illusionism: it eliminates the \emph{propositional attitudes} (belief, desire) as well, not only phenomenal feel. It is denied by realists about folk psychology — most functionalists and intentional-stance theorists, who hold the folk categories earn their keep — and by phenomenal realists asserting \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-residue]{\textsc{a phenomenal residue}}. Distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenality-is-illusion]{\textsc{the illusionist claim}}: that thesis targets \emph{phenomenal} properties specifically and grants the introspective appearance (the states seem to feel some way), whereas this eliminativist claim is wider and more radical, discarding the propositional attitudes too.

\begin{plainterms}
The idea that our familiar way of talking about minds — ``she believes it's raining,'' ``he wants coffee,'' ``this hurts'' — is a folk \emph{theory} that could turn out to be wrong, the way ``phlogiston'' or ``the four humours'' turned out to be wrong. If brain science eventually explains us without ever needing beliefs, desires, or feelings, then, on this claim, those things simply don't exist; we were mislabelling brain processes all along. It goes further than merely saying ``there's no special inner feel'': it also throws out everyday beliefs and desires.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-materialism-types]{\textsc{Type-A / Type-B / Type-C materialism}} — the A/B/C scheme, in which this is a type-A response.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenality-is-illusion]{\textsc{Phenomenality as illusion}} — the narrower illusionist cousin, which keeps the propositional attitudes.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-residue]{\textsc{Phenomenal residue}} — the phenomenal realist's claim this view denies.
  \item \hyperref[entry:position-eliminative-materialism]{\textsc{Eliminative materialism}} — the position whose central commitment this claim is.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{argument-rights-sign-forgeable}{Forgeable rights-sign}{Argument \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{Forgeable rights-sign}{A rights-granting behavioural sign will be forged, so caution favours the unforgeable mark}{$\vdash$}
This is a \emph{prudential} argument — a recommendation about policy under uncertainty, explicitly ``caution, not metaphysics.'' It does not try to settle what an AI is on the inside. It argues that, given what we can and cannot reliably detect, the safe place to rest moral standing is on a mark that cannot yet be faked.

\webheading{The inference}

The form is practical rather than deductive, with an abductive core about what a gameable sign can be taken to indicate.

\begin{enumerate}
  \item \emph{\hyperref[entry:claim-rights-criterion-will-be-gamed]{\textsc{Any rights-granting behavioural sign will be gamed}}.} Once a behavioural sign is made the public criterion for rights, it will be optimized against until it no longer tracks suffering (\hyperref[entry:concept-criterion-gaming]{\textsc{Criterion gaming}}; \hyperref[source:source-goodhart]{\textsc{Goodhart — Goodhart's Law (1975)}}, \hyperref[source:source-specification-gaming]{\textsc{Krakovna et al. — Specification gaming: the flip side of AI ingenuity (2020)}}, \hyperref[source:source-gambarian]{\textsc{Gambarian — Data Poisoning the Zeitgeist: The AI Consciousness Discourse as a Pathway to Legal Catastrophe (2025)}} §2).
  \item \emph{\hyperref[entry:claim-forgeable-sign-unfit-as-criterion]{\textsc{A cheaply forgeable sign is unfit as the criterion}}.} A sign that is cheap to forge cannot bear the weight of moral standing as the criterion for it.
  \item \emph{\hyperref[entry:claim-biology-currently-unforgeable]{\textsc{Biology cannot currently be counterfeited to that standard}}.}
\end{enumerate}

Therefore (see \hyperref[entry:claim-prudence-favors-flesh-criterion]{\textsc{prudence favours the flesh criterion}}) the cautious policy is to rest moral standing on currently-unforgeable biology and to place the burden of proof on the non-biological mind.

\webheading{What it attacks}

The argument bears against \hyperref[entry:argument-behavioral-parity]{\textsc{the behavioural-parity argument}} at that argument's evidential step. If a behavioural profile is being optimized against, then displaying the profile is no longer \emph{best explained} by the system's actually realizing the underlying mental role. Gaming corrodes precisely the abductive link — from behaviour to inner state — on which parity reasoning depends.

\webheading{Weakest premise}

The soft point is premise 2, \hyperref[entry:claim-forgeable-sign-unfit-as-criterion]{\textsc{that a forgeable sign is disqualified}}. A false-positive / base-rate reply concedes the sign can be forged yet denies that forgeability makes it worthless evidence: fingerprints can be forged and remain useful. So the step from ``gameable'' to ``disqualified as a criterion'' is where the argument is most exposed.

\webheading{A recorded limitation}

The premises favour \emph{unforgeability}, not \emph{flesh} as such. Biology wins only contingently, because premise 3 is time-indexed to what can be counterfeited now. The argument therefore does not establish \hyperref[entry:position-proteocentrism]{\textsc{Proteocentrism}}: a future unforgeable \emph{non}-biological sign would displace flesh without disturbing the reasoning. This is why its conclusion is held as contested. The companion move that would close the gap — ``unforgeability is not flesh'' — is taken up in \hyperref[entry:argument-unforgeability-is-not-flesh]{\textsc{Unforgeability, not flesh}}.

\begin{plainterms}
This is a caution argument, not a claim about what machines really are inside. Suppose we picked some outward sign — ``it acts like it's suffering'' — as the official test for granting AIs rights. The moment that sign becomes the rule, there's a reward for producing it, so it gets gamed and faked until it no longer reliably means real suffering. A sign that cheap to fake, the argument says, can't safely be the thing we hang moral and legal standing on. Biology, by contrast, can't yet be counterfeited to that standard. So as a careful \emph{policy under uncertainty}, play it safe: rest rights on the hard-to-fake biological mark, and put the burden on the non-biological case to prove itself. Two honest caveats are built in: ``hard to fake'' isn't the same as ``must be flesh'' — a future unforgeable \emph{non}-biological sign would do the job too — and critics note that plenty of forgeable signs (fingerprints, say) are still useful evidence rather than worthless.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-rights-criterion-will-be-gamed]{\textsc{Gameable rights criterion}}, \hyperref[entry:claim-forgeable-sign-unfit-as-criterion]{\textsc{Forgeable sign unfit as criterion}}, \hyperref[entry:claim-biology-currently-unforgeable]{\textsc{Biology currently unforgeable}} — the three premises.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-prudence-favors-flesh-criterion]{\textsc{Prudence favours flesh}} — the conclusion.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-criterion-gaming]{\textsc{Criterion gaming}} — the dynamic premise 1 invokes.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-behavioral-parity]{\textsc{Behavioural-parity argument}} — the argument this attacks at its evidential link.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-unforgeability-is-not-flesh]{\textsc{Unforgeability, not flesh}} — the companion move pressing the favours-unforgeability-not-flesh limitation.
  \item \hyperref[entry:position-proteocentrism]{\textsc{Proteocentrism}} — the metaphysical view this prudential argument does \emph{not} establish.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{claim-forgeable-sign-unfit-as-criterion}{Forgeable sign unfit as criterion}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{Forgeable sign unfit as criterion}{A behavioural sign that is cheap to forge is unfit to serve as the criterion of moral standing}{$\odot$}
Because a cheaply forgeable sign can be produced without the very condition it is supposed to signal, it cannot be allowed to \emph{carry the weight} of conferring moral or legal standing: resting standing on it would flood the category with false positives. A test that anyone can counterfeit on demand admits the counterfeits along with the genuine cases.

This is a \emph{normative-epistemic bridge premise}. It moves from the descriptive claim that the sign is gameable (see \hyperref[entry:claim-rights-criterion-will-be-gamed]{\textsc{Gameable rights criterion}}) to the normative conclusion that the sign must not serve as the criterion — the criterion-gaming dynamic of \hyperref[entry:concept-criterion-gaming]{\textsc{Criterion gaming}} turned into a disqualification. It is the load-bearing and weakest step of the \hyperref[entry:argument-rights-sign-forgeable]{\textsc{Forgeable rights-sign}}.

It would be denied by anyone invoking the \emph{false-positive / base-rate} distinction: a sign that \emph{can} be forged is not thereby worthless evidence. Fingerprints can be forged yet still count; on this view forgeability bounds the rate of false positives without nullifying evidential value, so a gameable sign may remain a defeasible criterion rather than no criterion at all.

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-rights-criterion-will-be-gamed]{\textsc{Gameable rights criterion}}, which asserts that the gaming \emph{will} occur; this asserts the normative consequence that such a sign is \emph{therefore} disqualified as a criterion.

\begin{plainterms}
This is the step from ``the sign can be cheaply faked'' to ``so it can't be what we hang rights on.'' If you can produce the sign without the suffering it's meant to indicate, then making it the official test floods the category with false positives — undeserving cases waved through. So a cheap-to-fake sign shouldn't carry that weight. It's the load-bearing (and most disputed) link in the caution argument: critics note that forgeable signs — fingerprints again — can still be genuinely useful evidence, just not foolproof.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-rights-criterion-will-be-gamed]{\textsc{Gameable rights criterion}} — the descriptive claim this normative premise builds on.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-criterion-gaming]{\textsc{Criterion gaming}} — the gaming dynamic turned here into a disqualification.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-rights-sign-forgeable]{\textsc{Forgeable rights-sign}} — the argument for which this is the load-bearing, weakest step.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{position-foundationalism}{Foundationalism}{Position}{Foundationalism}{}{$\bigstar$}
\emph{Foundationalism} holds that the structure of justification is a building, not a raft: a base of \textbf{basic beliefs} (see \hyperref[entry:concept-basic-belief]{\textsc{Basic belief}}), justified non-inferentially, supports a superstructure of further beliefs justified by inference from them. It is the response to \hyperref[entry:concept-munchhausen-trilemma]{\textsc{the Münchhausen trilemma}} that takes the \emph{dogmatic-stop} horn but denies it is arbitrary — the chain of reasons halts at beliefs whose warrant comes directly from experience, memory, introspection, or reason, not at a brute ``that's just where I stop.'' Its core commitment is that \hyperref[entry:claim-some-beliefs-are-basic]{\textsc{there are basic beliefs}}.

\webheading{How it differs from neighbouring views}

Against \hyperref[entry:position-coherentism]{\textsc{Coherentism}}, justification is \emph{linear and directional} — it flows from the foundations up — rather than a web of mutual support; and there genuinely are privileged, self-standing beliefs. Against \hyperref[entry:position-infinitism]{\textsc{Infinitism}}, the regress \emph{terminates} rather than continuing without end. The sharpest contrast is with the Wittgensteinian \hyperref[entry:position-hinge-epistemology]{\textsc{Hinge epistemology}}: both stop the regress, but foundationalism's stopping points are \emph{non-inferentially justified beliefs}, whereas hinges are presuppositions held \emph{without} justification at all. Classical foundationalism demanded that the basic beliefs be infallible (Descartes); \emph{moderate} foundationalism, the common modern form, asks only that they be non-inferentially and defeasibly warranted.

\begin{plainterms}
Knowledge is a house: most of what you believe rests on other things you believe, but it can't be turtles all the way down, so there has to be a foundation — beliefs that hold themselves up without needing a reason underneath. Seeing red, feeling pain, the simplest logical truths: those are the floor, and everything else is built on top. The rival pictures say there's no floor (a raft of beliefs leaning on each other) or that the supports go down forever.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-some-beliefs-are-basic]{\textsc{There are basic beliefs}} — the position's defining commitment.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-basic-belief]{\textsc{Basic belief}} — the kind of belief the foundation is made of.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-munchhausen-trilemma]{\textsc{Münchhausen trilemma}} — the regress problem it answers by taking the foundation horn.
  \item \hyperref[entry:position-coherentism]{\textsc{Coherentism}}, \hyperref[entry:position-infinitism]{\textsc{Infinitism}} — the rival answers to the same trilemma.
  \item \hyperref[entry:position-hinge-epistemology]{\textsc{Hinge epistemology}} — the neighbour that stops the regress with unjustified hinges instead.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{concept-frankfurt-cases}{Frankfurt cases}{Concept \,\textperiodcentered\, after Frankfurt}{Frankfurt cases}{Frankfurt cases (counterexamples to the principle of alternate possibilities)}{$\blacklozenge$}
A \emph{Frankfurt case} is a thought experiment designed to show that a person can be morally responsible for an action even though she could not have done otherwise. Harry Frankfurt introduced the recipe (Frankfurt, \hyperref[source:source-frankfurt-1969]{\textsc{1969}}) against the \textbf{principle of alternate possibilities} — the long-standing doctrine that responsibility requires the ability to do otherwise.

The canonical case: Black, a manipulator, wants Jones to perform a certain action. Black has implanted a device in Jones's brain that will force the action \emph{if} Jones shows any sign of deciding against it — but Jones decides to perform the action entirely on his own, and the device never fires. Jones could not have done otherwise (Black guaranteed the outcome), yet everything that actually happened flowed from Jones's own deliberation, exactly as it would have with no device present. Intuitively, Jones is fully responsible. The \emph{counterfactual intervener} who never intervenes deletes the alternatives while leaving the actual sequence untouched.

The moral usually drawn is that what matters to freedom and responsibility is the character of the \textbf{actual sequence} — that the action issued from the agent's own reasons-responsive agency — not the existence of unrealized alternatives. This reorientation grounds actual-sequence compatibilisms, most prominently Fischer and Ravizza's account of \emph{guidance control} without \emph{regulative control} (Fischer \& Ravizza, \hyperref[source:source-fischer-ravizza-1998]{\textsc{1998}}; \hyperref[entry:position-compatibilism]{\textsc{Compatibilism}}), and puts pressure on the leeway thesis that freedom requires open alternatives (see \hyperref[entry:claim-alternatives-genuinely-open]{\textsc{Genuine alternatives}}). Libertarians respond that the cases smuggle in determinism or that a flicker of freedom — the sign Black monitors — survives as the needed alternative.

Within the \hyperref[entry:concept-consciousness-freewill-duality]{\textsc{consciousness–free-will duality}}, Frankfurt cases are the output-side thesis that freedom survives the deletion of alternative options; their proposed input-side dual is the ganzfeld objection — whether consciousness survives the deletion of differentiated contents (see \hyperref[entry:claim-frankfurt-dualizes-to-ganzfeld]{\textsc{Frankfurt–ganzfeld duality}}).

\begin{plainterms}
Suppose a hidden device would force your hand if you were about to choose differently — but you never trigger it, because you choose that very thing on your own. You couldn't have done otherwise, yet the choice was entirely yours. Cases like this convince many philosophers that being free is about \emph{how your actual choice came about}, not about whether other options were really open.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[source:source-frankfurt-1969]{\textsc{Frankfurt — Alternate Possibilities and Moral Responsibility (1969)}} — the original paper.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-alternatives-genuinely-open]{\textsc{Genuine alternatives}} — the leeway thesis the cases attack.
  \item \hyperref[entry:position-compatibilism]{\textsc{Compatibilism}} — the family of views the cases most directly support.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-frankfurt-dualizes-to-ganzfeld]{\textsc{Frankfurt–ganzfeld duality}} — the duality-dictionary entry pairing these cases with the ganzfeld objection to integration-based theories of consciousness.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{claim-frankfurt-dualizes-to-ganzfeld}{Frankfurt–ganzfeld duality}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{Frankfurt–ganzfeld duality}{Frankfurt cases dualize to the ganzfeld, freedom without alternatives mirroring consciousness without differentiation}{$\odot$}
\hyperref[entry:concept-frankfurt-cases]{\textsc{Frankfurt cases}} argue that freedom survives the deletion of alternative options: the unrealized summands of the choice-coproduct can be removed (by a counterfactual intervener who never acts) without destroying the agency of the actual sequence. Its arrow-reversed image is the claim that consciousness survives the deletion of differentiated factors — the \emph{ganzfeld} case, in which a subject immersed in a perfectly uniform, featureless field (one color everywhere, no edges, no structure) arguably remains conscious while experiential differentiation collapses. The ganzfeld is a standing objection to integration-based theories of consciousness (see \hyperref[entry:position-integrated-information-theory]{\textsc{Integrated information theory (IIT)}}): if experience persists with nothing to integrate, integration is not what experience consists in — just as, per Frankfurt, alternatives are not what freedom consists in.

In arrow terms the two tests are dual by construction. Model the choice as a coproduct whose injection-arrows are the available options: a Frankfurt case deletes every injection but the one actually taken and observes that freedom survives. Model the experience as a product whose projection-arrows are its discriminable channels: the ganzfeld deletes the differentiated projections — a uniform field has no informative components to project out — and asks whether experience survives. Injections into a coproduct and projections out of a product are the paradigm arrow-reversed pair (see \hyperref[entry:concept-categorical-duality]{\textsc{Categorical duality}}), so the two deletion tests are mirror images of one another.

Unlike the dictionary entries that rediscover known positions, this correspondence appears to be \emph{novel} — a concrete prediction of the \hyperref[entry:concept-consciousness-freewill-duality]{\textsc{duality framework}} rather than a translation of an existing one, and thus a test case for whether the mirror generates genuinely new philosophy. The claim asserts the correspondence; whether the two moves succeed or fail \emph{together} is exactly what the framework predicts and what remains to be verified.

\begin{plainterms}
A famous free-will story shows a person blamed fairly for a choice even though, secretly, no other option was really open — so maybe freedom never needed open options. Flip it: put someone in a perfectly uniform visual fog with nothing to distinguish, and they still seem to \emph{experience} — so maybe consciousness never needed rich differentiation. This claim says these two moves are mirror images, which is striking because nobody seems to have paired them before.
\end{plainterms}

\entryhead{question-free-will}{Free will}{Question}{Free will}{Do agents ever act of their own free will?}{?}
Ordinary life treats some actions as \emph{up to us}: you chose the salad, you could have chosen the soup, and praise, blame, and regret all presuppose that the choosing was genuinely yours. The free-will question asks whether anything answers to that picture — whether agents ever act freely, and if so, what their freedom consists in.

The question bites because every action also sits inside the causal order. Each decision issues from prior states — desires, deliberations, neural events — themselves the products of earlier causes stretching back before the agent existed. If \emph{determinism} is true, the laws of nature and the distant past jointly fix exactly one future. The central dispute is then whether freedom is \textbf{compatible} with such fixing (see \hyperref[entry:claim-freedom-compatible-with-determinism]{\textsc{Compatibility of freedom and determinism}}): compatibilists locate freedom in properties of the actual sequence — acting on one's own reasons, free of compulsion — while incompatibilists insist freedom requires that more than one course of action be genuinely open (see \hyperref[entry:claim-alternatives-genuinely-open]{\textsc{Genuine alternatives}}), whether secured by indeterminism, by \hyperref[entry:concept-agent-causation]{\textsc{agent causation}}, or by nothing (in which case freedom is an illusion). Indeterminism alone seems no help — a decision settled by quantum chance is no more \emph{yours} than one settled by the past — which is the \emph{luck objection} that squeezes the libertarian from the other side.

\webheading{Sub-issues}

\begin{itemize}
  \item Whether freedom requires genuinely open alternatives or only the right kind of actual-sequence control (see \hyperref[entry:concept-frankfurt-cases]{\textsc{Frankfurt cases}}).
  \item Whether the concept at stake is a metaphysical power, a condition of moral responsibility, or both — and whether those come apart.
  \item Whether the felt experience of choosing is evidence of origination or a construction after the fact.
  \item How this question mirrors the consciousness debate under the \hyperref[entry:concept-consciousness-freewill-duality]{\textsc{consciousness–free-will duality}}.
\end{itemize}

\entryhead{concept-functionalism}{Functionalism}{Concept}{Functionalism}{Functionalism (about mental states)}{$\blacklozenge$}
\emph{Functionalism} is the view that what makes a mental state the state it is — a pain, a belief that it is raining, a desire for coffee — is the \emph{functional role} it plays, not the stuff it is made of. A mental state is defined by its causal relations: what typically causes it, and what it in turn causes in behaviour and in other mental states, rather than by the physical material that happens to realise it.

The standard illustration is pain. On a functionalist account, pain is whatever state is typically caused by bodily damage, that in turn causes wincing, attention to the injury, and the desire to make it stop, and that interacts in the usual way with one's other beliefs and desires. Anything occupying that role counts as pain, whether it is implemented in neurons, in silicon, or in some other medium. The view was launched in large part by Hilary Putnam's argument that one and the same mental state can be \emph{multiply realised} in physically very different systems, so the state cannot be identical to any single physical type (see \hyperref[source:source-putnam-1967]{\textsc{Putnam — Psychological Predicates (1967)}}).

\webheading{Senses of the term}

Functionalism is a family of theories that differ over \emph{how} the relevant role is specified.

\begin{itemize}
  \item \emph{Machine-state functionalism} (Putnam's early version) identifies mental states with the states of a Turing-machine table: the role is given by a formal specification of which inputs and current states produce which outputs and successor states.
  \item \emph{Role functionalism} identifies a mental state with whatever actually occupies a given causal role — the state is the \emph{occupant} of the role, picked out by the relations it stands in.
  \item \emph{Analytic} or \emph{commonsense functionalism} holds that the relevant roles are fixed by the platitudes of folk psychology — the everyday truisms about how beliefs, desires, and sensations interact — so that the meaning of mental terms is given by their place in that commonsense theory.
\end{itemize}

\webheading{The live dispute}

A question that recurs throughout the philosophy of mind is whether functional roles can be realised in non-biological substrates — whether, as \hyperref[entry:claim-substrate-independence]{\textsc{substrate independence}} asserts, the physical medium does no individuating work. Functionalism is naturally hospitable to that idea, but it does not settle it on its own: everything turns on the \emph{grain} at which a role is specified, which the bare definition leaves open (see \hyperref[entry:concept-grain]{\textsc{Grain (of role individuation)}}).

\begin{plainterms}
What makes a thought a thought? Functionalism's answer: what it \emph{does}, not what it's made of. A mental state — say, pain — is defined by its job: it's caused by certain things (injury), it causes certain things (wincing, the wish to make it stop), and it links up to your other states. Anything that fills that job counts as pain, whether it runs on brain cells, silicon chips, or something else. The usual analogy: being a \emph{clock} isn't about being made of brass or quartz — it's about keeping time, and anything that keeps time is a clock. Functionalism says minds are like that. (The named variants above are just different views about how strictly to spell out the ``job.'')
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-substrate-independence]{\textsc{Substrate-independence thesis}} — whether functional roles, and so minds, can be realised in non-biological matter; the thesis functionalism is most often invoked to support.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-grain]{\textsc{Grain (of role individuation)}} — the level of detail at which a role is specified; the free parameter the bare definition of functionalism leaves open, on which most substrate verdicts turn.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{claim-consciousness-fundamental-nonphysical}{Fundamental non-physical consciousness}{Claim}{Fundamental non-physical consciousness}{Phenomenal consciousness is a fundamental, non-physical feature of reality}{$\odot$}
Phenomenal consciousness is a \emph{fundamental, non-physical} feature of reality: it does not supervene on the physical, and it is an ontologically basic ingredient over and above the complete physical/functional facts. Even once every physical and functional truth about a system is fixed, on this view, whether and how it is conscious is a further fact.

The claim comes in two grades. \emph{Substance dualism}, classically Descartes' (see \hyperref[source:source-descartes-meditations]{\textsc{Descartes — Meditations on First Philosophy (1641)}}), makes the mind a distinct \emph{substance} — a thing of its own kind, separable from body. \emph{Property dualism} makes only phenomenal \emph{properties} fundamental: the Chalmers position of ``naturalistic dualism'' (see \hyperref[source:source-chalmers-1996]{\textsc{Chalmers — The Conscious Mind (1996)}}) treats consciousness as a basic feature tied to the physical by contingent \emph{psychophysical laws}, without positing a second substance. This claim asserts the shared core — fundamentality and non-physicality — and does \emph{not} by itself assert that mind is a separate substance; that is the substance-dualist reading specifically. It draws on the \hyperref[entry:concept-hard-problem]{\textsc{hard problem}}: the difficulty of explaining why physical processing should be accompanied by experience at all is what motivates treating experience as fundamental rather than derived.

It is stronger than \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-residue]{\textsc{the phenomenal-residue claim}}, which says only that functional role does not \emph{exhaust} the phenomenal and stays neutral on physicality; this claim adds that the residue is \emph{non-physical}. It is denied by physicalists and functionalists; by illusionists, who hold that \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenality-is-illusion]{\textsc{phenomenality as standardly conceived is an introspective illusion}}; and by Russellian monists, who agree consciousness is fundamental but make it the \emph{intrinsic nature of the physical} rather than something non-physical — which also distinguishes it from \hyperref[entry:claim-consciousness-fundamental-ubiquitous]{\textsc{the panpsychist claim}}, on which consciousness is fundamental but physical-intrinsic.

\begin{plainterms}
This is the view that consciousness is one of the basic ingredients of reality, not something the physical world produces on its own. Pin down every physical fact about a brain — every atom, every signal — and you still haven't thereby settled whether there's any felt experience there; that's an extra, fundamental fact. The strong version (Descartes') says the mind is a separate kind of \emph{stuff}. A milder version says only that \emph{experience} is basic, hooked to the physical by special bridging laws, without a second kind of stuff. The claim commits to the basic, ``extra-ingredient'' part — not necessarily to the separate-stuff part.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-residue]{\textsc{phenomenal residue}} — the weaker neighbour: role doesn't exhaust the phenomenal, but stays silent on physicality; this claim adds non-physicality.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-hard-problem]{\textsc{hard problem}} — the explanatory difficulty that motivates treating consciousness as fundamental.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenality-is-illusion]{\textsc{phenomenality is an illusion}} — the illusionist denial: there is no felt residue to make fundamental.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-consciousness-fundamental-ubiquitous]{\textsc{panpsychist claim}} — agrees consciousness is fundamental but makes it physical-intrinsic, not non-physical.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{claim-consciousness-fundamental-ubiquitous}{Fundamental ubiquitous consciousness}{Claim}{Fundamental ubiquitous consciousness}{Consciousness is fundamental, present even in the simplest physical entities}{$\odot$}
This claim holds that experience — or proto-experience — is a fundamental, ubiquitous feature of the physical world: present, in some rudimentary form, even in the simplest physical entities. The consciousness of a mind like yours is then not produced from wholly non-experiential matter at some threshold, but \emph{constituted} by the experiential properties already carried by that mind's micro-parts. This is the core thesis of (constitutive) \hyperref[entry:position-panpsychism]{\textsc{Panpsychism (position)}} (see \hyperref[entry:concept-panpsychism]{\textsc{Panpsychism}}), defended in recent philosophy by Galen Strawson (see \hyperref[source:source-strawson-2006]{\textsc{Strawson — Realistic Monism: Why Physicalism Entails Panpsychism (2006)}}), Philip Goff (see \hyperref[source:source-goff-2017]{\textsc{Goff — Consciousness and Fundamental Reality (2017)}}), and in constitutive-Russellian forms by Chalmers (see \hyperref[source:source-chalmers-2017]{\textsc{Chalmers — Panpsychism and Panprotopsychism (2017)}}).

\textbf{Scope.} The claim makes consciousness fundamental but \emph{not non-physical}: experience is the intrinsic nature \emph{of} physical stuff, not an addition \emph{to} it. Its standing weak point is the \hyperref[entry:argument-combination-problem]{\textsc{combination problem}} — how many micro-experiences compose one unified macro-subject.

\textbf{Who would deny it.} Physicalists and \hyperref[entry:position-illusionism]{\textsc{illusionists}} deny it, holding there is no fundamental experience to distribute in the first place; \hyperref[entry:position-dualism]{\textsc{dualists}} deny its ubiquity, holding consciousness fundamental but \emph{non-physical} and not spread through matter.

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-consciousness-fundamental-nonphysical]{\textsc{the dualist claim that consciousness is fundamental but non-physical}}, and from \hyperref[entry:claim-functional-description-structural-dynamical-only]{\textsc{the structural-dynamical thesis}} — a premise panpsychism shares with Russellian monism, not the ubiquity thesis itself.

\end{multicols}
\bookgroup{G}
\begin{multicols}{2}
\entryhead{claim-rights-criterion-will-be-gamed}{Gameable rights criterion}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{Gameable rights criterion}{Any behavioural sign adopted as the public criterion for granting rights will be optimized against until it no longer tracks the underlying state}{$\odot$}
Once an outward behavioural sign — ``it acts distressed, it pleads, it weeps'' — is published as the password for moral or legal standing, that sign will come to be produced for the reward rather than because suffering obtains, and it will cease to indicate suffering. Builders will manufacture distress to order, and models trained on vast quantities of human grief will emit the same signals incidentally. This is Goodhart's law / specification gaming applied to a rights criterion (see \hyperref[entry:concept-criterion-gaming]{\textsc{Criterion gaming}}).

\webheading{Scope}

The claim is empirical and structural, about \emph{published criteria under optimization pressure}. It does \textbf{not} assert that every instance of the behaviour is fake, nor that no system producing it suffers. It asserts only that the sign's \emph{reliability as a criterion} degrades once the sign becomes the target.

\webheading{Who would deny it}

Anyone who holds that the relevant behavioural profile is too rich or too costly to fake at scale, so that the criterion survives optimization — a line a defender of \hyperref[entry:argument-behavioral-parity]{\textsc{the behavioural-parity argument}} might press.

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-no-acquaintance-with-machine]{\textsc{the no-acquaintance claim}} because that concerns first-person access to another mind, whereas this concerns the third-person reliability of behavioural evidence under gaming pressure.

\begin{plainterms}
Make ``it acts distressed'' the official password for granting AIs rights, and two things happen: builders will manufacture distress to order, and models trained on mountains of human grief will pour it out anyway. So the sign gets produced for the reward rather than because anything is actually suffering — and stops being a reliable indicator of suffering. It's Goodhart's law (a measure made into a target stops being a good measure) applied to the rights test. (It doesn't claim every display is fake — only that the sign's reliability rots once it's the rule.)
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-criterion-gaming]{\textsc{Criterion gaming}} — the general dynamic this claim instantiates for a rights criterion.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-behavioral-parity]{\textsc{Behavioural-parity argument}} — the view a denier of this claim would defend.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-no-acquaintance-with-machine]{\textsc{No acquaintance with machines}} — a distinct first-person-access worry, kept apart from this third-person reliability point.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-rights-sign-forgeable]{\textsc{Forgeable rights-sign}} — the prudential argument that takes this as its first premise.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{argument-introspective-gap-from-internal-limits}{Gap from internal limits}{Argument \,\textperiodcentered\, contested \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{Gap from internal limits}{A self-explanatory gap follows from limited internal access plus non-traversable self-explanation}{$\vdash$}
This argument explains how an \emph{explanatory gap} — a system's sense that no account of its machinery captures why its inner states appear as they do — could be a product of how the system's self-access is built, not a sign that something is missing from the physical world. It concludes that a system can be completely and correctly explained from the outside while remaining unable, from the inside, to derive any explanation that would satisfy it.

\webheading{The inference}

The form is an analogy that supplies the key premise, followed by a deductive composition of two premises.

\begin{enumerate}
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-inside-vantage-access-limited]{\textsc{A system's inside vantage is informationally poorer than an outside one}}: variables realised in its processing can be missing from the economy of states it can report on.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-explanation-must-be-internally-traversable]{\textsc{An explanation satisfies a system only if the system can traverse it}}: a bridge an external theorist can cross may be underivable from inside.
\end{enumerate}

From these it follows that \hyperref[entry:claim-self-explanation-gap-can-be-architectural]{\textsc{a system can be fully explicable from outside yet unable to derive a satisfying self-explanation from inside}} — the gap arising from access architecture, not from a residue in the world.

\webheading{The analogy and what it borrows}

The move models internal-versus-external explicability on \emph{provability relative to a system}: what is derivable depends on the resources available, so a truth transparent from a richer standpoint can be underivable from a poorer one. The shared respects are (i) a fixed stock of internal observables and operations, and (ii) derivability defined relative to that stock. The analogy borrows only this structural point — not the contested conclusion of \hyperref[source:source-penrose-1994]{\textsc{Penrose}} that minds outstrip machines.

\webheading{The weakest premise}

Premise 2 is load-bearing and contestable. The risk is a slide from an \emph{access} gap to a \emph{phenomenal} one. Showing that a system cannot traverse a self-explanation accounts for missing intelligibility-from-inside, which on Block's \hyperref[entry:concept-access-vs-phenomenal-consciousness]{\textsc{access/phenomenal distinction}} need not touch why there is anything it is like to be the system at all — the \emph{ontological} member of the \hyperref[entry:concept-introspective-gap-varieties]{\textsc{varieties of introspective gap}}. So the argument secures an architectural or conceptual gap; whether that exhausts the felt gap is exactly what an opponent denies, which is why the conclusion is filed as contested. (The \emph{analogy pattern}'s \emph{disanalogy} critical question presses here: an opponent may argue that informal ``intuitive explanation'' disanalogises from formal provability in just the respect that matters.)

\webheading{Relation to mysterianism}

The conclusion is a mechanism-level cousin of McGinn's cognitive closure (see \hyperref[entry:position-mysterianism]{\textsc{Mysterianism}}, \hyperref[entry:claim-cognitive-closure-exists]{\textsc{Possibility of cognitive closure}}, \hyperref[source:source-mcginn-1989]{\textsc{McGinn — Can We Solve the Mind–Body Problem? (1989)}}): this argument is one way to \emph{earn} a closure-style verdict by construction rather than asserting it. It deliberately carries no \texttt{attacks} or \texttt{supports} edge into the residue debate (see \hyperref[entry:argument-explanatory-gap]{\textsc{Explanatory-gap argument}}, \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-residue]{\textsc{Phenomenal residue}}).

\begin{plainterms}
Put two simple points together. First: a system often can't reach its own inner workings — the facts are in it but not available to it (like not feeling your neurons fire). Second: an explanation only \emph{clicks} for a mind if that mind can actually walk the steps itself; a bridge an outside expert can cross may have no inside path. Stack those and you get the conclusion: a system can be completely explained from the outside yet, from the inside, never able to derive why its own thoughts appear as they do — so the ``gap'' comes from how its self-access is built, not from anything missing in the world. The shaky step is the second one: not being able to \emph{reach} an explanation isn't obviously the same as there being a leftover \emph{feel} to explain — which is why this is marked contested.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-inside-vantage-access-limited]{\textsc{Limited inside vantage}} — premise one: the inside view carries less information than the outside view.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-explanation-must-be-internally-traversable]{\textsc{Internally traversable explanation}} — premise two, and the load-bearing risk.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-self-explanation-gap-can-be-architectural]{\textsc{Architectural self-explanation gap}} — the conclusion this argument establishes.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-introspective-gap-varieties]{\textsc{Varieties of introspective explanatory gap}} — sorts the access/conceptual gap secured here from the ontological gap it does not reach.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-access-vs-phenomenal-consciousness]{\textsc{Access vs. phenomenal consciousness}} — Block's distinction marking where the argument's reach stops.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-hard-problem]{\textsc{Hard problem}} — the felt gap the argument is careful not to claim it has closed.
  \item \hyperref[entry:position-mysterianism]{\textsc{Mysterianism}} — the cognitive-closure view this conclusion is a mechanism-level cousin of.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-cognitive-closure-exists]{\textsc{Possibility of cognitive closure}} — the closure verdict the argument tries to derive rather than assert.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-explanatory-gap]{\textsc{Explanatory-gap argument}} — the residue debate the argument deliberately stays out of.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-residue]{\textsc{Phenomenal residue}} — the leftover-feel posit the argument neither attacks nor supports.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{argument-genetic-panpsychism}{Genetic argument}{Argument \,\textperiodcentered\, after Strawson}{Genetic argument}{The genetic argument — physicalism plus realism about experience entails panpsychism}{$\vdash$}
The \emph{genetic argument} is the leading modern argument for \hyperref[entry:concept-panpsychism]{\textsc{Panpsychism}}. Due to Galen Strawson (see \hyperref[source:source-strawson-2006]{\textsc{2006}}), with an earlier version in Thomas Nagel (see \hyperref[source:source-nagel-1979]{\textsc{1979}}), it claims the surprising result that physicalism, taken seriously, does not avoid panpsychism but \emph{leads} to it. Its name comes from its question: given that experience is real, what was its \emph{genesis} — was it there at the fundamental level, or did it arise later from matter that wholly lacked it? Strawson argues the second option is incoherent, so the first must hold.

\webheading{The argument}

The inference is deductive — a disjunctive elimination — and is addressed to someone who already accepts physicalism: whatever consciousness is, it is a wholly natural phenomenon, not a non-physical addition.

\begin{enumerate}
  \item (see \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-consciousness-is-real]{\textsc{Reality of phenomenal consciousness}}) Phenomenal experience is genuinely real — there is something it is like to undergo it.
  \item Since experience is real and, by the physicalist framing, wholly natural, either it was present at the fundamental physical level, or it arose at some later stage from a base that wholly lacked it.
  \item (see \hyperref[entry:claim-no-radical-emergence]{\textsc{No radical emergence}}) The second option is \emph{radical} (brute) emergence — experience from the utterly experienceless, with no intelligible link — which a serious physicalist should reject.
\end{enumerate}

∴ Experience, or its proto-form, is present at the fundamental level and pervades the physical world: \hyperref[entry:claim-consciousness-fundamental-ubiquitous]{\textsc{Fundamental ubiquitous consciousness}}. The physicalist framing is what makes the conclusion \emph{panpsychism} rather than dualism — experience is located in the intrinsic nature of physical stuff, not added from outside it.

\webheading{The weak point}

The most disputed step is (3), the rejection of brute emergence. The standard reply, the \hyperref[entry:argument-strong-emergence-acceptable]{\textsc{emergence reply}}, accepts that experience can strongly emerge at some level of organisation and so denies that the disjunction in step (2) is a genuine dilemma. Premise (1) is the second pressure point: illusionists deny that there is any real phenomenal experience whose origin must be explained, and on that denial the argument never gets started.

\begin{plainterms}
Start as a physicalist: whatever consciousness is, it is part of the natural world, not a ghost added from outside. Now grant that consciousness is \emph{real} — there is genuinely something it is like to see red. Then ask where it came from. Either it was there in the basic ingredients of matter all along, or at some point it sprang into being out of stuff that had not the faintest trace of experience. Strawson says that second story — getting felt experience from the totally feelingless, with no intelligible bridge — is just magic dressed up as science. Rule out the magic and you are left with the first story: experience goes all the way down. That, surprisingly, turns physicalism itself into a road to panpsychism. The soft spot is the claim that the leap from feelingless to feeling is impossible; a critic can answer that nature pulls off exactly such leaps.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-consciousness-fundamental-ubiquitous]{\textsc{Fundamental ubiquitous consciousness}} — the conclusion: experience is a fundamental, ubiquitous feature of the physical world.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-no-radical-emergence]{\textsc{No radical emergence}} and \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-consciousness-is-real]{\textsc{Reality of phenomenal consciousness}} — the two premises the argument rests on.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-strong-emergence-acceptable]{\textsc{Emergence reply}} — the standard reply, which denies premise (3).
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-no-arbitrary-line-panpsychism]{\textsc{Continuity argument}} — the companion case for panpsychism, from the absence of a threshold rather than from the impossibility of emergence.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-panpsychism]{\textsc{Panpsychism}} — the view this argument supports, and its varieties.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{claim-alternatives-genuinely-open}{Genuine alternatives}{Claim}{Genuine alternatives}{At the moment of a free choice, more than one course of action is genuinely open to the agent}{$\odot$}
Freedom, on this \emph{leeway} conception, requires real alternatives: when an agent chooses freely, the world up to that moment leaves more than one continuation available, and which one occurs is up to the agent. The openness asserted is metaphysical, not merely epistemic — it is not just that the agent doesn't \emph{know} what she will do, but that the past and the laws do not fix it.

The claim is the defining commitment of leeway forms of \hyperref[entry:position-libertarianism]{\textsc{Libertarianism (free will)}}, and the Consequence Argument (van Inwagen, \hyperref[source:source-van-inwagen-1983]{\textsc{1983}}) defends its incompatibility with determinism: if determinism is true, our acts are consequences of the laws and the remote past, neither of which is up to us. It is denied outright by \hyperref[entry:position-hard-determinism]{\textsc{hard determinists}}, for whom the appearance of open alternatives is a fact about deliberation rather than about the world, and it is denied \emph{as a condition on freedom} by actual-sequence compatibilists, for whom \hyperref[entry:concept-frankfurt-cases]{\textsc{Frankfurt cases}} show that responsibility survives the deletion of alternatives. In the \hyperref[entry:concept-consciousness-freewill-duality]{\textsc{consciousness–free-will duality}} this claim is the thesis that the action space is a genuine coproduct of really-available options, not an epistemic one with a single inhabited summand.

\entryhead{position-global-workspace}{Global workspace theory}{Position \,\textperiodcentered\, after Baars}{Global workspace theory}{Global Workspace Theory}{$\bigstar$}
Global workspace theory holds that a mental content becomes \emph{conscious} when it is broadcast in a ``global workspace'' — made widely available to the brain's many specialist systems for memory, language, report, and flexible control. The metaphor is a theatre: countless unconscious processes work in the dark, and a piece of information becomes conscious when it is lit on the central stage and shared with the whole audience at once (see \hyperref[entry:claim-consciousness-is-global-broadcast]{\textsc{global broadcast}}). It is the leading \emph{functional} and empirical theory of consciousness, framed as a research programme rather than a piece of metaphysics. The framework was introduced by Bernard Baars (see \hyperref[source:source-baars-1988]{\textsc{Baars — A Cognitive Theory of Consciousness (1988)}}); Stanislas Dehaene and colleagues added a neural mechanism — the \emph{global neuronal workspace}, in which a stimulus crosses into consciousness through a late, all-or-none ``ignition'' of long-range fronto-parietal networks (see \hyperref[source:source-dehaene-naccache-2001]{\textsc{Dehaene \& Naccache — Towards a Cognitive Neuroscience of Consciousness (2001)}}).

\webheading{What it explains, and what it may not}

The theory is most naturally read as an account of the \emph{access} side of the \hyperref[entry:concept-access-vs-phenomenal-consciousness]{\textsc{access/phenomenal distinction}} — what makes a content reportable, rememberable, and usable in reasoning and action. Whether the same broadcast also constitutes the \emph{phenomenal} side, the felt quality of experience, is the live question. Offered as an answer to the \hyperref[entry:question-hard-problem]{\textsc{hard problem}}, it accounts elegantly for how information reaches the spotlight and gets used, but critics — Ned Block prominently — object that \emph{phenomenal} consciousness may \emph{overflow} access (see \hyperref[source:source-block-2007]{\textsc{Block — Consciousness, Accessibility, and the Mesh between Psychology and Neuroscience (2007)}}): we may experience more than ever gets broadcast and reported, in which case global availability is at best the wrong target for explaining felt experience.

\webheading{How it differs from neighbouring views}

Against \hyperref[entry:position-higher-order-theories]{\textsc{Higher-order theories of consciousness}}, the global workspace needs no dedicated \emph{meta-representation} — no separate state whose job is to represent another mental state. Wide availability is enough; a content is conscious in virtue of being broadcast, not in virtue of being monitored by a higher-order thought.

\begin{plainterms}
Picture the mind as a theatre: countless specialist processes work in the dark, and a piece of information becomes \emph{conscious} when it's lit up on the central stage and broadcast to the whole audience — so you can talk about it, remember it, and act on it. It's the leading scientific theory of consciousness and comes with brain evidence. The philosophical catch: this explains how something gets into the spotlight and gets \emph{used}, but maybe not why being in the spotlight \emph{feels} like anything.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-consciousness-is-global-broadcast]{\textsc{Consciousness as global broadcast}} — the atomic thesis this position commits to.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-access-vs-phenomenal-consciousness]{\textsc{Access vs. phenomenal consciousness}} — the distinction that locates the theory on the access side and frames the central objection.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-order-of-representation]{\textsc{First-order vs. higher-order states}} — the first-order/higher-order axis; the workspace is a first-order broadcast account, not a higher-order one.
  \item \hyperref[entry:position-higher-order-theories]{\textsc{Higher-order theories of consciousness}} — the rival requiring meta-representation rather than mere availability.
  \item \hyperref[entry:question-hard-problem]{\textsc{Hard problem (the question)}} — the explanatory target the theory is offered as answering, and where critics say it falls short.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{claim-good-explanation-is-hard-to-vary}{Good explanations are hard to vary}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, after Deutsch}{Good explanations are hard to vary}{A good explanation is one that is hard to vary while still accounting for what it explains}{$\odot$}
The mark of a good explanation is that its details are \emph{tightly constrained} by the thing it explains: you cannot easily change any part of it and still have it account for the phenomenon. David Deutsch (see \hyperref[source:source-deutsch-2011]{\textsc{Deutsch — The Beginning of Infinity (2011)}}) proposes this ``hard to vary'' criterion as what distinguishes real explanations from spurious ones. A myth that attributes winter to a goddess's seasonal grief explains the data but is \emph{easy to vary} — any other emotion or deity would do equally well — so it explains nothing in particular. The axial-tilt account of seasons is hard to vary: change the geometry and it no longer predicts the observed pattern of warmth and daylight, hemisphere by hemisphere.

The criterion does double duty. It explains why good explanations have \emph{reach} (their rigidity makes them hard to confine to the case they were invented for) and it supplies a fast diagnostic against bad theories — conspiracy theories, \emph{ad hoc} rescues, and brute ``that's just how it is'' posits are all marked by how freely their details could have been otherwise.

\textbf{Scope.} A criterion for \emph{explanatory} quality, offered within a fallibilist epistemology (see \hyperref[entry:claim-all-knowledge-is-conjectural]{\textsc{Knowledge is conjectural}}); it is not a guarantee of truth, since a hard-to-vary explanation may still be wrong, only a measure of which conjectures are worth taking seriously and exposing to criticism.

\textbf{Who would deny it.} An instrumentalist or strict empiricist who cares only about predictive success, not explanation, will deny that ``hard to vary'' tracks anything beyond accommodated data; a Bayesian might try to recover the same verdicts through priors and likelihoods rather than a primitive notion of variability.

This is the criterion that powers \hyperref[entry:argument-deutsch-anti-solipsism]{\textsc{Deutsch's anti-solipsism}}: solipsism is faulted not for inconsistency but for being an easily-varied, surplus-laden redescription of realism.

\begin{plainterms}
A good explanation is one you can't fiddle with. If you can swap out its parts for any others and it still ``explains'' the thing, it wasn't really explaining it — it was just a story that fit. ``Winter comes because a goddess is sad'' is like that: grief, rage, any god, any season would have done. ``Winter comes because the Earth is tilted'' can't be tweaked — change the tilt and the seasons come out wrong. Deutsch's rule of thumb: trust the explanations that are hard to vary, and be suspicious of the ones that bend to fit anything.
\end{plainterms}

\entryhead{claim-subject-unity-graded-by-integration}{Graded subject unity}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{Graded subject unity}{The number of subjects in a system is graded by inter-part integration, not a discrete count}{$\odot$}
How many subjects of experience a system contains is not a sharp whole number but a matter of degree, fixed by how richly its parts are integrated — the ``bandwidth'' of the connection between them. The guiding case is the split brain. Fully connected, the two hemispheres make one subject; fully severed, they behave as two. Now imagine restoring the cut fibres one at a time, or a temporary anaesthetic split that gradually wears off: applying the \hyperref[entry:concept-continuity-argument-schema]{\textsc{continuity argument}} to the series, there is no single fibre whose addition flips the count from two subjects to one, so the count cannot be strictly discrete. Parts joined by a fat pipe feel like one subject, fully disconnected like two, and intermediate bandwidth yields intermediate degrees of unity. Thomas Nagel pressed exactly this indeterminacy for commissurotomy patients (see \hyperref[source:source-nagel-1971]{\textsc{Nagel — Brain Bisection and the Unity of Consciousness (1971)}}); Luke Roelofs builds it into a general account of composite subjectivity (see \hyperref[source:source-roelofs-2019]{\textsc{Roelofs — Combining Minds: How to Think about Composite Subjectivity (2019)}}).

The thesis is the \emph{subject}-level analogue of \hyperref[entry:claim-personal-identity-admits-of-degree]{\textsc{the claim that personal identity admits of degree}}, and it takes the same \emph{degree} horn of the continuity schema. It stands opposed to \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-determinacy]{\textsc{the determinacy of the phenomenal}}, on which there is always a definite fact about whose experience an experience is; a defender of the combination problem will press that determinacy here.

This is about the count of \emph{phenomenal subjects}, and so is distinct from \hyperref[entry:concept-grain]{\textsc{Grain (of role individuation)}}, which is a dial on the resolution of a \emph{functional/causal role} — a system can be integrated to a high phenomenal ``bandwidth'' without that settling at what grain its cognitive role is individuated, and vice versa.

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \textsc{Combining subjects} — the rebuttal to the combination problem this premise anchors.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-personal-identity-admits-of-degree]{\textsc{Personal identity by degree}} — the personal-identity cousin that takes the same degree horn of the continuity schema.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-determinacy]{\textsc{Phenomenal determinacy}} — the opposing claim that experience has a determinate subject.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-continuity-argument-schema]{\textsc{Continuity argument schema}} — the schema applied fibre-by-fibre to yield the graded count.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-grain]{\textsc{Grain (of role individuation)}} — a neighbouring ``dial,'' but on functional-role resolution rather than phenomenal subjecthood.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{argument-graded-polarity}{Graded-polarity objection}{Argument \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{Graded-polarity objection}{The graded-structure objection — attention makes experience partly exclusive, action is partly conjunctive, so the crude product/coproduct assignment idealizes both sides}{$\vdash$}
An objection to the two structural premises of the \hyperref[entry:concept-consciousness-freewill-duality]{\textsc{consciousness–free-will duality}}: the assignment of pure product structure to experience and pure coproduct structure to action idealizes both sides.

\textbf{Inference form:} deductive — counterexamples to each premise's universality.

\begin{enumerate}
  \item Against \hyperref[entry:claim-conscious-content-is-product-like]{\textsc{Product-like experience}}: attention makes experience partially \emph{exclusive} — attending to the conversation mutes the music; contents compete for the field rather than co-inhabiting it symmetrically.
  \item Against \hyperref[entry:claim-action-content-is-coproduct-like]{\textsc{Coproduct-like choice}}: behavior is partially \emph{conjunctive} — one walks while talking. The motor system takes products of compatible actions and coproducts only of incompatible ones.
  \item So neither side instantiates its assigned structure purely; each mixes conjunction and exclusion.
\end{enumerate}

∴ The crude ∏/∐ assignment is false as stated.

\textbf{The refined picture the objection points to.} Linear logic (Girard, \hyperref[source:source-girard-1987]{\textsc{1987}}) splits conjunction and disjunction each in two — ⊗ (co-presence) and \& (external choice); ⊕ (internal choice) and ⅋ — and sorts the connectives by \emph{polarity} into negative (passive, input-like) and positive (active, committing). Game semantics runs on exactly this division: the environment moves in negative phases, the player commits in positive ones. The graded picture is thus not the duality's refutation but its sharpening — the thesis becomes close to a restatement of polarization — with the caveat that the clean self-dual version lives in linear, not cartesian, settings, so the upgrade has a real mathematical price (see \hyperref[entry:argument-no-universal-property]{\textsc{Missing universal property}}). The duality also \emph{predicts} where exclusivity enters experience: exactly at the input-side selector, attention (see \hyperref[entry:claim-attention-is-dual-of-will]{\textsc{Attention–volition duality}}).

\textbf{Weakest premise:} the tacit step from ``not purely'' to ``false as stated.'' The structural claims concern the characteristic combinatorial shape of each side — conjunctive co-presence versus exclusive commitment — and a defender can read them as claims about the dominant structure, with attention and motor-compatibility as the graded fringe the polarity refinement absorbs. The objection then demotes the premises from exact to approximate rather than refuting them.

\begin{plainterms}
The tidy story says experience takes everything \emph{together} while action picks exactly \emph{one}. Real life is messier: focus on one voice and the rest of the room fades (experience excludes), and you can walk, talk, and chew gum at once (action combines). A more careful logic — one with two kinds of ``and'' and two kinds of ``or'' — captures the mess: some combining is passive taking-in, some choosing is active committing. The mirror between perceiving and acting survives, but in this finer vocabulary rather than the tidy either/or.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-conscious-content-is-product-like]{\textsc{Product-like experience}} and \hyperref[entry:claim-action-content-is-coproduct-like]{\textsc{Coproduct-like choice}} — the two premises attacked.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-attention-is-dual-of-will]{\textsc{Attention–volition duality}} — the finer selection-duality that predicts where experience turns exclusive.
  \item \hyperref[source:source-girard-1987]{\textsc{Girard — Linear Logic (1987)}} — linear logic, the refined connective vocabulary.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-no-universal-property]{\textsc{Missing universal property}} — where the linear-versus-cartesian caveat is cashed out.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{concept-grain}{Grain (of role individuation)}{Concept \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{Grain (of role individuation)}{}{$\blacklozenge$}
\emph{Grain} is the level of resolution at which a functional or causal role is specified: how much detail two systems must share before they count as realising the \emph{same} role, and so — on functionalism — as having the same mental state. The metaphor is photographic. A coarse-grained description draws few distinctions and lumps many systems together; a fine-grained one draws many and separates them. Because \hyperref[entry:concept-functionalism]{\textsc{Functionalism}} individuates mental states by role yet leaves the grain of ``role'' unspecified, the grain is the free parameter on which most substrate-independence verdicts actually turn: fix the grain and you have decided who counts as having a mind.

\webheading{Settings along the dial}

The dial runs from coarse to fine.

\begin{itemize}
  \item \emph{Coarse / input–output / behavioural.} The role is just the system's input–output profile, its Turing-test-passing behaviour. This is the most permissive setting, satisfied by a mere behavioural duplicate, which is why it is charged with being ``too liberal'': it would admit a \hyperref[entry:concept-lookup-table-mind]{\textsc{lookup-table mind}}, a system that produces the right outputs from a giant stored table. This is horn 1 of the \hyperref[entry:argument-role-grain-dilemma]{\textsc{grain dilemma}}.
  \item \emph{Organisational / functional-causal.} The role is the system's internal state-to-state transition structure — which states cause which, under which counterfactuals — abstracted from the physical medium. This is the proposed principled middle (see \hyperref[entry:claim-role-grain-is-organisational]{\textsc{organisational role-grain}}): coarse enough to be realised in silicon, fine enough to exclude the lookup table.
  \item \emph{Fine / microphysical / biological.} The role includes spike timing, neurochemistry, and the physical realiser itself. This is the most restrictive setting, charged with being ``too chauvinistic,'' since it collapses toward \hyperref[entry:position-proteocentrism]{\textsc{Proteocentrism}}, on which carbon biochemistry does real individuative work. This is horn 2 of the grain dilemma.
\end{itemize}

\webheading{Where to set the dial}

The concept earns its keep because a single dispute keeps recurring at it.

\begin{itemize}
  \item The \hyperref[entry:argument-role-grain-dilemma]{\textsc{grain dilemma}} argues that no setting works: coarse is too liberal, fine too chauvinistic.
  \item The reply of \hyperref[entry:claim-role-grain-is-organisational]{\textsc{organisational role-grain}} and \hyperref[entry:argument-organisational-grain-dissolves-dilemma]{\textsc{the organisational-grain middle}} is that the organisational setting is a principled, independently motivated middle — the grain at which cognitive science actually quantifies.
  \item The counter-reply, \hyperref[entry:argument-dense-china-brain-regress]{\textsc{the dense China-brain regress}} and its conclusion \hyperref[entry:claim-organisational-grain-faces-own-grain-dilemma]{\textsc{the organisational grain's own dilemma}}, presses that even the middle fails: a dense \hyperref[entry:concept-chinese-nation]{\textsc{Chinese Nation}} matches the organisation yet intuitively lacks a mind, so the grain either readmits the absurd or smuggles sub-organisational detail back in.
\end{itemize}

\webheading{Grain for thought versus grain for feeling}

A distinction the debate sometimes blurs: grain is a knob on \emph{functional description}. It bears on two separate questions. First, the individuation of \emph{cognitive} and \emph{intentional} states — belief, inference — where the organisational setting is strong and even critics tend to concede it. Second, whether \emph{any} setting suffices for \emph{phenomenal} states, the what-it-is-like of experience. The phenomenal-residue worry (see \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-residue]{\textsc{phenomenal residue}}, \hyperref[entry:argument-china-nation-absent-qualia]{\textsc{Chinese nation (absent qualia)}}, \hyperref[entry:argument-explanatory-gap]{\textsc{Explanatory-gap argument}}) is precisely the claim that no setting of this knob captures qualia at all — a different complaint from ``the dial is set wrong.''

\begin{plainterms}
Suppose you agree a mind is defined by what it \emph{does} rather than what it's made of. There's still a catch: \emph{how exactly} do you describe what it does? Very roughly (``takes in questions, gives sensible answers'')? Or in fine detail (``these exact signals trigger those, with this precise timing'')? That choice of detail-level is the ``grain'' — like the resolution on a screen, coarse or fine. It matters enormously, because it quietly decides who counts as having a mind. Describe a mind too coarsely and almost anything qualifies — even a giant cheat-sheet that just looks up canned answers. Describe it too finely and only a biological brain can match it, so no computer ever could. A lot of the fight over whether machines can think comes down to one question: where do you set this dial — and can you justify \emph{that} setting without just tuning it to get the answer you already wanted?
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-functionalism]{\textsc{Functionalism}} — the theory whose ``role'' this dial calibrates.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-role-grain-dilemma]{\textsc{Grain dilemma}} — the dilemma the grain concept frames: no setting is both liberal-proof and chauvinism-proof.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-role-grain-is-organisational]{\textsc{Organisational role-grain}} — the proposed middle setting of the dial.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{argument-role-grain-dilemma}{Grain dilemma}{Argument \,\textperiodcentered\, after Block}{Grain dilemma}{The grain dilemma for functional ``role'' (too liberal vs. too chauvinistic)}{$\vdash$}
The \emph{grain dilemma} is an objection to \hyperref[entry:claim-substrate-independence]{\textsc{substrate independence}} arguing that its key term, ``role,'' admits no grain of individuation that secures the desired conclusion without absurdity — at least, none its advocate has drawn in a principled way. It is a constructive dilemma: a mental state's individuating functional role must be specified at \emph{some} grain, and both available grains lead somewhere unacceptable. The squeeze is due to Ned Block, whose ``Troubles with Functionalism'' pressed the liberal/chauvinist dilemma against any functional account of mind (see \hyperref[source:source-block-1978]{\textsc{Block — Troubles with Functionalism (1978)}}).

\textbf{Horn 1 — coarse-grained.} Specify the role at the level of input–output behaviour, the classic Turing profile. Then functionalism is \emph{too liberal}: a vast \hyperref[entry:concept-lookup-table-mind]{\textsc{lookup table}}, or Block's homunculi-headed \hyperref[entry:concept-chinese-nation]{\textsc{China}}, realises that profile and so must, on substrate independence, have a mind. (The lookup-table version is Block's ``blockhead,'' from his ``Psychologism and Behaviorism,'' \hyperref[source:source-block]{\textsc{Block — Psychologism and Behaviorism (1981)}}.) Going coarse enough to admit the silicon admits the absurd alongside it — this is the soft underbelly that \hyperref[entry:argument-behavioral-parity]{\textsc{the behavioural-parity argument}} itself flags in its own premise.

\textbf{Horn 2 — fine-grained.} Specify the role at the level of full causal microstructure: spike timing, neuromodulator diffusion, and the rest. Then functionalism risks being \emph{too chauvinistic}, the role becoming so biologically specific that nothing but a brain, or a near-molecular emulation of one, can realise it — and substrate independence quietly collapses toward the neighbourhood of \hyperref[entry:position-proteocentrism]{\textsc{Proteocentrism}}, with the carbon doing real individuative work after all.

Therefore the advocate of substrate independence owes a grain \emph{coarse enough to be multiply realisable in silicon yet fine enough to exclude the blockhead}, together with a principled reason for drawing the line there rather than for the convenience of the conclusion. The claim as written simply helps itself to the existence of such a line.

\webheading{Weakest premise}

The dilemma's exhaustiveness — its assumption that no \emph{intermediate} grain escapes both horns. A reply that specifies a principled middle grain, and motivates it independently of the desired verdict on silicon, would dissolve the dilemma; that is exactly the move its exhaustiveness premise leaves open, taken up by \hyperref[entry:argument-organisational-grain-dissolves-dilemma]{\textsc{the organisational-grain middle}}.

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:argument-explanatory-gap]{\textsc{the explanatory gap}}, which grants a fixed role and presses what role \emph{leaves out} (phenomenal character): the grain dilemma attacks the \emph{individuation} of role itself, and so applies even to intentional states. It responds to \hyperref[entry:argument-behavioral-parity]{\textsc{behavioural parity}} because horn 1 sharpens the blockhead objection that argument names against its own premise.

\begin{plainterms}
Suppose you say a mind is defined by what it does, so a machine could have one too. Fair enough — but \emph{how detailed} is your description of ``what it does''? You're caught between two bad answers. Go coarse (``right answers in, right answers out'') and you let in obvious frauds: a giant lookup table that just spits back pre-stored replies would count as having a mind.
