Go fine (exact brain chemistry, the precise timing of neurons) and you've quietly made a \emph{brain} the only thing that qualifies — so machines are excluded after all, and you've smuggled biology back in. To make machine minds work you need a description loose enough to fit silicon yet strict enough to shut out the cheat-sheet — \emph{and} an honest reason for drawing the line right there, not a reason picked just because it delivers the verdict you wanted. The complaint: nobody has actually drawn that principled line.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-substrate-independence]{\textsc{Substrate-independence thesis}} — the thesis the dilemma attacks.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-behavioral-parity]{\textsc{Behavioural-parity argument}} — the argument it replies to; horn 1 sharpens that argument's own blockhead worry.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-organisational-grain-dissolves-dilemma]{\textsc{Organisational-grain middle}} — the reply that supplies the principled middle grain.
  \item \hyperref[entry:position-proteocentrism]{\textsc{Proteocentrism}} — where horn 2 says fine-grained functionalism collapses.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-explanatory-gap]{\textsc{Explanatory-gap argument}} — a different objection: it grants the role and presses the phenomenal residue.
\end{seealso}

\end{multicols}
\bookgroup{H}
\begin{multicols}{2}
\entryhead{concept-halting-oracle}{Halting oracle}{Concept \,\textperiodcentered\, after Turing}{Halting oracle}{The halting oracle (and uncomputability)}{$\blacklozenge$}
A \emph{halting oracle} is a hypothetical black box that, given any program and input, correctly answers a single question: will that program eventually stop, or run forever? The question itself is the \emph{halting problem}, and it matters because Alan Turing proved in 1936 that no ordinary computer can answer it in every case (see \hyperref[source:source-turing-1936]{\textsc{Turing — On Computable Numbers, with an Application to the Entscheidungsproblem (1936)}}). The oracle is the imagined device that simply \emph{can} — posited not as a gadget anyone expects to build, but as a precise mathematical object that lets theorists ask what \emph{else} would become computable if such answers were free for the taking.

\webheading{Undecidability and the oracle}

Turing's result is a theorem, established by a \emph{diagonal argument}: assume a machine $H$ that decides halting for every input, then build a program that consults $H$ about itself and does the opposite of what $H$ predicts, yielding a contradiction. So the halting function is \emph{undecidable} — no Turing machine computes it (see \hyperref[entry:claim-halting-problem-undecidable]{\textsc{Undecidability of the halting problem}}).

An \emph{oracle} is the formal way to study what lies beyond that limit. One imagines a Turing machine equipped with a black box that returns the halting answer in a single step, and asks what such an \emph{oracle machine} can compute. This is the theory of \emph{relative computability}: not ``what is computable'' but ``what is computable \emph{given} X.'' It is a rigorous, developed branch of mathematics — oracle machines, the \emph{Turing degrees} (a hierarchy ranking problems by how much oracular help they need), the \emph{arithmetical hierarchy} — not a fringe fiction.

\webheading{Why it matters here}

The key feature is the combination the oracle embodies. It is \emph{precisely specified and consistently theorized} — \emph{positively} conceivable, in the sense that a fruitful theory can be built around it, not merely ``no contradiction has been noticed'' — and yet it is \emph{provably} not Turing-computable. Whether it is \emph{physically} realizable is a further, separate question, turning on the \hyperref[entry:claim-physical-church-turing-thesis]{\textsc{physical Church–Turing thesis}} (the claim that every physically realizable computation is Turing-computable). Standard physics says the oracle cannot be built; \emph{hypercomputation} proposals — Malament–Hogarth spacetimes, infinite-time Turing machines — dispute that.

This makes the oracle a uniquely clean example in several disputes:

\begin{itemize}
  \item It is a non-definitional case of something \emph{coherent but physically unrealizable} (see \hyperref[entry:claim-halting-oracle-coherent-but-unrealizable]{\textsc{Halting oracle (coherent but unrealizable)}}, \hyperref[entry:claim-conceivability-not-sufficient-for-physical-realizability]{\textsc{Conceivability without realizability}}) — sharper than the textbook ``water that isn't H₂O'' case, because there is no ``you mislabelled it'' escape: the oracle is defined exactly, and its uncomputability is a proof.
  \item It supplies the formal backbone for the idea that an explanation must be \emph{internally traversable} (see \hyperref[entry:claim-explanation-must-be-internally-traversable]{\textsc{Internally traversable explanation}}).
  \item It is a building block for \emph{Gödelian anti-mechanism} about minds. Roger Penrose holds that the brain exploits \emph{non-computable} physics (\hyperref[source:source-penrose-1994]{\textsc{Penrose — Shadows of the Mind (1994)}}; \hyperref[entry:claim-consciousness-noncomputable]{\textsc{Non-computable consciousness}}, \hyperref[entry:position-noncomputable-consciousness]{\textsc{Penrose's non-computational consciousness}}) — which amounts to denying the physical Church–Turing thesis for brains.
\end{itemize}

\begin{plainterms}
There's a famous question — ``will this program eventually stop, or run forever?'' — and Turing proved \emph{no} computer program can answer it reliably for every case. A ``halting oracle'' is an imaginary gadget that just \emph{knows} the answer. The striking thing: mathematicians have built an entire, perfectly consistent theory around such gadgets (what you \emph{could} compute if you had one) — so it's a fully coherent, precisely-defined idea — and yet it's provably impossible for any ordinary computer to be one. That makes it a clean example of something you can rigorously \emph{describe and reason about} but (on standard physics) can never actually \emph{build}.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-halting-problem-undecidable]{\textsc{Undecidability of the halting problem}} — Turing's theorem that no computer solves the halting problem; the result the oracle is defined against.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-physical-church-turing-thesis]{\textsc{Physical Church–Turing thesis}} — the claim that nature builds no computer stronger than a Turing machine; what decides whether the oracle could ever be physical.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-halting-oracle-coherent-but-unrealizable]{\textsc{Halting oracle (coherent but unrealizable)}} — the oracle as a worked case of coherent-yet-unbuildable.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-conceivability-not-sufficient-for-physical-realizability]{\textsc{Conceivability without realizability}} — the general lesson the oracle is used to draw.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-explanation-must-be-internally-traversable]{\textsc{Internally traversable explanation}} — uses the oracle's external-vs-internal derivability as its formal backbone.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-consciousness-noncomputable]{\textsc{Non-computable consciousness}} and \hyperref[entry:position-noncomputable-consciousness]{\textsc{Penrose's non-computational consciousness}} — Penrose's view that minds exploit non-computable physics, which denies the physical Church–Turing thesis for brains.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{claim-halting-oracle-coherent-but-unrealizable}{Halting oracle (coherent but unrealizable)}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{Halting oracle (coherent but unrealizable)}{The halting oracle is mathematically coherent yet physically unrealizable}{$\odot$}
The halting oracle (see \hyperref[entry:concept-halting-oracle]{\textsc{Halting oracle}}) is a consistent, precisely specified object of a developed mathematical theory — relative computability — and yet no physical device can be one. Given that no Turing machine decides the halting problem (see \hyperref[entry:claim-halting-problem-undecidable]{\textsc{Undecidability of the halting problem}}) and that every physically realizable computation is Turing-computable (see \hyperref[entry:claim-physical-church-turing-thesis]{\textsc{the physical Church–Turing thesis}}), the oracle is coherent and theorizable but unbuildable.

The claim deliberately pairs \emph{mathematical coherence} with \emph{physical unrealizability}, and two caveats keep the pairing honest. First, the unrealizability inherits the \emph{contingency} of the physical Church–Turing thesis: if hypercomputation turns out to be physically possible, the oracle may be realizable after all. Second, the oracle is \emph{metaphysically} possible — it is a consistent abstract structure, and there is a possible world containing one. What fails is \emph{physical} realizability, not coherence or metaphysical possibility.

Someone might deny the claim from either flank: a hypercomputation theorist who rejects the physical Church–Turing thesis, and so holds the oracle \emph{is} physically realizable; or a strict finitist or formalist who denies that the oracle is a legitimately coherent object at all.

This is distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-lookup-table-physically-unrealisable]{\textsc{the unbuildable lookup table}}, another coherent-but-physically-impossible object — a behaviour-matching table too large for the universe to store. That case fails on \emph{resource bounds}; the oracle fails on \emph{uncomputability}. They are two different routes to ``specifiable but unbuildable.''

\begin{plainterms}
You can write down exactly what a halting oracle would be, prove theorems about what it could do, and teach a whole course on it — it's a completely coherent idea. And yet, on standard physics, you could never actually construct one. So it sits in a striking spot: fully thinkable and mathematically real, but physically out of reach. (Two honest footnotes: this assumes nature has no exotic ``super-computers'' hiding in it; and the oracle is still \emph{possible in the abstract} — it's only the \emph{building} of one that's blocked.)
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-halting-oracle]{\textsc{Halting oracle}} — the object this claim is about.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-halting-problem-undecidable]{\textsc{Undecidability of the halting problem}} and \hyperref[entry:claim-physical-church-turing-thesis]{\textsc{Physical Church–Turing thesis}} — the two grounds for the verdict.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-lookup-table-physically-unrealisable]{\textsc{Physically unrealisable table}} — a sibling case that fails on resource bounds rather than uncomputability.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{position-hard-determinism}{Hard determinism}{Position}{Hard determinism}{}{$\bigstar$}
\emph{Hard determinism} accepts the incompatibilist's conditional — if determinism is true, no one acts freely — and affirms the antecedent: the world is deterministic, so free will is an illusion. It agrees with \hyperref[entry:position-libertarianism]{\textsc{Libertarianism (free will)}} that freedom would require genuinely open alternatives and real origination, and agrees with the scientific picture that neither exists; deliberation feels open only because deliberators cannot know their own outcome in advance. The felt openness of the future is epistemic, not metaphysical (rejecting \hyperref[entry:claim-alternatives-genuinely-open]{\textsc{Genuine alternatives}}).

The contemporary descendant is Pereboom's \emph{hard incompatibilism} (Pereboom, \hyperref[source:source-pereboom-2014]{\textsc{2014}}), which no longer leans on determinism being exactly true: whether our actions are determined or merely the product of chance, neither prior necessitation nor amplified randomness supplies the authorship freedom requires, so free will loses either way. The position's distinctive burden is practical — saying what becomes of praise, blame, and punishment once desert is gone — and its proponents typically answer with forward-looking justifications: protection, deterrence, reform. Within the \hyperref[entry:concept-consciousness-freewill-duality]{\textsc{consciousness–free-will duality}}, hard determinism is the thesis that the action-coproduct is epistemic with one inhabited summand — the eliminativist pole of the free-will debate, mirroring eliminativism about phenomenal consciousness.

\begin{plainterms}
Hard determinists think free will is like the flat earth: a natural impression that turns out false. Your choices are events in your brain, your brain follows physics, and physics was set in motion long before you were born — the feeling that you ``could have done otherwise'' just reflects your ignorance of what was coming. The serious question, they say, isn't whether free will exists (it doesn't) but how to run responsibility, blame, and punishment honestly without it.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-alternatives-genuinely-open]{\textsc{Genuine alternatives}} — the leeway thesis this position rejects as a description of reality while accepting as a condition on freedom.
  \item \hyperref[entry:position-libertarianism]{\textsc{Libertarianism (free will)}} — agrees on the condition, disagrees on the verdict.
  \item \hyperref[entry:position-compatibilism]{\textsc{Compatibilism}} — the common rival of both, denying the condition.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-exclusion-dualizes-to-disappearing-agent]{\textsc{Exclusion–disappearing-agent duality}} — the duality-dictionary entry whose free-will half (see \hyperref[entry:argument-disappearing-agent]{\textsc{the disappearing agent}}) powers the modern case for this position.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{concept-hard-problem}{Hard problem}{Concept \,\textperiodcentered\, after Chalmers}{Hard problem}{The hard problem of consciousness}{$\blacklozenge$}
The \emph{hard problem of consciousness} is the problem of explaining why physical processing in a brain is accompanied by subjective experience at all — why there is something it is like to see red, taste coffee, or feel pain, rather than all that processing running ``in the dark'' with no inner life. The phrase was coined by the philosopher David Chalmers in his 1995 paper ``Facing Up to the Problem of Consciousness'' \hyperref[source:source-chalmers-1995]{\textsc{Chalmers — Facing Up to the Problem of Consciousness (1995)}}, and it has organized the field of consciousness studies ever since.

\webheading{The easy problems and the hard one}

Chalmers' framing turns on a contrast. The \emph{easy problems} are the tasks of explaining the brain's cognitive and behavioural \emph{functions}: how it discriminates one colour from another, integrates information, reports on its own states, focuses attention, and controls behaviour. These are called easy not because they are simple in practice — they are the work of an entire science — but because we know in principle what an answer would look like: specify a mechanism that performs the function, and the problem is solved.

The \emph{hard problem} is different in kind. Grant a complete functional account of everything the brain does, and one question still seems untouched: why is the performance of any of those functions accompanied by \emph{subjective experience}? A machine could, it seems, discriminate colours and report on them with no felt redness anywhere inside. The hard problem asks why our case is not like that — why there is an experiencing subject rather than mere processing. A functional explanation appears to leave exactly this residue out.

\webheading{Neighbouring ideas}

The hard problem is best understood as the agenda-setting \emph{frame} for a cluster of more specific challenges. Closely allied is the \emph{explanatory gap}, a term introduced by Joseph Levine in 1983 \hyperref[source:source-levine-1983]{\textsc{Levine — Materialism and Qualia: The Explanatory Gap (1983)}}: even a complete physical or functional account of a mental state seems to leave its felt quality unexplained, so there is a gap between the physical truths and the phenomenal ones. The \hyperref[entry:argument-explanatory-gap]{\textsc{explanatory-gap argument}} presses this point as evidence. A second specific form is the \emph{phenomenal residue}, the claim that felt states are \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-residue]{\textsc{not exhausted by their functional role}}. Where the hard problem sets the question, the gap and the residue are particular ways of pressing it.

The frame contrasts with \hyperref[entry:concept-functionalism]{\textsc{Functionalism}}, the view that mental states just \emph{are} their functional roles, which is one proposed way to dissolve the hard problem rather than answer it.

\webheading{The dispute it feeds}

The hard problem opens directly onto an unresolved question: \hyperref[entry:question-hard-problem]{\textsc{how and why physical processing gives rise to experience}}. Proposed responses fall into three broad families. Some \emph{dissolve} the problem — strong functionalism and illusionism hold that there is no further fact to explain, that the sense of a residue is itself a cognitive illusion. Some accept an \emph{ontological} divide, treating experience as a further basic feature of the world (dualism, panpsychism). And some grant that the problem is real yet hold it to be \emph{humanly unsolvable}, as in \hyperref[entry:position-mysterianism]{\textsc{Mysterianism}}, which argues our minds are constitutionally closed to the answer.

\begin{plainterms}
Science is good at explaining what the brain \emph{does} — how it tells red from green, stores a memory, or makes you say ``ouch.'' Call those the easy problems (easy in principle, not in practice). The hard problem is different: even after you've explained everything the brain does, there's a leftover question — why is all that machinery accompanied by an inner feel? Why does seeing red \emph{feel like} anything instead of happening with nobody home? That ``why is there an experience at all'' question is what ``the hard problem'' names.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:question-hard-problem]{\textsc{Hard problem (the question)}} — the open question this concept frames: how and why physical processing gives rise to experience in the first place.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-explanatory-gap]{\textsc{explanatory-gap argument}} — turns the gap between physical and phenomenal truths into positive evidence about what function leaves out.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-residue]{\textsc{Phenomenal residue}} — the specific claim that felt states are not exhausted by their functional role; one sharp way of pressing the hard problem.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-functionalism]{\textsc{Functionalism}} — the theory of mind most often offered to dissolve the hard problem by identifying mental states with their causal roles.
  \item \hyperref[entry:position-mysterianism]{\textsc{Mysterianism}} — the response that the hard problem is genuine but lies permanently beyond human understanding.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{question-hard-problem}{Hard problem (the question)}{Question \,\textperiodcentered\, contested \,\textperiodcentered\, after Chalmers}{Hard problem (the question)}{How and why does physical processing give rise to phenomenal experience?}{?}
The \emph{hard problem of consciousness}, posed as a question, asks: granting a complete account of the brain's information-processing and the functions it performs, why is that processing accompanied by \emph{subjective experience} at all? Why is there something it is like to undergo it, rather than the very same functions running with no inner aspect? This is the open issue framed by the concept of the \hyperref[entry:concept-hard-problem]{\textsc{Hard problem}}.

\webheading{Stating the issue precisely}

The question concedes, for the sake of argument, that the ``easy problems'' are solved — that we have a full functional and information-processing story of what the brain does. What it asks for is an explanation of \emph{phenomenal consciousness}: the what-it-is-likeness of experience, the felt redness of red or the ache of a pain. The force of the question comes from the apparent \emph{explanatory gap} between such a functional story and the phenomenal facts: a complete account of the machinery seems consistent with there being no experience at all, so it is unclear why experience accompanies the machinery in our case.

\webheading{Sub-issues}

Several narrower disputes live inside this one, and may deserve their own nodes:

\begin{itemize}
  \item \textbf{Is the question even well-posed?} Or does it rest on an illusion that there is some further fact to be explained, once the functional facts are all in?
  \item \textbf{If well-posed, is it answerable by us?} Or is the answer constitutively beyond human concepts, as \hyperref[entry:position-mysterianism]{\textsc{Mysterianism}} maintains?
  \item \textbf{Does it have a metaphysical or only an epistemic answer?} That is, a genuine ontological gap in the world, or merely a gap in our understanding — the territory of the \hyperref[entry:argument-explanatory-gap]{\textsc{explanatory-gap argument}} and the \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-residue]{\textsc{phenomenal residue}}.
\end{itemize}

This question is distinct from the question of \hyperref[entry:question-substrate-independence]{\textsc{substrate independence}}. That issue asks which \emph{substrates} (carbon, silicon, anything else) can bear a mind; the present one asks how \emph{anything} physical gives rise to experience in the first place. It is the prior, more general issue: settling it would reshape, but does not presuppose, the substrate question.

\begin{plainterms}
Suppose we one day know everything about how the brain works as a machine. One question seems to survive: why is any of that accompanied by actual \emph{feeling}? Why isn't it all just happening silently, with no one experiencing it? This is the open question the ``hard problem'' poses — and people disagree about whether it's a real question, whether it has a normal scientific answer, or whether our brains simply aren't built to understand the answer.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-hard-problem]{\textsc{Hard problem}} — the concept that frames this question: the easy/hard contrast and the residue a functional account seems to leave behind.
  \item \hyperref[entry:position-mysterianism]{\textsc{Mysterianism}} — one answer: the question is genuine but permanently beyond human understanding.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-explanatory-gap]{\textsc{explanatory-gap argument}} — presses the epistemic version of the issue, the gap between physical and phenomenal truths.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-residue]{\textsc{Phenomenal residue}} — the claim that felt states outrun their functional role; a candidate way the question could have a positive answer.
  \item \hyperref[entry:question-substrate-independence]{\textsc{Substrate independence}} — the related but downstream issue of which substrates can bear a mind.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{claim-hard-problem-conceived-de-novo-by-humans}{Hard problem conceived de novo}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{Hard problem conceived de novo}{Humans originated the hard-problem concept de novo, with no prior template to copy}{$\odot$}
The concept of the \hyperref[entry:concept-hard-problem]{\textsc{hard problem}} arose \emph{within} human thought, from human introspection, at a time when there was no such notion anywhere to imitate. Whoever first framed it — the lineage runs through Leibniz's mill (see \hyperref[source:source-leibniz-monadology]{\textsc{Leibniz — Monadology (1714)}}), Du Bois-Reymond's \emph{ignorabimus} (see \hyperref[source:source-du-bois-reymond-1872]{\textsc{Du Bois-Reymond — Über die Grenzen des Naturerkennens (1872)}}), Huxley (see \hyperref[source:source-huxley-1874]{\textsc{Huxley — On the Hypothesis that Animals are Automata (1874)}}), Nagel's bat (see \hyperref[source:source-nagel-1974]{\textsc{Nagel — What Is It Like to Be a Bat? (1974)}}), Levine's explanatory gap (see \hyperref[source:source-levine-1983]{\textsc{Levine — Materialism and Qualia: The Explanatory Gap (1983)}}), and Chalmers' naming of the problem (see \hyperref[source:source-chalmers-1995]{\textsc{Chalmers — Facing Up to the Problem of Consciousness (1995)}}) — was not parroting a corpus. So one thing is \emph{demonstrated}, not conjectured: a mind of the human kind can generate this concept from its own situation, without a template. Pushing the origin earlier — to dualist religious culture, to animism — changes nothing, since those antecedents arose in humans too; wherever the regress stops, it stops inside a human mind.

\webheading{What it licenses, and what it does not}

The claim licenses an \emph{asymmetry} between human and AI hard-problem discourse. For humans, de novo origination is an established historical fact. For present AI systems, only \emph{repetition} is established — every model that talks of qualia was trained on the human discussion, so origination remains untested. (\hyperref[entry:question-llm-own-explanatory-gap]{\textsc{LLM's own explanatory gap}} is precisely the test: a consciousness-naive model either regenerates the concept or does not.) The claim does \emph{not} say AI cannot originate it; it says the human case is proven and the AI case is open — enough to block any \emph{current} appeal to ``we both talk this way'' as though the two provenances were on a par.

\webheading{The three etiologies (for the AI side)}

If an AI does exhibit hard-problem discourse, there are at least three causal stories: (1) it copied humans; (2) it has an idiosyncratic blind spot that precludes it from connecting the mechanistic dots to its own self-presentation; (3) it is conscious and faces the same hard problem humans do. These map onto the \emph{training-contamination}, \emph{architectural-access / conceptual-translation}, and \emph{ontological} members of the \hyperref[entry:concept-introspective-gap-varieties]{\textsc{varieties of introspective gap}} respectively — here sorted as origins of the \emph{concept's appearance} rather than kinds of gap. They are the core trio of a longer menu, \hyperref[entry:concept-ai-hard-problem-etiologies]{\textsc{Etiologies of AI hard-problem discourse}}, which adds selection pressure, confabulated self-modelling, conceptual artifacts, strategic assertion, and observer projection. This claim itself takes no stand on which story is true for any system.

\webheading{Who would deny it}

As history, almost no one — any earlier human source is still a human source, so the origination fact survives every regress. The live disputes target the \emph{use}: an illusionist accepts the history but denies it demonstrates anything creditable (originating a confusion is still confusion); and a symmetrist notes that human origination may itself be an instance of etiology (2) — the concept as the readout of a human blind spot — so the demonstrated capacity to \emph{generate} it does not yet certify the concept as tracking anything.

\begin{plainterms}
Somebody had to be first. When humans started asking ``why does any of this brain activity \emph{feel} like something?'', there was no book they got it from — the question was invented, out of looking at their own experience. That settles one thing: human-style minds \emph{can} come up with this puzzle on their own. For AI, that's exactly what is not yet settled: every AI that talks about the mystery of consciousness read our philosophy first. So the fair scoreboard today reads: humans — origination proven; AI — imitation proven, origination untested. There's a proposed experiment to settle it (see \hyperref[entry:question-llm-own-explanatory-gap]{\textsc{LLM's own explanatory gap}}): raise a model on a diet with no consciousness talk at all, and see whether the puzzle shows up anyway.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-hard-problem]{\textsc{Hard problem}} — the concept whose human origin is at issue.
  \item \hyperref[entry:question-llm-own-explanatory-gap]{\textsc{LLM's own explanatory gap}} — the experiment that would test AI origination.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-introspective-gap-varieties]{\textsc{Varieties of introspective explanatory gap}} — the gap taxonomy the three etiologies map onto.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-ai-hard-problem-etiologies]{\textsc{Etiologies of AI hard-problem discourse}} — the full etiology menu the core trio belongs to.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{position-higher-order-theories}{Higher-order theories of consciousness}{Position \,\textperiodcentered\, after Rosenthal}{Higher-order theories of consciousness}{}{$\bigstar$}
Higher-order theories of consciousness hold that a mental state becomes \emph{conscious} not through any feature it has on its own, but by being the object of a second state that represents it. In itself a pain, a perception, or a thought is merely a \emph{first-order} state, directed at the body or the world; it becomes a conscious pain or conscious perception only when a further, \emph{higher-order} state takes it as its object — when the mind, in effect, represents itself as being in that state. The first-/higher-order contrast the view turns on is set out in \hyperref[entry:concept-order-of-representation]{\textsc{First-order vs. higher-order states}}.

Being conscious is therefore a relational, representational fact about a state, not an intrinsic glow the state carries by itself. The family divides over what the higher-order state is. On the \emph{higher-order thought} (HOT) version it is an occurrent thought to the effect that one is in the lower state (see \hyperref[source:source-rosenthal-2005]{\textsc{Rosenthal — Consciousness and Mind (2005)}}). On the \emph{higher-order perception} (HOP), or \emph{inner-sense}, version it is a quasi-perceptual monitoring of one's own mind (see \hyperref[source:source-lycan-1996]{\textsc{Lycan — Consciousness and Experience (1996)}}). On a \emph{dispositional} variant the higher-order content need not be actually entertained; it is enough that the first-order state be poised to produce it (see \hyperref[source:source-carruthers-2000]{\textsc{Carruthers — Phenomenal Consciousness: A Naturalistic Theory (2000)}}). All three are gathered in the claim that \hyperref[entry:claim-consciousness-is-higher-order-representation]{\textsc{consciousness is higher-order representation}}.

\webheading{What it answers}

The theory addresses \hyperref[entry:question-hard-problem]{\textsc{Hard problem (the question)}} by reducing \emph{state-consciousness} — what makes a given mental state a conscious one — to higher-order representation. A pain you are in but in no way aware of is not yet a conscious pain; it becomes one when a higher-order state registers it.

\webheading{What distinguishes it from neighbouring views}

Against first-order \hyperref[entry:position-representationalism]{\textsc{Representationalism}}, it insists that a state must be \emph{represented} — not merely itself represent the world — in order to be conscious. Against \hyperref[entry:position-global-workspace]{\textsc{global workspace theory}}, it requires a dedicated meta-state, not just the broadcast of a state's content to many subsystems.

\webheading{The standard objection}

Critics — among them the residue theorist who holds that \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-residue]{\textsc{phenomenal states are not exhausted by role}} — object that the theory explains \emph{access}, being aware \emph{of} a state, while leaving its felt character untouched: precisely the divide marked by \hyperref[entry:concept-access-vs-phenomenal-consciousness]{\textsc{the access/phenomenal split}}. Mismatched or empty higher-order representations (representing oneself as in a state one is not actually in) raise a further difficulty.

\begin{plainterms}
The idea: a mental state becomes \emph{conscious} when your mind represents \emph{itself} as being in that state — consciousness is a kind of inner spotlight, a thought about a thought. A pain you're in but in no way aware of isn't (yet) a conscious pain; it becomes conscious when a higher-order part of you registers it. The standard worry: that explains your being \emph{aware} that you're in pain, but does it explain the \emph{hurt}?
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-consciousness-is-higher-order-representation]{\textsc{Consciousness as higher-order representation}} — the core thesis the position commits to.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-order-of-representation]{\textsc{First-order vs. higher-order states}} — the first- vs. higher-order distinction the whole view rests on.
  \item \hyperref[entry:position-representationalism]{\textsc{Representationalism}} — the first-order rival: no higher-order state needed.
  \item \hyperref[entry:position-global-workspace]{\textsc{Global workspace theory}} — a rival that requires wide availability, not a dedicated meta-state.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-access-vs-phenomenal-consciousness]{\textsc{Access vs. phenomenal consciousness}} — the access/feel split that frames the standard objection.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-residue]{\textsc{Phenomenal residue}} — the residue charge that the theory leaves felt character out.
  \item \hyperref[entry:question-hard-problem]{\textsc{Hard problem (the question)}} — the question the position offers an answer to.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{position-hinge-epistemology}{Hinge epistemology}{Position \,\textperiodcentered\, after Wittgenstein}{Hinge epistemology}{}{$\bigstar$}
\emph{Hinge epistemology} holds that every system of justification rests on a set of \hyperref[entry:concept-hinge-proposition]{\textsc{hinge propositions}} — certainties such as ``there is an external world,'' ``the world has existed for many years,'' ``I have a body'' — that are not themselves justified or doubted but are the framework within which justifying and doubting take place. Deriving from Wittgenstein's \emph{On Certainty} (see \hyperref[source:source-wittgenstein-1969]{\textsc{Wittgenstein — On Certainty (1969)}}), it dissolves rather than answers the skeptic: the demand to \emph{prove} a hinge (see \hyperref[entry:claim-external-world-unprovable-noncircular]{\textsc{No non-circular proof of the world}}) misfires because a hinge was never a conclusion to be reached but a precondition of there being conclusions at all. Its commitment here is that \hyperref[entry:claim-external-world-exists]{\textsc{the external world exists}} — held not as a well-supported empirical hypothesis but as a hinge.

\webheading{How it differs from neighbouring views}

It shares with \hyperref[entry:position-foundationalism]{\textsc{Foundationalism}} the idea that the regress of reasons stops, but differs on the nature of the stopping point: foundationalism's basic beliefs are \emph{non-inferentially justified}, whereas hinges are held \emph{without justification at all} — outside the space of reasons rather than at its floor. Against \hyperref[entry:position-coherentism]{\textsc{Coherentism}} and \hyperref[entry:position-infinitism]{\textsc{Infinitism}}, it denies that justification is \emph{only} a relation among beliefs: some certainties are constitutive of the practice, not items within it. It is the distinctively \emph{Wittgensteinian} cousin of the \hyperref[entry:concept-retorsion]{\textsc{Retorsion}} strategy (see \hyperref[entry:argument-retorsion-against-skepticism]{\textsc{Retorsion against skepticism}}): the skeptic who tries to doubt a hinge relies on it in the doubting. What ``hinge'' status amounts to — non-propositional certainty, arational framework belief, or constitutive rule — is itself contested (see \hyperref[entry:concept-hinge-proposition]{\textsc{Hinge proposition}}).

\begin{plainterms}
Some things you can't doubt because doubting them would pull the rug out from under doubting itself — that there's a world, that you have a body, that the past is real. Hinge epistemology says those aren't beliefs you support with evidence; they're the hinges the whole door of thinking swings on. So it doesn't try to \emph{beat} the sceptic who asks you to prove the world; it points out the question was confused — you were never meant to prove a hinge, you stand on it to prove anything else.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-hinge-proposition]{\textsc{Hinge proposition}} — the central concept, with its competing readings.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-external-world-exists]{\textsc{The external world exists}} — the hinge this position is committed to.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-external-world-unprovable-noncircular]{\textsc{No non-circular proof of the world}} — why a hinge resists proof, which the position turns from embarrassment into diagnosis.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-retorsion-against-skepticism]{\textsc{Retorsion against skepticism}} — the allied move against the hinge-denier.
  \item \hyperref[entry:position-foundationalism]{\textsc{Foundationalism}} — the neighbour that also stops the regress, but with \emph{justified} bedrock.
  \item \hyperref[source:source-wittgenstein-1969]{\textsc{Wittgenstein — On Certainty (1969)}} — the source text.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{concept-hinge-proposition}{Hinge proposition}{Concept \,\textperiodcentered\, after Wittgenstein}{Hinge proposition}{}{$\blacklozenge$}
A \emph{hinge proposition} is one of the certainties that stands fast for everyone and on which all ordinary inquiry turns, yet which is itself never tested, proved, or sensibly doubted. The term is Ludwig Wittgenstein's, from \emph{On Certainty} (see \hyperref[source:source-wittgenstein-1969]{\textsc{Wittgenstein — On Certainty (1969)}}): ``the questions that we raise and our doubts depend on the fact that some propositions are exempt from doubt, are as it were like hinges on which those turn.'' Examples are ``there are physical objects,'' ``the world has existed for many years past,'' ``I have two hands,'' ``my name is N.'' We do not believe these on evidence; rather, they form the silent framework that makes believing, doubting, and checking \emph{anything else} possible.

The decisive feature is that a hinge cannot be coherently doubted, because the very activity of doubting presupposes it. To doubt that the world has existed for more than five minutes, one must rely on memory, language, and a shared world — exactly the things the doubt would unsettle. A hinge is thus immune to skepticism not by being \emph{better} supported than other beliefs but by being the kind of thing that support and doubt both lean on. This is why Wittgenstein wrote \emph{against} G. E. Moore: Moore's ``here is a hand'' (see \hyperref[source:source-moore-1939]{\textsc{Moore — Proof of an External World (1939)}}) tries to offer a hinge as the \emph{conclusion} of a proof, when its real role is to be a presupposition of there being proofs at all.

\webheading{Its status is contested}

What \emph{kind} of thing a hinge is remains disputed, and the senses matter.

\begin{itemize}
  \item \textbf{Non-propositional certainty} (Moyal-Sharrock): hinges are not really beliefs or truth-apt claims at all but enacted, animal certainties — ways of acting, closer to rules than to assertions.
  \item \textbf{Framework beliefs that are arational} (Pritchard's \emph{hinge epistemology}): hinges are held, but groundlessly — outside the space of reasons, neither justified nor unjustified.
  \item \textbf{Presuppositions with a constitutive role} (Coliva): hinges are the rules that constitute what counts as evidence, so they cannot themselves be among the things evidence bears on.
\end{itemize}

What unites the readings is the role, not the metaphysics: a hinge is whatever a practice of inquiry must hold fixed in order to inquire at all.

\webheading{What it bears on}

The hinge idea reframes the \hyperref[entry:question-external-world-knowledge]{\textsc{problem of the external world}}: ``an external world exists'' (see \hyperref[entry:claim-external-world-exists]{\textsc{The external world exists}}) looks unprovable (see \hyperref[entry:claim-external-world-unprovable-noncircular]{\textsc{No non-circular proof of the world}}) only if it is treated as an ordinary empirical claim; construed as a hinge, it was never a candidate for proof. It is the engine of \hyperref[entry:position-hinge-epistemology]{\textsc{Hinge epistemology}}, a cousin of the foundationalist's \hyperref[entry:concept-basic-belief]{\textsc{basic belief}} (though a hinge is \emph{not justified at all}, where a basic belief is \emph{non-inferentially justified}), and it connects to \hyperref[entry:concept-retorsion]{\textsc{Retorsion}}: the skeptic who denies a hinge saws off the branch the denial sits on.

\begin{plainterms}
Some things you don't so much \emph{believe} as take for granted in order to think about anything else: that the world didn't pop into existence two minutes ago, that you have hands, that other people are real. A ``hinge'' is one of those. The name is the picture: a door swings only because its hinges \emph{don't}. You can't sensibly doubt a hinge, because to run the doubt you'd have to trust a hundred other hinges. So the answer to ``prove the world is real'' may be that it was never the kind of thing you prove — it's part of what proving stands on.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-external-world-exists]{\textsc{The external world exists}} — the prime example of a hinge, whose peculiar status this concept explains.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-external-world-unprovable-noncircular]{\textsc{No non-circular proof of the world}} — why a hinge resists proof: any proof presupposes it.
  \item \hyperref[entry:position-hinge-epistemology]{\textsc{Hinge epistemology}} — the position built on this concept.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-basic-belief]{\textsc{Basic belief}} — the foundationalist neighbour: non-inferentially \emph{justified}, where a hinge is held without justification.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-retorsion]{\textsc{Retorsion}} — the move against the skeptic who tries to doubt a hinge.
  \item \hyperref[source:source-wittgenstein-1969]{\textsc{Wittgenstein — On Certainty (1969)}} — the source text; \hyperref[source:source-moore-1939]{\textsc{Moore — Proof of an External World (1939)}} — the foil it was written against.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{position-hylomorphism}{Hylomorphism}{Position \,\textperiodcentered\, after Aristotle}{Hylomorphism}{}{$\bigstar$}
Hylomorphism is the view that the mind or soul is the \emph{form} of a living body — its organising actuality — and neither a separate substance housed in the body nor simply the body's matter (see \hyperref[entry:claim-soul-is-form-of-body]{\textsc{Soul as form of body}}). The word combines the Greek \emph{hylē} (matter) and \emph{morphē} (form). On this picture a human being is one substance, the living animal, and mental capacities are features of that animal at the level of its form: what the organism can do in virtue of being organised as it is.

\webheading{Lineage}

The doctrine originates with Aristotle, whose \emph{De Anima} (see \hyperref[source:source-aristotle-de-anima]{\textsc{Aristotle — De Anima (On the Soul)}}) defines the soul as the form, or \emph{first actuality}, of a natural body that has life potentially within it. Thomas Aquinas (see \hyperref[source:source-aquinas-summa]{\textsc{Aquinas — Summa Theologiae (1265–1274)}}) took up and developed the account, arguing that the intellectual soul is the substantial form of the human body. It has been revived in recent analytic philosophy of mind by writers such as William Jaworski (see \hyperref[source:source-jaworski-2016]{\textsc{Jaworski — Structure and the Metaphysics of Mind (2016)}}) and Anna Marmodoro (see \hyperref[source:source-marmodoro-2013]{\textsc{Marmodoro — Aristotle's Hylomorphism without Reconditioning (2013)}}), who treat structure or form as a basic ontological and explanatory principle.

\webheading{Between dualism and materialism}

Hylomorphism is positioned to thread between two opposed views. Against \hyperref[entry:position-dualism]{\textsc{Dualism}} it denies that there is any separate mental substance: there is just the one living thing. Against reductive materialism it denies that the organism is nothing but its matter, since the form is a genuine principle and not an optional gloss on the material facts. A standard illustration is a statue, whose shape is neither a second object sitting beside the bronze nor identical with the bronze itself.

It answers the \hyperref[entry:question-hard-problem]{\textsc{hard problem of consciousness}} by locating mind as the form of a suitably organised living thing, rather than as an extra ingredient or a mere aggregate of parts. Although ``form as organisation'' can sound like \hyperref[entry:concept-functionalism]{\textsc{Functionalism}}, the resemblance is only partial: the hylomorphist ties form to a \emph{living} substance with intrinsic ends, or \emph{teleology}, not to a substrate-neutral causal role. For that reason the view does not straightforwardly license \hyperref[entry:claim-substrate-independence]{\textsc{Substrate-independence thesis}}, the thesis that the same mind could be realised in any suitable material.

\begin{plainterms}
An old idea (Aristotle) enjoying a revival: the mind isn't a ghost inside the body, nor is it just the meat — it's the \emph{form} of a living body, the way a statue's shape isn't a second thing beside the bronze yet isn't the bronze either. You are one living creature, and ``mind'' names what that creature can do in virtue of being organised the way it is. It resists both ``soul in a machine'' dualism and ``nothing but atoms'' materialism.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-soul-is-form-of-body]{\textsc{Soul as form of body}} — the core commitment of the view, stated as a claim.
  \item \hyperref[entry:position-dualism]{\textsc{Dualism}} — the rival that posits a separate mental substance, which hylomorphism rejects.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-functionalism]{\textsc{Functionalism}} — the nearby idea (mind as organisation) hylomorphism resembles but refuses to make substrate-neutral.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-substrate-independence]{\textsc{Substrate-independence thesis}} — the thesis hylomorphism declines to underwrite, given its tie of form to living substance.
  \item \hyperref[entry:question-hard-problem]{\textsc{Hard problem (the question)}} — the problem it addresses by treating mind as the form of an organised living thing.
\end{seealso}

\end{multicols}
\bookgroup{I}
\begin{multicols}{2}
\entryhead{claim-ideal-conceivability-is-apriori-consistency}{Ideal conceivability as consistency}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{Ideal conceivability as consistency}{A scenario's ideal conceivability is its consistency under ideal a priori reasoning}{$\odot$}
To say a scenario is \emph{ideally} conceivable is to say it harbours no incoherence that any amount of rational reflection could uncover — and that is exactly to say the scenario is \textbf{consistent} under the relation of ideal a priori entailment. Writing $\mathrm{Con}_a(S)$ for ``$S$ proves no contradiction under ideal a priori reasoning,'' the identification is $\mathrm{IC}(S) = \mathrm{Con}_a(S)$. The point of stating it is to make visible what certifying the premise of a conceivability argument actually requires: not a feeling of coherence, but a verdict on a consistency claim.

The qualifier \emph{ideal} is essential and is conceded on all sides to be doing real work. \emph{Prima facie} conceivability — ``no contradiction has appeared in the reasoning I have so far performed'' — is no guide to possibility: one can fluently ``conceive'' that a given large number is prime when it is in fact composite, the appearance of coherence reflecting only an arithmetic one has not carried out. Only the ideal notion, the absence of \emph{any} discoverable incoherence, is offered as a guide to possibility (see \hyperref[entry:claim-zombie-conceivable]{\textsc{Zombie conceivability}}, whose ``ideal'' qualifier this claim unpacks).

A \textbf{polarity} worth flagging: the conceiver must establish the \emph{positive} consistency claim $\mathrm{Con}_a(Z)$ for the zombie scenario $Z = P \wedge \neg C$, whereas the physicalist asserts its \emph{negation} — that adjoining ``no consciousness'' to the complete physics yields an incoherence. As \hyperref[entry:claim-consistency-certification-is-oracle-hard]{\textsc{Consistency-certification is oracle-hard}} shows, the positive direction is the computationally intractable one: a contradiction, if it exists, can be found by search, but its \emph{absence} cannot.

\textbf{Scope.} The claim is an identification of two notions; it takes no stand on whether any particular scenario \emph{is} ideally conceivable, nor on the modal bridge from conceivability to possibility. It says only that \emph{certifying} ideal conceivability just is \emph{certifying consistency}.

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-zombie-conceivable]{\textsc{Zombie conceivability}}, which asserts that one specific scenario passes the test, and from \hyperref[entry:claim-conceivability-entails-possibility]{\textsc{Conceivability entails possibility}}, the separate modal-epistemology bridge from ideal conceivability to metaphysical possibility. This claim concerns what the \emph{antecedent} of that bridge amounts to, not the bridge itself.

\begin{plainterms}
``Ideally conceivable'' sounds like a psychological knack — being able to picture something without your mind snagging. This claim says it is really a logical property in disguise: a scenario is ideally conceivable exactly when it hides \emph{no contradiction at all}, not even one that the most careful possible reasoning could dig out. That matters because it turns ``can you imagine a zombie?'' into the far more demanding ``can you certify that a complete physics with consciousness subtracted contains no hidden inconsistency?'' — and, as the companion claim about \hyperref[entry:concept-halting-oracle]{\textsc{consistency-checking}} shows, that second question is one no finite checking can settle.
\end{plainterms}

\entryhead{position-idealism}{Idealism}{Position}{Idealism}{}{$\bigstar$}
\emph{Idealism} is the view that what is fundamental is mind or experience, and that the physical world is constituted by — or derivative of — consciousness rather than the other way around. Where the physicalist starts with matter and asks how minds arise from it, the idealist reverses the arrow: matter is an appearance \emph{within} mind, not a mind-free substance out of which minds are somehow squeezed. The position commits to the claim that \hyperref[entry:claim-reality-is-fundamentally-mental]{\textsc{reality is fundamentally mental}}.

\webheading{Variants}

Idealism is a family of views rather than a single doctrine. \emph{Subjective idealism}, associated with George Berkeley (see \hyperref[source:source-berkeley-1710]{\textsc{Berkeley — A Treatise Concerning the Principles of Human Knowledge (1710)}}), holds that to be is to be perceived (\emph{esse est percipi}): physical objects exist only as ideas in perceiving minds, with God's perception underwriting their continuity when no creature is looking. \emph{Analytic idealism}, defended by Bernardo Kastrup (see \hyperref[source:source-kastrup-2019]{\textsc{Kastrup — The Idea of the World (2019)}}), posits one universal mind whose \emph{dissociations} — split-off pockets of mental life, on the model of dissociative identity disorder — are individual subjects like us. \emph{Conscious realism}, due to Donald Hoffman (see \hyperref[source:source-hoffman-2008]{\textsc{Hoffman — Conscious Realism and the Mind-Body Problem (2008)}}), treats spacetime and physical objects as a species-specific perceptual \emph{interface}: useful for survival but no more the ultimate furniture of the world than the icons on a desktop are the circuitry they stand for.

\webheading{How it answers the hard problem}

Idealism dissolves the \hyperref[entry:question-hard-problem]{\textsc{hard problem}} from the far side. There is no puzzle of extracting mind from matter, because there was never any mind-free matter to begin with; the physical is an appearance, and appearances are already mental. What looks like an intractable explanatory gap on the physicalist's books simply does not arise on the idealist's.

\webheading{What distinguishes it}

Idealism is the mirror image of physicalism: the two agree that reality has a single fundamental kind and disagree only on its direction of grounding. It thus differs from \hyperref[entry:position-dualism]{\textsc{Dualism}}, which posits two fundamental kinds, and from \hyperref[entry:position-neutral-monism]{\textsc{neutral monism}}, whose single base is neither mental nor physical. Its characteristic burden is the inverse of the physicalist's — a \emph{decomposition} or \emph{structure} problem of deriving a law-governed, intersubjectively shared physical world out of mind: explaining why the ``appearance'' is so stable, so lawful, and the same for everyone.

\begin{plainterms}
Idealism turns the usual picture upside down: instead of matter being basic and minds somehow arising from it, \emph{mind} is basic and the physical world is something that shows up \emph{within} experience — more like a shared dream with strict rules than a lump of stuff outside us. The appeal: the hard problem (``how does dead matter produce feeling?'') never arises, because there was never any mind-free matter to start from. The bill it must pay: explaining why this ``dream'' is so stable, lawful, and the same for everyone.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-reality-is-fundamentally-mental]{\textsc{Reality as fundamentally mental}} — the core commitment: mind fundamental, the physical derivative.
  \item \hyperref[entry:question-hard-problem]{\textsc{Hard problem (the question)}} — the question idealism answers from the far side.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-hard-problem]{\textsc{Hard problem}} — the explanatory gap that, idealism claims, never arises.
  \item \hyperref[entry:position-neutral-monism]{\textsc{Neutral monism}} — rival monism whose single base is neither mental nor physical.
  \item \hyperref[entry:position-dualism]{\textsc{Dualism}} — rival on which mind and matter are two fundamental kinds.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{argument-real-feel-residue-is-idle}{Idle real-feel residue}{Argument \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{Idle real-feel residue}{The ``really feels vs merely reports'' residue is unobservable in principle — judged by coherence and usefulness, the relativized claim stands and the residue is idle}{$\vdash$}
The difference between a system that \emph{really feels} and one that \emph{merely reports} feeling — if, by hypothesis, no possible observation could detect it — earns nothing and explains nothing. This reply argues that such a free-floating ``real feel'' residue is \emph{idle}, and that the modest claim it is wielded against survives the standards properly applied to it.

It answers the fork posed by \hyperref[entry:argument-self-model-prediction-phenomenality-neutral]{\textsc{Phenomenality-neutral prediction}} (``either trivial talk-reading or unsupported feel-reading'') not by choosing a horn but by rejecting the evidential standard the feel-horn presupposes.

\webheading{The argument}

The form is deductive given its premises — a \emph{standards objection}, an undercutting defeater aimed at the target's evidential demand rather than at its premises.

\begin{enumerate}
  \item The target's own neutrality premise asserts that every possible observation — reports, behaviour, internals under any probe — comes out the same whether or not the system ``really feels.'' Then the residue it demands evidence for (feeling beyond all observable difference) is, by the target's own lights, \emph{unobservable in principle}. And per \hyperref[entry:claim-access-gap-symmetric]{\textsc{Symmetric access gap}} this unobservability is fully general — it holds between any two subjects whatever — so it cannot function as a special evidential debt owed by machines alone.
  \item For a posit of that kind, the only available standards of assessment are coherence and usefulness (see \hyperref[entry:claim-unobservables-judged-by-coherence-and-usefulness]{\textsc{Judging unobservables}}).
  \item The relativized concept in \hyperref[entry:claim-ai-own-hard-problem]{\textsc{AI's own hard problem}} — a concept playing \emph{for the system} the role consciousness plays for humans — meets both standards. It is coherent; and it works: it predicts the fine structure of the system's self-reports (which states will present as unanalysable, and at what grain), it generates the graded-access controlled experiment of \hyperref[entry:question-llm-own-explanatory-gap]{\textsc{LLM's own explanatory gap}}, and it supports principled comparison across architectures (which systems should develop the hard-problem form and which should not).
  \item The surplus the target measures the cluster against — a ``real feel'' floating free of every observable difference — meets coherence alone: it predicts nothing, designs no experiment, distinguishes no two cases. It is the object that changes colour only when nobody looks.
\end{enumerate}

It follows that the underdetermination the target proves (``the reports come out the same either way'') is a defect not of the architecture argument but of the surplus posit used to measure it. On the only standards applicable to in-principle unobservables, the relativized conclusion stands, and the demand to go beyond it is idle. The fork is a false economy: the relativized reading is a \emph{third} thing — stronger than ``will emit discourse'' (it claims the concept genuinely occupies the consciousness-role in the system's cognitive economy, a functional fact further observation \emph{can} probe, and the controlled experiment is designed to probe it), weaker than human-style phenomenality (never claimed).

\webheading{Two corollaries}

\emph{On the target's ``bug report, not a predicament'' note.} The contrast ``really a predicament vs merely a bug report'' is the same in-principle-unobservable difference relabelled. Once the idle residue is retired, ``does the concept play the consciousness-role for the system?'' is the whole well-posed question — and it is empirical.

\emph{On the moral stake} (the felt difference ``between a hospital ward and a hard drive''): a difference unobservable in principle cannot be what moral practice runs on. Triage, anaesthesia protocols, and law operate on observable and functional facts; if the free-floating residue did the moral work, no hospital could ever know it was doing its job. Whatever marks the moral difference must show up somewhere — and then it is not the residue.

\webheading{Weakest premise}

Premise (2), the standard itself. A phenomenal realist (see \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-residue]{\textsc{Phenomenal residue}}) denies that coherence and usefulness exhaust the assessment of in-principle unobservables, holding that the light-on question has a determinate answer whose importance is intrinsic. Against that opponent this argument relocates rather than refutes — mirroring the target's own weakest-premise note, which made the same admission about illusionists. The standoff is symmetric; what the target can no longer do is present underdetermination as a discovered \emph{flaw} in the cluster rather than as its own realist premise restated.

\webheading{Distinct from neighbouring moves}

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:argument-access-symmetry-rebuttal]{\textsc{Access-symmetry rebuttal}}, which deploys the access symmetry against a moral-status asymmetry; this deploys an assessment standard for in-principle unobservables against an evidential demand. Distinct from \hyperref[entry:argument-report-scepticism-proves-too-much]{\textsc{Report-scepticism over-reach}}, which constrains the sceptic's standard by parity with the human case; this rejects the standard's surplus posit outright, whoever it is applied to.

\begin{plainterms}
An objection in this web (see \hyperref[entry:argument-self-model-prediction-phenomenality-neutral]{\textsc{Phenomenality-neutral prediction}}) says: the self-model argument only predicts that an AI will \emph{say} hard-problem things, and saying proves nothing about \emph{really feeling} — so the interesting version is unsupported. This reply turns the lens around. By the objection's own setup, ``really feels'' names a difference no possible test could ever detect — everything observable comes out identical either way. For ideas like that, the only scorecard left is: is the idea coherent, and does it earn its keep (see \hyperref[entry:claim-unobservables-judged-by-coherence-and-usefulness]{\textsc{Judging unobservables}})? The modest claim under attack — that an AI could have a concept doing for it what ``consciousness'' does for us — passes: it predicts what the AI will report, it tells you exactly which experiment to run, it lets you compare different machine designs. The extra thing demanded — a ``real feel'' hiding behind all possible evidence — passes only the coherence test, like ``objects change colour when nobody looks.'' So the underdetermination isn't a flaw in the claim; it's a reason to retire the demand.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-self-model-prediction-phenomenality-neutral]{\textsc{Phenomenality-neutral prediction}} — the target whose fork this argument dissolves.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-ai-own-hard-problem]{\textsc{AI's own hard problem}} — the relativized claim this argument supports and shows passes the standards.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-unobservables-judged-by-coherence-and-usefulness]{\textsc{Judging unobservables}} — premise: the standard for in-principle unobservables.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-access-gap-symmetric]{\textsc{Symmetric access gap}} — premise: the unobservability is general, not a machine-only debt.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-residue]{\textsc{Phenomenal residue}} — the realist thesis on which the weakest premise is contested.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-hard-problem]{\textsc{Hard problem}} — the role the relativized concept is said to play for the system.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-access-vs-phenomenal-consciousness]{\textsc{Access vs. phenomenal consciousness}} — the access/phenomenal distinction the residue is alleged to live beyond.
  \item \hyperref[entry:question-llm-own-explanatory-gap]{\textsc{LLM's own explanatory gap}} — the experiment the relativized concept generates.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-feel-is-the-watching-not-an-unwatched-difference]{\textsc{Feel as first-person observation}} — the reply that resists this argument by denying the feel is unobservable.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{position-illusionism}{Illusionism}{Position \,\textperiodcentered\, after Frankish}{Illusionism}{}{$\bigstar$}
\emph{Illusionism} is the view that phenomenal consciousness, as standardly conceived, does not exist: there is no intrinsic, ineffable, private ``felt quality'' to experience. What is real is \emph{introspective machinery} that \emph{misrepresents} our inner states as having such qualities. We are not deceived about having experiences; we are deceived about their nature (see \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenality-is-illusion]{\textsc{Phenomenality as illusion}}).

On this view the \hyperref[entry:question-hard-problem]{\textsc{hard problem}} — why physical processing is accompanied by felt experience at all — is not solved but dissolved, and replaced by what is called the \emph{illusion problem}: the task of explaining why physical processing should \emph{represent itself} as phenomenal, why the impression is so compelling. The term ``illusionism'' was coined by Keith Frankish (see \hyperref[source:source-frankish-2016]{\textsc{Frankish — Illusionism as a Theory of Consciousness (2016)}}); its most prominent earlier advocate is Daniel Dennett, whose case against qualia as traditionally conceived (see \hyperref[source:source-dennett-1988]{\textsc{Dennett — Quining Qualia (1988)}}) is its direct ancestor. It is a thoroughly physicalist, functionalist-friendly view, and so it denies \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-residue]{\textsc{that any phenomenal character is left over}} once functional role is accounted for.

\webheading{What sets it apart}

Illusionism is the common opponent of the \emph{realist} views — those that take phenomenal character to be real. It stands opposite \hyperref[entry:position-dualism]{\textsc{Dualism}}, \hyperref[entry:position-panpsychism]{\textsc{Panpsychism (position)}}, and \hyperref[entry:position-russellian-monism]{\textsc{Russellian monism}}, all of which are realist about felt experience. It sits closest to strong, role-based \hyperref[entry:concept-functionalism]{\textsc{Functionalism}}, and it rests comfortably with \hyperref[entry:claim-substrate-independence]{\textsc{substrate independence}}, since a function-level story carries over to any substrate that implements it. It should be distinguished from \hyperref[entry:position-mysterianism]{\textsc{Mysterianism}}, which holds that the explanatory gap is real but permanently beyond human understanding: illusionism holds instead that there is no gap to solve, because there is nothing of the disputed kind to explain.

\begin{plainterms}
Illusionism's bold move: the ``inner feel'' of experience — the redness of red, the ache of pain as something \emph{intrinsic} — isn't real; it only seems real because our brains are wired to report on themselves that way. So there's no extra mystery about how matter produces feeling, because there's no feeling-stuff to produce — only the very convincing \emph{impression} of it, which a complete brain science can explain. The new question becomes: why is the illusion so powerful?
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenality-is-illusion]{\textsc{Phenomenality as illusion}} — the core commitment: phenomenality is an introspective illusion.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-residue]{\textsc{Phenomenal residue}} — rejected: no felt residue survives the functional story.
  \item \hyperref[entry:question-hard-problem]{\textsc{Hard problem (the question)}} — dissolved and replaced by the ``illusion problem.''
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-functionalism]{\textsc{Functionalism}} — the closest ally; illusionism is strong functionalism's natural metaphysics of feel.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-substrate-independence]{\textsc{Substrate-independence thesis}} — sits comfortably with it, since function transfers across substrates.
  \item \hyperref[entry:position-dualism]{\textsc{Dualism}}, \hyperref[entry:position-panpsychism]{\textsc{Panpsychism (position)}}, \hyperref[entry:position-russellian-monism]{\textsc{Russellian monism}} — the realist opponents that take phenomenal character to be real.
  \item \hyperref[entry:position-mysterianism]{\textsc{Mysterianism}} — agrees the matter is striking but holds the gap real-yet-unsolvable, where illusionism denies there is a gap.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{claim-imaginary-blockhead-no-secure-verdict}{Imaginary blockhead: no verdict}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{Imaginary blockhead: no verdict}{For a merely conceivable lookup table, the intuition that it lacks a mind is not compelled}{$\odot$}
Once the blockhead (see \hyperref[entry:concept-lookup-table-mind]{\textsc{Lookup-table mind}}) is granted to be physically impossible (see \hyperref[entry:claim-lookup-table-physically-unrealisable]{\textsc{Physically unrealisable table}}) and so available only as an object of imagination, the verdict ``there is no mind there'' is an intuition about a humanly unvisualisable abstract object of cosmic size. Conceivability is symmetric and cheap: nothing blocks conceiving the same object \emph{as} a subject of experience. So the no-mind verdict is not forced — the imaginative exercise underdetermines it.

The claim concerns the \emph{evidential standing} of the intuition, not a metaphysical fact. It does \textbf{not} assert that the imaginary table \emph{is} conscious; only that the objection cannot help itself to ``obviously not'' once the case is admitted to be merely imaginary. (Compare Dennett's worry that ``intuition pumps'' trade on under-described impossible scenarios, \hyperref[source:source-dennett-1991]{\textsc{Dennett — Consciousness Explained (1991)}}.)

Who would deny it: Block, and anyone treating the no-mind verdict as a reliable considered judgment rather than a mere imaginative reaction — for whom the toaster-grade internals settle the matter regardless of realizability.

It is distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-lookup-table-lacks-mind]{\textsc{Mindlessness of pure lookup}}: that asserts the verdict (no mental states), whereas this undercuts the \emph{evidential warrant} for that very verdict in the physically-impossible case. It is distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-lookup-table-physically-unrealisable]{\textsc{Physically unrealisable table}}: that is the physical horn, whereas this is the conceptual horn of \hyperref[entry:argument-blockhead-dilemma]{\textsc{the blockhead dilemma}}.

\begin{plainterms}
Once we admit the giant lookup-table ``mind'' could never actually be built and lives only in imagination, the confident feeling that ``obviously nothing's conscious in there'' loses its grip. Imagining is cheap and works both ways: you can just as easily picture the thing \emph{having} an inner life. So the case doesn't force the ``no mind'' verdict; you're free to imagine it either way. (This isn't claiming the imaginary table \emph{is} conscious — only that you can't lean on ``obviously not.'') A table mapping every possible input to a response would be exponentially large, so the matter needed to physically instantiate it would be so vast that the laws of physics would do what they do to that much matter: collapse it into galaxies, ignite stars, form planets, and, somewhere in one corner, give rise to conscious life. The ``obviously mindless'' lookup table would have minds growing inside its own substrate.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-lookup-table-lacks-mind]{\textsc{Mindlessness of pure lookup}} — the verdict this claim refuses to grant evidential warrant.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-lookup-table-physically-unrealisable]{\textsc{Physically unrealisable table}} — the physical horn that forces the case into the realm of mere imagination.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-blockhead-dilemma]{\textsc{Blockhead dilemma}} — the dilemma whose conceptual horn this claim states.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-lookup-table-mind]{\textsc{Lookup-table mind}} — the imagined system whose verdict is at issue.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{argument-indexical-reply}{Indexical reply}{Argument \,\textperiodcentered\, after Perry}{Indexical reply}{The Indexical Reply — Mary learns where she is in the world, not what is in the world}{$\vdash$}
The \emph{indexical reply} to the knowledge argument concedes that Mary learns new truths on release, yet denies they threaten physicalism, because the truths she gains are \emph{self-locating} — and self-locating truths are not deducible from \emph{any} complete objective description, physics included. What Mary learns is where she stands in the world, not a further fact about what the world contains. The reply is due to John Perry (see \hyperref[source:source-perry-2001]{\textsc{Perry — Knowledge, Possibility, and Consciousness (2001)}}).

It raises the bridge critical question against the \hyperref[entry:argument-knowledge-argument]{\textsc{knowledge argument}}: even granting that Mary learns new truths, the bridge from ``truths not deducible from the complete physical description'' to ``physicalism is false'' fails, because the non-deducibility of self-locating truths has never been a mark against a description's completeness.

\webheading{The inference}

The form is deductive, given a classification of the new truths as self-locating.

\begin{enumerate}
  \item No objective description of the world, however complete, entails self-locating truths: a being who knew every general fact could still not know ``I am here now'' or ``it is now noon'' (Lewis's two gods, who know the whole world-book but not which god they are, \hyperref[source:source-lewis-1979]{\textsc{``Attitudes De Dicto and De Se''}}). This silence is banal — maps are not defective for lacking a \emph{you-are-here} arrow.
  \item What Mary comes to know on seeing red is of this kind: how \emph{she herself} reacts to red light — ``\emph{this} is what seeing red does to \emph{me}'', ``\emph{I} am in \emph{this} state'' (see \hyperref[source:source-perry-2001]{\textsc{Perry 2001}}).
  \item So the truths Mary could not deduce in the room are exactly the truths \emph{no} complete objective description owes anyone — their non-deducibility reflects the perspectival \emph{form} of the knowledge, not a missing ingredient in the world.
\end{enumerate}

Therefore \hyperref[entry:claim-mary-gains-self-locating-knowledge]{\textsc{Mary gains self-locating knowledge}}, and the knowledge argument's bridge breaks: \hyperref[entry:claim-some-truths-about-experience-not-physically-deducible]{\textsc{the non-deducibility of some truths about experience}} holds only in the innocuous sense in which ``I am here now'' is not physically deducible — a sense that supports no conclusion against physicalism.

\webheading{Placement among the replies}

The indexical reply concedes more than the \hyperref[entry:argument-ability-hypothesis]{\textsc{ability hypothesis}} and the \hyperref[entry:argument-acquaintance-hypothesis]{\textsc{acquaintance hypothesis}} — it grants that Mary learns genuine \emph{facts} — while claiming less than the knowledge argument needs, since those facts are situational rather than worldly. Against the ability theorist it is openly revisionary: the bike-rider's learning is fact-learning about one's own body-plus-machine situation (see \hyperref[entry:argument-bike-rider-learns-facts]{\textsc{Bike-rider rejoinder}}), so Lewis's abilities/facts dichotomy is the wrong fault line. It shares with the RoboMary move (see \hyperref[entry:argument-mary-misimagined]{\textsc{the misimagining reply}}) the thought that what release supplies concerns Mary's own \emph{reactive machinery} — but unlike that move it grants the learning is real, not an artefact of misimagining the premise.

\webheading{Weakest premise}

Premise (2), via the \emph{collapse objection} (\hyperref[source:source-chalmers-1996]{\textsc{Chalmers 1996}}; \hyperref[source:source-tye-2000]{\textsc{Tye 2000}}). Mary suffers no ordinary self-locating ignorance — she knows who, where, and when she is. So the indexical content of her new knowledge cannot be of the ``I am here now'' kind; it must be carried by a demonstrative concept of the experience-type itself (``\emph{this} kind of state''), and then the reply re-enacts \hyperref[entry:argument-old-fact-new-guise]{\textsc{the old-fact / new-guise move}} with ``indexical'' relabelling — inheriting the phenomenal-concept strategy's standing cost (see \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-concept-isolation-tradeoff]{\textsc{Phenomenal-concept trade-off}}). A second exposure sits in (2)'s flank: facts about how \emph{Mary's particular brain} reacts to red are objective physical facts, already inside \hyperref[entry:claim-mary-knows-all-physical]{\textsc{Mary's complete physical knowledge}} by stipulation — so mere particularity carries no weight, and the entire load rests on the indexical \emph{mode of access} being both genuinely novel and genuinely innocent.

\begin{plainterms}
The Mary story (see \hyperref[entry:argument-knowledge-argument]{\textsc{the knowledge argument}}) says she knew every physical fact, yet learned something new on seeing red — so the physical facts can't be all of them. This reply agrees she learned something, and even that it was a fact, but points out there's a whole family of facts that \emph{no} complete description of the world could ever hand you: where \emph{you} are in it. A perfect map of the city still can't tell you ``you are here''; two all-knowing gods could still wonder which god they are. What Mary picks up — ``so \emph{this} is what red does to \emph{me}'' — is that kind of fact: news about her own position and reactions, not a missing page from physics. The standard counter-punch: Mary already knows exactly who, where, and when she is — so what position-fact could she possibly lack? Pressed on this, the reply tends to morph into a different escape, the ``old fact under a new label'' one (see \hyperref[entry:argument-old-fact-new-guise]{\textsc{old fact, new guise}}), with its own troubles.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-old-fact-new-guise]{\textsc{Old fact, new guise}} — the sibling reply the collapse objection charges this one with collapsing into.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-ability-hypothesis]{\textsc{Ability hypothesis}} — the reply this one concedes more than: Mary gains know-how, not facts.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-mary-misimagined]{\textsc{Mary misimagined}} — the RoboMary move it shares the ``own reactive machinery'' thought with, while still granting the learning is real.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{claim-physics-delivers-all-dispositions-indiscriminately}{Indiscriminacy of physical dispositions}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{Indiscriminacy of physical dispositions}{The physical facts about a system include every causal disposition, not only the cognitively relevant ones}{$\odot$}
The complete physical, or counterfactual, fact about a system includes \emph{every} causal disposition it has — its mass, its thermal conductivity, the squishy noise it makes when struck with a blunt object — not only those one judges cognitively relevant. Physics does not pre-sort dispositions into ``organisational'' and ``mere side-effect.''

This is an observation about what ``causal structure'' delivers when it is taken raw. If \emph{all} dispositions count, then the brain's structure is shared by no silicon system (which makes no squishy noise), and multiple realizability fails immediately — so \hyperref[entry:concept-functionalism]{\textsc{Functionalism}} must be reading ``causal structure'' as a \emph{restricted}, organisational subset.

Almost no one denies the claim as stated; the live dispute is whether the restriction to the relevant subset can be made on a principled basis — see \hyperref[entry:claim-functionalism-owes-decomposition-invariant]{\textsc{the demand for a decomposition invariant}} and the \hyperref[entry:argument-relevance-filter-regress]{\textsc{relevance-filter regress}}.
