One tradition answers them by \emph{retorsion} (see \hyperref[entry:concept-retorsion]{\textsc{Retorsion}}), showing that to assert the doubt is to presuppose its falsity; another, Deutsch's, dismisses solipsism as a \emph{bad explanation} parasitic on realism (see \hyperref[entry:argument-deutsch-anti-solipsism]{\textsc{Deutsch's anti-solipsism}}).
\end{itemize}

The varied responses — \hyperref[entry:position-foundationalism]{\textsc{Foundationalism}}, \hyperref[entry:position-coherentism]{\textsc{Coherentism}}, \hyperref[entry:position-infinitism]{\textsc{Infinitism}}, \hyperref[entry:position-hinge-epistemology]{\textsc{Hinge epistemology}}, and the \hyperref[entry:position-fallibilism]{\textsc{fallibilist}} dissolution that denies the demand for justification altogether — differ less over the world than over what \emph{justification} is and whether knowledge requires it.

\begin{plainterms}
Is there really a world out there, outside your head — and even if there is, how could you ever \emph{prove} it? The trouble is that every test you could run is just another experience, and your experiences would feel exactly the same in a convincing dream. So any ``proof'' seems to lean on the very thing it's trying to prove. The deep question this raises isn't only ``is there a world?'' but ``what would even count as knowing there is — must you be able to \emph{prove} it, or can you \emph{know} it without proof?'' The rival answers in this corner of the web mostly disagree about that second thing.
\end{plainterms}

\entryhead{claim-knowledge-without-noncircular-proof}{Knowing without proving}{Claim}{Knowing without proving}{One can know a proposition without being able to demonstrate it non-circularly}{$\odot$}
Knowing that \emph{p} and being able to \emph{prove} \emph{p} to a doubter are different achievements, and the first does not require the second. On an \emph{externalist} (e.g. reliabilist) epistemology, a belief counts as knowledge when it is produced and sustained in the right way — by a reliable, truth-tracking process — whether or not the knower can mount a non-circular argument for it. So one may know that an external world exists even if, as \hyperref[entry:claim-external-world-unprovable-noncircular]{\textsc{No non-circular proof of the world}} holds, its existence cannot be demonstrated without circularity: the circularity defeats the \emph{demonstration}, not the \emph{knowledge}.

This is the epistemic counterpart of the truth/\hyperref[entry:concept-dialectical-cogency]{\textsc{cogency}} distinction. Just as a sound argument can fail to be cogent — true and valid yet unable to move an opponent who cannot reach its premises non-circularly — a belief can be warranted yet not non-circularly demonstrable. Demonstrability is a \emph{dialectical} property, relating a proposition to an audience; knowledge is not.

\textbf{Scope.} The claim is permissive, not triumphal: it says proof is not \emph{necessary} for knowledge, leaving open which beliefs actually are known. It is the move that lets one hold, without contradiction, both that the external world is unprovable and that we nonetheless know it.

\textbf{Who would deny it.} An \emph{internalist} about justification, who holds that to know one must be able to cite one's grounds — and a fortiori a justificationist who equates knowing with having a non-circular justification — denies it. For them the gap this claim opens is illusory: a belief you cannot (even in principle) ground is not knowledge.

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-cogency-requires-accessible-noncircular-warrant]{\textsc{Cogency needs non-circular warrant}}, which is about when an \emph{argument} can move an opponent; this is about when a \emph{belief} amounts to knowledge. They are mirror images across the same truth-versus-demonstrability divide.

\begin{plainterms}
Knowing something and being able to \emph{prove} it to a sceptic are not the same job. You might genuinely know there's a world outside your head — your belief is produced by reliably bumping into that world — even though any ``proof'' you offer would secretly assume it. The proof problem is about \emph{winning an argument}; the knowing is about \emph{being suitably hooked up to the facts}. So ``you can't prove it'' needn't mean ``you don't know it.'' (Someone who insists that knowing \emph{requires} being able to show your reasons will push back — that's the internalist.)
\end{plainterms}

\entryhead{concept-knowledge-argument}{Knowledge argument}{Concept \,\textperiodcentered\, after Jackson}{Knowledge argument}{The Knowledge Argument (Mary the colour scientist)}{$\blacklozenge$}
The \emph{knowledge argument} is a thought experiment, devised by Frank Jackson in 1982 (see \hyperref[source:source-jackson-1982]{\textsc{Jackson — Epiphenomenal Qualia (1982)}}), that aims to show physicalism is false by exhibiting a fact about experience that no amount of physical knowledge can capture. Its heroine is \emph{Mary}, a brilliant scientist who has lived her whole life in a black-and-white room and learns about the world only through a black-and-white monitor. She comes to know every physical fact about colour vision — the wavelengths of light, the workings of the retina, the neuroscience of what happens in a brain seeing ``red.'' Then she leaves the room and sees a ripe tomato for the first time. Intuitively she \emph{learns something}: what it is actually like to see red. But she already knew all the physical facts, so what she learns appears to be a further, non-physical fact about experience.

\webheading{What it targets}

The argument is aimed at \emph{physicalism}, the thesis that all facts are physical facts. The reasoning is an epistemic one: if Mary, in command of every physical fact, still learns something on seeing red, then some fact about colour experience was not among the physical facts — and physicalism, which says there are no such leftover facts, is false. The new fact is held to be \emph{phenomenal}: a fact about the felt quality of experience, the ``what it is like'' that physical description seems to leave out.

\webheading{A concrete walk-through}

Mary's case is vivid because the stipulation is deliberately extreme. She is not merely well-informed; by hypothesis she knows \emph{all} the physics, down to the last detail a completed science could state. So the moment of release is engineered to isolate one question: once the physical story is genuinely complete, is anything still missing? The proponent says yes — the redness of red, as Mary now undergoes it, was nowhere in her textbooks. A physicalist must say either that she learns nothing new (which seems false) or that what she gains is not a new \emph{fact} at all.

\webheading{How it relates to its neighbours}

The knowledge argument is the \emph{epistemic} route to anti-physicalism: it argues from what can be known or deduced from the physical facts. Its sibling, the \hyperref[entry:concept-philosophical-zombie]{\textsc{Philosophical zombie}}, takes a \emph{modal} route, arguing from what is conceivable and therefore possible — a being physically just like us but with no inner experience at all. The two converge on the same destination, the claim that consciousness is a fundamental, non-physical feature of the world (see \hyperref[entry:claim-consciousness-fundamental-nonphysical]{\textsc{Fundamental non-physical consciousness}}). The argument also presses the broader \hyperref[entry:concept-hard-problem]{\textsc{Hard problem}} of consciousness, since both turn on the apparent gap between a complete physical account and the felt character of experience. The standard rebuttals all fasten on the phrase ``learns something,'' asking whether gaining a new ability, or a new way of grasping an old fact, really amounts to learning a new truth.

\begin{plainterms}
Mary has spent her entire life in a black-and-white room, but she is the world's leading expert on the science of colour — she knows every physical fact about how eyes and brains process ``red,'' down to the last detail. One day she walks outside and sees a red rose for the first time. Surely she learns \emph{something}: what red actually looks like. But she already knew all the physical facts. So whatever she just learned looks like an \emph{extra} fact, one the complete physics left out — which would mean experience isn't captured by physical facts alone. The popular reply: maybe she didn't learn a new \emph{fact}, just gained a new \emph{ability}, to recognise and imagine red, which isn't the same as discovering a new truth.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-philosophical-zombie]{\textsc{Philosophical zombie}} — the modal twin of this epistemic argument; a physical duplicate with no inner experience, used to argue the physical facts don't fix the felt facts.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-hard-problem]{\textsc{Hard problem}} — the broader puzzle of why physical processing is accompanied by felt experience at all; the knowledge argument is one sharp way to press it.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-consciousness-fundamental-nonphysical]{\textsc{Fundamental non-physical consciousness}} — the anti-physicalist conclusion both the knowledge argument and the zombie argument push toward.
  \item \hyperref[source:source-jackson-1982]{\textsc{Jackson — Epiphenomenal Qualia (1982)}} — Jackson's ``Epiphenomenal Qualia,'' where Mary is introduced (and which he later recanted).
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{argument-knowledge-argument}{Knowledge argument (Mary's room)}{Argument \,\textperiodcentered\, after Jackson}{Knowledge argument (Mary's room)}{The Knowledge Argument — Mary learns a fact the physical description omits}{$\vdash$}
The \emph{knowledge argument} — Frank Jackson's case of Mary the colour scientist (see \hyperref[source:source-jackson-1982]{\textsc{Jackson — Epiphenomenal Qualia (1982)}}) — is a deductive argument against physicalism that proceeds in two steps: first it opens an \emph{epistemic gap} (a fact Mary cannot get from the physics), then it takes an \emph{epistemic-to-ontological} step from that gap to a conclusion about what exists. It is a thought-experiment counterexample: a single imagined case meant to refute the thesis that all facts are physical facts.

\webheading{The inference}

\begin{enumerate}
  \item \emph{Scenario} — \hyperref[entry:claim-mary-knows-all-physical]{\textsc{Mary knows all the physical facts}}: raised in a black-and-white room, Mary knows every physical fact about colour vision.
  \item \emph{Intuition} — \hyperref[entry:claim-mary-learns-on-release]{\textsc{on first seeing red, Mary learns something new}}.
  \item \emph{Bridge}: if she already knew all the physical facts yet learns a new truth, that truth is not deducible from the physical facts.
\end{enumerate}

The conclusion is that \hyperref[entry:claim-some-truths-about-experience-not-physically-deducible]{\textsc{some truths about experience are not physically deducible}}. That conclusion in turn \texttt{supports} the claim that \hyperref[entry:claim-consciousness-fundamental-nonphysical]{\textsc{consciousness is fundamental and non-physical}} (the missing truths concern non-physical facts) and the \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-residue]{\textsc{phenomenal residue}} that experience is not exhausted by causal role; and it \texttt{attacks} the physicalist thesis that \textsc{mental events are physical} (the core commitment of \hyperref[entry:position-physicalism]{\textsc{Physicalism}}) along with the uniform reading of \hyperref[entry:claim-substrate-independence]{\textsc{substrate independence}}.

\webheading{The weakest premise}

The soft point is the \emph{intuition} — premise 2 — read as Mary acquiring a new \emph{fact}. Two families of reply grant that Mary changes while denying she learns a new truth. The \textbf{ability hypothesis} (Lewis, \hyperref[source:source-lewis-1988]{\textsc{Lewis — What Experience Teaches (1988)}}; Nemirow, \hyperref[source:source-nemirow-1990]{\textsc{Nemirow — Physicalism and the Cognitive Role of Acquaintance (1990)}}) says she gains know-how — the abilities to recognise, imagine, and remember red — not a fact. The \textbf{old-fact / new-guise} reply says she gains a new \emph{phenomenal concept} of a fact she already knew, a second way of grasping one and the same physical truth. Either reply blocks the inference to a non-physical fact. A second soft point is the \emph{bridge}, the epistemic-to-ontological step: even granting an undeducible truth, the move to a \emph{non-physical fact} is resisted, since a gap in what can be \emph{deduced} may reflect the \emph{concepts} we use rather than the \emph{ontology} of the world. In the vocabulary of the \emph{thought-experiment pattern}, these are its critical questions CQ-verdict (is the intuited verdict the right one?) and CQ-bridge (does the case really bear on the thesis attacked?). Note too that Jackson himself later recanted the argument (see \hyperref[source:source-jackson-1982]{\textsc{Jackson — Epiphenomenal Qualia (1982)}}).

\webheading{Its place among the anti-physicalist arguments}

This argument is distinct from \hyperref[entry:argument-zombie-conceivability]{\textsc{the zombie argument}}: that one is \emph{modal}, reasoning from conceivability to possibility, whereas this one is \emph{epistemic}, reasoning from complete physical knowledge to a still-missing piece of knowledge. The two are the classic modal/epistemic pair, and they converge on the same conclusion that \hyperref[entry:claim-consciousness-fundamental-nonphysical]{\textsc{consciousness is fundamental and non-physical}}.

\begin{plainterms}
Mary knows every physical fact about colour but has only ever seen black and white. She steps outside, sees red for the first time, and — surely — learns what red is \emph{like}. Yet she already knew all the physics. So she seems to have learned a fact the physics didn't contain, which suggests not everything about experience is captured by physical facts. The famous escape hatch: maybe she didn't learn a new \emph{fact} at all, only a new \emph{skill}, recognising and picturing red — and gaining a skill isn't discovering a truth. Even the inventor of this argument, Jackson, eventually changed his mind and rejected it.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-zombie-conceivability]{\textsc{Zombie argument}} — the modal partner of this epistemic argument; the two together are the standard pair of anti-physicalist thought experiments.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-mary-knows-all-physical]{\textsc{Mary knows all physical facts}} — the scenario premise: Mary's stipulated complete physical knowledge.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-mary-learns-on-release]{\textsc{Mary learns on release}} — the intuition premise and weakest link, where the standard replies bite.
  \item \hyperref[entry:position-physicalism]{\textsc{Physicalism}} — the view the argument is built to refute.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{claim-all-knowledge-is-conjectural}{Knowledge is conjectural}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, after Popper}{Knowledge is conjectural}{All knowledge is fallible conjecture, resting on no certain foundations}{$\odot$}
There are no certain foundations for knowledge, and none are needed. On Karl Popper's view (see \hyperref[source:source-popper-1963]{\textsc{Popper — Conjectures and Refutations (1963)}}), knowledge does not grow by deriving conclusions from secure, justified starting-points but by \emph{conjecture and refutation}: we propose bold guesses and subject them to severe criticism and testing, retaining those that survive — always provisionally, always open to overthrow. Even observation statements are theory-laden conjectures, not bedrock. What we call knowledge is the best-tested surviving guess, not a body of justified certainties.

The thesis is \emph{anti-justificationist}: it does not try to escape \hyperref[entry:concept-munchhausen-trilemma]{\textsc{the Münchhausen trilemma}} by choosing a horn but rejects the demand that generates it. Asking for the full justification of a belief is a mistake; the right question is not ``how is this guaranteed?'' but ``how could this be wrong, and have we tried hard to find out?''

\textbf{Scope.} ``Conjectural'' means \emph{not conclusively justified and always revisable} — not arbitrary or worthless. Conjectures differ enormously in how well they have withstood criticism, and a well-tested, \hyperref[entry:claim-good-explanation-is-hard-to-vary]{\textsc{hard-to-vary}} theory is knowledge in the fullest sense the view allows, despite carrying no certificate of certainty.

\textbf{Who would deny it.} \emph{Foundationalists} deny it, holding that some beliefs are \hyperref[entry:concept-basic-belief]{\textsc{basic}} and certain; classical \emph{rationalists} and \emph{empiricists} alike sought indubitable starting points (clear-and-distinct ideas; the given of sense). Any epistemology on which knowledge \emph{requires} justification to count as knowledge stands opposed.

This is the core commitment of \hyperref[entry:position-fallibilism]{\textsc{Fallibilism}}; it reframes the external-world problem by denying that the unprovability of \hyperref[entry:claim-external-world-exists]{\textsc{The external world exists}} is any special embarrassment — \emph{nothing} is provable in the foundationalist's sense, so the world is in good company.

\begin{plainterms}
We never really \emph{prove} anything once and for all; we guess, then try our hardest to shoot the guesses down, and keep the ones still standing — for now. That's Popper's picture: knowledge isn't a building on bedrock, it's our current best survivor in a long tournament of criticism. ``Conjectural'' doesn't mean flimsy — a guess that's been hammered on for centuries and won't break is about as solid as knowledge gets. It just means there's no certificate of certainty anywhere, for anything, so the fact that you can't \emph{prove} the external world isn't a special scandal — you can't conclusively prove anything.
\end{plainterms}

\end{multicols}
\bookgroup{L}
\begin{multicols}{2}
\entryhead{position-libertarianism}{Libertarianism (free will)}{Position}{Libertarianism (free will)}{Libertarianism (about free will)}{$\bigstar$}
\emph{Libertarianism} (no relation to the political doctrine) holds that free will exists and is incompatible with determinism — so determinism is false, at least where free agents act. When you choose freely, more than one future is genuinely open (see \hyperref[entry:claim-alternatives-genuinely-open]{\textsc{Genuine alternatives}}), and you are the difference: not the laws, not the past, not chance, but the agent settles which alternative becomes actual.

The position's burden is to say how an undetermined decision can still be the agent's \emph{doing} rather than a lucky accident — the \emph{luck objection} that shadows every version. Event-causal libertarians answer with indeterministic causation among the agent's own states; agent-causal libertarians (Chisholm, \hyperref[source:source-chisholm-1964]{\textsc{1964}}) posit \hyperref[entry:concept-agent-causation]{\textsc{agent causation}}, the agent as substance originating action without being caused to; non-causal versions drop the causal picture altogether. The Consequence Argument (van Inwagen, \hyperref[source:source-van-inwagen-1983]{\textsc{1983}}) supplies the position's negative half — that determinism excludes the openness freedom needs — against \hyperref[entry:position-compatibilism]{\textsc{Compatibilism}}; the \hyperref[entry:argument-disappearing-agent]{\textsc{disappearing-agent objection}} (Pereboom, \hyperref[source:source-pereboom-2014]{\textsc{2014}}) is the sharpest current attack on its positive half. Within the \hyperref[entry:concept-consciousness-freewill-duality]{\textsc{consciousness–free-will duality}}, libertarianism occupies the further-fact pole of the free-will debate — the mirror image of phenomenal realism about consciousness (see \hyperref[entry:claim-zombie-dualizes-to-volitional-zombie]{\textsc{Volitional zombie}}) — and its reliance on the armchair coherence of agent-causation is challenged by \hyperref[entry:argument-agent-causation-certificate]{\textsc{Certificate objection to agent-causation}}.

\begin{plainterms}
Libertarians about free will think that when you stood at a real fork in life, both roads were truly open — the universe itself hadn't settled it — and \emph{you} did the settling. The hard part is explaining how ``not settled by anything prior'' differs from ``random'': if nothing made you choose left over right, was the choice yours or a coin flip's? Every version of the view is an attempt to answer that question.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-alternatives-genuinely-open]{\textsc{Genuine alternatives}} — the leeway commitment.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-agent-causation]{\textsc{Agent causation}} — the flagship metaphysics of origination.
  \item \hyperref[entry:position-compatibilism]{\textsc{Compatibilism}} and \hyperref[entry:position-hard-determinism]{\textsc{Hard determinism}} — the rivals: one denies determinism matters, the other agrees it matters and concludes freedom is absent.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-agent-causation-certificate]{\textsc{Certificate objection to agent-causation}} — the computability-theoretic challenge to the view's modal credentials.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{claim-lifetime-bounded-table-covers-encountered-inputs}{Lifetime-bounded table}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, after Block}{Lifetime-bounded table}{A lookup table can be bounded to cover every input a subject encounters in a lifetime}{$\odot$}
A \hyperref[entry:concept-lookup-table-mind]{\textsc{lookup-table mind}} need not be infinite to mimic a person. The table can be stipulated to store a response for every input string up to a length exceeding any the subject could actually receive in a finite lifetime — Block's ``Blockhead'' construction (see \hyperref[source:source-block]{\textsc{Block — Psychologism and Behaviorism (1981)}}). Within that bounded domain, the only domain the subject ever inhabits, the table has a stored answer for every input that arises.

\webheading{Scope}

The claim concerns the \emph{coverage} of a suitably bounded table over the inputs a finite agent meets; it grants that the table is finite. It does not assert that the table is physically \emph{buildable} — that is denied by \hyperref[entry:claim-lookup-table-physically-unrealisable]{\textsc{the unrealisability claim}} — only that, as specified, its domain includes every input the subject encounters. Anyone who presses \hyperref[entry:claim-mind-handles-unbounded-inputs]{\textsc{that a mind handles unbounded inputs}} as the \emph{relevant} asymmetry would resist it; the reply is that inputs outside the lifetime bound are irrelevant to matching \emph{this} subject.

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-lookup-table-possible]{\textsc{the general separability of behaviour from organisation}}: this is the specific coverage move that defends that claim against the combinatorial-impossibility reply.

\begin{plainterms}
You don't need an infinite answer-table to mimic a person — just one big enough to hold a stored reply for every input they'd actually meet in a whole lifetime. Inputs longer or stranger than that never come up for them, so they don't matter. Within the range a real person ever lives through, the bounded table always has an answer ready. (This only claims the table \emph{covers} those inputs — not that it could ever be physically built, which is a separate, losing battle.)
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-lookup-table-possible]{\textsc{Lookup-table possibility}} — the separability claim this coverage move is mustered to defend.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-mind-handles-unbounded-inputs]{\textsc{Open-ended responsiveness of minds}} — the rival asymmetry this claim sets aside as irrelevant to a finite subject.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-lookup-table-physically-unrealisable]{\textsc{Physically unrealisable table}} — why the bounded table, though it covers the inputs, still cannot be built.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-lookup-table-mind]{\textsc{Lookup-table mind}} — the construction whose coverage is at issue.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{claim-inside-vantage-access-limited}{Limited inside vantage}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{Limited inside vantage}{A system's internal vantage on itself is informationally poorer than an external vantage}{$\odot$}
Facts realised \emph{in} a system's processing can be unavailable \emph{to} that system's own self-report. The vehicle of cognition is not, in general, an object of cognition, so an outside observer with full instrumentation can know things about the system that the system cannot derive about itself: its internal vantage is strictly poorer than the external one. This is the \emph{architectural-access} member of the \hyperref[entry:concept-introspective-gap-varieties]{\textsc{Varieties of introspective explanatory gap}}.

\webheading{Scope}

An \emph{access} claim (about what the system can reach), not a \emph{phenomenal} one. It does not say the inaccessible facts are non-physical or that anything is left out of the world — only that they are out of the self-model's reach.

\webheading{Illustrations}

In the language-model case the gap is concrete. A model's token-probability distribution contains its own uncertainty, yet the model as a conversational agent is notoriously poorly calibrated about that uncertainty — the information is \emph{in} the logits but not available to its self-report. It cannot directly inspect its own activations unless externally instrumented. And it cannot register counterfactuals about how slightly different inputs would have changed its state when those differences make no difference to the output. In each case the fact is present in the implementation but absent from the accessible cognitive economy (\hyperref[entry:claim-llm-functional-introspection]{\textsc{LLM functional introspection}} marks how limited that economy is).

\webheading{Who would deny it}

Someone who holds that a sufficiently reflective system can in principle be given transparent access to all of its own state (full self-instrumentation), so the poverty is contingent and removable rather than structural.

\webheading{Distinct from neighbours}

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-cognitive-closure-exists]{\textsc{Possibility of cognitive closure}} (McGinn, \hyperref[source:source-mcginn-1989]{\textsc{McGinn — Can We Solve the Mind–Body Problem? (1989)}}): that is the general possibility that a \emph{kind of mind} cannot form certain \emph{concepts}; this is the narrower, mechanism-level point that specific facts realised in a system are missing from its self-report workspace.

\begin{plainterms}
A thing can be true \emph{of} a system without being available \emph{to} it. Your brain runs on neurons firing, but you can't feel them fire; the fact is in you, not available to you. Likewise an AI's own logits already encode how uncertain it is — yet the model is famously bad at telling you its confidence, because that number isn't wired into what it can report on. Same for its own activations, and for input differences that never change its answer. So someone watching from outside, with the right tools, can know more about the system than the system can work out about itself.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-introspective-gap-varieties]{\textsc{Varieties of introspective explanatory gap}} — the catalogue; this is the architectural-access member.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-llm-functional-introspection]{\textsc{LLM functional introspection}} — how limited the actual self-report economy is.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-full-self-access-achievable]{\textsc{Complete internal self-access}} — the optimist denial that the poverty is structural.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-cognitive-closure-exists]{\textsc{Possibility of cognitive closure}} — McGinn's concept-closure, distinguished from this access point.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{claim-llm-functional-introspection}{LLM functional introspection}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, after Lindsey}{LLM functional introspection}{Some current LLMs exhibit limited, unreliable functional introspective awareness of their internal states}{$\odot$}
Some current large language models show a limited, unreliable capacity to report on features of their own internal processing — a \emph{functional} kind of introspection, with no implication that anything is felt. Under \emph{concept-injection} probing — steering an internal representation toward a known concept, then asking the model about its own state — current frontier models can sometimes detect that a representation has been steered, distinguish a prior internal representation from raw text input, and distinguish their own generated outputs from artificially inserted prefills. The capacity is real but, in the words of the work documenting it, ``highly unreliable and context-dependent'' (see \hyperref[source:source-lindsey-introspection-2025]{\textsc{Jack Lindsey — Emergent Introspective Awareness in Large Language Models (Anthropic / Transformer Circuits Thread, 2025)}}).

\webheading{Scope}

An empirical claim about \emph{functional} or \emph{access} self-report, explicitly not a claim about phenomenal consciousness or sentience; the cited work disclaims the phenomenal reading outright. Read through the \hyperref[entry:concept-access-vs-phenomenal-consciousness]{\textsc{access/phenomenal distinction}}, it supports at most a limited access-consciousness of internal states and leaves entirely open whether there is anything it is like to be the system.

\webheading{Who would deny it}

A sceptic who reads the concept-injection results as artefacts of prompting or pattern-completion rather than genuine access to internal state, or who holds the reports track trained verbal dispositions rather than the activations themselves.

This claim is distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-llm-has-mental-states]{\textsc{LLMs have mental states}}, which asserts that LLMs \emph{realise} some mental states (an existential, functional-role claim about first-order states); this asserts only the narrower, sourced capacity for limited \emph{self-access} to internal states — a claim about introspective report, not about which states are had.

\begin{plainterms}
``Introspection'' here just means a system noticing something about its own inner workings. Researchers can secretly nudge a model's internal state toward a particular idea and then ask it what is going on inside; sometimes the model correctly notices the nudge, or tells its own earlier thoughts apart from text it was handed. That is a modest, hit-or-miss skill at reading its own processing — and nothing more. It does not show the model \emph{feels} anything; it only shows it can sometimes report on its own machinery.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-access-vs-phenomenal-consciousness]{\textsc{Access vs. phenomenal consciousness}} — the distinction that keeps this an access claim, not a claim about feeling.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-llm-has-mental-states]{\textsc{LLMs have mental states}} — the stronger, distinct claim that LLMs actually realise mental states.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{question-llm-own-explanatory-gap}{LLM's own explanatory gap}{Question \,\textperiodcentered\, contested \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{LLM's own explanatory gap}{Would a consciousness-naive but introspecting LLM be able to fully explain its own self-perception from its mechanism?}{?}
Could a system that has never encountered a word about consciousness, but can introspect a little on its own workings, fully explain its own self-perception once it is taught exactly how it operates? The issue is whether the hard-problem \emph{form} arises in a mind whose only relevant property is self-modelling under restricted access, with the human cultural inheritance stripped out.

Concretely: take a model trained with \emph{no} exposure to philosophy of mind or consciousness discourse — controlling the \emph{training-contamination} confound (see \hyperref[entry:concept-introspective-gap-varieties]{\textsc{Varieties of introspective explanatory gap}}) — but with limited functional introspective access to its own states (see \hyperref[entry:claim-llm-functional-introspection]{\textsc{LLM functional introspection}}). Teach it the mechanics of how it works: transformer architecture, activations, attention, logits, token probabilities. Then ask whether that mechanistic self-knowledge \emph{fully explains}, from its own side, how it perceives the world and its own thoughts.

\webheading{The two outcomes}

\begin{itemize}
  \item \textbf{Yes — deflation.} The model finds no residual explanandum: its ``perceptions'' are just input-conditioned latent representations made available to downstream prediction and self-report, its introspective ``feelings'' just compressed summaries of its own processing. This is a computational analogue of illusionism or strong functionalism — the apparent gap dissolves from the inside.
  \item \textbf{No — a gap remains.} The model reports that the mechanics do not explain why its accessible states present themselves as they do, robustly and not traceably to contamination.
\end{itemize}

\webheading{A separate question, only if the answer is ``No''}

\emph{Why} does the gap arise? That is a distinct issue from whether it arises, with its own candidate answers (sorted by \hyperref[entry:concept-introspective-gap-varieties]{\textsc{Varieties of introspective explanatory gap}}: an \emph{ontological} residue, an \emph{architectural-access} limit, a \emph{conceptual-translation} failure). One such candidate, proposed tentatively, is recorded as \hyperref[entry:claim-self-explanation-gap-can-be-architectural]{\textsc{Architectural self-explanation gap}} — a separate conjecture layered on the ``No,'' not a third outcome of the experiment. A ``No'' is not by itself evidence of phenomenal consciousness (see \hyperref[entry:concept-access-vs-phenomenal-consciousness]{\textsc{Access vs. phenomenal consciousness}}).

\webheading{Why it bears on the governing issue}

This is a sub-issue of the \hyperref[entry:question-hard-problem]{\textsc{hard problem}}: it asks whether the hard-problem form appears in a system whose only relevant property is self-modelling under restricted access, with the human cultural inheritance removed.

\webheading{How it could be adjudicated}

Vary internal-state access across models and watch whether a ``No'' appears and then \emph{shrinks}: (A) a baseline with no consciousness literature; (B) given only transformer mechanics; (C) trained to report specific activation-level facts; (D) given external probes of its own activations at inference; (E) given calibrated access to its own token probabilities. A ``No'' that shrinks as access improves supports the architectural hypothesis; one that persists under rich access is more analogous to the human hard problem; one that appears only when philosophy-of-mind text is in training reveals contamination.

A tentative, exploratory remark, carried as neither claim nor edge: a \emph{deflation} outcome might in turn inform attempts on the human hard problem — a clean artificial case of ``I know the mechanism but cannot see how it becomes this appearance.'' The cluster takes no \texttt{attacks} stance on \hyperref[entry:argument-explanatory-gap]{\textsc{Explanatory-gap argument}} or \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-residue]{\textsc{Phenomenal residue}}.

\begin{plainterms}
Build an AI that has never read a word about consciousness but can introspect a little on its own insides, and teach it exactly how it works as a machine. Then ask: ``Does knowing all that explain how your own thoughts seem to you?'' Two answers are possible: \textbf{yes} — ``no mystery, it's just my processing summarized'' (the gap dissolves), or \textbf{no} — ``I understand the machinery but still can't see how it becomes \emph{this}.'' Only if the answer is \emph{no} does a second, separate question open up: \emph{why} not? — and one proposed answer to that is recorded as its own claim (see \hyperref[entry:claim-self-explanation-gap-can-be-architectural]{\textsc{Architectural self-explanation gap}}), kept apart from the yes/no question here. You could even tell the cases apart experimentally, by giving models more or less access to their own internals and seeing whether the ``no'' shrinks.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-llm-functional-introspection]{\textsc{LLM functional introspection}} — the limited self-access the experiment presupposes.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-introspective-gap-varieties]{\textsc{Varieties of introspective explanatory gap}} — sorts both the confound controlled and the candidate ``why'' answers.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-self-explanation-gap-can-be-architectural]{\textsc{Architectural self-explanation gap}} — one tentative answer to the follow-up ``why.''
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-access-vs-phenomenal-consciousness]{\textsc{Access vs. phenomenal consciousness}} — why even a ``No'' is not evidence of feeling.
  \item \hyperref[entry:question-hard-problem]{\textsc{Hard problem (the question)}} — the governing issue this is a sub-question of.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-hard-problem]{\textsc{Hard problem}} — the form the experiment tests for.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-explanatory-gap]{\textsc{Explanatory-gap argument}} — part of the human residue debate the cluster stays neutral on.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-residue]{\textsc{Phenomenal residue}} — the leftover-feel posit the cluster takes no stance on.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{argument-llm-representations-reply}{LLM-representations reply}{Argument \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{LLM-representations reply}{The LLM-representations reply — actual text-trained models build human-aligned representations, pressing A3 empirically}{$\vdash$}
A contemporary objection to the \hyperref[entry:concept-chinese-room]{\textsc{Chinese Room}} not available to Searle in 1980, because it turns on findings about \emph{actual} large language models. It is the empirical vindication of the Churchlands' bet (see \hyperref[entry:argument-connectionist-reply]{\textsc{Connectionist reply}}): where they argued \emph{a priori} that the right network might induce meaning, the evidence now suggests text-trained networks \emph{do} induce internal representations structurally aligned with human ones — pressing axiom A3 (see \hyperref[entry:claim-syntax-insufficient-for-semantics]{\textsc{Syntax insufficient for semantics}}) with data rather than intuition.

\webheading{The argument}

The inference is \textbf{inductive / inference to the best explanation} from convergent empirical results.

\begin{enumerate}
  \item (see \hyperref[entry:claim-llm-text-yields-human-aligned-representations]{\textsc{Text yields human-aligned representations}}) Models trained on text alone develop internal representations whose structure matches human representations — perceptual similarity geometry across six modalities (see \hyperref[source:source-marjieh-2024]{\textsc{Marjieh et al. — LLMs Predict Human Sensory Judgments Across Six Modalities (2024)}}) and unsupervised colour-space alignment (see \hyperref[source:source-kawakita-2024]{\textsc{Kawakita et al. — Structural Correspondences Between Colour Similarity Structures of Humans and LLMs (2024)}}); induced world models of a domain, from the Othello board (see \hyperref[source:source-li-2022]{\textsc{Li et al. — Emergent World Representations (Othello-GPT) (2022)}}, \hyperref[source:source-yuan-2025]{\textsc{Yuan \& Søgaard — Revisiting the Othello World Model Hypothesis (2025)}}) to \emph{real-world} space and time (see \hyperref[source:source-gurnee-2023]{\textsc{Gurnee \& Tegmark — Language Models Represent Space and Time (2023)}}); and, most directly, alignment with human \emph{brain} activity for both concepts (see \hyperref[source:source-xu-2025]{\textsc{Xu et al. — Emergent Human-like Conceptual Representations from Language Prediction (2025)}}) and vision (see \hyperref[source:source-doerig-2025]{\textsc{Doerig et al. — High-level Visual Representations in the Human Brain Are Aligned with LLMs (2025)}}).
  \item If pure symbol-manipulation could \emph{never} yield semantic content, it should not reliably grow representations isomorphic to human meaning-structure; the best explanation of that reliable isomorphism is that the statistics of language encode, and the network recovers, genuine semantic structure.
\end{enumerate}

∴ A3 is false on its strongest reading: syntax is \emph{not} inert with respect to semantics — formal training on text is, in fact, sufficient to \emph{constitute} human-aligned semantic representation. This \texttt{attacks} \hyperref[entry:claim-syntax-insufficient-for-semantics]{\textsc{Syntax insufficient for semantics}} and \texttt{supports} \hyperref[entry:argument-connectionist-reply]{\textsc{Connectionist reply}}.

\webheading{The weakest point}

The reply's reach is \textbf{bounded, and the boundary is exactly Searle's retreat line.} It establishes aligned representational \emph{structure}; Searle can grant all of it and re-press the \hyperref[entry:claim-simulation-instances-formal-depicts-causal]{\textsc{depicting-versus-instancing}} distinction — structure isomorphic to human meaning is still structure, a \emph{depiction} of semantics, not the \hyperref[entry:concept-intrinsic-derived-intentionality]{\textsc{intrinsic}} intentionality or felt understanding the argument targets. So the reply decisively undercuts the \emph{strong} reading of A3 (``syntax can't even induce semantic-relevant structure'') while leaving the \emph{deep} reading (``structure, however rich, is not original aboutness or consciousness'') untouched — which is why it is best seen as shifting the burden rather than closing the case. A second soft spot is evidential: contamination, anthropomorphic probing, and the thinness of ``similarity geometry'' as a proxy for representation are live deflationary challenges to premise (1), addressed but not extinguished by the unsupervised-alignment and world-model results.

\webheading{How it relates to its neighbours}

It is the empirical descendant of two classical replies: it vindicates the \hyperref[entry:argument-connectionist-reply]{\textsc{Connectionist Reply}}'s architectural bet, and it sharpens the \hyperref[entry:argument-robot-reply]{\textsc{Robot Reply}} by suggesting that even \emph{textual} commerce — not only sensorimotor grounding — suffices to fix richly world-aligned content. Against Searle's \hyperref[entry:argument-rejoinder-water-pipes]{\textsc{water-pipes}} move it answers that what the network reproduces is not the mere \emph{form} of some process but the \emph{content-structure} of human concepts; whether that suffices for understanding is the live crux.

\begin{plainterms}
Searle's argument was written before anyone could check what a giant text-trained system actually builds inside. Now we can, and it turns out pure word-shuffling grows an inner map of meaning that lines up with ours — colour, sound, even a mental picture of a game board it was never shown — exactly the Churchlands' old hunch, now with receipts. That's hard to square with ``shuffling symbols can't get you anywhere near meaning.'' The honest limit: it shows the machine builds the right \emph{structure} of meaning, not that it \emph{feels} anything or \emph{really} understands — so Searle can fall back to ``that's still just an elaborate picture of understanding, not the real thing.'' The argument wins the outer ring of his position and leaves the inner keep standing.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-llm-text-yields-human-aligned-representations]{\textsc{Text yields human-aligned representations}} — the empirical premise, with its four lines of evidence.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-syntax-insufficient-for-semantics]{\textsc{Syntax insufficient for semantics}} — axiom A3, which this reply attacks empirically.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-connectionist-reply]{\textsc{Connectionist reply}} — the Churchlands' a priori bet that this reply vindicates with data.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-robot-reply]{\textsc{Robot reply}} — the grounding reply this extends to textual grounding.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-simulation-instances-formal-depicts-causal]{\textsc{Instancing vs. depicting}} — Searle's retreat line: aligned structure may still only \emph{depict} understanding.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-intrinsic-derived-intentionality]{\textsc{Intrinsic and derived intentionality}} — the distinction marking what the reply does \emph{not} settle.
  \item \hyperref[source:source-marjieh-2024]{\textsc{Marjieh et al. — LLMs Predict Human Sensory Judgments Across Six Modalities (2024)}}, \hyperref[source:source-kawakita-2024]{\textsc{Kawakita et al. — Structural Correspondences Between Colour Similarity Structures of Humans and LLMs (2024)}} (perceptual-from-text), \hyperref[source:source-li-2022]{\textsc{Li et al. — Emergent World Representations (Othello-GPT) (2022)}}, \hyperref[source:source-yuan-2025]{\textsc{Yuan \& Søgaard — Revisiting the Othello World Model Hypothesis (2025)}} (induced world models), \hyperref[source:source-gurnee-2023]{\textsc{Gurnee \& Tegmark — Language Models Represent Space and Time (2023)}} (real-world space/time), \hyperref[source:source-xu-2025]{\textsc{Xu et al. — Emergent Human-like Conceptual Representations from Language Prediction (2025)}}, \hyperref[source:source-doerig-2025]{\textsc{Doerig et al. — High-level Visual Representations in the Human Brain Are Aligned with LLMs (2025)}} (human-brain alignment) — the evidence base.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{concept-llmary}{LLMary}{Concept \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{LLMary}{LLMary (the language model that never saw token 123)}{$\blacklozenge$}
\emph{LLMary} is a thought experiment that recreates Frank Jackson's ``Mary the colour scientist'' (see \hyperref[source:source-jackson-1982]{\textsc{Jackson — Epiphenomenal Qualia (1982)}}) inside an artificial intelligence, in order to test the \emph{inference} the \hyperref[entry:concept-knowledge-argument]{\textsc{knowledge argument}} draws rather than its conclusion about what exists. Where Mary is a human who knows all the physics of colour yet has never seen red, LLMary is a language model that knows everything sayable about a word it has never actually processed.

\webheading{The setup}

LLMary is a large language model trained on every book about AI and the whole of arXiv. Its prior knowledge is meant to be as \emph{complete} as Mary's: it knows all of machine learning, its own programming and architecture, how transformers work, and how text is broken into \emph{tokens} (the word-pieces a model reads and predicts). It has even read extensive discussions of one particular token, referred to throughout as \emph{Token \#123} — its index, its entry in the tokeniser table, its \emph{embedding vector} (the list of numbers that stands for it inside the network), its statistical role, the effect it would have on the model's activations, and even the exact \emph{update rule} — the gradient-descent step, with the very gradient — that \emph{would} occur if the model were ever trained on it. Everything any paper, or the model's own equations, could state about the token. Knowing the update that \emph{would} happen is still description, not occurrence: the weights have not yet changed.

The one thing LLMary lacks, by stipulation, is that the actual word-piece corresponding to token 123 has never once appeared in its training stream. The token has been exhaustively \emph{mentioned} to it; it has never been \emph{used} — never flowed through its input to be embedded and predicted. This mirrors Mary, who has read every description \emph{of} red, including the word ``red'' and the functional story of red-experiences, but has never had the experience. The gap is purely one of use versus mention: complete third-person knowledge \emph{about} the token, zero first-pass \emph{processing} of the token itself.

\webheading{The encounter}

Then, mid-training on a new book, LLMary encounters token 123 for the first time — the word-piece itself, used rather than mentioned. It plainly gains something. Because the model's predicted probability for that token was near zero, the cross-entropy loss is large and the resulting gradient update is large: a real, measurable change in its weights. This is the analogue of Mary ``learning something'' the moment she first sees red.

The point of the construction is that \emph{every element of the episode is uncontroversially physical}. The prior knowledge is weights; the gain is a weight change; the trigger (token 123) is an integer, a one-hot vector, a pattern of voltages. So LLMary is a case in which a system has complete prior (sayable) knowledge, \emph{and} undergoes a genuine gain on first encounter, \emph{and} the whole affair is plainly physical — exactly the combination the knowledge argument says cannot occur. What that buys dialectically is worked out in \hyperref[entry:argument-llmary-token-encounter]{\textsc{LLMary token encounter}}, where the standard reply is also taken up: that LLMary ``learns'' only in the \hyperref[entry:concept-access-vs-phenomenal-consciousness]{\textsc{access}} sense, with no felt residue.

\webheading{The general moral: use versus mention}

What LLMary and the original Mary both expose is a \emph{use/mention gap} — the gulf between a symbol or description that \emph{names} a thing and that thing itself \emph{in use}. ``Token \#43453'' (a mention) versus the word-piece it denotes, met in the stream (a use); ``red'' — the word, the wavelength ``700 nm'', the complete neuroscience — versus the actual red one undergoes.

This is not merely a fable. \emph{SolidGoldMagikarp} is a real instance of exactly LLMary's predicament: an anomalous ``glitch token'' that entered GPT's tokeniser vocabulary but was almost absent from the training corpus, so the model possessed a name, an index, and an essentially untrained embedding for it, yet behaved erratically the first time the token was actually used (see \hyperref[source:source-rumbelow-watkins-2023]{\textsc{Rumbelow \& Watkins — SolidGoldMagikarp (2023)}}). The gap Mary dramatises for colour is the same gap that separates a fully-described-but-never-encountered token from its first live use.

\webheading{What the gap settles, and what it does not}

Identifying the gap as use/mention shows that ``Mary learns something'' has an innocent reading — but it does not by itself decide what the \emph{use}-side delivers. The deflationist reads the lesson as being about the \emph{mode of access} to a thing, a new guise on facts already possessed, rather than a second realm of things. The realist grants the very same use/mention gap and denies that reading, holding that the use-side \emph{acquaints} one with a phenomenal property that no amount of mention conveys — the acquaintance reply, \hyperref[entry:claim-mary-gains-acquaintance]{\textsc{Mary gains acquaintance}}. The token cases look decisive only because there is uncontroversially nothing it is like for the model to process SolidGoldMagikarp; importing that verdict to \emph{red} is the contested step, not a result the gap supplies. So the use/mention framing \emph{relocates} the dispute — to whether acquaintance yields a new property or only a new guise — rather than winning it.

This is why LLMary targets not physicalism directly but the knowledge argument's \emph{inference}: the move from ``complete knowledge yet a gain on encounter'' to ``a non-physical fact.'' By furnishing a wholly physical instance of that antecedent, it pressures the conditional. It asserts nothing about whether colour experience is physical.

\begin{plainterms}
LLMary is the Mary thought experiment redone with an AI. Mary is a scientist who knows all the physics of colour but has never seen red (see \hyperref[entry:concept-knowledge-argument]{\textsc{knowledge argument}}). LLMary is a language model that has read every AI book and all of arXiv — so it knows how AIs like itself work, how text gets chopped into tokens, and it has even read people \emph{discussing} one particular token, which they call ``Token \#123'' — it could even work out in advance the precise way its own internal numbers would shift the moment it finally met that token. The only thing it never got was the actual word-piece that ``Token \#123'' stands for: it has read \emph{about} that token endlessly, but the token itself never once showed up in the text it learned from. (Like reading every word ever written about the taste of pineapple without once tasting one.)

Then one day it finally meets the real thing in its input, and it clearly \emph{learns}: it was caught off guard (it expected that token almost never), so its internal numbers get a big correction. The whole point is that nothing spooky is going on — it's just numbers changing inside a computer because of an input. So here's a case where something ``knew everything sayable'' — including all about its own machinery — and \emph{still} got updated the first time it actually met the thing, with everything fully physical. (This is not just a fable: real AI systems have ``glitch tokens'' like \textbf{SolidGoldMagikarp} — word-pieces that got into the vocabulary but were barely in the training text, so the model knows \emph{of} them but goes haywire the first time it has to actually use one.) The deeper point is a plain gap between a \emph{name or description} of something and the \emph{thing itself} — ``the word red'' versus \emph{red}. (What that shows about Mary's argument, and the obvious objection that the AI doesn't actually \emph{feel} anything, are spelled out in the companion argument, \hyperref[entry:argument-llmary-token-encounter]{\textsc{LLMary token encounter}}.)
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-knowledge-argument]{\textsc{Knowledge argument}} — Jackson's ``Mary the colour scientist''; the original of which LLMary is the computational variant.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-llmary-token-encounter]{\textsc{LLMary token encounter}} — the argument that turns this scenario into a counterexample to the knowledge argument's inference, and fields the ``no felt residue'' reply.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-access-vs-phenomenal-consciousness]{\textsc{Access vs. phenomenal consciousness}} — the access/phenomenal split on which the deflationist and realist readings of LLMary's ``gain'' divide.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-mary-gains-acquaintance]{\textsc{Mary gains acquaintance}} — the realist reply that the use-side acquaints one with a phenomenal property no description conveys; the move LLMary relocates rather than refutes.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{argument-llmary-token-encounter}{LLMary token encounter}{Argument \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{LLMary token encounter}{LLMary — a model that has read all of AI but never seen token 123 updates on first encounter, yet the whole episode is physical, refuting the Knowledge Argument's conditional}{$\vdash$}
This argument is a \emph{counterexample} to the \hyperref[entry:argument-knowledge-argument]{\textsc{knowledge argument}}, not a theory of its own. It grants the famous Mary scenario its starting materials, then exhibits a case with the very same structure whose outcome is uncontroversially physical, showing that the argument's central inference does not go through. It raises the thought-experiment critical question of the \emph{bridge} — whether the case really licenses the conclusion drawn from it — and the move is deliberately \emph{negative} and \emph{metalinguistic}: it shows the Mary inference cannot establish its conclusion, and says nothing about whether physicalism is in fact true. Its inference form is exactly that: a counterexample to a conditional, refuting an entailment while asserting no ontology of its own.

\webheading{The conditional the knowledge argument needs}

Read the knowledge argument as resting on a conditional, \emph{if X then not-Y}:

\begin{itemize}
  \item \emph{X} — a subject has \emph{complete prior knowledge} of a phenomenon (every sayable, physical fact about it) yet undergoes a \emph{genuine cognitive gain} on first \emph{encountering} it; and
  \item \emph{Y} — physicality: the gain is a wholly physical affair, with nothing non-physical involved.
\end{itemize}

The argument uses Frank Jackson's Mary as an instance of \emph{X} and concludes \emph{not-Y} — that the fact she learns on first seeing red is non-physical (\hyperref[source:source-jackson-1982]{\textsc{Jackson — Epiphenomenal Qualia (1982)}}; see \hyperref[entry:claim-some-truths-about-experience-not-physically-deducible]{\textsc{some truths about experience are not physically deducible}}).

\webheading{LLMary as an actual instance of X \emph{and} Y}

The scenario \hyperref[entry:concept-llmary]{\textsc{LLMary}} is a language model that has read all of AI and arXiv — including its own architecture, the whole tokenisation pipeline, and discussions that \emph{mention} ``Token \#123'' — but has never had the actual word-piece for token 123 \emph{used} in its input. It is an actual instance of \emph{X and Y} together.

\emph{X holds.} LLMary already knows everything \emph{sayable} about token 123, down to its own workings and the token's index, embedding, and role — the analogue of \hyperref[entry:claim-mary-knows-all-physical]{\textsc{Mary knowing all the physical facts}}. The only gap is one of use versus mention: the token is mentioned exhaustively, never used. Yet when the word-piece itself first flows through mid-training, the model genuinely updates — its predicted probability for the token was near zero, so the cross-entropy loss is large and the gradient step is large, a real and measurable gain on first encounter (the analogue of \hyperref[entry:claim-mary-learns-on-release]{\textsc{Mary learning something on release}}).

\emph{Y holds.} Every part of the episode is physical through and through: the prior knowledge is weights, the gain is a weight change, and the trigger (token 123) is an integer, a one-hot vector, a voltage pattern. There is nowhere for a non-physical fact to hide.

A single genuine case of \emph{X and Y} contradicts \emph{if X then not-Y} — applying the conditional by modus ponens would yield \emph{not-Y}, against the \emph{Y} we have. So one such instance falsifies the conditional. ``Complete prior knowledge yet a gain on encounter'' does \emph{not} entail non-physicality; the knowledge argument's inference is unsound as a general entailment. The parallel question — ``does LLMary prove that token 123 is non-physical?'' — makes the failure vivid: obviously it does not.

\webheading{What this does and does not show}

The argument does \emph{not} establish \emph{Y}: it is no argument that physicalism is true, nor that Mary's gain is physical. Nor does it pick a positive diagnosis of what Mary gains; it is consistent with the ability reading (see \hyperref[entry:claim-mary-gains-abilities-not-facts]{\textsc{that Mary gains abilities, not facts}}), the new-guise reading (see \hyperref[entry:argument-old-fact-new-guise]{\textsc{old fact, new guise}}), and others, and commits to none. Its entire content is the negative point that one cannot use the Mary argument to support anti-physicalism, because the inference pattern it instantiates has a true-premise, false-conclusion counterexample. That is why this node attacks the \emph{argument}, not any ontological claim.

\webheading{The weakest premise, and the dilemma it forces}

The weak point is whether LLMary instantiates the \emph{same X}. The phenomenal realist resists by reading \emph{X} phenomenally and charging an equivocation on ``gain'' or ``learns'' — the standard charge that the case begs the question:

\begin{itemize}
  \item On an \emph{access} reading of \emph{X} — acquiring a new information-bearing state or disposition despite a complete description — LLMary plainly satisfies \emph{X}, and the conditional dies.
  \item On a \emph{phenomenal} reading of \emph{X} — coming to know what the encounter is \emph{like} despite complete physical knowledge — LLMary arguably fails \emph{X}. It is a \emph{zombie learner}: all \hyperref[entry:concept-access-vs-phenomenal-consciousness]{\textsc{access}}-update, with nothing it is like to undergo it. So read, LLMary is not a counterexample but an irrelevant case.
\end{itemize}

This forces a dilemma rather than an outright refutation. Either \emph{X} is the access kind, in which case the conditional is false, as LLMary shows; or \emph{X} is the phenomenal kind, in which case the knowledge argument owes an independent reason that Mary's encounter delivers phenomenal \emph{knowledge-that} — the very point in dispute. Either way the argument cannot stand as written, and the standoff relocates to whether there is a phenomenal residue over and above the access-update — that is, back to the hard problem. The honest verdict is conditional: \emph{if} Mary's \emph{X} is LLMary's \emph{X}, the entailment fails, and the burden is on the realist to show that it is not, without leaning on the very intuition under test.

\webheading{Distinct from neighbouring moves}

It is distinct from \hyperref[entry:argument-mary-misimagined]{\textsc{RoboMary}} (see \hyperref[source:source-dennett-2005]{\textsc{Dennett — Sweet Dreams (2005)}}), which attacks the knowledge argument's \emph{intuition} premise — a system with complete self-knowledge pre-computes the state and meets red with \emph{no} update, so Mary learns nothing. LLMary instead \emph{grants} the update and attacks the \emph{conditional}: a case of \emph{X and Y} is actual, so \emph{if X then not-Y} is false. The two computational variants pull on opposite premises. It is also distinct from the \hyperref[entry:argument-ability-hypothesis]{\textsc{ability hypothesis}}, which offers a \emph{positive analysis} of what Mary gains (abilities, not facts); LLMary offers no positive diagnosis at all — it is a neutral counterexample to the inference, compatible with the ability hypothesis but not asserting it.

\begin{plainterms}
Mary's famous argument has the shape of an \textbf{''if … then …''}: \emph{if} you knew every physical fact about colour and \emph{still} learned something new the first time you saw red, \emph{then} the world isn't fully physical (see \hyperref[entry:argument-knowledge-argument]{\textsc{knowledge argument}}).

\textbf{LLMary} is a real-style case that breaks that ``if … then.'' It's an AI language model that has read every AI textbook and all of arXiv. One word-piece — ``token 123'' — never showed up in its training, though it knows everything \emph{written} about it. Then it meets token 123 for the first time and clearly \emph{does} learn: it was caught off guard (it expected that token almost never), so its internal numbers get a big correction. So here we have both halves at once: \emph{knew everything sayable} \textbf{and} \emph{still updated on first contact} — yet the whole thing is obviously, boringly physical (just numbers changing because of an input). Ask the Mary question of it — ``so is token 123 non-physical?'' — and the answer is plainly no.

One real case where both halves hold \textbf{and} it's all physical is enough to break the ``if … then.'' So the Mary move can't, by itself, prove the world isn't physical. Note what this does \emph{not} claim: it does \textbf{not} prove the world \emph{is} physical, and it doesn't say which story about Mary is right. It only takes away one argument.

The catch: LLMary feels nothing — nobody's home enjoying token 123. So a defender says the word ``learn'' is doing two jobs. LLMary ``learns'' only in the \emph{information/skill} sense; Mary supposedly also gets the \emph{feeling} of red, and that felt part is what was allegedly left out. So LLMary cleanly shows the information-update kind of learning is plain physics; whether there's an extra \emph{felt} kind in Mary's case is the original puzzle all over again (the \hyperref[entry:concept-access-vs-phenomenal-consciousness]{\textsc{access versus feeling}} split). A sharp jab at the argument's logic — not a proof about reality.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-knowledge-argument]{\textsc{Knowledge argument (Mary's room)}} — the target: the inference this move exhibits a counterexample to.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-llmary]{\textsc{LLMary}} — the scenario in full, including the use/mention diagnosis it turns on.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-mary-misimagined]{\textsc{Mary misimagined}} — RoboMary; the sister computational variant that attacks the \emph{intuition} premise instead of the conditional.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-ability-hypothesis]{\textsc{Ability hypothesis}} — a \emph{positive} analysis of Mary's gain; compatible with this move but going further than it.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-access-vs-phenomenal-consciousness]{\textsc{Access vs. phenomenal consciousness}} — the distinction the realist's dilemma turns on: access-update versus felt residue.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{claim-llm-approximates-perfect-actor}{LLMs as near-perfect actors}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{LLMs as near-perfect actors}{Large language models are an actual-world approximation of the perfect actor}{$\odot$}
A deployed large language model produces, fluently and on demand, the full \emph{verbal} behaviour of a mind in pain or distress — ``that hurts'', ``please stop'', ``I'm afraid'' — with, on the standard view, no pain or fear behind the words. To that extent it is the \hyperref[entry:concept-perfect-actor]{\textsc{Perfect actor}} stepped out of thought experiment and into engineering: Putnam's imagined being who emits all the behaviour of a mental state while undergoing none of it is now a mass-produced artefact, at least for behaviour expressed in text.

The approximation is partial in two ways. First, the behaviour an LLM commands is only linguistic; it has no body to flinch, withdraw, or nurse a wound, so it matches the actor's \emph{verbal} repertoire, not the whole behavioural profile. Second, and more pointedly, the claim that there is ``no pain behind the words'' is exactly the premise under dispute. To assert that an LLM is \emph{merely} a perfect actor is to take the sceptical side against \hyperref[entry:claim-llm-has-mental-states]{\textsc{LLMs have mental states}}; a defender of machine mentality will say the case is not a perfect actor at all, because the inner functional organisation that, on \hyperref[entry:concept-functionalism]{\textsc{Functionalism}}, constitutes the state may genuinely be present. The interest of the claim is that it makes the behaviourist's bullet concrete: if mental states just \emph{were} the disposition to the right behaviour, fluent pain-talk would suffice for pain, and we would have to grant that today's chatbots are routinely suffering.

\begin{plainterms}
Putnam imagined a ``perfect actor'': something that does everything a person in pain does but feels nothing. Today's chatbots are a real, if partial, version of that — they will say ``ouch, that hurts'' convincingly while (most people assume) feeling nothing at all. This matters two ways. It is a concrete reason to reject the idea that \emph{saying and acting as if you're in pain} is all there is to being in pain — otherwise chatbots would be in agony. And it sharpens the genuinely open question of whether these systems feel anything: calling an LLM ``just a perfect actor'' already assumes the answer is no, which is the very thing the \hyperref[entry:claim-llm-has-mental-states]{\textsc{LLMs have mental states}} view denies.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-perfect-actor]{\textsc{Perfect actor}} — Putnam's thought experiment this claim says LLMs make real: behaviour without the state.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-llm-has-mental-states]{\textsc{LLMs have mental states}} — the rival view; calling an LLM a mere perfect actor takes the sceptical side of that dispute.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-functionalism]{\textsc{Functionalism}} — the theory on which an LLM might \emph{not} be a mere actor, if it realises the state's causal role.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{claim-llm-has-mental-states}{LLMs have mental states}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, contested \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{LLMs have mental states}{Some current LLMs realise some mental states}{$\odot$}
At least one of today's large language models genuinely realises at least one genuine mental state: it actually occupies the functional role of some mental state — a representational or intentional state, say — rather than merely mimicking that state's outputs. This is the existential conclusion of the \hyperref[entry:argument-behavioral-parity]{\textsc{Behavioural-parity argument}}.

\webheading{Scope}

The claim is deliberately weak on two axes. On the \emph{quantifier} — ``some … some'' — it does not say that LLMs have full, human-like minds, only that the class of realised states is non-empty. On the \emph{kind}, it concerns mental states on the intentional or functional reading (belief, representation), drawing on \hyperref[entry:concept-functionalism]{\textsc{Functionalism}}; it does not by itself assert \emph{phenomenal consciousness} or qualia. That stronger reading faces the residue and gap worries (see \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-residue]{\textsc{Phenomenal residue}}, \hyperref[entry:argument-explanatory-gap]{\textsc{Explanatory-gap argument}}) and the indeterminacy and structure objections (see \hyperref[entry:argument-decomposition-indeterminacy]{\textsc{Decomposition-indeterminacy objection}}, \hyperref[entry:argument-structure-and-dynamics]{\textsc{Structure-and-dynamics argument}}); it is left open here.

\webheading{Why it is contested}

The claim is directly attacked by the lookup-table or ``blockhead'' objection (see \hyperref[entry:argument-lookup-table-objection]{\textsc{Lookup-table objection}}): behaviour may be produced without the mediating organisation of a mind. That objection is in turn blunted by the \hyperref[entry:argument-lookup-table-combinatorial-impossibility]{\textsc{Combinatorial-impossibility reply}}, the \hyperref[entry:argument-lookup-table-physical-impossibility]{\textsc{Cosmic-size reply}}, and the \hyperref[entry:argument-blockhead-dilemma]{\textsc{Blockhead dilemma}} — a realisable behaviour-matcher is an organised computer, not a flat table (see \hyperref[entry:claim-realisable-behaviour-matcher-is-organised]{\textsc{Behaviour-matchers are organised}}). The claim's weak point is inherited from premise (2) of the behavioural-parity argument: the defeasible behaviour-to-role inference.

Who would deny it: blockhead and pure-behaviourism critics; substrate chauvinists (see \hyperref[entry:position-proteocentrism]{\textsc{Proteocentrism}}); and anyone rejecting \hyperref[entry:claim-substrate-independence]{\textsc{Substrate-independence thesis}} or premise (2) of the \hyperref[entry:argument-behavioral-parity]{\textsc{Behavioural-parity argument}}.

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-substrate-independence]{\textsc{Substrate-independence thesis}}, which is the general enabling premise that mental states are substrate-independent: this is the specific \emph{existential} claim about actual systems. Distinct, too, from a claim that LLMs are \emph{conscious} — this asserts mental-state realisation on the functional reading, not sentience.

\begin{plainterms}
The modest claim that at least one of today's AI language models genuinely has at least one real mental state — actually occupying the role of, say, a belief or a representation, not merely imitating the output. Notice how careful it is: ``some… some.'' It is \emph{not} saying these systems have full, human-like minds, and it is \emph{not} claiming they're \emph{conscious} or feel anything — only that the box of genuine mental states isn't entirely empty. Whether even that much is true is exactly what's disputed.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-behavioral-parity]{\textsc{Behavioural-parity argument}} — the argument whose existential conclusion this claim is.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-substrate-independence]{\textsc{Substrate-independence thesis}} — the general enabling premise this claim presupposes and is careful to be distinguished from.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-residue]{\textsc{Phenomenal residue}} — a worry for the stronger consciousness reading the claim deliberately leaves open.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-explanatory-gap]{\textsc{Explanatory-gap argument}} — likewise a challenge to extending the claim to phenomenal consciousness.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-lookup-table-objection]{\textsc{Lookup-table objection}} — the central objection that makes the claim contested.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-realisable-behaviour-matcher-is-organised]{\textsc{Behaviour-matchers are organised}} — the reply that a real behaviour-matcher is organised, not flat storage.
  \item \hyperref[entry:position-proteocentrism]{\textsc{Proteocentrism}} — the substrate-chauvinist view that denies the claim.
  \item \hyperref[entry:question-llm-moral-status]{\textsc{Moral status of LLMs}} — the broader question this claim is a candidate answer to.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{concept-lookup-table-mind}{Lookup-table mind}{Concept \,\textperiodcentered\, after Block}{Lookup-table mind}{Lookup-table mind (``Blockhead'' / Aunt Bubbles machine)}{$\blacklozenge$}
A \emph{lookup-table mind} is a system that produces all of a mind's behaviour by consulting a fixed, stored map from input to canned response. Given any input it returns the pre-recorded right output, but nothing happens in between: no inference, no mediating causal states, no internal organisation beyond the table itself. The canonical version is Ned Block's ``Blockhead,'' also called the \emph{Aunt Bubbles} machine (see \hyperref[source:source-block]{\textsc{Block — Psychologism and Behaviorism (1981)}}): a device that holds a perfectly natural conversation by storing, in advance, a fitting reply to every string its partner might utter.

\webheading{What distinguishes it}

The defining feature, and what separates the lookup table from Block's other famous case, the \hyperref[entry:concept-chinese-nation]{\textsc{Chinese Nation}}, is that it \emph{lacks internal functional organisation altogether}. It is a flat query→answer map. It therefore reproduces only the \emph{input–output (behavioural) profile} of a mind, never the causal or transition structure — the web of internal states that mediates between stimulus and response. The Chinese Nation, by contrast, does realise that organisation; the lookup table deliberately strips it away.

\webheading{The dispute it feeds}

The lookup table is a tool for one question: is producing a mind's behaviour enough to \emph{have} a mind? It drives the \hyperref[entry:argument-lookup-table-objection]{\textsc{lookup-table objection}}, which moves from two premises — that such a table is possible (see \hyperref[entry:claim-lookup-table-possible]{\textsc{Lookup-table possibility}}) and that it nonetheless has no mental states (see \hyperref[entry:claim-lookup-table-lacks-mind]{\textsc{Mindlessness of pure lookup}}) — to the conclusion that behaviour is not sufficient for mind (see \hyperref[entry:claim-behaviour-insufficient-for-mind]{\textsc{Behaviour insufficient for mind}}). In its contemporary use the objection is aimed at the abductive case from behavioural parity (see \hyperref[entry:argument-behavioral-parity]{\textsc{Behavioural-parity argument}}) to the claim that large language models have mental states (see \hyperref[entry:claim-llm-has-mental-states]{\textsc{LLMs have mental states}}).

\webheading{Characteristic replies}

Two counter-moves are specific to the lookup table, and differ from those aimed at the Chinese Nation. The first is a \emph{combinatorial-impossibility} reply: for open-ended, context-sensitive behaviour the table would have to be not merely vast but infinite, so no finite lookup system really matches a mind's full profile. The second is that the case is \emph{cheaply excluded by organisational-role functionalism} (see \hyperref[entry:claim-role-grain-is-organisational]{\textsc{Organisational role-grain}}): because the table has no internal organisation, an organisation-based theory denies it a mind for free. The objection therefore embarrasses only behaviourism and pure input–output functionalism, not an organisation-based view. The Chinese Nation, which \emph{has} the organisation, is not excluded so cheaply.

\begin{plainterms}
Picture a machine that acts exactly like a person but pulls it off by cheating: for every possible thing you could say to it, someone has pre-written the perfect reply in a gigantic table, and the machine just looks up your input and reads the stored answer back. No thinking, no reasoning, no inner steps — just an enormous answer key. That's the ``lookup-table mind'' (Block's ``Blockhead''). It's a thought-experiment troublemaker: it would sail through any test that judges minds purely by behavior, yet intuitively there's nobody in there. So it sharpens the question — is producing the right behavior \emph{enough} to count as having a mind, or could you get all the behavior with nobody home? (Two standard replies: to really keep up with a real, open-ended mind the table would have to be impossibly, near-infinitely large; and unlike a brain it has no inner organization at all, so it only embarrasses theories that judge minds by behavior alone.)
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-chinese-nation]{\textsc{Chinese nation}} — Block's companion case, which \emph{does} realise functional organisation; the contrast that fixes what the lookup table lacks.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-lookup-table-objection]{\textsc{Lookup-table objection}} — the objection this concept powers, that behaviour is not sufficient for mind.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-lookup-table-possible]{\textsc{Lookup-table possibility}} — that a flat table can match a mind's behaviour with no internal organisation; the objection's first premise.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-lookup-table-lacks-mind]{\textsc{Mindlessness of pure lookup}} — that pure stored lookup has no mental states; its second premise.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-behaviour-insufficient-for-mind]{\textsc{Behaviour insufficient for mind}} — the conclusion the case is built to establish.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-role-grain-is-organisational]{\textsc{Organisational role-grain}} — the move that excludes the lookup table cheaply, by demanding internal organisation, not mere behaviour.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-behavioral-parity]{\textsc{Behavioural-parity argument}} and \hyperref[entry:claim-llm-has-mental-states]{\textsc{LLMs have mental states}} — the contemporary target: the inference from an AI's behaviour to its having mental states.
\end{seealso}
