This is the second axiom (A2) of Searle's formal \hyperref[entry:concept-chinese-room]{\textsc{Chinese Room}} derivation (see \hyperref[source:source-searle-1990]{\textsc{Searle — Is the Brain's Mind a Computer Program? (1990)}}), and it is meant as a datum: whatever else is true of minds, they are not merely syntactic engines — there is something the states are \emph{about}.

\textbf{Scope.} A2 asserts that minds \emph{have} semantics; it takes no stand on \emph{how} they come to (causal, teleological, or other theories of content) or on whether that semantics is \hyperref[entry:concept-intrinsic-derived-intentionality]{\textsc{intrinsic}}. It is the modest realist premise that meaning is a genuine feature of the mental.

\textbf{Who would deny it.} \emph{Eliminativists} about content and strong \emph{illusionists} about intentionality deny that minds have intrinsic, determinate semantic content of the kind A2 asserts — for them, ``aboutness'' is either eliminable or itself a derived, interpretation-relative matter. On that denial the Chinese Room's contrast between a meaningful mind and a meaningless program loses its first foothold, since human minds would have no intrinsic semantics either.

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-syntax-insufficient-for-semantics]{\textsc{Syntax insufficient for semantics}}: A2 says minds \emph{have} semantics; A3 says syntax cannot \emph{produce} it. Together with \hyperref[entry:claim-programs-are-syntactic]{\textsc{A1}} they drive the conclusion that programs are not sufficient for minds.

\begin{plainterms}
Your thoughts actually \emph{mean} things — your thought about water is genuinely about water, not just a meaningless symbol in your head. That's all this step claims: minds traffic in meaning, not just in shapes. It sounds undeniable, and mostly it is — but a few philosophers argue that ``meaning'' is never really \emph{in} anything intrinsically, in which case our minds would be no better off than the computer, and Searle's contrast would lose its grip.
\end{plainterms}

\entryhead{claim-theory-of-consciousness-must-meet-minimal-requirements}{Minimal requirements}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{Minimal requirements}{A theory of consciousness must meet the minimal requirements to count as one}{$\odot$}
A candidate theory of consciousness meets the \hyperref[entry:concept-minimal-theory-of-consciousness]{\textsc{minimal requirements}} only if it does two things: give an account of \hyperref[entry:concept-access-consciousness]{\textsc{Access consciousness}}, and solve the meta-problem (see \hyperref[entry:concept-paradox-of-phenomenal-judgment]{\textsc{Paradox of phenomenal judgment}}) — explaining, in terms its own metaphysics can support, why we are compelled to talk of a hard problem. A theory silent on either explanandum has not cleared the entry bar, however rich its further apparatus.

The requirements are a floor, not a full account of what makes a theory good: they do not say that a theory clearing the bar is \emph{true}, only that one missing either item is disqualified. They would be rejected by a strong phenomenal realist who holds the meta-problem optional — that explaining experience need not include explaining our talk and judgments about it — and by a theorist who treats access as beside the point (some \hyperref[entry:position-integrated-information-theory]{\textsc{IIT}} readings, where a theory of report is not what is wanted). Distinct from the requirements it invokes: \hyperref[entry:concept-minimal-theory-of-consciousness]{\textsc{Minimal requirements for a theory of consciousness}} \emph{defines} the two explananda, while this claim \emph{asserts} that meeting them is a necessary condition on any theory of consciousness, and so is the premise an argument wields when it charges a rival with leaving one of the two boxes empty.

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-minimal-theory-of-consciousness]{\textsc{Minimal requirements for a theory of consciousness}} — the requirements this claim makes binding: the two mandatory explananda, plus what they leave open.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-access-consciousness]{\textsc{Access consciousness}} — the first explanandum a theory must discharge.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-paradox-of-phenomenal-judgment]{\textsc{Paradox of phenomenal judgment}} — the tension a solution to the meta-problem, the second explanandum, must resolve.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{concept-minimal-theory-of-consciousness}{Minimal requirements for a theory of consciousness}{Concept \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{Minimal requirements for a theory of consciousness}{}{$\blacklozenge$}
The \textbf{minimal requirements} for a theory of consciousness set a floor: whatever else it does, a theory earns the right to be called a \emph{theory of consciousness} only if it discharges two explananda. They are deliberately thin. They do not tell a theory what to conclude, do not require it to posit or to deny felt experience, and do not favour physicalism over dualism; they only name the two things every candidate must address before its verdicts can be assessed.

\webheading{The two mandatory explananda}

\textbf{(a) Access consciousness.} The theory must give an account of \hyperref[entry:concept-access-consciousness]{\textsc{Access consciousness}} — of which information a system can bring to bear on reasoning, report, and the control of action, which information it cannot, and how the accessing mechanism works. This is the ``easy problem'' side of the ledger in Chalmers's sense: hard in practice, but a matter of function and mechanism, and no theory that stays silent about it has described a mind at all.

\textbf{(b) The meta-problem.} The theory must explain why people are so strongly moved to think there is a \hyperref[entry:concept-hard-problem]{\textsc{Hard problem}} — why we produce talk of an explanatory gap, of ineffable qualia, of an inner light that no physical story seems to capture. This requirement is \emph{symmetric}, and it binds every theory whatever its metaphysics:

\begin{itemize}
  \item A theory that posits \textbf{no} phenomenality (an \hyperref[entry:position-illusionism]{\textsc{illusionist}} or purely functional theory) must still explain how creatures with no such property come to be so certain they have it, and come to insist it cannot be explained.
  \item A theory that \textbf{does} posit phenomenality (a realist, dualist, or panpsychist theory) does not get the meta-problem for free. It must explain how that phenomenality \emph{causes} the judgments, reports, and gap-talk in the first place — how the posited experience gets into the causal chain that ends in someone saying ``but you still haven't explained the redness''. Simply having phenomenality on the books does not by itself explain the behaviour of talking about it.
\end{itemize}

This is the demand sharpened by the \hyperref[entry:concept-paradox-of-phenomenal-judgment]{\textsc{Paradox of phenomenal judgment}} and generalized as the \emph{meta-problem of consciousness} (see \hyperref[source:source-chalmers-2018]{\textsc{Chalmers — The Meta-Problem of Consciousness (2018)}}): our judgments about experience are physical events with physical causes, so any theory owes an account of those causes that squares with what it says experience is.

\webheading{How common positions answer}

The requirements are easiest to see at work as a scorecard. Below, each common position is scored on the two explananda: a dash (—) marks a box the position characteristically leaves empty, and where it does answer, the note sketches how. The reading is broad-brush and a determined proponent can always say more, but the pattern is instructive: nearly every view has an account of access, while the meta-problem is where they part company — and where several leave a blank.

\begin{itemize}
  \item \hyperref[entry:position-global-workspace]{\textsc{Global workspace theory}} — \emph{(a)} access \emph{is} the global broadcast of information to consumer systems; \emph{(b)} —, typically silent on why a residue seems left over.
  \item \hyperref[entry:position-higher-order-theories]{\textsc{Higher-order theories}} — \emph{(a)} a state is conscious when suitably represented by a higher-order state, hence reportable; \emph{(b)} partial, in that the same machinery can be turned to why we judge ``something more,'' though that is not its aim.
  \item \hyperref[entry:position-type-a-materialism]{\textsc{Type-A materialism}} — \emph{(a)} functional analysis; access is all there is to explain; \emph{(b)} diagnoses the gap-intuition as conceptual confusion.
  \item \hyperref[entry:position-illusionism]{\textsc{Illusionism}} — \emph{(a)} functional/representational account of access and report; \emph{(b)} its defining task, to explain why introspection misrepresents us as phenomenally conscious.
  \item \hyperref[entry:position-eliminative-materialism]{\textsc{Eliminative materialism}} — \emph{(a)} a functional story of information-uptake survives; \emph{(b)} treats phenomenal talk as a false folk theory to be explained and replaced.
  \item \hyperref[entry:position-type-b-materialism]{\textsc{Type-B materialism}} — \emph{(a)} functional; \emph{(b)} the phenomenal-concepts strategy, on which the \emph{appearance} of a gap comes from the structure of phenomenal concepts, not a real gap.
  \item \hyperref[entry:position-dualism]{\textsc{Naturalistic / property dualism}} — \emph{(a)} the ``easy problems,'' explained functionally; \emph{(b)} acknowledged (Chalmers named it) but unresolved, with the \hyperref[entry:concept-paradox-of-phenomenal-judgment]{\textsc{Paradox of phenomenal judgment}} biting hardest here.
  \item \hyperref[entry:position-epiphenomenalism]{\textsc{Epiphenomenalism}} — \emph{(a)} functional; \emph{(b)} —, since inert qualia by definition cannot cause the very talk about them.
  \item \hyperref[entry:position-russellian-monism]{\textsc{Russellian monism}} / \hyperref[entry:position-panpsychism]{\textsc{Panpsychism (position)}} — \emph{(a)} structure and function, as physics delivers them; \emph{(b)} —, with no account of how micro-phenomenality drives our gap-talk (the combination problem sits upstream).
  \item \hyperref[entry:position-integrated-information-theory]{\textsc{Integrated information theory}} — \emph{(a)} —, deliberately orthogonal to access (Φ is not a theory of report); \emph{(b)} —.
  \item Substance (interactionist) dualism — \emph{(a)} —, access via a non-physical mind, largely stipulated; \emph{(b)} —, the soul's self-knowledge posited, not explained.
  \item \hyperref[entry:position-mysterianism]{\textsc{Mysterianism}} — \emph{(a)} grants the access/easy problems are tractable; \emph{(b)} —, declining in principle (the answer is held beyond our cognitive reach).
\end{itemize}

\webheading{What the requirements leave open}

The requirements are neutral on the substantive questions. Theories meeting them \textbf{may or may not posit an actual phenomenology} (see \hyperref[entry:concept-phenomenal-consciousness]{\textsc{Phenomenal consciousness}}); they may disagree about what is accessible, and they may make different empirical predictions. A theory is also free to introduce \textbf{any additional entities it likes} — souls, a universal ego, irreducible p-consciousness, a quantum amplitude. When it does, it owes the usual account of why those posits are worth introducing and what they mean; but that is the ordinary burden borne by \emph{any} theory in \emph{any} domain, not a special tax levied on theories of consciousness. The requirements add nothing here beyond standard theoretical hygiene.

\webheading{Minimal, not complete}

The word \emph{minimal} is meant strictly: (a) and (b) are a floor, not a ceiling. A good theory of consciousness will usually be far richer — offering an account of the self, of attention, of introspection, of the unity and temporal flow of experience, of why some functions feel like anything and others do not. The claim is only that a theory failing to address \emph{either} (a) or (b) has not yet met the entry requirement, however elaborate its further apparatus.

\begin{plainterms}
If someone hands you a ``theory of consciousness'', there are two questions it has to answer before it counts as one at all. First: how does the mind get hold of information — what can you notice, report, and act on, what stays hidden, and how does that machinery work? Second, and easy to forget: why do we all feel there's a deep mystery here — why do people say experience is special, private, impossible to put into words? A theory that says there's no inner glow still has to explain why we're so sure there is one; and a theory that says there \emph{is} an inner glow still has to explain how that glow makes us talk about it. Beyond these two, a theory can say whatever it wants and add whatever ingredients it wants (a soul, exotic physics, anything) — as long as it explains why those ingredients are worth having, which is just what we ask of any theory anywhere. And this is only the \emph{minimum}: real theories can and should be much richer.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-access-consciousness]{\textsc{Access consciousness}} — the first mandatory explanandum: the accessible, the inaccessible, and the mechanism between them.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-hard-problem]{\textsc{Hard problem}} — the felt mystery whose \emph{appearance} the second explanandum must account for, whether or not the theory grants the mystery is real.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-paradox-of-phenomenal-judgment]{\textsc{Paradox of phenomenal judgment}} — the tension that makes the meta-problem bite: our talk about experience has physical causes.
  \item \hyperref[source:source-chalmers-2018]{\textsc{Chalmers — The Meta-Problem of Consciousness (2018)}} — the meta-problem of consciousness, the general form of requirement (b).
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-phenomenal-consciousness]{\textsc{Phenomenal consciousness}} — the posit the requirements leave optional; theories may assert or deny it, but either way must discharge (b).
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{claim-consciousness-freewill-mirror}{Mirror thesis}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{Mirror thesis}{The free-will debate is the consciousness debate mirror-imaged along the perception–action axis}{$\odot$}
The two problem-families are structurally the same debate run in opposite directions: positions, objections, and argument-shapes on the consciousness side map systematically onto counterparts on the free-will side under reversal of the perception–action axis, and the mirroring is regular enough to \emph{predict} transferable moves before they are found (see \hyperref[entry:concept-consciousness-freewill-duality]{\textsc{Consciousness–free-will duality}}).

This is the hedged master thesis of the duality framework — deliberately weaker than \hyperref[entry:claim-consciousness-freewill-strict-duality]{\textsc{Strict duality thesis}}, which asserts a categorical duality outright. The mirror thesis commits only to a systematic correspondence of dialectics: a further-fact camp and a deflationary camp on each side, mirrored objections (exclusion and the \hyperref[entry:argument-disappearing-agent]{\textsc{disappearing agent}}), mirrored intuition pumps (zombies and volitional zombies), mirrored unity problems, and at least one verbatim transfer of formal machinery. It would be falsified if the dictionary's entries proved cherry-picked — if systematic translation attempts mostly landed on nothing. Established abductively by \hyperref[entry:argument-consciousness-freewill-duality]{\textsc{Duality argument}}; who denies it: anyone for whom the correspondences reflect only the generic shape of realist-versus-deflationist disputes, common to any philosophical stalemate, rather than a mirror specific to the perception–action axis.

\entryhead{argument-no-universal-property}{Missing universal property}{Argument \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{Missing universal property}{The missing universal property — without one, the duality licenses no theorem transfer}{$\vdash$}
Categorical duality earns its transfer-power from \emph{universal properties}: a theorem about limits in $C$ becomes a theorem about colimits in $C^{op}$ because limit and colimit are defined by unique factorization. The \hyperref[entry:claim-consciousness-freewill-strict-duality]{\textsc{strict duality thesis}} borrows that authority without paying for it — no universal property has been located on either pole.

\textbf{Inference form:} deductive, from the transfer conditions of \hyperref[entry:concept-categorical-duality]{\textsc{categorical duality}} itself.

\begin{enumerate}
  \item Theorem transfer across a duality fires only once a construction is located \emph{with} its universal property: something must factor uniquely through conscious integration and co-uniquely through choice. Fan-in/fan-out is the \emph{shape} of a universal property, not one.
  \item No completed universal property for either pole is currently on the table. The partial payments fall short in instructive ways: the pullback reading of binding (see \hyperref[entry:claim-binding-is-obstruction-to-product]{\textsc{Binding as failed factorization}}) \emph{is} a universal property, but what is missing is the empirical claim that neural integration satisfies it; linear-logic polarity is theorem-bearing (focalization) but lives in linear, not cartesian, settings (see \hyperref[entry:argument-graded-polarity]{\textsc{Graded-polarity objection}}); active inference (Friston, \hyperref[source:source-friston-2010]{\textsc{2010}}) makes perception and action dual minimizations of one variational functional — the closest existing formalization of the whole thesis — but supplies an optimization symmetry, not a factorization.
  \item The structure transferred must moreover be \textbf{non-temporal}: if the morphisms are just causal flow, ``consciousness and free will are duals'' reduces to ``input and output run in opposite temporal directions,'' which is nearly definitional. Only argument-shape and universal property earn transfer, and only the former is in hand.
\end{enumerate}

∴ Arrow-reversal between the domains yields a heuristic dictionary requiring per-entry verification, not a transfer theorem (see \hyperref[entry:claim-duality-dictionary-not-theorem]{\textsc{Dictionary, not theorem}}) — and the strict duality thesis, read as licensing more, is unsupported.

\textbf{Weakest premise:} step 1's implicit exclusivity — that nothing short of a full universal property licenses any transfer. Weaker structure (an adjunction, a mere functor, the active-inference functional) might license \emph{partial} transfer, in which case the gap between dictionary and theorem is a spectrum rather than a wall; the strict thesis would still overclaim, but by less.

\begin{plainterms}
In mathematics, flipping the arrows gives you new truths for free only when the things flipped are pinned down by an exact specification — a ``universal property.'' The duality between consciousness (many streams in) and free will (one action out) has the right \emph{silhouette} but, so far, no such specification on either side. So the flip generates good leads, not guaranteed results: every translated claim must still be argued on its own merits. This is the main reason the framework's own \hyperref[entry:claim-consciousness-freewill-mirror]{\textsc{mirror thesis}} is stated as a claim about matching dialectics rather than a mathematical theorem.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-duality-dictionary-not-theorem]{\textsc{Dictionary, not theorem}} — the conclusion established.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-consciousness-freewill-strict-duality]{\textsc{Strict duality thesis}} — the overreaching reading attacked.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-binding-is-obstruction-to-product]{\textsc{Binding as failed factorization}} — the pullback reading, the nearest thing to a universal property on the input side.
  \item \hyperref[source:source-friston-2010]{\textsc{Friston — The Free-Energy Principle (2010)}} — active inference, the closest existing formalization of the thesis.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{argument-modal-rationalism}{Modal rationalism}{Argument \,\textperiodcentered\, after Chalmers}{Modal rationalism}{Modal rationalism — ideal conceivability is a guide to possibility}{$\vdash$}
Modal rationalism is the thesis that ideal rational reflection is a reliable guide to what is metaphysically possible: what an idealized reasoner can conceive without hidden contradiction marks out the space of genuine possibility. David Chalmers (\hyperref[source:source-chalmers-1996]{\textsc{Chalmers — The Conscious Mind (1996)}}; refined in \hyperref[source:source-chalmers-2002]{\textsc{Chalmers — On Sense and Intension (2002)}}) defends it not as a brute premise but by inference to the best explanation in modal epistemology — by disarming the only serious apparent counterexamples, the Kripkean a-posteriori necessities.

\begin{enumerate}
  \item (see \hyperref[entry:claim-aposteriori-necessities-are-misdescribed-possibilities]{\textsc{A-posteriori necessities as misdescribed possibilities}}) Every Kripkean a-posteriori necessity (see \hyperref[source:source-kripke-1980]{\textsc{Kripke — Naming and Necessity (1980)}}) is a \emph{genuinely possible} scenario \emph{misdescribed}: under a term's \emph{primary} (epistemic) \hyperref[entry:concept-primary-intension]{\textsc{Primary intension}} the conceived case is possible, while the necessity attaches only to its \emph{secondary} \hyperref[entry:concept-secondary-intension]{\textsc{Secondary intension}}.
  \item These a-posteriori cases are the \emph{only} serious candidate counterexamples to ``conceivability entails possibility.'' (The burden is to exhibit a defeater that resists this diagnosis.)
\end{enumerate}

∴ (see \hyperref[entry:claim-conceivability-entails-possibility]{\textsc{Conceivability entails possibility}}) Ideal \emph{primary} conceivability entails \emph{primary} possibility: once the two intensions are distinguished, the apparent counterexamples evaporate and ideal rational conceiving is a reliable guide to the space of possibility. This \texttt{responds\_to} \hyperref[entry:argument-conceivability-not-guide-to-possibility]{\textsc{A-posteriori-necessity objection}} by reinterpreting its premise rather than denying it.

\webheading{Bearing on the zombie}

The defence is decisive for \hyperref[entry:argument-zombie-conceivability]{\textsc{Zombie argument}} only if the phenomenal case has \emph{no} primary/secondary gap to misdescribe — that is, only if for phenomenal terms the primary and secondary intensions coincide, so that ``what it is like'' carries no appearance/reality distinction. That further claim is exactly where the type-B physicalist (see \hyperref[entry:concept-materialism-types]{\textsc{Type-A / Type-B / Type-C materialism}}) re-engages.

The \textbf{weakest premise} is really two. Premise (2) — that \emph{no} a-posteriori necessity resists the misdescription diagnosis (a ``strong'' necessity would be one that does). And the application to consciousness — that phenomenal terms lack the appearance/reality wedge that lets the water case off the hook. Deny either and conceivability stops being a safe guide for the zombie specifically, even if it remains safe in general.

\begin{plainterms}
This is the defence of the rule the zombie argument needs: ``coherently imaginable ⟹ genuinely possible.'' The worry was ``water is H₂O'' — imaginable-otherwise yet impossible. The reply: when you ``imagine water that isn't H₂O,'' you really are imagining a genuine possibility — some watery stuff that isn't H₂O — you are just wrongly \emph{calling} it water. Separate ``the stuff that plays the water role'' from ``H₂O,'' and the imagined world is perfectly possible; the necessity was hiding in the labels. So imagination stays a reliable guide to what's possible, once you are careful about descriptions. The catch that keeps the consciousness debate alive: this rescue works only if ``feels like red'' has no similar gap between how it seems and what it is — and that is exactly what is disputed.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-aposteriori-necessities-are-misdescribed-possibilities]{\textsc{A-posteriori necessities as misdescribed possibilities}} — the premise: the Kripke cases are possibilities under a misleading description.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-conceivability-entails-possibility]{\textsc{Conceivability entails possibility}} — the bridge this argument establishes.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-conceivability-not-guide-to-possibility]{\textsc{A-posteriori-necessity objection}} — the objection it answers.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-primary-intension]{\textsc{Primary intension}} / \hyperref[entry:concept-secondary-intension]{\textsc{Secondary intension}} — the two intensions whose distinction does the work.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-zombie-conceivability]{\textsc{Zombie argument}} — the argument this defence is ultimately meant to secure.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-materialism-types]{\textsc{Type-A / Type-B / Type-C materialism}} — type-B physicalism, where the defence is resisted.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{argument-moore-proof-external-world}{Moore's proof}{Argument \,\textperiodcentered\, after Moore}{Moore's proof}{Moore's proof of an external world — here is a hand}{$\vdash$}
G. E. Moore's \emph{proof of an external world} (see \hyperref[source:source-moore-1939]{\textsc{Moore — Proof of an External World (1939)}}) is the most famous — and most disputed — direct answer to external-world skepticism. Holding up a hand, Moore says ``here is one hand''; raising the other, ``and here is another''; and concludes that at least two external objects exist, hence that an external world exists (see \hyperref[entry:claim-external-world-exists]{\textsc{The external world exists}}).

\webheading{The argument}

The inference is deductive.

\begin{enumerate}
  \item Here is one hand, and here is another. \emph{(perceptual premises, which Moore claims to know)}
  \item Hands are external objects — things whose existence does not depend on being perceived.
\end{enumerate}

∴ At least two external objects exist; therefore an external world exists.

Moore held that this is a perfectly good proof: the premises are \emph{different from} the conclusion, the conclusion \emph{follows} from them, and — crucially — he \emph{knew} the premises to be true. His deeper point is a comparison of certainties: he was more certain that he had a hand than he could ever be of any premise in a skeptical argument to the contrary. This is the \textbf{Moorean shift} — one philosopher's \emph{modus ponens} is another's \emph{modus tollens}. Where the skeptic argues ``if I cannot rule out dreaming, I do not know I have a hand; I cannot rule out dreaming; so I do not know,'' Moore runs it backwards: ``I \emph{do} know I have a hand; so the skeptical principle that I cannot must be false.''

\webheading{The weakest premise}

Everything turns on the warrant for premise (1) in the \emph{dialectical} setting. Moore may well \emph{know} he has a hand, yet the proof is widely judged to \textbf{beg the question} against the skeptic, because warrant for ``here is a hand'' is not available to anyone who does not already grant that perception reveals external things — the very point in dispute. This is the \hyperref[entry:argument-moore-proof-begs-the-question]{\textsc{circularity objection}}; whether it convicts the proof of \emph{failing} depends on whether knowing requires being able to prove non-circularly (see \hyperref[entry:claim-knowledge-without-noncircular-proof]{\textsc{Knowing without proving}}). Wittgenstein's \hyperref[source:source-wittgenstein-1969]{\textsc{On Certainty}} was written in response to Moore, recasting ``here is a hand'' not as something \emph{known} on evidence but as a \hyperref[entry:concept-hinge-proposition]{\textsc{hinge}}.

\begin{plainterms}
Moore's answer to ``prove there's a world outside your mind'' was disarmingly blunt: he held up a hand and said ``here's one,'' held up the other, ``here's another — so external things exist.'' His real thought was that he was \emph{more} sure he had hands than he could ever be of the fancy premises a sceptic uses to argue he doesn't — so if anything has to give, it's the sceptic's premise, not the hand. Critics answer that this only convinces people who weren't really doubting; to someone who genuinely is, ``I can see my hand'' already assumes the thing in question.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-external-world-exists]{\textsc{The external world exists}} — the conclusion.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-moore-proof-begs-the-question]{\textsc{Moore's proof begs the question}} — the standard circularity objection that this proof, though valid, lacks force against the skeptic.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-external-world-unprovable-noncircular]{\textsc{No non-circular proof of the world}} — the general lesson the objection draws.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-knowledge-without-noncircular-proof]{\textsc{Knowing without proving}} — the externalist resource a defender of Moore can invoke: he \emph{knows} the premise even if he cannot non-circularly prove it.
  \item \hyperref[source:source-wittgenstein-1969]{\textsc{Wittgenstein — On Certainty (1969)}} — the response that recasts Moore's premises as hinges.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{argument-moore-proof-begs-the-question}{Moore's proof begs the question}{Argument}{Moore's proof begs the question}{The circularity objection — Moore's proof begs the question against the skeptic}{$\vdash$}
The \emph{circularity objection} grants that \hyperref[entry:argument-moore-proof-external-world]{\textsc{Moore's proof}} is valid and that its premises may even be \emph{true} and \emph{known}, yet denies that it does what a proof against the skeptic must: it cannot rationally move someone who doubts the external world, because the warrant for its key premise presupposes the conclusion. The objection is therefore an \emph{undercutting} defeater aimed at the proof's \hyperref[entry:concept-dialectical-cogency]{\textsc{cogency}}, not a \emph{rebutting} one aimed at its truth.

\webheading{The argument}

\begin{enumerate}
  \item To be entitled to assert ``here is a hand,'' one must be entitled to take one's visual experience to reveal a mind-independent object.
  \item But that entitlement is exactly what the external-world skeptic withholds; for the skeptic, the experience is equally compatible with there being no hand (dream, demon, vat).
  \item So warrant for the premise is available only to someone who already grants that perception puts us in touch with external things — i.e. only to someone who already accepts the conclusion.
\end{enumerate}

∴ Moore's proof begs the question in the \emph{dialectical} sense: its premise cannot be reached without the conclusion, so the proof has no power to convert the doubter. This \texttt{attacks} \hyperref[entry:argument-moore-proof-external-world]{\textsc{Moore's proof}} and \texttt{supports} the general lesson that \hyperref[entry:claim-external-world-unprovable-noncircular]{\textsc{the external world cannot be proved without circularity}}. Wittgenstein's \hyperref[source:source-wittgenstein-1969]{\textsc{On Certainty}}, written against Moore, presses the related point that ``here is a hand'' functions as a \hyperref[entry:concept-hinge-proposition]{\textsc{hinge}}, not as evidence.

\webheading{An apocryphal illustration}

A teaching anecdote makes the point vivid. A student experimenting with the deliriant \emph{Artane} (trihexyphenidyl) — a drug whose hallucinations are notoriously of fully-formed, ordinary people behaving normally — reports seeing his philosophy professor standing in the kitchen, holding up each hand in turn: ``Here is one, here is the other.'' The hallucination performs \hyperref[entry:argument-moore-proof-external-world]{\textsc{Moore's proof}} with complete perceptual conviction and certifies nothing: there is no hand, no professor, no external thing in the scene at all. The case literalizes premises (2)–(3): the very experience meant to warrant ``here is a hand'' is one a deliriant can supply in the total absence of hands, so the warrant cannot lie in the having of the experience. (The student, the story goes, swore off drugs — the phantom proof of the external world having taught him to distrust exactly the perceptual conviction the proof leans on, a \hyperref[entry:argument-moore-proof-external-world]{\textsc{Moorean shift}} run in reverse.)

\webheading{The limit of the objection}

The objection establishes a failure of \emph{cogency}, not of \emph{knowledge}. If knowing does not require non-circular provability (see \hyperref[entry:claim-knowledge-without-noncircular-proof]{\textsc{Knowing without proving}}), then Moore may genuinely know he has a hand — his belief reliably produced by the hand — even though he cannot prove it to the skeptic. On that reading the circularity objection is \emph{correct but limited}: it shows the proof is dialectically inert, not that Moore lacks the knowledge he claims. This is the same truth/probative-force split that runs through \hyperref[entry:concept-dialectical-cogency]{\textsc{Dialectical cogency}}: an argument can be sound yet unable to move its target.

\begin{plainterms}
Moore holds up a hand to prove the world is real. The objection: that only works if you're already allowed to treat ``I see a hand'' as seeing a \emph{real} hand — which is the very thing the sceptic is questioning. So the proof quietly assumes its own conclusion and can't budge anyone who actually doubts. Note the careful limit, though: this shows Moore can't \emph{win the argument} against a determined sceptic — not that he doesn't \emph{know} he has hands. Knowing something and being able to prove it to a doubter are two different jobs.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-moore-proof-external-world]{\textsc{Moore's proof}} — the proof this objection undercuts.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-external-world-unprovable-noncircular]{\textsc{No non-circular proof of the world}} — the conclusion it supports.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-dialectical-cogency]{\textsc{Dialectical cogency}} — the level at which it bites: probative force, not truth.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-knowledge-without-noncircular-proof]{\textsc{Knowing without proving}} — the resource that bounds the objection: knowing needn't be proving.
  \item \hyperref[source:source-wittgenstein-1969]{\textsc{Wittgenstein — On Certainty (1969)}} — the allied response recasting Moore's premise as a hinge.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{question-llm-moral-status}{Moral status of LLMs}{Question \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{Moral status of LLMs}{Do large language models have moral status?}{?}
\emph{Moral status} is the property of mattering morally in one's own right: to have moral status is to be such that one's interests, if one has any, generate reasons for how others may treat one. A rock has none; an adult human has it in full; non-human animals are the classic disputed middle. This entry asks where today's \emph{large language models} — the AI systems behind chatbots and writing assistants — fall on that scale.

\webheading{The issue}

The question is not whether a language model is clever, useful, or convincingly human in conversation. It is whether there is \emph{anyone there}: a subject whose well-being places the rest of us under any obligation at all. A system can pass for a person in dialogue and still be, for moral purposes, an empty machine — or it might not be. Settling which requires saying what moral status demands and whether such a system could meet the demand.

\webheading{Sub-issues}

Three narrower questions feed this one and may earn their own entries: whether moral status requires \emph{phenomenal consciousness} (felt experience, a ``what it is like'' to be the system); whether it instead requires \emph{interests} (a genuine stake in how things go for one); and whether outward \emph{behaviour} is good evidence of either. Candidate answers are not listed here by hand; any node carrying an \texttt{answers: question-llm-moral-status} edge is collected automatically into the computed list in \texttt{web/INDEX.md}.

\begin{plainterms}
The big question this whole project circles. ``Moral status'' means: does a thing count morally in its own right — do its interests, if it has any, give the rest of us reasons to treat it one way rather than another? A rock has none; a person has full status; animals are argued over. The question here is where today's AI language models fall. Not ``are they clever'' or ``do they seem human,'' but: is there anyone in there whose well-being we're obliged to take into account?
\end{plainterms}

\entryhead{position-mysterianism}{Mysterianism}{Position \,\textperiodcentered\, after Mcginn}{Mysterianism}{Mysterianism (transcendental naturalism)}{$\bigstar$}
\emph{Mysterianism} holds that consciousness has a perfectly natural, physical explanation — there is some property of the brain in virtue of which experience arises — but that the human mind is \emph{constitutively} unable to grasp what that property is. The \hyperref[entry:concept-hard-problem]{\textsc{hard problem}} of consciousness is therefore real and has a real answer; we are simply built so that we can never reach it. The position is sometimes called \emph{transcendental naturalism}, the term coined by Colin McGinn in his 1989 paper ``Can We Solve the Mind–Body Problem?'' (see \hyperref[source:source-mcginn-1989]{\textsc{McGinn — Can We Solve the Mind–Body Problem? (1989)}}): realism about the answer, pessimism about our access to it.

The view pairs two commitments. The first is naturalist optimism about the world: the link between brain and experience is an ordinary physical fact, so consciousness \hyperref[entry:claim-consciousness-has-natural-explanation]{\textsc{has a natural explanation}}. The second is epistemic pessimism about us: \hyperref[entry:claim-humans-closed-to-psychophysical-link]{\textsc{humans are cognitively closed to that explanatory property}}, much as a dog is closed to arithmetic. Put together, they answer the question of how the brain produces consciousness (see \hyperref[entry:question-hard-problem]{\textsc{the hard problem}}) with the formula ``soluble in itself, insoluble by us.'' The reasons offered for this conclusion are set out in the \hyperref[entry:argument-cognitive-closure]{\textsc{cognitive-closure argument}}.

\webheading{What sets it apart}

Mysterianism occupies a narrow strip of logical space between the usual camps. Unlike \emph{dualism}, it is thoroughly naturalist: the psychophysical link is one more physical fact, not the work of a second, non-physical substance. Unlike \emph{reductive functionalism} and \emph{illusionism}, it grants that the hard problem is genuine and unsolved rather than dissolving it or declaring it a confusion. And unlike the anti-physicalist reading of the explanatory gap — the idea that \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-residue]{\textsc{felt character outruns any functional role}}, pressed in the \hyperref[entry:argument-explanatory-gap]{\textsc{explanatory-gap argument}} — it locates the gap in \emph{our cognitive limits} rather than in \emph{the world}. The felt shortfall, on this telling, is a fact about the reach of human concepts, not evidence of an ontological divide.

\begin{plainterms}
Mysterianism says: consciousness is a normal, physical part of nature — there's no magic — but our brains just aren't equipped to ever understand \emph{how} the trick is done, the way a dog can't grasp arithmetic no matter how long you teach it. So the mystery is permanent, yet there's nothing spooky behind it. It's a middle path between ``we'll crack it eventually'' and ``consciousness is non-physical.''
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-hard-problem]{\textsc{Hard problem}} — the problem mysterianism says is real but permanently beyond us.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-cognitive-closure]{\textsc{Cognitive closure}} — the argument that supplies the reasons for the closure claim.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-humans-closed-to-psychophysical-link]{\textsc{Closure to the psychophysical link}} — the pessimistic half: we cannot grasp the property.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-consciousness-has-natural-explanation]{\textsc{Natural explanation of consciousness}} — the optimistic half: there is a natural property to grasp.
  \item \hyperref[entry:question-hard-problem]{\textsc{Hard problem (the question)}} — the issue it answers, with ``soluble in itself, insoluble by us.''
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-explanatory-gap]{\textsc{Explanatory-gap argument}} — the rival reading that puts the gap in the world, not in us.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-residue]{\textsc{Phenomenal residue}} — the anti-physicalist diagnosis mysterianism declines, locating the gap in us rather than the world.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{concept-munchhausen-trilemma}{Münchhausen trilemma}{Concept}{Münchhausen trilemma}{The Münchhausen trilemma (Agrippa's regress)}{$\blacklozenge$}
The \emph{Münchhausen trilemma} is the claim that any attempt to fully \emph{justify} a belief — to give a reason, then a reason for the reason, and so on — must end in one of three unsatisfying ways:

\begin{enumerate}
  \item an \textbf{infinite regress} of reasons, each needing a further reason, never completed;
  \item a \textbf{circle}, in which some reason eventually rests on the belief it was meant to support;
  \item a \textbf{dogmatic break-off}, stopping at some reason simply asserted without further justification.
\end{enumerate}

None of the three looks like the secure grounding the demand for justification wanted. The name is Hans Albert's (see \hyperref[source:source-albert-1985]{\textsc{Albert — Treatise on Critical Reason (1985)}}) — after the Baron who pulled himself out of a swamp by his own hair — for what is essentially the ancient regress already catalogued among the \emph{modes of Agrippa} in Greek skepticism. Albert's own moral is radical: the demand for full justification (\emph{justificationism}) should be abandoned, not satisfied.

The trilemma is the skeleton of the whole problem of \hyperref[entry:question-external-world-knowledge]{\textsc{the external world}} and of justification generally, and the standard positions are best read as each \emph{choosing a horn}:

\begin{itemize}
  \item \textbf{\hyperref[entry:position-foundationalism]{\textsc{Foundationalism}}} accepts the third horn but reframes it: the stopping points are not arbitrary dogmas but \hyperref[entry:concept-basic-belief]{\textsc{basic beliefs}} that are justified non-inferentially.
  \item \textbf{\hyperref[entry:position-coherentism]{\textsc{Coherentism}}} embraces the second horn, denying it is vicious: justification is the \emph{mutual support} of a whole body of beliefs, a virtuous circle rather than a linear chain.
  \item \textbf{\hyperref[entry:position-infinitism]{\textsc{Infinitism}}} accepts the first horn, holding that justification really is an infinite, non-repeating chain of reasons.
  \item \textbf{\hyperref[entry:position-fallibilism]{\textsc{Fallibilism}}} rejects the question: since no belief is ever conclusively justified, we should stop seeking foundations and instead expose conjectures to criticism (see \hyperref[entry:claim-all-knowledge-is-conjectural]{\textsc{Knowledge is conjectural}}).
  \item A Wittgensteinian adds that the chain stops at \hyperref[entry:concept-hinge-proposition]{\textsc{hinges}} that are not justified at all because they are what justifying presupposes.
\end{itemize}

\begin{plainterms}
Ask ``how do you know?'' and whatever answer comes back, you can ask it again of that answer. Keep going and only three things can happen: the questions never stop (infinite regress), the answers eventually loop back on themselves (circle), or someone finally says ``that's just where I stop'' (a brute assertion). Every theory of knowledge is, at bottom, a choice about which of these three to live with — or, like the fallibilist, a decision that the whole ``justify it all the way down'' demand was a mistake to begin with.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-justification-terminates-in-trilemma]{\textsc{Justification's trilemma}} — the claim that states the trilemma as a thesis.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-munchhausen-trilemma]{\textsc{Münchhausen trilemma argument}} — the regress argument that establishes it.
  \item \hyperref[entry:position-foundationalism]{\textsc{Foundationalism}}, \hyperref[entry:position-coherentism]{\textsc{Coherentism}}, \hyperref[entry:position-infinitism]{\textsc{Infinitism}} — the three responses, one per horn.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-hinge-proposition]{\textsc{Hinge proposition}} — the Wittgensteinian alternative to a dogmatic stop.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-external-world-unprovable-noncircular]{\textsc{No non-circular proof of the world}} — the trilemma applied to one famous belief.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{argument-munchhausen-trilemma}{Münchhausen trilemma argument}{Argument}{Münchhausen trilemma argument}{The regress argument — complete justification must regress, circle, or break off}{$\vdash$}
The \emph{regress argument} establishes the \hyperref[entry:concept-munchhausen-trilemma]{\textsc{Münchhausen trilemma}}: that no belief can be justified all the way down. It is the engine behind the whole \hyperref[entry:question-external-world-knowledge]{\textsc{problem of the external world}} and, more generally, behind the architecture of every theory of justification.

\webheading{The argument}

The inference is deductive — an exhaustive division of cases.

\begin{enumerate}
  \item To justify a belief is to support it with a reason; and that reason, to do its work, must itself be something one is justified in holding.
  \item So the demand to justify a belief, pressed without exception, generates a chain: belief ← reason ← reason ← … .
  \item Such a chain can have only one of three fates. It continues without end (\textbf{infinite regress}); or some reason in it is supported, directly or eventually, by the belief it was meant to support (\textbf{circularity}); or it terminates at a reason offered without further support (\textbf{dogmatic break-off}).
\end{enumerate}

∴ Every attempt at complete justification ends in regress, circularity, or a dogmatic stopping point: \hyperref[entry:claim-justification-terminates-in-trilemma]{\textsc{Justification's trilemma}}. Since none of the three delivers the unconditional grounding the demand sought, the result forces every epistemology to \emph{choose a horn} — or to reject the demand itself.

\webheading{The weakest premise}

Premise (1) is the load-bearing assumption: that justification must take the form of \emph{believer-possessed reasons} arranged in a chain. The major escapes all attack it rather than the trichotomy, which is itself sound. \textbf{Externalism} denies (1): a belief can be warranted by a reliable process, not by the agent's further reasons, so there is no chain to regress (see \hyperref[entry:claim-knowledge-without-noncircular-proof]{\textsc{Knowing without proving}}). \textbf{Coherentism} rejects the picture of a \emph{linear} chain, replacing it with a web of mutual support (see \hyperref[entry:claim-justification-is-mutual-support]{\textsc{Justification as coherence}}). The \textbf{Wittgensteinian} holds the chain stops at \hyperref[entry:concept-hinge-proposition]{\textsc{hinges}} that are neither reasoned nor arbitrary, but presupposed by reasoning. And the \textbf{fallibilist} grants the trilemma in full and concludes that the demand for justification was the error, not its unsatisfiability (see \hyperref[entry:claim-all-knowledge-is-conjectural]{\textsc{Knowledge is conjectural}}). The trilemma's force is thus less to refute any view than to make explicit the price each must pay.

\begin{plainterms}
Push ``how do you know?'' relentlessly and a chain of reasons forms, and that chain can only do one of three things: go on forever, loop back on itself, or stop at something you just assert. None of those is the rock-solid bottom the question was hoping for. So the argument doesn't prove we know nothing — it proves that ``justify it all the way down'' can't be done, and quietly hands every theory of knowledge the same bill: pick which of the three you can live with, or admit the demand itself was a wild goose chase.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-justification-terminates-in-trilemma]{\textsc{Justification's trilemma}} — the conclusion established.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-munchhausen-trilemma]{\textsc{Münchhausen trilemma}} — the concept it anatomizes, with the map of which position takes which horn.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-knowledge-without-noncircular-proof]{\textsc{Knowing without proving}} — the externalist denial of premise (1).
  \item \hyperref[entry:position-foundationalism]{\textsc{Foundationalism}}, \hyperref[entry:position-coherentism]{\textsc{Coherentism}}, \hyperref[entry:position-infinitism]{\textsc{Infinitism}}, \hyperref[entry:position-fallibilism]{\textsc{Fallibilism}} — the responses the argument forces a choice among.
\end{seealso}

\end{multicols}
\bookgroup{N}
\begin{multicols}{2}
\entryhead{claim-consciousness-has-natural-explanation}{Natural explanation of consciousness}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, after Mcginn}{Natural explanation of consciousness}{There is a natural property of the brain that explains consciousness}{$\odot$}
There is some natural property \emph{P} of the brain in virtue of which the brain is the basis of consciousness. The link between neural process and felt experience is, on this claim, a \emph{natural} fact — not a miracle, not a non-physical addition, and not a brute, inexplicable surd. It is the naturalist commitment at the heart of McGinn's \emph{transcendental naturalism}: the view that consciousness has a perfectly natural explanation, even one we may be constitutionally unable to grasp (see \hyperref[source:source-mcginn-1989]{\textsc{McGinn — Can We Solve the Mind–Body Problem? (1989)}}).

The claim is realist and naturalist about the \emph{existence} of an explanation, not about its accessibility. It explicitly does not say the explanation is dualistic, nor that we can comprehend it — the latter is denied by \hyperref[entry:claim-humans-closed-to-psychophysical-link]{\textsc{the cognitive-closure claim}}. It is the premise that keeps mysterianism on the \emph{naturalist} side of the ledger rather than letting it collapse into dualism.

It is denied by the property dualist or panpsychist, who holds that no ordinary natural property could close the explanatory gap, and by the eliminativist or illusionist, who denies there is any phenomenal explanandum to be explained in the first place.

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-substrate-independence]{\textsc{the substrate-independence thesis}}: that concerns which \emph{substrates} can bear a mind, whereas this asserts only that \emph{some} natural brain property grounds consciousness, taking no stand on whether silicon could share it.

\begin{plainterms}
This says that whatever it is about the brain that produces conscious experience, it's an ordinary part of nature — some physical feature of the brain, not magic and not a soul added from outside. It deliberately stops short of two further claims: it doesn't say we humans could ever understand that feature (a separate, gloomier thesis, \hyperref[entry:claim-humans-closed-to-psychophysical-link]{\textsc{that we're cognitively closed to it}}), and it doesn't say the explanation is non-physical. It's the ``still natural'' half of the mysterian position: the mystery is in our reach, not in nature.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-humans-closed-to-psychophysical-link]{\textsc{Closure to the psychophysical link}} — the cognitive-closure half of mysterianism; this claim supplies the ``still natural'' half.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-substrate-independence]{\textsc{Substrate-independence thesis}} — a separate thesis about substrates, with which this is contrasted.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{claim-reality-fundamental-stuff-neutral}{Neutral fundamental stuff}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, after Russell}{Neutral fundamental stuff}{Reality's fundamental constituents are neither mental nor physical}{$\odot$}
The basic stuff of the world is \emph{neutral} — in itself neither mental nor physical — and the mental and the physical are two different \emph{arrangements} or groupings of these same neutral elements. Mind and matter are derivative patterns, not fundamental kinds. The idea traces to Mach's neutral \emph{elements} (see \hyperref[source:source-mach-1914]{\textsc{Mach — The Analysis of Sensations (1914)}}), James's \emph{pure experience} (see \hyperref[source:source-james-1904]{\textsc{James — Does 'Consciousness' Exist? (1904)}}), and Russell's neutral monism (see \hyperref[source:source-russell-1921]{\textsc{Russell — The Analysis of Mind (1921)}}).

The claim is a monism about the fundamental base together with a deflation of the mental/physical divide to a difference in arrangement. In its classic form it does \emph{not} say the neutral elements are proto-experiential; that extra step is what yields Russellian monism. It is denied by dualists (two fundamental kinds), physicalists (the base is physical), idealists (the base is mental), and Russellian monists, who hold the intrinsic natures are (proto)phenomenal rather than genuinely neutral.

Distinct from the base of \hyperref[entry:position-russellian-monism]{\textsc{Russellian monism}} (see \hyperref[entry:claim-consciousness-categorical-not-dispositional]{\textsc{its categorical base}}): neutral monism keeps the base \emph{neutral}, whereas Russellian monism makes that intrinsic base the very thing consciousness is. Neutral monism is the historical parent of the Russellian view.

\begin{plainterms}
Mind and matter aren't two ultimate kinds of thing, and neither is built from the other. Both are patterns in a single, more basic stuff that is in itself neither mental nor physical — like the same dots arranged once into a ``mental'' picture and once into a ``physical'' one.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:position-neutral-monism]{\textsc{Neutral monism}} — the position committed to this claim.
  \item \hyperref[entry:position-russellian-monism]{\textsc{Russellian monism}} — the descendant view; the base is (proto)phenomenal, not neutral.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-consciousness-categorical-not-dispositional]{\textsc{Categorical, not dispositional}} — the Russellian base; the contrast case.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{position-neutral-monism}{Neutral monism}{Position \,\textperiodcentered\, after Russell}{Neutral monism}{}{$\bigstar$}
\emph{Neutral monism} holds that the fundamental stuff of the world is \emph{neutral} — in itself neither mental nor physical — and that mind and matter are two different \emph{arrangements} of these same neutral elements. There is one basic kind of thing, but it belongs to neither of the two categories we ordinarily reach for; mind and matter are patterns in it, not fundamental kinds. The position commits to the claim that \hyperref[entry:claim-reality-fundamental-stuff-neutral]{\textsc{reality's fundamental constituents are neutral}}.

\webheading{Historical sources}

The view runs through late-nineteenth and early-twentieth-century philosophy and psychology. Ernst Mach analysed the world into neutral \emph{elements} — colours, sounds, pressures — that count as physical when considered in their mutual dependencies and as psychological when considered in their dependence on a living body (see \hyperref[source:source-mach-1914]{\textsc{Mach — The Analysis of Sensations (1914)}}). William James's doctrine of \emph{pure experience}, the proposal that there is one primal stuff of which both knower and known are made, gave the position its most famous slogan (see \hyperref[source:source-james-1904]{\textsc{James — Does 'Consciousness' Exist? (1904)}}). Bertrand Russell adopted and systematised neutral monism, most fully in \emph{The Analysis of Mind} (see \hyperref[source:source-russell-1921]{\textsc{Russell — The Analysis of Mind (1921)}}).

\webheading{How it answers the hard problem}

Neutral monism deflates the \hyperref[entry:question-hard-problem]{\textsc{hard problem}} by denying the divide it presupposes. The mind/matter distinction reduces to a difference in how neutral constituents are grouped, so neither is fundamental and neither must be ``got from'' the other. The demand to extract mind from matter — or matter from mind — is a trick question: both come from something underneath that is neither.

\webheading{What distinguishes it}

Neutral monism is the historical parent of \hyperref[entry:position-russellian-monism]{\textsc{Russellian monism}}, but stays \emph{neutral} where the Russellian view makes the intrinsic base (proto)phenomenal. Unlike \hyperref[entry:position-dualism]{\textsc{Dualism}} there is one kind of fundamental stuff; unlike physicalism and \hyperref[entry:position-idealism]{\textsc{Idealism}} that stuff is neither physical nor mental.

\begin{plainterms}
Neutral monism says mind and matter aren't two ultimate kinds of thing, nor is one built from the other — instead both are \emph{patterns} in a single, more basic stuff that is in itself neither mental nor physical. Think of the same dots forming, under one grouping, a ``mental'' picture and, under another, a ``physical'' one. So the hard problem's ``how do you get mind from matter (or matter from mind)?'' is a trick question: you get both from something underneath that is neither.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-reality-fundamental-stuff-neutral]{\textsc{Neutral fundamental stuff}} — the core commitment.
  \item \hyperref[entry:question-hard-problem]{\textsc{Hard problem (the question)}} — the question it deflates by denying the divide.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-hard-problem]{\textsc{Hard problem}} — the explanatory gap it dissolves.
  \item \hyperref[entry:position-russellian-monism]{\textsc{Russellian monism}} — its descendant; the intrinsic base is (proto)phenomenal, not neutral.
  \item \hyperref[entry:position-idealism]{\textsc{Idealism}} — rival monism on which the base is mental.
  \item \hyperref[entry:position-dualism]{\textsc{Dualism}} — rival on which there are two fundamental kinds.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{claim-access-grounds-no-asymmetry}{No access-based asymmetry}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{No access-based asymmetry}{First-person access grounds no moral-status asymmetry that singles out machines}{$\odot$}
The first-person/third-person access gap is reciprocal and fully general (see \hyperref[entry:claim-access-gap-symmetric]{\textsc{symmetric access gap}}): no subject can directly feel another's experience, for \emph{any} pair of minds. Because the gap holds everywhere, it cannot do the work of distinguishing a machine's moral standing from a human's. Any moral-status asymmetry must be argued on other grounds; first-person access (see \hyperref[entry:concept-first-person-access]{\textsc{First-person access}}) is not it.

This is the targeted denial of \hyperref[entry:claim-access-grounds-moral-status-asymmetry]{\textsc{the access-based moral asymmetry}}. Its scope is narrow: it does \emph{not} assert that machines \emph{have} moral status — that would need a positive argument, such as \hyperref[entry:argument-behavioral-parity]{\textsc{behavioural parity}} — but only removes first-person access as a ground for denying moral status to them. It would be resisted by a proponent of \hyperref[entry:argument-first-person-access]{\textsc{First-person-access asymmetry}} who insists the machine has no first-person standpoint at all, so that the symmetry is illusory.

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-substrate-independence]{\textsc{Substrate-independence thesis}} because that claim is about whether mental states depend on their physical substrate; this one is narrowly about whether \emph{access} can warrant a moral-status asymmetry.

\begin{plainterms}
Not being able to directly feel another's experience is true of \emph{every} pair of minds, not just human-and-machine — so it can't be the thing that makes a human matter morally while a machine doesn't. If there's any such moral difference, it has to be argued some other way; the inside-view gap won't deliver it. (This doesn't claim machines \emph{do} matter — it just removes one reason people give for saying they don't.)
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-access-gap-symmetric]{\textsc{Symmetric access gap}} — the symmetric, fully general gap this claim rests on.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-access-grounds-moral-status-asymmetry]{\textsc{Access-based moral asymmetry}} — the claim this is the targeted denial of.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-behavioral-parity]{\textsc{Behavioural-parity argument}} — the kind of positive argument that \emph{would} be needed to credit machines with moral status.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-first-person-access]{\textsc{First-person-access asymmetry}} — the argument whose proponent resists this claim.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-substrate-independence]{\textsc{Substrate-independence thesis}} — a distinct thesis about substrate dependence, kept apart from this access point.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{claim-no-acquaintance-with-machine}{No acquaintance with machines}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{No acquaintance with machines}{One has no first-person acquaintance with a machine's putative experience}{$\odot$}
Whatever it is (if anything) like to be a machine, one has no direct acquaintance with it. At most one can infer it from the machine's behaviour or construction; the inside view — the lived access a subject has to its own states (see \hyperref[entry:concept-first-person-access]{\textsc{First-person access}}) — is unavailable from the outside.

This is a claim about \emph{my} epistemic position toward the machine, stated from the human side. It does not assert that the machine has no experience, nor that the machine lacks first-person access to its own states; it asserts only that I do not share in whatever access there may be. As stated, virtually no one denies it — the disputes are all about what \emph{follows} from it.

What follows is exactly where it joins the larger debate. The \hyperref[entry:argument-access-symmetry-rebuttal]{\textsc{access-symmetry rebuttal}} turns on the fact that this non-acquaintance is reciprocal and fully general rather than a special deficit of machines. And the claim is distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-acquaintance-necessary-for-moral-status-warrant]{\textsc{Acquaintance as moral-status condition}}: that one supplies the necessity principle (acquaintance is required for warranted moral status), whereas this supplies the factual premise to which such a principle would be applied.

\begin{plainterms}
Whatever a machine may or may not feel inside, you have no direct line to it — you can only guess from how it behaves or how it's built. That much is almost undeniable; the real fights are all about what \emph{follows} from it. (Note what it does \emph{not} say: it doesn't claim the machine feels nothing, only that \emph{you} can't reach that feeling from the inside.)
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-first-person-access]{\textsc{First-person access}} — the inside access one lacks toward a machine.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-access-symmetry-rebuttal]{\textsc{Access-symmetry rebuttal}} — argues this deficit is reciprocal and general, not special to machines.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-acquaintance-necessary-for-moral-status-warrant]{\textsc{Acquaintance as moral-status condition}} — the necessity principle this factual premise gets fed into.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{claim-no-constitutive-fact-of-meaning}{No constitutive fact of meaning}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, contested \,\textperiodcentered\, after Kripke}{No constitutive fact of meaning}{There is no fact about a speaker that constitutes their meaning one thing rather than another by an expression}{$\odot$}
The skeptical conclusion of Kripke's Wittgenstein (see \hyperref[source:source-kripke-1982]{\textsc{Kripke — Wittgenstein on Rules and Private Language (1982)}}): nothing about a person — nothing in their past behaviour, their dispositions, their mental images, or any introspectible state — \emph{constitutes} the fact that they meant addition rather than some deviant function by ``+''. Generalised, no fact settles what anyone means by any expression, so there is no fact of the matter, in the truth-conditional sense, about meaning and content.

\webheading{Scope}

The claim is about the \emph{constitution} of meaning — what a meaning-fact could consist in — not about whether people communicate or use words consistently (plainly they do). It does not say words are meaningless; Kripke's Wittgenstein pairs it with a \emph{skeptical solution} that relocates meaning from truth-conditional facts to communal \emph{assertibility conditions} (the practice, sustained by agreement, of counting some uses as correct). It is a thesis about \emph{semantic} content; it is not the same as any thesis about the determinacy of \emph{phenomenal} character.

\webheading{Who would deny it}

Semantic realists who hold there is a genuine fact of meaning — whether grounded in suitably idealised \emph{dispositions} (what one would do under optimal conditions), in causal-informational or teleological relations between states and the world, or in a primitive, irreducible \emph{grasping} of a rule. Each is a candidate ``constituting fact'' the skeptical argument (see \hyperref[entry:argument-kripke-rule-following]{\textsc{Rule-following skeptical argument}}) tries to eliminate; denying the claim means defending one of them against that elimination.

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-syntax-insufficient-for-semantics]{\textsc{Syntax insufficient for semantics}} because that says formal symbol-manipulation does not by itself \emph{yield} meaning (a sufficiency claim about syntax); this says no fact of \emph{any} kind — syntactic or otherwise — constitutes determinate meaning in the first place. Distinct, too, from a claim about phenomenal determinacy: the indeterminacy here is of \emph{content}, not of \emph{feel}.

\begin{plainterms}
You've added numbers your whole life, but only finitely many times. Now someone asks: when you said you meant \emph{plus}, what made it \emph{plus} rather than a sneaky rule (``plus, except give 5 for big numbers you never actually tried'') that fits everything you've ever done equally well? Look for the fact that pins it down: your past sums are finite and fit both; your \emph{habits} are finite too and you sometimes slip up; picturing or feeling ``I mean addition'' doesn't spell out the answer for every new case. Kripke's unsettling claim is that there's \emph{no} such fact — nothing inside you nailed down which rule you meant. (His own way out isn't ``words are empty'' but ``meaning lives in the shared practice of a community,'' not in a private fact.)
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-kripke-rule-following]{\textsc{Rule-following skeptical argument}} — the skeptical argument that establishes this claim, and the community-based ``skeptical solution'' that accompanies it.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-intentionality]{\textsc{Intentionality}} — the aboutness/content of which this denies a constituting fact.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-syntax-insufficient-for-semantics]{\textsc{Syntax insufficient for semantics}} — a different, sufficiency-directed worry about meaning, kept distinct here.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{claim-external-world-unprovable-noncircular}{No non-circular proof of the world}{Claim}{No non-circular proof of the world}{The existence of the external world cannot be demonstrated without circularity}{$\odot$}
There is no proof of the existence of an external world that does not, somewhere, presuppose it. Every candidate proof must draw on premises — that I see a hand, that my senses are generally reliable, that memory is trustworthy — whose own warrant depends on there already being an external world delivering those perceptions. The reasoning therefore either circles back to its conclusion or stops at an external-world premise asserted without independent support: two of the horns of \hyperref[entry:concept-munchhausen-trilemma]{\textsc{the Münchhausen trilemma}}.

The textbook illustration is G. E. Moore's ``here is a hand'' (see \hyperref[source:source-moore-1939]{\textsc{Moore — Proof of an External World (1939)}}). The argument is valid and Moore arguably \emph{knows} its premise — but it has no force against the skeptic, because warrant for ``here is a hand'' is unavailable to anyone not already granting that perception puts us in touch with external things. It establishes its conclusion only for those who do not need it established.

\textbf{Scope.} The claim concerns \emph{demonstration}, not \emph{truth} and not \emph{knowledge}. It is fully compatible with the external world's existing (see \hyperref[entry:claim-external-world-exists]{\textsc{The external world exists}}) and even with our \emph{knowing} it (see \hyperref[entry:claim-knowledge-without-noncircular-proof]{\textsc{Knowing without proving}}); it denies only that the existence can be non-circularly proved to one who doubts it.

\textbf{Who would deny it.} A traditional foundationalist who thinks some perceptual or \emph{cogito}-like premise is both certain and not itself dependent on the external world might claim a non-circular proof; so might a proponent of an ampliative inference-to-the-best-explanation from experience to its external cause — though the latter wins only an \emph{abductive} case, not a demonstration, which is why it shades into \hyperref[entry:argument-deutsch-anti-solipsism]{\textsc{the bad-explanation strategy}} rather than refuting this claim.

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-justification-terminates-in-trilemma]{\textsc{Justification's trilemma}}, the \emph{general} thesis about all justification; this is its application to one famous proposition. And distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-external-world-exists]{\textsc{The external world exists}}: that the world \emph{is}, versus that it cannot be \emph{proved to be}.

\begin{plainterms}
Try to prove there's a real world outside your mind and you'll notice every step of the proof already helps itself to that world — ``I can see my hand'' only counts if your eyes are showing you something real. So the proof leans on the very thing it's trying to establish. Moore once tried it by literally holding up a hand; clever, but it convinces only people who didn't doubt it in the first place. Note the careful wording: this says the world can't be \emph{proved}, not that it isn't \emph{there} and not that we don't \emph{know} it.
\end{plainterms}

\entryhead{claim-no-radical-emergence}{No radical emergence}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, after Strawson}{No radical emergence}{Experience cannot emerge brutely from the wholly non-experiential}{$\odot$}
There can be no \emph{radical} (brute) emergence of experiential properties from a base that is wholly and utterly non-experiential. Where genuine emergence occurs in nature, the emergent feature is intelligibly grounded in the powers of its base — liquidity in the dynamics of water molecules, say — so that, given the base, the new feature is no surprise. To get \emph{experience}, the felt what-it-is-likeness, from the entirely experienceless would be emergence with no such intelligible link; and that, Strawson argues, is no better than magic. The claim does not deny emergence as such — only brute emergence across the experiential / non-experiential divide.

This is the load-bearing premise of the \hyperref[entry:argument-genetic-panpsychism]{\textsc{genetic argument}} for panpsychism: if experience is real and wholly natural, and cannot brutely emerge, then it must already be present at the fundamental level. It is denied by physicalists who accept strong emergence (see \hyperref[entry:claim-strong-emergence-is-acceptable]{\textsc{Acceptability of strong emergence}}), holding that experience can arise as a genuinely novel feature without being present in the parts — the standard reply to the genetic argument.

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-genetic-panpsychism]{\textsc{Genetic argument}} — the argument this premise anchors.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-strong-emergence-is-acceptable]{\textsc{Acceptability of strong emergence}} — its direct denial, and the engine of the emergence reply.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-consciousness-is-real]{\textsc{Reality of phenomenal consciousness}} — the realism premise the genetic argument pairs with this one.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-no-nonarbitrary-threshold-for-experience]{\textsc{Absence of an experiential threshold}} — a distinct premise that targets \emph{where} experience would switch on, not \emph{whether} it could brutely appear at all.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{claim-consciousness-noncomputable}{Non-computable consciousness}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, after Penrose}{Non-computable consciousness}{Consciousness involves non-computable physical processes}{$\odot$}
Conscious understanding is not algorithmic: no Turing-computable process reproduces it. The route to this conclusion is \emph{Gödelian} — for any consistent formal system adequate for arithmetic there are truths unprovable within it, yet a human mathematician can see that they are true, so human insight is held to outrun any fixed algorithm. Consciousness must therefore exploit \emph{non-computable} physics, proposed to be objective quantum-state reduction (orchestrated objective reduction, Orch-OR, with Stuart Hameroff). The Gödelian argument is J. R. Lucas's (1961; \hyperref[source:source-lucas-1961]{\textsc{Lucas — Minds, Machines and Gödel (1961)}}); the non-computability thesis is Roger Penrose's, in \emph{The Emperor's New Mind} (1989; \hyperref[source:source-penrose-1989]{\textsc{Penrose — The Emperor's New Mind (1989)}}) and \emph{Shadows of the Mind} (1994; \hyperref[source:source-penrose-1994]{\textsc{Penrose — Shadows of the Mind (1994)}}).

This is a claim about the \emph{computational character} of consciousness — that it is non-algorithmic — paired with a specific physical hypothesis, Orch-OR, as the proposed mechanism. Its most contested step is the Gödelian premise that human insight is non-algorithmic, disputed by Hilary Putnam, Solomon Feferman, and many others.

It is denied by computationalists and functionalists, for whom cognition just is computation, and by the critics of the Gödelian anti-mechanism argument.

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-substrate-independence]{\textsc{substrate independence}}: this does not demand a \emph{biological} substrate but one exploiting \emph{non-computable physics}, so it denies specifically the \emph{computational/algorithmic} realisation of mind — and hence bears against \hyperref[entry:claim-llm-has-mental-states]{\textsc{LLMs have mental states}}.

\begin{plainterms}
The idea that being conscious involves something a computer program can't do. The argument leans on a result from logic (Gödel's theorem): any fixed system of rules for proving mathematical truths will leave some true statements it can't prove, yet a human can often see that those statements are true. Penrose takes this to show that human understanding isn't just rule-following — it isn't an algorithm — so the brain must use a kind of physics ordinary computers can't imitate. Many logicians and philosophers dispute the key step from ``mathematicians can do this'' to ``no algorithm could.''
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-substrate-independence]{\textsc{Substrate-independence thesis}} — what it denies, on the computational reading.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-llm-has-mental-states]{\textsc{LLMs have mental states}} — bears against it: an LLM is an algorithm.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-hard-problem]{\textsc{Hard problem}} — the problem of consciousness in the background.
  \item \hyperref[entry:position-noncomputable-consciousness]{\textsc{Penrose's non-computational consciousness}} — the position built on this claim.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{claim-some-truths-about-experience-not-physically-deducible}{Non-deducible experiential truths}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, after Jackson}{Non-deducible experiential truths}{There are truths about conscious experience not deducible from the complete physical description}{$\odot$}
There are truths about conscious experience that cannot be deduced from the complete physical description of the world. This is the conclusion of the \hyperref[entry:concept-knowledge-argument]{\textsc{Knowledge argument}} in its \emph{epistemic} form. Frank Jackson's Mary knows every physical fact about colour vision yet, on first seeing red, appears to learn something new — what seeing red is \emph{like}. If she does, that truth was not contained in the physical facts she already had: a complete physics leaves some facts about experience out.

The claim is deliberately narrow. It is \emph{epistemic} — about what can be deduced or known — and is the immediate output of the Mary case, nothing more. The further step to the \emph{ontological} thesis that the missing truths concern \emph{non-physical} facts (see \hyperref[entry:claim-consciousness-fundamental-nonphysical]{\textsc{Fundamental non-physical consciousness}}) is a separate move. Physicalists can grant an epistemic gap — Mary gains a new concept or a new way of grasping an old fact — while denying that any non-physical \emph{fact} exists, so the epistemic conclusion does not by itself settle the metaphysics.

The natural way to resist the claim is upstream, at \hyperref[entry:claim-mary-learns-on-release]{\textsc{Mary learns on release}}: on the ability and old-fact/new-guise replies, Mary acquires no new \emph{truth} at all on release, and if she learns no truth, nothing follows about what is or is not deducible.

It should not be confused with two neighbours. \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-residue]{\textsc{Phenomenal residue}} says functional or causal role does not \emph{exhaust} the phenomenal; \hyperref[entry:claim-consciousness-fundamental-nonphysical]{\textsc{Fundamental non-physical consciousness}} is the ontological non-physicality thesis. This claim is the narrower bridge between them: that the phenomenal is not \emph{deducible} from the physical — the step that carries the Mary intuition toward, but not all the way to, the metaphysical conclusion.

\begin{plainterms}
