The lesson drawn from Mary: if someone could know \emph{every} physical fact and still learn something new on first seeing colour, then that something isn't contained in the physical facts — there are truths about what experience is \emph{like} that you can't derive from even a complete physics. Note this is a claim about what's \emph{deducible} or \emph{knowable}; getting from there to ``and therefore those truths are non-physical'' (see \hyperref[entry:claim-consciousness-fundamental-nonphysical]{\textsc{Fundamental non-physical consciousness}}) is an extra, separately disputed step.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-knowledge-argument]{\textsc{Knowledge argument}} — the thought experiment this claim is the epistemic conclusion of; Mary the colour scientist and what she does or doesn't gain on release.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-mary-learns-on-release]{\textsc{Mary learns on release}} — the premise upstream of this one; deny that Mary learns any new truth and this conclusion never gets going.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-consciousness-fundamental-nonphysical]{\textsc{Fundamental non-physical consciousness}} — the ontological thesis this claim stops short of; the contested further step from an epistemic gap to a non-physical fact.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-residue]{\textsc{Phenomenal residue}} — the distinct claim that role does not \emph{exhaust} the phenomenal, not to be conflated with the present claim about deducibility.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-hard-problem]{\textsc{Hard problem}} — why physical description seems to leave felt experience unexplained; the broader problem this deducibility gap is one sharp instance of.
  \item \hyperref[entry:question-what-does-mary-learn]{\textsc{What Mary learns}} — the open question this claim answers: what, if anything, Mary acquires on first seeing red.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{argument-counterfactuals-yield-non-triviality-not-uniqueness}{Non-triviality without uniqueness}{Argument \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{Non-triviality without uniqueness}{Counterfactuals buy non-triviality, not uniqueness}{$\vdash$}
This reply concedes the \hyperref[entry:argument-causal-structure-supplies-invariant]{\textsc{causal-structure reply}} its premises and then denies its conclusion: objective cause and effect \emph{narrows} the admissible carvings of a system, but it does not narrow them to one.

\webheading{The argument}

The inference is \emph{undercutting} — grant the premises of the causal-structure reply, deny what it draws from them.

\begin{enumerate}
  \item Counterfactual constraints exclude the gerrymandered mappings but leave a \emph{family} of admissible decompositions, differing in boundary, \hyperref[entry:concept-grain]{\textsc{Grain (of role individuation)}}, and choice of variable set (see \hyperref[entry:claim-counterfactual-structure-underdetermines-decomposition]{\textsc{Underdetermination of decomposition}}). The anti-triviality result (see \hyperref[source:source-chalmers-1996b]{\textsc{Chalmers — Does a Rock Implement Every Finite-State Automaton? (1996)}}) claims only non-triviality, never uniqueness.
\end{enumerate}

∴ \hyperref[entry:claim-causal-structure-fixes-decomposition]{\textsc{That causal structure fixes the decomposition}} does not follow: objective causal structure narrows but does not \emph{uniquely fix} the carving. The demand of \hyperref[entry:argument-decomposition-indeterminacy]{\textsc{the indeterminacy objection}} survives in attenuated form — so this reply supports \hyperref[entry:claim-functional-decomposition-relative]{\textsc{the relativity of functional decomposition}} — and it reconnects to the \hyperref[entry:argument-role-grain-dilemma]{\textsc{role-grain dilemma}}: which member of the surviving family is ``the'' organisation?

\webheading{The weakest premise}

The vulnerable premise is (1)'s claim that no further objective principle picks out a unique level or boundary. A functionalist may argue that nature itself marks one level — for instance, the grain at which the system forms a closed causal or informational unit. A principle of that kind, motivated independently of the verdict it is wanted to deliver, would defeat this reply.

This is distinct from the \hyperref[entry:argument-relevance-filter-regress]{\textsc{relevance-filter regress}}: that regress targets \emph{which dispositions} count as functional; this reply grants the relevant relations and targets \emph{which carving or level} counts among them. Two routes to one residual indeterminacy.

\begin{plainterms}
This grants a lot to the ``real cause-and-effect picks out one true organization'' reply (see \hyperref[entry:argument-causal-structure-supplies-invariant]{\textsc{Causal-structure reply}}), then stops it short. Yes — insisting on real, robust cause-and-effect (rather than any old after-the-fact relabeling) does throw out the obviously bogus ways of slicing a system into parts. But ``throwing out the bogus ones'' isn't the same as ``leaving exactly one.'' What survives is still a \emph{family} of perfectly reasonable carvings, differing in where you draw the boundaries and how fine-grained you go. So objective cause-and-effect \emph{narrows down} the options without \emph{pinning down a single one} — and the original question (``which carving is the real organization?'') comes back, just in a milder form. To close that gap you'd need some further, non-rigged principle that crowns one level as the true one — and nobody has produced that yet.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-causal-structure-supplies-invariant]{\textsc{Causal-structure reply}} — the reply this argument answers and undercuts.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-causal-structure-fixes-decomposition]{\textsc{Causal selection of decomposition}} — the conclusion it denies.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-functional-decomposition-relative]{\textsc{Observer-relative decomposition}} — the relativity claim it supports.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-role-grain-dilemma]{\textsc{Grain dilemma}} — where the residual ``which level?'' question reconnects.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-relevance-filter-regress]{\textsc{Relevance-filter regress}} — a sibling route to residual indeterminacy, attacking the \emph{selection} of relevant relations.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{claim-llm-attributes-empirically-non-unique}{Non-uniqueness of LLM attributes}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, after Dewynter}{Non-uniqueness of LLM attributes}{The anthropomorphic attributes ascribed to LLMs are empirically non-unique across substrates}{$\odot$}
The human-like attributes commonly ascribed to large language models are \emph{empirically non-unique}: they are not properties that single out LLMs from any other realization of the same computation. Some features are genuinely substrate-invariant — a network's response to a given prompt is fixed by the computation, and stays the same whether the network runs on silicon or on goats. But the \emph{interpretation} of that behaviour — reading it as understanding, morality, or sentience — is not fixed by the computation; it varies with the substrate and the observer. Because the very same trained network can be built in \emph{Age of Empires II} (see \hyperref[entry:claim-perceptron-realizable-in-any-substrate]{\textsc{Perceptron in any substrate}}), an attribute inferred from an LLM's behaviour cannot belong to the LLM uniquely. The upshot is not that the attributes are absent but that talk of them is empty until pinned to explicit measurement criteria.

This is the conclusion of \hyperref[entry:argument-aoe2-anthropomorphism]{\textsc{Age of Empires II argument}}, and it reframes debates such as whether \hyperref[entry:claim-llm-has-mental-states]{\textsc{LLMs have mental states}}: those questions are ill-posed until one says what, substrate-neutrally, would count as evidence.

\begin{plainterms}
When a chatbot says something wise, two different things are going on. One is the actual output, which would be identical no matter what the program ran on. The other is \emph{our reading} of it as ``wise'' or ``caring'' — and that reading is not in the machine; it shifts depending on whether we picture a sleek AI or a field of goats doing the identical sums. So calling these qualities the AI's own is premature: first you have to say what would count as really having them, in a way that does not depend on what the thing is made of.
\end{plainterms}

\end{multicols}
\bookgroup{O}
\begin{multicols}{2}
\entryhead{claim-counterfactual-structure-objective}{Objective counterfactual structure}{Claim}{Objective counterfactual structure}{Counterfactual-causal dependencies are objective, not observer-relative}{$\odot$}
Facts about what a system \emph{would} do under interventions are objective features of the world, not artifacts of an observer's chosen description. Whether intervening on X would change Y is settled by the world, not chosen — this is the \emph{interventionist} account of causation, on which a cause is something that, were it manipulated, would shift its effect (see \hyperref[source:source-woodward-2003]{\textsc{Woodward — Making Things Happen (2003)}}).

\webheading{Scope}

The claim concerns the objectivity of counterfactual-causal facts \emph{once a set of variables is fixed}. It does not claim that the \emph{choice of variables} is itself observer-independent — that gap is exactly what \hyperref[entry:claim-counterfactual-structure-underdetermines-decomposition]{\textsc{the underdetermination claim}} exploits.

\webheading{Who would deny it}

A thoroughgoing observer-relativist about causation and computation — Searle's ``syntax is not intrinsic to physics'' (see \hyperref[source:source-searle-1990b]{\textsc{Searle — Is the Brain a Digital Computer? (1990)}}), read strongly.

It is distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-implementation-requires-counterfactual-structure]{\textsc{the claim that implementation requires counterfactual structure}}: that says implementation \emph{needs} counterfactual structure, whereas this says such structure, once the variables are fixed, is \emph{objective}.

\begin{plainterms}
Whether poking a system \emph{here} would change what happens \emph{there} is a fact about the world, not something an observer just decides. These ``what would happen if…'' facts are real features of how the system is wired, the same for everyone who looks. (Fine print: this holds \emph{once you've agreed what counts as the system's parts} — which way to carve it up in the first place is a separate, thornier question.)
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-implementation-requires-counterfactual-structure]{\textsc{Counterfactual constraint on implementation}} — the companion claim that ties this objectivity to computational implementation.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-counterfactual-structure-underdetermines-decomposition]{\textsc{Underdetermination of decomposition}} — the limit on this claim: objectivity holds only after the variables are chosen.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{claim-functional-decomposition-relative}{Observer-relative decomposition}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, after Searle}{Observer-relative decomposition}{A physical system has no unique functional decomposition}{$\odot$}
A physical system has no unique functional decomposition. Which physical states count as inputs and which as outputs, where the system's boundary falls, and how the underlying microstates are grouped into ``the same'' functional state — all of these can be fixed in many equally admissible ways, each yielding a \emph{different} functional organisation. Functional or computational individuation is therefore description- or observer-relative (see \hyperref[source:source-searle-1990b]{\textsc{Searle — Is the Brain a Digital Computer? (1990)}}); in the limit, Putnam's result that an ordinary open system implements many automata under suitable mappings (see \hyperref[source:source-putnam-1988]{\textsc{Putnam — Representation and Reality (1988)}}).

\webheading{Scope}

The claim is about the \emph{individuation} of functional states, prior to any verdict about minds. It does not say every carving is equally \emph{useful or natural}, only that the physics alone singles out none as the system's \emph{real} organisation. It is distinct from grain: \hyperref[entry:concept-grain]{\textsc{Grain (of role individuation)}} concerns the \emph{resolution} of a role along one scale, whereas this concerns the \emph{non-uniqueness of the carving itself}, even at a fixed resolution.

\webheading{Who would deny it}

A functionalist who holds that objective counterfactual/causal structure fixes a privileged decomposition — so that only carvings respecting genuine causal dependencies count (see \hyperref[source:source-chalmers-1996b]{\textsc{Chalmers — Does a Rock Implement Every Finite-State Automaton? (1996)}}). That is the live reply to the \hyperref[entry:argument-decomposition-indeterminacy]{\textsc{decomposition-indeterminacy objection}}.

It is distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-residue]{\textsc{the claim that role leaves qualia out}}: this says role-talk does not even fix a determinate system — a prior, more general worry that applies to intentional states too.

\begin{plainterms}
There's no single fact about which bits of a physical system are its ``inputs,'' which are ``outputs,'' where it stops, or how to lump its tiny states into ``the same'' working part — you can do it in many equally legitimate ways, each giving a different ``organization.'' So describing something by its function is partly a matter of how you choose to look at it, not something read straight off the physics. (This is the strong version of the worry; a common reply is that real cause-and-effect does pin down one correct carving after all.)
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-functionalism]{\textsc{Functionalism}} — the doctrine whose individuation this claim challenges.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-grain]{\textsc{Grain (of role individuation)}} — distinguished: this is about the carving's non-uniqueness, not a role's resolution.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-decomposition-indeterminacy]{\textsc{Decomposition-indeterminacy objection}} — the objection that builds on this as its first premise.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-residue]{\textsc{Phenomenal residue}} — the residue worry this prior, more general claim is distinguished from.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{argument-old-fact-new-guise}{Old fact, new guise}{Argument \,\textperiodcentered\, after Loar}{Old fact, new guise}{Old fact, new guise — Mary's new knowledge is a new phenomenal concept, not a new fact}{$\vdash$}
The \emph{old-fact / new-guise} argument is the physicalist's reply that grants the knowledge argument both its premises yet blocks its conclusion. Even if Mary knew every physical fact and genuinely learns something new on seeing red, that new knowledge can be a new \emph{concept} of a fact she already knew rather than a new fact — so it gives the anti-physicalist no extra ingredient in the world. The move is the Mary-specific application of the \emph{phenomenal-concept strategy}, due to Brian Loar (see \hyperref[source:source-loar-1990]{\textsc{Loar — Phenomenal States (1990)}}).

It raises the bridge critical question against the \hyperref[entry:argument-knowledge-argument]{\textsc{knowledge argument}}: even granting that Mary knew all the physical facts and learns something genuinely new, the bridge from ``new knowledge'' to ``new, non-physical fact'' fails, because knowledge is individuated more finely than the facts known.

\webheading{The inference}

The form is deductive, turning on an independently motivated distinction.

\begin{enumerate}
  \item One fact can be known under distinct concepts, and coming to grasp it under a second concept is genuine epistemic progress without any new fact — the lesson of \emph{a posteriori} identities (see \hyperref[entry:claim-some-necessities-knowable-only-a-posteriori]{\textsc{Hesperus/Phosphorus, water/H₂O}}).
  \item Experiences give rise to \emph{phenomenal concepts}: first-person, recognitional concepts of inner states, cognitively isolated from the theoretical concepts of those same states (see \hyperref[source:source-loar-1990]{\textsc{Loar 1990}}).
  \item Inside the room Mary grasped the fact of seeing-red under theoretical concepts only; seeing red equips her with the phenomenal concept of that same state.
\end{enumerate}

Therefore \hyperref[entry:claim-mary-gains-old-fact-new-guise]{\textsc{her new knowledge is a new guise of an old fact}}, and \hyperref[entry:claim-some-truths-about-experience-not-physically-deducible]{\textsc{the non-deducibility of some truths about experience}} does not follow in any sense that troubles physicalism: what is not deducible is a \emph{way of thinking}, not a \emph{truth about the world}.

\webheading{Relation to the general strategy}

This is the Mary-specific case of the phenomenal-concept strategy whose general form — aimed at the explanatory gap rather than the knowledge argument — is \hyperref[entry:argument-gap-deflated-by-phenomenal-concepts]{\textsc{the gap-deflation argument}} (Prong A): the same concept-isolation machinery that predicts a felt explanatory gap predicts that Mary's book-learning could never substitute for the phenomenal concept. It accordingly inherits that strategy's standing attack. \hyperref[entry:argument-phenomenal-concept-overgeneration]{\textsc{The overgeneration objection}} presses that a concept isolated enough to do this work is too isolated to ground phenomenal self-knowledge (see \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-concept-isolation-tradeoff]{\textsc{Phenomenal-concept trade-off}}) — a cost this argument must pay too.

\webheading{Weakest premise}

Premise (2). If the phenomenal concept presents its referent via a substantive phenomenal property — a ``what-it-is-likeness'' the theoretical concepts miss — the anti-physicalist relocates the argument onto that property and the residue returns. If instead it presents its referent blankly, as a bare demonstrative, it is unclear why acquiring it should feel like the revelation the Mary intuition reports. Premise (1) is also pressured: in the Hesperus case the two guises connect to distinct \emph{causal routes} to one object, and a complete physical description arguably lets one deduce both routes — whereas Mary's case is precisely one where deduction fails, so the analogy may assume what it must prove.

\begin{plainterms}
The Mary story (see \hyperref[entry:argument-knowledge-argument]{\textsc{the knowledge argument}}) concludes that physics left a fact out, because book-perfect Mary still learns something at her first sight of red. This reply says: learning something new doesn't always mean learning a new \emph{fact}. Discovering that the Morning Star is the Evening Star felt like news, yet it added no new planet — just a second label connected to the same thing. Mary's first experience of red installs the inside-view label for a brain fact she already had the science-view label for. Real ``aha!'', same world. The standing objection (made elsewhere in this web against the same general strategy — see \hyperref[entry:argument-phenomenal-concept-overgeneration]{\textsc{the overgeneration objection}}): if the inside-view label is so walled off from the science-view one, it's hard to explain how it tells you anything about yourself at all.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-indexical-reply]{\textsc{Indexical reply}} — the sibling deflationary reply; the collapse objection charges that it re-enacts this very argument with an ``indexical'' relabelling.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{argument-olympia}{Olympia argument}{Argument \,\textperiodcentered\, after Maudlin}{Olympia argument}{Maudlin's Olympia argument against computationalism about consciousness}{$\vdash$}
Tim Maudlin's \emph{Olympia} argument (see \hyperref[source:source-maudlin-1989]{\textsc{Maudlin — Computation and Consciousness (1989)}}) aims to show that no computational theory of \emph{phenomenal consciousness} can be correct. (Olympia is the lifelike automaton of E. T. A. Hoffmann's \emph{The Sandman}.) Rather than appeal to the intuition that the ``wrong stuff'' cannot think — a move Maudlin explicitly disowns — it argues that three independently attractive theses form an \emph{inconsistent triad}, so that a computationalist about consciousness must abandon one of them. The target is the computational construal of \hyperref[entry:claim-substrate-independence]{\textsc{the substrate-independence thesis}}; it leaves non-computational physical theories of consciousness untouched.

\webheading{The inconsistent triad}

\begin{itemize}
  \item \emph{Sufficiency.} Running the right program suffices for the conscious state: any system that performs the computation π (on input \emph{r}) thereby supports the phenomenal state φ.
  \item \emph{Necessity.} Being a genuine computer running a \emph{nontrivial} program is necessary for φ — a system that merely runs through a sequence of states without the right computational structure is not conscious.
  \item \emph{Supervenience} (Maudlin's principle). Two systems engaged in precisely the same physical activity through a time support the same modes of consciousness. Equivalently: introducing an inert object that has no causal interaction with a system can neither create nor destroy its phenomenal states, because an occurrent experience is wholly realised in the activity going on during it.
\end{itemize}

Maudlin builds a single machine that makes these three collide.

\webheading{The machine: Klara and Olympia}

Start with \emph{Klara}, a genuine computer running the conscious-making program π. Klara may be built of anything — Maudlin imagines a clockwork so vast it would fill the solar system; her tape is a row of water troughs, each full or empty for one bit. Record the exact run Klara performs on the particular input \emph{r} that (by hypothesis) produces the conscious state φ, and reorder the troughs into the sequence that run visits.

Now build \emph{Olympia}. She is nothing but an \textbf{armature} that travels along the reordered troughs, filling and emptying each one to reproduce, step for step, the recorded run. Fed \emph{r}, Olympia's physically active events are identical to Klara's. But the bare armature, blindly replaying one fixed sequence, computes only a \emph{trivial constant function}; it has none of the counterfactual structure π requires. So by \emph{Necessity}, the bare armature is not conscious.

To supply the missing counterfactual structure — \emph{without adding any activity to the actual run} — Maudlin fits each trough with a latched \textbf{float}, set to the water level that trough has at that step of the recorded run, and chains the floats to \textbf{N + 2 further copies of Klara, each frozen (``blocked'') in one of π's machine states by a small block of wood jamming its gears}. Were the input ever to differ from \emph{r}, the relevant float would move, release its block, and that copy of Klara would wake up and take over the computation (shutting the armature down). With this apparatus Olympia genuinely \emph{implements} π: across the whole space of possible inputs her states stand in exactly the dispositional relations π demands (see \hyperref[entry:claim-implementation-requires-counterfactual-structure]{\textsc{Counterfactual constraint on implementation}}). Yet on the actual run \emph{r} not one float moves, not one blocked Klara stirs, and the chains hang slack: the entire added ``clockwork universe'' sits physically inert.

\webheading{The bind}

\begin{itemize}
  \item By \emph{Sufficiency}, Olympia-with-machinery — which really does implement π on \emph{r} — is conscious, enjoying φ.
  \item By \emph{Necessity}, the bare armature alone — a trivial constant function — is not conscious.
  \item But the bare armature and Olympia-with-machinery have \emph{identical actual physical activity}: the added machinery never acts. By \emph{Supervenience} they must be alike in consciousness.
\end{itemize}

The three verdicts cannot all stand. Whether φ is present turns, for the computationalist, on counterfactual structure that is carried entirely by \emph{physically inert} parts — and Maudlin's supervenience principle says inert, causally disconnected parts cannot make that difference. So consciousness cannot derive from computational structure; an acceptable computational theory of consciousness is not possible.

\webheading{The movie analogy}

Maudlin's image: Olympia creates the illusion of being Klara-in-action the way a film creates motion from still frames. Each frozen copy of Klara is a single frame — an inert object holding one captured moment of her operation — and the armature's passage briefly ``lights up'' each frame's dispositional structure in sequence, as a projector's lamp animates a strip of stills. ``The problem with computationalism is that it does not contain the conceptual resources to distinguish the flickering illusion from reality.''

\webheading{Weakest premise}

The supervenience principle — that only \emph{actually active}, causally engaged events, not standing unexercised dispositions, can make a difference to experience. The computationalist's natural escape is to reject it: counterfactual dispositions are categorical physical facts present throughout the run, so the bare armature and Olympia-with-machinery are \emph{not} doing ``the same thing'' after all. Maudlin presses this with cases in which counterfactual structure varies while actual activity plainly does not:

\begin{itemize}
  \item \emph{The pinball illustration} (due to Kevin Kelly): set a ball rolling through a board of pins; remove pins it never touches on that path. The trajectory, the energy transfers, the physicist's explanation are all unchanged — only the counterfactuals (what would have happened on a different push) differ.
  \item \emph{Argument by addition.} Fit each blocked Klara with a \emph{second} block that never touches anything but would jam the gears if the first block were removed. The counterfactuals now fail, so by computationalism Olympia is no longer conscious — though nothing physically active has changed.
  \item \emph{Argument by subtraction.} Let the float-chains slowly rust until they would snap rather than unblock a machine. At some unmarked instant the counterfactual support lapses and, by computationalism, the toothache winks out — with no physical event to mark the passage.
\end{itemize}

The cost of rejecting supervenience, Maudlin adds, is steep: the very same intuition powers the standard \emph{pro}-computationalist thought experiment of gradually replacing neurons with silicon (``why should it matter whether the impulse runs on a neuron or on a wire?''). A computationalist who lets an untouched, inert block decide presence or absence of experience forfeits the intuition that made substrate-neutrality attractive in the first place.

\webheading{Scope and conclusion}

The argument is aimed at \emph{occurrent} conscious states — datable events such as a sensation — but Maudlin extends the construction to intelligence and linguistic understanding: shift the blocks a few centimetres and Olympia ``solves the problem stupidly,'' or produces the words without the understanding. He reaches, by a consistency argument, the very conclusion John Searle sought by other means — that having a mind is \emph{not merely a matter of instantiating a program} — while rejecting Searle's ``fancy instantiation'' route through the wrong-stuff intuition. Olympia does not show that minds must be ``wet and squishy'': a silicon or even a hydraulic brain might, for all the argument says, be conscious. It shows only that consciousness cannot supervene on \emph{computational} structure, so ``some other level beside the computational must be sought'' — the counterfactuals that constitute a program must be carried by the system's actual causal activity, not by inert scaffolding.

\begin{plainterms}
Imagine a computer running a program that (suppose) makes it conscious. Film the exact physical steps it takes on one particular run, then build a dumb machine — Olympia — that just \emph{replays} those steps, like a player piano reproducing a recording. On that run Olympia physically does the very same things the computer did. But Olympia can't cope with any \emph{different} input, so computer scientists say she isn't really \emph{running the program} — running a program means being set up to respond correctly to all the inputs you \emph{might} get, not just the one you did.

So bolt on extra machines that \emph{would} kick in and do the right thing if the input were different — but that, on this actual input, just sit there, frozen, never moving. Now Olympia counts as running the program, yet literally nothing that actually happens has changed; the add-ons are dead weight on this run. Here's the squeeze. Did adding silent, never-used machines switch consciousness \emph{on}? That seems crazy — how could whether anyone's home depend on gadgets that never do anything? (Maudlin sharpens it: rust through the cables connecting them and consciousness would supposedly blink out at some moment nothing physical marks.) But if those idle machines \emph{didn't} switch consciousness on, then ``running the program'' was never what made the computer conscious to begin with. Either way, the idea that consciousness just \emph{is} running the right computation is in trouble. Maudlin's picture: Olympia is a \emph{movie} of a mind — a strip of frozen frames briefly lit up one after another — and computationalism can't tell the flickering movie from the real thing.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-substrate-independence]{\textsc{Substrate-independence thesis}} — the computational sufficiency thesis this argument attacks (in its computational construal).
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-implementation-requires-counterfactual-structure]{\textsc{Counterfactual constraint on implementation}} — the counterfactual account of implementation Olympia exploits and turns into a liability.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-functionalism]{\textsc{Functionalism}} — the broader view computationalism is a species of.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-decomposition-indeterminacy]{\textsc{Decomposition-indeterminacy objection}} — a companion pressure on whether ``which computation a physical system runs'' is objective enough to ground a mind.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-chinese-room]{\textsc{Chinese room argument}} — the other classic anti-computationalist thought experiment; Maudlin reaches its conclusion (a program is not enough for a mind) but rejects its wrong-stuff route, arguing instead from the supervenience of consciousness on actual physical activity.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{argument-olympia-trace-specialization}{Olympia as trace specialization}{Argument \,\textperiodcentered\, contested \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{Olympia as trace specialization}{The Klara→Olympia transformation is trace specialization with guarded deoptimization}{$\vdash$}
The step at the heart of \hyperref[entry:argument-olympia]{\textsc{Olympia argument}} — turning the computer Klara into the replaying automaton Olympia — can be stated as a purely computational, effectively computable program transformation, with the physical machinery of troughs, floats, and blocked copies appearing only as hardware for formally specified items. The transformation is one compiler engineers know well: \emph{trace specialization}, the technique behind tracing just-in-time compilation, in which a program's actual execution path on one input is recorded, replayed by branch-free straight-line code, and protected by guard checks that fall back (``deoptimize'') to the original program the moment the data deviate from the recording. Making this precise yields a theorem — Olympia really does implement Klara's program, on every input — and so supports the construction step of Maudlin's argument (see \hyperref[source:source-maudlin-1989]{\textsc{Maudlin — Computation and Consciousness (1989)}}). It also localizes exactly where the argument can be attacked, which is why this reconstruction is recorded as contested.

The move runs:

\begin{enumerate}
  \item A physical system implements a program π just in case its states, under some mapping, support the full counterfactual structure of π's machine table (see \hyperref[entry:claim-implementation-requires-counterfactual-structure]{\textsc{Counterfactual constraint on implementation}}).
  \item The guarded trace machine defined below is provably counterfactually equivalent to π, under an explicit state mapping, across \emph{all} inputs (the implementation theorem).
  \item Maudlin's physical Olympia realizes the guarded trace machine item for item (the dictionary below).
\end{enumerate}

∴ Olympia implements π, and the Klara→Olympia transformation is a syntactic operation computable from the program and the input alone — no appeal to anything non-computational is needed to build her.

\webheading{The setup: Klara's run as a trace log}

Model Klara as a deterministic Turing machine: a finite state set $Q$, tape alphabet $\Sigma$, and machine table $\delta : Q \times \Sigma \to \Sigma \times \{-1,+1\} \times Q$ encoding the program π. A \emph{configuration} is a triple $(S, a, T)$: current state, head address $a \in \mathbb{Z}$, and tape contents $T : \mathbb{Z} \to \Sigma$. One step: if $\delta(S, T(a)) = (w, d, S')$, the machine writes $w$ at $a$, moves to $a + d$, and enters $S'$ — the next configuration is $(S',\, a+d,\, T[a \mapsto w])$.

Fix the input tape $R$ (encoding the input $r$) and run Klara from $(S_0, a_0, R)$ for the $N$ steps of the episode of interest, obtaining configurations $(S_t, a_t, T_t)$ for $t = 0, \dots, N$. Recording every memory access gives the \textbf{trace log}

\[
\Lambda \;=\; \big\langle\, (S_t,\; a_t,\; v_t,\; w_t,\; d_t) \,\big\rangle_{t=0}^{N-1},
\]

where $v_t = T_t(a_t)$ and $(w_t, d_t, S_{t+1}) = \delta(S_t, v_t)$, together with the terminal pair $(S_N, a_N)$. Here $a_t$ is the address visited at step $t$, $v_t$ the value found there \emph{on arrival} (before that step's write), and $w_t$ the value written. The log is exactly the ``unrolled'' run: which cell, which value, at every step.

\emph{Log-consistency lemma.} For each $t$: if $k = \max\{\, j < t : a_j = a_t \,\}$ exists then $v_t = w_k$, otherwise $v_t = R(a_t)$. (Induction on $t$: the tape changes only at written addresses, $T_{j+1} = T_j[a_j \mapsto w_j]$, so the value met at $a_t$ is the last value written there, or the input value if the cell is untouched.) So the log never needs to be checked for coherence — it inherits it from being a genuine run.

\webheading{The transformation}

Klara's step $t$ is a \textbf{branch}: \emph{in state $S_t$, read address $a_t$, and do one of $|\Sigma|$ different things depending on the value found}. The transformation replaces each branch with a \textbf{guarded straight-line command}, using the log to pre-resolve both the branch and everything downstream of it:

\[
\begin{aligned}
&\textbf{for } t = 0, \dots, N-1: \\
&\quad \texttt{guard}_t:\; \textbf{if } T(a_t) \neq v_t \to \mathrm{DEOPT}(t)\\
&\quad \texttt{write}_t:\; T(a_t) := w_t\\
&\mathrm{DEOPT}(N)
\end{aligned}
\]

where $\mathrm{DEOPT}(t)$ means: shut the straight-line executor down and start a dormant full copy of Klara in configuration $(S_t,\, a_t,\, \text{current tape})$. The three ingredients of Klara's step — state, address, read value — are deleted from the \emph{program} and reappear as \emph{data}: $S_t$ labels the deoptimization target, $a_t$ becomes the guard's address, $v_t$ becomes the guard's expected value. The \textbf{bare armature} is this program with the guards deleted: branch-free writes that never read the tape at all.

The state mapping σ (Maudlin's own definition, restated): \emph{the system is in machine state $S$ iff either an awakened copy of Klara is in state $S$, or no copy is awake and the executor is at stage $t$ with $S = S_t$.}

The dictionary to Maudlin's hardware is exact:

\begin{itemize}
  \item \textbf{Time-indexing of memory} — re-index cells by step, so cell $t$ \emph{is} the access $a_t$ — is the reordering of the troughs into visit order. The index map $t \mapsto a_t$ is not injective; its fibers $\{\, i, j : a_i = a_j \,\}$ are precisely the trough-classes joined by pipes (bi-location). Computationally the reordering is an inessential change of coordinates, needed only so that the \emph{physical} executor can be dumb — an armature moving uniformly rightward, addressing by position rather than by lookup.
  \item $\texttt{write}_t$ — the armature's drain-rods and hose at trough $t$.
  \item $\texttt{guard}_t$ — the latched float at trough $t$, preset to $v_t$ and released as the armature arrives, \emph{before} the write (Maudlin's fn. 7 fixes exactly this ordering).
  \item $\mathrm{DEOPT}(t)$ — the chain from float $t$ to the wooden block of the Klara-copy frozen in state $S_t$ with its head at trough $t$; the return chain that kills the armature enforces ``guard before write''. $\mathrm{DEOPT}(N)$ is the final copy, started when the armature completes its trip. One dormant copy per guard plus the completion copy — Maudlin's $N+2$ machines.
\end{itemize}

\webheading{The implementation theorem}

\textbf{Theorem.} \emph{For every initial tape $T_0$ — not just $R$ — the guarded trace machine and Klara, started alike in $S_0$ with head at $a_0$, pass through the same sequence of machine states (under σ) and perform the same sequence of tape operations (same addresses, same values, same order). In particular they compute the same function and halt in the same configuration.}

\textbf{Proof (summary).} The proof is an induction along the recorded run; the full formal version is set out in the appendix that closes this entry. So long as every guard has passed, a step-by-step invariant holds: the trace machine's tape is identical to the tape Klara herself would have produced on $T_0$, and Klara's state and head position agree with the log. A passing guard means Klara, too, would be reading the logged value at that step, so determinism forces her to make exactly the logged transition — the very write the armature performs — and the invariant carries over to the next step. If every guard passes, the completion copy wakes in precisely Klara's final configuration and continues her run. If some guard fails first, it fails \emph{before} that step's write, so the awakened copy stands in precisely the configuration Klara herself would occupy at the first point of divergence, and determinism does the rest: identical configurations have identical futures. Either way, every write and every mapped state coincides with Klara's own run.

Three corollaries carry the philosophical weight:

\begin{itemize}
  \item \textbf{Implementation.} Under σ, the system's states satisfy every counterfactual of the machine table across the whole input space — precisely the criterion of premise 1. Olympia implements π.
  \item \textbf{Triviality of the bare armature.} Delete the guards and the write sequence $\langle (a_t, w_t) \rangle$ is fixed independently of the tape: the guard-free machine computes a constant function, exactly Maudlin's observation (fn. 6 of \hyperref[source:source-maudlin-1989]{\textsc{Maudlin — Computation and Consciousness (1989)}}) that the armature alone instantiates only a trivial program.
  \item \textbf{Effectiveness.} The whole transformation is computable from $(\pi, r)$ alone: run π on $r$, record $\Lambda$, emit the guarded program and the deoptimization table. Building Olympia requires nothing but the trace log.
\end{itemize}

On the input $r$ itself, no guard ever fires and no $\mathrm{DEOPT}$ executes: the entire counterfactual apparatus is, in compiler vocabulary, \emph{dynamically unused} on that run. Maudlin's ``physically inert'' machinery is the hardware shadow of this fact — specialization converts live branches into statically present but (on the training input) never-triggered fallbacks.

\webheading{Inference form and weakest premise}

The inference is deductive; the theorem itself is not in dispute. What the formalization does is force any attack on \hyperref[entry:argument-olympia]{\textsc{Olympia argument}}'s construction step to land on one of the bridge premises, and the weakest is \textbf{premise 3's inertness gloss} — the reading of the guards as \emph{no activity}. In the computational statement, $\texttt{guard}_t$ is an \textbf{executed instruction on every run, including the actual one}: at each step the tape value at $a_t$ is read and compared with $v_t$, and the check \emph{passes} — an event, not an absence. The triviality corollary shows the guards are ineliminable: remove them and implementation provably collapses into the constant machine. So all of the input-reading that π requires is being done, on the actual run, by the guards. Maudlin's hardware makes a passing check \emph{look} like inactivity — a latched float, released, sitting motionless in the very water level it tests — but the supervenience step of his argument needs ``no motion'' to entail ``no relevant physical activity'', and the formal restatement exposes that identification as a substantive further premise rather than an observation. A float testing a trough and finding the expected level is arguably a null-result measurement, physically realized by the water's support of the float; whether that counts as the system ``reading its tape'' is exactly what the argument must settle, and the trace-specialization framing suggests it cannot be settled by inspection. This is why the node is recorded as contested.

Two further, lesser attack points: the state mapping σ is disjunctive (``an awakened copy is in $S$, \emph{or} the armature is at stage $t$…''), inviting the observer-relativity worry pressed by \hyperref[entry:argument-decomposition-indeterminacy]{\textsc{Decomposition-indeterminacy objection}}; and the counterfactual support is \emph{parasitic} — carried by $N+2$ embedded whole copies of the original machine, so one may ask whether Olympia is a new implementation of π at all rather than a warehouse of blocked Klaras with a mechanical replayer in front.

\webheading{Appendix: proof of the implementation theorem}

Write $(S'_t, a'_t, T'_t)$ for Klara's run on $T_0$, and $T^{O}_t$ for the trace machine's tape after $t$ completed writes. Let $t^\ast$ be the least $t < N$ at which $\texttt{guard}_t$ fails, i.e. $T^{O}_t(a_t) \neq v_t$; set $t^\ast = N$ if every guard passes.

Establish by induction the invariant $I(t)$, for $0 \le t \le t^\ast$:

\[
I(t):\quad T^{O}_t = T'_t,\;\; S'_t = S_t,\;\; a'_t = a_t .
\]

\emph{Base.} $I(0)$ is immediate: both machines start on the same tape $T_0$ in state $S_0$ at address $a_0$.

\emph{Step.} Assume $I(t)$ with $t < t^\ast$. Since $t < t^\ast$, $\texttt{guard}_t$ passes: $T^{O}_t(a_t) = v_t$. By $I(t)$, Klara-on-$T_0$ is in state $S_t$ with head at $a_t$ reading $T'_t(a_t) = T^{O}_t(a_t) = v_t$ — the logged state reading the logged value. Determinism of $\delta$ then forces the logged transition $\delta(S_t, v_t) = (w_t, d_t, S_{t+1})$: Klara writes $w_t$ at $a_t$, moves to $a_t + d_t = a_{t+1}$, and enters $S_{t+1}$. The trace machine's $\texttt{write}_t$ makes the identical write. Hence

\[
\begin{aligned}
T^{O}_{t+1} &= T^{O}_t[a_t \mapsto w_t]\\
&= T'_t[a_t \mapsto w_t] = T'_{t+1},\\
S'_{t+1} &= S_{t+1}, \quad a'_{t+1} = a_{t+1},
\end{aligned}
\]

which is $I(t+1)$. Moreover the two machines' step-$t$ activity coincides: the same state under σ, the same address, the same write.

\emph{Case 1: $t^\ast = N$ (all guards pass).} $I(N)$ holds, so when the executor completes, $\mathrm{DEOPT}(N)$ awakens a copy of Klara in configuration $(S_N, a_N, T^{O}_N) = (S'_N, a'_N, T'_N)$ — Klara's own configuration at step $N$. A duplicate of Klara governed by the same $\delta$, started in the same configuration, runs step-for-step identically from there on. Together with the loop steps, the whole computations coincide.

\emph{Case 2: $t^\ast < N$.} Steps $0, \dots, t^\ast - 1$ coincide by the induction, and $I(t^\ast)$ holds. $\texttt{guard}_{t^\ast}$ fires \emph{before} $\texttt{write}_{t^\ast}$ executes, so the tape is still $T^{O}_{t^\ast} = T'_{t^\ast}$, and $\mathrm{DEOPT}(t^\ast)$ awakens a copy of Klara in configuration $(S_{t^\ast}, a_{t^\ast}, T'_{t^\ast})$ — by $I(t^\ast)$ exactly the configuration Klara-on-$T_0$ occupies at step $t^\ast$. Determinism again: identical configurations have identical futures. So the runs coincide from $t^\ast$ onward too. $\blacksquare$

\begin{plainterms}
Modern language runtimes speed programs up with a trick called a tracing JIT (``just-in-time'') compiler. Watch the program run once on real data and write down everything it actually did; replace the program with a dumb, super-fast replay of exactly those steps; and at every point where the replay silently \emph{assumed} something about the data, insert a cheap check — ``is this value still what it was last time?'' If a check ever fails, the replay is abandoned and the original, slow, fully general program takes over from that exact point. Maudlin's Olympia, described in the 1980s, is this trick built out of water troughs and clockwork: the armature is the dumb replay, the floats are the checks, and the frozen copies of Klara are the fully general program waiting to take over. This entry proves the folklore carefully: the replay-plus-checks machine really does behave exactly like the original computer on \emph{every} possible input, so by the standard definition it really is running the same program — which is the load-bearing step in Maudlin's Olympia argument (see \hyperref[entry:argument-olympia]{\textsc{Olympia argument}}) against the idea that consciousness is just computation.

But the proof also shows the fine print. The checks are not decoration: take them away and the machine provably stops counting as running the program. And a check \emph{runs} every single time, even when it passes — asking ``is this value what I expected?'' and getting \emph{yes} is still asking. Maudlin needs those passed checks to count as the machine doing \emph{nothing}; stated computationally, they look instead like the one place where the machine is still genuinely reading its input. That doubt is why this reconstruction is marked contested.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-olympia]{\textsc{Olympia argument}} — the argument whose Klara→Olympia construction step this entry formalizes and (as a matter of implementation) vindicates.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-implementation-requires-counterfactual-structure]{\textsc{Counterfactual constraint on implementation}} — the criterion of implementation the theorem discharges; premise 1 of this reconstruction.
  \item \hyperref[source:source-maudlin-1989]{\textsc{Maudlin — Computation and Consciousness (1989)}} — the source of the construction; footnotes 6 and 7 of the paper fix, respectively, the constant-function reading of the bare armature and the guard-before-write ordering the proof relies on.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-decomposition-indeterminacy]{\textsc{Decomposition-indeterminacy objection}} — the observer-relativity worry that bears on the disjunctive state mapping σ.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-lookup-table-mind]{\textsc{Lookup-table mind}} — the kindred replay structure: Blockhead pre-computes a table over \emph{all} possible inputs, where Olympia records the run on \emph{one} input and guards the rest; both raise the question whether canned behaviour plus dispositional backup amounts to the real computation.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{claim-mind-handles-unbounded-inputs}{Open-ended responsiveness of minds}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{Open-ended responsiveness of minds}{A genuine mind can respond appropriately to novel inputs beyond any fixed finite stock}{$\odot$}
A genuine mind can respond appropriately to inputs it has never met before — longer, more complex, or recombined in new ways — by working through their structure rather than matching them to a pre-stored whole. Its responsive capacity is \emph{productive}, or open-ended: confronted with the very inputs a finite table must miss (see \hyperref[entry:claim-finite-table-misses-inputs]{\textsc{Finite table misses inputs}}), a mind does not simply discard the excess.

\webheading{Scope}

This is an \emph{asymmetry} claim: a mind out-responds a finite table on inputs outside the table's domain. It does not claim that minds are infinite or unbounded in capacity — only that for any \emph{fixed} finite table the mind's competence outruns it on some inputs.

\webheading{Who would deny it}

A defender of Ned Block's ``Blockhead'' (see \hyperref[source:source-block]{\textsc{Block — Psychologism and Behaviorism (1981)}}). Block stipulates a table covering every input up to a length exceeding any the subject would receive in a normal lifetime, then argues that (i) within that bounded domain the table matches the mind, and (ii) a real mind is \emph{also} finite — past its own memory and attention limits it too ``ignores part of the input'' — so the asymmetry vanishes over the only domain that matters. This is the weakest link of \hyperref[entry:argument-lookup-table-combinatorial-impossibility]{\textsc{the combinatorial-impossibility reply}}, and why that argument's conclusion is held \texttt{contested}.

It should be distinguished from \hyperref[entry:claim-behaviour-insufficient-for-mind]{\textsc{behaviour insufficient for mind}} (about whether matching behaviour suffices for mind): this is the prior, factual claim that a finite table cannot even \emph{match} an open-ended mind's behaviour. It differs too from \hyperref[entry:claim-role-grain-is-organisational]{\textsc{Organisational role-grain}}, which individuates minds by internal organisation; this individuates by input–output competence over an unbounded input space, not by internals.

\begin{plainterms}
A real mind keeps up with an endless variety of new inputs — sentences and situations it has never met before — by actually working through their structure, not by recognising them off a stored list. Hand it something longer or stranger than anything it has seen, and it can still answer sensibly, where a fixed table just draws a blank. (It does not claim minds are limitless — only that for any finite stored table, a mind can handle some inputs the table cannot.)
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-lookup-table-mind]{\textsc{Lookup-table mind}} — the stored-answer machine this claim says a mind outruns.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-lookup-table-combinatorial-impossibility]{\textsc{Combinatorial-impossibility reply}} — the reply for which this is the key (and contested) premise.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-finite-table-misses-inputs]{\textsc{Finite table misses inputs}} — the companion claim about what any finite table must miss.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-behaviour-insufficient-for-mind]{\textsc{Behaviour insufficient for mind}} — distinguished: that is about sufficiency for mind; this is the prior factual claim about matching behaviour.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-role-grain-is-organisational]{\textsc{Organisational role-grain}} — distinguished: it individuates by internal organisation; this by input–output competence.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{claim-organisational-grain-faces-own-grain-dilemma}{Organisational grain's own dilemma}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{Organisational grain's own dilemma}{The organisational-role grain either readmits the blockhead or smuggles sub-organisational features back in}{$\odot$}
Against a \emph{dense China-brain} — a population each of whose members implements, with its counterfactuals preserved, one node of the target system's state-to-state transition structure (not a flat lookup map, but a by-construction instance of that structure) — organisational-role functionalism (see \hyperref[entry:claim-role-grain-is-organisational]{\textsc{the organisational role-grain}}) confronts a dilemma isomorphic to the one it was built to escape.

On the first horn, the dense China-brain \emph{has} a mind — in which case the organisational grain has readmitted the very absurdity it advertised excluding. (The original worry was never about lookup tables specifically; lookup was merely its crudest instance.) On the second horn, it \emph{lacks} a mind despite sharing the transition structure — in which case two systems share the grain yet differ in mind, and the individuative difference must lie in features \emph{below} the transition structure: speed, causal density, medium, continuity of the connecting ``glue.'' At that point the grain has slid back toward the biological. Either horn defeats the claim that the organisational grain is a principled middle.

This is a claim about the \emph{organisational} grain specifically, not about coarse input–output or biological microstructure. It asserts that the grain faces a renewed liberal/chauvinist dilemma. It does \emph{not} assert that silicon cannot be conscious, nor that the blockhead \emph{is} conscious; it asserts only that the grain cannot deliver a non-arbitrary verdict either way without paying one of the two prices.

It would be denied by organisational-role functionalists who hold that the dense China-brain either determinately fails the grain on purely organisational grounds or determinately has a mind without absurdity, and by anyone who denies that the China-brain is a genuine instance of the transition structure.

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-residue]{\textsc{the phenomenal-residue claim}}, which says role under-determines phenomenal character generally: this is a narrower structural claim about one proposed \emph{grain} of role, and would hold even if some other grain settled the phenomenal. Distinct from the content of \hyperref[entry:argument-role-grain-dilemma]{\textsc{the original grain dilemma}}, which ran over coarse-versus-biological-fine and was answered by the organisational middle: this claim is the regress that returns the dilemma \emph{one level up}, against that very middle — the conclusion the original dilemma's exhaustiveness premise was missing.

\begin{plainterms}
Someone proposes that what makes a mind is its inner pattern of cause-and-effect (see \hyperref[entry:claim-role-grain-is-organisational]{\textsc{Organisational role-grain}}) — loose enough to allow silicon, strict enough to shut out a dumb cheat-sheet. This claim says that proposal gets caught in the very trap it tried to escape. Build a giant crowd that genuinely carries out that exact inner pattern by hand. Either it counts as having a mind — and now an absurd flag-waving crowd is conscious, the thing we wanted to rule out — or it doesn't, in which case the real difference must come from something \emph{beneath} the pattern (speed, physical material), which quietly smuggles biology back in. So that ``inner pattern'' level isn't the clean middle ground it claimed to be.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-role-grain-is-organisational]{\textsc{Organisational role-grain}} — the middle grain this claim says is not stable.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-dense-china-brain-regress]{\textsc{Dense China-brain regress}} — the argument that concludes to this claim.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-role-grain-dilemma]{\textsc{Grain dilemma}} — the original dilemma this one reinstates a level up.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-residue]{\textsc{Phenomenal residue}} — the broader role-underdetermination worry this narrower structural claim is distinguished from.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-chinese-nation]{\textsc{Chinese nation}} — the population-as-mind construction the dense version elaborates.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{claim-role-grain-is-organisational}{Organisational role-grain}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{Organisational role-grain}{The mind-individuating functional role is fixed at the grain of internal counterfactual-causal organisation}{$\odot$}
The functional role by which a mental state is individuated is specified neither at the coarse grain of gross input–output behaviour nor at the fine grain of the physical realiser's microchemistry, but at the \emph{intermediate} grain of the system's internal counterfactual-causal organisation — the structure of state-to-state transitions among its components. Physical details such as spike timing, neuromodulator diffusion, or transistor physics matter to the role only insofar as they make a counterfactual difference to that transition structure.

This is a claim about the \emph{correct grain of role-individuation} for \hyperref[entry:concept-functionalism]{\textsc{Functionalism}}, not about whether any particular system meets it. It asserts that the grain is both substrate-neutral, hence multiply realisable in silicon, and demanding of internal organisation, hence not satisfied by mere input–output mimicry. It does not by itself say that any given machine — a language model, or the blockhead — meets or fails the grain; those are downstream applications.

It would be denied by defenders of pure input–output (machine-table or behaviourist) functionalism; by fine-grained chauvinists who hold that biologically specific microstructure is individuative; and by anyone who holds that \hyperref[entry:argument-role-grain-dilemma]{\textsc{the grain dilemma}} is exhaustive between coarse and biological-fine, with no principled middle.

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:concept-functionalism]{\textsc{Functionalism}} proper, which leaves the grain of ``role'' open (listing machine-state, role, and commonsense senses): this claim \emph{fixes} the grain at one level and motivates the choice. Distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-substrate-independence]{\textsc{substrate independence}}, which asserts that substrate does no individuative work: this asserts \emph{which level of organisation} does the work instead, and is the specification that, if true, secures substrate independence against the grain dilemma.

\begin{plainterms}
This picks the ``just right'' level of detail for defining a mind. Not too coarse (plain input-and-output behavior — that would let a dumb cheat-sheet count) and not too fine (exact brain chemistry — that would shut out every computer). The sweet spot in between: a mind \emph{is} its inner pattern of cause-and-effect — which inner states trigger which others. Physical details matter only insofar as they change that pattern. The payoff it's aiming for: the pattern can run on silicon (so machines aren't ruled out) yet a mere answer-fetcher lacks it (so behavior-fakers are ruled out).
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-grain]{\textsc{Grain (of role individuation)}} — the dial this claim sets to its middle position.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-role-grain-dilemma]{\textsc{Grain dilemma}} — the dilemma this grain is meant to thread.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-organisational-grain-dissolves-dilemma]{\textsc{Organisational-grain middle}} — the argument that deploys this claim as its premise against that dilemma.
  \item \hyperref[entry:question-substrate-independence]{\textsc{Substrate independence}} — the question this claim answers.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{argument-organisational-grain-dissolves-dilemma}{Organisational-grain middle}{Argument \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{Organisational-grain middle}{The organisational grain is a principled middle that escapes both horns of the grain dilemma}{$\vdash$}
The \emph{organisational-grain middle} is a rebuttal to \hyperref[entry:argument-role-grain-dilemma]{\textsc{the grain dilemma}}. That dilemma succeeds only if the ways of specifying a functional role are exhausted by ``coarse input–output'' and ``biological microstructure,'' leaving no middle ground. This argument denies that by supplying the middle, so the dilemma's exhaustiveness premise fails and \hyperref[entry:claim-substrate-independence]{\textsc{substrate independence}} is left standing.

\begin{enumerate}
  \item The mind-individuating role is fixed at the grain of internal counterfactual-causal organisation — the state-to-state transition structure among a system's components (see \hyperref[entry:claim-role-grain-is-organisational]{\textsc{organisational role-grain}}). This is a third option, distinct from both gross input–output and physical-realiser microchemistry.
  \item \emph{It escapes horn 1 (too liberal / the blockhead).} A \hyperref[entry:concept-lookup-table-mind]{\textsc{lookup table}} and the homunculi-headed \hyperref[entry:concept-chinese-nation]{\textsc{Chinese Nation}} realise the input–output profile precisely \emph{by lacking} the internal transition structure: the lookup table is a flat query→answer map with no mediating states in the right counterfactual web, and the China-brain mimics gross behaviour without the dense intra-system dependencies. They fail the organisational grain, excluded by a fact about \emph{causal topology}, not about substrate.
  \item \emph{It escapes horn 2 (too chauvinistic / collapse toward proteocentrism).} Transition structure is multiply realisable: silicon that reproduces it realises the role, and a molecular-twin brain is sufficient but not necessary. Carbon does no individuative work, so there is no collapse toward \hyperref[entry:position-proteocentrism]{\textsc{Proteocentrism}}.
  \item \emph{The line is principled, not gerrymandered for the conclusion.} It is independently the grain at which the explanatory and predictive generalisations of psychology and computational neuroscience quantify — gross input–output is too coarse to distinguish a calculator from an arithmetician, while carbon chemistry is too fine to figure in any cognitive generalisation. The scientifically load-bearing grain and the proposed grain coincide, and that coincidence is a reason to draw the line here that owes nothing to wanting silicon to qualify.
\end{enumerate}

Therefore the dilemma's exhaustiveness premise is false: substrate independence has a grain coarse enough for silicon, fine enough to exclude the blockhead, and motivated independently of the verdict it delivers. This is the principled intermediate grain the grain dilemma demanded.

\webheading{Weakest premise}

Premise 2 — whether the blockhead \emph{really} fails the organisational grain, or whether a sufficiently elaborated lookup or China system can be redescribed as instantiating the requisite transition structure after all. If it can, horn 1 reopens. This is precisely the opening pressed by \hyperref[entry:argument-dense-china-brain-regress]{\textsc{the dense China-brain regress}}.

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:argument-gap-deflated-by-phenomenal-concepts]{\textsc{the phenomenal-concepts reply}}, which addresses the phenomenal residue (what role leaves out): this argument addresses the individuation of role itself, and applies even to intentional states.

\begin{plainterms}
This answers the dilemma that says no good level of detail exists (see \hyperref[entry:argument-role-grain-dilemma]{\textsc{Grain dilemma}}) by offering one. That trap: describe a mind too loosely and a mere cheat-sheet machine qualifies; describe it too tightly and only biological brains qualify. The middle option proposed here: describe a mind by its \emph{inner wiring of cause-and-effect} — which inner states trigger which others, and how — rather than by its outward behavior or by its exact biology. That middle does both jobs at once. It shuts out the cheat-sheet, which has no such inner wiring (it just maps questions straight to stored answers). And it lets in silicon, because the same wiring pattern can be rebuilt in chips: the carbon was never doing the real work. And it isn't rigged to favor machines — it just happens to be the very level brain science and psychology already use to explain how minds work. The soft spot: maybe a clever enough cheat-sheet-style system \emph{could} be re-described as having the right inner wiring after all, and if so the cheat-sheet sneaks back in.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-role-grain-is-organisational]{\textsc{Organisational role-grain}} — the premise this argument deploys.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-role-grain-dilemma]{\textsc{Grain dilemma}} — the dilemma it answers.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-substrate-independence]{\textsc{Substrate-independence thesis}} — what it defends.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-dense-china-brain-regress]{\textsc{Dense China-brain regress}} — the counter-reply that says even this middle fails.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-gap-deflated-by-phenomenal-concepts]{\textsc{Phenomenal-concept deflation}} — a separate defence, aimed at the phenomenal residue rather than role-individuation.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{claim-organizational-invariance}{Organizational invariance}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, after Chalmers}{Organizational invariance}{Systems with the same fine-grained functional organization have qualitatively identical experiences}{$\odot$}
The \emph{Principle of Organizational Invariance} holds that conscious experience is fixed by a system's \emph{fine-grained functional organization} — the abstract pattern of causal interactions among its components, specified down to whatever level is causally relevant — so that any two systems sharing that organization have \emph{qualitatively identical} experiences, whatever they are physically made of. Replace each neuron with a silicon chip of identical causal profile and, if the principle holds, the experience is preserved exactly: same colours, same pains, nothing faded, absent, or inverted. David Chalmers defends the principle with the \hyperref[entry:argument-fading-qualia]{\textsc{fading qualia}} and \hyperref[entry:argument-dancing-qualia]{\textsc{dancing qualia}} arguments (\hyperref[source:source-chalmers-1995b]{\textsc{Chalmers — Absent Qualia, Fading Qualia, Dancing Qualia (1995)}}; \hyperref[source:source-chalmers-1996]{\textsc{Chalmers — The Conscious Mind (1996)}}, ch. 7).

\textbf{Scope, and a subtle point about its modal force.} The principle is a claim of \emph{nomological} (law-governed) determination, not metaphysical necessity. Chalmers, a property dualist, holds that \hyperref[entry:claim-zombie-conceivable]{\textsc{zombies are conceivable}} and that the phenomenal is \emph{metaphysically} distinct from the functional (see \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-residue]{\textsc{Phenomenal residue}}) — yet maintains that, given the actual psychophysical laws, experience cannot come apart from fine-grained organization. Determination is not constitution: the principle says organization \emph{fixes} qualia in our world, not that qualia are \emph{nothing over and above} organization. This is why it sits with, rather than against, Chalmers's anti-reductionism.

\textbf{What it entails and who denies it.} If true, the principle delivers \hyperref[entry:claim-substrate-independence]{\textsc{Substrate-independence thesis}} for consciousness — silicon, if organized like a brain, is conscious like a brain. It is denied by \emph{substrate chauvinists} and biological essentialists (see \hyperref[entry:position-proteocentrism]{\textsc{Proteocentrism}}, \hyperref[entry:position-biological-naturalism]{\textsc{Biological naturalism}}), who hold that the right organization in the wrong stuff yields absent or different qualia — exactly the possibility the fading- and dancing-qualia arguments attack.

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-substrate-independence]{\textsc{Substrate-independence thesis}}: that thesis (Putnam) says the \emph{substrate is irrelevant} because mental states are individuated by role; organizational invariance is the more specific positive principle naming \emph{what} is relevant — fine-grained causal organization — and arguing it \emph{suffices for qualia}. It is the engine that, if sound, drives substrate-independence for the phenomenal case in particular.

\begin{plainterms}
The idea: what gives you your exact experiences is the \emph{pattern of activity} in your brain — what affects what — not the stuff the pattern runs on. Keep the pattern identical but swap neurons for equivalent silicon chips, and your experience stays exactly the same: red stays red, pain still hurts. If that's right, then a machine wired up like a brain would feel like a brain. (Note the careful version: this says the pattern \emph{settles} your experience in the actual world — not that your experience is \emph{merely} a pattern with nothing extra to it, a further question Chalmers keeps open.)
\end{plainterms}

\entryhead{argument-other-minds-reply}{Other minds reply}{Argument}{Other minds reply}{The Other Minds Reply — we attribute understanding to others on behavioural grounds, so deny it to the room only by a double standard}{$\vdash$}
The \emph{Other Minds Reply} turns the problem of other minds against Searle. How do we know \emph{other people} understand? Only from their behaviour — what they say and do. The Chinese Room, by hypothesis, produces behaviour indistinguishable from a Chinese speaker's. So if behavioural evidence is good enough to credit \emph{humans} with understanding, consistency demands we credit the room too; to withhold it is an arbitrary double standard, a refusal to apply our only actual criterion for minds.

The reply attacks the \hyperref[entry:argument-chinese-room-derivation]{\textsc{derivation}} by challenging our entitlement to the verdict that drives A3: if the sole basis for attributing understanding anywhere is behaviour, then the intuition ``the room doesn't \emph{really} understand'' is unwarranted — we have no mind-detector that reaches past behaviour, so there is no standpoint from which to deny the room what we grant each other.

\webheading{The crux}

Searle's \hyperref[entry:argument-rejoinder-attribution-vs-detection]{\textsc{attribution-versus-detection rejoinder}} is that the reply changes the subject. The Chinese Room is about \emph{what understanding is} (its metaphysics), not \emph{how we find out} who has it (its epistemology). That we \emph{detect} others' minds behaviourally does not show that minds \emph{consist in} behaviour; the argument grants we might be fooled, and its point is precisely that the behaviour could be present while the understanding is absent. The reply's defender answers that without \emph{some} link between the criterion and the thing, ``understanding'' floats free of anything we could ever have reason to attribute — reviving a verificationist pressure Searle must resist.

\begin{plainterms}
How do you know anyone \emph{else} really understands anything? You don't peek inside their mind — you just go by how they talk and act. But the room talks and acts exactly like a fluent speaker. So if behaviour is good enough to credit your friends with understanding, why not the room? Denying it looks like a rigged game. Searle's answer: the question isn't \emph{how we tell} who understands — it's \emph{what understanding is}. We read minds from behaviour because we have to, but that doesn't make understanding \emph{the same thing as} behaving; the whole point is that the behaviour could be there with nobody home.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-chinese-room-derivation]{\textsc{Chinese room derivation}} — the argument attacked, via our entitlement to its key verdict.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-rejoinder-attribution-vs-detection]{\textsc{Attribution vs. detection}} — Searle's reply: how we detect minds ≠ what minds are.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-strong-weak-ai]{\textsc{Strong and weak AI}} — the behavioural criterion at issue connects to the Turing-test tradition.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-turing-test]{\textsc{Turing test}} — the operationalisation of the behavioural criterion this reply leans on.
\end{seealso}

\end{multicols}
\bookgroup{P}
\begin{multicols}{2}
\entryhead{concept-pain-asymbolia}{Pain asymbolia}{Concept}{Pain asymbolia}{Pain asymbolia (pain felt but not minded)}{$\blacklozenge$}
\emph{Pain asymbolia} is a rare neurological condition, typically following damage to the insula or its connections, in which a patient still feels pain but is no longer bothered by it. Asymbolics detect a noxious stimulus, localise it, and describe its sensory quality as sharp or burning, yet show none of the normal reaction: they do not withdraw, wince, or call it unpleasant, and may report the pain calmly or even with amusement. The clinical landmark is Berthier, Starkstein, and Leiguarda's study of six such patients, which characterised the syndrome as a \emph{sensory-limbic disconnection} — the sensory pathways that register tissue damage are intact, but their link to the affective systems that make damage \emph{matter} has been severed (see \hyperref[source:source-berthier-1988]{\textsc{Berthier et al. — Asymbolia for pain (1988)}}).

\webheading{Feeling pain versus being in pain}

The philosophical interest of asymbolia is that it prises apart two things ordinary pain fuses. Nikola Grahek frames the split as \emph{feeling pain} — the sensory-discriminative experience the asymbolic keeps — versus \emph{being in pain} — the affective-motivational state, the felt awfulness and the urge to make it stop, which the asymbolic loses (see \hyperref[source:source-grahek-2007]{\textsc{Grahek — Feeling Pain and Being in Pain (2007)}}). The case is thus a real-world dissociation of pain from its characteristic behaviour, and so belongs beside the behaviourist thought experiments of this web. But it cuts along a different seam. The \hyperref[entry:concept-super-spartan]{\textsc{Super-spartans}} still finds the pain awful and merely \emph{suppresses} the behaviour by an act of will; the asymbolic does not find it awful at all, so the behaviour is \emph{absent at the source}. And unlike the \hyperref[entry:concept-perfect-actor]{\textsc{Perfect actor}}, who has the behaviour without any pain, the asymbolic has the sensation without the behaviour-driving affect.

\webheading{The dispute it feeds}

Asymbolia is pressed on the question of whether pain is \emph{essentially} unpleasant — whether the ``badness'' of pain is built into the sensation or is a separable affective add-on. If asymbolia is a case of feeling a pain that is genuinely not bad, then unpleasantness is not intrinsic to pain, and theories tying pain's nature to its felt awfulness need revision. Critics reply that asymbolia may not be ``pure'' — that what is lost is partly the pain itself, not merely an attitude toward it — so that the case underdetermines the metaphysics it is summoned to settle.

\begin{plainterms}
Some people, after a particular kind of brain injury, can still feel pain — they will tell you exactly where it is and that it is sharp — but it simply doesn't trouble them. They don't pull away, don't mind it, sometimes laugh about it. This is strange because we normally think the \emph{hurt} and the \emph{sensation} are one thing. Asymbolia suggests they are two: a detecting part that says ``damage here'' and a caring part that says ``and it's awful, do something''. It is not the \hyperref[entry:concept-super-spartan]{\textsc{Super-spartans}}, who feels the full awfulness but hides it by willpower; here the awfulness itself is missing while the bare sensation stays.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-super-spartan]{\textsc{Super-spartans}} — feels the pain's awfulness fully but suppresses the behaviour by will, where the asymbolic loses the awfulness itself.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-perfect-actor]{\textsc{Perfect actor}} — the converse dissociation: pain-behaviour with no pain at all.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-jo-cameron]{\textsc{Jo Cameron}} — a real case at the far end: not pain stripped of its affect, but pain largely absent from the start.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-phenomenal-consciousness]{\textsc{Phenomenal consciousness}} — the felt character at issue; asymbolia asks whether pain's unpleasantness is part of that character or a separable layer.
  \item \hyperref[source:source-berthier-1988]{\textsc{Berthier et al. — Asymbolia for pain (1988)}} — the clinical study naming the syndrome a sensory-limbic disconnection.
  \item \hyperref[source:source-grahek-2007]{\textsc{Grahek — Feeling Pain and Being in Pain (2007)}} — the philosophical framing: feeling pain versus being in pain.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{concept-panpsychism}{Panpsychism}{Concept}{Panpsychism}{}{$\blacklozenge$}
\emph{Panpsychism} is the view that consciousness is a fundamental and ubiquitous feature of the physical world — that mind, or something mind-like, is present at the most basic level of nature rather than appearing only in brains. In its most discussed modern form it does \textbf{not} claim that rocks ponder or that electrons have thoughts; it claims that the simplest physical constituents carry some rudimentary form of experience, and that the rich consciousness of a human or an animal is built up from these elementary experiential ingredients. The word, from the Greek \emph{pan} (all) and \emph{psychē} (soul or mind), is old — versions appear among the Presocratics, in Spinoza, in Leibniz, and in William James — but the view has had a vigorous revival in analytic philosophy of mind as a proposed escape from the \hyperref[entry:concept-hard-problem]{\textsc{hard problem of consciousness}}.

This entry defines the doctrine and surveys its varieties. The arguments for and against it are developed as their own nodes and linked below; the committed \emph{position} that adopts panpsychism as its answer to the hard problem — what it commits to and what it denies — is recorded at \hyperref[entry:position-panpsychism]{\textsc{Panpsychism (position)}}.

\webheading{What the view reacts to}

Panpsychism is best understood as a third path between the two dominant options. \emph{Physicalism} holds that consciousness is nothing over and above physical processing, and faces the objection that no account of mere processing seems to explain why there is felt experience at all. \emph{Dualism} adds consciousness as a further, non-physical ingredient, and faces the objection that it leaves mind and matter mysteriously disconnected. Panpsychism agrees with the dualist that experience is fundamental — not reducible to non-experiential facts — but agrees with the physicalist that it belongs to the natural, physical world rather than arriving from outside it. Experience, on this view, was in the ingredients from the start; the brain does not conjure it at some threshold of complexity, it organizes what was already there.

\webheading{Varieties}

Panpsychism is a family, not a single thesis, and several distinctions sort its members. The taxonomy below largely follows David Chalmers \hyperref[source:source-chalmers-2017]{\textsc{Chalmers — Panpsychism and Panprotopsychism (2017)}}.

\begin{itemize}
