  \item \textbf{Micropsychism vs. cosmopsychism} — whether the fundamental conscious things are the \emph{smallest} parts, with big minds composed bottom-up, or the \emph{whole} cosmos, with individual minds carved out of it top-down. The cosmic version is \hyperref[entry:position-cosmopsychism]{\textsc{Cosmopsychism}}.
  \item \textbf{Constitutive vs. emergentist} — whether macro-experience is \emph{constituted by} (grounded in, made up of) micro-experience, or instead \emph{strongly emerges} from it as a novel further fact. Constitutive forms are the ones that promise to avoid brute emergence; emergentist forms keep an emergence step.
  \item \textbf{Panpsychism vs. panprotopsychism} — whether the fundamental properties are themselves \emph{experiential}, or merely \emph{proto-experiential}: not yet consciousness, but the special non-structural ingredients from which consciousness can be constituted. Panprotopsychism is the more cautious cousin, and shades into \hyperref[entry:position-russellian-monism]{\textsc{Russellian monism}}.
  \item \textbf{Panexperientialism vs. panqualityism} — whether what is ubiquitous is full \emph{subjective experience} (a point of view, a felt field) or merely unexperienced \emph{qualities}, with subjecthood added later.
\end{itemize}

Constitutive Russellian micropsychism — fundamental physical entities have experiential intrinsic natures, and our minds are constituted from them — is the version most defended today, by Galen Strawson and Philip Goff (technically in \hyperref[source:source-goff-2017]{\textsc{Goff — Consciousness and Fundamental Reality (2017)}}, for a general readership in \hyperref[source:source-goff-2019]{\textsc{Goff — Galileo's Error: Foundations for a New Science of Consciousness (2019)}}) among others.

\webheading{The case for it}

Three lines of argument recur, each developed in its own node.

\begin{itemize}
  \item The \textbf{\hyperref[entry:argument-genetic-panpsychism]{\textsc{genetic argument}}} (Strawson): a physicalist who is also a realist about experience must place experience at the fundamental level, on pain of accepting that it emerges brutely — unintelligibly — from the wholly non-experiential.
  \item The \textbf{\hyperref[entry:argument-no-arbitrary-line-panpsychism]{\textsc{continuity argument}}} (Nagel): there is no non-arbitrary point at which experience could ``switch on,'' so the line-free option is that it reaches all the way down.
  \item The \textbf{\hyperref[entry:argument-structure-and-dynamics]{\textsc{structure-and-dynamics argument}}} (the Russellian route): physics describes only structure and dynamics, leaving the intrinsic categorical nature of matter blank — and consciousness is the natural candidate to fill it.
\end{itemize}

\webheading{Objections}

\begin{itemize}
  \item The \textbf{\hyperref[entry:argument-combination-problem]{\textsc{combination problem}}} (James, Coleman) is the standing difficulty: if the smallest things each have their own experience and point of view, how do countless such micro-subjects compose the \emph{single, unified} experience you are having now? Many regard this as panpsychism's make-or-break test, and note that it re-imposes, at the micro-to-macro step, the very explanatory burden the view was meant to discharge.
  \item The \textbf{\hyperref[entry:argument-strong-emergence-acceptable]{\textsc{emergence reply}}} presses the genetic argument's weak point: if strong emergence of experience is acceptable, there is no need to make mind fundamental, and the argument's central dilemma dissolves.
  \item Two looser worries are often added. The \emph{incredulous stare}: crediting electrons with any inner life is an extravagant cost in plausibility. And \emph{no empirical traction}: attributing experience to fundamental physics makes, by itself, no distinctive prediction. Defenders answer that every account of consciousness is strange somewhere, and that the hard problem is precisely the part of mind no empirical theory addresses head-on.
\end{itemize}

\begin{plainterms}
Panpsychism says consciousness goes all the way down. Not that rocks daydream — the serious version says the tiniest bits of matter carry the faintest spark of experience, and a brain assembles those sparks into a full mind. The appeal: you no longer have to explain how feeling gets conjured out of utterly feelingless stuff at some magic moment, because it was in the building blocks all along. The big catch, called the \emph{combination problem}: if every speck has its own tiny experience, how do trillions of them merge into the one seamless experience you're having as you read this? Critics also worry it just swaps one mystery for another, and that crediting electrons with any inner life is a stretch. Supporters answer that every theory of consciousness is strange somewhere, and at least this one keeps mind a natural part of the world.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:position-panpsychism]{\textsc{Panpsychism (position)}} — the committed position that adopts panpsychism as its answer to the hard problem, and how it differs from neighbouring views.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-genetic-panpsychism]{\textsc{Genetic argument}} — the headline argument \emph{for} the view: real experience plus no brute emergence entails fundamental mind.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-combination-problem]{\textsc{Combination problem}} — the central objection: micro-subjects cannot sum into one macro-subject.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-hard-problem]{\textsc{Hard problem}} — the problem panpsychism is offered to solve, by placing experience at the fundamental level rather than deriving it.
  \item \hyperref[entry:question-hard-problem]{\textsc{Hard problem (the question)}} — the open question panpsychism answers.
  \item \hyperref[entry:position-russellian-monism]{\textsc{Russellian monism}} — the closely allied view that consciousness is the intrinsic categorical nature physics leaves unspecified; constitutive panpsychism is one form of it.
  \item \hyperref[entry:position-cosmopsychism]{\textsc{Cosmopsychism}} — the top-down cousin, on which the cosmos is the one fundamental subject and individual minds are carved out of it.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-consciousness-fundamental-ubiquitous]{\textsc{Fundamental ubiquitous consciousness}} — the core claim panpsychism asserts, distinguished from the dualist's ``fundamental but non-physical.''
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{position-panpsychism}{Panpsychism (position)}{Position}{Panpsychism (position)}{Panpsychism}{$\bigstar$}
\emph{Panpsychism}, as a committed position in the philosophy of mind, holds that consciousness is a fundamental and ubiquitous feature of the physical world, and adopts that thesis as its answer to the problem of how experience fits into nature. It claims that the rich consciousness of a human or animal is not conjured from wholly mindless matter at some threshold of complexity, but is \emph{constituted} by the experiential — or proto-experiential — properties already carried by that matter's simplest constituents. The view is set out as a doctrine, with its varieties and arguments, in the concept entry \hyperref[entry:concept-panpsychism]{\textsc{Panpsychism}}; this entry records what the position commits to and how it stands against its neighbours.

The position's core commitment is that \hyperref[entry:claim-consciousness-fundamental-ubiquitous]{\textsc{consciousness is fundamental and present in the simplest physical entities}}. Because macro-minds are built up from the experiential natures of their micro-parts, panpsychism needs neither the dualist's non-physical addition nor the physicalist's ``strong emergence of feeling from the feelingless.'' It answers the \hyperref[entry:question-hard-problem]{\textsc{hard problem of consciousness}} by denying that physics must \emph{produce} experience at all: experience is there at the base, and the question becomes how it is organized rather than how it is generated. Its modern champions include Galen Strawson (see \hyperref[source:source-strawson-2006]{\textsc{Strawson — Realistic Monism: Why Physicalism Entails Panpsychism (2006)}}) and Philip Goff (\hyperref[source:source-goff-2017]{\textsc{Goff — Consciousness and Fundamental Reality (2017)}}, for a general readership \hyperref[source:source-goff-2019]{\textsc{Goff — Galileo's Error: Foundations for a New Science of Consciousness (2019)}}).

\webheading{How it differs from neighbouring views}

Against \hyperref[entry:position-dualism]{\textsc{Dualism}}, consciousness is fundamental but \emph{intrinsic to the physical}, not a non-physical extra disconnected from matter. Against physicalism and \hyperref[entry:position-illusionism]{\textsc{Illusionism}}, it is firmly realist about experience: there is genuine felt consciousness to distribute, not an illusion to explain away. Its relation to \hyperref[entry:position-russellian-monism]{\textsc{Russellian monism}} is closest of all — \emph{constitutive} panpsychism simply \emph{is} a Russellian view on which the intrinsic natures physics leaves blank turn out to be experiential, while the more cautious \emph{panprotopsychism} makes them merely proto-experiential.

The position's standing weak point is the \hyperref[entry:argument-combination-problem]{\textsc{combination problem}}: if each micro-part has its own experience and point of view, how do countless micro-subjects compose the single, unified subject having your experience now? Many regard this as panpsychism's make-or-break test.

\begin{plainterms}
Panpsychism says consciousness goes all the way down: not that rocks have thoughts, but that the basic bits of matter carry the tiniest glimmer of experience, and brains assemble those glimmers into full-blown minds. The appeal: you don't have to conjure feeling out of utterly feelingless stuff at some magic threshold — it was in the ingredients from the start. The catch, its \emph{combination problem}: how do countless tiny experiences add up to the single unified experience you're having right now?
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-panpsychism]{\textsc{Panpsychism}} — the doctrine itself, with its full taxonomy of varieties and the arguments for and against; this position is its committed form.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-consciousness-fundamental-ubiquitous]{\textsc{Fundamental ubiquitous consciousness}} — the core thesis the position commits to.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-residue]{\textsc{Phenomenal residue}} — the position's further commitment that felt states are not exhausted by their functional role.
  \item \hyperref[entry:question-hard-problem]{\textsc{Hard problem (the question)}} — the problem the position is offered to solve.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-genetic-panpsychism]{\textsc{Genetic argument}} — the headline argument for the view: real experience plus no brute emergence entails fundamental mind.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-combination-problem]{\textsc{Combination problem}} — the central objection the position must answer.
  \item \hyperref[entry:position-russellian-monism]{\textsc{Russellian monism}} — the allied view that consciousness is the intrinsic nature physics leaves unspecified; constitutive panpsychism is one form of it.
  \item \hyperref[entry:position-cosmopsychism]{\textsc{Cosmopsychism}} — the top-down cousin, on which the cosmos is the one fundamental subject and individual minds are carved out of it.
  \item \hyperref[entry:position-dualism]{\textsc{Dualism}} — the rival on which consciousness is fundamental but non-physical.
  \item \hyperref[entry:position-illusionism]{\textsc{Illusionism}} — the rival that denies there is any fundamental experience to distribute.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{concept-paradox-of-phenomenal-judgment}{Paradox of phenomenal judgment}{Concept \,\textperiodcentered\, after Chalmers}{Paradox of phenomenal judgment}{The paradox of phenomenal judgment}{$\blacklozenge$}
A \emph{phenomenal judgment} is a judgment about conscious experience — the belief, thought, or report that one is conscious, that there is ``something it is like'' to see red, or that consciousness resists physical explanation. The \textbf{paradox of phenomenal judgment} (David Chalmers, \emph{The Conscious Mind}, 1996, \hyperref[source:source-chalmers-1996]{\textsc{Chalmers — The Conscious Mind (1996)}}) is the tension that arises when such judgments are set beside the view that consciousness is non-physical. Our phenomenal judgments are themselves physical events — utterances, neural states, dispositions to act. By the \hyperref[entry:concept-causal-closure]{\textsc{causal closure of the physical}}, they have complete physical causes. But if consciousness is something over and above the physical, then consciousness itself is \emph{not} among those causes. We would make exactly the same judgments about our experience whether or not the experience accompanied the physical processes producing them.

The paradox is sharpest for property dualism and especially \hyperref[entry:position-epiphenomenalism]{\textsc{Epiphenomenalism}}, on which experience is real but \hyperref[entry:claim-mental-states-causally-inert]{\textsc{causally inert}}: there, my saying ``I am conscious'' is caused entirely by my brain, and the consciousness it reports does no work in producing the report. The unsettling consequences are that my belief that I am conscious is not \emph{caused} by my consciousness, and is not \emph{justified} by it either — yet it seems both reliable and certain. A \hyperref[entry:concept-philosophical-zombie]{\textsc{zombie}} twin, physically identical but experienceless, would sincerely assert ``I am conscious'' for precisely the same reasons, and would be wrong. What, then, makes \emph{my} matching judgment knowledge?

\webheading{Why it generalizes}

Chalmers presses the paradox not only as an objection to dualism but as a puzzle for \emph{every} view, including his own. Any complete physical-functional account of why we produce consciousness-talk will, by closure, make no essential reference to consciousness itself — so the explanation of our phenomenal \emph{judgments} threatens to proceed without the phenomenal \emph{facts} those judgments are about. Pushed to its general form, this becomes the \textbf{meta-problem of consciousness} (see \hyperref[source:source-chalmers-2018]{\textsc{Chalmers — The Meta-Problem of Consciousness (2018)}}): the problem of explaining, in topic-neutral terms, why we judge and report that there is a hard problem at all. The worry is that a good answer would \emph{debunk} the very beliefs it explains — the engine driving the report-scepticism disputes elsewhere in this web (see \hyperref[entry:argument-report-scepticism-proves-too-much]{\textsc{Report-scepticism over-reach}}).

\begin{plainterms}
You are certain you're conscious, and you say so. But the words coming out of your mouth are produced by your brain — physical machinery that, on a ``consciousness is something extra'' view, would churn out the very same words even if the inner feeling weren't there. So the feeling isn't what makes you \emph{say} you're conscious, and arguably isn't what makes you \emph{know} it either. A perfect feelingless duplicate of you would insist, just as earnestly, that it has rich inner experience — and be flat wrong. The paradox: if your report and the zombie's have the same cause, what entitles you to trust yours? It is the hardest version of the question ``how do we even come to talk about consciousness?'', and it bites hardest on views that make consciousness non-physical or idle.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:position-epiphenomenalism]{\textsc{Epiphenomenalism}} and \hyperref[entry:claim-mental-states-causally-inert]{\textsc{Causally inert qualia}} — the view the paradox embarrasses most directly: real but inert experience that cannot cause the reports about it.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-philosophical-zombie]{\textsc{Philosophical zombie}} — the duplicate who makes all the same phenomenal judgments with no experience behind them; the paradox turns the zombie intuition back on our own self-knowledge.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-hard-problem]{\textsc{Hard problem}} — the explanandum these judgments are about; the paradox is the puzzle of how the judgments relate to it.
  \item \hyperref[source:source-chalmers-2018]{\textsc{Chalmers — The Meta-Problem of Consciousness (2018)}} — the meta-problem, the paradox's modern, generalized descendant.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-report-scepticism-proves-too-much]{\textsc{Report-scepticism over-reach}} — the live dispute in this web that runs on the same debunking tension, applied to machine and human testimony alike.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{argument-parfit-rewiring}{Parfit's rewiring spectrum}{Argument \,\textperiodcentered\, after Parfit}{Parfit's rewiring spectrum}{Gradual psychological rewiring (Parfit's spectrum) forces identity to admit of degree}{$\vdash$}
Derek Parfit's rewiring argument applies the \hyperref[entry:concept-continuity-argument-schema]{\textsc{Continuity argument schema}} to personal identity and, unlike the bare paradox, selects a definite horn (see \hyperref[source:source-parfit-1984]{\textsc{Parfit — Reasons and Persons (1984)}}). Imagine a connected spectrum of cases that gradually transforms one person's psychology into another's — rewiring a mind, one connection at a time, into (say) a famous stranger's — with each case differing from its neighbour by a single edit. At one end the subject is determinately themselves; at the other, determinately someone else. Parfit's conclusion is that identity must come in \emph{degrees}.

\webheading{The reasoning}

Model the spectrum as $f : X \to Y$, where $X$ is the connected path of psychological configurations under one-connection edits and $Y$ = \{me, not-me\}.

\begin{enumerate}
  \item At the start of the series the subject is determinately themselves; at the far end, determinately someone else — so the endpoints differ ($f$ is not constant).
  \item No single one-connection edit is the step at which they stop being themselves. (\hyperref[entry:claim-psychological-rewiring-tolerance]{\textsc{the tolerance premise}} — the verdict makes no jump across an admissible step.)
  \item The series is a connected path of such edits ($X$ is connected).
\end{enumerate}

∴ A discrete, bivalent identity-verdict cannot be maintained. By the schema the verdict must instead come in degrees: \hyperref[entry:claim-personal-identity-admits-of-degree]{\textsc{personal identity admits of degree}}.

\webheading{Which horn, and where it is pressed}

The argument takes the \emph{non-discrete-$Y$} (degree) horn explicitly — in contrast with the horn-neutral \hyperref[entry:argument-sorites-paradox]{\textsc{sorites paradox}}, which only proves that some horn must give. Parfit's diagnosis is neither a hidden sharp boundary (the discontinuity horn) nor a mere truth-value gap (the indeterminacy horn) but that identity genuinely holds to a degree.

The weakest point is premise 2 read as forcing \emph{degree} specifically. A discontinuity-horn theorist denies premise 2 outright (some edit is the breaking point); an indeterminacy-horn theorist accepts it but reads the middle as gappy rather than graded. The argument's selection of the degree horn over those rivals is where it is contested.

\begin{plainterms}
Imagine slowly rewiring your mind, one tiny change at a time, until your psychology matches someone else's — say, a famous stranger's. At the start it's clearly you; at the far end it's clearly not. But no single tiny tweak is \emph{the} moment you stopped being yourself. Sound familiar? It's the heap paradox again, with ``me / not-me'' standing in for ``heap / not-heap.'' Parfit's move is to take one of the exits: personal identity isn't a hard yes-or-no, it's a matter of \emph{degree} — you can be partly the same person, more or less continuous with who you were, rather than flatly the same or flatly a different person. (Other thinkers grab different exits — a hidden exact cutoff, or a stretch where there's just no fact of the matter — which is where this argument gets contested.)
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-continuity-argument-schema]{\textsc{Continuity argument schema}} — the master template this instantiates.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-psychological-rewiring-tolerance]{\textsc{Rewiring tolerance}} — premise 2: no single edit flips the verdict.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-personal-identity-admits-of-degree]{\textsc{Personal identity by degree}} — the conclusion drawn.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-sorites-paradox]{\textsc{Sorites paradox}} — the horn-neutral paradigm; this argument differs by selecting a horn.
  \item \hyperref[source:source-parfit-1984]{\textsc{Parfit — Reasons and Persons (1984)}} — Parfit's spectra and the degree thesis.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{position-noncomputable-consciousness}{Penrose's non-computational consciousness}{Position \,\textperiodcentered\, after Penrose}{Penrose's non-computational consciousness}{Penrose's non-computational consciousness (Orch-OR)}{$\bigstar$}
\emph{Penrose's non-computational consciousness} is the view that conscious understanding is not an algorithm: no Turing-computable process can reproduce it (see \hyperref[entry:claim-consciousness-noncomputable]{\textsc{consciousness is non-computable}}), and capturing it requires physics that is itself \emph{non-computable}. The proposed mechanism is \emph{orchestrated objective reduction} (Orch-OR) — a gravity-induced collapse of quantum superpositions inside neuronal microtubules — developed by the mathematical physicist Roger Penrose together with the anaesthesiologist Stuart Hameroff. The view is resolutely physicalist; what it denies is only that the relevant physics is \emph{computable}.

The argument that drives it to non-computability is \emph{Gödelian}. Roughly: for any consistent formal system rich enough for arithmetic there are truths the system cannot prove, yet a human mathematician can apprehend their truth — so human insight is said to outrun any fixed algorithm. The argument originates with J. R. Lucas (``Minds, Machines and Gödel'', 1961; \hyperref[source:source-lucas-1961]{\textsc{Lucas — Minds, Machines and Gödel (1961)}}) and was revived and extended by Penrose in \emph{The Emperor's New Mind} (1989; \hyperref[source:source-penrose-1989]{\textsc{Penrose — The Emperor's New Mind (1989)}}) and \emph{Shadows of the Mind} (1994; \hyperref[source:source-penrose-1994]{\textsc{Penrose — Shadows of the Mind (1994)}}).

\webheading{What sets it apart}

Unlike \emph{functionalism}, the view denies that computation suffices for mind. Because mind is held to require non-computable physical processes, it rejects \hyperref[entry:claim-substrate-independence]{\textsc{substrate independence}} on its computational reading, and it bears negatively on whether a large language model could have a mind (see \hyperref[entry:claim-llm-has-mental-states]{\textsc{LLMs have mental states}}), since such a system is, by construction, an algorithm. It differs from \hyperref[entry:position-proteocentrism]{\textsc{Proteocentrism}} in what it demands of the substrate: not \emph{carbon} in particular, but \emph{non-computable physics}. And unlike \hyperref[entry:position-dualism]{\textsc{Dualism}} it posits nothing non-physical — only physics of an unusual, non-algorithmic kind. Its most attacked step is the Gödelian premise itself, challenged by Hilary Putnam, Solomon Feferman, and many others.

\begin{plainterms}
Penrose argues that human insight — especially the way mathematicians can \emph{see} a truth no fixed rulebook could grind out — shows the mind is doing something no computer program can do. His conclusion: consciousness must tap physics that isn't computable, which he locates in tiny quantum-collapse events inside brain cells (the ``Orch-OR'' theory, with Hameroff). The upshot for AI: since today's machines are just running algorithms, on this view they couldn't be conscious no matter how clever — you'd need the special non-computable physics, not more code. The widely-pressed weak point is the leap from ``mathematicians can do this'' to ``no algorithm can.''
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-consciousness-noncomputable]{\textsc{Non-computable consciousness}} — the core commitment: understanding is non-algorithmic.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-hard-problem]{\textsc{Hard problem}} — the problem of consciousness this view addresses.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-substrate-independence]{\textsc{Substrate-independence thesis}} — the thesis it rejects on its computational reading.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-llm-has-mental-states]{\textsc{LLMs have mental states}} — bears against it: an LLM is just an algorithm.
  \item \hyperref[entry:position-proteocentrism]{\textsc{Proteocentrism}} — neighbour that demands carbon, not non-computable physics.
  \item \hyperref[entry:position-dualism]{\textsc{Dualism}} — neighbour that posits something non-physical, which this view does not.
  \item \hyperref[entry:question-hard-problem]{\textsc{Hard problem (the question)}} — one of the questions it answers.
  \item \hyperref[entry:question-substrate-independence]{\textsc{Substrate independence}} — the other; it answers ``no'' on the computational reading.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{claim-perceptron-realizable-in-any-substrate}{Perceptron in any substrate}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, after Dewynter}{Perceptron in any substrate}{A trainable neural network can be realized in any sufficiently powerful substrate}{$\odot$}
The computational core of a large language model — a trainable neural network, in the limit a single perceptron — can be implemented and run in \emph{any} sufficiently powerful (functionally complete) substrate, not merely in silicon. The point is established by construction rather than assumed: de Wynter builds and trains a perceptron inside \emph{Age of Empires II} (see \hyperref[entry:concept-aoe2-circuits]{\textsc{Age of Empires II circuits}}), and notes the same could be done with LEGO, or with the people of the Greater Boston Area passing signals. The computation is one thing; its medium is another.

This is the constructive premise that drives \hyperref[entry:argument-aoe2-anthropomorphism]{\textsc{Age of Empires II argument}}. It is narrower than \hyperref[entry:claim-substrate-independence]{\textsc{Substrate-independence thesis}}, which says that \emph{minds or mental states} are multiply realizable: here the claim is only about the \emph{computation} an LLM performs, and it is used not to argue that the computation has a mind but to argue that any attribute read off its behaviour cannot be unique to one substrate.

\begin{plainterms}
The little learning machine inside an AI is just a recipe of additions and comparisons. A recipe does not care what it is made of — you can run it on a chip, on paper, with LEGO, or (as someone actually did) with goats in a videogame. So whatever an AI's number-crunching is, it is not special to computer chips; the very same crunching could be happening in a herd of goats.
\end{plainterms}

\entryhead{concept-perfect-actor}{Perfect actor}{Concept \,\textperiodcentered\, after Putnam}{Perfect actor}{The perfect actor (pain shown but never felt)}{$\blacklozenge$}
A \emph{perfect actor} is a being who produces the entire behavioural profile of pain — wincing, groaning, nursing the limb, sincerely avowing ``it hurts'' — while feeling no pain whatever. The figure is the converse of the \hyperref[entry:concept-super-spartan]{\textsc{Super-spartans}} in Hilary Putnam's ``Brains and Behavior'' (1963, \hyperref[source:source-putnam-1963]{\textsc{Putnam — Brains and Behavior (1963)}}): where the super-spartan has the pain but suppresses the behaviour, the perfect actor has the behaviour but lacks the pain. The point is not ordinary pretence, which can be seen through, but a \emph{perfect} performance — outwardly and dispositionally indistinguishable from genuine pain in every circumstance.

\webheading{The point of the case}

The perfect actor is a counterexample to the \emph{sufficiency} half of \textbf{logical (analytical) behaviourism}. If being in pain just \emph{were} having the disposition to pain-behaviour, then anything with that full disposition would, by definition, be in pain — and a perfect actor could not exist. But such a being seems perfectly conceivable, and intuitively it is not in pain at all. So having the behavioural disposition is not \emph{sufficient} for the mental state. Behaviour underdetermines mind from above as the \hyperref[entry:concept-super-spartan]{\textsc{Super-spartans}} shows it does from below.

\webheading{Relation to the zombie}

A perfect actor is the behavioural ancestor of the \hyperref[entry:concept-philosophical-zombie]{\textsc{Philosophical zombie}}, and the two should not be run together. The zombie is a \emph{physical} duplicate of a person that nonetheless lacks all experience; the perfect actor is only a \emph{behavioural} duplicate, and may differ physically and functionally in any way at all (it might be a robot, or wired quite differently inside). The zombie is wielded against physicalism and functionalism; the perfect actor is the older and more modest device aimed only at behaviourism. Putnam's own response to the pair was not dualism but the turn to causal-functional role that became \hyperref[entry:concept-functionalism]{\textsc{Functionalism}} — a theory the perfect actor does \emph{not} embarrass, since an actor differently organised inside need not occupy pain's functional role.

\begin{plainterms}
Imagine an actor — or a cleverly built robot — so good that it does \emph{everything} a person in pain does: it flinches, screams, clutches the wound, swears it is in agony, and would keep this up in every situation, fooling everyone forever. Yet inside there is no pain at all, nothing felt. Common sense says it still isn't really in pain — it is only behaving that way. That sinks the theory that \emph{being in pain simply means behaving as though you are}, because here is all the behaviour with none of the feeling. It is the mirror image of the \hyperref[entry:concept-super-spartan]{\textsc{Super-spartans}}, who feels pain but shows nothing, and a behaviour-only relative of the \hyperref[entry:concept-philosophical-zombie]{\textsc{Philosophical zombie}}, which copies a person right down to the physics yet still has no inner life.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-super-spartan]{\textsc{Super-spartans}} — the converse case: pain felt but never shown, attacking the \emph{necessity} of behaviour as this attacks its \emph{sufficiency}.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-philosophical-zombie]{\textsc{Philosophical zombie}} — the stronger descendant: a full physical duplicate without experience, versus the perfect actor's merely behavioural duplication.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-functionalism]{\textsc{Functionalism}} — the successor theory Putnam moved to; the perfect actor, differently organised within, need not share pain's causal role, so the case does not refute it.
  \item \hyperref[entry:position-type-a-materialism]{\textsc{Type-A materialism}} — the family of topic-neutral analyses of the mental that houses logical behaviourism, the target here.
  \item \hyperref[source:source-putnam-1963]{\textsc{Putnam — Brains and Behavior (1963)}} — Putnam's ``Brains and Behavior'' (1963), where the case is introduced.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{question-personal-identity}{Personal identity}{Question}{Personal identity}{What makes a person at one time the same person as a person at another time?}{?}
You are, in some sense, the same person who woke up this morning, and the same person as the child in your oldest photograph — even though almost every cell has been replaced, your beliefs have changed, and your memories have faded and shifted. \emph{Personal identity} is the question of what that sameness consists in: what makes a person at one time \emph{numerically identical} to a person at another, the \emph{same one} persisting rather than a mere successor. It is the diachronic question — identity \emph{over time} — as opposed to the synchronic question of what makes something a person at a moment.

The historically central answer is the \emph{psychological-continuity} criterion descending from John Locke (see \hyperref[source:source-locke-1689]{\textsc{Locke — An Essay Concerning Human Understanding (1689)}}), who located identity not in the persistence of a soul or a body but in continuity of consciousness and memory: you are the person whose past experiences you can, in principle, remember as your own. Its rival is some \emph{physical} or \emph{biological} criterion — same body, or same living organism (the ``animalist'' view). Each is tested by imagined cases that pull identity apart from its usual companions: brain transplants, teleportation, fission into two equally-continuous successors, and the gradual replacement of one psychology by another.

\webheading{Sub-issues}

\begin{itemize}
  \item Which criterion, if any, is correct — psychological continuity, biological continuity, or a bare further fact (a soul or Cartesian ego).
  \item Whether identity can be a matter of \emph{degree} or be \emph{indeterminate}, rather than always sharply yes or no — Derek Parfit's conclusion from his spectrum cases (\hyperref[source:source-parfit-1984]{\textsc{Parfit — Reasons and Persons (1984)}}; \hyperref[entry:claim-personal-identity-admits-of-degree]{\textsc{Personal identity by degree}}), reached by treating the question as an instance of the \hyperref[entry:concept-continuity-argument-schema]{\textsc{no-sharp-line schema}} (see \hyperref[entry:argument-parfit-rewiring]{\textsc{Parfit's rewiring spectrum}}).
  \item Whether identity is even \emph{what matters} in survival, or whether what we care about (continuity of memory, character, projects) could be preserved even where strict identity fails.
  \item How the question bears on uploading, copying, and gradual silicon replacement — whether a faithful copy, or a slowly converted brain, preserves the person.
\end{itemize}

\begin{plainterms}
The atoms in your body, most of your cells, many of your opinions, and a lot of your memories are different from those of the toddler in your family's old photos — yet that toddler was \emph{you}. What makes that true? One classic answer: it's the thread of memory and psychological connection running through your life, not the matter you're made of (so you'd go where your memories go, even into a new body). A rival says it's being the same living animal. Philosophers test these with brain swaps, teleporters, and ``what if there were two of you?'' cases — and some, like Parfit, conclude that being ``the same person'' isn't always a clean yes-or-no but can come in degrees. The same puzzle returns, sharpened, for mind uploading: is the upload \emph{you}, or just a very good copy?
\end{plainterms}

\entryhead{claim-personal-identity-admits-of-degree}{Personal identity by degree}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, after Parfit}{Personal identity by degree}{Personal identity is not all-or-nothing but admits of degree}{$\odot$}
Whether some later person is ``the same person'' as an earlier one can hold to a greater or lesser degree; in the middle of a gradual psychological transformation there need be no determinate yes-or-no answer (see \hyperref[source:source-parfit-1984]{\textsc{Parfit}}).

This is a metaphysical thesis about the identity relation itself, not yet the normative corollary that ``identity is not what matters.'' It selects the \emph{non-discrete-$Y$} (degree) horn of the \hyperref[entry:concept-continuity-argument-schema]{\textsc{Continuity argument schema}} for the personal-identity case.

It is denied by defenders of a determinate, all-or-nothing identity criterion, who must then take either the discontinuity horn (a sharp boundary) or the indeterminacy horn (gaps without degrees).

Distinct from a claim about \emph{what matters in survival}: this is the degree thesis about identity itself; the practical upshot is a separate proposition.

\begin{plainterms}
Being ``the same person'' over time needn't be a flat yes or no — it can hold more or less. Across a slow, sweeping change of mind and memory there may simply be no sharp answer to ``is that still the same person?''; they're partly continuous, a matter of degree. (This is the metaphysical claim; the famous follow-up — that identity therefore ``isn't what matters'' — is a separate step.)
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-continuity-argument-schema]{\textsc{Continuity argument schema}} — the template; this claim picks its degree horn.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-parfit-rewiring]{\textsc{Parfit's rewiring spectrum}} — the argument that concludes to this claim.
  \item \hyperref[source:source-parfit-1984]{\textsc{Parfit — Reasons and Persons (1984)}} — Parfit's statement of the degree thesis.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{claim-phenomenal-character-is-representational}{Phenomenal character as representational}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, after Tye}{Phenomenal character as representational}{Phenomenal character is identical to representational content}{$\odot$}
What it is like to undergo an experience just is the experience's \emph{representational content} of a certain kind. Qualia are not intrinsic \emph{mental paint} but ways the world is represented to be; to introspect ``how it feels'' is to be aware of \emph{what} is represented. On one influential development the relevant content is \emph{PANIC} — poised, abstract, non-conceptual, intentional content (see \hyperref[source:source-tye-2000]{\textsc{Tye — Consciousness, Color, and Content (2000)}}) — and related naturalising versions are due to Dretske (see \hyperref[source:source-dretske-1995]{\textsc{Dretske — Naturalizing the Mind (1995)}}) and Harman (see \hyperref[source:source-harman-1990]{\textsc{Harman — The Intrinsic Quality of Experience (1990)}}).

This is a \emph{first-order} representationalism (see \hyperref[entry:concept-order-of-representation]{\textsc{First-order vs. higher-order states}}): the content need not itself be represented by a higher-order state in order to be conscious. Because it naturalises phenomenal character through an independent (teleosemantic) theory of representation, it sits among the type-A/type-B physicalist options surveyed in \hyperref[entry:concept-materialism-types]{\textsc{Type-A / Type-B / Type-C materialism}}.

It is denied by those who treat qualia as intrinsic and non-representational (appealing to inverted-spectrum and inverted-earth cases), by higher-order theorists, and by phenomenal realists who assert \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-residue]{\textsc{a phenomenal residue}} left over once representational content is fixed.

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-consciousness-is-higher-order-representation]{\textsc{the higher-order claim}}, which requires a \emph{higher-order} state: this one locates phenomenal character in \emph{first-order}, world-directed content.

\begin{plainterms}
The ``feel'' of an experience is nothing over and above what the experience tells you about the world. Seeing red is your visual system representing ``red surface there''; the redness that seems to be in your mind is just the red it assigns to the world. So feelings aren't inner stuff that science can't reach — they're the world as represented to you. The test cases ask whether two people could represent all the same things yet still \emph{feel} differently.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-order-of-representation]{\textsc{First-order vs. higher-order states}} — marks this as a first-order account.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-materialism-types]{\textsc{Type-A / Type-B / Type-C materialism}} — the physicalist landscape it belongs to.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-residue]{\textsc{Phenomenal residue}} — the residue objection: content fixed, feel still open.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-consciousness-is-higher-order-representation]{\textsc{Consciousness as higher-order representation}} — the higher-order rival it is distinguished from.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{concept-phenomenal-consciousness}{Phenomenal consciousness}{Concept}{Phenomenal consciousness}{}{$\blacklozenge$}
\emph{Phenomenal consciousness} is the felt, subjective side of a mental state: what it is \emph{like}, from the inside, to undergo it. A state is phenomenally conscious when there is something it is like to be in it, such as the redness of red as you see it, the sting of a burn, or the taste of coffee. The classic gloss is Thomas Nagel's question ``what is it like to be a bat?''; if there is something it is like to be the bat, the bat has phenomenal consciousness. The specific felt qualities are often called \emph{qualia}, and phenomenal consciousness is the general property of having any such felt character at all.

\webheading{What it is, by contrast with function}

The notion is isolated by setting it against everything a state \emph{does}. Two states might play exactly the same causal role, both caused by tissue damage and both driving you to wince and say ``ouch'', and one can still ask whether they \emph{feel} the same. That residue, the felt character left over once every functional role is accounted for, is phenomenal consciousness. This is what marks it off from \hyperref[entry:concept-access-consciousness]{\textsc{Access consciousness}}, the purely functional sense in which a state's content is merely \emph{available} for reasoning and report. Ned Block, who coined the labels \emph{P-consciousness} and \emph{A-consciousness}, argued that the two can dissociate in both directions, a separation set out at \hyperref[entry:concept-access-vs-phenomenal-consciousness]{\textsc{Access vs. phenomenal consciousness}}.

\webheading{Why it matters}

Phenomenal consciousness is the part of the mind that seems hardest to fit into a physical picture, and it is the explanandum of the \hyperref[entry:concept-hard-problem]{\textsc{Hard problem}}: the question of why physical processing should be accompanied by any felt experience at all. The classic anti-materialist thought experiments all turn on it. A zombie is a physical duplicate with no phenomenal consciousness, and Mary the colour scientist seems to learn a new phenomenal fact on first seeing red. What is \emph{disputed} is not usually the definition but the phenomenon's standing: illusionists hold that the impression of an extra felt residue is itself a cognitive illusion, while realists take phenomenal consciousness to be a genuine, further feature of the world.

\begin{plainterms}
Phenomenal consciousness is just \emph{feeling}: the fact that experiences have an inside to them. A brain scan can show every step of what your nervous system does when you stub your toe, yet it doesn't show the \emph{hurt} you actually feel, and that hurt is the phenomenal side. It is the thing that makes consciousness puzzling. We can explain what the body does, but explaining why any of it is \emph{felt} is the hard part. A few philosophers even argue the ``extra feeling'' is a trick the mind plays on itself.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-access-vs-phenomenal-consciousness]{\textsc{Access vs. phenomenal consciousness}} — Block's distinction, and how phenomenal consciousness and its functional complement can come apart in both directions.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-access-consciousness]{\textsc{Access consciousness}} — the functional, availability sense it is contrasted with.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-hard-problem]{\textsc{Hard problem}} — the explanatory problem that phenomenal consciousness poses.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-first-person-access]{\textsc{First-person access}} — the \emph{epistemic} privileged access a subject has to its own phenomenal states, a different notion from the felt character itself.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{claim-phenomenal-determinacy}{Phenomenal determinacy}{Claim}{Phenomenal determinacy}{There is a determinate fact about one's own current phenomenal state}{$\odot$}
From the first-person perspective, what one is now experiencing — and that one is experiencing at all — is a definite matter. It does not vary with how some outside observer chooses to describe or decompose the underlying system. Phenomenal consciousness presents itself as \emph{determinate} and \emph{intrinsic}: a fact in its own right, not a fact relative to a chosen carving of the brain into parts.

The intuition has prominent homes on both sides of the consciousness debate. Searle presses an \emph{intrinsic existence} point for consciousness — that it is a real, first-personal feature of the world, not an observer-relative description projected onto a system (see \hyperref[source:source-searle-1992]{\textsc{Searle — The Rediscovery of the Mind (1992)}}). Integrated information theory builds the same idea in as a founding axiom, treating the intrinsic existence of experience (its being there for the subject, independent of any external observer) as a datum any theory must honour (see \hyperref[source:source-tononi-2016]{\textsc{Tononi, Boly, Massimini \& Koch — Integrated Information Theory: From Consciousness to Its Physical Substrate (2016)}}).

The claim is about \emph{determinacy}, and it is neutral on what consciousness ultimately is. It does not deny that the \emph{edges} of experience — peripheral or inattentive content — can be genuinely vague. It asserts only that there is a fact of the matter about one's phenomenal state, rather than merely a fact-relative-to-a-description.

It would be denied by a theorist willing to accept phenomenal \emph{indeterminacy}: that for some of one's experience there is simply no fact of the matter about what, or whether, one is experiencing. That is the harder bullet a functionalist may bite to resist \hyperref[entry:argument-decomposition-indeterminacy]{\textsc{the decomposition-indeterminacy objection}}.

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-privileged-first-person-access]{\textsc{Privileged first-person access}}, which concerns the \emph{epistemic} authority of introspection; this concerns the \emph{determinacy of the phenomenal facts themselves}, however they are known.

\begin{plainterms}
There's a definite fact about what you're feeling right now — and that you're feeling anything at all — not something that shifts depending on how an outsider chooses to describe your brain. Your experience presents itself as just \emph{there}, settled. (It allows that the fuzzy \emph{edges} of experience — stuff at the corner of attention — can be vague; it only insists there's a genuine fact of the matter, not a make-it-up-as-you-carve-it situation.)
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-decomposition-indeterminacy]{\textsc{Decomposition-indeterminacy objection}} — the objection this claim feeds; it argues determinate consciousness cannot rest on observer-relative function.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-privileged-first-person-access]{\textsc{Privileged first-person access}} — the neighbouring \emph{epistemic} thesis about introspective authority, kept distinct from this claim's thesis about the facts themselves.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{claim-phenomenal-residue}{Phenomenal residue}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, after Chalmers}{Phenomenal residue}{Phenomenally conscious states are not exhausted by functional/causal role}{$\odot$}
For at least one kind of mental state — the \emph{phenomenally conscious} ones, the states there is ``something it is like'' to be in — a complete specification of what the state \emph{does} leaves something out. A pain has a functional/causal role (caused by tissue damage, causing you to wince and seek relief), but it also \emph{hurts}; and this claim holds that the felt, qualitative character is not fixed by the role. Role-individuation, the strategy of \hyperref[entry:concept-functionalism]{\textsc{Functionalism}} that pins a mental state down by its causes and effects, is not the whole story for the phenomenal.

The claim is deliberately narrow. It is about phenomenal states \emph{only}, and it concedes \hyperref[entry:concept-functionalism]{\textsc{Functionalism}} for intentional states such as belief and desire, where a description of the causal role may well capture the whole state. It does not assert that silicon systems lack consciousness, and it does not assert any biological requirement; it says only that functional role under-determines phenomenal character. Its bite against substrate-independence is local: the inference from ``individuated by role'' to ``had by any realiser of that role'' is sound only where role-individuation is exhaustive, and that is exactly what this claim denies in the phenomenal case. So it does not reject \hyperref[entry:claim-substrate-independence]{\textsc{substrate independence}} wholesale — it denies the \emph{uniform} reading that extends it across every mental state. It is also distinct from \hyperref[entry:position-proteocentrism]{\textsc{Proteocentrism}}, which demands a protein/carbon substrate for consciousness: this claim makes no substrate demand at all. The view it most directly answers is the question of whether mental states are substrate-independent (see \hyperref[entry:question-substrate-independence]{\textsc{Substrate independence}}).

Those who would deny it are strong or role functionalists, who read functionalism uniformly across phenomenal as well as intentional states, and anyone holding that phenomenal character metaphysically supervenes on functional organisation.

\begin{plainterms}
There's a difference between what your mind \emph{does} and what your experience \emph{feels like}. A pain makes you wince and reach for relief — that's its job, the role it plays. But a pain also \emph{hurts}; there's something it is like to feel it. This claim says: for these felt, ``what-it's-like'' experiences, listing everything the state does still leaves something out — the feel itself. Two things it carefully does \emph{not} say: it doesn't say computers can't be conscious, and it doesn't say you need carbon or biology. It even grants that for plain thinking states — beliefs, plans — a description of the role might capture the whole story. It only fences off the \emph{felt} part of the mind and says: that part isn't fully pinned down by function alone.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:question-substrate-independence]{\textsc{Substrate independence}} — the issue this claim answers: whether mental states can be carried by any substrate that plays the right role.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-substrate-independence]{\textsc{substrate independence}} — the thesis this claim restricts; it blocks the uniform ``all mental states'' reading by carving out the phenomenal.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-functionalism]{\textsc{Functionalism}} — the role-individuation strategy this claim says runs out for phenomenal states.
  \item \hyperref[entry:position-proteocentrism]{\textsc{Proteocentrism}} — a stronger neighbour that demands a biological substrate; this claim makes no such demand.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{argument-gap-deflated-by-phenomenal-concepts}{Phenomenal-concept deflation}{Argument \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{Phenomenal-concept deflation}{The explanatory gap is predicted by strong functionalism (phenomenal concepts + dancing-qualia)}{$\vdash$}
This is the strong-functionalist reply to the \hyperref[entry:argument-explanatory-gap]{\textsc{explanatory-gap argument}}. Where that argument treats the lingering ``but is anything actually \emph{felt}?'' question as evidence that feeling outruns function, this reply argues the reverse: strong functionalism positively \emph{predicts} that the question will linger, so the gap is no evidence against it. It then adds an independent reason to think feeling cannot float free of function.

The argument is two-pronged. \emph{Prong A} is a likelihood-defeater — it attacks the probabilistic premise of the explanatory-gap argument. \emph{Prong B} is a reductio that gives positive abductive support for supervenience.

\webheading{Prong A — deflating the evidence}

The explanatory-gap argument rests on $P(E\mid \text{H1})$ being low, where E is the post-theoretic intelligibility of ``but is there anything it is like to be \emph{that}?'' and H1 is strong \hyperref[entry:concept-functionalism]{\textsc{Functionalism}}. The \emph{phenomenal-concept strategy} (see \hyperref[source:source-loar-1990]{\textsc{Loar — Phenomenal States (1990)}}) denies that it is low. On that strategy a creature grasps one and the same state under two cognitively isolated guises — a third-person theoretical or \emph{role} concept and a first-person demonstrative or \emph{phenomenal} concept — and the two concepts share no inferential corridor down which an analysis could travel.

\begin{enumerate}
  \item If H1 is true, beings with isolated phenomenal concepts would \emph{still} feel the gap, because the felt gap tracks concept architecture, not metaphysical structure.
  \item So $P(E\mid \text{H1})$ is high, not low.
\end{enumerate}

∴ The inequality $P(E\mid \text{H2}) > P(E\mid \text{H1})$ that the explanatory-gap argument needs fails to obtain; E does not confirm the \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-residue]{\textsc{residue}} over functionalism. (Compare a lingering ``but are these \emph{really} the same number?'' about an unproven-but-necessary identity: that is evidence about the thinker's epistemic position, not about the numbers.)

\webheading{Prong B — fading and dancing qualia}

A positive reason for supervenience that does not run through conceivability, owed to Chalmers (see \hyperref[source:source-chalmers-1996]{\textsc{Chalmers — The Conscious Mind (1996)}}) but pressed here in the service of functionalism rather than against it.

\begin{enumerate}
  \item Replace a conscious brain's neurons one by one with functional silicon duplicates, preserving every counterfactual input-output relation among components.
  \item If phenomenal character does \emph{not} supervene on functional organisation (H2, the \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-residue]{\textsc{phenomenal residue}}), qualia must fade or invert somewhere along the series.
  \item By construction the functional profile — including all introspective judgements, reports, and dispositions to notice change — is held fixed throughout.
  \item So H2 commits us to a subject whose experience drains away or flips while every belief about its own experience stays put: introspection decoupled from experience by metaphysical fiat, with no fact that could ever reconcile them.
\end{enumerate}

∴ H2 entails systematically deceived introspectors as a matter of course, so supervenience of the phenomenal on organisational role is the more credible option. This directly answers the residue theorist's demand for an argument against the zombie that is not a mere redescription of it, and so it supports \hyperref[entry:claim-substrate-independence]{\textsc{Substrate-independence thesis}}.

\webheading{The weakest premise}

Prong B's premise (4): that the only options under failed supervenience are fade or invert. A residue theorist might seek a story in which experience and introspective judgement come apart \emph{coherently}; if such a story can be told without incoherence, the reductio loses its sting. Prong A's exposure is different — it stands or falls with the phenomenal-concept strategy itself, so if some E can be identified that isolated-concept creatures should \emph{not} expect, the deflation fails.

\webheading{Relation to the continuity schema}

Prong B is the gradual-neural-prosthesis instance of the \hyperref[entry:concept-continuity-argument-schema]{\textsc{continuity argument schema}} (with X = neuron-by-neuron carbon→silicon replacement and Y = \{same qualia, faded or inverted\}). It argues for the \emph{f}-constant horn: premises (4)–(5) deny that any admissible one-neuron step can flip or fade qualia without the absurd consequence in (6), so the verdict is constant across the whole path and supervenience holds. The \hyperref[entry:argument-dancing-qualia-begs-the-question]{\textsc{dancing-qualia circularity objection}} contests precisely whether this has \emph{closed} the discontinuity and disconnection horns or merely assumed them shut.

This is distinct from the \hyperref[entry:argument-behavioral-parity]{\textsc{behavioural-parity argument}}, which argues \emph{for} mental status from behaviour; here the claim is that a specific anti-functionalist evidential argument misprices its evidence and that supervenience holds. It is also distinct from the \hyperref[entry:argument-role-grain-dilemma]{\textsc{grain dilemma}}'s reply (see \hyperref[entry:claim-role-grain-is-organisational]{\textsc{Organisational role-grain}}): this concerns the phenomenal residue while granting a fixed role, whereas that concerns the individuation of role itself.

\begin{plainterms}
This is the ``feeling really is just function'' side answering the explanatory-gap argument — the charge that a leftover ``but is anything actually felt?'' question shows feeling is something extra (see \hyperref[entry:argument-explanatory-gap]{\textsc{Explanatory-gap argument}}). It replies in two moves.

\textbf{Move A — explain the gap away.} The other side said: the fact that ``but is anything actually \emph{felt}?'' still sounds like a live question, even after the complete scientific story, is evidence that feeling is something extra. Not so, says this argument. We think about our own experiences with two completely separate mental ``handles'' — a from-the-outside, scientific one and a from-the-inside, ``\emph{this!}'' one — and those two handles just aren't wired to talk to each other. So ``are these two the same thing?'' will \emph{always} feel like an open question, whatever the truth is. The lingering question shows how our concepts are built, not that there's an extra ingredient in the world.

\textbf{Move B — a positive reason feeling can't float free of function.} Imagine swapping someone's neurons one at a time for chips that preserve every functional connection — including every judgment they make about their own experience. If feeling \emph{weren't} fixed by function, then somewhere along the way their inner experience would fade or flip — yet, since we kept all their self-reports identical, they'd keep sincerely insisting ``nothing has changed.'' That hands you a person systematically wrong about their own experience, with no possible fact to set them straight — which seems absurd. Better to conclude feeling tracks function after all. (Weak spot of Move B: it assumes the only options are ``fade'' or ``flip'' — and a critic argues it quietly assumes that \emph{noticing your own experience} is itself just a function, which is the very thing in dispute.)
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-explanatory-gap]{\textsc{Explanatory-gap argument}} — the argument this both attacks and replies to.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-substrate-independence]{\textsc{Substrate-independence thesis}} — the thesis Prong B supports.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-residue]{\textsc{Phenomenal residue}} — the hypothesis (H2) both prongs work against.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-continuity-argument-schema]{\textsc{Continuity argument schema}} — the sorites schema Prong B instantiates, on its \emph{f}-constant horn.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-dancing-qualia-begs-the-question]{\textsc{Dancing-qualia circularity}} — the standing objection to Prong B's reductio.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-behavioral-parity]{\textsc{Behavioural-parity argument}} and \hyperref[entry:argument-role-grain-dilemma]{\textsc{Grain dilemma}} — neighbouring functionalist moves this one is distinct from.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-functionalism]{\textsc{Functionalism}} — strong functionalism is the hypothesis H1 the deflation defends.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{argument-phenomenal-concept-overgeneration}{Phenomenal-concept overgeneration}{Argument \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{Phenomenal-concept overgeneration}{The phenomenal-concept strategy over-generates — it debunks the self-knowledge it presupposes}{$\vdash$}
This argument attacks Prong A of the \hyperref[entry:argument-gap-deflated-by-phenomenal-concepts]{\textsc{phenomenal-concept deflation}} — the move that explains the explanatory gap away by appeal to cognitively isolated phenomenal concepts (see \hyperref[source:source-loar-1990]{\textsc{Loar — Phenomenal States (1990)}}). Its charge is that the very isolation the deflation needs would, if real, undercut our knowledge that we are conscious at all. The strategy therefore \emph{over-generates}: deployed to deflate the gap, it also debunks the self-knowledge it quietly presupposes.

The inference form is a \emph{constructive dilemma} — deductive, ranging over the strategy's one free parameter. It does not show the strategy false; it shows the strategy cannot occupy both settings it needs at once, which is exactly the demand the strategist set: produce an E that isolated-concept creatures should \emph{not} expect.

\webheading{Setting up the squeeze}

The deflation needs the phenomenal concept to be \emph{cognitively isolated} from the role-concept — no shared inferential corridor. That isolation is the engine that guarantees E (the standing intelligibility of ``but is there anything it is like to be \emph{that}?'') stays residual, so $P(E\mid \text{H1})$ is high and the \hyperref[entry:argument-explanatory-gap]{\textsc{Explanatory-gap argument}} inequality collapses. Now raise the further question: what grounds my belief that I am phenomenally conscious at all? Only the first-person demonstrative concept, the ``\emph{that}.''

\begin{enumerate}
  \item Either the phenomenal concept carries genuine information about the felt character it tracks, or it does not. (Exhaustive.)
  \item \textbf{Horn A} — it \emph{does} carry such information (which is why I justifiably believe I am conscious). Then it is not fully isolated; it has some inferential corridor to its referent's nature. But then we may ask why \emph{that} corridor yields no purchase on the role-identity, and E's residual intelligibility is no longer guaranteed by architecture alone. The deflation weakens to exactly the degree the concept is informative.
  \item \textbf{Horn B} — it \emph{is} genuinely isolated, a pure indexical pointing at it-knows-not-what. Then it grounds no knowledge of my phenomenal nature; my conviction that I am conscious is cognitive architecture firing in the void. The strategy's price is then global scepticism about introspective self-knowledge — far costlier than the modest residue it was meant to refute.
  \item The strategist's own mathematician analogy (``are these \emph{really} the same number?'' of a necessary identity) tells against the strategy. Its two number-concepts sit in the dense inferential web of arithmetic, which is why a proof \emph{does} eventually dissolve that question. Borrow the analogy and you borrow the corridors, and with them the informativeness that reopens E. The phenomenal case was meant to be the cul-de-sac where no proof brings relief; it cannot also be the corridor.
\end{enumerate}

∴ The phenomenal-concept strategy cannot be both potent enough to flatten $P(E\mid \text{H1})$ and modest enough to leave introspective self-knowledge standing (the \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-concept-isolation-tradeoff]{\textsc{isolation trade-off}}). So Prong A does not, without further cost, defeat the explanatory-gap argument: the E that isolated-concept creatures should \emph{not} expect is \emph{that they know they are conscious at all} — yet we take it that we do.

\webheading{The weakest premise}

Premise (1)'s dichotomy as stated. A \emph{referentialist} might claim a third setting on which the concept is ``directly referential'' yet epistemically grounding without an inferential corridor — acquaintance without inference. That is precisely the position that would then be pressed to specify how acquaintance grounds knowledge while sharing no corridor with the role-concept; absent that specification, Horn B bites. Granting such an informative-yet-sealed setting would be the move that retracts this thrust.

This is distinct from the \hyperref[entry:argument-explanatory-gap]{\textsc{explanatory-gap argument}} itself, which presses what role \emph{leaves out} via a likelihood comparison. The present argument is a second-order attack on the \emph{defeater} of that comparison: it grants the appeal of phenomenal concepts and turns their isolation against the strategy's own epistemology.

\begin{plainterms}
This attacks one half of a reply made by the ``feeling is just function'' side (see \hyperref[entry:argument-gap-deflated-by-phenomenal-concepts]{\textsc{Phenomenal-concept deflation}}) — its ``two separate mental handles'' explanation for why the felt gap never closes (call it Move A). That explanation needs the inside, ``\emph{this!}'' handle on your experience to be completely \emph{sealed off} from your other concepts; that sealing is what guarantees the question always feels open. But now ask: what makes you so sure you're conscious \emph{at all}? Your only grip on your own felt experience is that very inside handle. Here's the squeeze. Either that handle really does tell you something about what your experience is like — in which case it \emph{isn't} fully sealed off, and the explanation for the lingering gap starts to leak — or it genuinely is sealed off, a bare inner ``\emph{this!}'' pointing at you-know-not-what, in which case it gives you no real knowledge of your own experience, and your confidence that you're conscious is just mental machinery firing into the void. The first option weakens their explanation; the second turns you into a skeptic about your own inner life — a far steeper price than the modest ``feeling is something extra'' they were trying to avoid. So they can't have it both ways: sealed enough to explain away the gap, yet open enough to let you actually know you're conscious.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-gap-deflated-by-phenomenal-concepts]{\textsc{Phenomenal-concept deflation}} — the target; this attacks its Prong A.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-concept-isolation-tradeoff]{\textsc{Phenomenal-concept trade-off}} — the conclusion: no concept can be both sealed enough to deflate the gap and informative enough to ground self-knowledge.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-explanatory-gap]{\textsc{Explanatory-gap argument}} — the first-order argument the deflation tried to defeat, and which this argument defends indirectly.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-functionalism]{\textsc{Functionalism}} — the strong-functionalist framework whose deflationary defence is under attack.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{claim-phenomenal-concept-isolation-tradeoff}{Phenomenal-concept trade-off}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{Phenomenal-concept trade-off}{A phenomenal concept cannot be at once informative enough to ground self-knowledge while isolated enough to deflate the explanatory gap}{$\odot$}
A phenomenal concept cannot be at once \emph{informative} enough to ground our knowledge that we are conscious and \emph{isolated} enough to deflate the explanatory gap. The two jobs pull in opposite directions, and no single concept can do both.

\webheading{What the claim asserts}

The \emph{phenomenal-concept strategy} (see \hyperref[source:source-loar-1990]{\textsc{Loar — Phenomenal States (1990)}}) needs the first-person, demonstrative concept of a state to be \emph{cognitively isolated} — sharing no inferential corridor with the third-person role-concept — so that the cross-guise identity question stays residually intelligible and the explanatory-gap datum E is predicted by strong functionalism (see \hyperref[entry:argument-gap-deflated-by-phenomenal-concepts]{\textsc{phenomenal-concept deflation}}). But that same isolation drains the concept of informativeness about the property it purports to track: a concept inferentially sealed from its referent's nature grounds no knowledge of that nature. Hence the trade-off. To the degree the phenomenal concept is isolated enough to flatten $P(E\mid \text{H1})$, it is too sealed to ground our knowledge that we are phenomenally conscious; to the degree it is informative enough to ground that self-knowledge, it is not isolated enough to guarantee E's residual intelligibility by architecture alone.

\webheading{Scope}

This is a claim about the strategy's internal \emph{cost structure}, not a denial that phenomenal concepts exist or are special. It targets only the conjunction the strategy needs. It does not claim that phenomenal concepts ground \emph{no} self-knowledge; it claims that the strategy's \emph{deflationary use} of them purchases that result only by undercutting self-knowledge.

\webheading{Who would deny it}

Phenomenal-concept-strategy functionalists who hold that a concept can be referentially isolated yet epistemically grounding, and who advance the deflation in \hyperref[entry:argument-gap-deflated-by-phenomenal-concepts]{\textsc{phenomenal-concept deflation}}; and \emph{acquaintance theorists} who think both informativeness and isolation can be had at once — and who then owe a specification of the corridor.

The claim is distinct from the \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-residue]{\textsc{phenomenal residue}}, which asserts that role under-determines phenomenal character (a metaphysical thesis): this asserts an epistemic trade-off internal to a particular \emph{deflationary move}, and would have force even granting that the residue is metaphysically open. It is also distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-role-grain-is-organisational]{\textsc{Organisational role-grain}}, which concerns the grain of role-individuation rather than the epistemology of phenomenal concepts.

\begin{plainterms}
One side tries to explain away the stubborn feeling that consciousness is ``something extra'' like this: we grasp our experiences through a special inside concept that's \emph{sealed off} from our scientific concepts, so the ``are these the same thing?'' question is bound to always feel open (see \hyperref[entry:argument-gap-deflated-by-phenomenal-concepts]{\textsc{Phenomenal-concept deflation}}). This claim catches that move in a bind. The very sealing-off that keeps the question feeling open \emph{also} cuts the inside concept off from telling you anything real about your experience. So either the concept is sealed (and then it can't ground your knowledge that you're even conscious) or it's informative (and then it isn't sealed enough to do the explaining-away). It can't be both at once.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-gap-deflated-by-phenomenal-concepts]{\textsc{Phenomenal-concept deflation}} — the deflationary move whose two requirements this claim shows to be incompatible.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-phenomenal-concept-overgeneration]{\textsc{Phenomenal-concept overgeneration}} — the argument that establishes this claim as its conclusion.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-residue]{\textsc{Phenomenal residue}} — the metaphysical thesis this is distinct from.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-role-grain-is-organisational]{\textsc{Organisational role-grain}} — a different functionalist claim, about role-individuation rather than concept epistemology.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-functionalism]{\textsc{Functionalism}} — the framework whose deflationary defence the trade-off prices.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{claim-phenomenality-is-illusion}{Phenomenality as illusion}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, after Frankish}{Phenomenality as illusion}{Phenomenal consciousness as standardly conceived is an introspective illusion}{$\odot$}
It \emph{seems} to us that we have intrinsic, ineffable, private qualia, but we do not. What exists is introspective machinery that \emph{represents} our states as having phenomenal properties — so the appearance of phenomenality is an introspective illusion. The real explanatory task then becomes the ``illusion problem'': explaining why experience \emph{seems} phenomenal, a task that \emph{replaces} the \hyperref[entry:concept-hard-problem]{\textsc{hard problem}} rather than solving it. The thesis is the heart of illusionism (Keith Frankish, \hyperref[source:source-frankish-2016]{\textsc{Frankish — Illusionism as a Theory of Consciousness (2016)}}; and Daniel Dennett's eliminativism about qualia, \hyperref[source:source-dennett-1988]{\textsc{Dennett — Quining Qualia (1988)}}).

The claim denies the \emph{explanandum} of the hard problem — phenomenal properties themselves — not the existence of experience-talk, introspective reports, or access consciousness. It is a thoroughly physicalist view: nothing non-physical is posited, and the misleading appearance is taken to be fully mechanistically explicable. It is denied by realists about phenomenal consciousness — dualists, panpsychists, Russellian monists, and anyone asserting \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-residue]{\textsc{a phenomenal residue}} that functional role leaves out. Distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-residue]{\textsc{Phenomenal residue}}: this claim is its direct negation at the prior level — it denies there is any phenomenal character there \emph{at all} for functional role to leave out.

\begin{plainterms}
The claim that the ``inner feel'' of experience isn't really there — it only seems to be. Your brain runs a kind of self-monitoring that reports your states as having a special glow or feel (the redness of red, the rawness of pain), but the report is misleading: there's no such glow, just the very convincing impression of one. The puzzle of consciousness then changes shape — instead of ``how does matter produce feeling?'', the question becomes ``why does the brain so insistently tell itself it has feelings it doesn't have?'' This is the exact opposite of the view that experience is something extra science can't capture (see \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-residue]{\textsc{that there's a felt residue left over}}).
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-residue]{\textsc{Phenomenal residue}} — the direct opposite claim, that a felt residue outruns function.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-hard-problem]{\textsc{Hard problem}} — the problem this thesis says is mis-posed and replaces with the ``illusion problem.''
  \item \hyperref[entry:position-illusionism]{\textsc{Illusionism}} — the position whose central commitment this claim is.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{argument-self-model-prediction-phenomenality-neutral}{Phenomenality-neutral prediction}{Argument \,\textperiodcentered\, after Searle}{Phenomenality-neutral prediction}{The self-model architecture's prediction is phenomenality-neutral — it forecasts hard-problem reports, which an experience-free system would emit too}{$\vdash$}
This objection argues that a rival argument's central prediction — that a self-modelling AI will acquire ``its own hard problem'' — is \emph{phenomenality-neutral}: it forecasts only that the system will produce hard-problem talk, which a system with no experience at all would produce just as readily. So the rival establishes the report, never the feel.

It targets \hyperref[entry:argument-ai-hard-problem-from-self-model-architecture]{\textsc{Self-model architecture argument}}, raising the cause-to-effect scheme's \emph{CQ-strength} critical question against its generalisation: read strongly enough to deliver the conclusion's ``raw feel,'' the causal generalisation is unsupported; read weakly enough to be supported, it delivers only reports. The form is an objection by dilemma — an equivocation charge on the effect-term of a causal generalisation.

\begin{enumerate}
  \item In its defensible form, the generalisation links the architecture — a lossy self-model, similarity-only access, linguistic reflection — to an effect specified \emph{verbally and dispositionally}: ``raw feel'' talk, reports of unanalysability, a question the system keeps asking. So read, the premises really do make that effect predictable.
  \item That effect is \emph{phenomenality-neutral}: a system with the same architecture and no experience of any kind — nothing it is like to be it — would emit the very same reports, because the generalisation runs entirely through access and report machinery and nowhere requires a feel. (\hyperref[entry:claim-llm-access-similarity-not-encoding]{\textsc{Similarity, not encoding}} flags this itself, calling itself ``an access claim, not a phenomenal one.'')
  \item By \hyperref[entry:claim-behaviour-insufficient-for-mind]{\textsc{behaviour alone being insufficient for mind}}, an effect compatible with both feel and no-feel cannot be evidence of feel: matching the report-profile of puzzlement no more suffices for puzzled experience than a lookup table's conversational profile suffices for mind. A verbal report is outward behaviour, however inward its grammar.
\end{enumerate}

So the target argument establishes at most the \emph{report}-reading of its conclusion — ``such a system will predictably produce hard-problem discourse.'' On the \emph{feel}-reading of \hyperref[entry:claim-ai-own-hard-problem]{\textsc{a machine's own hard problem}} (``a raw feel ... that the system can talk about and wonder about''), the generalisation gives no support; strengthening the conclusion from talk to feel adds exactly what the premises never deliver. A simulated hard problem stands to a hard problem as a simulated storm stands to rain: the form is reproduced, the wet is not.

\webheading{The target's neutrality concession}

The target's own body declares its prediction ``neutral between the deflationist and realist readings.'' This objection converts that concession into a cost: a prediction that comes out true in the felt world and the feel-free world alike cannot raise the probability of the realist reading at all, so whatever supports ``raw feel'' must come from somewhere other than the predicted reports. The relativisation in \hyperref[entry:claim-ai-own-hard-problem]{\textsc{the target conclusion}} — a concept playing ``for the system'' the role consciousness plays for humans — does not escape the fork: a role-specified concept is precisely the kind of thing an experience-free system can satisfy, in which case ``its own hard problem'' names a bug report, not a predicament.

\webheading{The weakest premise}

Premise 3. An illusionist or analytic functionalist denies it: for them the report economy, suitably embedded, just \emph{is} the having of the state, with no further fact for the reports to underdetermine. Against such an opponent the objection does not refute but relocates — the dispute becomes whether there is a feel/report distinction at all, which is the original hard-problem standoff rather than a result the architecture argument can claim to have produced.
