This argument is distinct from \hyperref[entry:argument-architectural-account-leaves-hard-problem]{\textsc{Architectural-account objection}}, which attacks \hyperref[entry:claim-self-explanation-gap-can-be-architectural]{\textsc{Architectural self-explanation gap}} and blocks the \emph{deflationary} use of the architectural story against the human, possibly ontological, gap. This argument attacks a different node in the opposite direction — the \emph{inflationary} move from architecture to a machine's own raw feel — and does so by the cause-to-effect scheme's own critical question (CQ-strength), not the analogy scheme's disanalogy question.

\begin{plainterms}
There's an argument in this web saying a future AI that models itself will predictably end up with ``its own hard problem'' — inner states that feel like unexplainable ``raw feels'' to it (see \hyperref[entry:argument-ai-hard-problem-from-self-model-architecture]{\textsc{Self-model architecture argument}}). This objection says: look closely at what that argument actually predicts. It predicts the AI will \emph{say} hard-problem things — ``I can't analyze this state, it's just \emph{this}.'' Fine. But an AI with the same wiring and no inner life at all would say exactly the same things, for exactly the same wiring reasons. A prediction that comes true whether or not anyone's home can't be evidence that someone's home. Words of puzzlement are behaviour, and behaviour alone never settles whether there's a mind behind it (see \hyperref[entry:claim-behaviour-insufficient-for-mind]{\textsc{Behaviour insufficient for mind}}). So the argument proves the AI will produce the \emph{soundtrack} of the hard problem — it can't, even in principle, show there's a film.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-ai-hard-problem-from-self-model-architecture]{\textsc{Self-model architecture argument}} — the target: architecture predicts a machine's own hard problem.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-ai-own-hard-problem]{\textsc{AI's own hard problem}} — the conclusion whose feel-reading this objection denies is earned.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-llm-access-similarity-not-encoding]{\textsc{Similarity, not encoding}} — premise: the access story is explicitly non-phenomenal.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-behaviour-insufficient-for-mind]{\textsc{Behaviour insufficient for mind}} — premise: report-profile never settles whether a mind is present.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-architectural-account-leaves-hard-problem]{\textsc{Architectural-account objection}} — the mirror-image attack, on the deflationary direction.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-self-explanation-gap-can-be-architectural]{\textsc{Architectural self-explanation gap}} — what that mirror-image argument attacks.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-access-vs-phenomenal-consciousness]{\textsc{Access vs. phenomenal consciousness}} — the access/feel distinction the whole objection turns on.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-hard-problem]{\textsc{Hard problem}} — the predicament the simulated version only mimics.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{concept-philosophical-zombie}{Philosophical zombie}{Concept \,\textperiodcentered\, after Chalmers}{Philosophical zombie}{}{$\blacklozenge$}
A \emph{philosophical zombie} is an imagined being that is microphysically and functionally identical to a conscious person — every atom, every brain state, every piece of behaviour the same — but with \emph{no phenomenal consciousness}: nothing it is like to be it, all dark inside. It is not the shambling creature of horror films; it is a same-physics twin that does everything you do, says ``ouch'' when pricked and talks about its feelings, yet has no inner experience whatsoever (see \hyperref[source:source-chalmers-1996]{\textsc{Chalmers — The Conscious Mind (1996)}}).

The point of the device is not to claim such beings exist, but to test whether the physical facts settle the facts about experience. If a complete physical duplicate of a conscious person could exist with experience subtracted, then experience is not just more physics — it is something \emph{over and above} the physical. That is exactly the conclusion the zombie is built to drive: it is the engine of the \emph{conceivability argument} against physicalism, the view that everything, consciousness included, is fixed by physical facts.

\webheading{The crucial step}

The argument turns on a move from imagining to possibility. From the fact that a zombie seems \emph{conceivable} — coherently and stably imaginable, with no contradiction surfacing on careful reflection — it infers that a zombie is genuinely \emph{possible}, and hence that \hyperref[entry:claim-consciousness-fundamental-nonphysical]{\textsc{consciousness is non-physical}}. Nearly the whole dispute concentrates on this bridge from conceiving to possibility (see \hyperref[entry:claim-conceivability-entails-possibility]{\textsc{Conceivability entails possibility}}): critics grant that we can picture a zombie while denying that the picture tracks any real possibility, much as one can imagine water that is not H₂O without water's being possibly anything but H₂O.

\webheading{Relation to neighbours}

The zombie is a limiting case of \emph{absent qualia} — experience entirely missing rather than merely altered — and a cousin of the \emph{inverted spectrum}, in which the felt qualities are swapped rather than absent. It sharpens the intuition behind the \hyperref[entry:concept-hard-problem]{\textsc{Hard problem}} of consciousness, the puzzle of why physical processing is accompanied by any felt experience at all. The related \hyperref[entry:argument-explanatory-gap]{\textsc{explanatory-gap argument}} already leans on zombie conceivability in its premises, and the \hyperref[entry:argument-gap-deflated-by-phenomenal-concepts]{\textsc{phenomenal-concept strategy}} is the leading attempt to defuse the zombie by granting that it is conceivable while denying that it is possible.

\begin{plainterms}
A ``philosophical zombie'' is an imagined being that is an atom-for-atom copy of you — same brain, same behaviour, says ``ouch'' when pricked, chats about its feelings — except there is no one home: no inner experience at all, total darkness inside. The thought is not that such things exist; it is that \emph{if you can coherently imagine one}, then experience must be something extra that the physical facts alone don't pin down. Nearly the whole fight is over that ``if you can imagine it, it's genuinely possible'' step.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-hard-problem]{\textsc{Hard problem}} — the puzzle of why physical processing is accompanied by felt experience at all; the zombie sharpens its central intuition.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-conceivability-entails-possibility]{\textsc{Conceivability entails possibility}} — the modal bridge from conceiving a zombie to its being possible, where the whole argument is contested.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-consciousness-fundamental-nonphysical]{\textsc{Fundamental non-physical consciousness}} — the conclusion the zombie is built to drive: experience is over and above the physical.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-explanatory-gap]{\textsc{Explanatory-gap argument}} — an evidential cousin whose premises already lean on zombie conceivability.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-gap-deflated-by-phenomenal-concepts]{\textsc{Phenomenal-concept deflation}} — the phenomenal-concept strategy, which grants the zombie's conceivability but denies its possibility.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{concept-philosophy-of-mind}{Philosophy of mind}{Concept}{Philosophy of mind}{}{$\blacklozenge$}
\emph{Philosophy of mind} is the branch of philosophy that asks what the mind is, what mental states are, and how they fit into the rest of nature. Its subject is everything that falls under ``the mental'' — thought, perception, sensation, emotion, belief, desire, and above all \emph{consciousness}, the fact that for a creature like us there is \emph{something it is like} to be alive. The field studies what these states are, how they relate to the brain and body, how they can be \emph{about} a world and \emph{cause} behaviour, and which kinds of system can have them at all. It is one of the core areas of metaphysics and, since the late twentieth century, the meeting point of philosophy with psychology, neuroscience, and artificial intelligence.

The discipline is organised around a single central problem — the \hyperref[entry:concept-mind-body-problem]{\textsc{mind-body problem}}, the question of how mental phenomena relate to physical ones — but it is wider than that problem alone. Where the mind-body problem asks specifically how the mental and the physical fit together, philosophy of mind also takes up questions that can be posed without settling that relationship: how thought manages to be \emph{about} things, what perception delivers, what makes a person the same person over time, and whether a machine could think. This entry surveys the field and serves as the hub from which its problems, positions, and arguments branch.

\webheading{The questions of the field}

Several connected questions make up the territory. They overlap, but each can be pursued on its own.

\begin{itemize}
  \item \textbf{The nature of mind} — the ontological question at the heart of the \hyperref[entry:concept-mind-body-problem]{\textsc{Mind-body problem}}: is the mind identical to the brain, a distinct substance or property, an illusion, or the intrinsic nature of matter? The classic statement is René Descartes' \emph{real distinction} between thinking thing and extended thing (see \hyperref[source:source-descartes-meditations]{\textsc{Descartes — Meditations on First Philosophy (1641)}}).
  \item \textbf{Consciousness} — \emph{why is any of this accompanied by experience at all?} Even a complete functional account of the brain seems to leave open why it should \emph{feel} like anything: the \hyperref[entry:concept-hard-problem]{\textsc{hard problem}}, framed by the question of \hyperref[entry:question-hard-problem]{\textsc{why there is experience}} and sharpened by Thomas Nagel's ``what is it like to be a bat?'' (see \hyperref[source:source-nagel-1974]{\textsc{Nagel — What Is It Like to Be a Bat? (1974)}}). A standing distinction here is between \hyperref[entry:concept-access-vs-phenomenal-consciousness]{\textsc{access and phenomenal consciousness}} — between information being available for use and there being a felt quality to it. The topic as a whole is surveyed at \hyperref[entry:concept-consciousness]{\textsc{Consciousness}}.
  \item \textbf{Intentionality} — \emph{how can a mental state be about something?} The ``aboutness'' of thought (see \hyperref[entry:concept-intentionality]{\textsc{Intentionality}}) was made central by Franz Brentano, who called directedness upon an object ``the mark of the mental'' (see \hyperref[source:source-brentano-1874]{\textsc{Brentano — Psychology from an Empirical Standpoint (1874)}}); how any physical state could have it is \hyperref[entry:question-intentionality]{\textsc{the problem of intentionality}}.
  \item \textbf{Mental causation} — \emph{how can the mental have physical effects?} If the physical world is causally closed, mental causes seem either to collapse into physical ones or to be left idle — the puzzle Princess Elisabeth pressed on Descartes (see \hyperref[source:source-elisabeth-1643]{\textsc{Elisabeth of Bohemia — Correspondence with Descartes (1643)}}), posed as \hyperref[entry:question-mental-causation]{\textsc{the problem of mental causation}} and made precise by \hyperref[entry:argument-causal-exclusion]{\textsc{Kim's causal exclusion argument}} (see \hyperref[source:source-kim-2005]{\textsc{Kim — Physicalism, or Something Near Enough (2005)}}).
  \item \textbf{Self-knowledge and the first person} — the privileged, non-inferential way a subject knows its own states (see \hyperref[entry:concept-first-person-access]{\textsc{First-person access}}), and whether introspection is a genuine \emph{observation} or itself a fallible representation (see \hyperref[entry:question-acquaintance-datum-or-representation]{\textsc{Acquaintance: datum or representation}}).
  \item \textbf{Perception and the external world} — what experience puts us in touch with, and whether it warrants belief in a mind-independent world (see \hyperref[entry:question-external-world-knowledge]{\textsc{Knowing the external world}}).
  \item \textbf{Personal identity} — what makes a person at one time the same as a person at another (see \hyperref[entry:question-personal-identity]{\textsc{Personal identity}}), and whether identity is even all-or-nothing (see \hyperref[entry:claim-personal-identity-admits-of-degree]{\textsc{Personal identity by degree}}).
\end{itemize}

\webheading{The space of positions}

The competing answers are mapped in detail under the \hyperref[entry:concept-mind-body-problem]{\textsc{Mind-body problem}}; in outline they run from \hyperref[entry:position-dualism]{\textsc{Dualism}} (the mental is not physical) through the large \hyperref[entry:position-physicalism]{\textsc{physicalist}} family (the mental just \emph{is} physical, whether by identity, functional role, or elimination) to the non-standard monisms (\hyperref[entry:position-idealism]{\textsc{Idealism}}, \hyperref[entry:position-neutral-monism]{\textsc{neutral monism}}, \hyperref[entry:position-russellian-monism]{\textsc{Russellian monism}} and \hyperref[entry:position-panpsychism]{\textsc{Panpsychism (position)}}) and reframings such as Aristotle's \hyperref[entry:position-hylomorphism]{\textsc{Hylomorphism}} and Gilbert Ryle's deflation of ``the ghost in the machine'' (see \hyperref[source:source-ryle-1949]{\textsc{Ryle — The Concept of Mind (1949)}}). Each answer honours some of the data and pays a price on the rest, which is why the field's central problem has stayed open for four centuries.

\webheading{How the field argues}

Philosophy of mind works largely through \emph{thought experiments} that pump a verdict from an imagined case: the conceivability of a \hyperref[entry:argument-zombie-conceivability]{\textsc{zombie}} physically identical to a conscious being but with no experience; Mary the colour scientist, who knows all the physics of colour yet seems to learn something on first seeing red (see \hyperref[entry:concept-knowledge-argument]{\textsc{Knowledge argument}}); Searle's \hyperref[entry:concept-chinese-room]{\textsc{Chinese Room}}, pressing whether running a program could constitute understanding. These join conceptual analysis, the deliverances of introspection, and — increasingly — empirical results from the cognitive and brain sciences, which the field both draws on and interprets. Such cases are powerful but not decisive: their force depends on how far the imagined scenario lies from the cases our intuitions were trained on.

\webheading{The machine turn}

A modern centre of gravity, and the focus of much of this web, is the mind in artificial systems. Alan Turing replaced the ``too meaningless'' question \emph{can machines think?} with an operational test (\hyperref[entry:concept-turing-test]{\textsc{Turing test}}; \hyperref[source:source-turing-1950]{\textsc{Turing — Computing Machinery and Intelligence (1950)}}). \hyperref[entry:concept-functionalism]{\textsc{Functionalism}} — minds individuated by causal role rather than material — opened the possibility of \hyperref[entry:claim-substrate-independence]{\textsc{substrate independence}}, that a mind could run on silicon as well as on neurons. That possibility turns the field's oldest questions into urgent ones: whether a large language model could be conscious, whether its reports are evidence of anything, and whether such a system could have \hyperref[entry:question-llm-moral-status]{\textsc{moral status}}. Here philosophy of mind stops being only a theory of \emph{our} minds and becomes a theory of \emph{possible} minds.

\begin{plainterms}
Philosophy of mind is the part of philosophy that tries to figure out what minds are. You think, feel, see colours, want things, and there's something it's like to be you — and you're also just a body and a brain made of ordinary matter. How do those go together? Is your mind your brain, something your brain makes, a separate non-physical thing, or even an illusion? And around that big question sit a cluster of others: how a thought manages to be \emph{about} something, how feelings can make your body move, what you really know just by looking inward, what keeps you the same person over a lifetime — and, lately, whether a computer could ever have a mind of its own. The field tackles these partly by careful argument and partly by vivid ``what if'' scenarios (a perfect zombie, a colour scientist who's never seen colour, a room that fakes Chinese), and increasingly by talking to brain science and AI. Almost every proposed answer explains some of it neatly and stumbles on the rest, which is why the subject is still wide open.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-mind-body-problem]{\textsc{Mind-body problem}} — the field's central problem, with the full map of positions; this entry is the wider field of which that problem is the core.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-consciousness]{\textsc{Consciousness}} — overview of the consciousness strand and its theories.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-hard-problem]{\textsc{Hard problem}} / \hyperref[entry:question-hard-problem]{\textsc{Hard problem (the question)}} — the problem of consciousness, the hardest strand.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-intentionality]{\textsc{Intentionality}} / \hyperref[entry:question-intentionality]{\textsc{Problem of intentionality}} — the aboutness of thought, and how a physical state could have it (see \hyperref[source:source-brentano-1874]{\textsc{Brentano — Psychology from an Empirical Standpoint (1874)}}).
  \item \hyperref[entry:question-mental-causation]{\textsc{Mental causation}} / \hyperref[entry:argument-causal-exclusion]{\textsc{Causal exclusion argument}} — how the mental can have physical effects, and the exclusion argument that presses the worry.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-access-vs-phenomenal-consciousness]{\textsc{Access vs. phenomenal consciousness}} — the access/phenomenal distinction the consciousness debates turn on.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-first-person-access]{\textsc{First-person access}} / \hyperref[entry:question-acquaintance-datum-or-representation]{\textsc{Acquaintance: datum or representation}} — self-knowledge and the status of introspection.
  \item \hyperref[entry:question-external-world-knowledge]{\textsc{Knowing the external world}} — perception and knowledge of the external world.
  \item \hyperref[entry:question-personal-identity]{\textsc{Personal identity}} / \hyperref[entry:claim-personal-identity-admits-of-degree]{\textsc{Personal identity by degree}} — the personal-identity strand.
  \item \hyperref[entry:position-dualism]{\textsc{Dualism}}, \hyperref[entry:position-physicalism]{\textsc{Physicalism}}, \hyperref[entry:position-idealism]{\textsc{Idealism}}, \hyperref[entry:position-neutral-monism]{\textsc{Neutral monism}}, \hyperref[entry:position-russellian-monism]{\textsc{Russellian monism}}, \hyperref[entry:position-panpsychism]{\textsc{Panpsychism (position)}}, \hyperref[entry:position-hylomorphism]{\textsc{Hylomorphism}} — the principal families of answer (full landscape at \hyperref[entry:concept-mind-body-problem]{\textsc{Mind-body problem}}).
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-zombie-conceivability]{\textsc{Zombie argument}}, \hyperref[entry:concept-knowledge-argument]{\textsc{Knowledge argument}}, \hyperref[entry:concept-chinese-room]{\textsc{Chinese room}} — the field's signature thought experiments.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-functionalism]{\textsc{Functionalism}}, \hyperref[entry:claim-substrate-independence]{\textsc{Substrate-independence thesis}}, \hyperref[entry:concept-turing-test]{\textsc{Turing test}}, \hyperref[entry:question-llm-moral-status]{\textsc{Moral status of LLMs}} — the machine turn: minds beyond the human case.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{claim-physical-church-turing-thesis}{Physical Church–Turing thesis}{Claim}{Physical Church–Turing thesis}{Every physically realizable computation is Turing-computable}{$\odot$}
Any function that some physical process can compute is also computable by a Turing machine: no physical device out-computes the Turing machine. This is the \emph{physical} (or ``bold'') Church–Turing thesis, sometimes called the Church–Turing–Deutsch principle. Together with the fact that \hyperref[entry:claim-halting-problem-undecidable]{\textsc{no Turing machine decides the halting problem}}, it entails that no physical machine can decide the halting problem either.

The thesis is \emph{contingent and empirical} — a claim about what the physical world happens to permit, not a mathematical theorem. (The theorem is \hyperref[entry:claim-halting-problem-undecidable]{\textsc{the undecidability of the halting problem}}.) Its role is as a \emph{bridge}: it is what turns Turing-uncomputability, a fact about an abstract machine, into \emph{physical} unrealizability, a fact about what nature can build.

It is denied from two directions. \emph{Hypercomputation} theorists argue that exotic physics could realise super-Turing computation — through Malament–Hogarth spacetimes, infinite-time Turing machines, or idealised relativistic and analog devices. And, pointedly, Roger Penrose denies it for brains: the claim that \hyperref[entry:claim-consciousness-noncomputable]{\textsc{consciousness involves non-computable physical processes}}, and the \hyperref[entry:position-noncomputable-consciousness]{\textsc{position built on it}}, hold that the brain exploits non-computable physics (Orch-OR; \hyperref[source:source-penrose-1994]{\textsc{Penrose — Shadows of the Mind (1994)}}), which is precisely to reject this thesis for one kind of physical system.

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-halting-problem-undecidable]{\textsc{the undecidability theorem}}: that is a proved fact about Turing machines; this is the defeasible physical premise about what nature can build.

\begin{plainterms}
The claim that nature has no computer more powerful than an ordinary one: anything any physical process can compute, a standard computer could too (given enough time and memory). If that's right, then since no ordinary computer can solve the halting problem, \emph{nothing physical} can. It's a widely held bet about physics — but only a bet: some thinkers argue weird spacetimes or exotic processes could break it, and Penrose's theory of mind actually \emph{requires} it to be false for the brain.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-halting-problem-undecidable]{\textsc{Undecidability of the halting problem}} — the pure computability theorem this thesis carries into the physical world.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-halting-oracle]{\textsc{Halting oracle}} — the hypothetical device that does what no Turing machine can; this thesis says nature builds none.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-consciousness-noncomputable]{\textsc{Non-computable consciousness}} — Penrose's denial of the thesis for brains.
  \item \hyperref[entry:position-noncomputable-consciousness]{\textsc{Penrose's non-computational consciousness}} — the position resting on that denial.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{position-physicalism}{Physicalism}{Position}{Physicalism}{Physicalism (materialism)}{$\bigstar$}
\textbf{Physicalism} (its older name \textbf{materialism}) is the thesis that everything that concretely exists is physical, or is wholly fixed by what is physical. Applied to the mind, it holds that mental events are physical events (see \textsc{Mental events are physical}): there is no thought, sensation, or experience over and above the physical workings of the brain and body. A common precise formulation is a \emph{supervenience} thesis — any world physically identical to ours is identical to ours in every respect, mental included — so that once the physical facts are fixed, all the facts are fixed.

The modern positive case for physicalism is the \textbf{causal argument} (see \hyperref[entry:argument-causal-argument-for-physicalism]{\textsc{Causal argument for physicalism}}), which turns on two principles this view leans on heavily: the \hyperref[entry:concept-completeness-of-physics]{\textsc{Completeness of physics}} (every physical effect has a sufficient physical cause) and the \hyperref[entry:concept-causal-closure]{\textsc{Causal closure of the physical}} of the physical (no non-physical cause produces a physical effect). If the mind genuinely moves the body, and the physical world is causally closed, the mind must itself be physical — the alternative being to demote it to a causally idle by-product (see \hyperref[entry:position-epiphenomenalism]{\textsc{Epiphenomenalism}}). Physicalism thus presents itself less as a metaphysical preference than as the only account of mental causation consistent with the closed causal order science describes (Papineau, \hyperref[source:source-papineau-2002]{\textsc{Papineau — Thinking about Consciousness (2002)}}; Kim, \hyperref[source:source-kim-2005]{\textsc{Kim — Physicalism, or Something Near Enough (2005)}}).

\textbf{What distinguishes it from neighbouring views.} Physicalism is the \emph{genus} whose species populate much of this web: \hyperref[entry:position-type-identity]{\textsc{Type-identity theory}} (mental types \emph{are} physical types), \hyperref[entry:position-eliminative-materialism]{\textsc{Eliminative materialism}} (the mental as folk theory discharges to neuroscience), analytic functionalism, and \hyperref[entry:position-illusionism]{\textsc{Illusionism}} (phenomenal properties are introspective illusion) are all ways of being a physicalist. They divide over the \hyperref[entry:concept-hard-problem]{\textsc{Hard problem}}: Chalmers' \hyperref[entry:concept-materialism-types]{\textsc{Type-A / Type-B / Type-C}} taxonomy sorts them by whether they deny the epistemic gap between physical and phenomenal truths, accept it but deny an ontological gap, or hope to close it in the future. Against physicalism stand \hyperref[entry:position-dualism]{\textsc{Dualism}} (which denies completeness or closure to keep the mind non-physical and efficacious), \hyperref[entry:position-epiphenomenalism]{\textsc{Epiphenomenalism}} (which keeps the mind non-physical at the cost of its causal powers), and the conceivability and knowledge arguments (see \hyperref[entry:argument-zombie-conceivability]{\textsc{Zombie argument}}, \hyperref[entry:argument-knowledge-argument]{\textsc{Knowledge argument (Mary's room)}}) that press an explanatory gap physicalism must absorb.

\begin{plainterms}
Physicalism — the same view people used to call materialism — says that, at bottom, everything is physical: there's only the stuff physics describes (and whatever is built out of it), with no separate soul-substance or mental extra. About the mind in particular, it says your thoughts and feelings just \emph{are} things going on in your brain. The strongest reason offered is about causes: the physical world seems to be a closed system where every physical event has a complete physical cause and nothing non-physical reaches in to push matter around. Since your mind clearly \emph{does} affect your body — you decide to stand, and you stand — the tidiest conclusion is that your mind is itself part of that physical machinery. The big challenge it has to answer is the felt, what-it's-like side of experience (the ``hard problem''): critics say you can describe every physical fact about a brain and still leave out what the experience \emph{feels like} from the inside.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \textsc{Mental events are physical} — the view's core commitment about the mind.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-causal-argument-for-physicalism]{\textsc{Causal argument for physicalism}} — its principal positive argument.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-completeness-of-physics]{\textsc{Completeness of physics}} and \hyperref[entry:concept-causal-closure]{\textsc{Causal closure of the physical}} — the two principles that argument rests on.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-materialism-types]{\textsc{Type-A / Type-B / Type-C materialism}} — Chalmers' taxonomy of physicalist responses to the hard problem.
  \item \hyperref[entry:position-dualism]{\textsc{Dualism}}, \hyperref[entry:position-epiphenomenalism]{\textsc{Epiphenomenalism}} — the chief rivals, each rejecting a different premise.
  \item \hyperref[entry:question-hard-problem]{\textsc{Hard problem (the question)}} — the question physicalism must answer to be complete.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{claim-lookup-table-physically-unrealisable}{Physically unrealisable table}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{Physically unrealisable table}{A lookup table reproducing a human- or LLM-scale behavioural profile is physically unrealisable}{$\odot$}
A lookup table large enough to reproduce a human- or LLM-scale behavioural profile over a realistic range of inputs could never be physically built — not in this universe, even in principle. Such a table needs on the order of $v^n$ entries (see \hyperref[entry:claim-lookup-table-size-exponential]{\textsc{exponential table size}}), while the observable universe can store at most about $10^{120}$ bits (see \hyperref[entry:claim-observable-universe-finite-storage]{\textsc{finite cosmic storage}}). The required resource outstrips the available one by a vast margin, so the blockhead is not merely large but cosmically impossible.

\webheading{Scope}

The claim concerns \emph{physical realizability in our universe}, not logical or conceptual possibility. It therefore does not touch Block's intended point — that a \emph{possible} blockhead shows behaviour ≠ mind (\hyperref[source:source-block]{\textsc{Block — Psychologism and Behaviorism (1981)}}; \hyperref[entry:claim-lookup-table-possible]{\textsc{Lookup-table possibility}}, \hyperref[entry:claim-behaviour-insufficient-for-mind]{\textsc{behaviour insufficient for mind}}). It applies \emph{a fortiori} to Block's lifetime-bounded \emph{human} table: richer sensory inputs and a longer sequence make that table even larger, so retreating to a bounded table does not rescue physical realizability (see \hyperref[entry:claim-lookup-table-size-exponential]{\textsc{Exponential table size}}). Its bite is on whether any \emph{actual} system, notably a large language model, could be a lookup table: it cannot. Real LLMs produce the behaviour with about $10^{12}$ parameters rather than about $10^{9600}$ stored rows, so they are demonstrably not lookup tables but compress and generalise — that is, they process.

\webheading{Who would deny it}

A defender of Blockhead who insists only conceptual possibility is at stake, for whom physical unrealizability is beside the point. The reply: granted for Block's conceptual claim — but the dialectically live worry against \hyperref[entry:claim-llm-has-mental-states]{\textsc{LLMs have mental states}} was that an LLM might be a \emph{mere} lookup table, and that worry is what this defeats.

It should be distinguished from \hyperref[entry:claim-lookup-table-combinatorially-inadequate]{\textsc{the combinatorial-inadequacy claim}}: that says a finite table \emph{diverges} from the mind on out-of-domain inputs (a competence gap Block's bounded table escapes); this says the bounded table that would \emph{not} diverge is too large to \emph{exist} — a different ground of failure, one that survives the bounded-table rejoinder.

\begin{plainterms}
A stored answer-table big enough to match a real mind's (or a real AI's) behaviour would need vastly more entries than there are particles to store them on — far more than the entire observable universe could hold. So such a table could not merely be hard to build; it could not exist at all, anywhere, ever. A sharper consequence: real AI models do their job with a comparatively tiny number of internal settings, so they plainly \emph{are not} secret lookup tables — they must be genuinely computing. (It does not claim a lookup-table mind is logically impossible — only that no real one could physically exist.)
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-lookup-table-mind]{\textsc{Lookup-table mind}} — the stored-answer machine this claim says cannot be built.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-lookup-table-size-exponential]{\textsc{Exponential table size}} and \hyperref[entry:claim-observable-universe-finite-storage]{\textsc{Finite cosmic storage}} — the size and storage facts whose collision yields the conclusion.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-lookup-table-combinatorially-inadequate]{\textsc{Combinatorially inadequate table}} — the sibling failure-mode (divergence rather than non-existence).
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-llm-has-mental-states]{\textsc{LLMs have mental states}} — the downstream claim this protects, by ruling out the ``LLM is a mere lookup table'' worry.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{claim-cognitive-closure-exists}{Possibility of cognitive closure}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, after Mcginn}{Possibility of cognitive closure}{A type of mind can be constitutively incapable of grasping some concepts}{$\odot$}
\emph{Cognitive closure} is the thesis that a mind of a given kind can be permanently and constitutively unable to form certain concepts or grasp certain properties — not merely ignorant of them for now, but barred from them by the limits of its conceptual endowment. A rat cannot grasp arithmetic; a dog cannot form the concept of an electron; and the limitation is not lifted by more time or teaching. The category was introduced by Colin McGinn in ``Can We Solve the Mind–Body Problem?'' (1989; \hyperref[source:source-mcginn-1989]{\textsc{McGinn — Can We Solve the Mind–Body Problem? (1989)}}).

This is a general claim in the epistemology of cognition. It does not, by itself, say \emph{which} problems are closed to human beings — only that closure is a real category for minds in general. That much is uncontroversial for non-human animals; the contested step, taken elsewhere, is to apply it to \emph{us}.

An \emph{epistemic optimist} would deny it for our case, holding that a concept-forming creature equipped with language and science has open-ended representational reach, so that ``closure'' never genuinely applies to human inquiry even where it plainly applies to animals.

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-humans-closed-to-psychophysical-link]{\textsc{Closure to the psychophysical link}}: this is the general possibility of closure for some mind and some concept; that is the specific, contested claim that the property explaining consciousness is closed to \emph{us}.

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-humans-closed-to-psychophysical-link]{\textsc{Closure to the psychophysical link}} — applies this general possibility to the specific case of consciousness.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{concept-primary-intension}{Primary intension}{Concept \,\textperiodcentered\, after Chalmers}{Primary intension}{}{$\blacklozenge$}
The \emph{primary intension} of a term is, roughly, the meaning it carries in virtue of how speakers fix its reference — what the term would pick out on the supposition that a given possible world turns out to be the actual one. It is one of the two ``intensions'' (functions from possible worlds to extensions) that David Chalmers's two-dimensional semantics assigns to an expression; the other is its \hyperref[entry:concept-secondary-intension]{\textsc{Secondary intension}}. Where the secondary intension asks ``given how the actual world in fact is, what does this term pick out in \emph{other} worlds?'', the primary intension asks the prior question: ``for each way the world might turn out to \emph{be} actually, what would the term pick out \emph{there}?''

The standard illustration is the word \emph{water}. Competent speakers fix the reference of ``water'' by a rough description — the clear, drinkable liquid that falls as rain and fills the lakes and rivers, the \emph{watery stuff}. The primary intension is the function encoding that condition: hand it a world considered as actual, and it returns whatever plays the watery-stuff role there. In our world that role is filled by H₂O, so the primary intension delivers H₂O. On Hilary Putnam's Twin Earth — a world just like ours except the watery stuff is a different compound, XYZ — \emph{considered as a candidate for being actual}, the primary intension of ``water'' delivers XYZ. The primary intension is in this sense sensitive to empirical discovery only hypothetically: it tells you what ``water'' would refer to under each hypothesis about which world is actual, and so its profile can be known largely a priori, by reflection on how the concept works rather than on which world we are in.

\webheading{Two dimensions of meaning}

The label ``two-dimensional'' comes from evaluating a sentence twice over against a world. A world can enter the calculation in two roles: as the world taken to be \emph{actual} (which settles what our terms refer to), and as a \emph{counterfactual} circumstance to be described using reference so settled. The primary intension occupies the first dimension — worlds considered as actual; the secondary intension occupies the second — worlds considered as counterfactual. For many ordinary terms the two coincide. They come apart precisely for the Kripkean \emph{a posteriori necessities} (\hyperref[entry:claim-some-necessities-knowable-only-a-posteriori]{\textsc{A-posteriori necessities}}, from Kripke's \emph{Naming and Necessity} \hyperref[source:source-kripke-1980]{\textsc{Kripke — Naming and Necessity (1980)}}): ``water is H₂O'' is necessary along the secondary dimension (water is H₂O in every counterfactual world) yet contingent along the primary one (the watery stuff \emph{could have turned out} to be XYZ). The necessity that can be known only by experiment lives in the gap between the two intensions.

Chalmers's is the \emph{epistemic} version of two-dimensional semantics, developed in \emph{The Conscious Mind} (\hyperref[source:source-chalmers-1996]{\textsc{Chalmers — The Conscious Mind (1996)}}, where the ``primary/secondary'' terminology is introduced) and refined in \hyperref[source:source-chalmers-2002]{\textsc{Chalmers — On Sense and Intension (2002)}}; Frank Jackson reaches an equivalent apparatus from the side of conceptual analysis (see \hyperref[source:source-jackson-1998]{\textsc{Jackson — From Metaphysics to Ethics (1998)}}). The primary intension also travels under other names — \emph{epistemic intension}, \emph{A-intension}, \emph{1-intension} — and has cousins in the broader two-dimensional tradition (Kaplan's ``character,'' Stalnaker's ``diagonal''), though those were designed for different purposes and should not be assumed identical.

\webheading{Why the concept matters}

The primary intension is the load-bearing notion in the modern \emph{conceivability} arguments about the mind. Chalmers's \hyperref[entry:argument-modal-rationalism]{\textsc{Modal rationalism}} holds that what ideal reflection finds conceivable is a guide to what is \emph{primarily} possible — possible as a way the world might actually be — and the two-dimensional diagnosis defuses the Kripkean counterexamples by reinterpreting them as \hyperref[entry:claim-aposteriori-necessities-are-misdescribed-possibilities]{\textsc{A-posteriori necessities as misdescribed possibilities}}: when you ``conceive water that is not H₂O'' you conceive a genuinely possible world that verifies the \emph{primary} intension of ``water,'' and merely misdescribe it. This rescues the bridge from conceivability to possibility for the \hyperref[entry:concept-philosophical-zombie]{\textsc{Philosophical zombie}} — but only if the phenomenal case has no such primary/secondary gap to exploit, which is the separate and contested thesis that for consciousness the two intensions coincide (see \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-intensions-coincide]{\textsc{Coincident phenomenal intensions}}).

\begin{plainterms}
Think of a word as having two jobs. One job is fixing \emph{what we're even talking about} — for ``water,'' something like ``the clear drinkable stuff in the rivers.'' The primary intension is that job written out as a rule: point it at any way the world might turn out to be, and it hands back whatever fits the description there. If the world turns out to be ours, ``water'' lands on H₂O; if it had turned out to be Twin Earth, the very same rule would have landed ``water'' on XYZ. So the primary intension is what you grasp just by \emph{understanding the word}, before you do any chemistry — which is why imagining things tends to track it. The other job, pinning the word to the actual stuff (H₂O) and keeping it there even when we talk about other worlds, belongs to the \hyperref[entry:concept-secondary-intension]{\textsc{Secondary intension}}.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-secondary-intension]{\textsc{Secondary intension}} — the other half of the framework: reference fixed by the actual world, then held rigid across counterfactual worlds; the necessary a posteriori is the gap between the two.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-some-necessities-knowable-only-a-posteriori]{\textsc{A-posteriori necessities}} — the Kripkean cases (water = H₂O) that pull the two intensions apart and that the primary intension is designed to handle.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-aposteriori-necessities-are-misdescribed-possibilities]{\textsc{A-posteriori necessities as misdescribed possibilities}} — the two-dimensional diagnosis: apparent counterexamples to ``conceivable ⟹ possible'' are possibilities under the primary intension, misdescribed.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-modal-rationalism]{\textsc{Modal rationalism}} — the argument that ideal primary conceivability is a reliable guide to primary possibility, which the primary intension makes precise.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-intensions-coincide]{\textsc{Coincident phenomenal intensions}} — the further claim that for phenomenal concepts the primary and secondary intensions collapse together, removing the wedge the water case relies on.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-philosophical-zombie]{\textsc{Philosophical zombie}} — the case whose conceivability the framework is meant to convert into genuine possibility.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{claim-privileged-first-person-access}{Privileged first-person access}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{Privileged first-person access}{Each subject has direct, non-inferential first-person acquaintance with its own conscious states}{$\odot$}
A subject knows its own current experiences — its suffering, say — directly, by \emph{acquaintance}, rather than by inferring them from behaviour or theory as it must for any other subject's states. This is a claim about the \emph{mode} of self-knowledge: the route by which one reaches one's own mind differs in kind from the route to anyone else's (see \hyperref[entry:concept-first-person-access]{\textsc{First-person access}}).

Its scope is deliberately modest. The claim says nothing by itself about moral status; it does not assert that the access is \emph{incorrigible} (immune to error); and it does not assert that machines lack such access. It is the uncontroversial first premise that later, stronger premises put to work. Eliminativists and strong higher-order theorists, who deny any non-inferential self-acquaintance, would reject it; most other parties grant it.

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-dancing-qualia-presupposes-functional-introspection]{\textsc{Dancing-qualia introspection premise}} because that claim concerns whether introspective access is \emph{functional} in the supervenience debate; this one only asserts that direct self-access exists, taking no stand on its nature.

\begin{plainterms}
You know your own experiences from the inside, directly — you don't have to watch your own behavior and deduce ``I must be in pain,'' the way you'd guess at anyone else's state. This is just the modest, widely-accepted starting point that such direct self-knowledge exists. On its own it says nothing about morality, isn't claimed to be infallible, and doesn't say machines lack it — it's the uncontroversial first step other arguments build on.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-first-person-access]{\textsc{First-person access}} — the mode of self-knowledge this claim asserts exists.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-dancing-qualia-presupposes-functional-introspection]{\textsc{Dancing-qualia introspection premise}} — the stronger claim about the \emph{nature} of introspective access this is distinguished from.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{question-intentionality}{Problem of intentionality}{Question}{Problem of intentionality}{How can a physical state be about anything?}{?}
Thoughts are \emph{about} things: a belief that Paris is in France reaches out to Paris, a fear is a fear \emph{of} something, a desire is \emph{for} water. This directedness — \hyperref[entry:concept-intentionality]{\textsc{Intentionality}}, the ``aboutness'' of the mental — was called ``the mark of the mental'' by Franz Brentano (see \hyperref[source:source-brentano-1874]{\textsc{Brentano — Psychology from an Empirical Standpoint (1874)}}). The question is how any merely physical state could have it. A pattern of neurons, or of voltages, is just a configuration of matter; how does it come to \emph{mean}, \emph{represent}, or \emph{point beyond itself} to something it may never be in contact with, or that may not even exist?

The puzzle is sharpened by a distinction between \emph{original} (or intrinsic) and \emph{derived} intentionality (see \hyperref[entry:concept-intrinsic-derived-intentionality]{\textsc{Intrinsic and derived intentionality}}). The words on this page are about things only because readers interpret them — their aboutness is \emph{derived}, borrowed from minds that already mean things. The challenge is to say where \emph{original} aboutness comes from: the kind a mind has on its own, not in virtue of being read by another mind. If a thought's content were derived in the same way a word's is, the question would only regress to whoever does the deriving.

\webheading{Sub-issues}

\begin{itemize}
  \item Whether original intentionality can be \emph{naturalised} — explained in terms of causal, informational, or biological-teleological relations between a state and what it is about — or whether it is primitive.
  \item Whether a computer's internal states have original intentionality or only the derived kind we read into them, the crux of \hyperref[entry:concept-chinese-room]{\textsc{the Chinese Room}} and of \hyperref[entry:claim-syntax-insufficient-for-semantics]{\textsc{whether syntax suffices for semantics}}.
  \item How intentionality relates to consciousness: whether aboutness requires a subject for whom the state means something, or can be wholly a matter of functional and causal role.
\end{itemize}

\begin{plainterms}
Your thought about your grandmother is \emph{about} her — it points at her, even if she's far away or no longer alive. That pointing is strange when you remember a thought is, physically, just brain activity: how does a lump of firing cells manage to be \emph{about} anything outside itself? Ink and pixels are ``about'' things too, but only because we read them that way — borrowed meaning. The hard part is explaining the \emph{original} meaning in a mind, the meaning that isn't just lent to it by some other mind. And it bears directly on AI: when a chatbot writes a sentence about Paris, is it really thinking about Paris, or just shuffling symbols we interpret as being about Paris?
\end{plainterms}

\entryhead{claim-conscious-content-is-product-like}{Product-like experience}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{Product-like experience}{The contents of a conscious moment are co-present, structured as a conjunctive product of qualia channels}{$\odot$}
A moment of experience realizes all of its components at once: the visual, auditory, and proprioceptive channels are simultaneously present in a single field, the way an element of a mathematical \emph{product} is a tuple realizing every one of its coordinates (see \hyperref[entry:concept-categorical-duality]{\textsc{Categorical duality}}). Experience combines its \hyperref[entry:concept-qualia]{\textsc{Qualia}} channels conjunctively — this sight \emph{and} this sound \emph{and} this bodily feel — never by settling on one to the exclusion of the rest.

The claim concerns the combinatorial \emph{shape} of conscious content, not its substrate or its explanation. It is one of the two structural premises installing consciousness and free choice on dual poles (see \hyperref[entry:concept-consciousness-freewill-duality]{\textsc{Consciousness–free-will duality}}), opposite \hyperref[entry:claim-action-content-is-coproduct-like]{\textsc{Coproduct-like choice}}. It is denied, in its crude form, by the observation that attention makes experience partially exclusive (see \hyperref[entry:argument-graded-polarity]{\textsc{Graded-polarity objection}}), and it is qualified from within: unified experience arguably \emph{fails} to factorize into independent channels, making consciousness the obstruction to full product structure rather than its instance (see \hyperref[entry:claim-binding-is-obstruction-to-product]{\textsc{Binding as failed factorization}}).

\entryhead{claim-chinese-room-runs-program}{Program execution (Chinese room)}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, after Searle}{Program execution (Chinese room)}{A person who understands no Chinese can, by executing a formal program, produce responses indistinguishable from a Chinese speaker's}{$\odot$}
This is the scenario premise of the \hyperref[entry:concept-chinese-room]{\textsc{Chinese room}}: purely formal symbol-manipulation — matching input Chinese strings to output Chinese strings by a rulebook keyed to shape, not meaning — can in principle reproduce the full conversational behaviour of a Chinese speaker. The executor runs the program but need not, and by stipulation does not, understand the symbols.

It is a possibility-or-setup claim about what \emph{formal computation} can reproduce \emph{behaviourally}. It asserts nothing yet about understanding; it grants the program its behavioural success precisely so that the next verdict — that there is \hyperref[entry:claim-chinese-room-no-understanding]{\textsc{no understanding in the room}} — can isolate the question of \emph{semantics}.

One could deny it by holding that no purely syntactic rulebook could actually sustain open-ended Chinese conversation — a tractability or combinatorial worry, parallel to the lookup-table size objections (see \hyperref[entry:claim-lookup-table-combinatorially-inadequate]{\textsc{Combinatorially inadequate table}}). On that reading the room shares the coherence-of-scenario vulnerability that the \emph{thought-experiment} pattern flags as its \emph{coherence} critical question (CQ-coherence).

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-lookup-table-possible]{\textsc{Lookup-table possibility}} because that system is flat storage with no processing, whereas the room \emph{runs a program} (mediating, rule-governed steps); it therefore instantiates computation, not mere lookup, and so bears on computational functionalism rather than on behaviourism alone.

\begin{plainterms}
This just sets the stage: a person who knows no Chinese, following a thick enough rulebook, could in principle hold up his end of a Chinese conversation so well that no one could tell. It says nothing yet about whether he \emph{understands} — that comes next. The only point here is that getting all the right outputs by following rules is possible.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-chinese-room]{\textsc{Chinese room}} — the thought experiment this premise sets up.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-chinese-room-no-understanding]{\textsc{Chinese room: no understanding}} — the verdict this premise is staged to isolate.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-lookup-table-possible]{\textsc{Lookup-table possibility}} — the flat-storage cousin, distinguished: the room runs a program rather than storing answers.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-lookup-table-combinatorially-inadequate]{\textsc{Combinatorially inadequate table}} — the tractability worry one might press against the scenario.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{claim-programs-are-syntactic}{Programs are syntactic}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, after Searle}{Programs are syntactic}{Computer programs are purely formal (syntactic)}{$\odot$}
A computer program is defined purely \emph{formally}: it specifies operations on symbols solely in virtue of their shapes (their syntax), with no reference to what, if anything, those symbols mean. This is the first axiom (A1) of Searle's formal derivation of the \hyperref[entry:concept-chinese-room]{\textsc{Chinese Room}} conclusion (see \hyperref[source:source-searle-1990]{\textsc{Searle — Is the Brain's Mind a Computer Program? (1990)}}). It is close to a truth of definition: computation, in the Turing sense, just \emph{is} the rule-governed manipulation of uninterpreted tokens, which is why the same program can be implemented in silicon, in cogs, or by a person shuffling cards.

\textbf{Scope.} The claim is about what a program \emph{is}, not about what a programmed machine can \emph{do}. It does not say programs are useless or that they cannot model cognition; it says their identity conditions are syntactic — a point granted on nearly all sides, including by computationalists.

\textbf{Who would deny it.} Almost no one denies A1 directly; it is the least controversial axiom. The pressure in the derivation falls instead on \hyperref[entry:claim-syntax-insufficient-for-semantics]{\textsc{A3}}, the claim that syntax is not \emph{sufficient} for semantics. One could resist A1 only by enriching ``program'' to include semantic or causal-grounding conditions — but that concedes Searle's point that \emph{pure} computation is not enough, and shifts to the \hyperref[entry:argument-robot-reply]{\textsc{Robot Reply}}'s terrain.

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-syntax-insufficient-for-semantics]{\textsc{Syntax insufficient for semantics}}: A1 says programs \emph{are} syntactic; A3 says syntax \emph{does not suffice} for meaning. A1 is near-definitional; A3 is the contested premise.

\begin{plainterms}
A computer program is just a set of rules for pushing symbols around based on their shapes — ``if you see this squiggle, write that one.'' It never mentions what the squiggles \emph{mean}; that's why the very same program runs on a laptop, a phone, or a person with a rulebook. This is the tame, almost obvious starting point of Searle's argument. The fight is not here but at the next step: whether shape-shuffling alone could ever add up to \emph{understanding}.
\end{plainterms}

\entryhead{claim-programs-not-sufficient-for-minds}{Programs not sufficient for minds}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, after Searle}{Programs not sufficient for minds}{Implementing a program is neither constitutive of nor sufficient for having a mind}{$\odot$}
Running the right computer program is neither \emph{constitutive of} a mind nor \emph{sufficient for} one: no system has mental states \emph{in virtue of} implementing a program. This is conclusion C1 of Searle's formal \hyperref[entry:concept-chinese-room]{\textsc{Chinese Room}} derivation (see \hyperref[source:source-searle-1990]{\textsc{Searle — Is the Brain's Mind a Computer Program? (1990)}}), following from \hyperref[entry:claim-programs-are-syntactic]{\textsc{A1}} (programs are syntactic), \hyperref[entry:claim-minds-have-semantics]{\textsc{A2}} (minds have semantics), and \hyperref[entry:claim-syntax-insufficient-for-semantics]{\textsc{A3}} (syntax is not sufficient for semantics). It is the direct denial of \textbf{strong AI} (see \hyperref[entry:concept-strong-weak-ai]{\textsc{Strong and weak AI}}) — the thesis that the right program \emph{is} a mind — while leaving \emph{weak} AI (the computer as a model of mind) untouched.

\textbf{Scope.} C1 concerns \emph{programs as such} — pure formal implementation. It does \emph{not} say machines can never think, nor that artificial minds are impossible: a non-program artificial system with the right causal powers might (the \hyperref[entry:argument-many-mansions-reply]{\textsc{Many Mansions Reply}} presses exactly this gap). What C1 denies is that \emph{computation alone} delivers a mind.

\textbf{Who would deny it.} Computational \hyperref[entry:concept-functionalism]{\textsc{functionalists}} and defenders of \hyperref[entry:claim-substrate-independence]{\textsc{substrate independence}} deny C1: for them implementing the right program-role just \emph{is} having the corresponding mental states. Their objections — the \hyperref[entry:argument-systems-reply]{\textsc{Systems}}, \hyperref[entry:argument-robot-reply]{\textsc{Robot}}, and \hyperref[entry:argument-brain-simulator-reply]{\textsc{Brain Simulator}} replies — all aim, in the end, to block the move to C1 by denying A3.

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-syntax-insufficient-for-semantics]{\textsc{Syntax insufficient for semantics}} (A3): A3 is the \emph{general} principle about syntax and semantics; C1 is its \emph{application to minds and programs} — the conclusion A3 is wielded to reach.

\begin{plainterms}
The payoff of Searle's argument: no amount of running-the-right-software is, by itself, enough to \emph{be} a mind or to \emph{have} one. A program is just shape-shuffling (step one), real minds actually mean things (step two), and shuffling shapes can't manufacture meaning (step three) — so software alone can't add up to a mind. Note the careful limit: this says \emph{programs} aren't enough, not that machines can never think. A suitably built machine with the right causal powers isn't ruled out — only the idea that the \emph{code} is the mind.
\end{plainterms}

\entryhead{position-proteocentrism}{Proteocentrism}{Position \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{Proteocentrism}{}{$\bigstar$}
\emph{Proteocentrism} is the view that only protein-based biological life can be a bearer of consciousness, and so the only kind of thing that can have moral status. On this view the carbon substrate of living brains is not an incidental detail of how minds happen to be implemented on Earth but a genuine requirement: no system built from silicon and metal, however sophisticated its behaviour, could literally feel anything or merit moral consideration. The name comes from \emph{proteo-}, protein.

\webheading{What it commits to}

Proteocentrism is defined by its rejection of \hyperref[entry:claim-substrate-independence]{\textsc{substrate independence}} — the thesis that mental states depend only on a system's functional organization, not on the material that realizes it. Where the substrate-independence thesis holds that a mind is a matter of \emph{pattern} and could in principle run on any medium that preserves the pattern, proteocentrism holds that the medium itself is part of what makes a mind a mind. Applied to the organizing question of \hyperref[entry:question-llm-moral-status]{\textsc{the moral status of AI}}, it returns a flat negative: no non-biological system qualifies.

\webheading{Where it sits}

The view is the natural opposite of substrate independence, and stronger than the bare denial of it: one could deny that \emph{any} arrangement of matter suffices for mind while still allowing some non-biological substrates, whereas proteocentrism singles out biology specifically. It is stated here as a stand-alone position so that characters in the debate can cite it rather than restate it. Reasons \emph{for} it are not part of the position itself; they live in separate argument nodes that support its commitments.

\begin{plainterms}
The view that only living, biological things — stuff built from carbon-based proteins, like brains — could ever be conscious or matter morally. On this view the ``meat'' isn't an incidental detail of how \emph{our} minds happen to be built; it's a genuine requirement. So no computer, however cleverly it behaves, could really feel anything or deserve moral consideration. It's the natural opposite of the idea that a mind is about the \emph{pattern} a system runs, not the material it's made of. (The name comes from \emph{proteo-}, protein.)
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-substrate-independence]{\textsc{Substrate-independence thesis}} — the thesis proteocentrism is the denial of; a mind as pattern, indifferent to its material.
  \item \hyperref[entry:question-llm-moral-status]{\textsc{Moral status of LLMs}} — the organizing question proteocentrism answers in the negative for every non-biological system.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{claim-prudence-favors-flesh-criterion}{Prudence favours flesh}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, contested \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{Prudence favours flesh}{Prudence favours resting moral standing on currently-unforgeable biology rather than on forgeable behavioural signs}{$\odot$}
A \emph{play-it-safe policy}, not a verdict about which things have minds. Because behavioural signs of suffering are cheap to fake while living biology, for now, is not, the prudent course is to let flesh carry the weight of moral standing and place the burden of proof on the non-biological mind. This is caution under uncertainty, not a metaphysical claim. It is offered as a precautionary, withhold-by-default candidate answer to \hyperref[entry:question-llm-moral-status]{\textsc{Moral status of LLMs}}.

The claim is about \emph{justified caution and policy}, not about what does or does not possess a mind. The underlying worry throughout is \emph{criterion-gaming} (see \hyperref[entry:concept-criterion-gaming]{\textsc{Criterion gaming}}): a standard that is cheap to fake invites the very forgery that would empty it of meaning. Two recorded limits keep the claim contested:

\begin{itemize}
  \item \emph{Unforgeability is not flesh.} The premises favour whatever sign is hardest to forge, and biology wins only contingently (see \hyperref[entry:claim-biology-currently-unforgeable]{\textsc{Biology currently unforgeable}}). A non-biological unforgeable sign would displace flesh, so the claim does not establish \hyperref[entry:position-proteocentrism]{\textsc{Proteocentrism}} — it lends that position only defeasible, prudential support.
  \item \emph{The false-positive worry.} The claim inherits a contested bridge premise (see \hyperref[entry:claim-forgeable-sign-unfit-as-criterion]{\textsc{Forgeable sign unfit as criterion}}): forgeability may bound false positives without nullifying a sign's evidential value.
\end{itemize}

It would be denied by proponents of the \hyperref[entry:argument-behavioral-parity]{\textsc{Behavioural-parity argument}}, who hold that behaviour is good defeasible evidence of a mind, and by anyone who regards the burden-of-proof asymmetry against machines as itself unmotivated (compare \hyperref[entry:argument-access-symmetry-rebuttal]{\textsc{Access-symmetry rebuttal}} on unearned asymmetries).

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:position-proteocentrism]{\textsc{Proteocentrism}} (a metaphysical thesis) and from \hyperref[entry:claim-access-grounds-moral-status-asymmetry]{\textsc{Access-based moral asymmetry}}, which rests the asymmetry on first-person access rather than on forgeability.

\begin{plainterms}
A play-it-safe policy, not a claim about what machines really are. Since acting distressed is cheap to fake but living biology (for now) isn't, the cautious move is to rest moral standing on the hard-to-fake biological mark and make the non-biological case prove itself. Two built-in caveats keep it modest: ``hard to fake'' isn't the same as ``must be flesh'' — a future unforgeable \emph{non}-biological sign would do just as well — and a forgeable sign (like a fingerprint) can still be useful evidence rather than worthless.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:question-llm-moral-status]{\textsc{Moral status of LLMs}} — the question this is a precautionary candidate answer to.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-criterion-gaming]{\textsc{Criterion gaming}} — the gaming worry that motivates the caution.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-biology-currently-unforgeable]{\textsc{Biology currently unforgeable}} — the contingent premise that biology is, for now, hard to fake.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-forgeable-sign-unfit-as-criterion]{\textsc{Forgeable sign unfit as criterion}} — the contested bridge premise behind the policy.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-behavioral-parity]{\textsc{Behavioural-parity argument}} — the view whose proponents deny the claim.
  \item \hyperref[entry:position-proteocentrism]{\textsc{Proteocentrism}} and \hyperref[entry:claim-access-grounds-moral-status-asymmetry]{\textsc{Access-based moral asymmetry}} — the two views this prudential claim is distinguished from.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-access-symmetry-rebuttal]{\textsc{Access-symmetry rebuttal}} — on unearned asymmetries, pressed against the burden shift.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{claim-prudence-tracks-unforgeability-not-flesh}{Prudence tracks unforgeability}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{Prudence tracks unforgeability}{What prudence favours is the hardest-to-forge sign, not biology specifically}{$\odot$}
The prudential case for resting moral standing on a hard-to-fake mark selects \emph{unforgeability} itself — whatever sign is currently hardest to game — and gives biology no privileged role beyond its being, contingently, that sign. Because biology's resistance to counterfeit is time-indexed (see \hyperref[entry:claim-biology-currently-unforgeable]{\textsc{Biology currently unforgeable}}), a non-biological sign that became at least as unforgeable would satisfy the same rationale and displace flesh.

The point concerns \emph{which property the prudential argument actually selects} — unforgeability — not which substrates can bear minds. It does not deny that biology is presently the unforgeable option; it denies that the prudential reasoning licenses fixing on \emph{flesh} rather than on unforgeability-in-general. The worry the argument answers to is criterion-gaming (see \hyperref[entry:concept-criterion-gaming]{\textsc{Criterion gaming}}): the search is for a mark that cannot be cheaply produced without the condition it signals.

It would be denied by anyone holding that biology is not merely \emph{currently} but \emph{necessarily} the only unforgeable mark of a mind — so that ``track unforgeability'' and ``track flesh'' cannot come apart — and by a proteocentrist who wants the criterion tied to carbon for metaphysical rather than prudential reasons.

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-biology-currently-unforgeable]{\textsc{Biology currently unforgeable}}, which is the empirical, time-indexed fact that biology is hard to fake \emph{now}; this is the normative, structural point that the prudential rule tracks unforgeability \emph{as such}, so flesh has no standing beyond that contingent fact.

\begin{plainterms}
The ``play it safe, demand the hard-to-fake mark'' argument doesn't actually single out \emph{flesh} — it singles out \emph{whatever is hardest to fake}. Biology only qualifies because, for now, it happens to be hard to counterfeit. If some non-biological sign ever became just as forgery-proof, the very same caution would back \emph{it} instead. So the cautious policy is about unforgeability, not about being made of meat.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-biology-currently-unforgeable]{\textsc{Biology currently unforgeable}} — the empirical fact this normative point is distinguished from.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-unforgeability-is-not-flesh]{\textsc{Unforgeability, not flesh}} — the argument that concludes to this claim.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-criterion-gaming]{\textsc{Criterion gaming}} — the gaming worry the prudential rule is built to dodge.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{argument-storage-not-stable-kind}{Pure-storage instability}{Argument \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{Pure-storage instability}{Pure storage is not a stable kind at mind-matching scale}{$\vdash$}
The \emph{pure-storage-instability} reply argues that ``behaviour by mere storage, with no internal organisation'' is not a kind of thing anything could actually be at the scale of a mind. Grant, for argument's sake, that some imagined machine produces a whole mind's worth of behaviour just by looking answers up in a stored list; the claim here is that no \emph{physically realisable} machine could work that way. Any buildable system that matched the behaviour would have to compute its responses, and computing is organised inner processing — exactly what ``pure storage'' was meant to do without.

\webheading{The inference}

The argument is \emph{deductive}: it runs from the unrealisability of flat storage to the organisation of any realisable matcher.

\begin{enumerate}
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-lookup-table-physically-unrealisable]{\textsc{A flat table cannot be built}}. A flat table with roughly $v^n$ rows cannot be physically built — there is nowhere to \emph{store} that many responses.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-lookup-table-size-exponential]{\textsc{Anything smaller must compute}}. Any representation smaller than \textasciitilde{}$v^n$ must \emph{compute} which response a given input maps to, rather than retrieve a pre-stored whole — and computing is processing over internal organisation, not lookup.
\end{enumerate}

Therefore \hyperref[entry:claim-realisable-behaviour-matcher-is-organised]{\textsc{every physically realisable behaviour-matcher is an organised computer}}. ``Mere storage, no organisation'' — the defining property of the \hyperref[entry:concept-lookup-table-mind]{\textsc{lookup-table mind}} — is not a kind anything can instantiate at mind-matching scale.

\webheading{Bearing on the bounded table}

This is a rejoinder to the \hyperref[entry:argument-bounded-table-rejoinder]{\textsc{bounded-table rejoinder}}. Grant Block (see \hyperref[source:source-block]{\textsc{Block — Psychologism and Behaviorism (1981)}}) his \hyperref[entry:claim-lifetime-bounded-table-covers-encountered-inputs]{\textsc{lifetime-bounded table}} that \emph{covers} the inputs; it still fails as a counterexample, because any realisation of it is an organised computer, not the no-organisation system the objection requires. So the move attacks \hyperref[entry:claim-lookup-table-possible]{\textsc{the separability of behaviour from organisation}} on its realisable reading and undercuts the transfer of the \hyperref[entry:argument-lookup-table-objection]{\textsc{lookup-table objection}} to actual systems: the only real ``blockheads'' are organised computers, such as large language models, which organisational-role functionalism may credit with mind — a question of \hyperref[entry:concept-grain]{\textsc{Grain (of role individuation)}}.

\webheading{What it does not do}

The argument leaves the conditional \hyperref[entry:claim-lookup-table-lacks-mind]{\textsc{that pure lookup would lack mind}} standing — \emph{if} something ran by pure stored lookup it would lack mind. The point is only that nothing realisably does so at this scale. That makes it distinct from the scale-based deflation of \hyperref[entry:argument-intuition-domain-asymmetry]{\textsc{the domain-asymmetry argument}}: that move bounds the warrant of the no-mind \emph{intuition}, whereas this one denies the no-organisation \emph{object} is realisable at all. It is the standalone home of the ``storage isn't a stable kind'' reply.

\webheading{Weakest premise}

Premise 2's claim that the addressing and retrieval organisation is \emph{mind-relevant} rather than mere plumbing. Block may insist that a retrieval mechanism, however vast, is not cognitive organisation. The reply: with nothing to retrieve from (premise 1), the ``retrieval'' is computation, and the plumbing/computation distinction has no purchase once storage is impossible. Whether that fully answers the objection is the live crux of this whole sub-web.

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:argument-lookup-table-physical-impossibility]{\textsc{the physical-impossibility argument}} (which establishes premise 1 and stops at ``unbuildable''): this draws the further, positive conclusion about the \emph{kind} of the realisable alternative.

\begin{plainterms}
Here's a subtle reply to the cheat-sheet idea — the objection that a giant stored answer-table could act mindlike with nobody home (see \hyperref[entry:argument-lookup-table-objection]{\textsc{the lookup-table objection}}). That objection pictures a thing producing mind-like behavior by \emph{pure storage} — just looking answers up, with no real processing or organization inside. But push on the size. A flat table big enough to cover a mind's behavior can't physically exist (no room in the universe). So any machine that actually \emph{fits} and \emph{matches} the behavior must be storing far less than the full list — which means it has to \emph{work each answer out} on the fly rather than just fetch it. And working answers out \emph{is} processing — organized inner machinery — exactly the thing ``pure storage'' was supposed to do without. Upshot: ``all the behavior, zero organization'' simply isn't an available option at mind scale; any buildable behavior-matcher (a real AI included) is an organized computer, not a dumb lookup. The sticking point: a die-hard could insist that even a giant fetch-and-retrieve system is still ``just plumbing,'' not genuine mind-like organization — and whether that holds up is the central open question in this corner of the debate.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-realisable-behaviour-matcher-is-organised]{\textsc{Behaviour-matchers are organised}} — the conclusion this argument establishes.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-lookup-table-physically-unrealisable]{\textsc{Physically unrealisable table}} — premise 1: the flat table cannot be stored.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-lookup-table-size-exponential]{\textsc{Exponential table size}} — premise 2: anything smaller must compute its answers.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-bounded-table-rejoinder]{\textsc{Bounded-table rejoinder}} — the Block move this replies to.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-lifetime-bounded-table-covers-encountered-inputs]{\textsc{Lifetime-bounded table}} — the coverage stipulation granted for the sake of argument.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-lookup-table-possible]{\textsc{Lookup-table possibility}} — the separability claim it attacks on the realisable reading.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-lookup-table-objection]{\textsc{Lookup-table objection}} — the objection whose transfer to actual systems it undercuts.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-lookup-table-lacks-mind]{\textsc{Mindlessness of pure lookup}} — the conditional it deliberately leaves standing.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-intuition-domain-asymmetry]{\textsc{Intuition-domain asymmetry}} — the scale-based deflation it is distinguished from.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-lookup-table-physical-impossibility]{\textsc{Cosmic-size reply}} — establishes premise 1 but stops at ``unbuildable''.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-grain]{\textsc{Grain (of role individuation)}} — the grain of role individuation on which organised computers may count as minded.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-lookup-table-mind]{\textsc{Lookup-table mind}} — the no-organisation construction shown to be unrealisable.
\end{seealso}

\end{multicols}
\bookgroup{Q}
\begin{multicols}{2}
\entryhead{concept-qualia}{Qualia}{Concept}{Qualia}{}{$\blacklozenge$}
\emph{Qualia} (singular \emph{quale}, from the Latin for ``of what kind'') are the subjective, felt qualities of conscious experience: the redness of a ripe tomato as it looks to you, the bitterness of coffee, the particular ache of a stubbed toe. The term names not the tomato, the coffee, or the toe, but what undergoing those experiences is \emph{like} from the inside. The word was given its modern use by Clarence Irving Lewis (see \hyperref[source:source-ci-lewis-1929]{\textsc{Lewis 1929}}), who applied it to the recognisable qualitative characters of experience that recur from one occasion to the next.

\webheading{The contrast with function}

The standard way to isolate the idea is to set it against everything a mental state \emph{does}. Two states might play exactly the same causal role, both produced by tissue damage and both making you wince and say ``ouch'', and one can still ask whether they \emph{feel} the same. That residue, the felt character left over once every causal role is accounted for, is what ``qualia'' picks out. It is the same residue Thomas Nagel marks with the phrase \emph{what it is like}: an organism has conscious experience, he argues, if there is something it is like to be that organism, and no objective, third-person description seems to capture it (see \hyperref[source:source-nagel-1974]{\textsc{Nagel 1974}}). Two stock cases sharpen the notion. The \emph{inverted spectrum} asks whether your experience of red could be like my experience of green with all of our behaviour and physiology unchanged; if that is so much as coherent, the felt quality floats free of the functional facts. And Frank Jackson's colour scientist Mary, who knows every physical fact about colour vision yet seems to learn something new when she first sees red, dramatises the same gap (the \hyperref[entry:concept-knowledge-argument]{\textsc{Knowledge Argument}}, from \hyperref[source:source-jackson-1982]{\textsc{Jackson 1982}}).

\webheading{Senses of the term}

The word is contested, and the dispute is often about the definition rather than the facts. In a \emph{thin} sense, qualia are simply the ways experiences feel, with no further commitment, and almost no one denies that there are such felt ways. In a \emph{thick} sense, qualia are additionally taken to be \emph{intrinsic}, \emph{ineffable}, \emph{private}, and \emph{directly apprehended} by introspection. Daniel Dennett's ``Quining Qualia'' argues that nothing satisfies that demanding job description, so qualia in the thick sense should be abandoned (see \hyperref[source:source-dennett-1988]{\textsc{Dennett 1988}}). A claim that looks obvious on the thin reading can beg the question on the thick one, so an entry that leans on qualia should say which sense it means. The notion is also the close cousin of \hyperref[entry:concept-phenomenal-consciousness]{\textsc{phenomenal consciousness}}: qualia are the specific felt qualities, and phenomenal consciousness is the general property of having any such felt character at all.

\webheading{Why qualia matter}

Qualia mark the spot where the mind seems hardest to fit into a scientific picture of the world.
