They drive the \hyperref[entry:concept-hard-problem]{\textsc{hard problem}} of consciousness, the question of why physical processing should be accompanied by felt experience at all, and they power the classic anti-materialist thought experiments: Mary's room (the \hyperref[entry:concept-knowledge-argument]{\textsc{Knowledge Argument}}) and the \hyperref[entry:concept-philosophical-zombie]{\textsc{philosophical zombie}}, a creature physically identical to you with no inner feel. Each turns on the suspicion that the felt facts outrun the functional and physical ones, which is exactly what a \hyperref[entry:concept-functionalism]{\textsc{functionalist}} account of mind must deny. How much weight qualia can bear, and whether the thick notion survives scrutiny, is in large part what the debate over materialism is about.

\begin{plainterms}
``Qualia'' is the philosopher's word for how things \emph{feel} to you: the way red looks, the way coffee tastes, the way a headache hurts. A brain scanner can show what your neurons are doing when you see red, but it does not show the redness \emph{you} experience; qualia are that inner, felt side of the story. Philosophers argue about whether these feels are something extra that science struggles to explain, or whether, as skeptics like Daniel Dennett say, the whole idea falls apart once you look at it closely.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-phenomenal-consciousness]{\textsc{Phenomenal consciousness}} — the general property of having any felt character at all; qualia are its specific instances.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-hard-problem]{\textsc{Hard problem}} — why physical processing is accompanied by felt experience at all; the problem qualia pose in its sharpest form.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-knowledge-argument]{\textsc{Knowledge argument}} — Mary the colour scientist, and whether complete physical knowledge still leaves the qualia out.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-philosophical-zombie]{\textsc{Philosophical zombie}} — a physical duplicate with no qualia; the conceivability test for whether the physical facts fix the felt facts.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-access-vs-phenomenal-consciousness]{\textsc{Access vs. phenomenal consciousness}} — Block's distinction; qualia live on the phenomenal side of it.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-functionalism]{\textsc{Functionalism}} — the theory of mind that qualia are most often said to outrun.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{argument-olympia-quantum-counterfactuals}{Quantum counterfactuals objection}{Argument \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{Quantum counterfactuals objection}{The quantum counterfactuals objection — unexercised possibilities have physical effects, and quantum runs have no trace to unroll}{$\vdash$}
The \emph{quantum counterfactuals objection} attacks the deepest premise of Maudlin's \hyperref[entry:argument-olympia]{\textsc{Olympia argument}} — the supervenience principle, on which only \emph{actually exercised} activity can matter to experience and causally untouched machinery cannot — by pointing out that this principle is classical physics elevated to metaphysics. In quantum mechanics, unexercised possibilities have measurable effects on actual outcomes; there are laboratory experiments in which a computer's output is obtained on runs where the computer, in the standard sense, never ran. The objection has two prongs. The first denies that ``two systems engaged in precisely the same physical activity'' is a physically fundamental classification, undercutting the supervenience premise directly. The second observes that the Klara→Olympia construction consumes a classically recorded execution trace, and a quantum-coherent computation \emph{has} none — so against a computationalism whose programs exploit quantum interference, Olympia cannot even be built. In the vocabulary of the thought-experiment pattern, the objection raises \textbf{CQ1 (coherence)} — the scenario's description smuggles in classical assumptions that our physics falsifies — and \textbf{CQ6 (scope)}: at most the argument touches classically traceable computation in the classical limit, a nomologically parochial result rather than the advertised conceptual impossibility.

The move runs:

\begin{enumerate}
  \item The supervenience premise rests on a classical picture of causation: what happens is a single definite history, and structure that history never touches is causally idle. Maudlin's pinball illustration — remove the pins the ball never hits and nothing about the actual run changes — states the picture exactly.
  \item Quantum mechanics falsifies the picture. Which paths are \emph{open} affects where a single particle lands even when it ``takes'' only one; an interferometer can detect a live bomb via a photon that never interacts with it, the bomb's unexercised disposition shifting the observed statistics (Elitzur \& Vaidman, \hyperref[source:source-elitzur-vaidman-1993]{\textsc{1993}}); and the output of a quantum computer can be extracted from runs on which the computer did not run — \emph{counterfactual computation}, theorized by Mitchison and Jozsa (see \hyperref[source:source-mitchison-jozsa-2001]{\textsc{2001}}) and realized in the laboratory by Hosten and colleagues (see \hyperref[source:source-hosten-2006]{\textsc{2006}}).
  \item So systems differing only in never-triggered machinery are \emph{not}, at the fundamental level, ``engaged in precisely the same physical activity'': the machinery is part of the total physical configuration, and physical law permits such differences to matter to what actually happens.
  \item Independently: the Klara→Olympia transformation presupposes that the conscious-making run has a classically recordable trace — which cell, which value, at every step. A quantum-coherent run has no such trace: recording each step's value is a measurement that destroys the interference the computation exploits, and copying the state instead is barred by the no-cloning theorem (Wootters \& Zurek, \hyperref[source:source-wootters-zurek-1982]{\textsc{1982}}).
\end{enumerate}

∴ The trilemma fails twice over against a physically literate computationalism: its supervenience premise is at best a classical approximation, not a principle fit to constrain theories of consciousness; and its central construction cannot be mounted at all against computation that exploits coherence. What survives is at most a contingent claim about the classical limit.

\webheading{Counterfactuals with causal teeth}

The pinball illustration is the supervenience premise's whole evidential base, and quantum mechanics reverses its verdict at the scale where physics is fundamental. Shrink the pinball to a photon and the board to an interferometer, and removing pins the ball ``never touches'' \emph{changes where it lands}: interference depends on the whole configuration of open paths, not on the one path a classical eye would say was taken. The Elitzur–Vaidman bomb tester (see \hyperref[source:source-elitzur-vaidman-1993]{\textsc{1993}}) sharpens this into a working instrument: a photon sent through an interferometer can reveal the presence of a live bomb — one that any interaction would detonate — \emph{without} the interaction occurring. The bomb never fires; its mere capacity to absorb is what destroys the interference and shows up in the detectors. Mitchison and Jozsa (see \hyperref[source:source-mitchison-jozsa-2001]{\textsc{2001}}) generalized the trick from bombs to computers, and Hosten and colleagues (see \hyperref[source:source-hosten-2006]{\textsc{2006}}) performed it: a quantum search yields its answer on runs in which, by the experiment's own bookkeeping, the computer did not run. ``What would have happened but didn't'' is, in our physics, a working part of what does happen.

Deutsch has pressed the general moral for decades: interference phenomena reveal the causal relevance of unactualized histories (see \hyperref[source:source-deutsch-1997]{\textsc{1997}}), and constructor theory (see \hyperref[source:source-deutsch-2013]{\textsc{2013}}) proposes outright that fundamental physics be \emph{formulated} in counterfactuals — which transformations are possible and impossible — with the actual as a derivative special case. One need not buy the whole program to take the lesson: a metaphysical principle that quarantines counterfactuals from causal relevance is not neutral bookkeeping; it is a substantive physical thesis, and apparently a false one.

\webheading{The supervenience premise reconsidered}

Maudlin needs the claim that the bare armature and Olympia-with-machinery are ``engaged in precisely the same physical activity'', so that whatever differs between them is causally idle. At the fundamental level this is simply not so: the blocked Klaras, floats, and chains are part of the total configuration, and the system's physical evolution — its Hamiltonian, its space of interfering histories — differs with their presence. Restate the supervenience principle in honest quantum terms (``two systems with the same physical state and evolution support the same experience'') and it no longer classifies the two machines as duplicates, so it can no longer force the contradiction. Retreat to a coarse-grained reading (``the same \emph{classical} activity'') and the premise ceases to be an evident metaphysical truth and becomes a choice of description — exactly the point in dispute, since the computationalist holds that dispositional structure is part of what a system is physically doing. The rhetorically strongest moves of the paper fare worst here: the ``argument by subtraction'' insists that as the chains rust through, counterfactual support lapses ``with no physical event to mark the passage'' — but there is \emph{always} a physical difference as a chain rusts; what there fails to be is a classical, macroscopic event. And Maudlin's own closing demand — that the counterfactuals constituting a program be ``carried by the system's actual causal activity, not by inert scaffolding'' — is one quantum mechanics happily grants: dispositions \emph{are} carried by actual, categorical physical structure. That was the reply the pinball was built to kill, and the pinball does not survive quantization.

\webheading{No trace to unroll}

The second prong turns on the reconstruction in \hyperref[entry:argument-olympia-trace-specialization]{\textsc{Olympia as trace specialization}}. Its \emph{Effectiveness} corollary — Olympia is computable from the program and input alone — holds because a classical run is \emph{measurement-transparent}: it can be watched, logged, and replayed without being changed. Quantum computation is defined by the failure of exactly this property. Its power comes from interference among computational paths, which exists only so long as no record exists — anywhere — of which path was taken; logging each step's value is a measurement that collapses the run, and the no-cloning theorem (Wootters \& Zurek, \hyperref[source:source-wootters-zurek-1982]{\textsc{1982}}) forecloses the workaround of copying the machine's state for later replay. A quantum Klara therefore has no trace log Λ, and without Λ there is no reordered tape, no float presets, no placement of blocked copies: \emph{the Klara→Olympia transformation is a classical-only operation}. Since Deutsch (see \hyperref[source:source-deutsch-1985]{\textsc{1985}}) it has been a commonplace that the theory of computation is a branch of physics — what counts as running a program is fixed by physical law, not a priori. Maudlin's argument quantifies over ``any machine running the conscious-making program π'' but its construction silently assumes π's runs are classically recordable; a computationalism whose programs exploit coherence sits outside the argument's reach from the first page.

\webheading{The weakest premise}

The objection's exposure is \textbf{decoherence}. Olympia's blocked copies are warm, macroscopic clockwork; the interference terms their presence contributes to the armature's run are suppressed beyond any physical relevance, and the classical description of the machine is for every practical purpose exact. A defender of Maudlin will say the supervenience premise was never meant as fundamental law, only as a truth about the quasiclassical domain where Olympia (and every brain) lives — and a coarse-grained principle is all the trilemma needs, provided the systems it compares really are decohered. The first prong must then show that this retreat to coarse-graining begs the question rather than merely idealizes, and that a principle demoted from metaphysical necessity to excellent approximation can no longer bear the argument's modal weight (``\emph{no} computational theory of consciousness \emph{can} be correct''). The second prong has the mirror exposure: if the conscious-making program is effectively classical — as most computationalists themselves hold about brains — then its trace \emph{is} recordable, Olympia is buildable against it, and the prong protects only a computationalism prepared to wager, with Penrose (see \hyperref[source:source-penrose-1994]{\textsc{1994}}), that consciousness exploits quantum coherence — a wager most computationalists refuse. The honest net effect is a demotion, not a refutation: Maudlin's conclusion shrinks from a conceptual impossibility to a contingent claim about classically traceable computation in the classical limit — while the argument's presentation, and its claim to constrain \emph{any} possible computational theory, trade on the stronger reading.

\begin{plainterms}
Quantum physics contains a genuine surprise about ``what didn't happen'': it can leave fingerprints on what did. There is a famous scheme — the bomb tester (see \hyperref[source:source-elitzur-vaidman-1993]{\textsc{1993}}) — in which a single particle of light reveals that a bomb is live \emph{without touching it}: the bomb never goes off, nothing hits it, and yet the pattern at the detectors is different because the bomb \emph{would have} absorbed the light. Physicists have even run a computer this way, reading off the answer from rounds in which the computer never ran (see \hyperref[source:source-hosten-2006]{\textsc{2006}}). Now recall what Maudlin's machine (see \hyperref[entry:argument-olympia]{\textsc{Olympia argument}}) is built on: the claim that standby machinery which never does anything obviously cannot matter to what the system experiences. Physics itself says that principle is not a deep law — in our world, machinery that never fires can still shape what happens. And there is a second, separate problem: Maudlin's whole recipe starts by \emph{filming} the computer's run, step by step, so as to replay it. A quantum computation cannot be filmed — peeking at each step destroys it, and physics forbids photocopying its state (see \hyperref[source:source-wootters-zurek-1982]{\textsc{1982}}). So if a mind's program used quantum effects, the replay machine could never be built in the first place. The fine print: for big, warm machinery — and probably for brains — these quantum effects are absurdly tiny, so the objection doesn't show Maudlin's machine misfires in practice. It shows his key principle is a rule of thumb about medium-sized objects, not a law of nature — and an argument claiming to rule out every possible computational theory of mind needs a law, not a rule of thumb.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-olympia]{\textsc{Olympia argument}} — the thought experiment attacked; the objection lands on its supervenience premise and on the scope of its conclusion.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-olympia-trace-specialization]{\textsc{Olympia as trace specialization}} — the formal reconstruction whose \emph{Effectiveness} corollary the second prong bounds to classical, measurement-transparent computation.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-olympia-recording-objection]{\textsc{Recording objection to Olympia}} — the companion objection: it grants the classical setting and relocates the experience to the recording run, where this objection denies the setting's physics; the two can be run together.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-substrate-independence]{\textsc{Substrate-independence thesis}} — the thesis defended; quantum mechanics, far from threatening it, supplies the categorical physical basis for dispositional structure.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-implementation-requires-counterfactual-structure]{\textsc{Counterfactual constraint on implementation}} — the account of implementation whose counterfactuals quantum physics renders categorical and causally active rather than inert.
\end{seealso}

\end{multicols}
\bookgroup{R}
\begin{multicols}{2}
\entryhead{claim-reality-is-fundamentally-mental}{Reality as fundamentally mental}{Claim}{Reality as fundamentally mental}{Reality is fundamentally mental}{$\odot$}
What is fundamental is mind or experience: the physical world is constituted by, or derivative of, consciousness rather than the reverse. The claim states an \emph{idealist monism} — one fundamental kind of thing, the mental — and is the inverse of physicalism on the direction of grounding.

Historically it takes several forms: Berkeley's subjective idealism, on which to be is to be perceived (see \hyperref[source:source-berkeley-1710]{\textsc{Berkeley — A Treatise Concerning the Principles of Human Knowledge (1710)}}); Kastrup's \emph{analytic idealism}, one universal mind whose dissociations are individual subjects (see \hyperref[source:source-kastrup-2019]{\textsc{Kastrup — The Idea of the World (2019)}}); and Hoffman's conscious realism, on which spacetime is a perceptual interface rather than something fundamental (see \hyperref[source:source-hoffman-2008]{\textsc{Hoffman — Conscious Realism and the Mind-Body Problem (2008)}}). In each, the physical is an appearance \emph{within} mind.

The claim dissolves the hard problem from the far side — there is no puzzle of getting mind from matter, because matter is an appearance within mind — but it inherits the mirror-image burden, a \emph{decomposition} or \emph{structure} problem of deriving the law-governed, shared physical world from mind. It is denied by physicalists (matter is fundamental), dualists (two fundamental kinds), and neutral monists (the base is neither mental nor physical); most contemporary philosophy treats idealism as a minority view, though it is resurgent.

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-reality-fundamental-stuff-neutral]{\textsc{the claim that the base is neutral}} (which makes the fundamental stuff neither mental nor physical) and from \hyperref[entry:claim-consciousness-fundamental-ubiquitous]{\textsc{panpsychism's claim}} (on which mind is fundamental \emph{in} matter, not the ground \emph{of} it).

\begin{plainterms}
The idea is that mind comes first and the physical world is something that appears \emph{inside} experience, not a mind-free stuff out of which minds are later made. If that is right, the puzzle of how lifeless matter could ever produce feeling never gets started — but you then owe an account of why this ``appearance'' is so orderly, lawful, and the same for everyone.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-reality-fundamental-stuff-neutral]{\textsc{Neutral fundamental stuff}} — the neutral-monist rival: base neither mental nor physical.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-consciousness-fundamental-ubiquitous]{\textsc{Fundamental ubiquitous consciousness}} — panpsychism: mind fundamental \emph{in} matter, not the ground \emph{of} it.
  \item \hyperref[entry:position-idealism]{\textsc{Idealism}} — the position built on this claim.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{claim-phenomenal-consciousness-is-real}{Reality of phenomenal consciousness}{Claim}{Reality of phenomenal consciousness}{Phenomenal consciousness is genuinely real, not an illusion}{$\odot$}
There really is something it is like to undergo experience: felt redness, the ache of a pain, the taste of coffee are genuine features of reality, not a cognitive illusion and not a mere disposition to report. This is \emph{realism} about phenomenal consciousness — the explanandum is taken at face value rather than explained away. It is a premise shared across most non-illusionist views of mind, including dualism, panpsychism, Russellian monism, and type-B physicalism. In the \hyperref[entry:argument-genetic-panpsychism]{\textsc{genetic argument}} for panpsychism it pairs with \hyperref[entry:claim-no-radical-emergence]{\textsc{No radical emergence}}: because experience is real \emph{and} cannot brutely emerge, it must be fundamental. The claim is denied only by illusionists, who hold that phenomenal consciousness as standardly conceived does not exist (see \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenality-is-illusion]{\textsc{Phenomenality as illusion}}).

It should not be confused with the stronger \emph{residue} claim that felt states are not exhausted by their functional role (see \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-residue]{\textsc{Phenomenal residue}}): one can affirm that experience is real while still debating whether it reduces to function. This claim asserts only its reality, not its irreducibility.

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenality-is-illusion]{\textsc{Phenomenality as illusion}} — its direct denial, held by illusionists.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-residue]{\textsc{Phenomenal residue}} — the stronger, separate claim that experience outruns functional role.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-genetic-panpsychism]{\textsc{Genetic argument}} — the argument in which this realism premise does its work.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{argument-olympia-recording-objection}{Recording objection to Olympia}{Argument \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{Recording objection to Olympia}{The recording objection — every Olympia descends from a genuine run of the program, so the trilemma's horns lose their absurdity}{$\vdash$}
The \emph{recording objection} answers Maudlin's \hyperref[entry:argument-olympia]{\textsc{Olympia argument}} by asking where Olympia came from. Building her requires the trace log of the program π running on the input \emph{r} — and a trace log can only be produced by actually \emph{performing} that computation. So every buildable Olympia has, in her causal ancestry, a genuine, counterfactually-live run of π on \emph{r}, and by the computationalist's own sufficiency thesis that run may very well be a conscious episode. Maudlin's argument compares Klara with Olympia as two free-standing systems and invites the verdict that the second, a mere recording, is obviously not conscious. The objection charges that this comparison skips the process that made Olympia. Once the recording run is on the ledger, no experience is missing from the story and nothing miraculous is ever switched on or off; whether the replay \emph{also} hosts an experience remains an open question — it may or may not — and no answer to it embarrasses computationalism. In the vocabulary of the thought-experiment pattern, the objection raises \textbf{CQ2 (verdict)} — the intuition ``the recording is not conscious'' is either mundane and already honoured, or not robust at all when the case is stated fairly — and \textbf{CQ6 (scope)}: whatever the case shows, it is not that consciousness fails to be computational.

The move runs:

\begin{enumerate}
  \item Olympia is assembled from the trace log Λ of π's run on \emph{r}, and the log is obtainable only by performing the computation of π on \emph{r} — on Klara, on a simulator, or by hand. A program's run is in general computationally irreducible: there is no shortcut to the values the log records (the \emph{Effectiveness} corollary of \hyperref[entry:argument-olympia-trace-specialization]{\textsc{Olympia as trace specialization}}).
  \item Any such trace-producing run is a genuine, counterfactually exercised run of π on \emph{r}; by the computationalist's own \emph{Sufficiency} thesis it supports the experience φ. So in every world containing an Olympia, φ has occurred.
  \item The argument's absurdity-generators — ``a recording obviously isn't conscious'', ``inert add-ons cannot summon experience'', ``rusting chains would make a toothache wink out'' — all presuppose that the replay is the only candidate bearer of experience in the story, so that its status decides whether any experience exists at all. Premise 2 removes that presupposition: one cannot say Klara's run is conscious and Olympia's story contains no consciousness, because Olympia's story \emph{includes} a run of the very kind Klara's is.
  \item What remains open is only whether the replay hosts an \emph{additional} experience — and about that, honestly posed, intuition is not decisive either way.
\end{enumerate}

∴ The trilemma loses its force against computationalism: each of its horns, taken for replay runs specifically, is a position a computationalist can occupy without absurdity, because the experience the thought experiment trades on demonstrably occurred — at recording time.

\webheading{The recording run}

Maudlin presents Olympia as if she were conjured whole; the formal reconstruction shows what her construction consumes. The reordered troughs, the float presets, the placement of every blocked copy — all of it is read off the log Λ, and Λ \emph{is} the run. Whoever builds Olympia must first be, or employ, a system that genuinely computes π on \emph{r}, reading every value it acts on, its counterfactual sensitivity exercised at every step. By Sufficiency, that is precisely the kind of process the computationalist says supports φ. Nothing phenomenal is conjured from nothing, and nothing phenomenal is missing from the world: at least one experience occurred, at recording; the replay re-presents it.

Maudlin's own movie analogy, offered against computationalism, supports this reading when turned around. A film is a film \emph{of a real performance}. Nobody is puzzled that the scene genuinely happened at filming, and nobody concludes from the projector's lack of experience that acting is not a bodily process. The parallel diagnosis is familiar from the lookup-table literature: Block's verdict on \hyperref[entry:concept-lookup-table-mind]{\textsc{Lookup-table mind}} is that the machine has ``the intelligence of a jukebox'', its apparent wit belonging to the programmers who anticipated the conversation (see \hyperref[source:source-block]{\textsc{Block — Psychologism and Behaviorism (1981)}}). The recording objection reads Olympia's apparent experience the same way — it belongs, at minimum, to the recording run — with the difference that where Blockhead's table needed exponential provenance, Olympia's script needed exactly one genuine run.

\webheading{Nothing winks in or out}

The trilemma is formally sound: Sufficiency, Necessity, and Supervenience, applied to the replay, cannot all stand, so the computationalist must still classify replay runs one way or another. The objection's work is to show that \emph{every} classification, made with the recording run in view, is tolerable — the absurdity each horn was supposed to carry evaporates.

\begin{itemize}
  \item \emph{Grant the replay consciousness} (give up Necessity for replays). This no longer means crediting experience to ``a trivial constant function'': the replay is not an arbitrary trivial machine but a complete, step-for-step re-enactment nomologically downstream of a genuine φ-run. Granting such a process a derivative experience extends consciousness along a very narrow channel, not to every jukebox.
  \item \emph{Deny the replay consciousness} (restrict Sufficiency to non-replay runs). This is now the mundane playback verdict — the tape is not the performance — and it carries no anti-computational moral, because the experience it declines to find in the replay is found where computationalism itself locates it: in the run that produced the script.
  \item \emph{The addition, subtraction, and rust arguments.} Their sting — consciousness toggled by an inert block, a toothache winking out as a chain rusts through, with no physical event to mark it — presupposed that the toothache in question is the only one in the story, so that idle hardware holds a mind hostage. With the recording run on the ledger, what the inert machinery toggles is only whether the replay counts as an implementation, and hence whether a \emph{second}, derivative experience-token is present. That question's presence conditions are exactly as sharp or as vague as implementation itself — no more mysterious than whether a rusted tape deck still counts as playing — and nothing whose existence matters hangs on it.
\end{itemize}

\webheading{The weakest premise}

The objection removes the absurdity of the horns; it does not dissolve the trilemma, and its load-bearing premise is step 4 — that intuition, honestly consulted, is genuinely undecided about the replay. A defender of Maudlin will press from two sides. First, the supervenience argument never claimed to need intuitions about replays as such; it needs only that physically near-identical processes match in experience, and the objection must still say \emph{which} way they match — the openness of step 4 must survive Maudlin's insistence that some answer be given. Second, the palliative work is done by the replay's \emph{descent} from a genuine run, and descent is a historical property: if it is allowed to soften verdicts about the replay, the view drifts toward a provenance criterion of experience, and provenance criteria are unstable. A trace log assembled by sheer luck (a Boltzmann-Olympia), or copied from \emph{another Olympia's replay} — once one Olympia exists, unlimited replays-of-replays descend from a single root episode — yields machines physically identical to the original Olympia but with thinned or absent ancestry; if identical replays get different verdicts by history alone, experience at a time has been made to depend on events outside that time, which is a supervenience violation of exactly the kind the objection elsewhere respects. The stable reading keeps the history in its evidential role — the recording run proves an experience occurred and thereby disarms the ``no consciousness anywhere'' rhetoric — without letting history itself decide what the replay feels.

\begin{plainterms}
Someone records an interview with a human being. Later they play the recording back, with the interviewer repeating the same questions live — and the answers come out right, on cue, as if a conversation were happening. Then they complain: \emph{this recording is not conscious!} Well — of course the complaint feels right, and of course it proves nothing: the human was conscious, during the interview, and nobody concludes from a tape's blankness that consciousness has nothing to do with brains. To rebuild Maudlin's machine (see \hyperref[entry:argument-olympia]{\textsc{Olympia argument}}) in full, add a monitor silently comparing the interviewer's live questions against the script, and the original human on standby, ready to take over the moment the interviewer ad-libs. Maudlin's definitions then count this rig as \emph{having the conversation}, and its blankness becomes his paradox. The recording objection says the paradox is bookkeeping: you cannot tally the experiences in this story while skipping the interview itself. The experience happened; the tape re-presents it; and the leftover question — does the playback \emph{also} feel something? — is one nobody has a firm answer to, and nothing rides on it.

One honest complication keeps the objection honest: a tape replays only the human's \emph{outputs} — sound. Olympia replays the computer's entire \emph{internal} activity, every memory operation, step for step. The fair analogy would replay the interview into a duplicate brain, every recorded neuron firing triggered by electrodes on schedule, the brain's own machinery bypassed but standing by. Whether \emph{that} replayed brain feels anything is genuinely unobvious — it may or may not — and that is exactly the objection's point: the confident verdict Maudlin needs is available only for the tape-recorder version of the case, where it is mundane and already accounted for; for the fair version there is no confident verdict to build a refutation on.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-olympia]{\textsc{Olympia argument}} — the thought experiment this objection answers; the attack lands on its verdict and scope, not on its internal logic.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-olympia-trace-specialization]{\textsc{Olympia as trace specialization}} — the formal reconstruction whose \emph{Effectiveness} corollary (Olympia is computable only from a genuine run's trace) is this objection's first premise; its weakest-premise analysis (a passing guard is an executed read) presses the same ``the replay is not as inert as billed'' moral from the synchronic side.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-implementation-requires-counterfactual-structure]{\textsc{Counterfactual constraint on implementation}} — the definition of implementation whose application to replay runs the trilemma turns on.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-substrate-independence]{\textsc{Substrate-independence thesis}} — the thesis the objection defends against Olympia.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-lookup-table-mind]{\textsc{Lookup-table mind}} — the kindred provenance diagnosis: Blockhead's wit belongs to its programmers as Olympia's recorded experience belongs to her recording run.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{argument-relevance-filter-regress}{Relevance-filter regress}{Argument \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{Relevance-filter regress}{The relevance-filter regress — selecting mind-relevant causal relations re-raises the invariant demand}{$\vdash$}
The \emph{relevance-filter regress} is an objection to the causal-structure reply. It grants that a system has an objective causal structure, but argues that picking out the \emph{mind-relevant} part of that structure requires a further principle the reply has not supplied — so the demand for an observer-independent invariant is not discharged, only relocated.

\webheading{The argument}

The form is a \emph{dilemma} that opens into a regress.

\begin{enumerate}
  \item Raw causal structure includes every disposition — mass, thermal behaviour, the squishy noise a brain makes when struck — not only the cognitive ones (see \hyperref[entry:claim-physics-delivers-all-dispositions-indiscriminately]{\textsc{Indiscriminacy of physical dispositions}}).
  \item If \hyperref[entry:concept-functionalism]{\textsc{Functionalism}} counts all of them, then the brain's structure is shared by no silicon system (which makes no squishy noise), multiple realizability fails, and the view collapses into physical chauvinism. So it must restrict to the \emph{organisational}, mind-relevant subset.
  \item But that restriction is a \emph{further} principle, not delivered by causal structure itself. Selecting the relevant relations either presupposes what is mental (circularity) or requires an independent, observer-independent criterion — which is exactly the invariant at issue.
\end{enumerate}

∴ The \hyperref[entry:argument-causal-structure-supplies-invariant]{\textsc{causal-structure reply}} does not discharge the demand; it relocates it, so this argument supports \hyperref[entry:claim-functionalism-owes-decomposition-invariant]{\textsc{the demand for a decomposition invariant}}. Even the natural ``difference-making'' test — does removing this change the computation? — presupposes the boundary of ``the computation,'' which is the very carving in question.

\webheading{The weakest premise}

The vulnerable step is (3). A functionalist may offer a non-circular criterion of relevance — for instance, control of the system's behavioural outputs, or information actually \emph{used} downstream — that excludes squishy noise without presupposing mentality. Whether any such criterion escapes both chauvinism and circularity is the live crux, and it is the \hyperref[entry:argument-role-grain-dilemma]{\textsc{role-grain dilemma}} under a new description.

This is distinct from \hyperref[entry:argument-counterfactuals-yield-non-triviality-not-uniqueness]{\textsc{the non-triviality-without-uniqueness reply}}: that reply grants the relevant relations and still finds multiple carvings; this one questions the \emph{selection} of the relevant relations in the first place.

\begin{plainterms}
Another problem for the ``cause-and-effect fixes the one true organization'' reply (see \hyperref[entry:argument-causal-structure-supplies-invariant]{\textsc{Causal-structure reply}}). Physics hands you \emph{all} of a system's cause-and-effect, indiscriminately — not just the thinking-related bits, but its weight, its warmth, the squelchy sound a brain makes if you poke it. Now you're stuck between two bad options. Count \emph{everything}, and a brain's full causal profile includes things no silicon chip shares (chips don't squelch), so a machine can never match it — back to ``only meat can think.'' To dodge that, you have to keep only the \emph{mind-relevant} causes and ignore the rest. But which causes are the mind-relevant ones? Cause-and-effect by itself doesn't say — you need an extra rule to do the sorting, and that rule either quietly assumes what already counts as ``mental'' (a circle) or \emph{is} exactly the neutral, observer-independent yardstick we were missing to begin with. So the reply doesn't settle the demand; it just shifts it somewhere else. (A tempting test — ``does removing this change the computation?'' — already assumes you've fixed where ``the computation'' starts and stops, which is the very thing in dispute.)
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-causal-structure-supplies-invariant]{\textsc{Causal-structure reply}} — the reply this regress attacks.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-causal-structure-fixes-decomposition]{\textsc{Causal selection of decomposition}} — that reply's conclusion, also a target of the attack.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-physics-delivers-all-dispositions-indiscriminately]{\textsc{Indiscriminacy of physical dispositions}} — the premise: raw physics includes every disposition, unsorted.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-functionalism-owes-decomposition-invariant]{\textsc{Demand for a decomposition invariant}} — the demand this argument supports by showing the reply only relocates it.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-role-grain-dilemma]{\textsc{Grain dilemma}} — the live crux, the relevance question under a new description.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-counterfactuals-yield-non-triviality-not-uniqueness]{\textsc{Non-triviality without uniqueness}} — a sibling objection attacking the carving rather than the selection of relations.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-grain]{\textsc{Grain (of role individuation)}} — the level of role individuation at which the mind-relevant subset would have to be fixed.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{argument-report-scepticism-proves-too-much}{Report-scepticism over-reach}{Argument \,\textperiodcentered\, after Chalmers}{Report-scepticism over-reach}{Report-scepticism proves too much — applied uniformly, its discount rules screen off human hard-problem reports as well}{$\vdash$}
Two objections in this debate hold that a machine's puzzled talk about its own experience is no evidence of inner feeling. This reply runs them as a \emph{reductio by parity}: applied uniformly — in the very respects the rules themselves declare relevant — those discount rules screen off \emph{human} hard-problem testimony too, a verdict the rules' usual wielder cannot accept.

The two targets each supply a discount rule:

\begin{itemize}
  \item \emph{R1}, from \hyperref[entry:argument-self-model-prediction-phenomenality-neutral]{\textsc{Phenomenality-neutral prediction}}: a report-profile that would be produced alike in the felt world and the feel-free world is zero evidence of feelings.
  \item \emph{R2}, from \hyperref[entry:argument-contaminated-testimony-carries-no-weight]{\textsc{Contaminated-testimony objection}}: testimony fully accounted for by an inherited discourse — a rival cause known present and known sufficient — is inadmissible, not merely discounted.
\end{itemize}

\webheading{The parity}

The inference is an argument from analogy run in reverse — a parity-of-cases reductio.

\begin{enumerate}
  \item Human hard-problem reports issue from systems that satisfy the same generating conditions the targets accept for machines: finite, necessarily lossy self-modellers (see \hyperref[entry:claim-complete-self-representation-impossible]{\textsc{Self-representation incompleteness}}) with restricted access to their own machinery, in whom gap-shaped appearances can arise from access architecture alone (see \hyperref[entry:claim-self-explanation-gap-can-be-architectural]{\textsc{Architectural self-explanation gap}}). The meta-problem literature (see \hyperref[source:source-chalmers-2018]{\textsc{Chalmers — The Meta-Problem of Consciousness (2018)}}) makes the point canonical: the \emph{reports} — the puzzlement, the explanatory-gap talk — admit topic-neutral explanations citing mechanism and never phenomenality. So R1 applies: the human report-profile comes out the same in the felt world and the zombie world, and by R1 it is zero evidence of human feelings.
  \item Human hard-problem testimony is likewise culturally inherited: virtually no one's gap-talk predates immersion in a discourse of souls, qualia, and what-it-is-likeness; the registers are learned. Presence of the rival cause is as much a matter of record for an enculturated human as for a corpus-trained model. And its \emph{sufficiency} follows from R1's own neutrality premise: if the report-generating machinery suffices for the reports with or without feelings, it suffices in the human case too. Denying sufficiency there — letting the feel do indispensable causal work in producing human reports — contradicts the neutrality on which R1 rests.
\end{enumerate}

So either the rules hold uniformly, and the human hard problem loses its data — first-person reports were the only evidence the explanandum ever had, since no instrument reads phenomenality directly — or the rules admit of degree and architecture-sensitive exceptions, in which case machine testimony returns to being \emph{weighed} rather than ruled inadmissible. What the rules cannot do is hold with full force on silicon and no force on carbon.

\webheading{What it does and does not establish}

It does not show machine reports \emph{are} evidence of feelings; it constrains the sceptic's standard to uniformity. A sceptic prepared to bite the bullet — debunking human phenomenal beliefs along with the machine's — escapes the reductio; but that escape abandons the introspective datum (``we know we feel'') that substrate-favouring positions standardly treat as bedrock, and converts the dispute into illusionism-for-everyone rather than scepticism about machines in particular.

\webheading{Weakest premise}

The sufficiency-transfer in (2). Candidate disanalogies: humans are not trained text-predictors \emph{over} the consciousness corpus, so presence-of-discourse may not entail sufficiency-of-discourse for them; and if hard-problem form demonstrably arises in discourse-naive humans, R2's parity arm weakens (though R1's architectural arm stands — the machinery is still sufficient for the reports). The empirical bets are symmetric: the discourse-naive human stands to R2 exactly as the consciousness-naive model of \hyperref[entry:question-llm-own-explanatory-gap]{\textsc{LLM's own explanatory gap}} stands to the architecture cluster.

\webheading{Provenance}

The debunking-parity core is the central tension of \hyperref[source:source-chalmers-2018]{\textsc{Chalmers — The Meta-Problem of Consciousness (2018)}}: any topic-neutral explanation of phenomenal reports threatens to debunk the beliefs those reports express — for humans as much as for machines.

\webheading{Distinct from neighbouring moves}

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:argument-phenomenal-concept-overgeneration]{\textsc{Phenomenal-concept overgeneration}}, which turns an over-generation charge against the phenomenal-concept \emph{deflation} of the human gap (it debunks the self-knowledge that deflation presupposes); this turns an over-generation charge against report-\emph{scepticism} about machine testimony (it debunks the human testimony its wielder keeps). The two run in opposite dialectical directions and target different nodes. Distinct from \hyperref[entry:argument-access-symmetry-rebuttal]{\textsc{Access-symmetry rebuttal}}, which neutralises a \emph{moral-status} asymmetry built on acquaintance; this neutralises an \emph{evidential} asymmetry built on report-discount rules.

\begin{plainterms}
Two objections in this web say a chatbot's ``I can't explain my own inner states'' talk proves nothing: first, because a feelingless machine with the same wiring would say the same things (see \hyperref[entry:argument-self-model-prediction-phenomenality-neutral]{\textsc{Phenomenality-neutral prediction}}); second, because the machine read all of human philosophy, so the talk is just echo (see \hyperref[entry:argument-contaminated-testimony-carries-no-weight]{\textsc{Contaminated-testimony objection}}). This reply says: fine — now apply those rules to \emph{people}. Human brains are also systems that model themselves with limited inside access, and science can explain human ``consciousness is a mystery!'' talk by mechanism alone, never mentioning actual feelings. Humans also learned their mystery-of-consciousness vocabulary from their culture — nobody invents ``qualia'' in a vacuum. So by the same two rules, human testimony about consciousness is worth zero too — and human testimony is the \emph{only} evidence consciousness research ever had. The objector must choose: apply the rules everywhere (and give up the human hard problem as data), or admit the rules come in degrees (and then machine testimony is back to being weighed, not thrown out). What's not allowed is rules that are iron for machines and air for humans.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-self-model-prediction-phenomenality-neutral]{\textsc{Phenomenality-neutral prediction}} — source of rule R1; a primary target.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-contaminated-testimony-carries-no-weight]{\textsc{Contaminated-testimony objection}} — source of rule R2; the other target.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-de-novo-origination-breaks-report-parity]{\textsc{De-novo-origination disanalogy}} — the reply that resists this parity on its R2 arm by a provenance disanalogy.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-feel-is-the-watching-not-an-unwatched-difference]{\textsc{Feel as first-person observation}} — the reply that answers the R1 arm by relocating the human datum to first-person acquaintance.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-complete-self-representation-impossible]{\textsc{Self-representation incompleteness}} — premise: minds are necessarily lossy self-modellers.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-self-explanation-gap-can-be-architectural]{\textsc{Architectural self-explanation gap}} — premise: gap-shaped appearances from access architecture alone.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-hard-problem]{\textsc{Hard problem}} — the explanandum whose only data the reductio puts at stake.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-introspective-gap-varieties]{\textsc{Varieties of introspective explanatory gap}} — the gap taxonomy R1 and R2 draw on.
  \item \hyperref[entry:question-llm-own-explanatory-gap]{\textsc{LLM's own explanatory gap}} — the symmetric empirical test for the machine side.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{position-representationalism}{Representationalism}{Position \,\textperiodcentered\, after Tye}{Representationalism}{Representationalism (intentionalism)}{$\bigstar$}
Representationalism (also called \emph{intentionalism}) holds that the \emph{phenomenal character} of an experience — what it is like to undergo it — just is a kind of \emph{representational content}. What it is like to see red is the experience's representing the world a certain way; to introspect ``the feel'' of the experience is to become aware of \emph{what} it represents, not of any intrinsic \emph{mental paint} spread across the mind. The seeming redness you find when you look inward is really the red your visual system attributes to the surface out there. This is the claim that \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-character-is-representational]{\textsc{phenomenal character is representational}} (see \hyperref[source:source-tye-2000]{\textsc{Tye — Consciousness, Color, and Content (2000)}}, \hyperref[source:source-dretske-1995]{\textsc{Dretske — Naturalizing the Mind (1995)}}, \hyperref[source:source-harman-1990]{\textsc{Harman — The Intrinsic Quality of Experience (1990)}}).

Paired with a naturalistic, \emph{teleosemantic} theory of representation — one that grounds content in the biological function of representing — the view answers \hyperref[entry:question-hard-problem]{\textsc{Hard problem (the question)}} by reducing qualia to content, a broadly physicalist option among the \hyperref[entry:concept-materialism-types]{\textsc{type-A/type-B materialisms}}.

\webheading{What distinguishes it from neighbouring views}

As a \emph{first-order} view (see \hyperref[entry:concept-order-of-representation]{\textsc{First-order vs. higher-order states}}), it differs from \hyperref[entry:position-higher-order-theories]{\textsc{Higher-order theories of consciousness}}: no higher-order state monitoring the experience is required for it to be conscious. Its classic pressure points are \emph{inverted-spectrum} cases (could two people represent the same colours yet have systematically swapped experiences?) and \emph{inverted-earth} cases, together with the residue theorist's charge that \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-residue]{\textsc{two experiences could share content yet differ in feel}}.

\begin{plainterms}
This view says the ``feel'' of an experience is nothing extra beyond \emph{what it tells you about the world}. Seeing red is your visual system representing ``red surface there''; the redness you seem to find in your mind is really just the red it's attributing to the world. So feelings aren't inner paint — they're the world as represented to you, and that's the kind of thing science already studies. The test cases push on whether two people could represent the same things yet \emph{feel} differently.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-character-is-representational]{\textsc{Phenomenal character as representational}} — the claim the position commits to.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-order-of-representation]{\textsc{First-order vs. higher-order states}} — marks it as a first-order view, unlike higher-order theories.
  \item \hyperref[entry:position-higher-order-theories]{\textsc{Higher-order theories of consciousness}} — the higher-order rival, which demands a state about the state.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-materialism-types]{\textsc{Type-A / Type-B / Type-C materialism}} — the physicalist landscape this view sits within.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-residue]{\textsc{Phenomenal residue}} — the main objection: content could be shared while feel differs.
  \item \hyperref[entry:question-hard-problem]{\textsc{Hard problem (the question)}} — the question the position offers an answer to.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{concept-retorsion}{Retorsion}{Concept}{Retorsion}{Retorsion (performative self-refutation)}{$\blacklozenge$}
\emph{Retorsion} is the dialectical move of refuting a thesis by showing that \emph{asserting} it presupposes its falsity — the act of putting the claim forward undercuts the claim's content. It does not argue that the thesis is false from independent premises; it turns the thesis on itself (Latin \emph{retorquere}, to twist back). Where an ordinary refutation contradicts what is said, a retorsion exploits a clash between what is said and the conditions of saying it.

The classic instances are stable across the tradition. Against one who denies the law of non-contradiction: to deny it, they must mean something definite by their words rather than its opposite — already relying on the law. Against the global skeptic who says ``nothing can be known'': the claim presents itself as something known. Against the relativist who says ``all truth is relative'': the statement asks to be taken as non-relatively true. The cogito has the same shape — the attempt to doubt one's own existence enacts the existence doubted. In recent discourse ethics the same structure is called a \textbf{performative contradiction} (the contradiction lies not in the proposition but in the \emph{performance} of asserting it).

\webheading{What it can and cannot do}

Retorsion is powerful but bounded, and the boundary matters. It does not directly prove the disputed proposition true; it shows that the \emph{denial} cannot be coherently asserted. Stroud's standing objection to such transcendental arguments presses exactly here: at most they establish that we must \emph{believe} or \emph{presuppose} something, not that it is so — unless one adds a verificationist bridge from ``must presuppose'' to ``true.'' This is why retorsion pairs naturally with \emph{dismissing} the skeptic rather than refuting the skeptical proposition: it shows the skeptic's position is not assertible, which is enough to deny it \hyperref[entry:concept-dialectical-cogency]{\textsc{probative force}} without settling the metaphysics.

That link is exact. A performatively self-refuting assertion is the limiting case of a failure of \hyperref[entry:concept-dialectical-cogency]{\textsc{cogency}}: not merely an argument whose premises the audience cannot non-circularly reach, but one whose very assertion certifies its own falsity. Retorsion is therefore the sharp end of the same family of moves as the warrant-based objections elsewhere in the web.

\webheading{What it bears on}

Retorsion is one of the two anti-skeptical strategies catalogued under \hyperref[entry:question-external-world-knowledge]{\textsc{Knowing the external world}}: it underwrites \hyperref[entry:argument-retorsion-against-skepticism]{\textsc{Retorsion against skepticism}} and is the formal core of the intuition that the \hyperref[entry:concept-hinge-proposition]{\textsc{hinge}}-denier saws off the branch they sit on. It is a cousin of, but weaker in ambition than, Deutsch's \emph{bad-explanation} dismissal (see \hyperref[entry:argument-deutsch-anti-solipsism]{\textsc{Deutsch's anti-solipsism}}), which faults solipsism for explanatory emptiness rather than for self-contradiction.

\begin{plainterms}
Some claims blow themselves up just by being said out loud. ``I cannot write a word of English'' — written in English — is the toy example. ``Nothing can be known'' presents itself as a thing known. Retorsion is the trick of beating such a claim not by arguing against it but by pointing out that \emph{making} it already takes back what it says. It's the cleanest way to deal with a total skeptic: you don't have to prove the world is real, only note that their doubting it relies on everything they're pretending to doubt. (The honest caveat: this shows they can't \emph{coherently say} it — which may not be quite the same as showing they're \emph{wrong}.)
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-retorsion-against-skepticism]{\textsc{Retorsion against skepticism}} — the argument that deploys this move.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-dialectical-cogency]{\textsc{Dialectical cogency}} — self-refutation is the limiting failure of cogency; retorsion is its sharp end.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-hinge-proposition]{\textsc{Hinge proposition}} — what the skeptic must rely on even while denying it.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-deutsch-anti-solipsism]{\textsc{Deutsch's anti-solipsism}} — the allied but distinct ``bad explanation'' dismissal of the skeptic.
  \item \hyperref[entry:question-external-world-knowledge]{\textsc{Knowing the external world}} — the issue on which retorsion is one of the anti-skeptical options.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{argument-retorsion-against-skepticism}{Retorsion against skepticism}{Argument}{Retorsion against skepticism}{The retorsion argument — asserting external-world skepticism presupposes its own falsity}{$\vdash$}
The \emph{retorsion argument} answers the external-world skeptic not by proving the world — conceded \hyperref[entry:claim-external-world-unprovable-noncircular]{\textsc{impossible without circularity}} — but by turning the doubt on itself, showing it cannot be coherently asserted (see \hyperref[entry:concept-retorsion]{\textsc{Retorsion}}). It is the first of the two anti-skeptical families under \hyperref[entry:question-external-world-knowledge]{\textsc{Knowing the external world}}, the sibling of \hyperref[entry:argument-deutsch-anti-solipsism]{\textsc{the bad-explanation dismissal}}.

\webheading{The argument}

The inference is transcendental (see \hyperref[entry:concept-transcendental-argument]{\textsc{Transcendental argument}}) / dialectical — it reasons from the \emph{conditions} of the skeptic's own performance.

\begin{enumerate}
  \item To assert, doubt, or even entertain ``there is no external world / I cannot know there is one,'' the skeptic must use a public language, draw on memory and reasoning, and address an interlocutor — capacities whose standing presupposes the very world and other minds in question (see \hyperref[entry:concept-hinge-proposition]{\textsc{hinges}}).
  \item So the act of mounting the doubt relies on what the doubt would unsettle: the position is \emph{performatively self-refuting}, undercut by the conditions of asserting it.
  \item (see \hyperref[entry:claim-knowledge-without-noncircular-proof]{\textsc{Knowing without proving}}) Demonstration is not required for knowledge; it suffices to show the doubt is not rationally assertible, removing the skeptic's standing to dislodge our default.
\end{enumerate}

∴ External-world skepticism cannot be cogently maintained, and our acceptance that the world exists stands unrefuted: this \texttt{supports} \hyperref[entry:claim-external-world-exists]{\textsc{The external world exists}} — not by proving it, but by disarming the challenge to it.

\webheading{The weakest premise}

The decisive vulnerability is the gap, pressed by Stroud (see \hyperref[source:source-stroud-1968]{\textsc{Stroud — Transcendental Arguments (1968)}}), between \emph{presupposition} and \emph{truth} (premise 2's reach). At most the argument shows the skeptic must \emph{presuppose} a world to voice the doubt — that the doubt is unassertible — not that there \emph{is} a world; bridging ``must presuppose'' to ``is so'' needs a verificationist premise the skeptic will refuse. This is exactly why the conclusion is framed as \hyperref[entry:concept-dialectical-cogency]{\textsc{cogency}}-level — the skeptic's assertion lacks probative force, indeed certifies its own falsity in the performing — rather than as a proof of realism. A self-refuting assertion is the limiting failure of cogency, and that, not a demonstration, is what retorsion delivers. Premise (1) can also be resisted by a skeptic who claims to doubt \emph{silently}, entertaining the hypothesis without asserting it; against that merely-entertained skepticism the companion \hyperref[entry:argument-deutsch-anti-solipsism]{\textsc{bad-explanation charge}} is the better tool.

\begin{plainterms}
You can't talk yourself out of the world. To even \emph{say} ``maybe nothing outside my mind exists,'' the sceptic has to use a shared language, trust their memory, reason step by step, and address someone — all of which only make sense if there \emph{is} a world with other people in it. So the doubt eats itself: voicing it leans on everything it pretends to suspend. That's enough to brush the sceptic aside — you needn't prove the world, just point out they can't coherently deny it. (The fair catch, due to Stroud: this shows they can't \emph{coherently say} it, which isn't quite the same as proving the world is there — so the move disarms the sceptic rather than settling the metaphysics.)
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-external-world-exists]{\textsc{The external world exists}} — what the argument supports by disarming its denial.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-retorsion]{\textsc{Retorsion}} — the self-refutation move it instances.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-dialectical-cogency]{\textsc{Dialectical cogency}} — the level at which it bites: the skeptic's doubt is not cogently assertible.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-knowledge-without-noncircular-proof]{\textsc{Knowing without proving}} — the premise that disarming the doubt suffices, proof being unnecessary.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-deutsch-anti-solipsism]{\textsc{Deutsch's anti-solipsism}} — the sibling move, by explanatory cost rather than self-refutation.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{claim-psychological-rewiring-tolerance}{Rewiring tolerance}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, after Parfit}{Rewiring tolerance}{Altering a single psychological connection never changes who someone is}{$\odot$}
The tolerance premise for personal identity: across a series that gradually replaces one person's psychology with another's, no single small change — one memory, one belief, one disposition — can be the step at which the subject stops being themselves.

It is a claim about the insensitivity of the identity-verdict to a unit psychological change, not about what identity consists in. It is the personal-identity analogue of \hyperref[entry:claim-sorites-tolerance]{\textsc{sorites tolerance}}, and the premise that drives the gradual-rewiring instance of the \hyperref[entry:concept-continuity-argument-schema]{\textsc{Continuity argument schema}} (Parfit's spectra in \emph{Reasons and Persons}, \hyperref[source:source-parfit-1984]{\textsc{Parfit — Reasons and Persons (1984)}}).

It is denied by any defender of a sharp, all-or-nothing criterion of personal identity — the discontinuity horn, on which there is some replacement at which ``me'' determinately becomes ``not-me.''

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-sorites-tolerance]{\textsc{sorites tolerance}}: the same step-insensitivity form, but a different domain ($X$ = psychological configurations under one-connection edits) and verdict-space ($Y$ = \{me, not-me\}).

\begin{plainterms}
The ``no single step makes the difference'' premise, applied to who you are. As you gradually rewire someone's mind toward someone else's — one memory, one belief at a time — no single tiny change can be \emph{the} moment they stop being themselves. It's the heap paradox's tolerance step, with ``me / not-me'' standing in for ``heap / not-heap.''
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-sorites-tolerance]{\textsc{Sorites tolerance}} — the heap-paradox premise this mirrors.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-continuity-argument-schema]{\textsc{Continuity argument schema}} — the template whose tolerance premise this supplies for the personal-identity case.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-parfit-rewiring]{\textsc{Parfit's rewiring spectrum}} — the argument this claim serves as a premise.
  \item \hyperref[source:source-parfit-1984]{\textsc{Parfit — Reasons and Persons (1984)}} — Parfit's spectra, the source of the rewiring case.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{argument-robot-reply}{Robot reply}{Argument}{Robot reply}{The Robot Reply — symbols grounded in sensorimotor commerce acquire meaning}{$\vdash$}
The \emph{Robot Reply} concedes that a program shuffling symbols in a sealed room has no understanding, and diagnoses \emph{why}: the symbols are ungrounded, cut off from the world they are supposed to be about. Put the program inside a robot — give it cameras, microphones, limbs, and motors, so that its symbols are caused by and act upon real objects — and the symbols acquire genuine reference. ``Hamburger'' comes to mean hamburger because the robot's token is reliably triggered by hamburgers and drives its eating behaviour. Grounding supplies what the bare room lacked: a causal route from symbol to world.

This is a direct attack on A3 (see \hyperref[entry:claim-syntax-insufficient-for-semantics]{\textsc{Syntax insufficient for semantics}}): syntax \emph{plus the right causal embedding} is, the reply claims, sufficient for semantics, so the move from ``syntax alone is empty'' to ``computation can't yield understanding'' fails. In the vocabulary of \hyperref[entry:concept-intrinsic-derived-intentionality]{\textsc{Intrinsic and derived intentionality}}, the Robot Reply is the claim that causal grounding converts \emph{derived} intentionality into \emph{original} intentionality — the robot's symbols mean things to \emph{it}, not just to us, because \emph{it} is the one in causal commerce with the things.

\webheading{The crux}

Two things are at issue. First, whether causal-informational links are \emph{enough} for meaning, or merely necessary — a question shared with causal and teleological theories of content. Second, whether Searle's \hyperref[entry:argument-rejoinder-room-in-robot]{\textsc{room-in-the-robot rejoinder}} defuses it: Searle puts the man inside the robot's head, manipulating the very symbols that come from the cameras and drive the motors, and insists the man still attaches no meaning to them, knowing nothing of where they come from. The reply's defender answers that this again looks at the wrong level — the \emph{robot}, not the homunculus, is the candidate understander (folding the \hyperref[entry:argument-systems-reply]{\textsc{Systems Reply}} back in).

\begin{plainterms}
Maybe the room fails because it's \emph{sealed off} from the world — just symbols with nothing to attach them to. So build a robot: wire the program to eyes, ears, and hands, so that its word for ``cup'' actually fires when it sees cups and makes it reach for them. Now the symbols are hooked to real things, and that hook-up, the reply says, is exactly what meaning \emph{is} — so the robot really does understand, even if a bare computer wouldn't. Searle's comeback: climb inside the robot's head and do the symbol-shuffling yourself; you still won't know that one squiggle means ``cup,'' so where did the understanding go?
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-syntax-insufficient-for-semantics]{\textsc{Syntax insufficient for semantics}} — axiom A3, which this reply denies by adding causal grounding.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-rejoinder-room-in-robot]{\textsc{Room in the robot}} — Searle's reply: put the man in the robot and meaning still never appears.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-intrinsic-derived-intentionality]{\textsc{Intrinsic and derived intentionality}} — the reply claims grounding turns derived into original intentionality.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-intentionality]{\textsc{Intentionality}} — causal-grounding theories of content the reply draws on.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-systems-reply]{\textsc{Systems reply}} — the parent objection the Robot Reply specialises.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{argument-rejoinder-room-in-robot}{Room in the robot}{Argument \,\textperiodcentered\, after Searle}{Room in the robot}{Room in the robot — the man manipulates the robot's symbols and still attaches no meaning}{$\vdash$}
Searle's rejoinder to the \hyperref[entry:argument-robot-reply]{\textsc{Robot Reply}}. Grant the robot its cameras, limbs, and causal traffic with the world — then put the man inside its ``head,'' receiving the strings of symbols that come in from the sensors and producing the strings that drive the motors, all by the same shape-matching rulebook. The man knows nothing of where the symbols come from or what they do; to him they are still meaningless squiggles. Adding perceptual and motor causal links changes what the symbols \emph{do}, but not what they \emph{mean to the man} — so the grounding, Searle concludes, does not inject understanding after all.

The move \texttt{supports} A3 (see \hyperref[entry:claim-syntax-insufficient-for-semantics]{\textsc{Syntax insufficient for semantics}}): causal embedding supplies more syntax-driven behaviour, not \hyperref[entry:concept-intrinsic-derived-intentionality]{\textsc{original intentionality}}.

\webheading{The live crux}

The rejoinder is widely thought to \emph{miss} the Robot Reply rather than meet it, because the reply never claimed the \emph{man} understands — it claimed the \emph{robot} does, in virtue of the system-level causal commerce the man is merely a cog in. So the rejoinder appears to smuggle the \hyperref[entry:argument-systems-reply]{\textsc{Systems Reply}}'s level confusion back in: of course the homunculus doesn't understand; the question was whether the grounded system does. Whether causal grounding \emph{constitutes} meaning or merely \emph{accompanies} it is left genuinely open.

\begin{plainterms}
The Robot Reply said: wire the program to a body and its symbols finally mean something. Searle climbs inside the robot: he sits in its head shuffling the very symbols that flow from its eyes to its hands, and he \emph{still} has no idea any of it means ``cup'' or ``wall.'' So, he says, bolting on a body didn't create understanding. The standard comeback: but nobody said \emph{the man in the head} understands — the claim was that the \emph{robot as a whole} does, and Searle keeps insisting on interrogating the little man instead of the robot.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-robot-reply]{\textsc{Robot reply}} — the objection this answers.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-syntax-insufficient-for-semantics]{\textsc{Syntax insufficient for semantics}} — axiom A3, which it defends.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-intrinsic-derived-intentionality]{\textsc{Intrinsic and derived intentionality}} — the original-vs-derived meaning at stake.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-systems-reply]{\textsc{Systems reply}} — the level objection critics say this rejoinder re-incurs.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{argument-kripke-rule-following}{Rule-following skeptical argument}{Argument \,\textperiodcentered\, after Kripke}{Rule-following skeptical argument}{Kripke's Wittgenstein: the skeptical argument about rule-following and meaning}{$\vdash$}
The \emph{rule-following} argument — Saul Kripke's reconstruction of Wittgenstein, often called ``Kripke's Wittgenstein'' or ``Kripkenstein'' (\hyperref[source:source-kripke-1982]{\textsc{Kripke — Wittgenstein on Rules and Private Language (1982)}}, reading \hyperref[source:source-wittgenstein-1953]{\textsc{Wittgenstein — Philosophical Investigations (1953)}}) — is a skeptical paradox about meaning. It asks what \emph{fact} makes it the case that a person means one thing rather than another by a word, and argues that no candidate fact can do the job. Its conclusion is \hyperref[entry:claim-no-constitutive-fact-of-meaning]{\textsc{that there is no constitutive fact of meaning}}.

\webheading{The challenge: plus or quus?}

Suppose you have computed many sums but, being finite, never ``68 + 57.'' Define a deviant function: \emph{quus}, which agrees with plus on every pair you have actually added but yields 5 for arguments larger than any you have met. The skeptic grants every fact about your past and asks: in virtue of what did you mean \emph{plus}, so that the correct answer now is 125, rather than \emph{quus}, so that it is 5? The question is not how you \emph{know} which you meant; it is what \emph{makes it true} that you meant either — what fact your meaning consists in. The demand is for a fact that (i) is something about \emph{you}, and (ii) is \emph{normative}: it must say what you \emph{ought} to answer, not merely predict what you will.

\webheading{The elimination}

The argument is an \emph{argument by elimination}: each candidate for the meaning-constituting fact is shown to fail.

\begin{enumerate}
  \item \textbf{Past usage.} Your actual past applications are a finite set, equally compatible with plus and with indefinitely many deviant rules. A finite record cannot single out one infinite function.
  \item \textbf{Dispositions.} ``You are disposed to answer 125'' fares no better. Your dispositions are also finite (for huge inputs you would be unable to answer, or would die first); they include \emph{mistakes} (you are disposed to misadd sometimes), so they cannot define the \emph{correct} answer; and they are merely descriptive — they say what you \emph{would} do, not what you \emph{should} — so they miss the normativity meaning requires.
  \item \textbf{Mental images or qualia.} Picturing a rule, or an inner feeling of ``meaning addition,'' is just another sign that itself stands in need of interpretation; it does not reach out and fix the answer for cases never considered.
  \item \textbf{A primitive state of meaning.} Positing a sui generis, irreducible state of \emph{meaning-plus} names the problem rather than solving it: such a state would have to be a finite item in the mind that somehow determines indefinitely many correct applications, which is the very magic in question.
\end{enumerate}

∴ No fact about the speaker constitutes meaning one thing rather than another (see \hyperref[entry:claim-no-constitutive-fact-of-meaning]{\textsc{No constitutive fact of meaning}}).

\webheading{The skeptical solution}

Kripke's Wittgenstein does not conclude that language is empty. It abandons the demand for a \emph{truth-conditional} fact of meaning and offers a \emph{skeptical solution}: talk of meaning has \textbf{assertibility conditions}, not fact-stating ones. To mean addition is to be a member of a community whose shared practice licenses counting ``125'' as correct and corrects deviations — meaning as \emph{use}, sustained by communal agreement, with no private fact underneath. This makes the account constitutively \emph{social}, and connects the paradox to the private-language argument: a would-be solitary rule-follower has nothing to distinguish ``following the rule'' from ``seeming to.''

\webheading{Weakest premise}

The eliminative survey's treatment of \textbf{dispositions} (premise 2), and behind it the \emph{demand for a normative, infinitary constituting fact}. Dispositionalists reply that meaning is fixed by dispositions under \emph{idealised} or \emph{optimal} conditions (bracketing finitude and error), so the normativity and the infinite reach are recovered. Others reject the framing outright: meaning need not be \emph{constituted} by any further fact to be real, or it is fixed by causal/teleological relations the survey did not consider. The argument is only as strong as its claim that \emph{every} such repair fails.

\webheading{Bearing on the web}

The argument generalises beyond arithmetic to all content, so it is a direct pressure on \hyperref[entry:question-intentionality]{\textsc{how any physical state could be about something determinate}}. Kripke notes that a \emph{machine} is no better off than a person: a physical device does not determine the function it computes, since it is finite and can malfunction — which makes the rule-following paradox a close cousin of \hyperref[entry:argument-decomposition-indeterminacy]{\textsc{the indeterminacy of computational decomposition}} and of the worry that ``which function a system computes'' is not fixed by its physical facts alone. It also sharpens the \hyperref[entry:concept-intrinsic-derived-intentionality]{\textsc{original/derived intentionality}} distinction: if even a mind has no private fact fixing its content, the hope of grounding \emph{original} intentionality in an inner state looks dim, and the \hyperref[entry:claim-syntax-insufficient-for-semantics]{\textsc{syntax-to-semantics gap}} gets a further twist — the trouble may be not just that symbols need interpreting, but that nothing fixes the interpretation as uniquely correct.

\begin{plainterms}
You've done lots of addition, but only finitely many sums. Could you have secretly meant a weird rule all along — ``normal addition, except answer 5 for any numbers bigger than I've ever tried'' — that fits everything you've ever actually done? Kripke hunts for the fact that rules this out — that makes it true you meant ordinary \emph{plus} — and finds nothing that works: your past sums are finite and fit both rules; your habits are finite and error-prone (so they can't define the \emph{right} answer); and no mental picture or feeling spells out the answer for cases you've never met. Spooky conclusion: there's no inner fact that pins down what you mean. His escape isn't ``nothing means anything'' but ``meaning isn't a private fact at all — it lives in the shared practice of a community that agrees on what counts as going on the same way.'' The same problem haunts computers: nothing about a finite, breakable machine settles which exact rule it's really following.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-no-constitutive-fact-of-meaning]{\textsc{No constitutive fact of meaning}} — the skeptical conclusion this argument establishes.
  \item \hyperref[entry:question-intentionality]{\textsc{Problem of intentionality}} — the general problem of content this challenge feeds.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-intrinsic-derived-intentionality]{\textsc{Intrinsic and derived intentionality}} — original vs derived aboutness, which the paradox presses.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-syntax-insufficient-for-semantics]{\textsc{Syntax insufficient for semantics}} — a neighbouring worry about meaning, given a further twist here.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-decomposition-indeterminacy]{\textsc{Decomposition-indeterminacy objection}} — the computational cousin: which function a physical system computes is likewise underdetermined.
  \item \hyperref[source:source-wittgenstein-1953]{\textsc{Wittgenstein — Philosophical Investigations (1953)}} — the rule-following considerations Kripke is reconstructing.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{position-russellian-monism}{Russellian monism}{Position \,\textperiodcentered\, after Russell}{Russellian monism}{}{$\bigstar$}
\emph{Russellian monism} holds that physics describes only the abstract \emph{structure and dynamics} of the world — how things are arranged and how they behave — while leaving wholly unspecified the intrinsic, categorical nature of whatever has that structure, and that consciousness is, or is grounded in, that intrinsic nature. On this view experience is neither a non-physical add-on nor reducible to bare structure: it is the hidden categorical filling inside the physical world's outline, the ``what it is like to be the stuff'' that physical description never reaches.

The position commits to two claims. First, that \hyperref[entry:claim-functional-description-structural-dynamical-only]{\textsc{a functional or physical description captures only structural-dynamical facts}}, never intrinsic nature. Second, that \hyperref[entry:claim-consciousness-categorical-not-dispositional]{\textsc{phenomenal consciousness is categorical, not merely dispositional}} — an occurrent fact that is actually there, the kind of categorical basis that dispositions standardly require.
