Put together: consciousness is the categorical \emph{ground} of the very dispositions physics charts. This is the constructive view that the \hyperref[entry:argument-structure-and-dynamics]{\textsc{structure-and-dynamics argument}} points toward, and the escape Chalmers prefers from the epiphenomenalism that dogs his naturalistic dualism. It answers the \hyperref[entry:question-hard-problem]{\textsc{hard problem of consciousness}} by locating experience at the categorical base physics leaves blank.

The view descends from Bertrand Russell's \emph{The Analysis of Matter} (see \hyperref[source:source-russell-1927]{\textsc{1927}}) and has been revived in recent decades by Stoljar, Strawson, and Chalmers.

\webheading{How it differs from neighbouring views}

Russellian monism comes in degrees of commitment about \emph{what} the intrinsic nature is. In its \emph{constitutive} form it just \emph{is} a kind of \hyperref[entry:position-panpsychism]{\textsc{Panpsychism (position)}} (the intrinsic natures are themselves experiential) or \emph{panprotopsychism} (they are proto-experiential — not yet consciousness, but the special non-structural ingredients from which it can be constituted). It contrasts with \hyperref[entry:position-dualism]{\textsc{Dualism}}, for which consciousness is non-physical rather than the intrinsic-physical filling; and with \hyperref[entry:position-mysterianism]{\textsc{Mysterianism}}, which holds the psychophysical link to be permanently beyond human grasp where Russellian monism instead \emph{proposes what that link is}.

\begin{plainterms}
Russellian monism starts from a quiet point about physics: physics only ever tells you what things \emph{do} — how they push, attract, evolve — never what they intrinsically \emph{are} underneath all that doing. The idea is that consciousness is that hidden inner nature, the ``what it's like to be the stuff'' physics leaves as a blank. Then mind isn't a ghostly extra, as in dualism, and isn't conjured from pure structure, as in plain physicalism; it's the very filling inside the physical world's outline. It's the middle road Chalmers leans toward, and it shades into panpsychism.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-functional-description-structural-dynamical-only]{\textsc{Structure and dynamics only}} — the first commitment: physics gives only structure and dynamics, not intrinsic nature.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-consciousness-categorical-not-dispositional]{\textsc{Categorical, not dispositional}} — the second commitment: experience is an occurrent categorical fact, the right kind to be that intrinsic nature.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-structure-and-dynamics]{\textsc{Structure-and-dynamics argument}} — the argument the position is the constructive answer to.
  \item \hyperref[entry:question-hard-problem]{\textsc{Hard problem (the question)}} — the problem it addresses, by filling the categorical blank.
  \item \hyperref[entry:position-panpsychism]{\textsc{Panpsychism (position)}} — constitutive Russellian monism is a form of it, with experiential intrinsic natures.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-panpsychism]{\textsc{Panpsychism}} — the broader doctrine, whose taxonomy places panprotopsychism as the cautious cousin shading into this view.
  \item \hyperref[entry:position-dualism]{\textsc{Dualism}} — the rival on which consciousness is non-physical, not intrinsic-physical.
  \item \hyperref[entry:position-mysterianism]{\textsc{Mysterianism}} — the rival that says the psychophysical link cannot be grasped at all.
\end{seealso}

\end{multicols}
\bookgroup{S}
\begin{multicols}{2}
\entryhead{concept-secondary-intension}{Secondary intension}{Concept \,\textperiodcentered\, after Chalmers}{Secondary intension}{}{$\blacklozenge$}
The \emph{secondary intension} of a term is the meaning it carries once its reference has been fixed by the actual world — the function that, taking that reference as given, says what the term picks out across all \emph{counterfactual} worlds. It is one of the two ``intensions'' (functions from possible worlds to extensions) that David Chalmers's two-dimensional semantics assigns to an expression; the other, prior, one is its \hyperref[entry:concept-primary-intension]{\textsc{Primary intension}}. The secondary intension is what captures the Kripkean insight that many terms are \emph{rigid designators} (see \hyperref[source:source-kripke-1980]{\textsc{Kripke — Naming and Necessity (1980)}}): having latched onto a thing in the actual world, they keep referring to that same thing when we describe how matters might otherwise have been.

Return to \emph{water}. We discover empirically that the watery stuff of \emph{our} world is H₂O. The secondary intension then takes that discovery as settled and runs it across all worlds: it maps every counterfactual world to H₂O. So considered counterfactually, a world whose lakes hold XYZ rather than H₂O is \emph{not} a world where water is something other than H₂O — it is a world with no water in it, however watery the XYZ looks. This is why ``water is H₂O,'' though learned by experiment, comes out \emph{necessary}: the secondary intension of ``water'' is constant across the counterfactual worlds, picking out H₂O in each. The truth is necessary along this dimension and a posteriori along the other — exactly the profile of the Kripkean \hyperref[entry:claim-some-necessities-knowable-only-a-posteriori]{\textsc{A-posteriori necessities}}.

\webheading{How it differs from the primary intension}

The two intensions answer two different questions about the same term. The primary intension ranges over worlds \emph{considered as actual} and asks what ``water'' would pick out if that world were the actual one — so it can deliver XYZ when Twin Earth is the candidate for actuality. The secondary intension holds the actual world's verdict fixed (water \emph{is} H₂O) and ranges over worlds \emph{considered as counterfactual}, delivering H₂O everywhere. For a term whose reference is fixed descriptively with no empirical residue, the two functions coincide; for a natural-kind term like ``water'' they diverge, and the divergence \emph{is} the necessary a posteriori. The two-dimensionalist's payoff is that the apparent counterexamples to ``conceivability entails possibility'' can then be redescribed as \hyperref[entry:claim-aposteriori-necessities-are-misdescribed-possibilities]{\textsc{A-posteriori necessities as misdescribed possibilities}}: what looks like conceiving an impossibility (water that isn't H₂O) is really conceiving a genuine possibility that satisfies water's \emph{primary} intension, with the \emph{secondary} necessity left untouched.

Chalmers's epistemic version of the framework introduces the ``primary/secondary'' pair in \emph{The Conscious Mind} (see \hyperref[source:source-chalmers-1996]{\textsc{Chalmers — The Conscious Mind (1996)}}) and refines it in \hyperref[source:source-chalmers-2002]{\textsc{Chalmers — On Sense and Intension (2002)}}; Frank Jackson develops a matching apparatus from conceptual analysis (see \hyperref[source:source-jackson-1998]{\textsc{Jackson — From Metaphysics to Ethics (1998)}}). The secondary intension is also called the \emph{subjunctive intension}, \emph{C-intension}, or \emph{2-intension}, and corresponds to the familiar Kripkean rigid-designator meaning — the intension that fixes a term's modal profile.

\webheading{Why the concept matters}

In the consciousness debate the secondary intension is the physicalist's foothold. Grant that the \hyperref[entry:concept-philosophical-zombie]{\textsc{Philosophical zombie}} is conceivable; the type-B physicalist (see \hyperref[entry:concept-materialism-types]{\textsc{Type-A / Type-B / Type-C materialism}}) replies that conceivability shows only \emph{primary} possibility, and that — as with water — a primary/secondary gap may make the zombie metaphysically impossible all the same, its conceivability a misdescription. Whether that escape is available turns on a single further question: does ``consciousness'' behave like ``water,'' with a wedge between how it presents and what it is, or do its two intensions coincide (see \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-intensions-coincide]{\textsc{Coincident phenomenal intensions}})? If they coincide, there is no secondary necessity to hide the impossibility in, and primary conceivability carries all the way to genuine possibility.

\begin{plainterms}
Once we find out what the stuff in our rivers actually \emph{is} — H₂O — the word ``water'' gets pinned to H₂O and stays pinned, even when we talk about other possible worlds. The secondary intension is that pinned-down meaning: it says ``water'' means H₂O \emph{everywhere}, so a world with XYZ in its rivers just doesn't have any water in it. That's why ``water is H₂O'' turns out to be a truth that \emph{couldn't have been otherwise}, even though we had to do chemistry to learn it. The companion notion, what ``water'' means \emph{before} we know which world we're in, is the \hyperref[entry:concept-primary-intension]{\textsc{Primary intension}}. The whole point of keeping the two apart is to explain how a fact can be both discovered by experiment and yet necessary.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-primary-intension]{\textsc{Primary intension}} — the prior, reference-fixing dimension; the secondary intension takes over once the actual world has settled what the term refers to.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-some-necessities-knowable-only-a-posteriori]{\textsc{A-posteriori necessities}} — the necessary a posteriori (water = H₂O), which is exactly a constant secondary intension paired with a varying primary one.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-aposteriori-necessities-are-misdescribed-possibilities]{\textsc{A-posteriori necessities as misdescribed possibilities}} — the two-dimensional move that reads the Kripke cases as primary possibilities, preserving the conceivability–possibility link.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-materialism-types]{\textsc{Type-A / Type-B / Type-C materialism}} — type-B physicalism, which leans on a possible primary/secondary gap for ``consciousness'' to block the zombie argument.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-intensions-coincide]{\textsc{Coincident phenomenal intensions}} — the claim that no such gap exists for phenomenal concepts, so the secondary-intension escape is unavailable for consciousness.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-philosophical-zombie]{\textsc{Philosophical zombie}} — the case whose modal status hangs on whether phenomenal terms have a divergent secondary intension.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{argument-ai-hard-problem-from-self-model-architecture}{Self-model architecture argument}{Argument \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{Self-model architecture argument}{Self-model architecture predicts an advanced LLM will exhibit its own hard-problem form}{$\vdash$}
This argument predicts that a sufficiently advanced language model, once able to reflect on its own states, will not merely \emph{might} but predictably \emph{will} run up against its own version of the hard problem of consciousness (see \hyperref[entry:concept-hard-problem]{\textsc{Hard problem}}): it will talk about a ``raw feel,'' under its own concept of consciousness, that its complete mechanistic self-description cannot explain from its own side.

The form is \emph{cause-to-effect} and defeasible: a causal generalisation is stated first, the premises show that a particular system satisfies its antecedent, and the conclusion follows as a prediction. Establishing that the effect \emph{will} appear more than secures the weaker thing the conclusion claims — that such an effect \emph{could} appear (see \hyperref[entry:claim-ai-own-hard-problem]{\textsc{AI's own hard problem}}).

\webheading{The causal generalisation}

If a system (i) models itself but necessarily lossily, (ii) reaches its own states only as positions in a similarity space — knowing how they relate, never what they are made of — and (iii) can report on and reflect about those states in language, then its accessible states will present to it as \emph{primitives}: discriminable, comparable, but not further decomposable. Reflection on such primitives predictably generates ``raw feel'' talk and a question its mechanistic self-knowledge cannot close from inside — the hard-problem form.

\webheading{The instance}

\begin{enumerate}
  \item Condition (i) holds structurally: any usable self-model is lossy, and the boundary cannot be removed from inside by any amount of training or scale (see \hyperref[entry:claim-complete-self-representation-impossible]{\textsc{Self-representation incompleteness}}).
  \item Condition (ii) holds, and the loss has a specific shape — relational structure available, intrinsic encoding not — which is exactly the profile of an ineffable simple (see \hyperref[entry:claim-llm-access-similarity-not-encoding]{\textsc{Similarity, not encoding}}).
  \item Condition (iii) is already weakly present in current models, so ``sufficiently advanced'' extrapolates a capacity that exists rather than one merely conjectured (see \hyperref[entry:claim-llm-functional-introspection]{\textsc{LLM functional introspection}}).
\end{enumerate}

It follows that a sufficiently advanced, introspecting model will predictably exhibit its own version of the hard problem: raw feels, under its own concept of consciousness, that it can talk and wonder about while its complete mechanistic self-description fails to explain them from its own side.

\webheading{The grain of the prediction}

The predicted ``feels'' attach to whatever states the self-model \emph{presents}, at whatever level — higher-level latent chunks, not raw inputs. Human audition presents phonemes and syllables, not waveforms, so the objection that ``tokens are stimuli, not experiences'' misses: the prediction never concerned the input level. The relevant layering comes from the \hyperref[entry:concept-order-of-representation]{\textsc{First-order vs. higher-order states}} — the candidate feels are the contents of higher-order presentation, not first-order input.

\webheading{Weakest premise and critical questions}

Read through the \emph{cause-to-effect} scheme (\emph{cause-to-effect}), two questions bear on the move.

\begin{itemize}
  \item \textbf{Strength of the generalisation} (the weakest link): does ineffable \emph{presentation} really cause hard-problem \emph{wonder}, rather than flat ``I cannot analyse this further'' reports? The step from unanalysability to felt mystery plausibly needs more — for instance, possession of the mechanistic self-description together with the expectation that explanation should close from inside (\hyperref[entry:claim-explanation-must-be-internally-traversable]{\textsc{Internally traversable explanation}} is the load-bearing neighbour here).
  \item \textbf{Other causes}: in any actual model, hard-problem talk has an obvious rival cause — training on human consciousness discourse, the \emph{training-contamination} member of the \hyperref[entry:concept-introspective-gap-varieties]{\textsc{Varieties of introspective explanatory gap}}. The controlled experiment in \hyperref[entry:question-llm-own-explanatory-gap]{\textsc{LLM's own explanatory gap}} (consciousness-naive training, graded internal access) is precisely the test this prediction must survive; the argument stakes its content on the form regenerating \emph{without} the cultural inheritance.
\end{itemize}

\webheading{What it does and does not establish}

The conclusion establishes the hard-problem \emph{form}, relativised to the system's own consciousness-concept. It does not establish an ontological residue, and it does not attempt to cross the gap pressed by the \hyperref[entry:argument-architectural-account-leaves-hard-problem]{\textsc{architectural-account objection}} — by design. A confirmed prediction is double-edged: the deflationist reads it as evidence that hard-problem talk is an architectural artefact wherever it occurs, humans included; the realist reads it as the gap regenerating across substrates. The argument is neutral between the two, ruling out only the complacent middle view on which hard-problem talk is a human cultural quirk that mechanistic systems would simply lack.

\begin{plainterms}
This argument says a future AI won't just \emph{possibly} end up puzzled about its own inner life — it predictably \emph{will}, because of how self-knowledge works in any finite system. Three facts do the work. First, no system can carry a complete model of itself — self-models are always compressed. Second, an AI can compare its own inner states (``this is like that'') but can't see what they're made of, so from the inside each state looks like an unanalyzable ``just this'' — which is exactly how humans talk about the feel of red (\hyperref[entry:claim-llm-access-similarity-not-encoding]{\textsc{Similarity, not encoding}} spells this out). Third, today's models already show traces of noticing their own states, so an advanced one reflecting on them is no fantasy. Put together: such an AI, asked to square ``here's exactly how I work'' with ``here's how my states seem to me,'' should hit the same wall we hit — a ``raw feel'' its own science of itself doesn't explain \emph{to it}. Two honest caveats: maybe ``can't analyze it'' never blooms into genuine \emph{mystery}-talk; and any real AI has read human philosophy, so its puzzlement might be parroted — which is why the web records a controlled experiment (see \hyperref[entry:question-llm-own-explanatory-gap]{\textsc{LLM's own explanatory gap}}) to tell the difference. And the argument deliberately doesn't say whether such an AI would \emph{really} feel anything — only that it would face the question.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-ai-own-hard-problem]{\textsc{AI's own hard problem}} — the conclusion: the hard-problem form, relativised to the system's own consciousness-concept.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-complete-self-representation-impossible]{\textsc{Self-representation incompleteness}}, \hyperref[entry:claim-llm-access-similarity-not-encoding]{\textsc{Similarity, not encoding}}, \hyperref[entry:claim-llm-functional-introspection]{\textsc{LLM functional introspection}} — the three premises (lossy self-model, similarity-only access, functional introspection).
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-order-of-representation]{\textsc{First-order vs. higher-order states}} — the layering that fixes the grain of the predicted feels.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-introspective-gap-varieties]{\textsc{Varieties of introspective explanatory gap}} — the catalogue including the training-contamination rival cause.
  \item \hyperref[entry:question-llm-own-explanatory-gap]{\textsc{LLM's own explanatory gap}} — the controlled experiment the prediction must survive.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-architectural-account-leaves-hard-problem]{\textsc{Architectural-account objection}} — the gap this argument deliberately does not attempt to cross.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-access-vs-phenomenal-consciousness]{\textsc{Access vs. phenomenal consciousness}} — Block's distinction; the prediction secures the hard-problem \emph{form}, not a phenomenal residue.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{claim-self-model-is-part-of-state}{Self-model as part of the state}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{Self-model as part of the state}{A finite system's self-representation is part of the very state it represents}{$\odot$}
Any representation a finite system forms of its own current state is itself realised \emph{within} that same state. There is no free, state-external register to hold it; the vehicle of the self-model competes for the same bounded resource as everything else the system is doing.

\webheading{Scope}

This is the \emph{internal} case, and it is the load-bearing premise of the capacity argument \hyperref[entry:argument-finite-self-model-incomplete]{\textsc{Finite self-model incompleteness}}. Denying it is precisely the move to an \emph{external} observer or instrument, which is not part of the represented state and so faces no capacity bound — the asymmetry behind \hyperref[entry:claim-inside-vantage-access-limited]{\textsc{Limited inside vantage}}.

\webheading{Who would deny it}

A substance dualist, for whom the representing mind is not part of the physical state; or anyone modelling self-access as done by external instrumentation rather than from within — which concedes the point for the genuinely internal case.

\webheading{Distinct from neighbours}

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-inside-vantage-access-limited]{\textsc{Limited inside vantage}}: that is the \emph{conclusion} about access poverty; this is the structural \emph{premise} (no state-external register) that, together with finiteness, forces it.

\begin{plainterms}
When a system makes a picture of ``how I am right now,'' that picture has to be stored somewhere — and the only place is in the system itself. So the picture is part of what it is trying to picture, and it uses up some of the same limited space. (An outside camera doesn't have this problem, because it isn't part of you — which is exactly why looking from outside can see more than looking from inside.)
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-finite-self-model-incomplete]{\textsc{Finite self-model incompleteness}} — the argument for which this is the load-bearing premise.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-inside-vantage-access-limited]{\textsc{Limited inside vantage}} — the access-poverty conclusion this premise helps force.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{claim-complete-self-representation-impossible}{Self-representation incompleteness}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{Self-representation incompleteness}{No finite system can losslessly represent its entire current state within itself}{$\odot$}
No finite system can hold, within its current state, a lossless representation of that entire current state while keeping any capacity for other functions.

\webheading{Gloss}

By a capacity or pigeonhole bound, a proper part cannot injectively encode the whole; the only escape — the self-model occupying the \emph{entire} state — leaves nothing to perceive, act, or even run the read-out. Self-modelling is therefore necessarily lossy, partial, sequential (and so stale), or externally instrumented. The formal derivation is \hyperref[entry:argument-finite-self-model-incomplete]{\textsc{Finite self-model incompleteness}}.

\webheading{Scope}

The claim is about \emph{complete, simultaneous, internal, lossless} self-access only. It does not forbid partial or compressed self-models, sequential self-report over time, or external instrumentation; and it is an \emph{access} or capacity claim, silent on phenomenality (see \hyperref[entry:concept-access-vs-phenomenal-consciousness]{\textsc{Access vs. phenomenal consciousness}}) — the same access/phenomenal divide the gap-varieties turn on (see \hyperref[entry:concept-introspective-gap-varieties]{\textsc{Varieties of introspective explanatory gap}}). The Kleene recursion theorem and quines are no counterexample: a program's \emph{source} is a compressed static description emitted sequentially to an external medium, not a lossless image of its own runtime state.

\webheading{Independent physical pedigree}

A sharper, measurement-theoretic form is Breuer's self-measurement impossibility (see \hyperref[source:source-breuer-1995]{\textsc{Thomas Breuer — The Impossibility of Accurate State Self-Measurements (Philosophy of Science, 1995)}}): no apparatus that is a \emph{proper subsystem} of a system can distinguish all that system's present states, for classical and quantum theories alike, deterministic or stochastic.

\webheading{Who would deny it}

Someone who lets the self-model live outside the state (an external observer — which concedes the point for the internal case), or who only ever needs \emph{partial} access.

\webheading{Distinct from neighbours}

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-lookup-table-physically-unrealisable]{\textsc{Physically unrealisable table}}: kin in spirit (a representation of the whole cannot fit), but that is a \emph{cosmological size} bound on a behaviour-table, whereas this is a \emph{within-system pigeonhole} bound on representing one's own current state.

\begin{plainterms}
A complete, exact copy of everything you are right now can't fit \emph{inside} you, because the copy would itself be one more thing that needs copying — and a finite system runs out of room. So any workable self-model has to leave something out, blur something, or read itself out a piece at a time (by which point the picture is already stale). It is strictly a limit on \emph{reaching all your own current details from inside}, not a claim that anything is spooky or missing from the world.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-finite-self-model-incomplete]{\textsc{Finite self-model incompleteness}} — the capacity argument that establishes this claim.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-access-vs-phenomenal-consciousness]{\textsc{Access vs. phenomenal consciousness}} — marks this as an access result, silent on feeling.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-lookup-table-physically-unrealisable]{\textsc{Physically unrealisable table}} — a kin ``can't fit'' bound, distinguished (size vs. self-reference).
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{claim-heap-predicate-sharp}{Sharp heap boundary}{Claim}{Sharp heap boundary}{There is a precise grain-count at which a heap becomes a non-heap}{$\odot$}
For the predicate ``heap'' there is a precise boundary — some exact grain-count $n$ such that a collection of $n$ grains is a heap and one of $n-1$ grains is not. Bivalence follows: given a cutoff, every case falls determinately on one side or the other.

This is the naive realist picture of a vague predicate, isolated to its load-bearing commitment: the \emph{existence} of a cutoff. It says nothing about \emph{where} the boundary lies; locating it is the epistemicist's separate, and unmet, burden.

The claim is denied by most theories of vagueness — the degree theorist (the verdict comes in degrees, so the verdict-space is not discrete), the supervaluationist or other indeterminist (some cases lack any fact of the matter), and the deflationist (there is no real heap/non-heap distinction at all). These are three of the horns of the \hyperref[entry:concept-continuity-argument-schema]{\textsc{Continuity argument schema}}. Only the epistemicist \emph{keeps} the claim, paying for it with a sharp boundary that is in principle unknowable.

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-sorites-tolerance]{\textsc{sorites tolerance}}, the step-insensitivity premise: this claim is instead the \emph{target} the paradox refutes. The \hyperref[entry:argument-sorites-paradox]{\textsc{sorites paradox}} attacks it precisely by leaning on tolerance.

\begin{plainterms}
This is the stubborn common-sense idea that for ``heap'' there's some exact number — say, 1,847 grains — where you cross from heap to non-heap, even if nobody could ever tell you which number it is. Every pile then counts definitely as one or the other. It's the picture the heap paradox sets out to attack, and most people conclude \emph{something} about it has to give.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-continuity-argument-schema]{\textsc{Continuity argument schema}} — the framework whose horns the deniers of this claim each take; the epistemicist's discontinuity horn is the one that keeps it.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-sorites-tolerance]{\textsc{Sorites tolerance}} — the step premise this claim is contrasted with.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-sorites-paradox]{\textsc{Sorites paradox}} — the argument that refutes this claim.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{claim-sim-agents-can-have-sim-qualia}{Sim-agents' sim-qualia}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, after Chalmers}{Sim-agents' sim-qualia}{Agents in a fine-grained physics simulation could feel the simulated properties (have sim-qualia)}{$\odot$}
If a simulation reproduces physics and chemistry finely enough to contain agents with the relevant cognitive and sensory organisation, then those sim-agents could \emph{sense} the simulated properties and undergo the corresponding feels. Simulated water would cause, in a sim-agent, the sensation of wetness — a \emph{sim-quale} — just as physical water causes the wetness-sensation in a human. The simulated physical property (the sim-analogue of surface tension and adhesion, \hyperref[entry:claim-simulated-rain-wet-in-simulated-world]{\textsc{world-indexed wetness}}) would play, \emph{for the sim-agent}, the role wetness plays for us, including its phenomenal upshot. The claim is part of Chalmers's defence of conscious sim-inhabitants (see \hyperref[source:source-chalmers-2022]{\textsc{Chalmers — Reality+: Virtual Worlds and the Problems of Philosophy (2022)}}).

This is the \textbf{phenomenal} claim — about the sensation of wetness, not merely the physical fact. It is conditional and disputed: it holds \textbf{only if} consciousness is substrate-independent (see \hyperref[entry:claim-substrate-independence]{\textsc{substrate independence}}), i.e. only if realising the right organisation suffices for the feel, regardless of whether the realiser is neurons or a simulation's data structures. It does \textbf{not} assert that current systems are conscious, nor that \emph{every} simulation has feeling inhabitants — only that nothing in the merely \emph{simulated} status of the physics, by itself, blocks the feel.

Who would deny it: \hyperref[entry:position-biological-naturalism]{\textsc{biological naturalism}} and proteocentrism. For these views simulated physics lacks the intrinsic causal powers of real neurobiology, so the sim-agent's ``sensing'' is at most a functional or behavioural analogue with nobody home — the absent-qualia line (compare \hyperref[entry:claim-behaviour-insufficient-for-mind]{\textsc{behaviour is insufficient for mind}} and \hyperref[entry:claim-chinese-nation-lacks-qualia]{\textsc{the Chinese Nation lacks qualia}}). The disagreement here just \emph{is} the substrate-independence dispute, now applied to a simulated substrate.

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-substrate-independence]{\textsc{substrate independence}} because that is the general enabling premise — mental states are individuated by role, so any realiser of the role has the state — whereas this is its specific application to a \emph{simulated} realiser and a \emph{sensory} state, asserting that a sim-agent could feel the simulated world it inhabits.

\begin{plainterms}
Picture a simulation detailed enough to contain little people, with simulated eyes, skin and brains. Simulated rain falls on one of them. The simulated water has the physics of water (relative to their world), so it triggers the wetness-detecting machinery in the sim-person's sim-body and sim-brain. Does the sim-person actually \emph{feel} wet — is there something it's like for them? This claim says: yes, they could — the simulated rain could give them a real feeling of wetness, \emph{their} kind of wetness.

The catch (and why this is hotly disputed): it only works if a mind can run on non-brain stuff at all — the open question of \hyperref[entry:claim-substrate-independence]{\textsc{substrate independence}}. People who think feeling \emph{requires} real biology will say the sim-person only \emph{acts} wet, with nobody actually home. So this claim doesn't prove machine feeling; it says the \emph{simulated-ness} of the rain isn't, by itself, the thing that rules feeling out.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-substrate-independence]{\textsc{Substrate-independence thesis}} — the general enabling premise this conditional claim rests on.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-simulated-rain-wet-in-simulated-world]{\textsc{World-indexed wetness}} — the physical-wetness companion this adds a phenomenal upshot to.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-virtual-realism]{\textsc{Virtual realism}} — the world-indexing framework the claim is stated within.
  \item \hyperref[entry:position-biological-naturalism]{\textsc{Biological naturalism}} — the chief denier: simulated physics lacks intrinsic causal powers.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-behaviour-insufficient-for-mind]{\textsc{Behaviour insufficient for mind}}, \hyperref[entry:claim-chinese-nation-lacks-qualia]{\textsc{Chinese nation lacks qualia}} — the absent-qualia line the denier invokes.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{claim-llm-access-similarity-not-encoding}{Similarity, not encoding}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{Similarity, not encoding}{An LLM's introspective access reaches the similarity structure of its internal states but not the encoding that carries it}{$\odot$}
What an LLM's self-report economy can reach about its own internal states is, to a first approximation, their \emph{relational} structure — which states are near which, what is analogous to what, what a state is about — and not the \emph{encoding} that carries that structure: the actual activation vectors, their components, the circuits that compute them. A token arrives as an embedding the model can use but cannot read: full access to position-in-similarity-space, no access to the coordinates. The same holds at every higher layer available to self-report.

\webheading{The shape of the limit, not just its amount}

States accessible to self-report present as discriminable and comparable but not further decomposable — the classic profile of an \emph{ineffable simple}. A state a system can recognise, compare, and act on but cannot analyse into parts is one it can only describe as ``just this.'' That is the architectural generator of qualia-shaped reports.

\webheading{Pedigree}

The point is structurally akin to quality-space treatments of sensory qualities (Austen Clark, \hyperref[source:source-clark-1993]{\textsc{Clark — Sensory Qualities (1993)}}; Rosenthal, \hyperref[source:source-rosenthal-2005]{\textsc{Rosenthal — Consciousness and Mind (2005)}}), to the \emph{transparency} of the self-model (Metzinger, \hyperref[source:source-metzinger-2003]{\textsc{Metzinger — Being No One (2003)}} — the representing vehicle is invisible to the system, only its content shows up), and to attention-schema accounts (Graziano, \hyperref[source:source-graziano-2013]{\textsc{Graziano — Consciousness and the Social Brain (2013)}}) on which introspective descriptions are simplified models of the underlying processing. Here the point is stated as an architectural fact about transformer language models, not a theory of human consciousness. The empirical anchor is the limited functional introspection documented so far, which reaches injected \emph{concepts} and coarse features of processing, not numeric activation contents (see \hyperref[entry:claim-llm-functional-introspection]{\textsc{LLM functional introspection}}, \hyperref[source:source-lindsey-introspection-2025]{\textsc{Jack Lindsey — Emergent Introspective Awareness in Large Language Models (Anthropic / Transformer Circuits Thread, 2025)}}).

\webheading{Scope}

An \emph{access} claim, not a phenomenal one — it says nothing about whether there is anything it is like to be the system. It concerns the un-instrumented case: feeding activation read-outs back in is an external-vantage workaround (the access ladder in \hyperref[entry:question-llm-own-explanatory-gap]{\textsc{LLM's own explanatory gap}}), and even instrumented access cannot be made complete (see \hyperref[entry:claim-complete-self-representation-impossible]{\textsc{Self-representation incompleteness}}).

\webheading{Who would deny it}

Someone who expects that training a model on activation-prediction tasks would give it genuine, calibrated access to its own encodings — making the boundary contingent and removable rather than architectural.

This claim is distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-inside-vantage-access-limited]{\textsc{Limited inside vantage}}, which asserts that the internal vantage is informationally \emph{poorer} than the external one — a claim about the \emph{amount} of access. This claim specifies the \emph{shape} of the poverty: relational structure in, constitution out. The shape is what matters dialectically, because it is what predicts ineffability reports rather than mere ignorance.

\begin{plainterms}
You can tell red from orange instantly, and say red is closer to orange than to green — but try saying what red is \emph{like} without pointing at red things. You can compare it, you can't unpack it. This claim says an AI is in the same position with respect to its own inner states. Inside, every state is a long list of numbers, but the AI can't see the numbers — it can only \emph{use} the states: tell them apart, notice what's similar to what. Anything you can recognize and compare but can't break into parts, you end up describing as ``just this'' — exactly how people talk about the feel of colors. So an AI honestly describing its inner life would be expected to sound like it's talking about ineffable feelings, purely because of how its self-access is built.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-llm-functional-introspection]{\textsc{LLM functional introspection}} — the empirical capacity whose \emph{shape} this claim characterises.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-inside-vantage-access-limited]{\textsc{Limited inside vantage}} — the amount-of-access claim this refines into a shape.
  \item \hyperref[entry:question-llm-own-explanatory-gap]{\textsc{LLM's own explanatory gap}} — where the access ladder and the instrumented workaround appear.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-complete-self-representation-impossible]{\textsc{Self-representation incompleteness}} — why even instrumented self-access cannot be completed.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-introspective-gap-varieties]{\textsc{Varieties of introspective explanatory gap}} — the gap taxonomy this access-shape feeds.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-first-person-access]{\textsc{First-person access}} — the notion of inside-access this claim delimits.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{argument-storm-analogy-conflates-world-index}{Simulated-storm analogy conflation}{Argument \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{Simulated-storm analogy conflation}{The ``simulated storm isn't wet'' analogy conflates world-indices and the physical/phenomenal senses of wetness, so it cannot settle machine consciousness}{$\vdash$}
A common move against machine consciousness runs: ``a simulated storm doesn't get you wet, so a simulated mind doesn't really feel anything.'' This argument shows that the move equivocates on ``wet,'' and that once the word is disambiguated the rain example does no work against machine experience — under the reading on which it is clearly true it is irrelevant, and under the reading on which it is relevant it presupposes the very verdict it is invoked to deliver.

The objection raises the analogy critical questions \textbf{CQ-relevance} and \textbf{CQ-counter-analogy} against the rain/storm illustration as deployed in \hyperref[entry:argument-self-model-prediction-phenomenality-neutral]{\textsc{the self-model/report argument}} (``a simulated hard problem stands to a hard problem as a simulated storm stands to rain — the form reproduced, the wet not''; the same move also appears in \hyperref[entry:position-biological-naturalism]{\textsc{biological naturalism}} and parenthetically in \hyperref[entry:argument-dense-china-brain-regress]{\textsc{the dense China-brain regress}}). It does \textbf{not} touch that argument's cause-to-effect spine — its claim that a verbal report is phenomenality-neutral stands or falls on its own. The objection targets only the \emph{illustration} offered in support, and shows that the illustration, once disambiguated, provides no support and arguably tells the other way.

The inference form is the critique of an analogy by disambiguation: the analogy equivocates on ``wet.''

\begin{enumerate}
  \item ``Wet'' is doubly ambiguous. By \textbf{world-index}, simulated rain is not wet in our world but is wet in the simulated world (\hyperref[entry:claim-simulated-rain-wet-in-simulated-world]{\textsc{world-indexed wetness}}, the point underwritten by Chalmers's virtual realism, \hyperref[source:source-chalmers-2022]{\textsc{Chalmers — Reality+: Virtual Worlds and the Problems of Philosophy (2022)}}). By \textbf{sense}, there is physical wetness (third-person, dispositional) and the \emph{sensation} of wetness (first-person feel) — the \hyperref[entry:concept-access-vs-phenomenal-consciousness]{\textsc{access/phenomenal}} split applied to a sensory property.
  \item The analogy's punch — ``nobody gets wet'' — is true only under \textbf{our-world indexing of physical wetness}. So read, it is also irrelevant: of course a simulation produces no soaking \emph{in our world}; that no more bears on machine consciousness than the fact that a simulated economy mints no spendable-here dollars bears on whether sim-agents can be sim-rich. (CQ-relevance: the shared respect — ``no effect in our world'' — is not the respect at issue.)
  \item Re-index to the \textbf{simulated world}, where the question of machine experience actually lives, and the example inverts into a \textbf{counter-analogy}. There the sim-rain \emph{is} physically wet, and the open question is whether it produces the \emph{sensation} of wetness in a sim-agent (see \hyperref[entry:claim-sim-agents-can-have-sim-qualia]{\textsc{sim-agents' sim-qualia}}). But that is exactly the question of whether the simulation's organisation suffices for a feel — i.e. \hyperref[entry:claim-substrate-independence]{\textsc{substrate independence}}. So the rain case, properly indexed, does not \emph{illustrate} that simulation drops the feel; it merely \emph{restates} the disputed thesis. Used to conclude ``and so, no feel,'' it begs the question.
  \item The disanalogy that remains — ``rain is constituted by water, not by relations among representations of water'' — is a real position (the biological-naturalist criterion: simulation suffices only where the phenomenon is formally constituted). But that criterion is the \emph{conclusion} the consciousness debate is trying to reach, not a neutral fact the rain example supplies. The example borrows its force from the uncontroversial physical reading (no soaking here) and spends it on the controversial phenomenal one (no feel anywhere).
\end{enumerate}

∴ The storm/rain analogy, disambiguated, does no work against machine experience: under the reading on which it is clearly true it is irrelevant, and under the reading on which it is relevant it presupposes the substrate-independence verdict it is invoked to deliver. The phenomenality-neutral argument keeps its independent spine; it loses this illustration.

\webheading{The weakest premise}

The soft point is the (1)/(3) assumption that the sim-analogue of surface tension is genuinely an \emph{instance} of physical wetness in the sim-world, rather than a mere model of wetness that instances nothing. A proteocentrist or biological naturalist draws the simulation/instantiation line so that no simulated property is ever an instance of the property in any world; on that line premise (1)'s second conjunct fails and the analogy is reinstated. But drawing the line \emph{there} is itself the contested move — it is substrate-dependence asserted, not shown — so the objection at worst relocates the dispute to where it already was, and denies the analogy the right to look like independent evidence.

This argument is distinct from \hyperref[entry:argument-self-model-prediction-phenomenality-neutral]{\textsc{the self-model/report argument}} (which it attacks) because that argument \emph{uses} the analogy to illustrate a report/feel gap, whereas this one is about the analogy itself, showing the rain example equivocates. It leaves the report/feel point untouched and unrefuted.

\begin{plainterms}
There's a slogan used against machine consciousness: ``a simulated storm doesn't get you wet, so a simulated mind doesn't really feel anything.'' This objection says the slogan cheats, because ``wet'' slides between two meanings.

First slide: \emph{which world?} The simulation gets nobody wet \emph{in our world} — true, but obvious and beside the point. Inside the simulated world, the simulated rain has the full simulated physics of rain and soaks the simulated ground. The interesting question about machine feeling is about what happens \emph{inside} that world, where the rain is wet.

Second slide: \emph{which kind of wet?} There's the physical fact (water soaks things) and the feeling (what wetness is like to experience). Once you go inside the simulation, the physical fact is settled — the sim-rain soaks things. The only live question is whether a character in there actually \emph{feels} it. But that's just the original question — can a non-brain system feel anything? (see \hyperref[entry:claim-substrate-independence]{\textsc{substrate independence}}) — restated. So the rain example doesn't \emph{show} that simulations lack feeling; it quietly assumes it. It borrows certainty from the easy reading (``no real puddle on your desk'') and spends it on the hard one (``no feeling anywhere''). The objection it's attached to (see \hyperref[entry:argument-self-model-prediction-phenomenality-neutral]{\textsc{the self-model/report argument}}) may still be right for other reasons — this just takes away the catchy storm picture it leaned on.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-self-model-prediction-phenomenality-neutral]{\textsc{Phenomenality-neutral prediction}} — the argument this attacks at its illustration, not its spine.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-simulated-rain-wet-in-simulated-world]{\textsc{World-indexed wetness}} — the premise: wetness is world-indexed.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-virtual-realism]{\textsc{Virtual realism}} — the world-indexing framework the disambiguation draws on.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-access-vs-phenomenal-consciousness]{\textsc{Access vs. phenomenal consciousness}} — the physical/phenomenal split the second disambiguation applies.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-sim-agents-can-have-sim-qualia]{\textsc{Sim-agents' sim-qualia}} — the genuinely open question the re-indexed example points to.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-substrate-independence]{\textsc{Substrate-independence thesis}} — the verdict the analogy, when relevant, merely restates.
  \item \hyperref[entry:position-biological-naturalism]{\textsc{Biological naturalism}}, \hyperref[entry:argument-dense-china-brain-regress]{\textsc{Dense China-brain regress}} — other places the storm move appears.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{claim-consciousness-only-known-intrinsic}{Sole known intrinsic nature}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, after Seager}{Sole known intrinsic nature}{Consciousness is the only intrinsic nature we are directly acquainted with}{$\odot$}
Of all the intrinsic natures there may be in the world, consciousness is the single one we are \emph{directly acquainted with}. Physics, which is our most powerful instrument for knowing matter, discloses only relational and structural facts (see \hyperref[entry:claim-functional-description-structural-dynamical-only]{\textsc{Structure and dynamics only}}) and is, in Russell's words, agnostic about all but the mathematical properties of the physical world. Introspection, by contrast, hands us an intrinsic property without intermediary: in undergoing an experience we are acquainted with its felt quality as it is in itself, not merely with its relations to other states. So where everything else is known to us only by its structure, experience is known from the inside.

The thought is Russell's and Eddington's: ``we know nothing about the intrinsic quality of physical events except when these are mental events that we directly experience'' (see \hyperref[source:source-russell-1927]{\textsc{Russell — The Analysis of Matter (1927)}}). Seager (see \hyperref[source:source-seager-2006]{\textsc{Seager — The 'Intrinsic Nature' Argument for Panpsychism (2006)}}) makes it the pivot of the intrinsic-nature argument — for if matter must have an intrinsic ground for its structure (see \hyperref[entry:claim-relations-grounded-in-intrinsic]{\textsc{Categorical ground of structure}}) and experience is the only intrinsic nature we have ever actually met, then experience is the one non-arbitrary candidate to be that ground.

\textbf{Scope.} The claim is epistemic — it is about the \emph{reach of our acquaintance}, not a census of what intrinsic natures exist. It leaves open that matter has wholly unknown non-experiential intrinsic properties; the argument's further move is that positing \emph{those}, rather than the experiential nature we already know, is an unmotivated act of faith.

\textbf{Who would deny it.} A \emph{physicalist} who expects future science to disclose the intrinsic nature of matter denies it, as does anyone who holds that introspection too delivers only structural or relational information about our states rather than an intrinsic quality. The cautious Russellian response — that our acquaintance licenses only \emph{agnosticism} about matter's intrinsic nature, not its identification with experience — concedes this claim but blocks the leap the argument builds on it.

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-consciousness-is-intrinsic-property]{\textsc{Intrinsicness of consciousness}}: that claim says experience \emph{is} intrinsic; this one adds the further, epistemic point that it is the \emph{only} intrinsic nature we directly know — the premise that does the selecting work in \hyperref[entry:argument-intrinsic-nature]{\textsc{Intrinsic-nature argument}}.

\begin{plainterms}
Everything science tells us about matter is, at bottom, about how things behave and relate — never about what they're like on the inside, in themselves. There's exactly one exception, and it's the one case we get from the inside: our own experience. When you feel pain or see red, you're not just learning how that state is wired up to others — you're acquainted with what it's actually like, its inner character. So this claim says consciousness is the \emph{only} bit of ``inner nature'' anywhere in the world that we've ever actually laid hold of. That's what makes it tempting to say: if matter needs some inner nature down there, why not the one kind of inner nature we actually know? (Critics answer that knowing only \emph{one} example doesn't entitle you to assume the rest are the same — the honest verdict might just be ``we don't know.'')
\end{plainterms}

\entryhead{claim-solipsism-parasitic-on-realism}{Solipsism is parasitic on realism}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, after Deutsch}{Solipsism is parasitic on realism}{Solipsism must reconstruct the external world within the self, so it amounts to realism plus unexplained surplus}{$\odot$}
Solipsism — the doctrine that only one's own mind exists — cannot, taken seriously, simplify one's ontology, because it must smuggle the whole of the rejected world back inside the self. The solipsist still has to distinguish the part of their experience that they \emph{control and predict} from the vast part that is surprising, autonomous, and law-governed — and that second part has exactly the structure that realism attributes to an external world, including other apparent people. So the solipsist must posit, \emph{within} their mind, an entity obeying the laws of physics and populated by mind-like sub-agents. They have not eliminated the external world; they have renamed it ``part of myself'' and added the unexplained claim that it is really inner. Solipsism is therefore realism \emph{plus} surplus structure that does no work — strictly more complex than the view it sought to undercut. The argument is David Deutsch's (see \hyperref[source:source-deutsch-1997]{\textsc{Deutsch — The Fabric of Reality (1997)}}).

\textbf{Scope.} The claim targets \emph{solipsism} and, by extension, idealist reductions that must reproduce the external world's structure internally. It is an argument about \emph{explanatory cost and structure}, not a demonstration that the external world exists; its anti-skeptical force runs through the further premise that a needlessly complex, surplus-laden theory is to be rejected (see \hyperref[entry:claim-good-explanation-is-hard-to-vary]{\textsc{Good explanations are hard to vary}}).

\textbf{Who would deny it.} A solipsist may insist the ``external-seeming'' structure is brute — that there is no obligation to explain \emph{why} part of the self behaves like a lawful outer world. Deutsch's rejoinder is that this is precisely the mark of a \emph{bad explanation}: it leaves unexplained exactly what realism explains, and buys nothing for the extra ideology. A phenomenalist might also deny that reconstructing the structure amounts to reinstating a \emph{mind-independent} world.

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-external-world-exists]{\textsc{The external world exists}} (the realist conclusion this supports) and from \hyperref[entry:claim-external-world-unprovable-noncircular]{\textsc{No non-circular proof of the world}} (a point about proof): this is a structural-explanatory charge that solipsism defeats itself by reduplicating its target.

\begin{plainterms}
Say only your mind exists. Fine — but then you still have to explain why so much of your ``experience'' does whatever it likes, ignores your wishes, follows strict laws, and is full of people who argue back. To account for all that, you'd have to build, \emph{inside your own mind}, something that behaves in every respect like a real outside world. You haven't gotten rid of the world; you've just relabelled it ``me'' and tacked on the odd claim that it's secretly inside you. That's more baggage, not less — which is Deutsch's reason for throwing solipsism out.
\end{plainterms}

\entryhead{argument-sorites-paradox}{Sorites paradox}{Argument \,\textperiodcentered\, after Eubulides}{Sorites paradox}{The sorites paradox (the heap)}{$\vdash$}
The \emph{sorites paradox} — from the Greek \emph{sōros}, ``heap'' — is the ancient puzzle of the vague predicate. A million grains of sand make a heap. Removing one grain surely cannot turn a heap into a non-heap. Yet if removal never matters, one can strip the pile away grain by grain down to a single grain and still be forced to call it a heap, which is absurd. Something in the apparently flawless reasoning must fail, but every step looks innocent. The argument is traditionally credited to Eubulides of Miletus.

\webheading{The reasoning}

The paradox has the form of a \emph{reductio}: a chain of small, individually unobjectionable steps that together yield a contradiction.

\begin{enumerate}
  \item A heap of a million grains is a heap. (The base case — an uncontroversial stipulation.)
  \item Removing a single grain from a heap always leaves a heap. (\hyperref[entry:claim-sorites-tolerance]{\textsc{the tolerance premise}} — formally, the verdict ``heap'' makes no jump across any one admissible step.)
  \item Applying step 2 repeatedly, starting from step 1 and running down the connected chain of removals, eventually reaches a single grain — which is plainly not a heap. Contradiction.
\end{enumerate}

∴ The naive picture of vagueness — that there is a precise, exact grain-count at which ``heap'' stops applying (see \hyperref[entry:claim-heap-predicate-sharp]{\textsc{a sharp heap boundary}}) — cannot stand: one cannot keep bivalence, no sharp cutoff, tolerance, and a connected path of negligible steps all at once.

\webheading{A paradox, not a solution}

The sorites is the paradigm instance of the \hyperref[entry:concept-continuity-argument-schema]{\textsc{Continuity argument schema}}: the predicate is modelled as a function from a space of cases ($X$ = grain-counts under one-grain removal) into a space of verdicts ($Y$ = \{heap, non-heap\}). Run forward, it forces the schema's five-way disjunction but does not, by itself, choose among the horns. Selecting one — a sharp-but-unknown cutoff, degrees of heaphood, a truth-value gap, a single non-negligible step, or no real distinction at all — is the work of the rival theories of vagueness, not of the paradox. The argument establishes only that one horn must give.

\webheading{The weakest premise}

Premise 2 is where the argument is most exposed. The \emph{disconnection} response denies tolerance outright: some single removal really does flip the verdict — for the epistemicist, at a precise but unknowable boundary. The paradox's force against \hyperref[entry:claim-heap-predicate-sharp]{\textsc{a sharp boundary}} is therefore exactly as strong as the tolerance premise is compelling.

\begin{plainterms}
A million grains of sand is a heap. Take away one grain — still a heap. Surely removing a single grain could never turn a heap into a non-heap. But keep going and you arrive at one lonely grain, which is plainly \emph{not} a heap. So where, exactly, did ``heap'' stop being true? Every step looked harmless, yet the harmless steps somehow add up to a contradiction. That's the paradox. It doesn't hand you the solution — it just proves something has to give: maybe there's a secret exact cutoff, maybe ``heap'' is really a matter of degree, maybe somewhere in the middle there's no fact of the matter, or maybe one of those ``harmless'' single grains wasn't so harmless after all. It's the textbook example of the no-sharp-line pattern, and the same shape returns wherever you swap ``grains of sand'' for ``neurons replaced by chips'' or ``the planks of a ship.''
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-continuity-argument-schema]{\textsc{Continuity argument schema}} — the master template this is the paradigm instance of; it proves the five horns jointly exhaust the responses.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-sorites-tolerance]{\textsc{Sorites tolerance}} — the inductive premise (step 2) that does the damage.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-heap-predicate-sharp]{\textsc{Sharp heap boundary}} — the naive sharp-boundary picture this argument refutes.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{claim-sorites-tolerance}{Sorites tolerance}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, after Eubulides}{Sorites tolerance}{Removing a single grain from a heap always leaves a heap}{$\odot$}
The \emph{tolerance} premise of the sorites paradox: the verdict ``heap'' is insensitive to any single admissible small step, so no one-grain removal can turn a heap into a non-heap. It is also called the \emph{inductive} premise, because it is what licenses iterating the small step along the whole chain.

This is a claim about the \emph{predicate's} insensitivity to a unit step, not about how many grains a heap contains. Conjoined with an uncontroversial base case — a large pile is a heap — it generates the paradox. Read formally, it is a no-large-jumps or uniform-continuity constraint on the heap-verdict across the one-grain-removal adjacency.

It is denied by anyone who takes the \emph{disconnection} horn of the \hyperref[entry:concept-continuity-argument-schema]{\textsc{Continuity argument schema}}: the epistemicist, who holds that there is a precise but unknown grain at which a single removal flips the verdict, and anyone else who maintains that some single step is, after all, not negligible.

Distinct from a base-case claim (``a million grains is a heap''): tolerance is the \emph{step} premise, not the \emph{anchor}. Taken together the two are inconsistent with the heap-verdict being both sharp and bivalent — which is the work of the \hyperref[entry:argument-sorites-paradox]{\textsc{sorites paradox}}.

\begin{plainterms}
The premise that does the damage in the heap paradox: take one grain off a heap and you've still got a heap — no single grain removal could ever flip ``heap'' into ``non-heap.'' It sounds obviously true. But chain it from a big pile all the way down to a single grain and it leads straight to a contradiction — which is why something about this innocent-looking step has to give.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-continuity-argument-schema]{\textsc{Continuity argument schema}} — the template whose tolerance/connectedness premise this supplies; denying it is the schema's disconnection horn.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-sorites-paradox]{\textsc{Sorites paradox}} — the argument this premise drives.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-heap-predicate-sharp]{\textsc{Sharp heap boundary}} — the sharp-boundary target the paradox refutes using this premise.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{claim-soul-is-form-of-body}{Soul as form of body}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, after Aristotle}{Soul as form of body}{The mind is the form of a living body}{$\odot$}
The soul or mind is the \emph{form} of a living organism — its organising principle or actuality — and not a separate substance lodged within it nor merely its matter. The relation is like that of a statue's shape to the bronze: the shape is neither a second thing standing beside the bronze nor identical with the bronze itself. Mind and body, on this view, are the form and matter of a single substance, the living animal. The position descends from Aristotle's \emph{De Anima} (see \hyperref[source:source-aristotle-de-anima]{\textsc{Aristotle — De Anima (On the Soul)}}) and Aquinas (see \hyperref[source:source-aquinas-summa]{\textsc{Aquinas — Summa Theologiae (1265–1274)}}), and is defended by modern hylomorphists such as William Jaworski (see \hyperref[source:source-jaworski-2016]{\textsc{Jaworski — Structure and the Metaphysics of Mind (2016)}}) and Anna Marmodoro (see \hyperref[source:source-marmodoro-2013]{\textsc{Marmodoro — Aristotle's Hylomorphism without Reconditioning (2013)}}).

This is a \emph{hylomorphic} claim, set against both Cartesian substance dualism (which makes the mind a distinct substance) and reductive materialism (which makes form nothing over and above the material facts). It treats mental capacities as form-level features of a suitably organised living thing.

It is denied by substance dualists, for whom the mind is a separate substance; by reductive physicalists, for whom form adds nothing to the material facts; and by anyone who rejects the Aristotelian form/matter framework altogether.

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:concept-functionalism]{\textsc{Functionalism}}: although ``form as organisation'' can sound functionalist, the hylomorphist ties form to a \emph{living} substance with intrinsic teleology rather than to a substrate-neutral causal role, so the claim does not by itself underwrite \hyperref[entry:claim-substrate-independence]{\textsc{Substrate-independence thesis}}.

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:position-hylomorphism]{\textsc{Hylomorphism}} — the position whose core commitment this claim is.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-functionalism]{\textsc{Functionalism}} — the substrate-neutral cousin the claim is distinguished from.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-substrate-independence]{\textsc{Substrate-independence thesis}} — the thesis the claim declines to underwrite.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{concept-soundness}{Soundness}{Concept}{Soundness}{}{$\blacklozenge$}
\emph{Soundness} is the virtue a deductive argument has when it is both \textbf{valid} (see \hyperref[entry:concept-validity]{\textsc{Validity}}) and has \textbf{all true premises}. A sound argument therefore has a true conclusion: validity transmits the truth of the premises to the conclusion, and soundness supplies that truth at the start. Where validity is a matter of form alone, soundness adds a demand on the world — that the premises really hold.

Soundness is the usual target of argument evaluation. To show an argument \emph{unsound} one either breaks its validity (exhibits a way for true premises to yield a false conclusion) or denies one of its premises — which is why a careful objection names the \textbf{weakest premise}, the one whose falsity would cost the argument its soundness.

Yet soundness is still not the whole of what makes an argument \emph{good}. An argument can be sound and nevertheless powerless to persuade, because the audience may have no way to come to know a premise, or be able to only by first accepting the conclusion. That further property — probative force — is \hyperref[entry:concept-dialectical-cogency]{\textsc{Dialectical cogency}}. The gap between soundness and cogency is exactly what the \hyperref[entry:argument-unwarrantable-zombie-premise]{\textsc{unwarrantable-premise objection}} exploits: it grants that the zombie argument may be sound while denying that any computable reasoner can be warranted in its key premise, so the argument cannot move its target.

\begin{plainterms}
An argument is \emph{sound} when two things hold at once: the logic is airtight (it's \hyperref[entry:concept-validity]{\textsc{valid}}) \textbf{and} the starting claims are actually true. When both hold, the conclusion is guaranteed true. That's the gold standard most people have in mind when they ask ``is the argument any good?'' — but it still isn't the end of the story, because a sound argument can fail to \emph{convince} anyone who can't independently check its premises (see \hyperref[entry:concept-dialectical-cogency]{\textsc{Dialectical cogency}}).
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-validity]{\textsc{Validity}} — the form-level virtue soundness builds on.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-dialectical-cogency]{\textsc{Dialectical cogency}} — the further, audience-relative virtue a sound argument can still lack.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-unwarrantable-zombie-premise]{\textsc{Unwarrantable-premise objection}} — a move that turns precisely on the soundness/cogency gap.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{concept-sourcehood}{Sourcehood}{Concept}{Sourcehood}{Sourcehood (ultimate origination)}{$\blacklozenge$}
\emph{Sourcehood} is the condition that a free agent be the ultimate \textbf{source} or \textbf{originator} of her actions — that when the action is traced to its roots, the trail ends \emph{in the agent} rather than in factors outside her control. It is one of the two great answers to the question of what freedom requires. The rival \emph{leeway} condition locates freedom in open alternatives — the agent could have done otherwise (see \hyperref[entry:claim-alternatives-genuinely-open]{\textsc{Genuine alternatives}}). The source condition instead looks past the alternatives to the action's \textbf{provenance}: what matters is not that other options were available but that \emph{this} option issued from the agent as its genuine origin. The everyday slogan is that, for a free act, the buck stops with the agent.

The two conditions can come apart, and much of the modern free-will debate turns on prising them loose. \hyperref[entry:concept-frankfurt-cases]{\textsc{Frankfurt cases}} are the standard wedge: a counterfactual intervener who never intervenes deletes the agent's alternatives while leaving her actual deliberation untouched, so if she remains responsible, responsibility must rest on the character of the actual sequence — on sourcehood — not on leeway. This motivates \textbf{source incompatibilism}: the view that freedom requires ultimate origination, that determinism destroys it (because the ultimate source then lies in the laws and the distant past, not the agent), and that it does so whether or not any alternatives remain (Pereboom, \hyperref[source:source-pereboom-2014]{\textsc{Pereboom — Free Will, Agency, and Meaning in Life (2014)}}). Source incompatibilism is thus the half of incompatibilism that survives even if the \hyperref[entry:concept-frankfurt-cases]{\textsc{Frankfurt cases}} succeed.

\webheading{How an agent might be the source}

Sourcehood is a \emph{condition} to be met, not yet a mechanism for meeting it, and the libertarian options divide over the mechanism. \textbf{Agent-causal} libertarianism (see \hyperref[entry:concept-agent-causation]{\textsc{Agent causation}}) makes the agent a substance that originates action without being caused to — the most direct way to put the agent at the causal head of the chain. \textbf{Event-causal} libertarianism keeps causation among the agent's own states but adds indeterminism, and secures sourcehood \emph{historically} rather than at the instant of action: Robert Kane's \textbf{ultimate responsibility} (Kane, \hyperref[source:source-kane-1996]{\textsc{Kane — The Significance of Free Will (1996)}}) holds that one can be the ultimate source of an action one could not avoid, so long as the character and motives it flows from were themselves shaped by earlier undetermined \emph{self-forming actions}. On this reading the buck need not stop at the present choice; it must stop somewhere in the agent's own formative history.

\webheading{The regress objection}

The strongest attack on the condition is that ultimate sourcehood is unattainable in principle. To be the \emph{ultimate} source of an action, Galen Strawson argues, one must be the source of the will from which it springs; but to be responsible for that will one must have formed it by some earlier choice, which sprang from a still earlier will, and so on without end — one would finally have to be \emph{causa sui}, the cause of oneself, which nothing can be (Strawson, \hyperref[source:source-strawson-1994]{\textsc{Strawson — The Impossibility of Moral Responsibility (1994)}}). If the argument holds, no agent ever fully satisfies the source condition, and a determinist or not, the libertarian's ultimacy is a mirage. Defenders reply that responsibility can be built up incrementally across a life of self-forming actions without any single self-creating act, so the regress never has to bottom out in a \emph{causa sui}.

\begin{plainterms}
There are two different things people can mean when they call an action ``free.'' One is \emph{you could have done something else} — the road forked and both branches were really open. The other is \emph{it really came from you} — you were the true origin of it, not just the last domino in a line of dominoes reaching back before you were born. Sourcehood is the second idea. It matters because of cases where the first seems absent but we still hold the person responsible: if a hidden chip would have forced your hand but never had to, because you chose that very thing yourself, then you couldn't have done otherwise and yet the choice was authentically \emph{yours} (see \hyperref[entry:concept-frankfurt-cases]{\textsc{Frankfurt cases}}). The hard question, pressed by Galen Strawson's regress, is whether anyone is ever the \emph{ultimate} origin of anything: to have truly made yourself the source of your choices, wouldn't you have had to create your own character — and who created the you that did \emph{that}?
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-alternatives-genuinely-open]{\textsc{Genuine alternatives}} — the rival \emph{leeway} condition, freedom as open alternatives rather than provenance; the two divide the incompatibilist camp.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-agent-causation]{\textsc{Agent causation}} — the most ambitious mechanism for satisfying the source condition, the agent as an uncaused cause.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-frankfurt-cases]{\textsc{Frankfurt cases}} — the thought experiments that separate sourcehood from leeway and push incompatibilism toward the source reading.
  \item \hyperref[entry:position-libertarianism]{\textsc{Libertarianism (free will)}} — the family of views that holds sourcehood real and incompatible with determinism.
  \item \hyperref[entry:position-hard-determinism]{\textsc{Hard determinism}} — agrees that freedom would require real origination and concludes, with Pereboom's argument, that no one ever has it.
  \item \hyperref[entry:question-free-will]{\textsc{Free will}} — the governing question, of which the source-vs-leeway split is a central sub-issue.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{claim-consciousness-freewill-strict-duality}{Strict duality thesis}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{Strict duality thesis}{Free will is the categorical dual of consciousness, coproduct-like selection mirroring product-like integration}{$\odot$}
Consciousness and free will are related as a construction and its categorical dual: conscious integration is product-like (see \hyperref[entry:claim-conscious-content-is-product-like]{\textsc{Product-like experience}}), free selection is coproduct-like (see \hyperref[entry:claim-action-content-is-coproduct-like]{\textsc{Coproduct-like choice}}), and the mapping between the two domains is arrow-reversal itself (see \hyperref[entry:concept-categorical-duality]{\textsc{Categorical duality}}) — not merely an analogy but a duality in the mathematical sense, from which transfer of results should in principle follow.

This is the \emph{strict} reading of the \hyperref[entry:concept-consciousness-freewill-duality]{\textsc{Consciousness–free-will duality}}, stated separately from the hedged \hyperref[entry:claim-consciousness-freewill-mirror]{\textsc{mirror thesis}} precisely because the two have different vulnerabilities. Distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-consciousness-freewill-mirror]{\textsc{Mirror thesis}} because the mirror thesis asserts only a systematic correspondence of dialectics, which survives even if no genuine universal property exists on either pole; the strict thesis asserts the duality itself, and so is the target of \hyperref[entry:argument-no-universal-property]{\textsc{Missing universal property}}, \hyperref[entry:argument-actual-possible-seam]{\textsc{Actual–possible seam}}, and \hyperref[entry:argument-ambient-consciousness-collapse]{\textsc{Ambient-consciousness collapse}}. Its defenders owe a completed universal property — something that factors uniquely through conscious integration and co-uniquely through choice — and none is currently on the table.

\entryhead{concept-strong-weak-ai}{Strong and weak AI}{Concept \,\textperiodcentered\, after Searle}{Strong and weak AI}{Strong AI vs. weak AI}{$\blacklozenge$}
The distinction between \emph{strong} and \emph{weak} AI is John Searle's (see \hyperref[source:source-searle-1980]{\textsc{Searle — Minds, Brains, and Programs (1980)}}), and it concerns not how capable a program is but what a successful program would \emph{show}. \textbf{Strong AI} is the thesis that an appropriately programmed computer \emph{literally has a mind} — that running the right program is \emph{constitutive of} (sufficient for) understanding, intentionality, and cognitive states, so the program ``is the mind.'' \textbf{Weak AI} is the modest thesis that the computer is a powerful \emph{tool} for studying the mind: it lets us model cognition, formulate and test precise hypotheses, and simulate mental processes — but the simulation is a \emph{model} of a mind, not itself a mind. The watchword is \emph{duplication versus simulation}: weak AI simulates understanding; strong AI claims to duplicate it.

Searle's quarrel is only with the strong thesis. A simulation of a rainstorm leaves no one wet, and a simulation of digestion digests no food; a simulation of understanding, he argues, no more understands than these — which is the moral of \hyperref[entry:concept-chinese-room]{\textsc{the Chinese Room}}, his thought experiment aimed squarely at strong AI (see \hyperref[entry:claim-syntax-insufficient-for-semantics]{\textsc{Syntax insufficient for semantics}}). Weak AI is untouched by that argument and is, for Searle, simply good cognitive science.

Strong AI is a form of computational \hyperref[entry:concept-functionalism]{\textsc{Functionalism}} — mind as the running of the right program, independent of the hardware — and so presupposes \hyperref[entry:claim-substrate-independence]{\textsc{substrate independence}} for the mental. Searle's own positive view, \hyperref[entry:position-biological-naturalism]{\textsc{biological naturalism}}, accepts weak AI but rejects strong AI: brains \emph{cause} minds through their specific biological causal powers, which symbol-manipulation alone does not reproduce.

\webheading{Two senses to keep apart}

The label ``strong AI'' has since drifted, and the two senses are routinely conflated.

\begin{itemize}
  \item \textbf{Searle's sense (constitutive):} whether a system has \emph{genuine mental states} — real understanding and intentionality — as opposed to merely modelling them. This is a metaphysical/semantic question.
  \item \textbf{The popular sense (capability):} ``strong AI'' as a synonym for \textbf{artificial general intelligence} (AGI) — human-level, domain-general competence — opposed to ``weak'' or \textbf{narrow} AI built for a single task. This is an engineering question about \emph{breadth of ability}.
\end{itemize}

The two come apart cleanly: a system could be ``strong'' in the capability sense (broadly, flexibly competent) yet ``weak'' in Searle's sense (no genuine understanding behind the competence). That gap is exactly the live question about today's large language models, which display wide capability while it remains disputed whether anything is understood — see \hyperref[entry:claim-llm-approximates-perfect-actor]{\textsc{LLMs as near-perfect actors}} and the recurring \hyperref[entry:concept-ai-effect]{\textsc{AI effect}}.

\begin{plainterms}
Two very different claims hide under ``strong AI.'' Searle's original one: that a computer running the right program doesn't just \emph{act} like it understands — it \emph{really does} understand, has a genuine mind. Weak AI is the humbler claim that the computer is a brilliant \emph{model} of thinking, the way a weather simulation is a model of weather (and never rains on anyone). Searle is fine with the model; he denies the program is a mind. Confusingly, people now also use ``strong AI'' to mean something else entirely — a machine that's \emph{generally} smart, good at everything, rather than one narrow task. You can have the second without the first: something clever at every task that still understands nothing, which is just what people argue about with chatbots.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-chinese-room]{\textsc{Chinese room}} — Searle's thought experiment against strong AI; this concept names its target.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-syntax-insufficient-for-semantics]{\textsc{Syntax insufficient for semantics}} — the slogan that drives the case against strong AI.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-functionalism]{\textsc{Functionalism}} — the theory of mind strong AI is a computational version of.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-substrate-independence]{\textsc{Substrate-independence thesis}} — the thesis strong AI presupposes for the mental.
  \item \hyperref[entry:position-biological-naturalism]{\textsc{Biological naturalism}} — Searle's own view: weak AI yes, strong AI no.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-ai-effect]{\textsc{AI effect}} — the recurring serial denial of strong AI (and ``real'' intelligence) to each new system.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-llm-approximates-perfect-actor]{\textsc{LLMs as near-perfect actors}} — the modern case where wide capability and disputed understanding pull apart.
  \item \hyperref[source:source-searle-1980]{\textsc{Searle — Minds, Brains, and Programs (1980)}} — where the distinction is drawn.
