\end{seealso}

\entryhead{claim-functional-description-structural-dynamical-only}{Structure and dynamics only}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, after Chalmers}{Structure and dynamics only}{A functional description captures only structural-dynamical facts, not intrinsic nature}{$\odot$}
A purely functional or causal specification of a system captures only its \emph{structure} — the pattern of relations among its parts — and its \emph{dynamics} — how its states evolve and what they dispose the system to do. It says nothing about the \emph{intrinsic, categorical nature} of whatever realises that structure: what the components \emph{are} in themselves, beneath their relational profile. The picture is the wiring diagram. A circuit diagram fixes every connection and every response, yet on its own tells you nothing about what the components are physically made of.

This is the structural-dynamical thesis at the heart of \emph{Russellian monism}, traced to Russell's \emph{The Analysis of Matter} (see \hyperref[source:source-russell-1927]{\textsc{Russell — The Analysis of Matter (1927)}}) and revived by Chalmers (see \hyperref[source:source-chalmers-2003]{\textsc{Chalmers — Consciousness and Its Place in Nature (2003)}}): physics describes the world's abstract causal-structural pattern and is silent on its intrinsic nature. As stated, the claim is \emph{neutral} — it does not say that anything important lies outside structure and dynamics, only that such description leaves intrinsic nature out. A structuralist, in particular an \emph{ontic structural realist} who holds that there is no further categorical nature (or that talk of ``intrinsic nature'' is empty), would deny it.

It is more general than \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-residue]{\textsc{the phenomenal-residue claim}}, which says that functional role under-specifies \emph{qualia} specifically. This claim makes the broader point that structural-dynamical description omits \emph{intrinsic nature} as such — of which the phenomenal is only the most salient candidate to fill the gap.

\begin{plainterms}
Describe anything purely by its function and you capture its \emph{structure} (how the parts relate) and its \emph{dynamics} (how its states change over time) — but nothing about what the parts \emph{are} in themselves, their inner nature. It's the wiring-diagram point again: the diagram shows the connections, never what the components are actually made of. On its own this stays neutral about whether anything important hides in that left-out inner nature — it just marks that function leaves it out.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-residue]{\textsc{phenomenal residue}} — the narrower companion: function under-specifies \emph{qualia}; this claim generalises to intrinsic nature as such.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-functionalism]{\textsc{Functionalism}} — the role-based view of mind whose descriptive reach this claim bounds.
  \item \hyperref[entry:position-russellian-monism]{\textsc{Russellian monism}} — the position built on this thesis: consciousness as (or grounding) the intrinsic nature physics leaves blank.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{argument-structure-and-dynamics}{Structure-and-dynamics argument}{Argument \,\textperiodcentered\, after Chalmers}{Structure-and-dynamics argument}{The structure-and-dynamics argument — function omits the categorical/intrinsic}{$\vdash$}
The \emph{structure-and-dynamics argument} holds that no purely functional description can capture consciousness, because such a description expresses only the structural and dynamical features of a system — what is connected to what, and what leads to what — never the intrinsic, \emph{categorical} nature of whatever fills those roles. Felt experience, the argument contends, has just such an intrinsic nature, so a description confined to structure and dynamics is bound to leave it out. The argument is due to David Chalmers, developing a point from Bertrand Russell (\hyperref[source:source-russell-1927]{\textsc{Russell — The Analysis of Matter (1927)}}; \hyperref[source:source-chalmers-2003]{\textsc{Chalmers — Consciousness and Its Place in Nature (2003)}}).

Its inference form is \emph{deductive} — an \emph{omission} argument: it shows that one kind of description omits something another kind requires, rather than weighing evidence for and against a hypothesis.

\begin{enumerate}
  \item (see \hyperref[entry:claim-functional-description-structural-dynamical-only]{\textsc{Structure and dynamics only}}) Functional and causal description captures only structure and dynamics, not intrinsic categorical nature.
  \item (see \hyperref[entry:claim-consciousness-categorical-not-dispositional]{\textsc{Categorical, not dispositional}}) Phenomenal consciousness is categorical or occurrent, not a merely dispositional profile.
\end{enumerate}

∴ Functional description omits what is essential to consciousness; function cannot by itself constitute the phenomenal.

This tells against \hyperref[entry:claim-substrate-independence]{\textsc{Substrate-independence thesis}} and supports the \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-residue]{\textsc{phenomenal residue}} by a route that is independent of conceivability. It also underwrites the \hyperref[entry:argument-decomposition-indeterminacy]{\textsc{decomposition-indeterminacy argument}}: the categorical, intrinsic character of experience is the deeper basis of that argument's premise that consciousness is determinate (see \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-determinacy]{\textsc{phenomenal determinacy}}) rather than relative to a structural carving.

\webheading{The positive moral: Russellian monism}

If experience is the intrinsic nature that structural-dynamical physics leaves blank, then consciousness is neither reducible to function nor a spooky addition: it is the categorical ground of the very dispositions that function describes. This is the Russellian-monist reading of the argument (\hyperref[entry:position-russellian-monism]{\textsc{Russellian monism}}; \hyperref[source:source-russell-1927]{\textsc{Russell — The Analysis of Matter (1927)}}; \hyperref[source:source-chalmers-2003]{\textsc{Chalmers — Consciousness and Its Place in Nature (2003)}}).

\webheading{The weakest premises}

Both premises, plus the bridge between them. Premise (1) is denied by \emph{ontic structural realists}, who hold there is no intrinsic nature beyond structure. Premise (2) is denied by \emph{dispositionalists} about mind. And the inference from ``omits intrinsic nature'' to ``omits \emph{consciousness}'' assumes that consciousness is, or is grounded in, intrinsic nature — the Russellian gamble, not a neutral datum.

This is distinct from the \hyperref[entry:argument-explanatory-gap]{\textsc{explanatory-gap argument}} (an \emph{epistemic} gap between functional and phenomenal truths) and from the \hyperref[entry:argument-decomposition-indeterminacy]{\textsc{decomposition-indeterminacy argument}} (an \emph{indeterminacy} worry): it is an \emph{omission} argument about the expressive limits of structural description — the metaphysical engine the other two share.

\begin{plainterms}
Here's one reason to doubt that ``what a mind does'' could ever capture consciousness. Any purely functional description tells you about \emph{structure and behavior} — what's connected to what, what leads to what. It's all relationships and patterns. What it never tells you is what the things in those relationships \emph{are} in themselves. Think of a wiring diagram: it shows how the parts connect, but says nothing about what the parts are actually made of. The argument runs: a felt experience isn't just a pattern of connections — it has an actual felt quality, something there in its own right. So a description that only ever captures structure-and-behavior is bound to leave that quality out. (An optional, bolder add-on some defenders make: maybe consciousness just \emph{is} that hidden inner nature the wiring diagram never reaches — not magic, not reducible to function, but the very stuff the diagram is a diagram \emph{of}. That last step is a bet, not a proven fact.)
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-functional-description-structural-dynamical-only]{\textsc{Structure and dynamics only}} — premise (1): function fixes only structure and dynamics.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-consciousness-categorical-not-dispositional]{\textsc{Categorical, not dispositional}} — premise (2): experience is categorical, not a mere disposition.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-substrate-independence]{\textsc{Substrate-independence thesis}} — the thesis the argument tells against.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-residue]{\textsc{Phenomenal residue}} — the conclusion it supports, by a route that bypasses conceivability.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-decomposition-indeterminacy]{\textsc{Decomposition-indeterminacy objection}} — a companion argument it underwrites via \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-determinacy]{\textsc{Phenomenal determinacy}}.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-explanatory-gap]{\textsc{Explanatory-gap argument}} — the epistemic-gap cousin; this is its metaphysical engine.
  \item \hyperref[entry:position-russellian-monism]{\textsc{Russellian monism}} — the positive view the argument's moral points toward.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-functionalism]{\textsc{Functionalism}} — the theory whose expressive limits are at issue.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{claim-subjects-actually-merge-and-divide}{Subjects merge and divide}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{Subjects merge and divide}{There are actual cases in which subject boundaries shift with the degree of integration}{$\odot$}
The boundary around a conscious subject is not fixed once and for all but appears, in real cases, to shift with how tightly the underlying parts are integrated. Three kinds of case point the same way. In \emph{split-brain} patients, severing the corpus callosum can leave the two hemispheres behaving as partly independent centres of experience that a healthy, fully connected brain does not display (see \hyperref[source:source-nagel-1971]{\textsc{Nagel — Brain Bisection and the Unity of Consciousness (1971)}}). In \emph{craniopagus} twins whose brains are joined — the Hogan twins, linked by a ``thalamic bridge'' — there are reports of sensory experience shared across the two bodies (see \hyperref[source:source-dominus-2011]{\textsc{Dominus — Could Conjoined Twins Share a Mind? (2011)}}). And at the \emph{group} level, practitioners of certain meditative and tantric techniques, people on some psychedelics, and members of a jazz band or a well-synced team report a felt collapse of the boundary between several minds into a single acting ``we.''

These cases are offered as a defeater for the claim that subject-summing never happens: they suggest that whether a system houses one subject or several can vary with its degree of internal integration. The cases are contested in interpretation — a sceptic will read the group ``we'' as a vivid \emph{representation} of unity rather than a literal further subject, and dispute how many subjects a split brain ``really'' contains — which is why this claim states only that such cases \emph{occur and shift with integration}, leaving the inference to genuine combination to the argument it feeds, \textsc{Combining subjects}. Luke Roelofs surveys this same range of cases at length (see \hyperref[source:source-roelofs-2019]{\textsc{Roelofs — Combining Minds: How to Think about Composite Subjectivity (2019)}}).

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \textsc{Combining subjects} — the rebuttal to the combination problem that this claim helps support.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-subject-unity-graded-by-integration]{\textsc{Graded subject unity}} — the companion premise that reads these cases as a continuous, integration-dependent gradient of subjecthood.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-micro-subjects-do-not-sum]{\textsc{Micro-subjects do not sum}} — the claim these cases are marshalled against.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{question-substrate-independence}{Substrate independence}{Question \,\textperiodcentered\, contested \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{Substrate independence}{Are mental states substrate-independent?}{?}
\emph{Are mental states substrate-independent?} That is: is whether a system can be in a given mental state fixed entirely by which functional or causal role it realises, with no further constraint from the physical material — carbon, silicon, and so on — that realises the role? This is the governing issue behind every dispute over whether an artificial system could have a mind.

The ``substrate-independent'' answer says the material drops out: realise the role, in any medium, and you have the state. The denial says the material itself can constrain which minds are possible, so that no pattern of cause and effect in the wrong stuff would suffice.

\webheading{Terms to pin down}

Two terms carry the weight. First, \emph{mental state}: does the question range over all mental states uniformly, or do phenomenally-conscious states (what an experience feels like) behave differently from merely functional or intentional ones such as belief? Second, \emph{role}: how finely is the realised role individuated — a coarse input-output profile, or a fine-grained internal causal organisation? This is the question of \hyperref[entry:concept-grain]{\textsc{Grain (of role individuation)}}, and the answer shifts what ``realising the role'' demands. Behind both lies \hyperref[entry:concept-functionalism]{\textsc{Functionalism}}, which individuates the mental by causal role in the first place.

\webheading{How it sits in the debate}

The issue is narrower than \hyperref[entry:question-llm-moral-status]{\textsc{the moral-status question}} and logically upstream of it. If mental states are \emph{not} substrate-independent, then no behavioural parity argument can carry a machine to mental states at all. If they \emph{are}, substrate-independence is still only an enabling premise, not yet a verdict on any actual system. Sub-issues that may deserve their own question nodes: whether phenomenal consciousness is separable from functional organisation, and whether multiple-realizability entails substrate-independence.

\begin{plainterms}
Could a mind be made of something other than brain-stuff? That's the question. ``Substrate'' just means what a thing is built from. The issue isn't whether a machine \emph{acts} mind-like — it's whether being a mind depends on the material at all, or only on the pattern of cause-and-effect that the material happens to carry out. If minds do depend on the material, then no amount of acting human ever gets a computer there. If they don't, the door is open — though actually showing that some particular machine has a mind would still be a further step.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-functionalism]{\textsc{Functionalism}} — the account of mind that makes substrate-independence a live option: states fixed by causal role.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-grain]{\textsc{Grain (of role individuation)}} — how finely the role is individuated; the second of the two terms the question turns on.
  \item \hyperref[entry:question-llm-moral-status]{\textsc{Moral status of LLMs}} — the broader, downstream issue this question is narrower than and upstream of.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{claim-substrate-independence}{Substrate-independence thesis}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, contested \,\textperiodcentered\, after Putnam}{Substrate-independence thesis}{Mental states are substrate-independent}{$\odot$}
The \emph{substrate-independence thesis} holds that what makes a mental state the state it is depends on the functional role it plays — what causes it and what it in turn causes — not on the physical stuff that carries out that role. If that is right, then any system implementing the relevant role thereby has the state, whether it is built from carbon neurons or silicon circuits.

The thesis follows from \hyperref[entry:concept-functionalism]{\textsc{Functionalism}}, the view that mental states are individuated by causal role rather than by physical constitution. It restates the point made by the multiple-realizability argument that launched functionalism (see \hyperref[source:source-putnam-1967]{\textsc{Putnam — Psychological Predicates (1967)}}): if pain is whatever occupies the pain-role, then a mollusc, a mammal, or a machine can each be in pain, provided each realises that role.

\webheading{Scope}

This is a claim about mental states \emph{in general}, and a deliberately modest one. It does not by itself assert that any actual machine realises the relevant roles; whether some real system does is the separate work of the \hyperref[entry:argument-behavioral-parity]{\textsc{Behavioural-parity argument}}. Nor is it the stronger claim that silicon systems are conscious. Substrate-independence is the weaker enabling premise, not that conclusion: it unlocks the door without claiming anyone has walked through.

Who would deny it: \emph{substrate chauvinists}, who hold that the physical material itself constrains which minds are possible — see \hyperref[entry:position-proteocentrism]{\textsc{Proteocentrism}}, on which only protein-based biology can support a mind.

\begin{plainterms}
Could a mind run on something other than a brain? This claim says: yes, at least in principle. The reasoning — if what makes a mental state the state it is, is the \emph{role} it plays (what causes it, what it goes on to cause), then anything that plays that role has that state, no matter what it's built from: carbon, silicon, whatever. Notice how modest this is. It does \emph{not} say today's computers or chatbots actually have minds. It only says that being made of brain-stuff isn't, all by itself, a requirement. It unlocks the door; it doesn't claim anyone has walked through.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-functionalism]{\textsc{Functionalism}} — the theory of mind the thesis rests on: states individuated by causal role, not material.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-behavioral-parity]{\textsc{Behavioural-parity argument}} — the move that tries to carry an actual system from behaviour to mental states, using this thesis as a premise.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-llm-has-mental-states]{\textsc{LLMs have mental states}} — the downstream existential claim about real systems, for which this is the general enabling premise.
  \item \hyperref[entry:position-proteocentrism]{\textsc{Proteocentrism}} — the substrate-chauvinist view that denies the thesis: mind requires protein-based biology.
  \item \hyperref[entry:question-substrate-independence]{\textsc{Substrate independence}} — the governing question to which this claim is the affirmative answer.
  \item \hyperref[entry:question-llm-moral-status]{\textsc{Moral status of LLMs}} — the further issue this claim helps bear on once paired with a parity argument.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{concept-super-spartan}{Super-spartans}{Concept \,\textperiodcentered\, after Putnam}{Super-spartans}{Super-spartans (pain felt but never shown)}{$\blacklozenge$}
A \emph{super-spartan} is a member of an imagined community, introduced by Hilary Putnam in ``Brains and Behavior'' (1963, \hyperref[source:source-putnam-1963]{\textsc{Putnam — Brains and Behavior (1963)}}), who feels pain as keenly as anyone but has, out of a fierce cultural pride, trained away every outward sign of it. Super-spartans do not wince, groan, nurse the injured part, or even say that anything hurts; pressed, they will calmly report a pain while displaying none of the behaviour we ordinarily take pain to consist in. Putnam pushes the case further to the \emph{super-super-spartans} of an ``X-world'', who have suppressed pain-behaviour for so many generations that the very talk of pain has dropped out of their language — yet, he insists, they still have pains and know perfectly well that they do.

\webheading{The point of the case}

The super-spartan is a counterexample to \textbf{logical (analytical) behaviourism}: the thesis that to be in pain just \emph{is} to behave, or be disposed to behave, in characteristic ways — to wince, withdraw, complain. If a super-spartan is so much as possible, then one can be in pain while having no disposition whatever to pain-behaviour. So pain-behaviour is not a \emph{necessary} condition of pain, and the behaviourist's proposed analysis of the mental state fails. The mental state and the behaviour come apart.

It is important that the suppression be total and dispositional, not merely a stiff upper lip in the moment. A behaviourist can absorb someone who hides a pain \emph{now} by appealing to what they \emph{would} do in other circumstances. Putnam's super-super-spartans are built precisely to close that escape: there is no circumstance in which their pain issues in the canonical behaviour, and still — by stipulation — the pain is there.

\webheading{Relation to its converse}

The super-spartan is one half of a matched pair. Its mirror image, the \hyperref[entry:concept-perfect-actor]{\textsc{Perfect actor}}, attacks the same theory from the other side: a perfect actor produces all the behaviour of pain while feeling none, showing that the behaviour is not \emph{sufficient} either. Putnam himself drew from the pair not the dualist conclusion but the lesson that mental states should be individuated by their \emph{causal role}, the move that seeded \hyperref[entry:concept-functionalism]{\textsc{Functionalism}}. Logical behaviourism is one member of the broad family of topic-neutral analyses catalogued under \hyperref[entry:position-type-a-materialism]{\textsc{Type-A materialism}}.

\begin{plainterms}
Picture a culture so proud that nobody ever shows pain: they feel the burn exactly as you do, but they never flinch, never cry out, and eventually don't even have a word for it. Common sense says they are still in pain — they just don't show it. That sinks the theory that \emph{being in pain simply means behaving as though you are in pain}, because here is pain with none of the behaviour. It is the exact opposite of the \hyperref[entry:concept-perfect-actor]{\textsc{Perfect actor}}, who shows all the behaviour while feeling nothing, and different again from a \hyperref[entry:concept-philosophical-zombie]{\textsc{Philosophical zombie}}, which is missing the inner feeling rather than just the outward show.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-perfect-actor]{\textsc{Perfect actor}} — the converse case: full pain-behaviour with no pain, attacking the \emph{sufficiency} of behaviour as this attacks its \emph{necessity}.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-functionalism]{\textsc{Functionalism}} — the successor theory Putnam moved to, which replaces behavioural dispositions with causal-functional role and so is not refuted by the case.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-philosophical-zombie]{\textsc{Philosophical zombie}} — a sharper cousin: the zombie lacks the experience itself, where the super-spartan keeps the experience and drops only the behaviour.
  \item \hyperref[entry:position-type-a-materialism]{\textsc{Type-A materialism}} — the family of topic-neutral analyses of the mental that houses logical behaviourism, the target here.
  \item \hyperref[source:source-putnam-1963]{\textsc{Putnam — Brains and Behavior (1963)}} — Putnam's ``Brains and Behavior'' (1963), where the super-spartans are introduced.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{claim-access-gap-symmetric}{Symmetric access gap}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{Symmetric access gap}{The first-person/third-person access gap is symmetric across any two subjects, never singling out machines}{$\odot$}
The barrier between first-person experience and third-person observation is \emph{reciprocal}: it stands equally between any two subjects, so it cannot single machines out as a special case. First-person acquaintance — the direct, lived access a subject has to its own inner states (see \hyperref[entry:concept-first-person-access]{\textsc{First-person access}}) — is non-transferable in \emph{both} directions at once. A machine would have access to its own substrate and none to mine, by exactly the measure I have access to mine and none to its. The divide is between first and third person as such, holding between any two subjects whatever, not between human and machine.

This is a structural claim about the \emph{reciprocity} of access, not about what anything in fact feels. It does not assert that the machine has experience; it asserts a conditional symmetry: to whatever extent a subject has a first-person standpoint, every other subject is excluded from it equally. Its dialectical job is narrow but pointed — to deny that any access asymmetry capable of singling machines out exists.

The natural objection targets the claim's second arm. If a machine has no first-person standpoint at all, then nothing on its side is reciprocally inaccessible, and the symmetry collapses: one side is empty rather than merely hidden. This is distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-no-acquaintance-with-machine]{\textsc{No acquaintance with machines}}, which records only the human-side deficit; the present claim adds the reciprocal, fully general structure that neutralises the \emph{asymmetry}. The point that consciousness is mutually unprovable between minds — that the wall runs symmetrically, neither party able to demonstrate an inner life to the other — is due to Stanisław Lem (see \hyperref[source:source-lem]{\textsc{Stanisław Lem — A History of Bitic Literature (1973)}}).

\begin{plainterms}
You can never directly feel anyone else's experience — and that cuts both ways. If a machine has any inner life at all, it's just as shut out of yours as you are of its. The wall isn't ``humans on one side, machines on the other''; it's ``me versus everyone else,'' and it stands equally between any two minds. So that wall can't be used to single machines out as the doubtful case.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-first-person-access]{\textsc{First-person access}} — the privileged but non-shareable standpoint whose reciprocity this claim asserts.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-no-acquaintance-with-machine]{\textsc{No acquaintance with machines}} — the one-directional, human-side deficit this claim generalises into a symmetry.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-access-symmetry-rebuttal]{\textsc{Access-symmetry rebuttal}} — the argument that wields this symmetry to undercut the first-person-access argument for a moral-status asymmetry.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{claim-syntax-insufficient-for-semantics}{Syntax insufficient for semantics}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, after Searle}{Syntax insufficient for semantics}{Executing a formal program is not sufficient for understanding}{$\odot$}
Searle's thesis, in his own slogan, is that \emph{syntax is not sufficient for semantics} (see \hyperref[source:source-searle-1980]{\textsc{Searle — Minds, Brains, and Programs (1980)}}). Running the right program — formal operations defined over symbol shapes — does not by itself constitute or suffice for understanding, meaning, or intentionality. Whatever else a mind is, it is not merely the implementing of a formal program.

This is a \emph{sufficiency} claim about computation and understanding. It does not say machines cannot understand (Searle holds that brains are machines that do), nor that computation is irrelevant — only that program-execution alone is not enough for semantics. Its target is \emph{strong AI} and computational \hyperref[entry:concept-functionalism]{\textsc{Functionalism}}, not materialism.

It is denied by computational functionalists and strong-AI proponents, and by anyone who accepts the \emph{systems} or \emph{robot} reply, on which a system running the program, suitably embedded, does understand. The deeper crux is whether ``the right program'' can be specified richly enough — with causal embedding and grounding — to close the gap between syntax and semantics.

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-behaviour-insufficient-for-mind]{\textsc{Behaviour insufficient for mind}} because that denies the sufficiency of \emph{behaviour} (a lookup table matches behaviour without mind), whereas this denies the sufficiency of \emph{computation / syntax} for \emph{understanding} — a stronger target, since the room runs a program rather than a flat table. Distinct too from \hyperref[entry:claim-substrate-independence]{\textsc{substrate independence}}, which it attacks: substrate independence says realising the functional role suffices regardless of substrate; this says realising the formal program does not suffice for the semantic part of mind.

\begin{plainterms}
The headline Searle wants you to take away: shuffling symbols by their shapes — which is all a running program does — is never enough, on its own, to \emph{understand} what the symbols mean. You can get all the right outputs without anyone in there grasping anything. So if having a mind includes really understanding things, then ``it runs the right software'' can't be the whole story. (Note what he's \emph{not} saying: not that machines can't think — he thinks brains are machines — just that being a \emph{program} is not what does it.)
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[source:source-searle-1980]{\textsc{Searle — Minds, Brains, and Programs (1980)}} — Searle's ``Minds, Brains, and Programs,'' where the slogan is set out.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-functionalism]{\textsc{Functionalism}} — the computational target the thesis is aimed at.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-behaviour-insufficient-for-mind]{\textsc{Behaviour insufficient for mind}} — Block's behaviour-sufficiency denial, distinguished from this computation-sufficiency denial.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-substrate-independence]{\textsc{Substrate-independence thesis}} — the thesis this claim attacks on its sufficiency direction.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{argument-systems-reply}{Systems reply}{Argument}{Systems reply}{The Systems Reply — the whole system understands, though the person does not}{$\vdash$}
The \emph{Systems Reply} is the most common objection to the \hyperref[entry:concept-chinese-room]{\textsc{Chinese Room}}. It grants that the person in the room understands no Chinese but denies that this settles anything: understanding is not to be located in the person, who is merely the \emph{central processor}, but in the \textbf{whole system} — the person together with the rulebook, the symbol store, the paper, and the input/output channels. No single neuron in a Chinese speaker's brain understands Chinese either, yet the brain does; likewise no part of the room need understand for the system to.

Against the \hyperref[entry:argument-chinese-room-derivation]{\textsc{derivation}}, the reply is in effect a denial of A3 (see \hyperref[entry:claim-syntax-insufficient-for-semantics]{\textsc{Syntax insufficient for semantics}}): the relevant bearer of semantics is the organised system, and Searle's argument illicitly looks for understanding in a \emph{component} (the person) rather than in the system that runs the program. Against the \hyperref[entry:argument-chinese-room]{\textsc{intuition-pump version}} it raises the \emph{thought-experiment} pattern's \textbf{scope} critical question: the verdict ``he understands nothing'' is true of the person but does not generalise to the system the experiment was meant to be about.

\webheading{The crux}

The reply's force depends on whether a system can have genuine understanding that none of its parts has — \emph{emergent} system-level intentionality. Searle answers with the \hyperref[entry:argument-rejoinder-internalize-rulebook]{\textsc{internalisation rejoinder}}: let the person memorise the rulebook and do all the work in their head, so that the person \emph{is} the entire system, with nothing left over to be the understander. Whether that rejoinder succeeds — whether internalising the system really leaves no room for understanding, or merely hides the system inside the person — is the live question. The Systems Reply is also the parent of the \hyperref[entry:argument-robot-reply]{\textsc{Robot}} and \hyperref[entry:argument-brain-simulator-reply]{\textsc{Brain Simulator}} replies, which specify \emph{what more} the system would need.

\begin{plainterms}
The classic comeback to the Chinese Room: sure, the \emph{man} in the room doesn't understand Chinese — but the man isn't the whole story. The man plus the giant rulebook plus the files plus the whole setup, working together, might understand, even though no single piece does. After all, no one brain cell of yours understands English, yet \emph{you} do. So Searle picked the wrong thing to look at: don't ask whether the clerk understands, ask whether the \emph{system} does. Searle's famous reply: fine, have the man memorise the entire rulebook and throw the room away — now he \emph{is} the whole system, and he still doesn't understand a word.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-chinese-room-derivation]{\textsc{Chinese room derivation}} — the argument this objection attacks, by denying A3.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-rejoinder-internalize-rulebook]{\textsc{Internalise the rulebook}} — Searle's reply: internalise the system and nothing is left to understand.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-robot-reply]{\textsc{Robot reply}}, \hyperref[entry:argument-brain-simulator-reply]{\textsc{Brain simulator reply}} — descendants that say what the system would need to add.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-chinese-nation]{\textsc{Chinese nation}} — the neighbouring system-level case, pressed against qualia rather than understanding.
\end{seealso}

\end{multicols}
\bookgroup{T}
\begin{multicols}{2}
\entryhead{claim-blockhead-deflation-targeted-not-global}{Targeted blockhead deflation}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{Targeted blockhead deflation}{Deflating the cosmic-table intuition does not deflate intuitions about brain-scale duplicates}{$\odot$}
Deflating our distrust of one bizarre case need not deflate our intuitions about a quite different case. The \emph{targeted} version of the blockhead deflation withdraws warrant from the ``no mind'' verdict about the cosmic \hyperref[entry:concept-lookup-table-mind]{\textsc{lookup table}} — because that object is a far extrapolation from any paradigm mind (see \hyperref[entry:claim-lookup-table-physically-unrealisable]{\textsc{it is not even physically realisable}}), alien in both scale and internal structure — while leaving untouched the intuition about a brain-scale, brain-structured silicon isomorph, a near neighbour of paradigm minds.

This is the \hyperref[entry:claim-imaginary-blockhead-no-secure-verdict]{\textsc{conceptual-horn deflation}} applied with a boundary. Its consequence is that the second horn of \hyperref[entry:argument-blockhead-dilemma]{\textsc{the blockhead dilemma}} does not ``prove too much'' against \hyperref[entry:claim-substrate-independence]{\textsc{substrate independence}}: doubting the wild, far-out case does not silently undercut the close, familiar one.

\webheading{Scope}

The claim answers the over-generalisation worry about \hyperref[entry:argument-blockhead-dilemma]{\textsc{the blockhead dilemma}}. It does not assert that the isomorph intuition is \emph{correct}, only that it is not impugned by the very move that impugns the blockhead intuition. The asymmetry is epistemic — distance from exemplars — not a smuggled metaphysics of which features constitute mind, so it does not beg the question against Block.

The view it must answer to is Block's (see \hyperref[source:source-block]{\textsc{Block — Psychologism and Behaviorism (1981)}}): he would relocate his verdict from \emph{scale} to \emph{kind}, denying mind to brute storage of any size, so that a scale-based asymmetry (he says) misses the point. That rejoinder is taken up at the weakest premise of \hyperref[entry:argument-intuition-domain-asymmetry]{\textsc{the domain-asymmetry argument}}.

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-imaginary-blockhead-no-secure-verdict]{\textsc{the deflation itself}}: that move performs the deflation; this one bounds its reach.

\begin{plainterms}
If we distrust our gut reaction to a bizarre, universe-sized lookup-table ``mind'' — because it's nothing like any real mind we've ever met — that distrust doesn't automatically spread to a normal-sized silicon copy of a brain, which \emph{is} very much like a real mind. Doubting the wild, far-out case leaves the close, familiar case standing. So that doubt doesn't accidentally undercut the idea that a brain-like machine could be conscious.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-imaginary-blockhead-no-secure-verdict]{\textsc{Imaginary blockhead: no verdict}} — the conceptual-horn deflation whose reach this claim bounds.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-blockhead-dilemma]{\textsc{Blockhead dilemma}} — the dilemma whose over-generalisation worry this answers.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-substrate-independence]{\textsc{Substrate-independence thesis}} — the thesis the deflation is shown not to undercut.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-intuition-domain-asymmetry]{\textsc{Intuition-domain asymmetry}} — where Block's scale-to-kind relocation is taken up at the weakest premise.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-lookup-table-physically-unrealisable]{\textsc{Physically unrealisable table}} — why the cosmic table is a far extrapolation from any paradigm mind.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-lookup-table-mind]{\textsc{Lookup-table mind}} — the cosmic-table construction the targeted deflation is about.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{claim-llm-text-yields-human-aligned-representations}{Text yields human-aligned representations}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{Text yields human-aligned representations}{Language models trained on text alone develop internal representations structurally aligned with human ones}{$\odot$}
Large language models trained purely on text develop \emph{internal representations} whose relational structure matches that of human representations of the same domain — not merely matching output labels, but recovering the same similarity geometry and, in some settings, an explicit model of the world the symbols describe. Four independent lines of evidence converge.

\begin{itemize}
  \item \textbf{Perceptual structure from text.} GPT models' pairwise similarity judgments track human data across \emph{six} perceptual modalities, reconstructing canonical structures like the colour wheel and pitch spiral from text, with no gain from added visual training (see \hyperref[source:source-marjieh-2024]{\textsc{Marjieh et al. — LLMs Predict Human Sensory Judgments Across Six Modalities (2024)}}); and the \emph{unsupervised} alignment of GPT-4's colour-similarity space with that of colour-neurotypical humans — optimal transport assuming no label correspondence — shows the match lies in the internal structure, not the names (see \hyperref[source:source-kawakita-2024]{\textsc{Kawakita et al. — Structural Correspondences Between Colour Similarity Structures of Humans and LLMs (2024)}}).
  \item \textbf{Induced world models.} Sequence-only models build an explicit model of the domain they predict: a GPT trained on Othello moves maintains a causally-effective representation of the board (see \hyperref[source:source-li-2022]{\textsc{Li et al. — Emergent World Representations (Othello-GPT) (2022)}}), robust across architectures (see \hyperref[source:source-yuan-2025]{\textsc{Yuan \& Søgaard — Revisiting the Othello World Model Hypothesis (2025)}}).
  \item \textbf{Real-world structure.} Beyond games, LLMs carry \emph{linear} representations of space and time at multiple scales, with individual neurons encoding coordinates — the ``basic ingredients of a world model'' (see \hyperref[source:source-gurnee-2023]{\textsc{Gurnee \& Tegmark — Language Models Represent Space and Time (2023)}}).
  \item \textbf{Brain alignment.} Most directly for the ``human-aligned'' wording, next-token training yields conceptual representations that \emph{align with human brain activity}, emerging without real-world grounding (see \hyperref[source:source-xu-2025]{\textsc{Xu et al. — Emergent Human-like Conceptual Representations from Language Prediction (2025)}}), and LLM embeddings of scene captions predict visual-cortex activity well enough to reconstruct the caption from brain data (see \hyperref[source:source-doerig-2025]{\textsc{Doerig et al. — High-level Visual Representations in the Human Brain Are Aligned with LLMs (2025)}}).
\end{itemize}

\textbf{Scope.} The claim is about \emph{structural/representational} alignment — the geometry of the model's internal states matching the human one — established as a \emph{lower bound} on what is latent in language. It does \textbf{not} by itself assert that the models have phenomenal experience, consciousness, or \hyperref[entry:concept-intrinsic-derived-intentionality]{\textsc{intrinsic}} (rather than derived) intentionality; those are further questions the alignment does not settle. Its force is to show that \emph{syntax} (text statistics) is, as a matter of fact, enough to \emph{induce} richly human-like semantic \emph{structure}.

\textbf{Who would deny it, and how.} A Searlean grants the structural finding but denies it touches the real issue: aligned representational structure is still structure — a \emph{depiction} of human semantics, not the intrinsic understanding the \hyperref[entry:concept-chinese-room]{\textsc{Chinese Room}} is about (see \hyperref[entry:claim-simulation-instances-formal-depicts-causal]{\textsc{Instancing vs. depicting}}). Sceptics about the evidence add deflationary readings: training-data contamination, anthropomorphic probing, or that ``similarity geometry'' is a thin proxy for representation. The claim is framed to survive these as a structural result while conceding it does not, alone, reach the phenomenal.

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-llm-approximates-perfect-actor]{\textsc{LLMs as near-perfect actors}} (about \emph{behavioural} indistinguishability) and from \hyperref[entry:claim-llm-access-similarity-not-encoding]{\textsc{Similarity, not encoding}}: this claim is specifically about the \emph{internal representational structure} matching the human one, the empirical pressure point on Searle's third axiom.

\begin{plainterms}
Feed a model nothing but text and, surprisingly, it builds an inner ``map'' of things that lines up with the map in our heads. Ask GPT-4 which colours are similar and its answer reconstructs the human colour wheel; the same holds for sound, taste, and three other senses — and it doesn't even help to also train it on images, because the structure was already hiding in the words. Train a model only on Othello moves and it quietly builds a picture of the board. None of this proves the model \emph{feels} anything or \emph{really} understands — but it does show that pure word-shuffling can grow a genuinely human-shaped \emph{web of meaning}, which is exactly the thing Searle's argument says shuffling alone can never do.
\end{plainterms}

\entryhead{claim-some-beliefs-are-basic}{There are basic beliefs}{Claim}{There are basic beliefs}{Some beliefs are justified non-inferentially rather than by derivation from other beliefs}{$\odot$}
Not every justified belief owes its justification to other beliefs. Some — \hyperref[entry:concept-basic-belief]{\textsc{basic beliefs}} — are warranted directly: by perception, memory, introspection, or rational insight, without passing through an inference from further premises. This is the foundationalist's central thesis and its answer to the regress: the chain of justification halts at beliefs that are self-standing, terminating \hyperref[entry:concept-munchhausen-trilemma]{\textsc{the Münchhausen trilemma}}'s regress at a principled floor rather than an arbitrary dogma.

\textbf{Scope.} The claim asserts that \emph{some} warrant is non-inferential; it is silent on which beliefs are basic and on whether basic beliefs must be infallible. Moderate foundationalism takes them to be defeasible (a perceptual belief warranted on sight, but revisable); classical foundationalism demanded indubitability.

\textbf{Who would deny it.} \emph{Coherentists} (see \hyperref[entry:position-coherentism]{\textsc{Coherentism}}) deny it outright: there are no non-inferentially justified beliefs, since justification is always a matter of a belief's relations to \emph{other} beliefs. \emph{Infinitists} (see \hyperref[entry:position-infinitism]{\textsc{Infinitism}}) likewise deny a terminating floor. And a \emph{Wittgensteinian} resists from the other side: the regress-stoppers are not \emph{justified} beliefs at all but \hyperref[entry:concept-hinge-proposition]{\textsc{hinges}} held without justification.

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-justification-terminates-in-trilemma]{\textsc{Justification's trilemma}} (the neutral statement of the regress problem, which all parties can accept) — this claim takes one side, the foundationalist's, on how the regress ends.

\begin{plainterms}
Foundationalism's key idea: a few of your beliefs don't need any \emph{other} belief to back them up — seeing red justifies ``that's red'' all on its own. Call those ``basic.'' They're meant to be the ground floor that stops the endless ``but why believe \emph{that}?'' Some philosophers think there's no such ground floor (everything is justified by other things, or by nothing at all) — and this claim is exactly what they're denying.
\end{plainterms}

\entryhead{concept-transcendental-argument}{Transcendental argument}{Concept}{Transcendental argument}{}{$\blacklozenge$}
A \emph{transcendental argument} reasons from some feature of experience or thought that even an opponent must grant, to the \emph{necessary conditions} of that feature's being possible — concluding that those conditions must obtain. Its canonical form: \emph{F is actual (and undeniable); F is possible only if C; therefore C.} The name and the strategy are Kant's, who argued that the very possibility of unified, objective experience presupposes a law-governed world of enduring objects — so the skeptic who enjoys such experience cannot consistently doubt its framework. The form was revived in analytic philosophy by P. F. Strawson's \emph{Individuals} (see \hyperref[source:source-strawson-1959]{\textsc{Strawson — Individuals (1959)}}), as a method of ``descriptive metaphysics.''

The appeal against skepticism is that it does not try to \emph{prove} the contested conclusion from neutral evidence — known to fail for the external world (see \hyperref[entry:claim-external-world-unprovable-noncircular]{\textsc{No non-circular proof of the world}}) — but extracts it from what the skeptic is already doing. To raise the doubt at all, the skeptic must think, mean, remember, and refer; if those activities have presuppositions, the presuppositions come along free.

\webheading{Its limit}

The standing objection is Barry Stroud's (see \hyperref[source:source-stroud-1968]{\textsc{Stroud — Transcendental Arguments (1968)}}): a transcendental argument, by itself, shows at most that we must \emph{believe} or \emph{conceive} things to be a certain way in order to think or speak — not that they \emph{are} that way. Closing the gap from ``must be presupposed'' to ``is true'' needs an extra, verificationist premise (roughly, that what we must conceive to be so \emph{is} so), which the skeptic will deny. So unsupplemented transcendental arguments tend to establish \emph{psychological} or \emph{conceptual} necessities rather than facts about the world — which is why they pair naturally with \emph{disarming} the skeptic rather than refuting the skeptical proposition.

\webheading{Relation to retorsion}

\hyperref[entry:concept-retorsion]{\textsc{Retorsion}} is the limiting, dialectical case of the transcendental strategy: where a general transcendental argument identifies the conditions of some broad feature like objective experience, retorsion targets the \emph{specific act of assertion}, showing that to assert the skeptical thesis is to rely on its falsity. Retorsion thus inherits both the strength (it engages the skeptic on their own activity) and the Stroudian limit (it shows the denial unassertible, not the world real) of transcendental arguments generally.

\begin{plainterms}
A transcendental argument is a clever sidestep: instead of proving ``there's a world'' from scratch, you start from something your opponent can't deny — that they think, speak, experience an ordered world — and ask what has to be true for \emph{that} to be possible. Whatever the answer is, they're stuck with it, because they already granted the starting point. The catch, due to Stroud: this may only show what they have to \emph{assume}, not what's actually \emph{so} — proving how we must think isn't quite proving how things are.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-retorsion-against-skepticism]{\textsc{Retorsion against skepticism}} — the anti-skeptical argument of this form deployed in the web.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-retorsion]{\textsc{Retorsion}} — the assertion-focused special case of the strategy.
  \item \hyperref[source:source-stroud-1968]{\textsc{Stroud — Transcendental Arguments (1968)}} — the objection that bounds what these arguments can show.
  \item \hyperref[source:source-strawson-1959]{\textsc{Strawson — Individuals (1959)}} — the modern revival of the form.
  \item \hyperref[entry:question-external-world-knowledge]{\textsc{Knowing the external world}} — the issue on which transcendental arguments are one anti-skeptical option.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{argument-rejoinder-trivializes-strong-ai}{Trivialises strong AI}{Argument \,\textperiodcentered\, after Searle}{Trivialises strong AI}{Many Mansions trivialises strong AI — the reply concedes the thesis it was meant to save}{$\vdash$}
Searle's rejoinder to the \hyperref[entry:argument-many-mansions-reply]{\textsc{Many Mansions Reply}}. The reply is not really an objection at all: it \emph{concedes} the Chinese Room's target and rebrands the concession as optimism. Strong AI (see \hyperref[entry:concept-strong-weak-ai]{\textsc{Strong and weak AI}}) was the precise, falsifiable thesis that \emph{mental processes are computational processes} — that implementing the right \emph{program} is sufficient for a mind. The Many Mansions Reply gives exactly that up, retreating to the claim that \emph{some} future artificial means, not necessarily computational, might produce a mind. But that weakened claim is one Searle already grants (it is the import of A4, \hyperref[entry:claim-brains-cause-minds]{\textsc{Brains cause minds}}): of course an artificial system with the right causal powers could think. By abandoning the computational thesis, the reply trivialises strong AI rather than defending it.

The move \texttt{supports} the \hyperref[entry:argument-chinese-room-derivation]{\textsc{derivation}} by clarifying its scope: C1 was only ever about \emph{programs}, and a reply that drops programs concedes C1.

\webheading{The live crux}

The reply's defender can shift from logic to rhetoric: Searle's \emph{official} conclusion is narrow (programs don't suffice), but the Chinese Room is routinely \emph{presented} as a sweeping case against machine minds, and the Many Mansions Reply rightly resists \emph{that} over-reading. So the rejoinder wins the strict point while leaving the broader cultural claim — that the Room shows machines can't think — undefended, as it should be.

\begin{plainterms}
The Many Mansions Reply said ``maybe some future tech, not ordinary code, will build a mind.'' Searle's answer: sure — but that was never what I denied. I denied that \emph{running a program} is enough. The moment you say ``well, maybe something \emph{other} than a program,'' you've agreed with me and quietly dropped the exciting claim (``the program \emph{is} the mind'') that the whole debate was about. So it's not a rescue of strong AI; it's a polite surrender.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-many-mansions-reply]{\textsc{Many mansions reply}} — the objection this answers.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-strong-weak-ai]{\textsc{Strong and weak AI}} — the computational thesis the reply abandons.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-brains-cause-minds]{\textsc{Brains cause minds}} — A4, which already concedes non-program artificial minds.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-chinese-room-derivation]{\textsc{Chinese room derivation}} — the argument whose narrow scope this clarifies.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{concept-turing-test}{Turing test}{Concept \,\textperiodcentered\, after Turing}{Turing test}{The Turing test (the imitation game)}{$\blacklozenge$}
The \emph{Turing test} is Alan Turing's proposed replacement for the question ``Can machines think?'' (see \hyperref[source:source-turing-1950]{\textsc{Turing — Computing Machinery and Intelligence (1950)}}). Finding that question ``too meaningless to deserve discussion,'' Turing recast it operationally as the \textbf{imitation game}: a human interrogator holds text-only conversations with two unseen partners — one human, one machine, each trying to be judged the human — and if, after suitable interrogation, the machine is misidentified as often as a human is, it passes. The test deliberately routes around looks and voice to isolate \emph{conversational behaviour} as the criterion, and substitutes a question we can settle by observation for one Turing thought we could not settle at all.

The test embodies a broadly \textbf{behaviourist or operationalist} stance: it treats indistinguishable verbal behaviour as the relevant mark of mind, declining to ask what, if anything, lies behind it. That is exactly its power and its vulnerability. Turing himself offered it less as a \emph{definition} of thinking than as a prediction and a reframing, and he pre-emptively answered nine objections (theological, mathematical, the ``Lady Lovelace'' originality objection, and others).

\webheading{The standing objections}

\begin{itemize}
  \item \textbf{The Chinese Room} (see \hyperref[entry:concept-chinese-room]{\textsc{Chinese room}}): Searle grants a system might \emph{pass} yet insist it understands nothing — passing certifies the right \emph{behaviour} (syntax), not \emph{understanding} (semantics). So a pass, even if it is evidence for \hyperref[entry:concept-strong-weak-ai]{\textsc{weak AI}}, does not establish strong AI.
  \item \textbf{The Blockhead / lookup-table objection} (see \hyperref[entry:concept-lookup-table-mind]{\textsc{Lookup-table mind}}): Ned Block notes that a machine could in principle pass with a gigantic pre-computed table of canned replies and \emph{no} cognitive processing at all — so passing shows nothing about \emph{how} the responses are produced, and behaviour alone cannot be sufficient for intelligence (psychologism versus behaviourism).
  \item \textbf{The \hyperref[entry:concept-ai-effect]{\textsc{AI effect}}}: as systems approach a pass, the response ``that is not \emph{really} thinking'' reliably recurs, so the test's verdict is socially contested whatever the transcripts show.
\end{itemize}

\webheading{What it bears on}

The Turing test is the operational counterpart of the \hyperref[entry:concept-perfect-actor]{\textsc{perfect actor}} — a system whose outward behaviour is indistinguishable from a genuine mind's — and so sits at the centre of the dispute over whether behavioural success suffices for mind. Today's large language models press the question concretely: they approximate that actor (see \hyperref[entry:claim-llm-approximates-perfect-actor]{\textsc{LLMs as near-perfect actors}}) and routinely pass informal versions of the test, which sharpens rather than settles the issue, since the test is silent on the very \hyperref[entry:concept-strong-weak-ai]{\textsc{understanding}} its critics demand.

\begin{plainterms}
Turing's move was to dodge the bottomless question ``can a machine \emph{think}?'' and ask a crisp one instead: could a machine hold a typed conversation so well that a person couldn't tell it from another person? If yes, he said, treat it as thinking — stop fussing about what's ``really'' inside. Critics answer that fooling the judge isn't the same as understanding: Searle's locked room shuffles symbols convincingly with no comprehension, and Ned Block points out you could pass with a giant cheat-sheet of canned answers and no thought at all. With chatbots now passing casual versions of the test, the old argument is suddenly very live.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-perfect-actor]{\textsc{Perfect actor}} — the behavioural ideal the test operationalises.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-chinese-room]{\textsc{Chinese room}} — the objection that passing is behaviour without understanding.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-lookup-table-mind]{\textsc{Lookup-table mind}} — Block's objection that a canned-answer table could pass with no cognition.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-strong-weak-ai]{\textsc{Strong and weak AI}} — a pass is at most evidence for weak AI, not strong AI.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-ai-effect]{\textsc{AI effect}} — why a pass tends to be met with ``that's still not thinking.''
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-llm-approximates-perfect-actor]{\textsc{LLMs as near-perfect actors}} — the contemporary system that brings the test to life.
  \item \hyperref[source:source-turing-1950]{\textsc{Turing — Computing Machinery and Intelligence (1950)}} — the original proposal.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{claim-reality-two-aspects-mental-physical}{Two-aspect reality}{Claim}{Two-aspect reality}{Reality has two irreducible aspects, the mental as well as the physical}{$\odot$}
There is a single underlying reality that presents two irreducible \emph{aspects} — an inner, mental aspect and an outer, physical aspect — neither reducible to the other, both aspects of one thing. The locus classicus is Spinoza's one substance, grasped under the attributes of thought as well as extension (see \hyperref[source:source-spinoza-ethics]{\textsc{Spinoza — Ethics (1677)}}); a twentieth-century version is the Pauli–Jung dual-aspect view (see \hyperref[source:source-pauli-jung-1955]{\textsc{Jung \& Pauli — The Interpretation of Nature and the Psyche (1955)}}). Mind and matter are one reality seen two ways.

The claim is a monism about substance carrying an irreducible duality of aspects. It differs from \emph{property} dualism — which posits two kinds of property in or of physical things — by making both the mental and the physical \emph{aspects of a prior unity}. It is denied by substance dualists (two things, not two aspects), reductive physicalists (the mental aspect reduces), and by idealists or physicalists who privilege one side.

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-reality-fundamental-stuff-neutral]{\textsc{the neutral-base claim}} (neutral monism makes the base \emph{neutral} and mind/matter arrangements of it; here the base intrinsically bears \emph{both} aspects) and from \hyperref[entry:claim-consciousness-categorical-not-dispositional]{\textsc{the Russellian categorical-base claim}} (which grounds the physical in the phenomenal rather than holding the two on a par).

\begin{plainterms}
There's just one underlying reality, but it always shows up with two sides — an inner, felt side and an outer, physical side — the way one coin always has heads and tails. Because they are two faces of a single thing, the felt and the physical are bound to line up.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:position-dual-aspect-monism]{\textsc{Dual-aspect monism}} — the position committed to this claim.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-reality-fundamental-stuff-neutral]{\textsc{Neutral fundamental stuff}} — kin claim; a neutral base of which mind/matter are arrangements, versus a base that bears both aspects.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-consciousness-categorical-not-dispositional]{\textsc{Categorical, not dispositional}} — the Russellian grounding contrast.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{concept-materialism-types}{Type-A / Type-B / Type-C materialism}{Concept \,\textperiodcentered\, after Chalmers}{Type-A / Type-B / Type-C materialism}{Type-A / Type-B / Type-C materialism (responses to the hard problem)}{$\blacklozenge$}
Chalmers' \textbf{A/B/C taxonomy} is a way of sorting \emph{physicalist} responses to the \hyperref[entry:concept-hard-problem]{\textsc{Hard problem}} of consciousness — the problem of why physical processing should be accompanied by felt experience at all. It classifies them not by their metaphysics, on which all physicalists agree (everything is, at bottom, physical), but by a single epistemological question: how does each treat the \textbf{epistemic gap} between the physical truths and the truths about experience — the apparent fact that no amount of physical information lets you \emph{deduce}, a priori, what an experience is like? The scheme was introduced by David Chalmers in ``Consciousness and Its Place in Nature'' (\hyperref[source:source-chalmers-2003]{\textsc{Chalmers — Consciousness and Its Place in Nature (2003)}}; the underlying apparatus is developed in \hyperref[source:source-chalmers-1996]{\textsc{Chalmers — The Conscious Mind (1996)}}), and the three physicalist options it labels are Type-A, Type-B, and Type-C.

\webheading{The three types}

\begin{itemize}
  \item \emph{Type-A materialism} \textbf{denies the epistemic gap.} Once the functions are explained — discrimination, reportability, the control of behaviour — there is, on this view, no further fact left over: a \hyperref[entry:concept-philosophical-zombie]{\textsc{Philosophical zombie}} is not really conceivable on reflection, and Mary (see \hyperref[entry:concept-knowledge-argument]{\textsc{Knowledge argument}}) learns no new fact when she first sees red. Analytic functionalism, behaviourism, eliminativism, and \textbf{illusionism} (see \hyperref[entry:position-illusionism]{\textsc{Illusionism}}, \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenality-is-illusion]{\textsc{Phenomenality as illusion}}) are its species; the standalone entry is \hyperref[entry:position-type-a-materialism]{\textsc{Type-A materialism}}.
  \item \emph{Type-B materialism} \textbf{accepts the epistemic gap but denies an ontological one.} Phenomenal and physical truths are linked by identities that are \emph{necessary but knowable only a posteriori}, on the model of ``water is H₂O'', so a zombie is conceivable yet metaphysically impossible — which is why \hyperref[entry:claim-conceivability-entails-possibility]{\textsc{Conceivability entails possibility}} is denied as a guide (see \hyperref[entry:argument-conceivability-not-guide-to-possibility]{\textsc{A-posteriori-necessity objection}}). The gap is \emph{explained}, not closed, by the phenomenal-concept strategy (see \hyperref[entry:argument-gap-deflated-by-phenomenal-concepts]{\textsc{Phenomenal-concept deflation}}, \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-concept-isolation-tradeoff]{\textsc{Phenomenal-concept trade-off}}). The standalone entry is \hyperref[entry:position-type-b-materialism]{\textsc{Type-B materialism}}.
  \item \emph{Type-C materialism} concedes the gap looks unbridgeable \emph{now} but holds it is closable \textbf{in principle}, with conceptual or scientific progress. Chalmers argues the position is unstable, collapsing on reflection into Type-A (if the gap really closes) or Type-B (if it remains but proves harmless). The standalone entry is \hyperref[entry:position-type-c-materialism]{\textsc{Type-C materialism}}.
\end{itemize}

\webheading{The non-materialist remainder}

For orientation, the same scheme runs on past physicalism. \emph{Type-D} is interactionist dualism and \emph{Type-E} is epiphenomenalism (both \hyperref[entry:position-dualism]{\textsc{Dualism}}); \emph{Type-F} is Russellian monism / panprotopsychism (see \hyperref[entry:position-russellian-monism]{\textsc{Russellian monism}}, \hyperref[entry:position-panpsychism]{\textsc{Panpsychism (position)}}). Type-A/B/C are the \emph{physicalist} options, on which consciousness reduces to or is wholly fixed by the physical; D/E/F keep consciousness fundamental. The dividing line is whether the epistemic gap is taken to mark a real division in nature.

\webheading{The dispute it feeds}

The taxonomy is a map of the live replies to the anti-physicalist thought experiments — the \hyperref[entry:argument-zombie-conceivability]{\textsc{Zombie argument}} and the \hyperref[entry:concept-knowledge-argument]{\textsc{Knowledge argument}}. Almost every current reply is Type-A (deny the intuition the experiment trades on) or Type-B (grant the intuition, deny its modal or ontological force), which is why so much of the debate turns on the two questions those types divide over: is the epistemic gap real, and if so does it entail an ontological gap?

\begin{plainterms}
A cheat-sheet for the ways an ``it's all physical'' theorist can answer the hard problem. \textbf{Type-A:} deny there's any real puzzle — once you've explained what the brain \emph{does}, there's nothing left to explain (the ``you only \emph{think} there's an extra feel'' camp). \textbf{Type-B:} admit it \emph{feels} like there's an unexplained extra, but insist that's just how our concepts work — experience really \emph{is} some physical process, the way water really is H₂O even though it didn't have to feel that way. \textbf{Type-C:} ``we can't bridge it yet, but we will'' — which, Chalmers argues, quietly turns into A or B once you push on it. (Beyond these lie the non-physical options — dualism, and the views on which consciousness stays fundamental, like Russellian monism and panpsychism.)
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:position-type-a-materialism]{\textsc{Type-A materialism}}, \hyperref[entry:position-type-b-materialism]{\textsc{Type-B materialism}}, \hyperref[entry:position-type-c-materialism]{\textsc{Type-C materialism}} — the three physicalist wings as standalone position entries.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-hard-problem]{\textsc{Hard problem}} — the problem the whole taxonomy sorts responses to.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-knowledge-argument]{\textsc{Knowledge argument}} and \hyperref[entry:argument-zombie-conceivability]{\textsc{Zombie argument}} — the anti-physicalist thought experiments the types are replies to.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-conceivability-entails-possibility]{\textsc{Conceivability entails possibility}} and \hyperref[entry:argument-conceivability-not-guide-to-possibility]{\textsc{A-posteriori-necessity objection}} — the bridge principle Type-B rejects and the argument that does the rejecting.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-gap-deflated-by-phenomenal-concepts]{\textsc{Phenomenal-concept deflation}} and \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-concept-isolation-tradeoff]{\textsc{Phenomenal-concept trade-off}} — the phenomenal-concept strategy by which Type-B explains the gap.
  \item \hyperref[entry:position-illusionism]{\textsc{Illusionism}} and \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenality-is-illusion]{\textsc{Phenomenality as illusion}} — the strongest Type-A line, that the felt residue is an introspective illusion.
  \item \hyperref[entry:position-dualism]{\textsc{Dualism}}, \hyperref[entry:position-russellian-monism]{\textsc{Russellian monism}}, \hyperref[entry:position-panpsychism]{\textsc{Panpsychism (position)}} — the non-physicalist Types D/E/F that keep consciousness fundamental.
  \item \hyperref[source:source-chalmers-2003]{\textsc{Chalmers — Consciousness and Its Place in Nature (2003)}}, \hyperref[source:source-chalmers-1996]{\textsc{Chalmers — The Conscious Mind (1996)}} — Chalmers' statement of the taxonomy and the apparatus behind it.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{position-type-a-materialism}{Type-A materialism}{Position \,\textperiodcentered\, after Chalmers}{Type-A materialism}{Type-A materialism (denial of the epistemic gap)}{$\bigstar$}
\textbf{Type-A materialism} is the most deflationary wing of physicalism: it denies that there is any \emph{epistemic gap} between the physical truths and the truths about consciousness. Once you have explained all the functions — the discrimination, the reportability, the control of behaviour, the access of information to reasoning — there is, on this view, simply nothing further left to explain. The felt, ``what-it's-like'' residue that the \hyperref[entry:concept-hard-problem]{\textsc{Hard problem}} points at is not a hard remainder but a non-existent one, or at most an artefact of how we introspect. Accordingly a type-A theorist holds that a \hyperref[entry:concept-philosophical-zombie]{\textsc{Philosophical zombie}} is not really conceivable on reflection (\hyperref[entry:claim-zombie-conceivable]{\textsc{Zombie conceivability}} is denied) and that Mary (see \hyperref[entry:concept-knowledge-argument]{\textsc{Knowledge argument}}), leaving her black-and-white room, learns no new \emph{fact} — at most a new ability (see \hyperref[entry:claim-mary-gains-abilities-not-facts]{\textsc{Mary gains abilities}}). It commits to the core physicalist thesis that the mental just is the physical (see \textsc{Mental events are physical}) and rejects any leftover phenomenal fact beyond the functional story (see \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-residue]{\textsc{Phenomenal residue}}). It is \textbf{type-A} in Chalmers' taxonomy (\hyperref[entry:concept-materialism-types]{\textsc{Type-A / Type-B / Type-C materialism}}; \hyperref[source:source-chalmers-2003]{\textsc{Chalmers — Consciousness and Its Place in Nature (2003)}}).

\textbf{What distinguishes it from neighbouring views.} Type-A is a \emph{family}, not a single doctrine: analytic functionalism, logical behaviourism, \hyperref[entry:position-eliminative-materialism]{\textsc{Eliminative materialism}}, and \hyperref[entry:position-illusionism]{\textsc{Illusionism}} are all type-A, because each in its own way refuses to grant the explanatory gap any genuine purchase. What unites them and sets them apart from the rest of physicalism is the denial that the gap is even real. This is exactly the point on which it splits from \hyperref[entry:position-type-b-materialism]{\textsc{Type-B materialism}}, which \emph{concedes} that the gap is real (the zombie is conceivable, Mary's surprise genuine) and tries instead to show it is merely epistemic; and from \hyperref[entry:position-type-c-materialism]{\textsc{Type-C materialism}}, which concedes the gap looks unbridgeable for now but bets it will close. Against all of them stand the realist anti-physicalisms (see \hyperref[entry:position-dualism]{\textsc{Dualism}}, \hyperref[entry:position-russellian-monism]{\textsc{Russellian monism}}) that take the gap to mark a real division in nature.

\begin{plainterms}
The hard-nosed ``there's nothing to explain'' camp. When people insist that even after brain science is finished there's still a left-over mystery — the \emph{feel} of pain, the \emph{redness} of red — a type-A materialist answers: no, there isn't. Explain everything the brain \emph{does} and the job is done; the sense that something extra has been skipped is itself just a trick of how we look inward. So on this view a perfect zombie (physically just like you but with the lights off inside) isn't really imaginable once you think it through, and the famous colour-scientist Mary learns a new \emph{skill}, not a new \emph{fact}, when she first sees red. It is the most demanding line a physicalist can take, because it asks you to give up the very intuition the other camps try to keep. Contrast the \hyperref[entry:position-type-b-materialism]{\textsc{Type-B materialism}} camp, which admits the puzzle feels real and then explains it away.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-materialism-types]{\textsc{Type-A / Type-B / Type-C materialism}} — the A/B/C taxonomy that defines this position and places it among its siblings.
  \item \textsc{Mental events are physical} — the core commitment it shares with all physicalisms.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-zombie-conceivable]{\textsc{Zombie conceivability}} and \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-residue]{\textsc{Phenomenal residue}} — the two claims it denies, marking its denial of the gap.
  \item \hyperref[entry:position-eliminative-materialism]{\textsc{Eliminative materialism}} and \hyperref[entry:position-illusionism]{\textsc{Illusionism}} — its leading species, each a distinct way of refusing the gap.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-knowledge-argument]{\textsc{Knowledge argument}} and \hyperref[entry:claim-mary-gains-abilities-not-facts]{\textsc{Mary gains abilities}} — Mary's case and the ability-hypothesis reading type-A gives it.
  \item \hyperref[entry:position-type-b-materialism]{\textsc{Type-B materialism}}, \hyperref[entry:position-type-c-materialism]{\textsc{Type-C materialism}} — the other physicalist wings, which grant the gap and then either deflate or defer it.
  \item \hyperref[entry:question-hard-problem]{\textsc{Hard problem (the question)}} — the question it answers by dissolving the explanandum.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{position-type-b-materialism}{Type-B materialism}{Position \,\textperiodcentered\, after Chalmers}{Type-B materialism}{Type-B materialism (epistemic gap without ontological gap)}{$\bigstar$}
\textbf{Type-B materialism} is the moderate, and today the most populous, wing of physicalism. Unlike \hyperref[entry:position-type-a-materialism]{\textsc{Type-A materialism}}, it \emph{grants} the epistemic gap: it concedes that a \hyperref[entry:concept-philosophical-zombie]{\textsc{Philosophical zombie}} is genuinely conceivable, that Mary (see \hyperref[entry:concept-knowledge-argument]{\textsc{Knowledge argument}}) is genuinely surprised when she first sees red, and that no amount of physical information makes the felt character of experience deducible \emph{a priori}. What it denies is that this epistemic gap reflects an \textbf{ontological} one. Physical and phenomenal truths are connected, on this view, by identities that are \emph{necessary but knowable only a posteriori} — on the model of ``water is H₂O'' or ``heat is molecular motion,'' truths that could not have been false yet had to be discovered rather than reasoned out. The mental still \emph{is} the physical (see \textsc{Mental events are physical}); the appearance of a further fact is a feature of our concepts, not of the world. The decisive move is therefore to reject the bridge from conceivability to possibility (see \hyperref[entry:claim-conceivability-entails-possibility]{\textsc{Conceivability entails possibility}}): the zombie is conceivable yet metaphysically impossible, so its conceivability proves nothing about what exists. It is \textbf{type-B} in Chalmers' taxonomy (\hyperref[entry:concept-materialism-types]{\textsc{Type-A / Type-B / Type-C materialism}}; \hyperref[source:source-chalmers-2003]{\textsc{Chalmers — Consciousness and Its Place in Nature (2003)}}).

\textbf{What distinguishes it from neighbouring views.} Type-B occupies the middle ground and is defined by both its borders. Against \hyperref[entry:position-type-a-materialism]{\textsc{Type-A materialism}} it insists the gap is real and must be \emph{explained} rather than denied — typically by the phenomenal-concept strategy, which argues that our special first-person concepts of experience are cognitively isolated in a way that generates the illusion of a further fact. Against the anti-physicalist (see \hyperref[entry:position-dualism]{\textsc{Dualism}}) it insists the gap is \emph{merely} epistemic and carries no modal force. \hyperref[entry:position-type-identity]{\textsc{Type-identity theory}} is the classic species of type-B: it identifies mental types with brain-state types as an a-posteriori discovery, exactly the necessary-but-empirical identity type-B trades on. The standing worry is that the a-posteriori identities type-B needs look unlike ordinary scientific identities — ``water is H₂O'' can itself be explained structurally, whereas ``pain is C-fibre firing'' seems to leave the very thing in question untouched.

\begin{plainterms}
The ``yes it feels like a mystery, but it isn't really one'' camp — and the most common view among physicalist philosophers today. It agrees with the critics on the \emph{feeling}: you really can imagine a zombie, and Mary really is in for a surprise when she first sees colour. But it says that surprise is about how our minds grasp the facts, not about there being two kinds of stuff. Compare ``water is H₂O'': that turned out to be a discovery, something you couldn't have worked out from the armchair, even though water could not have been anything other than H₂O. Type-B says consciousness is like that — experience genuinely \emph{is} some brain process, even though it never \emph{feels} deducible from the physics. So being able to imagine a zombie tells you about the limits of imagination, not about what could really exist. The sharper, gap-denying alternative is \hyperref[entry:position-type-a-materialism]{\textsc{Type-A materialism}}.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-materialism-types]{\textsc{Type-A / Type-B / Type-C materialism}} — the A/B/C taxonomy that defines this position.
  \item \textsc{Mental events are physical} — the core physicalist commitment it keeps.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-conceivability-entails-possibility]{\textsc{Conceivability entails possibility}} — the bridge principle it must reject to absorb the conceivable zombie.
  \item \hyperref[entry:position-type-identity]{\textsc{Type-identity theory}} — its classic species, an a-posteriori identity physicalism.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-philosophical-zombie]{\textsc{Philosophical zombie}} and \hyperref[entry:concept-knowledge-argument]{\textsc{Knowledge argument}} — the two thought experiments whose intuitions type-B grants but defuses.
  \item \hyperref[entry:position-type-a-materialism]{\textsc{Type-A materialism}}, \hyperref[entry:position-type-c-materialism]{\textsc{Type-C materialism}} — the sibling wings that deny the gap or defer it.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{position-type-c-materialism}{Type-C materialism}{Position \,\textperiodcentered\, after Chalmers}{Type-C materialism}{Type-C materialism (the gap is closable in principle)}{$\bigstar$}
\textbf{Type-C materialism} is the optimistic, transitional wing of physicalism. It concedes that the epistemic gap between physical and phenomenal truths (see \hyperref[entry:concept-hard-problem]{\textsc{Hard problem}}) looks unbridgeable \emph{as things now stand} — we cannot presently see how the physical facts could entail the facts about experience — but it holds that the gap is closable \textbf{in principle}, that conceptual or scientific advance will eventually let us see the connection we now cannot. On this view our current sense of mystery reflects the immaturity of our concepts, not a permanent feature of the world; given the right future theory, a \hyperref[entry:concept-philosophical-zombie]{\textsc{Philosophical zombie}} would come to look as plainly impossible as a married bachelor.
