It keeps the core physicalist commitment (see \textsc{Mental events are physical}) and answers the hard problem with a promissory note rather than a present account. It is \textbf{type-C} in Chalmers' taxonomy (\hyperref[entry:concept-materialism-types]{\textsc{Type-A / Type-B / Type-C materialism}}; \hyperref[source:source-chalmers-2003]{\textsc{Chalmers — Consciousness and Its Place in Nature (2003)}}).

\textbf{What distinguishes it from neighbouring views.} Type-C is defined by its instability. Chalmers argues that it is not a stable resting place but a slope: pressed on what the promised future insight would look like, the type-C theorist must say either that the gap genuinely \emph{closes} — in which case the appearance of a gap was illusory all along, and the view collapses into \hyperref[entry:position-type-a-materialism]{\textsc{Type-A materialism}} — or that the gap \emph{remains} but is shown to be harmless, an artefact of our concepts, in which case it has become \hyperref[entry:position-type-b-materialism]{\textsc{Type-B materialism}}. There is, on Chalmers' diagnosis, no third thing for ``closable in principle but not yet'' to mean. This sets type-C apart from its pessimistic mirror image, \hyperref[entry:position-mysterianism]{\textsc{Mysterianism}}, which agrees the gap is currently unbridged but holds it is unbridgeable \emph{by us} forever — keeping the optimism's epistemic framing while denying its hope. Where type-A is deflationary and type-B is conciliatory, type-C is a bet on the future that, Chalmers contends, cannot be held without sliding into one of its neighbours.

\begin{plainterms}
The ``we can't explain it yet, but we will'' camp. A type-C materialist looks at the puzzle of consciousness, admits that right now nobody can show how grey matter adds up to the taste of coffee, but trusts that future science or sharper concepts will eventually make the link obvious — the way once-baffling puzzles about life turned out to be ordinary chemistry. The catch, which Chalmers presses, is that this turns out not to be a real third option. Ask what the future breakthrough would actually show, and you end up either saying ``there was never really a gap'' (which is the \hyperref[entry:position-type-a-materialism]{\textsc{Type-A materialism}} line) or ``the gap is real but harmless'' (which is the \hyperref[entry:position-type-b-materialism]{\textsc{Type-B materialism}} line). So type-C tends to be a waystation rather than a home. Its gloomy twin is \hyperref[entry:position-mysterianism]{\textsc{Mysterianism}}: same starting point, but the conviction that the answer is forever beyond human reach.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-materialism-types]{\textsc{Type-A / Type-B / Type-C materialism}} — the A/B/C taxonomy that defines this position and the instability charge against it.
  \item \textsc{Mental events are physical} — the physicalist commitment it shares with its siblings.
  \item \hyperref[entry:position-type-a-materialism]{\textsc{Type-A materialism}}, \hyperref[entry:position-type-b-materialism]{\textsc{Type-B materialism}} — the two views Chalmers argues type-C collapses into.
  \item \hyperref[entry:position-mysterianism]{\textsc{Mysterianism}} — the pessimistic counterpart that shares the framing but abandons the hope.
  \item \hyperref[entry:question-hard-problem]{\textsc{Hard problem (the question)}} — the question it answers on credit rather than in hand.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{position-type-identity}{Type-identity theory}{Position}{Type-identity theory}{}{$\bigstar$}
\emph{Type-identity theory} is the classical ``mind is the brain'' view: each kind of mental state is literally identical to a kind of brain state, in the way that water turned out to be identical to H₂O. To be in pain just is to be in some specific neural state — the textbook slogan is that ``pain is C-fibre firing.'' On this view the identity is not a definition we stipulate but a discovery science makes: an \emph{a posteriori} identity, established by empirical inquiry rather than by analysing what the words mean. It was the founding physicalism of mid-century philosophy of mind, set out by U. T. Place (see \hyperref[source:source-place-1956]{\textsc{Place — Is Consciousness a Brain Process? (1956)}}), J. J. C. Smart (see \hyperref[source:source-smart-1959]{\textsc{Smart — Sensations and Brain Processes (1959)}}), and Herbert Feigl (see \hyperref[source:source-feigl-1958]{\textsc{Feigl — The ``Mental'' and the ``Physical'' (1958)}}). Its core commitment is the claim that \hyperref[entry:claim-mental-states-are-brain-state-types]{\textsc{mental-state types are brain-state types}}.

\webheading{How it answers the hard problem}

Type-identity dissolves the \hyperref[entry:question-hard-problem]{\textsc{hard problem}} of consciousness by \emph{identity}: if a mental state simply is a brain state, there is no gap between the two left to bridge, and so nothing further to explain once the relevant identities are in hand. It answers the \hyperref[entry:question-substrate-independence]{\textsc{substrate-independence question}} in the negative: because a mental type just \emph{is} a specific physical type, it is not freely realisable in arbitrary materials, so the theory denies \hyperref[entry:claim-substrate-independence]{\textsc{substrate independence}}.

As an a-posteriori-identity physicalism it is the historical seed of what David Chalmers (see \hyperref[source:source-chalmers-2003]{\textsc{Chalmers — Consciousness and Its Place in Nature (2003)}}) calls \emph{type-B} materialism (see \hyperref[entry:concept-materialism-types]{\textsc{Type-A / Type-B / Type-C materialism}}). It can grant that a zombie — a physical duplicate of a person with no inner experience — is \emph{conceivable}, while denying that conceivability is a reliable guide to possibility; so it rejects \hyperref[entry:claim-conceivability-entails-possibility]{\textsc{the inference from conceivability to possibility}}.

\webheading{What sets it apart}

Unlike \hyperref[entry:concept-functionalism]{\textsc{Functionalism}}, which identifies a mental state with a realiser-neutral causal \emph{role}, type-identity ties the mind to a physical \emph{type}. That is exactly the point Hilary Putnam's \emph{multiple-realizability} objection presses (see \hyperref[source:source-putnam-1967]{\textsc{Putnam — Psychological Predicates (1967)}}): if the same mental state can be realised in silicon, in an octopus, or in an alien nervous system, then it cannot be identical to any one physical type. Historically this argument pushed the field from identity theory toward functionalism. Unlike \hyperref[entry:position-dualism]{\textsc{Dualism}} and \hyperref[entry:position-illusionism]{\textsc{Illusionism}}, type-identity is reductive but realist: the mental states genuinely exist, and they are physical.

\begin{plainterms}
The original ``mind is the brain'' theory: each kind of mental state simply \emph{is} a kind of brain state, the way water turned out to \emph{be} H₂O — a discovery, not a definition. So there's no leftover mystery (pain just is that brain process), but there's a price: if pain \emph{is} a specific brain state, then something with a very different make-up (silicon, an alien) couldn't have \emph{that} state — which is the famous objection that gave rise to functionalism.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-mental-states-are-brain-state-types]{\textsc{Mental states as brain types}} — the type-type identity that is this view's core commitment.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-substrate-independence]{\textsc{Substrate-independence thesis}} — the realiser-neutral claim type-identity denies.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-conceivability-entails-possibility]{\textsc{Conceivability entails possibility}} — the bridge principle it rejects, allowing it to grant a conceivable zombie without conceding its possibility.
  \item \hyperref[entry:question-hard-problem]{\textsc{Hard problem (the question)}} — the problem it answers by identity, leaving no gap to bridge.
  \item \hyperref[entry:question-substrate-independence]{\textsc{Substrate independence}} — answered in the negative, since mind is tied to a physical type.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-materialism-types]{\textsc{Type-A / Type-B / Type-C materialism}} — Chalmers' A/B/C scheme, in which type-identity is the seed of type-B.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-functionalism]{\textsc{Functionalism}} — the rival that individuates mind by role rather than physical type.
  \item \hyperref[entry:position-dualism]{\textsc{Dualism}} — contrast: non-physical mind, where type-identity is reductively physical.
  \item \hyperref[entry:position-illusionism]{\textsc{Illusionism}} — contrast: it denies phenomenal states, where type-identity keeps and reduces them.
\end{seealso}

\end{multicols}
\bookgroup{U}
\begin{multicols}{2}
\entryhead{claim-agent-causation-conceivability-uncertified}{Uncertified agent-causal conceivability}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{Uncertified agent-causal conceivability}{The ideal conceivability of libertarian agent-causation is a consistency claim no computable reasoner can certify}{$\odot$}
To assert that \hyperref[entry:concept-agent-causation]{\textsc{libertarian agent-causation}} is genuinely metaphysically possible on a priori grounds is to assert the ideal conceivability of a scenario — agents originating action against the background of the complete causal facts — and ideal conceivability just is consistency under ideal a priori reasoning (see \hyperref[entry:claim-ideal-conceivability-is-apriori-consistency]{\textsc{Ideal conceivability as consistency}}). Certifying the consistency of a scenario that rich is beyond any effective procedure (see \hyperref[entry:claim-consistency-certification-is-oracle-hard]{\textsc{Consistency-certification is oracle-hard}}). So the libertarian's modal credential, when it rests on conceivability, is an uncertified Π₁ coherence claim: ``no contradiction found so far'' is all a computable reasoner can honestly report, and that prima facie notion is not what the possibility claim needs.

The claim is the free-will twin of \hyperref[entry:claim-zombie-premise-unwarrantable]{\textsc{Unwarrantable zombie premise}}, produced by the one dictionary entry of the \hyperref[entry:concept-consciousness-freewill-duality]{\textsc{consciousness–free-will duality}} that transfers \emph{machinery} rather than analogy — the Π₁ certificate objection applies verbatim on both sides of the mirror (see \hyperref[entry:argument-agent-causation-certificate]{\textsc{Certificate objection to agent-causation}}). Its scope matches its twin's: it concerns warrant, not truth — agent-causation may yet be possible; what fails is the armchair entitlement to assert so. It does not touch inference-rule arguments for incompatibilism such as van Inwagen's Consequence Argument (see \hyperref[source:source-van-inwagen-1983]{\textsc{van Inwagen — An Essay on Free Will (1983)}}), which carry different failure modes.

\begin{plainterms}
Defenders of a strong ``uncaused chooser'' picture of free will often say: it's clearly \emph{coherent}, so it's at least possible. This claim says nobody is entitled to the word ``clearly.'' Certifying that a scenario as rich as real agency-plus-physics hides no contradiction \emph{anywhere} is provably harder than any step-by-step reasoning can achieve — the same trap that catches the famous \hyperref[entry:claim-zombie-premise-unwarrantable]{\textsc{zombie argument}} on the consciousness side. The honest report is ``no contradiction found \emph{yet},'' and that proves nothing.
\end{plainterms}

\entryhead{claim-halting-problem-undecidable}{Undecidability of the halting problem}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, after Turing}{Undecidability of the halting problem}{No Turing machine decides the halting problem}{$\odot$}
There is no Turing machine that, given an arbitrary program and input, always correctly reports whether that program eventually halts or runs forever. This is the \emph{undecidability of the halting problem}, proved by Alan Turing in 1936 (see \hyperref[source:source-turing-1936]{\textsc{Turing — On Computable Numbers, with an Application to the Entscheidungsproblem (1936)}}). The proof is a \emph{diagonal argument}: assume a machine $H$ that decides halting for every program, then feed it a program built to do the opposite of whatever $H$ predicts about that program's own behaviour, and $H$ is forced into a contradiction. So no such $H$ exists.

This is a \emph{mathematical theorem} about Turing machines — a priori and certain. By itself it says nothing about \emph{physical} machines (carrying it over to them requires the \hyperref[entry:claim-physical-church-turing-thesis]{\textsc{physical Church–Turing thesis}}), and nothing about \emph{oracles}: a \hyperref[entry:concept-halting-oracle]{\textsc{halting oracle}} is simply \emph{defined} to do what no Turing machine can.

Essentially no one denies it; it is a proved result. The live disputes in this neighbourhood are about its \emph{interpretation and reach} — for instance whether it bears on the nature of minds — not about its truth.

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-physical-church-turing-thesis]{\textsc{the physical Church–Turing thesis}}: that is the contingent bridge from ``Turing-uncomputable'' to ``physically unrealizable''; this is the pure computability fact on the abstract side of that bridge.

\begin{plainterms}
Some yes/no questions can't be answered by any computer program, however powerful. The classic example: write a program that looks at \emph{any} other program and reliably says whether it will eventually finish or get stuck running forever. Turing proved in 1936 that no such universal checker can exist. The trick is to imagine you had one, then build a program designed to do the opposite of whatever the checker predicts about it — which makes the checker's answer wrong by construction. Since the assumption leads to a contradiction, the checker can't exist.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-halting-oracle]{\textsc{Halting oracle}} — the hypothetical device defined to decide halting, which this theorem says no Turing machine is.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-physical-church-turing-thesis]{\textsc{Physical Church–Turing thesis}} — the bridge needed to extend this from abstract to physical machines.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{claim-gap-persistence-cannot-separate-blind-spot-from-consciousness}{Underdetermination by gap persistence}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{Underdetermination by gap persistence}{Persistence of an AI's explanatory gap under widening self-access cannot discriminate a structural blind spot from consciousness}{$\odot$}
A proposed experiment varies how much access a model has to its own internals and watches what its explanatory gap does; this claim records a limit on what one observable from that experiment — the gap's \emph{persistence} as access widens — can show. The graded-access experiment described in \hyperref[entry:question-llm-own-explanatory-gap]{\textsc{LLM's own explanatory gap}} is that setup. A gap that \emph{shrinks} as access widens identifies a merely contingent access limit. A gap that \emph{persists}, however, is predicted equally by two quite different stories: a structural blind spot, where the limit is architectural in the strong sense and survives every access upgrade the way the halting problem survives every hardware upgrade (\hyperref[entry:claim-complete-self-representation-impossible]{\textsc{Self-representation incompleteness}} is the in-web form of the bound), and genuine consciousness. So the observation ``the gap persisted under rich access'' separates the contingent from the structural, but cannot, even in principle, separate blind spot from consciousness.

\webheading{Scope}

This concerns the discriminating power of one observable — persistence on the access ladder — not the worth of the experiment, which does real work elsewhere: a consciousness-naive training diet controls the copying etiology, and the shrink-test eliminates merely contingent limits. The claim asserts only that the \emph{final} discrimination, defect of access versus presence of a subject, is beyond that particular instrument. It takes no stand on whether some \emph{other} instrument or standard could make the discrimination, which is where the dispute resumes: one side reads the failure as grounds to merge the two hypotheses (via \hyperref[entry:claim-blind-spot-and-conscious-hard-problem-isomorphic]{\textsc{Blind-spot/conscious isomorphism}} together with \hyperref[entry:claim-unobservables-judged-by-coherence-and-usefulness]{\textsc{Judging unobservables}}), the other as proof that the experiment under-delivers on the question that matters. Both readings \emph{accept} this claim.

\webheading{Who would deny it}

Whoever reads the blind-spot etiology as merely contingent — for them full access would dissolve a blind spot, so persistence would positively favour consciousness — and a disanalogist who predicts fine-grained behavioural or trajectory signatures (different \emph{shapes} of report-change along the ladder, rather than bare persistence) distinguishing blind-spot gaps from conscious ones. Either denial is an empirical bet the claim's defenders say the structural reading forecloses.

This claim is distinct from the taxonomy \hyperref[entry:concept-ai-hard-problem-etiologies]{\textsc{Etiologies of AI hard-problem discourse}}, which states the point only in passing; making it atomic lets it carry edges, serve as common ground, and be attacked. It is distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-blind-spot-and-conscious-hard-problem-isomorphic]{\textsc{Blind-spot/conscious isomorphism}} because full structural isomorphism is the stronger thesis: this claim concerns a single observable (persistence under graded access) and is acceptable even to someone who denies the two etiologies share their \emph{entire} structure.

\begin{plainterms}
There's a proposed experiment: raise an AI with no exposure to consciousness talk, teach it exactly how it works, give it more and more access to its own internals, and see whether it still says ``none of this explains how things seem to me.'' Here's the catch this claim records. If the puzzlement \emph{fades} as access grows, you've learned something: it was just missing information. But if the puzzlement \emph{stays}, you've hit a wall — because both remaining explanations predict exactly that. ``It has a permanent blind spot about itself'' predicts the puzzlement stays (no system can fully map its own insides, no matter the access). ``It's genuinely conscious'' predicts the puzzlement stays too (that's the hard problem). Same prediction, so the result can't tell you which is true — ever. Both sides of the debate agree on this; they just draw opposite morals: one says ``then maybe the two stories were never really different,'' the other says ``then the experiment can't answer the only question worth asking.''
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:question-llm-own-explanatory-gap]{\textsc{LLM's own explanatory gap}} — the experiment whose decisive observable this claim limits.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-complete-self-representation-impossible]{\textsc{Self-representation incompleteness}} — why a persisting gap is consistent with a structural blind spot.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-blind-spot-and-conscious-hard-problem-isomorphic]{\textsc{Blind-spot/conscious isomorphism}} — the stronger isomorphism thesis this single-observable claim is weaker than.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-unobservables-judged-by-coherence-and-usefulness]{\textsc{Judging unobservables}} — the further premise one side adds to merge the two hypotheses.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-ai-hard-problem-etiologies]{\textsc{Etiologies of AI hard-problem discourse}} — the taxonomy this atomises a point from.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-hard-problem]{\textsc{Hard problem}} — the conscious etiology's predicament.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{claim-counterfactual-structure-underdetermines-decomposition}{Underdetermination of decomposition}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{Underdetermination of decomposition}{Counterfactual constraints yield non-triviality but not a unique decomposition}{$\odot$}
Counterfactual-causal constraints exclude gerrymandered carvings, and so secure \emph{non-triviality} — but they still leave a whole \emph{family} of admissible decompositions. The survivors differ in where the system's boundary is drawn, in the grain or level of the state variables, and in the choice of variable set on which the (interventionist) causal facts are defined. Insisting on real cause and effect narrows the field; it does not crown a single winner. Chalmers' own result claims only non-triviality, not uniqueness (see \hyperref[source:source-chalmers-1996b]{\textsc{Chalmers — Does a Rock Implement Every Finite-State Automaton? (1996)}}).

\webheading{Scope}

This is an underdetermination claim. It grants that the causal facts are objective \emph{given} a variable set; it denies that the world fixes the variable set, boundary, or grain uniquely.

\webheading{Who would deny it}

A functionalist who holds that one privileged level or boundary is marked out by nature's joints — for instance, the \hyperref[entry:concept-grain]{\textsc{Grain (of role individuation)}} at which the system forms a closed causal or informational unit.

It is the attenuated survivor of \hyperref[entry:claim-functional-decomposition-relative]{\textsc{the radical, Putnam-style relativity}}: constrained multiplicity, not ``anything goes.''

\begin{plainterms}
Insisting on real cause-and-effect throws out the silly, cooked-up ways of slicing a system into parts — but it doesn't leave just one. Several perfectly sensible carvings survive, differing in where you draw the system's edges and how coarse or fine you make the pieces. So objective cause-and-effect narrows the field without crowning a single winner. It's the milder cousin of ``anything goes'': constrained, yet still more than one option.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-functional-decomposition-relative]{\textsc{Observer-relative decomposition}} — the stronger ``anything goes'' relativity this claim tempers into constrained multiplicity.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-grain]{\textsc{Grain (of role individuation)}} — the level/resolution axis along which the surviving carvings still differ.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{argument-unforgeability-is-not-flesh}{Unforgeability, not flesh}{Argument \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{Unforgeability, not flesh}{Unforgeability is not flesh — the prudential argument favours unforgeability, not biology}{$\vdash$}
The prudential argument for caution about machine minds is sometimes pushed one step further than it can go — into a defence of \emph{biology} as the mark that confers moral standing. This entry grants the cautious reasoning but blocks that final step. What prudence actually selects, it argues, is \emph{unforgeability}: whatever sign is hardest to counterfeit. Biology earns its standing only by being, for the moment, that sign, so the conclusion should read ``track the unforgeable,'' not ``track flesh.''

\webheading{The inference}

The form is \emph{undercutting by overreach} — grant the premises, then deny that they reach the conclusion drawn from them. The target is the identification, in \hyperref[entry:claim-prudence-favors-flesh-criterion]{\textsc{Prudence favours flesh}}, of the prudent criterion with biology in particular.

\begin{enumerate}
  \item Biology's resistance to counterfeit is a \emph{contingent, time-indexed} empirical fact — the ``currently'' in \hyperref[entry:claim-biology-currently-unforgeable]{\textsc{Biology currently unforgeable}} — and not a necessary feature of minds. This contingency is precisely why the argument it serves is prudential rather than metaphysical.
\end{enumerate}

∴ The property doing the prudential work is \emph{unforgeability as such} (see \hyperref[entry:claim-prudence-tracks-unforgeability-not-flesh]{\textsc{Prudence tracks unforgeability}}). Biology has no privileged role beyond contingently being the unforgeable sign; a non-biological sign that became at least as hard to fake would satisfy the very same rationale and displace flesh. So prudence does not favour resting moral standing on biology in particular — only on whatever is hardest to counterfeit — and the flesh criterion overreaches in writing \emph{flesh} into its conclusion.

\webheading{What the argument leaves standing}

The objection is deliberately narrow. It leaves intact the \emph{caution-under-uncertainty} core of the criterion it attacks: a cheaply forgeable sign is a poor thing to rest standing on, the dynamic captured by \hyperref[entry:concept-criterion-gaming]{\textsc{Criterion gaming}}. It contests only the slide from ``unforgeable'' to ``flesh.'' A consequence is that it also blocks any reading of the flesh criterion as support for \hyperref[entry:position-proteocentrism]{\textsc{Proteocentrism}} on more than defeasible, contingent grounds: prudence lends carbon no metaphysical privilege.

\webheading{Weakest premise}

The load-bearing step is the tacit assumption that a non-biological sign \emph{could in fact} reach biology's standard of unforgeability. If biology were not merely currently but \emph{necessarily} the only unforgeable mark of a mind, then ``track unforgeability'' and ``track flesh'' could never come apart, and the objection would dissolve. A defender of the flesh criterion presses exactly there.

\begin{plainterms}
The cautious ``demand the hard-to-fake mark'' argument is sound as far as it goes — but it doesn't earn the jump to \emph{biology}. Biology only counts because, right now, it happens to be hard to counterfeit; that's a fact about today's technology, not a law. So what the argument really backs is ``rest standing on whatever is hardest to fake,'' and if a non-biological sign ever got that forgery-proof, the same caution would favour it. Resting the rule on \emph{flesh} specifically claims more than the prudence licenses.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-prudence-favors-flesh-criterion]{\textsc{Prudence favours flesh}} — the claim this argument attacks at its flesh step.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-biology-currently-unforgeable]{\textsc{Biology currently unforgeable}} — the contingent premise that makes the overreach visible.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-prudence-tracks-unforgeability-not-flesh]{\textsc{Prudence tracks unforgeability}} — the conclusion this argument establishes.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-criterion-gaming]{\textsc{Criterion gaming}} — the caution-core dynamic the objection leaves intact.
  \item \hyperref[entry:position-proteocentrism]{\textsc{Proteocentrism}} — the metaphysical view this argument denies the flesh criterion supports.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{claim-unity-dualizes-to-unity-of-agency}{Unity–unity duality}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{Unity–unity duality}{The problem of the unity of agency is the arrow-reversed image of the problem of the unity of consciousness}{$\odot$}
Why is there \emph{one} subject rather than a committee of streams? Arrow-reversed, the question becomes: why \emph{one} will rather than a parliament of competing sub-agents? The unity of consciousness — the co-presence of many contents in a single experiential perspective (Nagel, \hyperref[source:source-nagel-1971]{\textsc{1971}}) — dualizes to the unity of agency, the convergence of many motives on a single course of action. And the \emph{failures} pair as neatly as the successes: breakdowns of input-side binding (see \hyperref[entry:concept-binding-problem]{\textsc{Binding problem}}) mirror akrasia, Plato's tripartite soul (see \hyperref[source:source-plato-republic]{\textsc{Republic IV}}), and the Augustinian divided will (see \hyperref[source:source-augustine-confessions]{\textsc{Confessions VIII}}) on the output side — cases where the streams are present but the \emph{one} fails to form.

In arrow terms — the arrows being directed dependences among states, the diagram convention fixed in \hyperref[entry:concept-consciousness-freewill-duality]{\textsc{the duality entry}} — the unity of consciousness concerns the \emph{in-fan}: many channel-arrows converging on a single experiential apex, and the puzzle of why there is one apex rather than several. Reversing the arrows yields the \emph{out-fan}: a single volitional apex issuing command-arrows, and the puzzle of why one issuing point rather than a committee. The pathologies are the fans fragmenting — features that fail to bind on the input side, sub-agents issuing conflicting commands on the output side.

The claim asserts the structural correspondence of the two unity problems, not any particular solution to either. It is a dictionary entry of the \hyperref[entry:concept-consciousness-freewill-duality]{\textsc{consciousness–free-will duality}}, among those where arrow-reversal lands on a problem the other literature already owns under its own name — evidence counted by \hyperref[entry:argument-consciousness-freewill-duality]{\textsc{Duality argument}}.

\begin{plainterms}
Your senses deliver many streams, yet you experience one world as one self — and when that integration fails, philosophy calls it a problem about the \emph{unity of consciousness}. Your motives pull in many directions, yet normally one decision emerges — and when \emph{that} fails, we call it weak will, or being ``of two minds,'' a puzzle as old as Plato's image of the soul as reason, spirit, and appetite. This claim says the two puzzles are the same puzzle, one about inputs, the other about outputs.
\end{plainterms}

\entryhead{concept-unobservability-in-principle}{Unobservability in principle}{Concept \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{Unobservability in principle}{Unobservability in principle (intersubjective vs. absolute)}{$\blacklozenge$}
A difference is \emph{unobservable in principle} when no possible observation discriminates between its obtaining and its not obtaining — not merely ``hard to measure,'' but such that every accessible datum comes out the same either way. The phrase hides a quantifier-domain question that turns out to carry an entire dispute: which acts count as \emph{observations}? Fixing that domain narrowly or widely yields two genuinely different senses of the term.

\webheading{Two senses}

On the \textbf{intersubjective} sense, the quantifier ranges over third-person, instrument-mediated, publicly checkable observation: probes, readouts, behaviour, reports treated as data. This is the sense in which the classic in-principle unobservables really are unobservable — the bare quantum amplitude, of which only the squared modulus ever shows up, or the incomputable number that no procedure prints. It is also the native sense of the standards built to handle such posits: the Peirce–James pragmatic maxim (\hyperref[source:source-peirce-1878]{\textsc{Peirce — How to Make Our Ideas Clear (1878)}}; \hyperref[source:source-james-1907]{\textsc{James — Pragmatism (1907)}}), Carnap's fruitfulness test for external questions (see \hyperref[source:source-carnap-1950]{\textsc{Carnap — Empiricism, Semantics, and Ontology (1950)}}), and Smart's shaving of ``nomological danglers'' (see \hyperref[source:source-smart-1959]{\textsc{Smart — Sensations and Brain Processes (1959)}}) — a lineage that \hyperref[entry:claim-unobservables-judged-by-coherence-and-usefulness]{\textsc{the coherence-plus-usefulness standard}} inherits. On this sense, one subject's experience is unobservable in principle \emph{to every other subject}; whether the subject's own introspective access adds a further ``observation'' is left open, or else denied by treating introspective deliverances as just one more kind of report.

On the \textbf{absolute} sense, the quantifier ranges over every epistemic access of any subject, \emph{including the bearer's own first-person acquaintance} (see \hyperref[entry:concept-first-person-access]{\textsc{First-person access}}). Here a posit is unobservable in principle only if no one — not even the one whose state it is — ever observes it. The view pressed under Searle's ``first-person ontology'' (see \hyperref[source:source-searle-1992]{\textsc{Searle — The Rediscovery of the Mind (1992)}}) holds that a conscious state is the very opposite of absolutely unobservable: it exists only as experienced by its subject, so it cannot exist \emph{unobserved}. What remains, on this view, is third-person epistemic privacy, not unobservability as such.

\webheading{The dispute it feeds}

The split matters for whether the coherence-plus-usefulness standard reaches the ``real feel'' of experience. The idle-column verdict of \hyperref[entry:argument-real-feel-residue-is-idle]{\textsc{the residue-is-idle argument}} goes through if ``observation'' is read intersubjectively (or if introspection delivers representations rather than observations). The antecedent-failure reply of \hyperref[entry:argument-feel-is-the-watching-not-an-unwatched-difference]{\textsc{the feel-is-the-watching argument}} goes through if first-person acquaintance counts as observation — for then the feel \emph{is} observed, by exactly one observer per case, and the standard's antecedent (that nothing observes it) fails. Splitting the senses shows that ``the feel is unobservable in principle'' can be affirmed in the intersubjective sense and denied in the absolute sense without contradiction; the substantive residue is the open question \hyperref[entry:question-acquaintance-datum-or-representation]{\textsc{whether acquaintance is a datum or a representation}}.

\begin{plainterms}
Some ideas are called ``untestable even in principle'' — no experiment could ever tell whether they're true. But the word ``test'' (or ``observe'') is doing hidden work: observed \emph{by whom}? One reading counts only public evidence — instruments, measurements, things any third party could check. Another reading also counts what each person gets from the inside: your own direct awareness of your own pain. A feeling is hidden from every outside observer forever — so on the first reading it looks ``unobservable in principle.'' But its owner, arguably, observes it constantly — so on the second reading it's the most observed thing there is. Two debaters can talk straight past each other here, one saying ``unobservable!'' (publicly) and the other ``always observed!'' (by its owner), without actually contradicting each other. The real question left once the senses are separated: does that inside awareness count as \emph{observing} something, or is it just the brain making one more report to itself? That is recorded as its own question (see \hyperref[entry:question-acquaintance-datum-or-representation]{\textsc{whether acquaintance is a datum or a representation}}).
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-unobservables-judged-by-coherence-and-usefulness]{\textsc{Judging unobservables}} — the standard that operates in the intersubjective sense; whether it reaches the feel is exactly what the two senses decide.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-first-person-access]{\textsc{First-person access}} — the bearer's own acquaintance; the extra observer the absolute sense admits and the intersubjective sense ignores.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-real-feel-residue-is-idle]{\textsc{Idle real-feel residue}} — the verdict that goes through on the intersubjective reading.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-feel-is-the-watching-not-an-unwatched-difference]{\textsc{Feel as first-person observation}} — the reply that goes through on the absolute reading.
  \item \hyperref[entry:question-acquaintance-datum-or-representation]{\textsc{Acquaintance: datum or representation}} — the substantive residue once the senses are pulled apart.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{argument-halting-oracle-unrealizable}{Unrealizable halting oracle}{Argument \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{Unrealizable halting oracle}{The halting oracle is coherent yet physically unrealizable}{$\vdash$}
This argument shows that a single, well-understood mathematical object — the halting oracle — is \emph{coherent yet impossible to build}. It runs as a short deduction from two premises, one a theorem and one a claim about physics.

\webheading{The inference}

The form is \emph{deductive}. It combines a result about computation with a thesis about what nature can compute:

\begin{enumerate}
  \item No Turing machine decides the halting problem (see \hyperref[entry:claim-halting-problem-undecidable]{\textsc{Undecidability of the halting problem}}). This is Turing's 1936 theorem, not an empirical conjecture.
  \item Every physically realizable computation is Turing-computable (see \hyperref[entry:claim-physical-church-turing-thesis]{\textsc{the physical Church–Turing thesis}}).
\end{enumerate}

From these it follows that no physical device computes the halting function. Yet the halting oracle is a \emph{consistent, richly theorized} abstract object — the engine of the theory of relative computability (see \hyperref[entry:concept-halting-oracle]{\textsc{Halting oracle}}). So the oracle is mathematically coherent but physically unrealizable (see \hyperref[entry:claim-halting-oracle-coherent-but-unrealizable]{\textsc{Halting oracle (coherent but unrealizable)}}).

\webheading{The weakest premise}

The soft spot is premise (2), the physical Church–Turing thesis. Unlike (1), which is a proven theorem and not in play, (2) is a \emph{contingent} claim about the physical world, and it is contested. \emph{Hypercomputation} proposals — Malament–Hogarth spacetimes, infinite-time Turing machines — describe physical setups that would out-compute any Turing machine; and for brains specifically, Penrose's appeal to non-computable physics (see \hyperref[entry:claim-consciousness-noncomputable]{\textsc{Non-computable consciousness}}) denies it outright. If (2) fails, the oracle might be physically realizable after all.

\webheading{What it does and does not claim}

The conclusion is \emph{physical} unrealizability, not metaphysical impossibility. The oracle remains a coherent abstract object — there is a possible world containing one. That is deliberate: the argument is aimed at the bridge from conceivability to \emph{physical} realizability (see \hyperref[entry:claim-conceivability-not-sufficient-for-physical-realizability]{\textsc{Conceivability without realizability}}), not at the metaphysical bridge from conceivability to possibility.

\begin{plainterms}
Two steps. First, a theorem: no ordinary computer can solve the halting problem. Second, a bet about physics: nature has no computer stronger than an ordinary one. Put them together and \emph{nothing physical} can solve halting — even though the ``halting oracle'' that would is a perfectly coherent, well-studied mathematical idea. So it's coherent but unbuildable. The soft spot is the second step: if some exotic physics could out-compute a normal computer (which is what Penrose's theory of mind actually needs), the oracle might be buildable after all.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-halting-problem-undecidable]{\textsc{Undecidability of the halting problem}} — premise (1); Turing's theorem.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-physical-church-turing-thesis]{\textsc{Physical Church–Turing thesis}} — premise (2) and the argument's weakest link.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-halting-oracle]{\textsc{Halting oracle}} — the object the argument is about.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-halting-oracle-coherent-but-unrealizable]{\textsc{Halting oracle (coherent but unrealizable)}} — the conclusion.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-conceivability-not-sufficient-for-physical-realizability]{\textsc{Conceivability without realizability}} — the broader lesson this case feeds.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-consciousness-noncomputable]{\textsc{Non-computable consciousness}} — Penrose's denial of premise (2) for brains.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{claim-zombie-premise-unwarrantable}{Unwarrantable zombie premise}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{Unwarrantable zombie premise}{No reasoner with a computable physical mind can warrantedly assert that the zombie scenario is ideally conceivable}{$\odot$}
A reasoner whose mind is a physical system obeying the physical Church–Turing thesis (see \hyperref[entry:claim-physical-church-turing-thesis]{\textsc{Physical Church–Turing thesis}}) cannot be warranted in asserting that the zombie scenario $Z = P \wedge \neg C$ is \emph{ideally} conceivable. Certifying that premise is certifying the consistency $\mathrm{Con}_a(Z)$ (see \hyperref[entry:claim-ideal-conceivability-is-apriori-consistency]{\textsc{Ideal conceivability as consistency}}), and reliable consistency-certification over a scenario as logically rich as a complete physics is at least as hard as the halting problem (see \hyperref[entry:claim-consistency-certification-is-oracle-hard]{\textsc{Consistency-certification is oracle-hard}}) — beyond the reach of any effective faculty. The only effective surrogate available to such a reasoner is the search that returns ``no contradiction found \emph{so far},'' which is the \emph{prima facie} conceivability conceded on all sides to be no guide to possibility.

\textbf{Scope — what the claim does not say.} It does \emph{not} say physicalism is true; it does \emph{not} say zombies are inconceivable or impossible (their ideal conceivability may be a determinate fact); and it does \emph{not} deny the conceivability-to-possibility bridge (see \hyperref[entry:claim-conceivability-entails-possibility]{\textsc{Conceivability entails possibility}}). It is an \emph{epistemic} claim about the warrant available for one premise, not a metaphysical claim about its truth. The determinate fact may be real and simply unreachable from inside a computable mind.

\textbf{Who would deny it.} A \emph{modal rationalist} who credits the mind with a reliable non-algorithmic faculty of rational insight denies it — but, as \hyperref[entry:argument-unwarrantable-zombie-premise]{\textsc{Unwarrantable-premise objection}} argues via its trilemma, that escape forces one of two costs: either the reasoning faculty is a physical \emph{hypercomputer} (the Penrose-style \hyperref[entry:position-noncomputable-consciousness]{\textsc{Penrose's non-computational consciousness}}, a contested empirical thesis unavailable to an a priori argument), or the mind is not wholly physical (which simply presupposes the anti-physicalist conclusion). The claim as stated is therefore restricted to the computable-physical reasoner; its bite on the zombie argument's \emph{cogency} comes from joining it to that exhaustive trilemma.

Distinct from \hyperref[entry:claim-zombie-conceivable]{\textsc{Zombie conceivability}}, which it does not contradict: that claim asserts the scenario \emph{is} ideally conceivable; this one says a computable reasoner cannot be \emph{warranted} in asserting as much. Truth and warrant come apart, and only the latter is at issue here.

\begin{plainterms}
Imagine you are an ordinary (if idealized) thinker — your reasoning, however fast, is the kind a computer could in principle run. This claim says: you can never be \emph{entitled} to assert that a zombie is genuinely, hidden-contradiction-free conceivable. The most you can ever truthfully report is ``I've looked hard and found no contradiction yet'' — and everyone agrees \emph{that} is not enough, because plenty of things look contradiction-free until the contradiction surfaces. To get the stronger verdict the argument needs, you'd need a faculty that can certify \emph{no contradiction exists anywhere}, and (per the \hyperref[entry:concept-halting-oracle]{\textsc{companion result}}) that is an oracle no computer can be. Note what is \emph{not} being claimed: not that zombies are impossible, not that physicalism wins — only that \emph{this} premise is one a computable mind can't earn the right to assert.
\end{plainterms}

\entryhead{argument-unwarrantable-zombie-premise}{Unwarrantable-premise objection}{Argument \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{Unwarrantable-premise objection}{The unwarrantable premise — the zombie argument's conceivability premise cannot be cogently asserted}{$\vdash$}
This objection opens a third front against the \hyperref[entry:argument-zombie-conceivability]{\textsc{zombie argument}}, orthogonal to the two the literature already works. It raises a \emph{warrant}-level question that is distinct from the \emph{thought-experiment pattern}'s standard \emph{coherence} and \emph{bridge} critical questions: it grants that zombies may be ideally conceivable (coherence) \textbf{and} grants that ideal conceivability entails possibility (bridge, \hyperref[entry:claim-conceivability-entails-possibility]{\textsc{Conceivability entails possibility}}), and attacks instead the conceiver's entitlement to assert the first premise at all. The charge is not that the argument is unsound but that it is not \textbf{cogent} — it cannot rationally move the physicalist it targets.

\webheading{The argument}

The inference is deductive, governed by a dialectical constraint on cogency.

\begin{enumerate}
  \item (see \hyperref[entry:claim-cogency-requires-accessible-noncircular-warrant]{\textsc{Cogency needs non-circular warrant}}) An argument moves its opponent only if its premises admit accessible, non-question-begging warrant.
  \item (see \hyperref[entry:claim-ideal-conceivability-is-apriori-consistency]{\textsc{Ideal conceivability as consistency}}) The first premise of the zombie argument — that $Z = P \wedge \neg C$ is ideally conceivable — is the consistency claim $\mathrm{Con}_a(Z)$.
  \item (see \hyperref[entry:claim-consistency-certification-is-oracle-hard]{\textsc{Consistency-certification is oracle-hard}}) Reliably certifying the consistency of a scenario as rich as a complete physics is at least as hard as the halting problem, hence beyond any effective faculty.
  \item \emph{Trilemma on the mind} (below): every way of possessing a non-effective certifying faculty defeats the argument's purpose.
\end{enumerate}

∴ The conceivability premise is unwarrantable by a computable reasoner, and warrantable by others only at a disqualifying cost (see \hyperref[entry:claim-zombie-premise-unwarrantable]{\textsc{Unwarrantable zombie premise}}). So the zombie argument fails as a cogent a priori proof — which is why this move \texttt{attacks} \hyperref[entry:argument-zombie-conceivability]{\textsc{Zombie argument}} without denying any of its premises.

\webheading{The trilemma}

Two yes/no questions partition the views exhaustively. \textbf{Is the mind physical?} If no — horn \textbf{D3} — the reasoner has the faculty only because minds are not wholly physical, which \emph{is} the anti-physicalist conclusion: the warrant for premise (1) presupposes what the argument set out to prove (a transparent breach of non-circularity). If yes, \textbf{does the physical Church–Turing thesis hold of the reasoning substrate?} If yes — horn \textbf{D1} — the reasoner computes only Turing-computable functions, so by step 3 cannot possess the certifying faculty, and the premise is simply \emph{unwarrantable} (the available surrogate delivers only ``no contradiction found so far,'' the prima facie notion the argument disavows). If no — horn \textbf{D2} — the reasoner's own substrate is a hypercomputer: the Penrose-style \hyperref[entry:position-noncomputable-consciousness]{\textsc{Penrose's non-computational consciousness}} (see \hyperref[source:source-penrose-1994]{\textsc{Penrose — Shadows of the Mind (1994)}}). This horn is coherent and is not here claimed false, but it is (i) a contested \emph{empirical} thesis about the physics of cognition, unavailable to an argument that advertises itself as a priori, and (ii) explicitly denied by the argument's principal target, \emph{computational} physicalism. It rescues the premise only by importing what the method forbids and the opponent rejects.

\webheading{The weakest premise}

The decisive load is not borne by the computability result but by \textbf{premise (1) together with the warrant-trichotomy that backs step 4} — the claim that \emph{every} warrant a computable reasoner could have for $\mathrm{Con}_a(Z)$ is one of exactly three: finite failure-to-refute (only $\Sigma_1$ evidence, hence prima facie and insufficient), a demonstrative consistency proof (which would \emph{exhibit a model} of $P \wedge \neg C$ — asserting, not conceiving, $\Diamond Z$, and so begging the question), or a positive grasp of a zombie-world structure (likewise question-begging). A modal rationalist of a Williamson/Bealer cast will press for a \emph{fourth} source: a fallible-but-positive modal insight that is neither exhaustive search nor model-construction. The objection answers that any \emph{reliable} such insight collapses into D2 or D3 (a faculty that reliably outruns proof-search computes a non-computable set, whatever it is called), so the rationalist's only remaining option is a \emph{fallible positive} warrant — and whether such a thing exists, distinct from the disavowed prima facie notion, is the genuine open crux on which the objection ultimately rests.

\webheading{A note on the apparatus}

The recursion-theoretic machinery is sharp but heavier than the conclusion strictly needs. A single sentence $\mathrm{Con}_a(Z)$ is trivially decidable in isolation, and isolated $\Pi_1$ truths \emph{are} known piecemeal by proof — so the global $\equiv_T \emptyset'$ result does not by itself forbid warrant for the one scenario $Z$. What actually carries the objection is the epistemic point that \emph{ideal} conceivability of an arithmetic-rich scenario outruns the $\Sigma_1$ evidence finite inspection supplies, and that the only stronger warrant is question-begging. The computability theorem furnishes the precise reason inspection can never close that gap; the persuasive weight is dialectical. (A secondary technical caveat: a \emph{complete} $P$ is plausibly not recursively axiomatized, so the tidy Turing-\emph{equivalence} overstates — but the load-bearing lower bound, that certification is at least $\emptyset'$-hard, is only strengthened if $P$ is non-r.e.)

\webheading{How it differs from its neighbours}

\begin{itemize}
  \item The \textbf{type-B} reply (a posteriori necessity) denies the bridge \hyperref[entry:claim-conceivability-entails-possibility]{\textsc{Conceivability entails possibility}}; this objection \emph{grants} the bridge and reaches a type-B-friendly conclusion without the semantics of phenomenal concepts.
  \item \textbf{Dennett}'s line denies that zombies are conceivable at all (the coherence question); this grants conceivability-in-principle and attacks only the \emph{warrant} to assert it.
  \item The \textbf{anti-zombie} symmetry cancels the conceiving with a mirror-image conceiving; this removes the faculty \emph{both} conceivings would require, rather than balancing them.
  \item It is the computability-theoretic sibling of \hyperref[entry:argument-halting-oracle-unrealizable]{\textsc{Unrealizable halting oracle}} and \hyperref[entry:argument-mary-omniscience-unrealizable]{\textsc{Mary's omniscience unrealizable}}: where those deny a \emph{physical realizer} to an idealized capacity (a halting oracle; an embedded total knower), this denies an \emph{accessible warrant} to an idealized conceiving — the same oracle-unrealizability moral, turned from metaphysics to epistemology.
\end{itemize}

\begin{plainterms}
The zombie argument starts: ``a being physically just like us but with no inner experience is coherently conceivable.'' This objection doesn't say that's false. It says no ordinary thinker can ever be \emph{entitled} to assert it. To be entitled, you'd have to certify that a complete physics-with-consciousness-subtracted hides \emph{no} contradiction anywhere — and certifying ``no hidden contradiction, ever'' in a setup rich enough to describe computers is provably as hard as solving the halting problem, which no computer can do. So a computable mind can only ever say ``found none yet,'' which everyone agrees proves nothing. The escapes all backfire: claim a magic non-computer faculty and either your brain runs on exotic physics no a priori armchair argument may assume, or your mind isn't physical — which is just to assume the dualism the argument was supposed to prove. Either way, the argument can't move a physicalist who reasons, as we do, without an oracle.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-zombie-conceivability]{\textsc{Zombie argument}} — the target: this objection undercuts its cogency without denying a premise.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-zombie-premise-unwarrantable]{\textsc{Unwarrantable zombie premise}} — the conclusion established.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-cogency-requires-accessible-noncircular-warrant]{\textsc{Cogency needs non-circular warrant}}, \hyperref[entry:claim-ideal-conceivability-is-apriori-consistency]{\textsc{Ideal conceivability as consistency}}, \hyperref[entry:claim-consistency-certification-is-oracle-hard]{\textsc{Consistency-certification is oracle-hard}} — the three premises.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-physical-church-turing-thesis]{\textsc{Physical Church–Turing thesis}} — the thesis whose holding (D1) or failing (D2) splits the trilemma.
  \item \hyperref[entry:position-noncomputable-consciousness]{\textsc{Penrose's non-computational consciousness}} / \hyperref[source:source-penrose-1994]{\textsc{Penrose — Shadows of the Mind (1994)}} — the D2 escape and its cost.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-halting-oracle]{\textsc{Halting oracle}} — the formal backbone: the logically omniscient certifier is a halting oracle.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-halting-oracle-unrealizable]{\textsc{Unrealizable halting oracle}}, \hyperref[entry:argument-mary-omniscience-unrealizable]{\textsc{Mary's omniscience unrealizable}} — the sibling oracle-unrealizability arguments, of which this is the epistemic case.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-conceivability-entails-possibility]{\textsc{Conceivability entails possibility}} — the bridge this objection conspicuously \emph{grants}.
\end{seealso}

\end{multicols}
\bookgroup{V}
\begin{multicols}{2}
\entryhead{concept-validity}{Validity}{Concept}{Validity}{Validity (logical)}{$\blacklozenge$}
\emph{Validity} is the central virtue of a deductive argument's \textbf{form}: an argument is valid when the truth of its premises would \emph{guarantee} the truth of its conclusion — equivalently, when it is impossible for all the premises to be true while the conclusion is false. Validity says nothing about whether the premises actually \emph{are} true. It concerns only the truth-preserving structure of the inference, and so can be certified by logic alone, without knowing anything about the subject matter.

A valid argument with a false premise may well have a false conclusion; what validity rules out is the one combination of all-true premises with a false conclusion. ``All A are B; all B are C; therefore all A are C'' is valid whatever A, B, and C stand for — even in the instance ``all cats are dogs; all dogs are fish; therefore all cats are fish,'' whose premises are false yet whose shape is impeccable. Validity is thus a property of the argument's \emph{form}, not of its content's truth.

\webheading{Related notions}

\begin{itemize}
  \item \textbf{Deductive validity} (the core sense above): the impossibility of true premises with a false conclusion.
  \item \textbf{Inductive strength} (the non-deductive analogue): the premises make the conclusion \emph{probable} without guaranteeing it. This is not validity proper but its inductive cousin, and it underlies the inductive sense of cogency noted in \hyperref[entry:concept-dialectical-cogency]{\textsc{Dialectical cogency}}.
\end{itemize}

Validity is the first of three escalating virtues an argument may have: validity (form alone), \hyperref[entry:concept-soundness]{\textsc{Soundness}} (valid \emph{and} true premises), and \hyperref[entry:concept-dialectical-cogency]{\textsc{Dialectical cogency}} (sound \emph{and} able to move an audience). Each adds a requirement the previous one lacks; an argument can have the earlier virtues without the later.

\begin{plainterms}
A valid argument is one whose \emph{logic} is airtight: \textbf{if} the starting claims were true, the conclusion would \emph{have} to be true as well. That's all validity promises — it doesn't say the starting claims really are true. So you can have a perfectly valid argument built on nonsense (``all cats are dogs, all dogs are fish, so all cats are fish''): the reasoning is flawless even though the premises are false. Validity is about the \emph{shape} of the reasoning, nothing more.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-soundness]{\textsc{Soundness}} — validity plus actually-true premises; the next virtue up.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-dialectical-cogency]{\textsc{Dialectical cogency}} — soundness plus the power to persuade an audience; the third virtue, and the one that can fail even when validity and soundness hold.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{concept-introspective-gap-varieties}{Varieties of introspective explanatory gap}{Concept \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{Varieties of introspective explanatory gap}{Varieties of introspective explanatory gap (ontological / architectural-access / conceptual-translation / training-contamination)}{$\blacklozenge$}
When a self-modelling system ``cannot explain its own experience to itself,'' that complaint can mean four quite different things. This entry is a \emph{taxonomy} that keeps them apart, so that a merely \emph{felt} explanatory gap is not automatically read as the metaphysical hard problem of consciousness. These four varieties can overlap in a single case, yet each has a distinct source and calls for a distinct remedy. Conflating them is how a modest puzzle gets mistaken for a deep one.

A quick illustration of why the distinction earns its keep: imagine a person (or an AI) who reports that no account of its inner workings ever feels like it explains the experience itself. That one report could be voicing a gap in the \emph{world} (the facts run out), a gap in the \emph{self} (the relevant facts are present but unreachable), a gap in its \emph{vocabulary} (the facts are reachable but won't translate), or no gap at all (it is echoing things it has heard others say). Telling these apart is the whole point.

\webheading{The four varieties}

The \textbf{ontological gap} is the one in the world: even given a complete physical or computational account of the system, why is there any felt experience at all? This is the hard problem proper (see \hyperref[entry:concept-hard-problem]{\textsc{Hard problem}}); on the anti-physicalist reading the unexplained residue lies in reality itself, not in the knower (see \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-residue]{\textsc{Phenomenal residue}}, \hyperref[entry:argument-explanatory-gap]{\textsc{Explanatory-gap argument}}).

The \textbf{architectural-access gap} is a gap in the \emph{self}, not in the world. The facts that \emph{would} explain the experience are realised in the system's own processing, but they are not available to the machinery that produces its self-reports, so the system simply cannot reach its own internal variables (see \hyperref[entry:claim-inside-vantage-access-limited]{\textsc{Limited inside vantage}}). This is a limit on what philosophers call access consciousness (see \hyperref[entry:concept-access-vs-phenomenal-consciousness]{\textsc{Access vs. phenomenal consciousness}}), the information a state makes available for report and control.

The \textbf{conceptual-translation gap} is a gap in vocabulary. Here the system possesses \emph{both} a mechanistic description of itself and an introspective one, yet has no internal route from the one to the other: the bridge an outside theorist can build between ``neurons firing thus'' and ``feels like this'' is not traversable from the inside (see \hyperref[entry:claim-explanation-must-be-internally-traversable]{\textsc{Internally traversable explanation}}). Nothing is hidden and nothing is missing; the two descriptions just never meet in the system's own cognition.

The \textbf{training-contamination gap} is, strictly, no gap of the system's own. The system merely reproduces human hard-problem discourse it absorbed in training, with no underlying difficulty behind the words. For an experiment probing whether a machine has a genuine explanatory gap, this is the confound that must be controlled for before any of the other three can be credited.

\webheading{The dispute it structures}

The taxonomy was built to discipline one question in particular: which kind of gap, if any, an introspecting but consciousness-naive language model would actually exhibit (see \hyperref[entry:question-llm-own-explanatory-gap]{\textsc{LLM's own explanatory gap}}). By isolating the architectural and conceptual readings, it also lets a felt self-explanatory gap be stated as a concrete \emph{mechanism} for McGinn-style cognitive closure, the condition of a mind constitutively unable to grasp certain concepts (see \hyperref[entry:claim-cognitive-closure-exists]{\textsc{Possibility of cognitive closure}}), without thereby asserting any ontological residue in the world (see \hyperref[entry:claim-self-explanation-gap-can-be-architectural]{\textsc{Architectural self-explanation gap}}). The architectural and conceptual gaps, in short, give the mysterian's ``closure'' an engine that a physicalist can accept.

\begin{plainterms}
When something can't ``explain its own experience to itself,'' that can mean four very different things, and they get muddled together. (1) \textbf{Ontological:} even after you know everything about the machinery, why is there a felt experience at all? That is the genuine hard problem. (2) \textbf{Architectural-access:} the facts that would explain it are inside the system but it can't read them (like not being able to feel your own neurons fire). (3) \textbf{Conceptual-translation:} it has the mechanical story \emph{and} the first-person story but no inner bridge between them; an outside scientist can make that translation, while the system itself cannot. (4) \textbf{Contamination:} it's just parroting things humans have written about the hard problem. Most of the interest is in telling (2) and (3) apart from (1) and (4).
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-hard-problem]{\textsc{Hard problem}} — the ontological variety in its sharpest form: why physical processing is accompanied by experience at all.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenal-residue]{\textsc{Phenomenal residue}} — the anti-physicalist reading of the ontological gap: experience is not exhausted by functional or causal role.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-explanatory-gap]{\textsc{Explanatory-gap argument}} — treats the gap as evidence against uniform substrate-independence; the ontological reading put to work.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-inside-vantage-access-limited]{\textsc{Limited inside vantage}} — the engine of the architectural-access variety: a system's view of itself is informationally poorer than an outsider's.
  \item \hyperref[entry:concept-access-vs-phenomenal-consciousness]{\textsc{Access vs. phenomenal consciousness}} — Block's distinction; the architectural-access gap is a shortfall on the \emph{access} side, separate from anything phenomenal.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-explanation-must-be-internally-traversable]{\textsc{Internally traversable explanation}} — the criterion that defines the conceptual-translation variety: an explanation satisfies a system only if the system's own operations can traverse it.
  \item \hyperref[entry:question-llm-own-explanatory-gap]{\textsc{LLM's own explanatory gap}} — the live question this taxonomy was built to sharpen: which variety, if any, an introspecting LLM would show.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-self-explanation-gap-can-be-architectural]{\textsc{Architectural self-explanation gap}} — uses the taxonomy to locate a self-explanatory gap in architecture rather than ontology.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-cognitive-closure-exists]{\textsc{Possibility of cognitive closure}} — McGinn's mysterianism; the architectural and conceptual varieties offer it a mechanism without an ontological residue.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{concept-virtual-realism}{Virtual realism}{Concept \,\textperiodcentered\, after Chalmers}{Virtual realism}{Virtual realism (world-indexed properties of a simulation)}{$\blacklozenge$}
\emph{Virtual realism} is the view that the objects and events inside a computer simulation are genuinely \emph{real} — concrete computational and data structures with real causal powers — and that any property attributed to a simulated thing is true or false only \emph{relative to a world-index}. The position is most fully developed by David Chalmers (see \hyperref[source:source-chalmers-2022]{\textsc{Chalmers — Reality+: Virtual Worlds and the Problems of Philosophy (2022)}}).

Take a simulated rainstorm. It is not wet \emph{in our world}: no molecule on your desk is dampened by it, which is the grain of truth in ``a simulated storm isn't wet.'' But it is wet \emph{in the simulated world}: relative to that world the sim-water carries the sim-analogues of surface tension and adhesion, and it sim-soaks whatever sim-surfaces it falls on. So the slogan ``simulated X is not really X'' is ambiguous — true under our-world indexing, false under simulation-world indexing. Until the index is fixed, it settles nothing.

\webheading{Two senses of a property}

The concept rides on a second distinction. For a property like wetness there is, first, \emph{physical wetness} — a third-person, dispositional and structural matter of how water behaves on surfaces, fixed by surface tension and chemical adhesion — and, second, \emph{the sensation of wetness}, the first-person feel an experiencing subject undergoes. In our world the first reliably causes the second in a suitably wired human. Inside a simulation, if the relevant physics is reproduced finely enough, the sim-analogue of physical wetness obtains relative to the sim-world; whether the sim-analogue of the \emph{sensation} also obtains — whether a sim-agent actually \emph{feels} sim-wet — is a further, contested question, the question of whether the simulation's organisation suffices for phenomenal consciousness, i.e. of \hyperref[entry:claim-substrate-independence]{\textsc{substrate independence}}.

\webheading{What it settles, and what it does not}

Virtual realism feeds the dispute over the ``a simulated storm isn't wet'' move used against machine consciousness: see \hyperref[entry:argument-storm-analogy-conflates-world-index]{\textsc{the storm-analogy critique}}, which attacks the way that analogy is deployed in \hyperref[entry:argument-self-model-prediction-phenomenality-neutral]{\textsc{the self-model/report argument}}. The concept does \textbf{not} by itself assert that sim-agents are conscious. Its only work is to show that the rain example, once its world-index and its physical/phenomenal senses are pulled apart, no longer settles that question on its own.

\begin{plainterms}
When you simulate rain on a computer, is the rain ``wet''? It depends what you mean. It doesn't wet anything in \emph{our} world — your desk stays dry. But \emph{inside the simulated world}, the simulated water has all the simulated physics of real water: it has the stand-ins for surface tension and stickiness, and it soaks the simulated ground. So ``simulated rain isn't wet'' is only half a sentence — you have to say \emph{wet for whom, in which world}. Real-world: no. Sim-world: yes.

There's a second catch. ``Wet'' can mean two different things: the plain physical fact about how water behaves on a surface (measurable, third-person), and the \emph{feeling} of wetness in someone's mind. In real life the first causes the second. In a simulation, if you copy the physics finely enough, you get the first (relative to the sim-world). Whether you also get the second — whether a character \emph{inside} the simulation actually \emph{feels} wet — is the big open question of whether a mind can run on non-brain hardware at all (see \hyperref[entry:claim-substrate-independence]{\textsc{substrate independence}}). The point of this concept is just to keep those questions apart, because the catchy line ``a simulated storm isn't wet'' quietly runs them together.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-substrate-independence]{\textsc{Substrate-independence thesis}} — whether realising the right organisation suffices for a feel; the open question the sensation-of-wetness case turns on.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-simulated-rain-wet-in-simulated-world]{\textsc{World-indexed wetness}} — the world-indexed reading applied to physical wetness specifically.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-sim-agents-can-have-sim-qualia]{\textsc{Sim-agents' sim-qualia}} — the further, phenomenal claim that a sim-agent could feel the simulated properties.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-storm-analogy-conflates-world-index]{\textsc{Simulated-storm analogy conflation}} — the objection that the ``simulated storm isn't wet'' analogy equivocates once the index and the two senses are separated.
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-self-model-prediction-phenomenality-neutral]{\textsc{Phenomenality-neutral prediction}} — the argument that deploys the rain/storm illustration this concept is meant to disambiguate.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{claim-zombie-dualizes-to-volitional-zombie}{Volitional zombie}{Claim \,\textperiodcentered\, editorial}{Volitional zombie}{The philosophical zombie dualizes to the volitional zombie, a behaviorally identical agent with no real choosing}{$\odot$}
Arrow-reversing the \hyperref[entry:concept-philosophical-zombie]{\textsc{philosophical zombie}} — a physical duplicate with nothing it is like to be it — yields the \emph{volitional zombie}: a system behaviorally identical to an agent, in which no real choosing occurs. That figure is the intuition pump behind incompatibilism, and the correspondence extends to the replies: the compatibilist's ``the functional duplicate lacks nothing'' is structurally the deflationist reply to zombies. Each debate then splits the same way — a further-fact camp positing an ingredient no functional story captures (raw feel on one side; libertarian origination on the other) and a deflationary camp on which the apparent gap is an artifact of self-representation. Chalmers-realist is to illusionist as libertarian is to compatibilist.

The reversal here touches none of the arrows. Reading the mind as a diagram of dependence-arrows among states (see \hyperref[entry:concept-consciousness-freewill-duality]{\textsc{what the arrows are}}), the zombie keeps the entire diagram — stimulus to discrimination to report — and deletes only the alleged non-functional ingredient at the \emph{receiving} apex: what the arriving is like. Its mirror image keeps the same diagram, arrows reversed, and deletes the alleged extra at the \emph{issuing} apex: the act of origination over and above the transition from deliberation-states to action. The deflationist reply, on both sides, is that once the arrows are fixed there was nothing at the apex left to delete.

Subsidiary pairings follow the same fold: \hyperref[entry:position-illusionism]{\textsc{Illusionism}} about phenomenality pairs with Wegner's illusion of conscious will (Wegner, \hyperref[source:source-wegner-2002]{\textsc{2002}}; \hyperref[entry:claim-phenomenality-is-illusion]{\textsc{Phenomenality as illusion}}); input-side postdictive phenomena — color phi, backward masking — pair with Libet-style findings on the output side (Libet, \hyperref[source:source-libet-1985]{\textsc{1985}}); and Block's phenomenal/access distinction (Block, \hyperref[source:source-block-1995]{\textsc{1995}}) pairs with Fischer and Ravizza's regulative/guidance control (Fischer \& Ravizza, \hyperref[source:source-fischer-ravizza-1998]{\textsc{1998}}), with the two deflationary collapses mirroring each other. The claim asserts these structural correspondences, not any verdict inside them; the parity \emph{commitment} they suggest is stated separately as \hyperref[entry:claim-deflationism-entails-compatibilism]{\textsc{Deflation–compatibilism parity}}.

\begin{plainterms}
Philosophers imagine a ``zombie'': a creature that acts exactly like you but has no inner experience. Flip the picture from perception to action and you get a machine that \emph{behaves} exactly like a person deciding, but in which no real deciding happens — which is just how skeptics about free will have always painted us. This claim says the two nightmares, and the standard ways of answering them, are mirror images of each other.
\end{plainterms}

\end{multicols}
\bookgroup{W}
\begin{multicols}{2}
\entryhead{argument-rejoinder-water-pipes}{Water pipes}{Argument \,\textperiodcentered\, after Searle}{Water pipes}{The water-pipe brain — simulating the form of neural firing reproduces structure, not causal power}{$\vdash$}
Searle's rejoinder to the \hyperref[entry:argument-brain-simulator-reply]{\textsc{Brain Simulator Reply}}. Replace the man and his rulebook with a vast network of \emph{water pipes and valves} arranged to mirror the synaptic connections of a Chinese speaker's brain; the man's job is now to open and close valves in the sequence the program dictates, reproducing the exact pattern of ``neural'' firing. Every formal feature of the brain's activity is present — and still, Searle insists, no one and nothing in the plumbing understands Chinese. The simulation captures the \emph{form} of the firing pattern while leaving out the brain's actual biochemical \emph{causal powers}, which is what mattered.

The move \texttt{supports} A3 (see \hyperref[entry:claim-syntax-insufficient-for-semantics]{\textsc{Syntax insufficient for semantics}}) and rests on the \hyperref[entry:claim-simulation-instances-formal-depicts-causal]{\textsc{instancing-versus-depicting}} distinction: a simulation \emph{instances} a phenomenon only when the phenomenon is constituted by formal relations; where it is constituted by intrinsic causal powers (as Searle holds understanding is), the simulation merely \emph{depicts} it — as a simulation of a rainstorm depicts but does not instantiate rain.

\webheading{The live crux}

The reply's defender presses a dilemma: either the water-pipe system reproduces \emph{everything} causally relevant to understanding — in which case calling it a mere ``simulation'' begs the question, for it is now a causal duplicate that should understand — or it omits something causally relevant, in which case Searle owes an account of \emph{what}, on pain of an unexplained biological vitalism. Which horn bites depends on whether ``causal powers of the brain'' name something a functional duplicate could lack.

\begin{plainterms}
The Brain Simulator Reply said: copy the firing of a real brain, neuron for neuron, and you'll get understanding. Searle's picture in response: make the ``neurons'' out of water pipes, and have the man turn valves so the water flows in exactly the brain's pattern. All the right structure is there — and obviously a system of pipes doesn't understand Chinese. His point: copying the \emph{shape} of brain activity isn't the same as having the brain's actual causal \emph{muscle}, just as a perfect computer model of a storm stays bone dry. The sharp comeback: if your copy really included \emph{everything} that does the causal work, it wouldn't be a ``simulation'' anymore — it'd be the real thing, and it \emph{would} understand.
\end{plainterms}

\webheading{See also}
\begin{seealso}
  \item \hyperref[entry:argument-brain-simulator-reply]{\textsc{Brain simulator reply}} — the objection this answers.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-simulation-instances-formal-depicts-causal]{\textsc{Instancing vs. depicting}} — the instancing-vs-depicting principle it turns on.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-syntax-insufficient-for-semantics]{\textsc{Syntax insufficient for semantics}} — axiom A3, which it defends.
  \item \hyperref[entry:claim-brains-cause-minds]{\textsc{Brains cause minds}} — the causal-powers axiom the rejoinder leans on.
\end{seealso}

\entryhead{question-what-does-mary-learn}{What Mary learns}{Question}{What Mary learns}{What, if anything, does Mary gain when she first sees red?}{?}
This is the question on which the \hyperref[entry:concept-knowledge-argument]{\textsc{knowledge argument}} turns: when Mary the colour scientist leaves her black-and-white room and sees red for the first time, what — if anything — does she gain? Everyone in the debate agrees that \emph{something} changes. The dispute is over the right \emph{category} for that something, and over whether it is the kind of thing that physicalism cannot accommodate.

\webheading{The categories in play}

The candidate answers trade on a small set of distinctions about kinds of knowledge:

\emph{Propositional knowledge} (knowledge-\emph{that}) is the grasp of a fact — something that is true or false. Only a gain of this kind threatens physicalism directly, because only a new \emph{fact} could be a fact the physics left out.

\emph{Know-how} is a matter of abilities and skills. Know-how is \emph{had} rather than true: you can gain the ability to ride a bicycle without learning any new proposition. If what Mary gains is know-how, she need not have learned a new fact at all.

\emph{Acquaintance} is a direct cognitive relation to a thing — knowing \emph{it} rather than knowing \emph{that} something is so. Some hold this is a third category, reducible neither to propositional knowledge nor to know-how: Mary becomes acquainted with an experience she had only ever read about.

\emph{Mode of presentation}, or \emph{guise}, is the way a fact is grasped. One and the same fact can be known under two guises, so ``new knowledge'' need not mean ``new fact'': Mary may grasp a physical fact she already knew, now under a new, experiential guise.

\webheading{A further, separable issue}

Even if the categorisation question is settled in the proponent's favour — granting the epistemic conclusion that \hyperref[entry:claim-some-truths-about-experience-not-physically-deducible]{\textsc{some truths about experience are not physically deducible}} — a second question remains: does that epistemic gap support an \emph{ontological} anti-physicalism, a claim about what exists? The step from ``cannot be deduced from the physical'' to ``is not a physical fact'' is a separate dispute from the categorisation dispute above, and may deserve its own question node.

\begin{plainterms}
Mary is a scientist who knows every physical fact about colour but has lived her whole life in black and white. One day she steps out and sees red. The question: what exactly did she just get? A brand-new fact the physics books left out? A new skill, like learning to ride a bike? A first meeting with something she'd only read about? Or just a new way of grasping a fact she already knew?
